<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Sensor Performance Characteristics — The "Vital Statistics" of a Sensor

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, a sensor is a device that converts a physical, chemical, or biological stimulus into a measurable electrical signal. Before any sensor can be deployed in a robotic system, industrial automation line, or IoT application, its **performance must be quantified** using a standardized set of static and dynamic characteristics. These characteristics form the *lingua franca* of instrumentation engineering.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Module 1 explicitly tests the student's ability to **define, distinguish, and apply** the following sensor metrics. Marks are frequently awarded for crisp definitions and labeled diagrams.

---

### 1.2 Definitions with Real-World Analogies

#### 1. **Sensitivity (S)**
- **Formal Definition:** Sensitivity is the ratio of the change in output signal to the corresponding change in the input physical quantity (measurand). Mathematically, it is the **slope of the calibration curve** at a specific operating point.
- **Units:** Volts per unit of measurand (e.g., V/°C, V/N, mV/mm).
- **Real-World Analogy:** Imagine two bathroom scales — Scale A deflects the needle 1 cm when you place a 1 kg weight, while Scale B deflects 5 cm for the same 1 kg. Scale B is **5× more sensitive**. A highly sensitive sensor "notices" smaller changes in the environment.

> [!NOTE]
> High sensitivity does **not** automatically mean a good sensor. A sensor can be highly sensitive to the desired measurand but also to unwanted inputs (noise, temperature drift) — this is captured in the **selectivity** metric.

#### 2. **Linearity**
- **Formal Definition:** Linearity describes how closely the sensor's actual output (calibration curve) follows a **straight line** over its operating range. It is quantified as the **maximum deviation** of the actual curve from the best-fit straight line, expressed as a percentage of the full-scale output.
- **Ideal Behavior:** A perfectly linear sensor obeys $y = mx + c$ exactly, where $m$ is constant sensitivity.
- **Real-World Analogy:** Think of a mercury thermometer. Ideally, every 1 °C rise should expand the mercury by exactly the same amount. In reality, the expansion is *almost* linear between 0 °C and 100 °C, but deviates at the extremes. That deviation is the **non-linearity error**.

#### 3. **Measurement Range / Dynamic Range**
- **Measurement Range:** The span between the **minimum** and **maximum** values of the input that the sensor can reliably measure.
- **Dynamic Range (DR):** The ratio of the largest measurable input to the smallest detectable input, typically expressed in **decibels (dB)**:
$$\text{DR (dB)} = 20 \log_{10}\left(\frac{I_{\max}}{I_{\min}}\right)$$
- **Real-World Analogy:** A human ear can hear from a whisper (~20 µPa) to a jet engine (~200 Pa). That huge span — about 10 million to 1 — is its dynamic range (~140 dB). A cheap microphone may only span 60 dB, missing the extremes.

#### 4. **Response Time (τ)**
- **Formal Definition:** The time required for the sensor output to reach a specified percentage (commonly **90 %** or **63.2 %**) of its final steady-state value in response to a step change in the input.
- **Real-World Analogy:** When you step into a hot shower, the water temperature takes a few seconds to stabilize. That delay is the *response time* of the shower's thermostat + your skin's thermal response.

#### 5. **Accuracy**
- **Formal Definition:** The closeness of a measured value to the **true (accepted) value** of the measurand. Accuracy is expressed as **% of Full Scale (FS)** or **% of Reading**.
- **Real-World Analogy:** If the actual room temperature is 25.0 °C, a thermometer reading 24.9 °C is **highly accurate**, while one reading 30 °C is **inaccurate**.

#### 6. **Repeatability**
- **Formal Definition:** The ability of a sensor to produce the **same output** for the same input, when the input is approached from the same direction, under identical environmental conditions, over multiple trials.
- **Real-World Analogy:** A reliable weighing scale will display "70.0 kg" every time the same person steps on it, day after day, without any drift.

#### 7. **Precision**
- **Formal Definition:** Precision describes the **degree of mutual agreement** among a series of repeated measurements — i.e., how tightly the readings cluster, *regardless of how close they are to the true value*.
- **Real-World Analogy:** A dartboard: precision means all darts land close together (tight cluster), accuracy means they hit the bullseye. A sensor can be **precise but inaccurate** (systematic bias), or **accurate but imprecise** (random scatter around the true value), or both.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Favorite Question:** *"Distinguish between Accuracy, Repeatability, and Precision with a suitable diagram."* (3 or 7 marks)

#### 8. **Resolution**
- **Formal Definition:** The **smallest detectable change** in the input that produces a distinguishable change in the output. Resolution defines the **finest gradation** the sensor can resolve.
- **Real-World Analogy:** A digital weighing scale that reads in steps of 0.1 kg has a resolution of 100 g. A more expensive one with 0.01 kg steps has 10× better resolution.

#### 9. **Threshold**
- **Formal Definition:** The **minimum input magnitude** required to produce a detectable output. Below the threshold, the sensor output is indistinguishable from zero (or noise floor).
- **Real-World Analogy:** The human eye cannot see a single photon — there is a minimum number of photons (≈ 5–7) needed to trigger a visual response. That is the threshold of human vision.

#### 10. **Bandwidth (BW)**
- **Formal Definition:** The range of input signal frequencies over which the sensor responds with acceptable amplitude and phase characteristics. It is typically defined as the frequency at which the output power drops to **half (-3 dB point)** of its maximum.
- **Real-World Analogy:** A hi-fi speaker has a stated bandwidth of 20 Hz – 20 kHz. Below 20 Hz (sub-bass) and above 20 kHz (ultrasonic) it cannot reproduce the sound faithfully. A sensor's bandwidth tells you how "fast" the events it can track can be.

---

## 1.3 Sensor Classification — A Bird's-Eye View

Sensors are classified based on several criteria. The KTU 2024 syllabus emphasizes classification by **function/application**:

| Class | Sub-Classes | What They Sense |
|---|---|---|
| **Exteroceptive** | Touch, Force, Proximity, Vision | External environment |
| **Proprioceptive / Internal** | Position, Velocity, Acceleration, Torque | Robot's own state |
| **Position Encoders** | Linear / Rotary, Incremental / Absolute | Angular or linear displacement |

> [!NOTE]
> **Exteroceptive** = "extero" (external) — sensors that perceive the **outside world**.
> **Proprioceptive** = "proprio" (self) — sensors that perceive the **robot's own internal state**, like joint angle, wheel speed, battery voltage.

### 1.3.1 Touch, Force, Proximity, Vision Sensors — Exteroceptive

- **Touch Sensor:** Detects physical contact. Simplest form: a mechanical microswitch. Advanced form: capacitive/resistive touch panels.
- **Force Sensor:** Quantifies the magnitude of contact force. Common technologies: strain gauge load cells, piezoelectric force sensors.
- **Proximity Sensor:** Detects the *presence* of an object **without physical contact**. Technologies: inductive, capacitive, ultrasonic, infrared.
- **Vision Sensor:** Captures 2D/3D images of the environment. Technologies: CCD, CMOS cameras, LiDAR.

### 1.3.2 Proprioceptive / Internal Sensors
These tell the robot about **itself** — joint angles (encoders), wheel speed (tachometers), acceleration (IMU), battery state, motor current, etc.

### 1.3.3 Position Sensors — Encoders
Encoders are electromechanical devices that convert **linear or angular position** into an electrical signal (typically a digital pulse train or a coded word). They are the workhorses of motion control.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Encoders are the most frequently tested topic in Module 1.** Expect a 14-mark ESE question comparing incremental vs. absolute, or linear vs. rotary.

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Deep Dive into Sensor Static Characteristics

### 2.1.1 Sensitivity (S) — The Slope

For a sensor with input $x$ and output $y$, sensitivity is mathematically defined as:

$$S = \frac{\Delta y}{\Delta x} = \frac{dy}{dx}$$

For a linear sensor, $y = mx + c$, the sensitivity is **constant** and equals $m$. For a non-linear sensor, sensitivity is the **derivative at the operating point**, which means it varies with the input.

**Why Sensitivity Matters in Engineering:**
- High sensitivity enables the detection of minute signals (medical diagnostics, scientific instruments).
- Low sensitivity may be desirable in **high-amplitude** applications to avoid saturation (e.g., accelerometers in car crash testing).

### 2.1.2 Linearity — Best-Fit Straightness

The **non-linearity error** is computed as:

$$\text{Non-linearity (\% FS)} = \frac{\Delta y_{\max}}{y_{FS}} \times 100$$

where $\Delta y_{\max}$ is the maximum deviation of the actual curve from the best-fit straight line, and $y_{FS}$ is the full-scale output.

**Why Linearity Matters:**
- Linear sensors are **easy to calibrate** (single multiplication factor).
- Non-linear outputs require **lookup tables** or **polynomial correction** in the microcontroller.
- In analog-to-digital systems, linearity directly affects the **effective number of bits (ENOB)**.

### 2.1.3 Static vs. Dynamic Characteristics

| Type | Characteristics Covered |
|---|---|
| **Static** | Sensitivity, Linearity, Accuracy, Repeatability, Precision, Resolution, Threshold, Range, Drift, Hysteresis |
| **Dynamic** | Response time, Time constant, Bandwidth, Rise time, Settling time, Damping ratio, Natural frequency |

### 2.1.4 The Critical Distinction: Accuracy vs. Precision

| Property | Question Answered | Affected By |
|---|---|---|
| **Accuracy** | "How close to the true value?" | Systematic errors (calibration, bias) |
| **Precision** | "How close are repeated measurements to each other?" | Random errors (noise, jitter) |
| **Repeatability** | "Can it reproduce the same reading under identical conditions?" | Sensor fatigue, hysteresis, drift |

### 2.1.5 Resolution, Threshold & Dynamic Range

These three are **interrelated**:

- **Threshold** is the smallest input that produces a *detectable non-zero* output.
- **Resolution** is the smallest input *change* that produces a *detectable output change*.
- **Dynamic Range** = $\frac{\text{Maximum measurable input}}{\text{Minimum resolvable input}}$ — it spans the entire operational envelope.

In an ideal **n-bit** digital system:
$$\text{Resolution (quantization)} = \frac{\text{Full Scale Range}}{2^n}$$

---

## 2.2 Dynamic Characteristics — Frequency Response

### 2.2.1 First-Order System Model

Most sensors can be modeled as a first-order low-pass filter:

$$\tau \frac{dy}{dt} + y(t) = K \cdot x(t)$$

where $\tau$ is the time constant, $K$ is the static sensitivity.

**Step Response** (for $x(t) = X_0 \cdot u(t)$):

$$y(t) = K X_0 \left(1 - e^{-t/\tau}\right)$$

Key observations:
- At $t = \tau$, $y(\tau) = 0.632 \, K X_0$ → 63.2 % of final value
- At $t = 5\tau$, $y \approx 0.993 \, K X_0$ → effectively settled (used as "5τ rule")
- **Time constant $\tau$** = product of resistance and capacitance (electrical), or mass and damping coefficient (mechanical).

### 2.2.2 Bandwidth

For the first-order system, the transfer function is:

$$H(j\omega) = \frac{K}{1 + j\omega\tau}$$

The magnitude is:

$$\vert H(j\omega) \vert = \frac{K}{\sqrt{1 + (\omega \tau)^2}}$$

At $\omega = 0$ (DC), $\vert H \vert = K$ (max). As $\omega \to \infty$, $\vert H \vert \to 0$.

The **-3 dB bandwidth** is the frequency at which the power drops to half:

$$\omega_{BW} = \frac{1}{\tau} \quad \Rightarrow \quad f_{BW} = \frac{1}{2\pi\tau}$$

---

## 2.3 Position Sensors — Encoders: Deep Analysis

### 2.3.1 Why Encoders?
Encoders are the **backbone of motion control** in robotics, CNC machines, and servo systems. They provide **real-time, high-resolution** position feedback.

### 2.3.2 Linear Encoder vs. Rotary Encoder

| Feature | Linear Encoder | Rotary Encoder |
|---|---|---|
| **Measures** | Linear displacement along a straight axis | Angular displacement of a rotating shaft |
| **Output** | Pulses or absolute code per unit length | Pulses or absolute code per revolution |
| **Typical Use** | CNC machine slides, printers, 3D printer X-Y axes | Robot joints, motor shafts, steering wheels |
| **Form Factor** | Strip + readhead | Disc + readhead |

### 2.3.3 Incremental vs. Absolute — The Core Distinction

| Feature | Incremental Encoder | Absolute Encoder |
|---|---|---|
| **Output** | Pulses; position is **relative** to a reference (index) pulse | Unique code for **every** position |
| **Power Loss** | **Loses position** — needs re-homing on power-up | **Retains position** — no re-homing needed |
| **Resolution** | Determined by pulses per revolution (PPR) | Determined by number of tracks (bits) |
| **Cost** | Lower | Higher |
| **Speed** | Higher max speed | Limited by code bit transitions |
| **Complexity** | Simple counter logic | Requires code interpretation (Gray, Binary) |

### 2.3.4 Incremental Linear Encoder

- A **scale (strip)** with equally spaced markings (optical, magnetic, or capacitive).
- A **readhead** detects markings as the strip moves.
- Outputs **two square waves (A and B)** in **quadrature** (90° phase shift) — allows direction detection.
- An **index pulse (Z or I)** gives a once-per-revolution/once-per-length reference.

**Resolution Enhancement by Quadrature Decoding:**

$$R_{\text{effective}} = 4 \times \text{Lines per unit length}$$

> *Example:* A linear encoder with 1000 lines/mm and 4× decoding gives an effective resolution of **4 µm per count**.

### 2.3.5 Absolute Linear Encoder

- The scale contains **multiple parallel tracks**, each with distinct markings representing different bit weights.
- A readhead with **multiple sensors** reads all tracks simultaneously.
- The combined bit pattern gives a **unique binary (or Gray) code** for every position.
- **No homing required** — position is known instantly on power-up.

### 2.3.6 Incremental Rotary Encoder

- A **disc** with radial slits (slots) rotates between a light source and a photodetector.
- Outputs two channels A and B in quadrature.
- Resolution is specified in **PPR (Pulses Per Revolution)**.
- Disc resolution: $\text{Angular resolution} = \frac{360°}{\text{PPR} \times 4}$ (with 4× decoding)

### 2.3.7 Absolute Rotary Encoder

- Disc has **N concentric tracks**, each track being either opaque or transparent at a given angular position.
- With N tracks, the encoder can resolve $2^N$ unique positions per revolution.
- **Resolution:** $\text{Angular resolution} = \frac{360°}{2^N}$
- Output code is often **Gray code** (only one bit changes between adjacent positions) to prevent false readings due to misaligned track transitions.

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorize this table** — every KTU ESE question on Module 1 will use at least one of these formulas.

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Sensitivity | $S = \dfrac{\Delta y}{\Delta x} = \dfrac{dy}{dx}$ | Output units / Input units (V/°C, m/N) |
| 2 | Non-linearity | $\%NL = \dfrac{\Delta y_{\max}}{y_{FS}} \times 100$ | Expressed as % of Full Scale |
| 3 | Dynamic Range | $DR_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{I_{\max}}{I_{\min}}\right)$ | Decibels (dB) |
| 4 | Resolution (ADC) | $\text{Res} = \dfrac{FSR}{2^n}$ | Where $n$ = number of bits |
| 5 | Time constant | $\tau = RC$ (electrical) or $\tau = m/b$ (mechanical) | Seconds |
| 6 | Settling time | $t_s \approx 5\tau$ (within 1 % of final) | Seconds |
| 7 | 63.2 % response | $y(\tau) = 0.632\,Kx_0$ | One time constant |
| 8 | Bandwidth (-3 dB) | $f_{BW} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi\tau}$ | Hertz |
| 9 | Encoder resolution (linear) | $R = \dfrac{\text{Line spacing}}{4}$ (with 4× quadrature) | meters / count |
| 10 | Encoder resolution (rotary) | $\theta_{\min} = \dfrac{360°}{N_{PPR} \times 4}$ (incremental, 4×) | degrees / count |
| 11 | Absolute encoder resolution | $\theta_{\min} = \dfrac{360°}{2^N}$ | $N$ = number of tracks / bits |
| 12 | Linear displacement from pulses | $x = \dfrac{N_{\text{pulses}}}{\text{Lines per mm}}$ | mm or m |
| 13 | Angular displacement from pulses | $\theta = \dfrac{N_{\text{pulses}} \times 360°}{N_{PPR}}$ | degrees |
| 14 | Hysteresis error | $\%H = \dfrac{y_{FS,\text{up}} - y_{FS,\text{down}}}{y_{FS}} \times 100$ | % FS |

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application | Sensors Used | Critical Characteristic |
|---|---|---|
| **Robotic arm** (welding) | Position encoders, force sensor | Repeatability, Resolution |
| **CNC milling** | Linear encoders on all axes | Resolution, Linearity |
| **Automotive airbag** | Accelerometer (MEMS) | Response time, Bandwidth |
| **Medical ventilator** | Pressure sensor, flow sensor | Accuracy, Reliability |
| **Smartphone (touch screen)** | Capacitive touch array | Resolution, Response time |
| **Industrial sorting line** | Vision sensor, proximity sensor | Dynamic range, Accuracy |
| **Servo motor control** | Rotary encoder | Resolution, Bandwidth, Repeatability |

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: First-Order Sensor Step Response

**Starting point:** A typical sensor (e.g., a thermistor in a thermal bath, or a strain gauge in a load cell) can be modeled as a first-order system with differential equation:

$$\tau \frac{dy(t)}{dt} + y(t) = K \cdot x(t)$$

where:
- $y(t)$ is the sensor output
- $x(t)$ is the input measurand
- $\tau$ is the time constant
- $K$ is the static sensitivity

**Step 1:** Apply a step input $x(t) = X_0 \cdot u(t)$ (where $u(t)$ is the unit step).

The steady-state (DC) solution is found by setting $\frac{dy}{dt} = 0$:

$$y_{ss} = K X_0$$

**Step 2:** Solve the homogeneous part $\tau \frac{dy}{dt} + y = 0$:

$$y_h(t) = A e^{-t/\tau}$$

**Step 3:** General solution is the sum of particular + homogeneous:

$$y(t) = K X_0 + A e^{-t/\tau}$$

**Step 4:** Apply the initial condition $y(0) = 0$ (sensor at rest at $t = 0$):

$$0 = K X_0 + A \cdot 1 \quad \Rightarrow \quad A = -K X_0$$

**Step 5:** Final step response:

$$\boxed{y(t) = K X_0 \left(1 - e^{-t/\tau}\right)}$$

**Step 6:** Key time values:

$$y(\tau) = K X_0 (1 - e^{-1}) = 0.632 \, K X_0$$

$$y(2\tau) = K X_0 (1 - e^{-2}) = 0.865 \, K X_0$$

$$y(3\tau) = K X_0 (1 - e^{-3}) = 0.950 \, K X_0$$

$$y(5\tau) = K X_0 (1 - e^{-5}) = 0.993 \, K X_0 \approx K X_0$$

This justifies the **"5τ rule"** — the sensor effectively settles in five time constants.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Bandwidth from Frequency Response

**Starting point:** The transfer function of a first-order sensor:

$$H(j\omega) = \frac{Y(j\omega)}{X(j\omega)} = \frac{K}{1 + j\omega\tau}$$

**Step 1:** Compute the magnitude squared:

$$\vert H(j\omega) \vert^2 = \frac{K^2}{1 + (\omega\tau)^2}$$

**Step 2:** The -3 dB bandwidth is defined as the frequency where the output power drops to half its maximum. Since power is proportional to $\vert H \vert^2$:

$$\frac{\vert H(j\omega_{BW}) \vert^2}{\vert H(0) \vert^2} = \frac{1}{2}$$

**Step 3:** Substituting:

$$\frac{1}{1 + (\omega_{BW} \tau)^2} = \frac{1}{2}$$

**Step 4:** Cross-multiply:

$$1 + (\omega_{BW} \tau)^2 = 2$$

**Step 5:** Solve for $\omega_{BW}$:

$$(\omega_{BW} \tau)^2 = 1 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \omega_{BW} = \frac{1}{\tau}$$

**Step 6:** Convert to Hz:

$$\boxed{f_{BW} = \frac{\omega_{BW}}{2\pi} = \frac{1}{2\pi\tau}}$$

**Engineering Insight:** A sensor with a small time constant $\tau$ has a **large bandwidth** — it can track rapidly changing inputs. This is why MEMS accelerometers used in crash sensing are designed for very small $\tau$.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Absolute Encoder Resolution

**Starting point:** An absolute rotary encoder has $N$ concentric tracks on its disc. Each track is patterned in a binary fashion — each angular position corresponds to a unique $N$-bit code.

**Step 1:** Total number of unique codes possible with $N$ bits:

$$M = 2^N$$

**Step 2:** The disc rotates through a full revolution of $360°$.

**Step 3:** Each unique code corresponds to a unique angular position. The minimum resolvable angular increment is:

$$\boxed{\theta_{\min} = \frac{360°}{2^N}}$$

**Numerical Example:** A 10-bit absolute encoder has:
- $2^{10} = 1024$ unique positions
- $\theta_{\min} = 360° / 1024 \approx 0.352°$ per count

**Upgrade path:**
- 12-bit encoder → $\theta_{\min} = 360° / 4096 \approx 0.088°$
- 16-bit encoder → $\theta_{\min} = 360° / 65536 \approx 0.0055°$

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Incremental Encoder Resolution with Quadrature Decoding

**Starting point:** A rotary incremental encoder has $N_{PPR}$ pulses per revolution on channel A, and another $N_{PPR}$ pulses on channel B, with B shifted 90° from A.

**Step 1:** With a single channel, each rising edge represents one count, giving $N_{PPR}$ counts per revolution.

**Step 2:** With quadrature decoding, **four** transitions are detected per cycle (rising A, rising B, falling A, falling B). The effective count per revolution becomes:

$$N_{\text{effective}} = 4 \times N_{PPR}$$

**Step 3:** Minimum resolvable angle:

$$\boxed{\theta_{\min} = \frac{360°}{4 \times N_{PPR}} = \frac{90°}{N_{PPR}}}$$

**Numerical Example:** A 1000 PPR encoder with 4× decoding:
- $N_{\text{effective}} = 4000$ counts/rev
- $\theta_{\min} = 360° / 4000 = 0.09°$ per count

---

## 3.5 Worked Example 1: Sensor Sensitivity & Linearity

**Problem:** A temperature sensor produces an output voltage described by:
$$V(T) = 0.05 T^2 - 0.1 T + 1.0 \quad \text{(V, with T in °C)}$$

Calculate:
(a) Sensitivity at $T = 25$ °C.
(b) Sensitivity at $T = 100$ °C.
(c) Comment on linearity.

**Solution:**

**(a)** Sensitivity is $\dfrac{dV}{dT}$:

$$\frac{dV}{dT} = 0.10 T - 0.10$$

At $T = 25$ °C:

$$S_{25} = 0.10 (25) - 0.10 = 2.50 - 0.10 = \boxed{2.40 \text{ V/°C}}$$

**(b)** At $T = 100$ °C:

$$S_{100} = 0.10 (100) - 0.10 = 10.00 - 0.10 = \boxed{9.90 \text{ V/°C}}$$

**(c)** The sensitivity is **not constant** — it changes with temperature. This indicates the sensor is **non-linear**. As $T$ increases, the sensitivity increases, making the sensor increasingly responsive at higher temperatures. Such a sensor would require **temperature-dependent calibration**.

---

## 3.6 Worked Example 2: Encoder Position Calculation

**Problem:** A linear incremental encoder has 500 lines/mm. A readhead with 4× quadrature decoding is used. The controller counts 12,000 pulses. What is the linear displacement?

**Solution:**

**Step 1:** Effective resolution:

$$R_{\text{eff}} = \frac{1 \text{ mm}}{500 \text{ lines}} \times \frac{1}{4} = 0.0005 \text{ mm/count} = 0.5 \, \mu m/\text{count}$$

**Step 2:** Total displacement:

$$x = N_{\text{pulses}} \times R_{\text{eff}} = 12000 \times 0.0005 \text{ mm}$$

$$\boxed{x = 6.0 \text{ mm}}$$

---

## 3.7 Analytical Matrix: Performance Characteristics Summary

| Characteristic | Type | Unit | Effect of Improvement | Typical Trade-off |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Sensitivity | Static | Output/Input | Better signal level | Higher noise pickup |
| Linearity | Static | % FS | Easier calibration | More complex sensor design |
| Accuracy | Static | % FS | Closer to true value | Costlier components |
| Precision | Static | Std. Dev. | Tighter measurement spread | May still be biased |
| Repeatability | Static | % FS | Reliable outputs | Sensor aging limits |
| Resolution | Static | Input units | Detects smaller changes | Lower max range |
| Threshold | Static | Input units | Detects fainter signals | Higher noise floor |
| Dynamic Range | Static | dB | Wider measurement envelope | Complex signal conditioning |
| Response Time | Dynamic | seconds | Faster reaction | Higher bandwidth noise |
| Bandwidth | Dynamic | Hz | Tracks faster signals | Sensitive to high-freq. noise |
| Hysteresis | Static | % FS | Less memory effect | Mechanical design complexity |
| Drift | Static (over time) | Units/time | Long-term stability | Expensive materials needed |

---

## 3.8 KTU Numerical Problem Bank (with Full Solutions)

### Problem A: ADC Resolution
A 12-bit ADC is used in a temperature measurement system with a sensor range of -50 °C to +150 °C.

**Solution:**

$$FSR = 150 - (-50) = 200 \text{ °C}$$

$$2^{12} = 4096 \text{ levels}$$

$$\text{Resolution} = \frac{200}{4096} = 0.0488 \text{ °C/count} \approx 0.05 \text{ °C}$$

### Problem B: Encoder Angular Displacement
A 12-bit absolute rotary encoder is fitted on a motor shaft. The shaft rotates by 540° from home. What is the new binary output code (initial code = 0000 0000 0000)?

**Solution:**

Resolution: $\theta_{\min} = 360°/4096 = 0.0879°/\text{count}$

Number of counts: $N = 540° / 0.0879° = 6144$ counts (but this exceeds $2^{12} = 4096$)

Since 540° > 360°, the shaft has completed **1 full revolution + 180°** additional. So after one revolution, the code returns to 0000 0000 0000, and 180° corresponds to 2048 counts:

$$N_{180°} = \frac{180°}{0.0879°} = 2048 \text{ counts}$$

In binary: $2048 = 2^{11} = 1000\,0000\,0000$

So the final code is **1000 0000 0000**.

### Problem C: Bandwidth
A pressure sensor has a time constant of 0.5 ms. Find its -3 dB bandwidth.

**Solution:**

$$f_{BW} = \frac{1}{2\pi \tau} = \frac{1}{2\pi \times 0.0005} = 318.3 \text{ Hz}$$

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Sensor Classification Tree (KTU Module 1 Mapping)

```mermaid
graph TD
    rootA[Sensors] --> rootB[Exteroceptive External Environment]
    rootA --> rootC[Proprioceptive Internal State]
    rootA --> rootD[Position Encoders]

    rootB --> subB1[Touch Sensor]
    rootB --> subB2[Force Sensor]
    rootB --> subB3[Proximity Sensor]
    rootB --> subB4[Vision Sensor]

    subB1 --> subB1a[Mechanical Switch]
    subB1 --> subB1b[Capacitive Touch]
    subB1 --> subB1c[Resistive FSR]

    subB2 --> subB2a[Strain Gauge Load Cell]
    subB2 --> subB2b[Piezoelectric Sensor]
    subB2 --> subB2c[Capacitive Force Sensor]

    subB3 --> subB3a[Inductive]
    subB3 --> subB3b[Capacitive]
    subB3 --> subC[Ultrasonic]
    subB3 --> subD[Infrared]

    subB4 --> subB4a[CCD Camera]
    subB4 --> subB4b[CMOS Camera]
    subB4 --> subB4c[LiDAR]

    rootC --> subC1[Joint Angle Encoder]
    rootC --> subC2[IMU Accelerometer Gyro]
    rootC --> subC3[Tachometer]

    rootD --> rootD1[Linear Encoder]
    rootD --> rootD2[Rotary Encoder]
    rootD1 --> rootD1a[Incremental Strip]
    rootD1 --> rootD1b[Absolute Multi Track]
    rootD2 --> rootD2a[Incremental Disc]
    rootD2 --> rootD2b[Absolute Multi Track]
```

---

## 4.2 Encoder Family Comparison Flow

```mermaid
graph LR
    encA[Position Encoder] --> encB{Motion Type}
    encB --> encC[Linear Encoder]
    encB --> encD[Rotary Encoder]
    encC --> encE{Output Type}
    encD --> encF{Output Type}
    encE --> encG[Incremental]
    encE --> encH[Absolute]
    encF --> encI[Incremental]
    encF --> encJ[Absolute]
    encG --> encG1[Quadrature A B Index]
    encI --> encI1[Quadrature A B Index]
    encH --> encH1[Binary or Gray Code]
    encJ --> encJ1[Binary or Gray Code]
```

---

## 4.3 Incremental Rotary Encoder — Block-Level Architecture

```mermaid
graph LR
    lightA[LED Light Source] --> lensA[Collimating Lens]
    lensA --> discA[Slotted Disc Rotating with Shaft]
    discA --> maskA[Stationary Slit Mask]
    maskA --> pdA[Photodetector A]
    maskA --> pdB[Photodetector B]
    maskA --> pdC[Photodetector Z Index]
    pdA --> ampA[Amplifier and Comparator]
    pdB --> ampB[Amplifier and Comparator]
    pdC --> ampC[Amplifier and Comparator]
    ampA --> outA[Square Wave A]
    ampB --> outB[Square Wave B 90 deg Phase Shift]
    ampC --> outC[Index Pulse Z once per Rev]
```

---

## 4.4 Absolute Rotary Encoder — Block-Level Architecture

```mermaid
graph TD
    srcA[LED Source] --> condA[Collimator]
    condA --> discA[Multi Track Coded Disc]
    discA --> sensA[Photodetector Array N Channels]
    sensA --> condB[Signal Conditioning and Latch]
    condB --> outA[Parallel N bit Binary or Gray Code Output]
    condB --> outB[Serial SSI or BiSS Output]
```

---

## 4.5 Sensor Characteristics Relationship Map

```mermaid
graph TD
    chA[Static Characteristics] --> chA1[Sensitivity]
    chA --> chA2[Linearity]
    chA --> chA3[Accuracy]
    chA --> chA4[Precision]
    chA --> chA5[Resolution]
    chA --> chA6[Threshold]
    chA --> chA7[Dynamic Range]
    chA --> chA8[Repeatability]
    chA --> chA9[Hysteresis]
    chA --> chA10[Drift]
    chB[Dynamic Characteristics] --> chB1[Response Time Time Constant]
    chB --> chB2[Bandwidth]
    chB --> chB3[Rise Time]
    chB --> chB4[Settling Time]
    chB --> chB5[Damping Ratio]
    chA1 --> rel1[Slope of Calibration Curve]
    chA2 --> rel2[Deviation from Best Fit Line]
    chA3 --> rel3[Closeness to True Value]
    chA4 --> rel4[Spread of Repeated Measurements]
    chA5 --> rel5[Smallest Detectable Input Change]
    chB1 --> rel6[Time to Reach 63.2 percent of Final]
    chB2 --> rel7[Frequency Range with Acceptable Response]
```

---

## 4.6 Accuracy vs. Precision vs. Repeatability (Dartboard Analogy)

```mermaid
graph TD
    dartA[High Accuracy plus High Precision] --> dartA1[Bullseye Tight Cluster]
    dartB[High Precision Low Accuracy] --> dartB1[Tight Cluster Off Center]
    dartC[High Accuracy Low Precision] --> dartC1[Spread around Bullseye]
    dartD[Low Accuracy Low Precision] --> dartD1[Random Scattered]
```

> **Reading the diagram for KTU exams:** If the KTU question asks you to "illustrate with a dartboard diagram", draw four dartboards with darts in each configuration above and label them.

---

## 4.7 Incremental Encoder Quadrature Waveform Schematic

```mermaid
graph TD
    waveA[Channel A Square Wave] --> ph1[Rising and Falling Edges]
    waveB[Channel B 90 deg Phase Shifted] --> ph2[Direction Decode]
    waveA --> ph2
    ph1 --> countA[Edge Counter X4]
    ph2 --> dirA[Direction Logic Up or Down]
    countA --> posA[Position Register]
    dirA --> posA
    waveC[Index Z once per revolution] --> homeA[Home Reference]
    homeA --> posA
    posA --> outA[Absolute Position from Home]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> KTU Part A questions test **definitional clarity** and crisp one-line or short-paragraph answers. Each carries **3 marks** and expects a labeled diagram wherever applicable.

---

### **Question A1** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`
**Define the following with suitable examples:**
(i) Repeatability
(ii) Resolution
(iii) Threshold

**Model Answer:**

**(i) Repeatability:** The ability of a sensor to produce the same output for the same input value, when the input is approached repeatedly under the same conditions. *Example:* A load cell that displays 100.0 kg three times in a row for a 100 kg standard weight has high repeatability.

**(ii) Resolution:** The smallest change in the input that produces a detectable change in the output. *Example:* A digital voltmeter that displays in 1 mV steps has a resolution of 1 mV.

**(iii) Threshold:** The minimum magnitude of input required to produce a detectable output change from zero. Below the threshold, the output is indistinguishable from noise. *Example:* A photodiode that does not respond to light intensity below 1 lux has a threshold of 1 lux.

---

### **Question A2** `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`
**Differentiate between accuracy and precision. How is precision related to repeatability?**

**Model Answer:**

| Accuracy | Precision |
|---|---|
| Closeness of a measurement to the **true value** | Closeness of multiple measurements **to each other** |
| Affected by systematic errors (calibration, bias) | Affected by random errors (noise, resolution) |

**Precision is a necessary but not sufficient condition for repeatability.** Precision quantifies the *spread* of measurements, while repeatability is the ability to *reproduce the same result* under identical conditions. A highly repeatable sensor is precise, but a precise sensor may not be repeatable if the cluster shifts between sessions (due to drift, hysteresis, or environmental change).

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU Part B questions carry **14 marks** and feature **internal choice** (Module-level). Below are **two fully-solved alternatives** — a student answers EITHER Question A OR Question B.

---

### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam — May 2023]`
**(a)** Explain in detail the static and dynamic characteristics of a sensor. (7 marks)
**(b)** Describe the construction and working of an absolute rotary encoder. How is it different from an incremental rotary encoder? (7 marks)

### **Model Solution to Question A(a):**

**Static Characteristics** (those measured when the input is constant or varies slowly):

1. **Sensitivity:** The ratio of change in output to change in input. For a linear sensor, $S = \dfrac{\Delta y}{\Delta x}$, which is the slope of the calibration curve.
2. **Linearity:** The degree to which the actual output follows a straight line. Non-linearity is expressed as % of full-scale output.
3. **Accuracy:** The closeness of a measured value to the true value. Expressed as % of FS.
4. **Precision:** The degree of mutual agreement among repeated measurements (tightness of cluster).
5. **Repeatability:** The ability to produce the same output for the same input repeatedly.
6. **Resolution:** The smallest change in input that produces a detectable change in output.
7. **Threshold:** The minimum input that produces a detectable output.
8. **Dynamic Range:** The ratio of max to min input (in dB).
9. **Hysteresis:** The difference in output when the input is approached from increasing vs. decreasing direction.
10. **Drift:** The slow change in output with time, even when input is constant.

**Dynamic Characteristics** (those measured when the input varies with time):

1. **Response Time / Time Constant ($\tau$):** Time to reach 63.2 % of final value for a step input.
2. **Rise Time ($t_r$):** Time to go from 10 % to 90 % of the final value.
3. **Settling Time ($t_s$):** Time to settle within ±2 % (or ±5 %) of the final value (≈ 4τ to 5τ).
4. **Bandwidth (BW):** The range of frequencies over which the sensor responds with acceptable fidelity (typically defined at -3 dB).
5. **Damping Ratio ($\zeta$):** Indicates how oscillatory the response is.
6. **Natural Frequency ($\omega_n$):** The frequency at which the sensor would oscillate in absence of damping.

For a first-order sensor: $y(t) = K X_0 (1 - e^{-t/\tau})$, and $f_{BW} = 1/(2\pi\tau)$.

### **Model Solution to Question A(b):**

**Absolute Rotary Encoder — Construction:**

An absolute rotary encoder consists of:
- A **light source** (LED).
- A **collimating lens** to produce parallel light.
- A **coded disc** with N concentric tracks, each patterned with opaque and transparent segments corresponding to a specific bit of the output code.
- **N photodetectors**, one per track.
- **Signal conditioning and latching** electronics.
- An **output interface** providing parallel or serial (SSI, BiSS) digital code.

**Working Principle:**

1. The LED illuminates the coded disc.
2. Light passes through transparent segments and is blocked by opaque ones.
3. Each photodetector reads its own track, producing a binary 1 or 0.
4. The N-bit code is latched and output in parallel (e.g., 10-bit binary) or serialized.
5. Every angular position produces a **unique code** — hence the name "absolute".

**Resolution:** $\theta_{\min} = 360°/2^N$ — for a 12-bit encoder, $\theta_{\min} = 360°/4096 \approx 0.088°$.

**Differences from Incremental Encoder:**

| Aspect | Incremental | Absolute |
|---|---|---|
| Output | Pulses (A, B, Z) | Unique N-bit code |
| Position after power-off | **Lost** — needs re-homing | **Retained** — known on power-up |
| Cost | Lower | Higher (more tracks, more detectors) |
| Code type | Quadrature | Binary or Gray |
| Best use | High-speed, low-cost systems | Safety-critical, long-term operation |

**Gray Code Advantage:** Absolute encoders often output **Gray code** rather than binary. In Gray code, only **one bit changes** between adjacent positions, eliminating false readings from track misalignments at transition points.

---

### **QUESTION B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]`
**(a)** With neat diagrams, explain the construction and working of (i) incremental linear encoder, and (ii) absolute linear encoder. (7 marks)
**(b)** A load cell is used in a force measurement system. It has a sensitivity of 2 mV/V at full scale of 1000 N, excited by 10 V. Calculate (i) full-scale output voltage, (ii) voltage output at 250 N, (iii) sensitivity in V/N, (iv) the maximum non-linearity if the actual output at 500 N is 9.95 mV. (7 marks)

### **Model Solution to Question B(a):**

**(i) Incremental Linear Encoder:**

**Construction:**
- A **glass or metal scale** with equally spaced opaque and transparent markings (e.g., 1000 lines/mm).
- An **LED source** on one side of the scale.
- **Photodetectors** on the other side (typically arranged in quadrature: A, A', B, B').
- An **index track** with a single reference mark.
- A **readhead assembly** containing the source, detectors, and electronics.

**Working:**
1. The scale moves linearly relative to the stationary readhead.
2. As the scale moves, the photodetectors see alternating light and dark.
3. Channels A and B produce **two square waves in quadrature** (90° phase shift).
4. The controller counts rising/falling edges (4× decoding) for high resolution.
5. The **direction** of motion is determined by which channel leads.
6. The **index pulse (Z)** provides a once-per-length reference for homing.

**Resolution:** $R = \dfrac{1}{4 \times \text{lines per mm}}$ — for 1000 lines/mm, $R = 0.25 \, \mu m$.

**(ii) Absolute Linear Encoder:**

**Construction:**
- A scale with **multiple parallel tracks**, each carrying a unique code pattern (like a barcode).
- A readhead with **multiple photodetectors**, one per track.
- Output electronics for code interpretation (often Gray-to-binary conversion).

**Working:**
1. Each track represents a different bit of the position code.
2. The readhead reads **all tracks simultaneously**.
3. The combined bit pattern uniquely identifies the position.
4. **No homing required** — position is absolute and known at power-up.

**Advantage over incremental:** In incremental encoders, a power interruption loses position; in absolute encoders, position is always known.

### **Model Solution to Question B(b):**

**Given:**
- Sensitivity = 2 mV/V at FS = 1000 N
- Excitation voltage $V_{ex}$ = 10 V

**(i) Full-scale output voltage:**

$$V_{FS} = S \times V_{ex} = 2 \text{ mV/V} \times 10 \text{ V} = 20 \text{ mV}$$

**(ii) Voltage output at 250 N:**

Assuming linear behavior:

$$V_{250} = V_{FS} \times \frac{250}{1000} = 20 \text{ mV} \times 0.25 = 5.0 \text{ mV}$$

**(iii) Sensitivity in V/N:**

$$S = \frac{V_{FS}}{F_{FS}} = \frac{20 \text{ mV}}{1000 \text{ N}} = 0.02 \text{ mV/N} = 2 \times 10^{-5} \text{ V/N}$$

**(iv) Maximum non-linearity:**

Expected (ideal) output at 500 N:

$$V_{ideal,500} = 20 \text{ mV} \times \frac{500}{1000} = 10.00 \text{ mV}$$

Actual output: $V_{actual,500} = 9.95 \text{ mV}$

Deviation:

$$\Delta V = \vert 10.00 - 9.95 \vert = 0.05 \text{ mV}$$

Non-linearity:

$$\%NL = \frac{\Delta V}{V_{FS}} \times 100 = \frac{0.05}{20} \times 100 = \boxed{0.25 \% FS}$$

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common ways students LOSE marks in Module 1 questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing "precision" with "accuracy"** in dartboard/darts questions — examiners specifically look for the *correct* label on each dartboard (Cluster + Bullseye, Cluster off-center, Spread on bullseye, Random).
>
> 2. **Forgetting units in sensitivity** — a sensitivity written as "2" without "mV/N" is incomplete and may lose 1 mark.
>
> 3. **Mixing up incremental and absolute encoder terminology** — writing "incremental encoder gives absolute position" is a glaring error worth 2–3 marks.
>
> 4. **Drawing a "linear encoder" diagram but labeling it as "rotary"** — examiners pay close attention to axis labels (linear: X-mm; rotary: θ°).
>
> 5. **Using binary code without mentioning Gray code for absolute encoders** — Gray code is the *de facto* standard for absolute encoders; omitting it costs a key concept mark.
>
> 6. **Not mentioning the index pulse (Z) in incremental encoder diagrams** — Z is the reference for homing; omitting it is a 1-mark loss.
>
> 7. **Incorrect formula for encoder resolution** — using $2^N$ for incremental or $4 \times \text{PPR}$ for absolute is a sign of not understanding the underlying principle.
>
> 8. **In linearity problems, computing deviation as $\Delta V / V_{actual}$ instead of $\Delta V / V_{FS}$** — the standard reference is always Full Scale.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Last-minute revision checklist — go through each bullet before the exam.**

### Sensor Performance Characteristics
- **Sensitivity** = slope of calibration curve; $S = \Delta y / \Delta x$.
- **Linearity** = deviation of actual output from best-fit straight line; expressed as **% FS**.
- **Accuracy** = closeness to true value; affected by **systematic** errors.
- **Precision** = tightness of cluster of repeated readings; affected by **random** errors.
- **Repeatability** = ability to reproduce the same output under same conditions.
- **Resolution** = smallest detectable change in input.
- **Threshold** = minimum input to produce a detectable output.
- **Dynamic Range** = $20 \log_{10}(I_{max}/I_{min})$ in dB.
- **Response Time** = time to reach 63.2 % (1τ) of final value.
- **Settling Time** = 5τ (within 1 % of final).
- **Bandwidth** = $f_{BW} = 1/(2\pi\tau)$ (for first-order sensor).
- **Hysteresis** = difference in output for increasing vs. decreasing input.
- **Drift** = slow change in output with time at constant input.

### Sensor Classification
- **Exteroceptive:** Touch, Force, Proximity, Vision (perceive **external** world).
- **Proprioceptive/Internal:** Encoders, IMU, tachometer (perceive **self** state).
- **Touch:** Mechanical, capacitive, resistive (FSR).
- **Force:** Strain gauge, piezoelectric, capacitive.
- **Proximity:** Inductive, capacitive, ultrasonic, infrared.
- **Vision:** CCD, CMOS, LiDAR.

### Encoders — The Module 1 Showstopper
- **Two motion types:** Linear (strip) and Rotary (disc).
- **Two output philosophies:** Incremental (pulses) and Absolute (code).
- **Incremental output:** Quadrature A & B + Index Z. Position is **relative** to index. **Loses position on power-off.**
- **Absolute output:** Unique N-bit code (often **Gray**) for every position. **Retains position on power-off.**
- **Resolution:**
  - Incremental linear: $R = 1/(4 \cdot \text{lines/mm})$ with 4× decoding.
  - Incremental rotary: $\theta_{min} = 360°/(4 \cdot \text{PPR})$ with 4× decoding.
  - Absolute rotary: $\theta_{min} = 360°/2^N$.
- **Gray code advantage:** Only **one bit changes** between adjacent positions, preventing false reads.
- **Quadrature:** A leads B for one direction, B leads A for the other — allows **direction** detection.
- **Index pulse Z:** One pulse per revolution/length — reference for **homing**.

### KTU-Friendly Memory Aids
- **"S = slope"** for sensitivity.
- **"5τ ≈ done"** for settling.
- **"$-3 \text{ dB} = 1/(2\pi\tau)$"** for bandwidth.
- **"$2^N$ positions"** for absolute encoder.
- **"$4 \times$** for quadrature" for incremental resolution.
- **"Gray = Good"** — Gray code avoids glitchy transitions.

### Numerical Problem Quick-Reference
- Sensitivity = $\Delta y / \Delta x$ — **always include units**.
- Non-linearity = $\Delta V / V_{FS} \times 100$ — **FS, not Reading**.
- ADC resolution = $FSR / 2^n$ — **n = number of bits**.
- Encoder displacement = (Counts × line spacing) / (4 if 4× decoding).
- Settling time = $5\tau$.

> **Final Exam Tip:** When answering KTU Part B questions, **always draw a labeled diagram** — even a simple one earns at least 1–2 extra marks. Use **proper SI units** throughout. Mention **at least one real-world application** of the sensor/encoder you are describing — examiners love to see engineering context.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Potentiometers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBRT303 - SENSORS AND ACTUATORS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Potentiometers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Potentiometer** (also called a **Pot** or **POT**) is a three-terminal **passive resistive transducer** that converts linear or angular mechanical displacement into a proportional change in electrical resistance, and when connected to a reference voltage, produces a variable output voltage that is a function of the wiper position. According to the KTU 2024 scheme classification, the potentiometer belongs to the family of **Variable Resistance Sensors** under **Resistive Position Transducers**.

Mathematically, it operates on the classical **Voltage Divider Principle**:

$$V_{out} = V_{in} \cdot \frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2}$$

where $R_1$ and $R_2$ are the two segments of the resistance track divided by the wiper position.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Potentiometers are categorized as a *contact-type displacement sensor*. The core sensing element is a uniform resistance track (carbon film, cermet, wire-wound, or conductive plastic) over which a sliding wiper mechanically moves.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy & Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain English Analogy:** Imagine a long water pipe (the resistance track) with a sliding valve (the wiper) that you can move left or right. The further you slide the valve from the water source, the less water pressure (voltage) comes out the other end. The pipe offers resistance to water flow just as the resistance track opposes current flow. The valve position directly tells you how much output you're getting. This is exactly how a potentiometer works — a moving contact taps a portion of a fixed total resistance, and that tapped portion determines the output voltage.

Think of the **volume knob on an old radio** — turning it changes the resistance and therefore changes the loudness. The knob is mechanically linked to a wiper, which slides across a resistive element. The physical rotation is the *input*, and the resulting voltage at the wiper terminal is the *output*. This is the **potentiometric transduction principle** in its simplest form.

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Nominal Resistance Range:** $1\ \text{k}\Omega$ to $1\ \text{M}\Omega$ (commonly $5\ \text{k}\Omega$, $10\ \text{k}\Omega$)
- **Standard Power Rating:** $0.25\ \text{W}$, $0.5\ \text{W}$, $1\ \text{W}$, $2\ \text{W}$, $5\ \text{W}$
- **Operating Temperature:** $-55^\circ\text{C}$ to $+125^\circ\text{C}$ (military grade)
- **Linearity Tolerance:** $\pm 0.1\%$ to $\pm 5\%$ depending on grade
- **Resistivity ($\rho$) of common track materials:** Carbon $\approx 35 \times 10^{-6}\ \Omega\cdot\text{m}$, Constantan $\approx 4.9 \times 10^{-7}\ \Omega\cdot\text{m}$
- **Resolution (Wire-wound):** $1/N$, where $N$ is the number of turns

## 1.4 Classification of Potentiometers

Potentiometers are broadly classified into two main types based on the type of mechanical input:

| **Category** | **Sub-Type** | **Mechanical Input** | **Typical Application** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Linear Potentiometer** | Slide Pot | Linear translation | Liquid level sensors, position feedback |
| **Rotary Potentiometer** | Single-turn, Multi-turn | Angular rotation (typically $\leq 360^\circ$) | Joysticks, throttle control, volume knobs |
| **Special Type** | Trimmer (Trimpot) | Screwdriver adjustment | Calibration, fine-tuning |
| **Special Type** | Digital Potentiometer | Electronic (no mechanical wiper) | Microcontroller-based systems |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Focus:** The most common exam questions ask to differentiate between **Linear** and **Rotary** potentiometers, explain the **working principle**, and derive the **output voltage equation**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Working Principle — Step-by-Step Operational Logic

The potentiometer operates on the **Voltage Divider Rule** of a series resistive network. The operational steps are:

1. **Excitation Application:** A stable DC reference voltage $V_{in}$ (or AC for special applications) is applied across the two end terminals (Terminal 1 and Terminal 3) of the resistance track.
2. **Resistance Track:** The track is a uniform resistive element with total resistance $R_T$. The resistance per unit length is $\rho_\ell$ (resistance per cm or per degree), assumed constant for an ideal linear device.
3. **Wiper Action:** An external mechanical force (linear motion or rotation) drives the wiper (Terminal 2) along the track. This splits the total resistance into two parts:
   - $R_1$ = Resistance between Terminal 1 and Wiper
   - $R_2$ = Resistance between Wiper and Terminal 3
   - **Constraint:** $R_1 + R_2 = R_T$ (constant)
4. **Voltage Division:** Since the wiper draws negligible current (high-impedance load assumed), the output voltage $V_{out}$ at the wiper terminal with respect to Terminal 3 is proportional to $R_2$.
5. **Displacement Mapping:** The mechanical displacement $x$ (linear) or $\theta$ (angular) is directly mapped to $V_{out}$ via the linear resistance distribution.

> [!NOTE]
> **The 'Why' Behind the 'How':** The wiper is mechanically decoupled from the electrical load (assuming an ideal voltmeter/buffer with infinite input impedance). This is critical — if the load draws significant current, the output voltage deviates from the ideal value, causing a **loading error** that KTU examiners frequently test.

## 2.2 Constructional Details

A potentiometer consists of the following sub-components:

- **Resistance Element (Track):** Made of carbon film, cermet, conductive plastic, or wire-wound on a substrate (phenolic resin, ceramic, or fiberglass).
- **Wiper:** A spring-loaded metal contact (often phosphor bronze or precious metal alloy) that slides over the track.
- **Shaft/Actuator:** The mechanical input is applied here (rotation or linear push).
- **Housing & Bearings:** Provide mechanical support and protect the track.
- **Terminals:** Three terminals — two for the resistance ends and one for the wiper.
- **Terminal Board:** Provides external electrical connections.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL KTU 2024 Formula Bank — Memorize all entries below.**

| **#** | **Parameter** | **Formula** | **Units** | **Boundary/Condition** |
|:---:|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Output Voltage (Ideal) | $V_{out} = V_{in} \cdot \dfrac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2}$ | Volts (V) | $0 \le V_{out} \le V_{in}$ |
| 2 | Output Voltage (Linear, displacement $x$) | $V_{out} = V_{in} \cdot \dfrac{x}{L}$ | Volts (V) | $0 \le x \le L$ |
| 3 | Output Voltage (Rotary, angle $\theta$) | $V_{out} = V_{in} \cdot \dfrac{\theta}{\theta_{max}}$ | Volts (V) | $0 \le \theta \le \theta_{max}$ |
| 4 | Total Track Resistance | $R_T = \dfrac{\rho \cdot L}{A}$ | Ohms ($\Omega$) | $\rho$ = resistivity |
| 5 | Resistance per unit length | $R_\ell = \dfrac{R_T}{L}$ | $\Omega/\text{m}$ | Constant for uniform track |
| 6 | Wiper segment resistance | $R_2 = R_\ell \cdot x$ | Ohms ($\Omega$) | Linear assumption |
| 7 | Loading Error (finite load $R_L$) | $\varepsilon_L = \dfrac{1}{1 + \dfrac{R_T}{R_L} \cdot \dfrac{R_1 R_2}{R_T^2}}$ (approx.) | Dimensionless | $R_L \gg R_T$ for low error |
| 8 | Resolution (Wire-wound) | $R_{res} = \dfrac{1}{N} \times 100\%$ | Percent (%) | $N$ = number of turns |
| 9 | Power Dissipation | $P = \dfrac{V_{in}^2}{R_T}$ | Watts (W) | Must be $\le$ rated power |
| 10 | Sensitivity (Linear) | $S = \dfrac{dV_{out}}{dx} = \dfrac{V_{in}}{L}$ | $\text{V}/\text{m}$ | Constant for ideal |
| 11 | Sensitivity (Rotary) | $S = \dfrac{dV_{out}}{d\theta} = \dfrac{V_{in}}{\theta_{max}}$ | $\text{V}/\text{rad}$ | Constant for ideal |
| 12 | Non-linearity Error | $NL(\%) = \dfrac{\vert \Delta V_{max} \vert}{V_{in}} \times 100\%$ | Percent (%) | Measured at mid-stroke |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Markdown Note on Vertical Pipes:** Per the KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE formatting rule, the absolute value symbol in the table above is rendered as `\vert \Delta V_{max} \vert` to avoid breaking the markdown table syntax.

## 2.4 Types of Resistance Tracks — Engineering Trade-offs

| **Track Material** | **Resistivity Range** | **Power Rating** | **Resolution** | **Life Cycle** | **Cost** |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Carbon Film** | $1\ \text{k}\Omega$ – $1\ \text{M}\Omega$ | Low–Medium | Infinite | $10^5$ cycles | Low |
| **Cermet (Ceramic+Metal)** | $10\ \Omega$ – $5\ \text{M}\Omega$ | High | Infinite | $10^5$ – $10^6$ cycles | Medium |
| **Wire-Wound (Constantan/Nichrome)** | $10\ \Omega$ – $100\ \text{k}\Omega$ | Very High | Limited (stepped) | $10^6$ cycles | Medium–High |
| **Conductive Plastic** | $1\ \text{k}\Omega$ – $1\ \text{M}\Omega$ | Medium | Infinite (smooth) | $10^7$ – $10^8$ cycles | High |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive:** Throttle Position Sensor (TPS), accelerator pedal position, suspension height sensor
- **Industrial Automation:** Robotic arm joint position, valve position feedback
- **Audio Engineering:** Volume, tone, and balance controls
- **Aerospace:** Flight control surface position feedback (now largely replaced by LVDTs and encoders)
- **Medical:** Infusion pump flow regulators, surgical robot position feedback
- **Consumer Electronics:** Joystick controllers, dimmer switches, slider controls on mixing consoles
- **Measurement & Instrumentation:** Calibration of instruments, null-balance potentiometers (used historically in precision measurement)

## 2.6 Advantages & Limitations

> [!NOTE]
> **Advantages:**
> - Simple, low-cost construction
> - Generates a usable output voltage directly (no signal conditioning required for low frequencies)
> - Operates on DC or AC excitation
> - Wide range of resistance values available
> - Easy to interface with ADC and microcontrollers

> [!WARNING]
> **Limitations:**
> - **Mechanical wear** due to sliding contact — finite life cycle
> - **Contact noise** and "scratchy" output due to wiper-track contact irregularities
> - **Loading error** if output is not buffered
> - **Limited resolution** in wire-wound types (stepped output)
> - **Friction** between wiper and track introduces mechanical loading on the input
> - **Susceptible to vibration and contamination** (dust, moisture)
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Output Voltage vs. Linear Displacement

**Problem Setup:** Consider an ideal linear potentiometer of total length $L$ (in meters) with uniform resistance $R_T$ across its track. A stable DC excitation voltage $V_{in}$ is applied across the two end terminals. A wiper is moved by a linear displacement $x$ from the reference (ground) end. Derive the output voltage $V_{out}$ as a function of $x$.

### Step-by-Step Derivation:

**Step 1 — Identify the resistance per unit length.**
Since the track is uniform, the resistance per unit length is constant:

$$R_\ell = \frac{R_T}{L}$$

This means for a displacement of $x$ meters, the resistance from the wiper to the far terminal is:

$$R_2 = R_\ell \cdot x = \frac{R_T \cdot x}{L}$$

**Step 2 — Write the total resistance relation.**

The two segments of the track are $R_1$ (from start to wiper) and $R_2$ (from wiper to end). They are in series:

$$R_1 + R_2 = R_T$$

Therefore:

$$R_1 = R_T - R_2 = R_T - \frac{R_T \cdot x}{L} = R_T \left(1 - \frac{x}{L}\right)$$

**Step 3 — Apply the Voltage Divider Rule.**

The output voltage is taken across $R_2$ (between the wiper and the end terminal). Using the voltage divider:

$$V_{out} = V_{in} \cdot \frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute the expressions for $R_1$ and $R_2$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{out} &= V_{in} \cdot \frac{\dfrac{R_T \cdot x}{L}}{R_T \left(1 - \dfrac{x}{L}\right) + \dfrac{R_T \cdot x}{L}} \\
&= V_{in} \cdot \frac{\dfrac{R_T \cdot x}{L}}{R_T \left(1 - \dfrac{x}{L} + \dfrac{x}{L}\right)} \\
&= V_{in} \cdot \frac{\dfrac{R_T \cdot x}{L}}{R_T} \\
&= V_{in} \cdot \frac{x}{L}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Interpret the result.**

$$V_{out}(x) = V_{in} \cdot \frac{x}{L}$$

**Conclusion:** For an **ideal linear potentiometer**, the output voltage is a **linear function of mechanical displacement**. The sensitivity (slope) is $V_{in}/L$. When $x = 0$, $V_{out} = 0$ (wiper at ground end). When $x = L$, $V_{out} = V_{in}$ (wiper at $V_{in}$ end). This proves the potentiometer acts as an **adjustable voltage divider**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conversion Logic Used:** In Step 4, the denominator simplifies because $R_1 + R_2 = R_T$ by definition. This algebraic identity is what eliminates the explicit resistance values, leaving only the geometric ratio $x/L$. This is why the calibration of a potentiometer is **independent of $R_T$** in theory — a fact that KTU examiners love to highlight as a conceptual question.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Loading Error Due to Finite Load Resistance

**Problem Setup:** In a real circuit, the output of the potentiometer is connected to a load (e.g., an ADC input, voltmeter, or amplifier with finite input impedance $R_L$). This load is in **parallel** with $R_2$, altering the effective resistance and thus the output voltage. Derive the loading error.

### Step-by-Step Derivation:

**Step 1 — Define the effective load resistance.**

The load $R_L$ is in parallel with $R_2$:

$$R_{2,eff} = \frac{R_2 \cdot R_L}{R_2 + R_L}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the voltage divider with the effective resistance.**

The new output voltage (loaded) is:

$$V_{out,loaded} = V_{in} \cdot \frac{R_{2,eff}}{R_1 + R_{2,eff}}$$

**Step 3 — Define the ideal (unloaded) output voltage.**

$$V_{out,ideal} = V_{in} \cdot \frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2} = V_{in} \cdot \frac{R_2}{R_T}$$

**Step 4 — Express the loading ratio.**

The ratio of loaded to ideal output is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{V_{out,loaded}}{V_{out,ideal}} &= \frac{\dfrac{R_{2,eff}}{R_1 + R_{2,eff}}}{\dfrac{R_2}{R_T}} \\
&= \frac{R_{2,eff} \cdot R_T}{R_2 \cdot (R_1 + R_{2,eff})}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Substitute $R_{2,eff}$ and simplify.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{V_{out,loaded}}{V_{out,ideal}} &= \frac{\dfrac{R_2 R_L}{R_2 + R_L} \cdot R_T}{R_2 \left(R_1 + \dfrac{R_2 R_L}{R_2 + R_L}\right)} \\
&= \frac{\dfrac{R_L}{R_2 + R_L} \cdot R_T}{R_1 + \dfrac{R_2 R_L}{R_2 + R_L}} \\
&= \frac{R_L \cdot R_T}{R_1(R_2 + R_L) + R_2 R_L} \\
&= \frac{R_L \cdot R_T}{R_1 R_2 + R_1 R_L + R_2 R_L}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Define the loading error as a percentage deviation.**

$$\varepsilon_L = \frac{V_{out,ideal} - V_{out,loaded}}{V_{out,ideal}} \times 100\% = \left(1 - \frac{V_{out,loaded}}{V_{out,ideal}}\right) \times 100\%$$

**Step 7 — Boundary case analysis.**

- If $R_L \to \infty$ (ideal voltmeter): $\varepsilon_L \to 0$ (no error)
- If $R_L = R_T$ (load equal to track): significant non-linearity introduced
- **Rule of Thumb:** $R_L \ge 10 \cdot R_T$ keeps the loading error below $\approx 5\%$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Remedy:** Always buffer the potentiometer output using a **voltage follower (op-amp unity gain buffer)** before connecting to any load. This isolates the potentiometer from loading effects and makes the output drive-capable.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Resolution of a Wire-Wound Potentiometer

**Problem Setup:** A wire-wound potentiometer is constructed by winding $N$ turns of resistance wire uniformly on a former. Each turn contributes a discrete step to the resistance. Derive the theoretical resolution.

### Step-by-Step Derivation:

**Step 1 — Resistance per turn.**

The total resistance $R_T$ is distributed equally across $N$ turns:

$$R_{turn} = \frac{R_T}{N}$$

**Step 2 — Minimum detectable resistance change.**

The wiper can only transition from one turn to the next, so the smallest resistance change is one turn:

$$\Delta R_{min} = R_{turn} = \frac{R_T}{N}$$

**Step 3 — Resolution in percentage form.**

$$R_{res}(\%) = \frac{\Delta R_{min}}{R_T} \times 100\% = \frac{1}{N} \times 100\%$$

**Step 4 — Example calculation.**

If $N = 200$ turns, then:

$$R_{res} = \frac{1}{200} \times 100\% = 0.5\%$$

**Conclusion:** Higher turn count $\Rightarrow$ better resolution. However, more turns mean thinner wire $\Rightarrow$ higher resistance per unit length and lower power handling. This is a **fundamental engineering trade-off** in wire-wound potentiometer design.

## 3.4 Analytical Problem-Solving Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Pattern — Practice these derivation types before every exam:**

| **Problem Type** | **Key Equation to Use** | **Common Mistake to Avoid** |
|---|---|---|
| Find $V_{out}$ for given $x$ | $V_{out} = V_{in} \cdot \dfrac{x}{L}$ | Confusing $x$ with $R_2$ value |
| Find displacement for given $V_{out}$ | $x = \dfrac{V_{out}}{V_{in}} \cdot L$ | Unit mismatch (mm vs. m) |
| Loading error analysis | Use $\varepsilon_L$ formula above | Forgetting $R_L$ is in **parallel** with $R_2$ |
| Resolution calculation | $R_{res} = 1/N$ | Using $N$ as number of taps, not turns |
| Power dissipation check | $P = V_{in}^2 / R_T$ | Exceeding rated power causes burnout |
| Non-linearity calculation | $NL = \dfrac{\vert V_{actual} - V_{ideal} \vert}{V_{in}} \times 100\%$ | Sampling only at endpoints, ignoring mid-stroke |

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow (Mermaid)

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation Guide:** This Mermaid block represents the **functional signal flow** of a potentiometric sensor system, mapping the interactions between the mechanical input, transduction element, signal conditioning, and output stage. This is the **adapted block diagram** that satisfies the KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE rule for complex physical drawings.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    MechInputA[Mechanical Input Linear Displacement x] --> MechConv
    MechInputB[Mechanical Input Angular Displacement theta] --> MechConv
    MechConv{Mechanical Coupling Shaft or Slider} --> ResistTrack
    ResistTrack[Resistive Track Carbon Cermet or Wire Wound] --> Wiper
    Wiper[Sliding Wiper Contact] --> VoltDiv[Voltage Divider Network R1 and R2]
    VoltDiv --> Buffer
    ExcV[Excitation Source V_in DC or AC] --> VoltDiv
    Buffer[Unity Gain Op-Amp Voltage Follower] --> Output
    Buffer --> Load
    Output[Buffered Output V_out to ADC or Display] --> End1((End))
    Load[Load Resistance R_L] --> End2((End))
    VoltDiv --> LoadError{Loading Error Analysis}
    LoadError --> NonLin[Non-Linearity Correction Look-Up Table]
    NonLin --> Output
```

## 4.2 Detailed Functional Stage Description

### Stage 1 — Mechanical Input Stage
- **Inputs:** Linear displacement $x$ (in m or mm) OR angular displacement $\theta$ (in degrees or radians)
- **Element:** Mechanical coupling (shaft, slider, or lever)
- **Function:** Converts external physical motion into a position that drives the wiper

### Stage 2 — Transduction Stage
- **Element:** Resistive track + wiper
- **Physical Phenomenon:** Mechanical motion $\rightarrow$ Change in tapped resistance $\rightarrow$ Voltage division
- **Key Relationship:** $R_2 = R_\ell \cdot x$ (linear) or $R_2 = R_\ell \cdot \theta$ (rotary)

### Stage 3 — Excitation Stage
- **Source:** Stable DC voltage reference (e.g., $V_{in} = 5\ \text{V}$ for ADC compatibility)
- **Constraint:** Power dissipation $P = V_{in}^2 / R_T$ must be within the rated power

### Stage 4 — Signal Conditioning Stage
- **Component:** Voltage follower (op-amp buffer)
- **Purpose:** Eliminates loading error by providing high input impedance and low output impedance
- **Optional Additions:** Anti-aliasing filter, instrumentation amplifier for gain adjustment

### Stage 5 — Output Stage
- **Destination:** ADC, microcontroller, display, or feedback controller
- **Output Range:** $0\ \text{V}$ to $V_{in}$ (corresponding to full displacement range)

## 4.3 Physical Schematic (Conceptual ASCII Representation)

```
              [End Terminal 1: +V_in]
                     │
                     │
              ┌──────┴──────┐
              │   Resistive  │
              │     Track    │
              │   R_T (Ω)    │
              │  ═══════════ │
              │  ──────────  │  ← wiper sliding contact
              │      │       │
              └──────┼───────┘
                     │ Wiper (Terminal 2: V_out)
                     │
                     ├────► To Buffer / Load
                     │
              ┌──────┴──────┐
              │   Resistive  │
              │  Continuation│
              │   R_T (Ω)    │
              │  ═══════════ │
              └──────┬───────┘
                     │
                     │
              [End Terminal 3: GND]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Schematic Drawing Tip:** In board exams, always draw the **potentiometer as a rectangular resistor with an arrow (wiper) pointing into the track**, label all three terminals clearly (1, 2, 3 or A, W, B), and show the excitation $V_{in}$ and the output $V_{out}$ with polarity markings.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Exam Pattern:** Part A questions are direct, definition-based, or short derivation problems. Each answer should be **80–120 words** with at least one **formula** and one **labeled diagram mention**.

### **Question A1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Define a potentiometer. List its main components and explain its working principle in brief.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
A potentiometer is a **three-terminal passive resistive transducer** that converts linear or angular mechanical displacement into a proportional output voltage. Its main components are: (1) **Resistive track** (carbon/cermet/wire-wound), (2) **Sliding wiper**, (3) **Shaft or slider** for mechanical input, (4) **Three terminals** (two end terminals and one wiper), and (5) **Housing with bearings**. Working principle: A reference voltage $V_{in}$ is applied across the two end terminals. The wiper divides the total resistance $R_T$ into $R_1$ and $R_2$. The output voltage follows the voltage divider rule:

$$V_{out} = V_{in} \cdot \frac{R_2}{R_T} = V_{in} \cdot \frac{x}{L}$$

for a linear displacement $x$ over a total track length $L$. **(3 Marks)**

### **Question A2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Compare linear and rotary potentiometers. State two applications of each.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| **Parameter** | **Linear Potentiometer** | **Rotary Potentiometer** |
|---|---|---|
| Mechanical Input | Linear translation $x$ | Angular rotation $\theta$ |
| Output Relation | $V_{out} = V_{in} \cdot x/L$ | $V_{out} = V_{in} \cdot \theta/\theta_{max}$ |
| Typical Range | Few mm to several meters | Typically $\le 360^\circ$ |

**Linear applications:** (1) Liquid level sensor, (2) Joystick X-axis slider.
**Rotary applications:** (1) Volume control in audio, (2) Throttle position sensor in automobiles.
**(3 Marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern:** Part B questions carry 14 marks each and may have internal choice. Each long question typically has **two sub-parts (a) and (b)** of 7 marks each. Provide diagrams and step-by-step derivations to score full marks.

### **Question B-A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

#### **Part (a) — 7 Marks**
**With a neat schematic diagram, explain the construction and working of a linear potentiometer. Derive the relationship between output voltage and mechanical displacement.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Schematic Diagram (2 Marks):**
Draw a rectangular resistance track with two end terminals (1 and 3) and a wiper terminal (2). Show $V_{in}$ applied across terminals 1 and 3, and $V_{out}$ taken between wiper (2) and terminal (3). Label the track length as $L$ and wiper position as $x$.

**Step 2 — Construction Details (2 Marks):**
The linear potentiometer consists of:
- A **straight resistance track** (carbon film on phenolic substrate)
- A **sliding wiper** (spring-loaded metal contact)
- A **mechanical actuator** (slider rod) that drives the wiper
- **Three terminals** mounted on a housing

**Step 3 — Working Principle (1 Mark):**
When the slider is displaced by $x$, the track resistance is divided into $R_1$ (length $x$) and $R_2$ (length $L - x$). A DC voltage $V_{in}$ is applied across the full track, and the output is the voltage drop across $R_2$.

**Step 4 — Derivation (2 Marks):**
Resistance per unit length: $R_\ell = R_T / L$
Therefore: $R_2 = R_\ell \cdot (L - x) = R_T (L - x)/L$

Applying voltage divider (with output across $R_1$ from wiper to start):

$$V_{out} = V_{in} \cdot \frac{R_1}{R_T} = V_{in} \cdot \frac{x}{L}$$

This is a **linear** relationship between $V_{out}$ and $x$. **(7 Marks Total)**

#### **Part (b) — 7 Marks**
**A linear potentiometer of total resistance $10\ \text{k}\Omega$ is used to measure displacement. The track length is $200\ \text{mm}$ and an excitation voltage of $5\ \text{V}$ is applied. If the wiper is at $60\ \text{mm}$ from the reference end, find (i) the output voltage, (ii) the resistance between the wiper and the ground end, and (iii) the sensitivity.**

**Given Data:**
- $R_T = 10\ \text{k}\Omega = 10{,}000\ \Omega$
- $L = 200\ \text{mm}$
- $V_{in} = 5\ \text{V}$
- $x = 60\ \text{mm}$

**Solution:**

**(i) Output Voltage (3 Marks):**

$$V_{out} = V_{in} \cdot \frac{x}{L} = 5 \cdot \frac{60}{200} = 5 \cdot 0.3 = 1.5\ \text{V}$$

**(ii) Resistance between wiper and ground end (2 Marks):**

The resistance from the wiper to the ground end corresponds to the length $x = 60\ \text{mm}$:

$$R_1 = \frac{R_T \cdot x}{L} = \frac{10{,}000 \cdot 60}{200} = 3000\ \Omega = 3\ \text{k}\Omega$$

The resistance from the wiper to the $V_{in}$ end is:

$$R_2 = R_T - R_1 = 10{,}000 - 3{,}000 = 7{,}000\ \Omega = 7\ \text{k}\Omega$$

Assuming the wiper is referenced to the ground end, the resistance between the wiper and ground is:

$$R_{wiper-GND} = R_1 = 3\ \text{k}\Omega$$

**(iii) Sensitivity (2 Marks):**

$$S = \frac{dV_{out}}{dx} = \frac{V_{in}}{L} = \frac{5\ \text{V}}{200\ \text{mm}} = 0.025\ \text{V/mm} = 25\ \text{mV/mm}$$

**Final Answers:** $V_{out} = 1.5\ \text{V}$, $R_{wiper-GND} = 3\ \text{k}\Omega$, $S = 25\ \text{mV/mm}$ **(7 Marks Total)**

---

### **Question B-B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] — Alternative Choice**

#### **Part (a) — 7 Marks**
**Explain the construction and working of a rotary potentiometer. Derive its transfer function in terms of angular displacement.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Construction (2 Marks):**
A rotary potentiometer consists of:
- A **circular (arc-shaped) resistance track** mounted on a circular substrate
- A **rotating wiper** attached to a central shaft
- The shaft rotates through a specified angular range (typically $0^\circ$ to $300^\circ$ for single-turn, or $0^\circ$ to $3600^\circ$ for multi-turn)
- Three terminals: two at the track ends and one on the wiper/shaft

**Step 2 — Working (2 Marks):**
When the shaft rotates by an angle $\theta$, the wiper moves along the curved track, changing the tapped resistance. The track has a uniform resistance per unit angle $R_\alpha = R_T / \theta_{max}$. A reference voltage $V_{in}$ is applied across the track ends, and the output is taken from the wiper.

**Step 3 — Derivation (3 Marks):**
Let $\theta$ be the angular displacement from the zero-output reference. The resistance between the wiper and the reference (ground) end is:

$$R_1 = R_\alpha \cdot \theta = \frac{R_T \cdot \theta}{\theta_{max}}$$

Applying the voltage divider rule:

$$V_{out} = V_{in} \cdot \frac{R_1}{R_1 + R_2} = V_{in} \cdot \frac{R_1}{R_T} = V_{in} \cdot \frac{\theta}{\theta_{max}}$$

**Transfer Function:**

$$\boxed{V_{out}(\theta) = V_{in} \cdot \frac{\theta}{\theta_{max}}}$$

This is **linear** with respect to angular displacement. Sensitivity is $S = V_{in}/\theta_{max}$ in V/rad. **(7 Marks Total)**

#### **Part (b) — 7 Marks**
**Discuss the loading effect on a potentiometer. A $5\ \text{k}\Omega$ potentiometer with a $5\ \text{V}$ excitation is connected to a load of $10\ \text{k}\Omega$. If the wiper is at the midpoint, calculate the (i) ideal output voltage, (ii) actual loaded output voltage, and (iii) percentage loading error.**

**Given Data:**
- $R_T = 5\ \text{k}\Omega$, $V_{in} = 5\ \text{V}$, $R_L = 10\ \text{k}\Omega$
- Wiper at midpoint: $R_1 = R_2 = R_T/2 = 2.5\ \text{k}\Omega$

**Solution:**

**(i) Ideal Output Voltage (2 Marks):**
At midpoint, the ideal output is:

$$V_{out,ideal} = V_{in} \cdot \frac{R_2}{R_T} = 5 \cdot \frac{2.5}{5} = 2.5\ \text{V}$$

**(ii) Loaded Output Voltage (3 Marks):**
With load $R_L$ in parallel with $R_2$:

$$R_{2,eff} = \frac{R_2 \cdot R_L}{R_2 + R_L} = \frac{2.5 \cdot 10}{2.5 + 10} = \frac{25}{12.5} = 2\ \text{k}\Omega$$

The loaded output:

$$V_{out,loaded} = V_{in} \cdot \frac{R_{2,eff}}{R_1 + R_{2,eff}} = 5 \cdot \frac{2}{2.5 + 2} = 5 \cdot \frac{2}{4.5} = \frac{10}{4.5} \approx 2.222\ \text{V}$$

**(iii) Percentage Loading Error (2 Marks):**

$$\varepsilon_L = \frac{V_{out,ideal} - V_{out,loaded}}{V_{out,ideal}} \times 100\% = \frac{2.5 - 2.222}{2.5} \times 100\% = \frac{0.278}{2.5} \times 100\% \approx 11.11\%$$

**Final Answers:** $V_{out,ideal} = 2.5\ \text{V}$, $V_{out,loaded} \approx 2.222\ \text{V}$, $\varepsilon_L \approx 11.11\%$ **(7 Marks Total)**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Board Exams:**
> 1. **Unit Inconsistency:** Mixing mm and m in displacement calculations. Always convert to SI units (meters) before substituting into formulas.
> 2. **Wiper Position Confusion:** Students often confuse $R_1$ and $R_2$ definitions. **Always clearly state which segment the output is measured across.**
> 3. **Loading Error Omission:** Many students ignore the loading effect entirely. If the problem mentions a load $R_L$, you **must** account for it.
> 4. **Missing Diagrams:** Even for derivation questions, a small labeled schematic of the potentiometer (with terminals, wiper, and $V_{in}/V_{out}$ markings) fetches **1–2 easy marks**.
> 5. **Resolution Misconception:** Resolution applies to **wire-wound** potentiometers only; carbon film and conductive plastic have **infinite (theoretical) resolution** because the track is continuous.
> 6. **Power Rating Neglect:** Failing to verify that $P = V_{in}^2/R_T$ is within the rated power can lead to a deduction of 1 mark in design-oriented questions.
> 7. **Sign Convention:** In rotary potentiometers, define the zero reference angle clearly; otherwise, the sign of $V_{out}$ becomes ambiguous.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Read this section 10 minutes before the exam:**

- ☐ A **potentiometer** is a **three-terminal passive resistive transducer** based on the **voltage divider principle**.
- ☐ Two main types: **Linear** ($V_{out} = V_{in} \cdot x/L$) and **Rotary** ($V_{out} = V_{in} \cdot \theta/\theta_{max}$).
- ☐ The **total track resistance** $R_T$ is the sum $R_1 + R_2$ at all wiper positions.
- ☐ The output voltage is **independent of $R_T$** in theory — it depends only on the geometric ratio ($x/L$ or $\theta/\theta_{max}$).
- ☐ **Loading error** occurs when the load $R_L$ is comparable to or less than $R_T$. Always buffer with a **voltage follower**.
- ☐ **Sensitivity** for linear: $S = V_{in}/L$ (V/m); for rotary: $S = V_{in}/\theta_{max}$ (V/rad).
- ☐ **Wire-wound** potentiometers have **limited resolution** = $1/N$ where $N$ is the number of turns. **Carbon/cermet/conductive plastic** have **infinite (continuous) resolution**.
- ☐ **Advantages:** Simple, cheap, direct voltage output, no signal conditioning needed for low frequencies.
- ☐ **Disadvantages:** Mechanical wear, contact noise, loading error, friction on input.
- ☐ **Applications:** Throttle position sensors, joysticks, volume controls, liquid level sensors, calibration trimmers.
- ☐ **Power dissipation limit:** $P = V_{in}^2/R_T$ must be within rated power to prevent burnout.
- ☐ **Three terminal labels:** Terminal 1 (V_in), Terminal 2 (Wiper / V_out), Terminal 3 (GND).
- ☐ **Buffering** with op-amp voltage follower eliminates loading error and makes the output drive-capable.
- ☐ Always include a **labeled schematic diagram** in your answer for full marks.
- ☐ Standard unit conversions: $1\ \text{mm} = 10^{-3}\ \text{m}$, $1\ \text{k}\Omega = 10^3\ \Omega$, $1^\circ = \pi/180\ \text{rad}$.

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
