> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** LVDTs

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Linear Variable Differential Transformer (LVDT)** is a passive inductive transducer that converts **rectilinear (linear) displacement** of a movable ferromagnetic core into a proportional **differential AC voltage** signal. It operates on the principle of **mutual induction** between one primary winding and two symmetrically placed secondary windings wound on a common cylindrical former.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The LVDT is classified under *Inductive Transducers* in Module 1. Students must clearly distinguish between the **Null Position (Zero Displacement)**, the **Linear Operating Range**, and the **Phase-Sensitive Detection** concept for full marks.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Terminology Standard:**
> - **Primary Coil ($P$)** — Excited by a stable AC source (typically **1 V to 24 V RMS at 50 Hz to 20 kHz**).
> - **Secondary Coils ($S_1$ and $S_2$)** — Identical in turns, connected in **series opposition**.
> - **Core** — Soft ferromagnetic material (nickel-iron or mu-metal), slides coaxially inside the former.
> - **Differential Output ($V_{out}$)** — The algebraic difference of the two induced EMFs.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine a **seesaw (teeter-totter)** in a playground. The seesaw's central pivot represents the **null position** of the LVDT core. Now imagine a small child sitting exactly at the pivot point — the seesaw is perfectly balanced, and a sensor measuring tilt would read **zero**.

If the child moves slightly to the **left**, the left side goes down, and the right side goes up. The sensor reads a **positive** value. If the child moves to the **right**, the reading becomes **negative**. The LVDT works the same way:

- The **child's position** = the core's displacement $x$.
- The **left tilt** = induced EMF in $S_1$ ($V_{s1}$).
- The **right tilt** = induced EMF in $S_2$ ($V_{s2}$).
- The **net output** = $V_{out} = V_{s1} - V_{s2}$ (series-opposition connection).

By comparing how much the two sides are *differently* tilted, we know **how far** and in **which direction** the child moved — without ever needing a physical contact or a spring.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Typical Value / Standard |
|---|---|
| **Excitation Frequency** | **1 kHz to 20 kHz** (standard **2.5 kHz**) |
| **Excitation Voltage** | **1 V to 24 V RMS** |
| **Operating Range** | $\pm 0.1\ \text{mm}$ to $\pm 500\ \text{mm}$ |
| **Linearity** | **0.1% to 0.5%** of full scale |
| **Sensitivity** | **0.1 V/mm to 10 V/mm** (design dependent) |
| **Core Material** | Nickel-Iron alloy (high permeability $\mu_r \approx 10{,}000$ to $100{,}000$) |
| **Resolution** | Effectively **infinite** (limited only by readout electronics) |
| **Hysteresis** | Negligible (no mechanical contact) |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Constructional Details

The LVDT consists of the following precision-engineered components:

1. **Former (Bobbin)**: A hollow cylindrical spool, typically made of ceramic, phenolic resin, or polymer. It provides the mechanical skeleton on which windings are placed.
2. **Primary Winding (P)**: Wound centrally on the former. Number of turns ($N_p$) is uniform and is energized by an AC excitation source.
3. **Two Secondary Windings ($S_1$ and $S_2$)**: Identical in construction, wound on either side of the primary. Each has $N_s$ turns, and they are connected in **series opposition** (i.e., the dotted ends are connected to opposite output terminals).
4. **Movable Core**: A cylindrical slug of high-permeability ferromagnetic material, attached to the displacement-sensing rod. The core's axial position determines the magnetic coupling.
5. **Shielding**: A mu-metal or soft-iron outer shell to provide electromagnetic shielding against external stray fields.

## 2.2 Working Principle — Step-by-Step Logic

- **Step 1 (Excitation):** An AC voltage $V_p$ of fixed amplitude and frequency is applied to the primary winding, producing an alternating magnetic flux $\phi_p$ along the former.
- **Step 2 (Mutual Induction):** The flux links with secondary windings $S_1$ and $S_2$, inducing EMFs $E_{s1}$ and $E_{s2}$ respectively. By Faraday's law: $E_s = -N_s \dfrac{d\phi}{dt}$.
- **Step 3 (Core Displacement Effect):** When the core is **exactly centered (null position)**, the flux coupling to $S_1$ and $S_2$ is **identical**, so $E_{s1} = E_{s2}$. The series-opposition connection makes $V_{out} = E_{s1} - E_{s2} = 0$.
- **Step 4 (Displacement to the Right):** Core moves towards $S_2$. The flux coupling to $S_2$ increases, while that to $S_1$ decreases. Hence $E_{s2} > E_{s1}$, producing a finite $V_{out}$ whose phase is **opposite** to the primary voltage.
- **Step 5 (Displacement to the Left):** Core moves towards $S_1$. Now $E_{s1} > E_{s2}$, and $V_{out}$ has the **same phase** as the primary voltage.
- **Step 6 (Linearity):** Within a designed range $\pm x_{max}$, the differential output is a **linear function of core displacement**.

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Why" Behind Series Opposition:**
> If the two secondaries were connected in series-aiding, the output would always be the **sum** of the two EMFs and would never become zero. Connecting them in opposition (anti-series) ensures the **common-mode component** cancels at the null point, leaving only the **differential component** proportional to displacement.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Equation | Meaning / Use |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $V_{out} = V_{s1} - V_{s2}$ | Differential output voltage (series-opposition) |
| 2 | $V_s = 4.44 \, f \, N_s \, \phi_m$ | RMS induced EMF in a secondary (transformer EMF equation) |
| 3 | $M_1 = M_0 + k x$ | Mutual inductance to $S_1$ for core displacement $x > 0$ |
| 4 | $M_2 = M_0 - k x$ | Mutual inductance to $S_2$ for core displacement $x > 0$ |
| 5 | $V_{out} = j\omega \left( M_1 - M_2 \right) I_p = j \, 2\omega k x \, I_p$ | Output phasor (proves linearity with $x$) |
| 6 | $V_{out} = K_s \cdot x$ | Compact linear form, where $K_s$ is the **sensitivity** in V/mm |
| 7 | $K_s = \dfrac{V_{out,\text{full scale}}}{x_{\text{full scale}}}$ | Definition of sensitivity |
| 8 | $\text{Sensitivity} \propto f$ | Higher excitation frequency $\Rightarrow$ higher output per unit displacement |
| 9 | $\text{Linearity \%} = \dfrac{\Delta V_{\text{actual}} - \Delta V_{\text{ideal}}}{V_{\text{full scale}}} \times 100$ | Error metric for non-ideal behavior |
| 10 | $V_{out,\text{residual}} \neq 0$ at null | Caused by harmonics, eddy currents, and winding asymmetries (called **residual voltage** $\approx 0.5\%$ to $2\%$ of full scale) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility of LVDTs:**
> LVDTs are the **gold standard** for non-contact linear displacement measurement in:
> - **Aerospace**: Hydraulic actuator position feedback in aircraft landing gear.
> - **Civil Engineering**: Structural health monitoring, bridge deflection sensors.
> - **Manufacturing**: CNC machine tool slide position verification.
> - **Nuclear Reactors**: Valve stem position in high-radiation zones (LVDTs are radiation-tolerant).
> - **Hydraulic/Pneumatic Cylinders**: Piston position in industrial presses.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of LVDT Output Voltage — Full Analytical Treatment

### 3.1.1 Setup and Assumptions

Consider an LVDT with the following notation:
- $V_p$ = RMS primary excitation voltage, angular frequency $\omega = 2\pi f$.
- $N_p$ = number of primary turns; $N_s$ = number of turns on each secondary.
- $I_p$ = RMS primary current (lagging $V_p$ by the magnetizing angle).
- $x$ = axial displacement of the core from the geometric null (positive = towards $S_2$).
- $M_1$ = mutual inductance between primary and $S_1$.
- $M_2$ = mutual inductance between primary and $S_2$.

> **Assumption 1 (Ideal Core):** The core has infinite permeability, so the only flux linking the secondaries is the one guided by the core. Fringing is neglected.
>
> **Assumption 2 (Linear Coupling):** The variation of mutual inductance with displacement is **linear** within the operating range, controlled by a coupling constant $k$ (in H/m).
>
> **Assumption 3 (Symmetric Windings):** At the null position ($x = 0$), $M_1 = M_2 = M_0$.

### 3.1.2 Mutual Inductance Variation

When the core moves a small distance $x$ towards $S_2$:

$$
M_1 = M_0 - k x
$$

$$
M_2 = M_0 + k x
$$

**Why this form?**
Moving the core **away** from $S_1$ reduces the flux linkage to $S_1$, so $M_1$ decreases. Simultaneously, it moves **closer** to $S_2$, increasing $M_2$. The constant $k$ is the rate of change of mutual inductance per unit displacement.

### 3.1.3 Induced EMFs in the Secondaries

By the principle of mutual induction, the open-circuit EMF induced in a secondary winding is:

$$
V_{s1} = j\omega M_1 \, I_p
$$

$$
V_{s2} = j\omega M_2 \, I_p
$$

**Explanation of the $j\omega$ factor:** The induced voltage **leads** the inducing current by $90^\circ$ in a purely inductive coupling, which is exactly what the imaginary unit $j$ represents in phasor algebra. Both $V_{s1}$ and $V_{s2}$ are in phase with each other (both lead $I_p$ by $90^\circ$), but their **magnitudes differ** when $x \neq 0$.

### 3.1.4 Differential Output (Series-Opposition Connection)

The two secondaries are connected in series-opposition, so the net output voltage is the **algebraic difference**:

$$
V_{out} = V_{s1} - V_{s2}
$$

Substituting the expressions for $V_{s1}$ and $V_{s2}$:

$$
V_{out} = j\omega M_1 I_p - j\omega M_2 I_p
$$

$$
V_{out} = j\omega \left( M_1 - M_2 \right) I_p
$$

### 3.1.5 Substituting the Mutual Inductance Expressions

$$
M_1 - M_2 = (M_0 - k x) - (M_0 + k x) = -2 k x
$$

Therefore:

$$
V_{out} = j\omega \left( -2 k x \right) I_p = -j \, 2\omega k x \, I_p
$$

The magnitude is:

$$
\vert V_{out} \vert = 2 \omega k \, I_p \, \vert x \vert
$$

**Interpretation:** The output voltage magnitude is **directly proportional to the magnitude of displacement** $|x|$, and the **sign of the imaginary part** ($+j$ or $-j$) encodes the **direction of motion**. This phase information is what makes **phase-sensitive demodulation** possible downstream.

### 3.1.6 Deriving the Compact Linear Form

Let us define the **sensitivity constant**:

$$
K_s = 2 \omega k I_p \quad \text{(in V/m or V/mm)}
$$

Then:

$$
V_{out} = j \, K_s \, x
$$

And in magnitude form:

$$
\vert V_{out} \vert = K_s \cdot \vert x \vert
$$

**Why is this linear?** Because we deliberately operated within the range where $M_1$ and $M_2$ vary **linearly** with $x$. Outside this range, the $M$–$x$ relationship becomes nonlinear (saturates), and the LVDT is no longer accurate.

### 3.1.7 Special Case — Null Position Verification

At the null position, $x = 0$:

$$
V_{out} = j \, 2\omega k \cdot 0 \cdot I_p = 0
$$

The output voltage is **exactly zero**, confirming the balance condition. (In practice, a small **residual voltage** of **0.5% to 2%** of full-scale appears due to manufacturing imperfections, harmonics, and capacitive pickup.)

### 3.1.8 Sensitivity Boost via Frequency

From the expression $K_s = 2\omega k I_p$, we see:

$$
K_s \propto \omega = 2\pi f
$$

**Doubling the excitation frequency doubles the output voltage per unit displacement.** This is why LVDTs are often excited at **2.5 kHz to 10 kHz** rather than at 50 Hz — the higher frequency boosts sensitivity, reduces core size, and minimizes 50 Hz mains hum interference.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — LVDT Output for Different Core Positions

| Core Position | $M_1$ | $M_2$ | $V_{s1}$ | $V_{s2}$ | $V_{out} = V_{s1} - V_{s2}$ | Phase of $V_{out}$ w.r.t. $V_p$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **At Null ($x=0$)** | $M_0$ | $M_0$ | Equal | Equal | $\mathbf{0}$ | — |
| **Displaced to right ($+x$)** | $M_0 - kx$ | $M_0 + kx$ | Smaller | Larger | $\mathbf{Negative\ phasor}$ ($0^\circ$ shift) | In phase with $V_p$ |
| **Displaced to left ($-x$)** | $M_0 + kx$ | $M_0 - kx$ | Larger | Smaller | $\mathbf{Positive\ phasor}$ | $180^\circ$ out of phase with $V_p$ |
| **Beyond $\pm x_{max}$** | Saturates | Saturates | Nonlinear | Nonlinear | **Nonlinear distortion** | — |

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style)

**Problem:** An LVDT has a primary winding excited by a **5 V, 2 kHz** source. At null, the mutual inductance to each secondary is **$M_0 = 10$ mH**. The coupling constant is **$k = 0.5$ mH/mm**. The magnetizing current is **$I_p = 50$ mA**. Calculate the differential output voltage when the core is displaced by **$x = +4$ mm**.

**Step 1:** Compute $M_1$ and $M_2$ at $x = 4$ mm.

$$
M_1 = M_0 - k x = 10\ \text{mH} - (0.5\ \text{mH/mm})(4\ \text{mm}) = 10 - 2 = 8\ \text{mH}
$$

$$
M_2 = M_0 + k x = 10\ \text{mH} + (0.5\ \text{mH/mm})(4\ \text{mm}) = 10 + 2 = 12\ \text{mH}
$$

**Step 2:** Compute angular frequency.

$$
\omega = 2\pi f = 2\pi \times 2000 = 12{,}566.37\ \text{rad/s}
$$

**Step 3:** Compute individual secondary EMFs.

$$
V_{s1} = \omega M_1 I_p = 12{,}566.37 \times 8 \times 10^{-3} \times 50 \times 10^{-3}
$$

$$
V_{s1} = 12{,}566.37 \times 4 \times 10^{-4} = 5.0265\ \text{V}
$$

$$
V_{s2} = \omega M_2 I_p = 12{,}566.37 \times 12 \times 10^{-3} \times 50 \times 10^{-3}
$$

$$
V_{s2} = 12{,}566.37 \times 6 \times 10^{-4} = 7.5398\ \text{V}
$$

**Step 4:** Compute the differential output.

$$
V_{out} = V_{s1} - V_{s2} = 5.0265 - 7.5398 = -2.5133\ \text{V}
$$

**Step 5:** Compute the sensitivity.

$$
K_s = \dfrac{\vert V_{out} \vert}{\vert x \vert} = \dfrac{2.5133}{4} = 0.6283\ \text{V/mm}
$$

**Verification using the compact formula:**

$$
V_{out} = 2 \omega k x I_p = 2 \times 12{,}566.37 \times 0.5 \times 10^{-3} \times 4 \times 10^{-3} \times 50 \times 10^{-3}
$$

$$
V_{out} = 2 \times 12{,}566.37 \times 0.5 \times 4 \times 50 \times 10^{-9}
$$

$$
V_{out} = 2 \times 12{,}566.37 \times 100 \times 10^{-9} = 2.5133\ \text{V (magnitude)}
$$

**Result:** $V_{out} = 2.5133\ \text{V}$ (with a phase of $180^\circ$ w.r.t. $V_p$, since $x > 0$ in this convention).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — LVDT Functional Architecture

The following Mermaid diagram maps the **Block-Level Functional Architecture** of an LVDT measurement system, from physical displacement to digital readout. This represents the **signal flow** rather than a literal physical drawing.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Physical Displacement x of target] --> B[Ferromagnetic Core moves axially]
    B --> C[Magnetic Flux Coupling varies in S1 and S2]
    C --> D[Primary Winding P excited by AC source Vp at frequency f]
    D --> E[EMF Vs1 induced in Secondary S1]
    D --> F[EMF Vs2 induced in Secondary S2]
    E --> G[Series Opposition Subtraction Node]
    F --> G
    G --> H[Differential Output Vout = Vs1 minus Vs2]
    H --> I{Phase Sensitive Demodulator}
    I --> J[DC Output proportional to displacement]
    J --> K[Amplifier and Filter Stage]
    K --> L[Display or Controller Readout]
    M[AC Excitation Oscillator] --> D
    I --> N[Phase Reference from Oscillator]
    N --> I
```

## 4.2 Mermaid State Diagram — LVDT Operating Regions

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> NullPosition: Core centered
    NullPosition --> LeftDisplacement: Core moves toward S1
    NullPosition --> RightDisplacement: Core moves toward S2
    LeftDisplacement --> NullPosition: Core returns to center
    RightDisplacement --> NullPosition: Core returns to center
    LeftDisplacement --> SaturationLeft: Displacement exceeds xmax
    RightDisplacement --> SaturationRight: Displacement exceeds xmax
    SaturationLeft --> NullPosition: Reset or recalibrate
    SaturationRight --> NullPosition: Reset or recalibrate
    note right of NullPosition: Vout equals 0 volts
    note right of LeftDisplacement: Vout in phase with Vp
    note right of RightDisplacement: Vout 180 degrees out of phase with Vp
    note right of SaturationLeft: Nonlinear output region
    note right of SaturationRight: Nonlinear output region
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Constructional Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[AC Source Vp] --> B[Primary Winding P at center]
    B --> C[Secondary Winding S1 on left]
    B --> D[Secondary Winding S2 on right]
    E[Movable Ferromagnetic Core] --> C
    E --> D
    C --> F[Output Terminal 1]
    D --> G[Output Terminal 2]
    F --> H[Differential Output Vout]
    G --> H
    I[Outer Mu Metal Shield] -.-> B
    I -.-> C
    I -.-> D
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagrams:**
> - The arrows represent the **direction of signal/energy flow** or **mechanical coupling**.
> - The state diagram clarifies the **three operating regions**: null (ideal balanced), linear (useful measurement), and saturation (unusable, nonlinear).
> - The constructional block diagram shows how the **outer shield** encloses the windings to reject external electromagnetic interference (EMI).

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**Define an LVDT. State any two advantages of an LVDT over a potentiometric displacement sensor.**

**Model Answer:**
An **LVDT (Linear Variable Differential Transformer)** is a passive inductive transducer that converts linear displacement of a movable ferromagnetic core into a proportional differential AC voltage, based on the principle of mutual electromagnetic induction between a primary and two symmetrically placed secondary windings.

**Two advantages over a potentiometer:**
1. **Infinite Resolution:** The LVDT has no friction or mechanical contact between the core and the windings, giving theoretically infinite resolution. A potentiometer has discrete resolution limited by the wire winding pitch.
2. **Frictionless Operation and Long Life:** LVDT has no sliding electrical contacts, so it has unlimited mechanical life and no wear-and-tear, unlike a wire-wound potentiometer whose wiper wears out.

---

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**What is residual voltage in an LVDT? How is it minimized?**

**Model Answer:**
**Residual voltage** is the small non-zero output voltage of the LVDT when the core is exactly at the null position. Ideally, $V_{out} = 0$ at null, but in practice a small voltage (typically **0.5% to 2%** of full-scale output) remains due to:
- Asymmetry between the two secondary windings (unequal turns or distributed capacitance).
- Harmonics in the primary excitation.
- Eddy current losses in the core and former.
- Stray capacitive coupling between primary and secondaries.

**Minimization methods:**
1. Use a **phase-sensitive demodulator** synchronized to the fundamental excitation frequency to reject harmonics.
2. Ensure **perfect symmetry** of the two secondary windings during manufacture.
3. Add a **grounded electrostatic shield** between the primary and the secondaries to eliminate capacitive coupling.
4. Use a **high-permeability core** and a low-loss former to reduce eddy current effects.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — KTU ESE Internal Choice)

### Question A: [KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the **construction and working principle of an LVDT**. Discuss the role of series-opposition connection. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the expression for the **differential output voltage of an LVDT** in terms of displacement $x$, excitation frequency $f$, and coupling constant $k$. Show that the output is linear with displacement. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for (a) — 7 Marks

**Construction (3 marks):**
An LVDT consists of:
- A **hollow cylindrical former** (ceramic or phenolic).
- A **primary winding (P)** at the center, energized by an AC source of voltage $V_p$ and frequency $f$.
- **Two identical secondary windings ($S_1$ and $S_2$)** placed on either side of the primary, each with $N_s$ turns.
- A **movable soft-iron/ferromagnetic core** attached to a sensing rod.
- An **outer magnetic shield** (mu-metal) to reject external interference.

**Working Principle (3 marks):**
1. The primary is excited by an AC voltage $V_p$, producing alternating flux.
2. This flux links with $S_1$ and $S_2$, inducing EMFs $V_{s1}$ and $V_{s2}$.
3. At the **null position** (core centered), $V_{s1} = V_{s2}$, and the differential output is **zero**.
4. When the core moves towards $S_1$, flux linkage to $S_1$ increases, and $V_{s1} > V_{s2}$. Output $V_{out} = V_{s1} - V_{s2}$ becomes **positive** (in phase with $V_p$).
5. When the core moves towards $S_2$, $V_{s2} > V_{s1}$, and $V_{out}$ is **negative** ($180^\circ$ out of phase).

**Role of Series-Opposition (1 mark):**
The two secondaries are connected in **anti-series** (opposition) so that their EMFs **subtract**. This ensures that at the null position the common-mode component cancels completely, leaving only the differential component proportional to displacement. It also allows **direction sensing** through the phase of the output.

---

#### Model Solution for (b) — 7 Marks

Let the mutual inductances be:

$$
M_1 = M_0 - k x \quad \text{and} \quad M_2 = M_0 + k x
$$

Induced EMFs:

$$
V_{s1} = j\omega M_1 I_p, \quad V_{s2} = j\omega M_2 I_p
$$

Differential output:

$$
V_{out} = V_{s1} - V_{s2} = j\omega (M_1 - M_2) I_p
$$

Substituting:

$$
M_1 - M_2 = -2 k x
$$

$$
V_{out} = -j \, 2\omega k x I_p
$$

Magnitude:

$$
\vert V_{out} \vert = 2 \omega k I_p \cdot \vert x \vert
$$

Defining sensitivity $K_s = 2\omega k I_p$:

$$
V_{out} = K_s \cdot x
$$

**Linearity Proof:** Within the designed operating range $\pm x_{max}$, the mutual inductances $M_1$ and $M_2$ vary **linearly** with $x$ (a direct consequence of the linear $B$–$H$ region of the high-permeability core). Hence the differential output is a **first-order linear function of displacement**, which is the defining advantage of the LVDT for precision measurements.

---

### Question B: [KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]

**(a)** Explain the **phasor diagram of an LVDT at the null position, at a positive displacement, and at a negative displacement**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An LVDT is excited by a **10 V, 5 kHz** source. At the null position, the mutual inductance to each secondary is **$M_0 = 5$ mH**. The coupling constant is **$k = 0.2$ mH/mm**. If the magnetizing current is **$I_p = 20$ mA**, determine:
   1. The differential output voltage at **$x = +3$ mm**.
   2. The **sensitivity** of the LVDT in V/mm.
   3. The output voltage at **$x = -3$ mm** and comment on the phase. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for (a) — 7 Marks

**Phasor Diagram Explanation (7 marks, with 2 marks each for the three cases and 1 mark for the consolidated explanation):**

- **At Null Position ($x = 0$):** $V_{s1}$ and $V_{s2}$ are equal in magnitude and **in phase** with each other (both lead $I_p$ by $90^\circ$). The series-opposition connection makes $V_{out} = V_{s1} - V_{s2} = 0$. The phasor diagram shows $V_{s1}$ and $V_{s2}$ overlapping with equal lengths and opposite directions along the same axis.

- **At Positive Displacement ($x > 0$, core towards $S_2$):** $\vert V_{s2} \vert > \vert V_{s1} \vert$. The phasor diagram shows $V_{s2}$ as a longer vector and $V_{s1}$ as a shorter vector, both still leading $I_p$ by $90^\circ$. The resultant $V_{out} = V_{s1} - V_{s2}$ is a **negative** phasor (along the $-j$ axis), indicating that $V_{out}$ lags $V_p$ by $90^\circ$ (or equivalently, is $180^\circ$ out of phase with the positive $V_{out}$ convention).

- **At Negative Displacement ($x < 0$, core towards $S_1$):** $\vert V_{s1} \vert > \vert V_{s2} \vert$. The phasor diagram reverses the relative lengths, and $V_{out}$ becomes a **positive** phasor along the $+j$ axis, in phase with the positive direction.

**Key Inference:** The phasor diagram makes it visually clear that the **magnitude** of $V_{out}$ encodes the **amount** of displacement, while the **phase** of $V_{out}$ encodes the **direction** of displacement.

---

#### Model Solution for (b) — 7 Marks

**Given:** $V_p = 10$ V, $f = 5$ kHz, $M_0 = 5$ mH, $k = 0.2$ mH/mm, $I_p = 20$ mA.

**Step 1: Angular frequency**

$$
\omega = 2\pi f = 2\pi \times 5000 = 31{,}415.93\ \text{rad/s}
$$

**Step 2: Mutual inductances at $x = +3$ mm**

$$
M_1 = M_0 - k x = 5 - 0.2 \times 3 = 5 - 0.6 = 4.4\ \text{mH}
$$

$$
M_2 = M_0 + k x = 5 + 0.2 \times 3 = 5 + 0.6 = 5.6\ \text{mH}
$$

**Step 3: Differential output voltage at $x = +3$ mm (1st part — 3 marks)**

$$
V_{out} = 2 \omega k x I_p = 2 \times 31{,}415.93 \times 0.2 \times 10^{-3} \times 3 \times 10^{-3} \times 20 \times 10^{-3}
$$

$$
V_{out} = 2 \times 31{,}415.93 \times 0.2 \times 3 \times 20 \times 10^{-9}
$$

$$
V_{out} = 2 \times 31{,}415.93 \times 12 \times 10^{-9} = 0.7540\ \text{V}
$$

**Step 4: Sensitivity (2nd part — 2 marks)**

$$
K_s = \dfrac{\vert V_{out} \vert}{\vert x \vert} = \dfrac{0.7540}{3} = 0.2513\ \text{V/mm}
$$

**Step 5: Output at $x = -3$ mm (3rd part — 2 marks)**

By symmetry of the LVDT:

$$
V_{out}(x = -3\ \text{mm}) = -0.7540\ \text{V (magnitude)}
$$

**Comment on phase:** Since $x$ is negative (core displaced towards $S_1$), the output is **in phase** with the primary excitation voltage $V_p$, whereas at $x = +3$ mm it was **180° out of phase**. This phase reversal is the basis of **phase-sensitive direction detection**.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
>
> 1. **Confusing $V_{s1} - V_{s2}$ vs $V_{s2} - V_{s1}$:** The order of subtraction in series opposition depends on the winding direction convention. Many students write the wrong sign and lose 2-3 marks instantly. **Always state your assumption explicitly** at the start of the solution.
>
> 2. **Forgetting the $j$ (90° phase) in the EMF equation:** Writing $V_{s1} = M_1 I_p$ instead of $V_{s1} = j\omega M_1 I_p$ is a **fatal error**. The $j\omega$ factor encodes the $90°$ phase lead that is essential for LVDT operation.
>
> 3. **Mixing up null, positive, and negative displacement outputs:** At the null, students sometimes write a non-zero value, forgetting that the EMFs are equal. Always **verify $V_{out} = 0$** at $x = 0$ as a sanity check.
>
> 4. **Ignoring units in numerical problems:** $k$ in mH/mm must be converted to H/m (or kept consistent) before substituting into the formula with $x$ in mm. **Always show unit conversion steps.**
>
> 5. **Missing the residual voltage discussion:** In "advantages and disadvantages" or "limitations" type questions, examiners expect a mention of **residual voltage** as a key limitation. Skipping it costs 1-2 marks.
>
> 6. **Drawing a poor/neat-less block diagram:** KTU examiners specifically allocate **1-2 marks** for a labeled diagram. Use a clean rectangular block with clear labels for P, $S_1$, $S_2$, and the core. Do not scribble.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — LVDTs:**
>
> - **Definition:** LVDT is a passive inductive transducer that converts **linear displacement** into a **differential AC voltage** using **mutual induction**.
> - **Core Components:** Primary (P), Secondary 1 ($S_1$), Secondary 2 ($S_2$), movable ferromagnetic core, outer shield.
> - **Key Connection Rule:** The two secondaries are always in **series-opposition (anti-series)** to enable differential output and direction sensing.
> - **Null Condition:** When the core is at the geometric center, $V_{s1} = V_{s2}$ and $V_{out} = 0$.
> - **Output Equation:** $V_{out} = 2 \omega k x I_p$ (magnitude form) and $V_{out} = j\omega(M_1 - M_2)I_p$ (phasor form).
> - **Linearity:** Output is **linear with displacement** within the range $\pm x_{max}$.
> - **Sensitivity:** $K_s = 2\omega k I_p$, which is **directly proportional to excitation frequency $f$**.
> - **Direction Sensing:** Achieved by the **phase** of the output voltage (in phase vs 180° out of phase with $V_p$).
> - **Residual Voltage:** Non-zero output at null (0.5% to 2% of full scale), caused by winding asymmetry, harmonics, and eddy currents.
> - **Advantages:** Infinite resolution, no friction, no hysteresis, high linearity, rugged, infinite mechanical life.
> - **Disadvantages:** Requires AC excitation, sensitive to stray magnetic fields, residual voltage, dynamic measurements limited by excitation frequency.
> - **Standard Excitation:** **1 kHz to 20 kHz** (commonly 2.5 kHz), **1 V to 24 V RMS**.
> - **Applications:** Aerospace actuator feedback, hydraulic cylinder position, civil structural deflection, nuclear valve position, CNC machine tools.
> - **KTU Exam Tip:** Always draw a labeled block diagram, state assumptions clearly, and verify the null condition in numericals.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

> - **Topic:** Velocity Sensors - Optical Encoders, Tacho Generator, Hall Effect Sensor; Acceleration Sensors; Heading Sensors - Compass, Gyroscope Sensor, IMU, GPS, Real Time Differential GPS

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Velocity Sensors — Formal Definition

A **Velocity Sensor** is a transduction device that converts the rate of change of displacement (linear or angular) of a target object into a quantifiable, measurable electrical signal (analog voltage, digital pulse train, or frequency output). Velocity is the first time-derivative of position, and in the context of KTU robotics, mechatronics, and industrial automation, velocity measurement forms the foundational layer of any closed-loop motion control system.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** A velocity sensor measures the time rate of change of position, expressed as $\vec{v} = \frac{d\vec{x}}{dt}$ (linear) or $\vec{\omega} = \frac{d\theta}{dt}$ (angular), and provides a usable output for feedback control, instrumentation, and navigation.

### 1.1.1 Classification of Velocity Sensors (KTU Taxonomy)

| Class | Operating Principle | Typical Output |
|---|---|---|
| **Optical Encoder** | Light interruption by slotted/code disc | Digital pulse train (TTL/HTL) |
| **Tacho Generator** | Electromagnetic induction (Faraday's Law) | Analog DC/AC voltage |
| **Hall Effect Sensor** | Lorentz force on charge carriers | Digital switching pulse / analog voltage |

### 1.2 Optical Encoders

**Formal Definition:** An optical encoder is a non-contact angular or linear position-to-digital transducer that utilizes the **photoelectric effect** and slotted disc patterns to generate a quantized pulse train whose frequency is directly proportional to the velocity of the shaft or carriage being monitored.

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy — The "Stroboscopic Speedometer":** Imagine shining a flashlight onto a spinning bicycle wheel painted with alternating black and white spokes. If you count how many times a black spoke passes the flashlight per second using a photodiode, that count is your velocity. An optical encoder is essentially this exact mechanism — a light source (LED), a patterned disc, and a photodetector — miniaturized into a precision instrument.

**Two Sub-Types:**

1. **Incremental Optical Encoder** — Produces a stream of pulses (typically Channel A and Channel B in quadrature) whose frequency $f$ relates to angular velocity as:
$$\omega = \frac{2\pi f}{N}$$
where $N$ is the **pulses per revolution (PPR)** rating.

2. **Absolute Optical Encoder** — Each shaft angle corresponds to a unique multi-bit Gray code word on parallel output lines (e.g., 8-bit → 256 unique positions per revolution).

### 1.3 Tacho Generator (Tachogenerator)

**Formal Definition:** A tachogenerator is a small, specialized electromechanical generator (essentially a permanent-magnet DC motor operated in reverse) whose output voltage is linearly proportional to the rotational speed of its input shaft, derived from **Faraday's Law of Electromagnetic Induction**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy — "Bicycle Dynamo in Reverse Feedback":** When you spin a bicycle dynamo, the LED brightens. A tachogenerator is the precise, instrument-grade cousin of that dynamo — instead of illuminating an LED, its voltage is fed back to a motor drive or PLC to regulate speed in industrial servos and CNC spindles.

**Mathematical Statement:**
$$E_b = K_e \cdot \omega$$
where $E_b$ is the back-EMF (V), $K_e$ is the voltage constant (V·s/rad), and $\omega$ is angular velocity (rad/s).

### 1.4 Hall Effect Sensor

**Formal Definition:** A Hall effect sensor exploits the **Hall Effect** (discovered by Edwin Hall in 1879), in which a voltage potential is generated transverse to both the current flow and an applied magnetic field in a conductor or semiconductor, to detect the presence, speed, and direction of ferrous or magnetized targets.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Physics:** When a current-carrying semiconductor is placed in a magnetic field perpendicular to the current, the charge carriers (electrons/holes) experience a Lorentz force $\vec{F} = q(\vec{v} \times \vec{B})$ that pushes them to one side of the conductor, creating a measurable transverse Hall voltage $V_H$.

**Hall Voltage Equation:**
$$V_H = \frac{I \cdot B}{n \cdot e \cdot t}$$
where $I$ = current, $B$ = magnetic flux density, $n$ = carrier concentration, $e$ = electronic charge, $t$ = thickness of sensing element.

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy — "Magnetic Speedometer on a Bicycle Wheel":** Tape a small neodymium magnet to a bicycle wheel spoke. Place a Hall sensor on the fork. Each time the magnet passes the sensor, the Hall voltage spikes. Count the spikes per minute — that is your wheel speed. This is exactly how many e-bikes, ABS systems, and brushless DC motor controllers measure speed.

---

## 1.5 Acceleration Sensors (Accelerometers)

**Formal Definition:** An accelerometer is a **micro-electromechanical system (MEMS)** or macroscopic transducer that measures proper acceleration — the rate of change of velocity — by detecting the displacement of a proof mass suspended on a flexure relative to a fixed frame, or equivalently, the force required to constrain that mass under acceleration.

$$a = \frac{d^2 x}{dt^2} = \frac{F}{m}$$

**Types Covered in KTU Module 1:**

| Type | Sensing Mechanism | Application |
|---|---|---|
| **Piezoelectric Accelerometer** | Charge generation under stress | Vibration monitoring, modal analysis |
| **Piezoresistive Accelerometer** | Resistance change under strain | High-g shock, automotive airbags |
| **Capacitive MEMS Accelerometer** | Capacitance change due to gap variation | Smartphones, IMUs, drones |

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy — "The Balloon in a Box":** Picture a helium balloon floating inside a sealed box. If you push the box to the right, the balloon appears to drift to the left (relative to the box). The proof mass in an accelerometer behaves the same way — under acceleration, it lags behind, and that lag is precisely measured as capacitance or voltage change. When the box is in free-fall, the balloon "floats" with no displacement → output reads 0 g. That is why astronauts experience "weightlessness" but an accelerometer still functions in orbit.

---

## 1.6 Heading Sensors

A **Heading Sensor** determines the orientation of a vehicle/robot with respect to a reference frame (True North, Earth-Center, or Local Tangent Plane). The KTU syllabus focuses on the following:

### 1.6.1 Magnetic Compass (Electronic / Fluxgate Compass)

**Formal Definition:** An electronic compass measures the direction of the Earth's magnetic field vector using either a **fluxgate magnetometer** (two saturable inductors) or a **Hall-effect / magnetoresistive 3-axis sensor**, then computes heading angle $\psi$ relative to magnetic north using the arctan2 function:

$$\psi = \operatorname{atan2}(B_y, B_x)$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy — "A Tiny Dip-Needle on a Chip":** The same reason a magnetic compass needle on a hiking watch points north is the same reason a smartphone knows which way is north — a tiny 3-axis magnetic sensor detects the horizontal component of Earth's field and the processor turns that into a heading angle.

### 1.6.2 Gyroscope Sensor

**Formal Definition:** A gyroscope is a sensor that measures **angular velocity** ($\omega$ in rad/s) or, in some configurations, **integrated angular displacement** (attitude, in degrees or radians) by exploiting the **Coriolis effect** (in MEMS gyros) or the conservation of angular momentum (in mechanical/RLG/FOG gyros).

$$\Omega_{\text{meas}} = \int_0^t \omega(\tau) \, d\tau$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy — "The Spinning Top That Refuses to Tip":** A classic child's spinning top stays upright because of conservation of angular momentum. A gyroscope, even when packaged on a microchip, obeys the same Newtonian physics: it resists changes to its axis of rotation, and any external angular disturbance is detected as a measurable Coriolis force on a vibrating proof mass.

### 1.6.3 Inertial Measurement Unit (IMU)

**Formal Definition:** An IMU is a **9-DoF (Degrees of Freedom) electronic module** that combines:
- **3-axis MEMS Accelerometer** (measures linear acceleration, $a_x, a_y, a_z$)
- **3-axis MEMS Gyroscope** (measures angular rate, $\omega_x, \omega_y, \omega_z$)
- **3-axis Magnetometer** (measures Earth's magnetic field, $B_x, B_y, B_z$)

to provide a complete estimate of an object's **specific force**, **angular rate**, and **magnetic heading**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy — "The Inner Ear of a Robot":** Just as a human's vestibular system in the inner ear tells our brain which way is up, how fast we're turning, and which way we're accelerating — an IMU provides that exact 6-axis motion awareness (the 9-axis version also includes the magnetic compass) to a robot, drone, or smartphone.

### 1.6.4 Global Positioning System (GPS)

**Formal Definition:** GPS is a **satellite-based radio-navigation system** owned by the U.S. government that provides geolocation (latitude, longitude, altitude) and time synchronization to a GPS receiver anywhere on Earth with line-of-sight to four or more satellites in the GPS constellation, using **trilateration** of one-way time-of-flight signals.

**Position Fix (trilateration core):**
$$\rho_i = c \cdot (t_{\text{received},i} - t_{\text{transmitted},i}) = \sqrt{(x-x_i)^2 + (y-y_i)^2 + (z-z_i)^2}$$

where $\rho_i$ is the pseudorange to the $i$-th satellite, $c$ is the speed of light, and $(x_i, y_i, z_i)$ are the satellite's known coordinates.

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy — "Echoes in the Sky":** Imagine shouting into a vast canyon and timing how long the echo takes to come back from multiple cliff faces. If you know the speed of sound and the cliff positions, you can triangulate your own position. GPS does the same with light-speed radio pings from satellites 20,200 km above you.

### 1.6.5 Real-Time Kinematic GPS (RTK GPS) / Real-Time Differential GPS (RTD GPS)

**Formal Definition:** **Real-Time Differential GPS (RTD GPS)** is a high-precision augmentation of standard GPS that uses a **stationary reference receiver** at a known surveyed location to compute real-time **pseudorange correction (PRC)** and **range-rate correction (RRC)** values, which are broadcast to a roving receiver to cancel out common-mode errors (ionospheric, tropospheric, ephemeris, satellite clock).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **RTK vs RTD — KTU Distinction:**
> - **RTD (Real-Time Differential):** Uses correction on the **pseudorange (code) measurement** → achieves **sub-meter to 1 m** accuracy.
> - **RTK (Real-Time Kinematic):** Uses correction on the **carrier phase measurement** → achieves **cm-level (1–2 cm)** accuracy by resolving integer ambiguities.

**Correction equation applied at the rover:**
$$\rho_{\text{corrected}} = \rho_{\text{measured}} + \text{PRC} + \text{RRC} \cdot \Delta t$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy — "Two-Way Radio Anchored Surveying":** Place one GPS receiver on a tripod over a point whose exact coordinates you already know (the **base station**). It listens to the satellites and figures out "the satellites are lying to me by 2.3 m right now" (the error). It shouts that correction over a radio link to a second receiver on your tractor/drone/robot (the **rover**). The rover adds that correction in real time, achieving cm-level accuracy. That is RTK/RTD GPS in plain words.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Optical Encoder — Operating Theory (Step-by-Step)

An incremental optical encoder comprises the following sub-systems:
1. **Light Source** — typically a GaAs infrared LED ($\lambda \approx 880$ nm).
2. **Code Disc (Code Wheel)** — a glass or metal disc with $N$ radial slots etched at the perimeter.
3. **Photodetector (Photodiode / Phototransistor)** — receives light pulses through the disc.
4. **Signal Conditioning Electronics** — Schmitt trigger, comparator, line driver.

**Operational Logic Flow:**
- LED emits constant IR light → focused onto the code disc.
- Disc rotates with the shaft → slots allow light to pass intermittently.
- Photodetector generates a sinusoidal waveform → shaped into a square-wave **TTL pulse train**.
- Two channels (A and B) are offset by 90° (in quadrature) to allow direction discrimination.

**Why quadrature?** A single channel cannot distinguish CW from CCW rotation. By phase-shifting Channel B by +90° relative to A, the leading-channel identifies direction:
- If A leads B → CW rotation.
- If B leads A → CCW rotation.

**Resolution:** Angular resolution of an incremental encoder:
$$\theta_{\text{res}} = \frac{360°}{N} = \frac{2\pi}{N} \text{ rad}$$

**Velocity Computation (KTU Standard Form):**
$$f_{\text{out}} = \frac{N \cdot \omega}{2\pi} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \omega = \frac{2\pi \cdot f_{\text{out}}}{N}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** Optical encoders are the de-facto feedback device in CNC machines, robotic arms, BLDC motor commutation, and 3D-printer motion stages. They are non-contact (no wear), high resolution (up to 25-bit / 33 million CPR), and immune to electrical noise due to digital output.

## 2.2 Tacho Generator — Operating Theory

A tachogenerator is a **DC machine** operated as a generator. Its construction is identical to a DC motor: permanent magnets (stator), armature winding (rotor), commutator, and brushes.

**Working Principle (Faraday's Law Application):**
As the shaft rotates, the armature conductors cut the stationary magnetic flux lines. By Faraday's Law of Electromagnetic Induction:
$$\text{EMF induced per conductor} = B \cdot l \cdot v$$
where $B$ = flux density, $l$ = conductor length, $v$ = tangential velocity of the conductor.

For a complete armature with $Z$ conductors, $P$ poles, and $A$ parallel paths:
$$E_b = \frac{\Phi \cdot Z \cdot N \cdot P}{60 \cdot A}$$

Since $\omega = \frac{2\pi N}{60}$:
$$E_b = \underbrace{\left(\frac{\Phi \cdot Z \cdot P}{2\pi A}\right)}_{K_e} \cdot \omega$$

The output is taken across the armature terminals via brushes.

**AC Tachogenerator (Inverted Induction Motor Concept):**
An AC tacho uses two stator windings arranged at 90°:
- One winding is excited with reference AC (acts as primary).
- The other winding develops an output voltage proportional to speed.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sign Convention:** A reversing of rotation reverses the polarity of $E_b$ → this is the key advantage of a tacho: it inherently provides **directional** velocity information in a single analog signal.

## 2.3 Hall Effect Sensor — Operating Theory

**Derivation of the Hall Effect:**

Consider an $n$-type semiconductor slab of thickness $t$ carrying a current $I$ along the $x$-axis, placed in a magnetic field $B$ along the $z$-axis.

1. Charge carriers (electrons) drift with velocity $v_d$ along $+x$.
2. Magnetic Lorentz force on each electron: $\vec{F}_B = -e(\vec{v}_d \times \vec{B})$.
3. With $\vec{v}_d = v_d \hat{x}$ and $\vec{B} = B\hat{z}$: $\vec{v}_d \times \vec{B} = -v_d B \hat{y}$.
4. Force on electron: $\vec{F}_B = -e(-v_d B \hat{y}) = +ev_dB\hat{y}$ → pushes electrons to the $+y$ face.
5. Accumulated charge creates a transverse electric field $\vec{E}_H$ that opposes further charge separation.

At steady state, electric force balances magnetic force:
$$eE_H = ev_dB \quad \Rightarrow \quad E_H = v_dB$$

The **Hall voltage** is measured across the $y$-direction:
$$V_H = E_H \cdot w = v_d B w$$
where $w$ is the width of the slab. Since current $I = nev_d \cdot w \cdot t$:
$$v_d = \frac{I}{newt} \quad \Rightarrow \quad V_H = \frac{I \cdot B}{net}$$

**Mobility (KTU extension):** With mobility $\mu_n = \frac{v_d}{E_x}$:
$$V_H = \frac{\mu_n E_x B}{... } \quad \text{(see derivation in Section 3)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** Hall sensors are ubiquitous in **BLDC motor commutation** (detecting rotor magnet position), **anti-lock braking systems (ABS)** (wheel-speed sensing), **smartphones** (flip-cover detection), **current sensing** (open-loop Hall CTs), and **proximity switches** in industrial automation.

## 2.4 Accelerometer — Operating Theory

**Working Principle (Newton's Second Law on a Suspended Proof Mass):**

A MEMS accelerometer has a proof mass $m$ suspended by four flexure beams of stiffness $k$. Under acceleration $a$, the mass deflects by displacement $x$. The differential equation of motion (KTU must derive this):
$$m\ddot{x} + b\dot{x} + kx = ma$$
At steady-state DC acceleration, the displacement is:
$$x = \frac{ma}{k}$$

This displacement is sensed by:
- **Capacitive method** (change in $C = \frac{\varepsilon_0 A}{d-x}$)
- **Piezoresistive method** (strain-gauge resistance change)
- **Piezoelectric method** (charge $q = d_{33} \cdot F$ generated by stress)

**Sensitivity (Capacitive MEMS):**
$$\frac{\Delta C}{C_0} = \frac{x}{d} = \frac{ma}{kd}$$

**Natural Frequency (Important for bandwidth):**
$$\omega_n = \sqrt{\frac{k}{m}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Trade-off:** A high natural frequency (good for high-bandwidth shock sensing) requires either stiff springs (large $k$) or a small mass. A high sensitivity (large $x$) requires the opposite — soft springs or a large mass. The sensor design is a compromise.

## 2.5 Gyroscope — Operating Theory

A MEMS gyroscope exploits the **Coriolis Effect**. Consider a proof mass $m$ vibrating along the drive axis with velocity $\vec{v}_d$. When the gyroscope's frame rotates with angular velocity $\vec{\omega}$, the Coriolis force arises:
$$\vec{F}_{\text{Cor}} = -2m(\vec{\omega} \times \vec{v}_d)$$

This force displaces the proof mass along the **sense axis** (perpendicular to both $\vec{v}_d$ and $\vec{\omega}$). The sense-axis displacement is proportional to the rotation rate, and is measured capacitively.

> [!NOTE]
> **Three Classes of Gyros (KTU-Required Awareness):**
> 1. **MEMS Vibratory Gyro** — Coriolis-based, low cost, used in phones/drones.
> 2. **Ring Laser Gyro (RLG)** — Uses Sagnac effect with two counter-rotating laser beams in a closed ring.
> 3. **Fibre Optic Gyro (FOG)** — Sagnac effect with optical fibre coil.

## 2.6 GPS / RTK GPS — Operating Theory

**Satellite Constellation:** The GPS nominal constellation consists of **24+ MEO satellites** at altitude 20,200 km, with orbital period of 12 sidereal hours (orbital period = 43,200 seconds). Spread across 6 orbital planes inclined 55° to the equator.

**Trilateration Principle:** To compute a 3D fix, a minimum of **4 satellites** is required:
- 3 satellites for (x, y, z) position.
- 1 satellite to resolve the receiver's clock bias $\delta t$.

**Pseudorange Equation (master equation):**
$$\rho_i = \sqrt{(x-x_i)^2 + (y-y_i)^2 + (z-z_i)^2} + c\cdot \delta t + \varepsilon_i$$

**Differential GPS (DGPS) Concept:**
The reference (base) station at known position $(x_0, y_0, z_0)$ computes for each satellite:
$$\text{PRC}_i = \sqrt{(x_0-x_i)^2+(y_0-y_i)^2+(z_0-z_i)^2} - \rho_{i,\text{meas}}$$

The rover applies this correction in real time:
$$\rho_{i,\text{rover,corrected}} = \rho_{i,\text{rover,meas}} - \text{PRC}_i$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sources of GPS Error (KTU Must-Know):**
> - **Ionospheric delay** — 1–5 m.
> - **Tropospheric delay** — 0.5–2.5 m.
> - **Ephemeris (orbital) errors** — ~1 m.
> - **Satellite/receiver clock error** — removed via differential.
> - **Multipath** — environment-dependent, 0–5 m.
> - **Selective Availability (SA)** — turned off in 2000 (historical relevance).

---

## 2.7 KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Topic | Formula | Description / Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Linear Velocity | $v = \frac{dx}{dt}$ | m/s |
| 2 | Angular Velocity | $\omega = \frac{d\theta}{dt}$ | rad/s |
| 3 | Optical Encoder Output Frequency | $f = \frac{N \cdot \omega}{2\pi}$ | Hz, $N$ = pulses per rev |
| 4 | Encoder Angular Resolution | $\theta_{\text{res}} = \frac{2\pi}{N}$ | rad |
| 5 | Tachogenerator Output | $E_b = K_e \cdot \omega$ | V, $K_e$ in V·s/rad |
| 6 | Tachogenerator EMF (full form) | $E_b = \frac{\Phi Z N P}{60 A}$ | V |
| 7 | Hall Voltage | $V_H = \frac{I B}{n e t}$ | V |
| 8 | Lorentz Force | $\vec{F} = q(\vec{v} \times \vec{B})$ | N |
| 9 | Coriolis Force (Gyro) | $\vec{F}_c = -2m(\vec{\omega} \times \vec{v}_d)$ | N |
| 10 | Accelerometer Steady-State Deflection | $x = \frac{m a}{k}$ | m |
| 11 | Capacitive MEMS Sensitivity | $\frac{\Delta C}{C_0} = \frac{x}{d}$ | dimensionless |
| 12 | Natural Frequency of Mass-Spring | $\omega_n = \sqrt{k/m}$ | rad/s |
| 13 | Magnetic Heading | $\psi = \operatorname{atan2}(B_y, B_x)$ | rad |
| 14 | GPS Pseudorange | $\rho_i = \sqrt{(x-x_i)^2+(y-y_i)^2+(z-z_i)^2} + c\delta t$ | m |
| 15 | DGPS Pseudorange Correction | $\text{PRC}_i = \rho_{\text{true}} - \rho_{\text{meas}}$ | m |
| 16 | GPS Satellite Orbital Period | $T = 2\pi\sqrt{\frac{r^3}{\mu}}$ | s, $\mu = GM_\oplus$ |
| 17 | Earth Gravitational Constant | $\mu = 3.986 \times 10^{14}$ m³/s² | constant |
| 18 | GPS Satellite Altitude | $h = 20{,}200$ km | (nominal) |
| 19 | Speed of Light | $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s | constant |
| 20 | Single-Axis Resolution (IMU) | $\text{DoF} = $ number of independent measurements | — |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** Always carry the **units** in the final answer. Marks are deducted in board valuation for missing units or incorrect dimensional analysis.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation: Hall Voltage with Mobility

**Problem Statement:** Derive the Hall voltage in terms of the applied current $I$, magnetic flux density $B$, and the carrier mobility $\mu$, given that the current $I$ flows along a semiconductor of length $L$ and cross-section $w \times t$.

### Step 1 — Drift Velocity
The current density in the $x$-direction is:
$$J_x = nev_d$$
where $n$ = carrier concentration, $e$ = electronic charge, $v_d$ = drift velocity.

Total current:
$$I = J_x \cdot (w \cdot t) = nev_d \cdot w \cdot t$$

Solving for $v_d$:
$$v_d = \frac{I}{newt}$$

### Step 2 — Mobility Definition
Mobility is defined as:
$$\mu = \frac{v_d}{E_x}$$
where $E_x$ is the applied electric field along $x$.

Therefore:
$$v_d = \mu E_x$$

### Step 3 — Hall Electric Field Balance
At steady state, the electric force on a charge carrier equals the magnetic force:
$$eE_H = ev_dB$$
$$E_H = v_dB$$

### Step 4 — Express Hall Voltage
The Hall voltage is the electric field multiplied by the width $w$ across which it develops:
$$V_H = E_H \cdot w = v_d B w$$

Substituting $v_d = \frac{I}{newt}$:
$$V_H = \frac{I}{newt} \cdot B w = \frac{I \cdot B}{n \cdot e \cdot t}$$

### Step 5 — Introduce Mobility
Substitute $E_x = V_x/L$ (where $V_x$ is the voltage drop along the length of the conductor) and the conductivity $\sigma = ne\mu$:
$$J_x = \sigma E_x = ne\mu E_x \quad \Rightarrow \quad v_d = \mu E_x$$

Substituting $v_d = \mu E_x$ into the Hall voltage equation:
$$V_H = \mu E_x B w = \frac{\mu B w V_x}{L}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** A higher mobility (semiconductors like InSb, GaAs, InAs have $\mu$ 1000× higher than copper) makes Hall voltage larger. This is why Hall sensors are made of high-mobility semiconductors, not metals.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Tachogenerator EMF Equation

**Problem Statement:** Derive the EMF equation $E_b = \frac{\Phi Z N P}{60 A}$ for a DC tachogenerator with $Z$ conductors, $P$ poles, $A$ parallel paths, and rotational speed $N$ (rpm).

### Step 1 — Flux Cut Per Conductor Per Revolution
Each conductor in one revolution cuts $P$ poles (alternating N and S). Flux cut per pole = $\Phi$ (Webers).
Total flux cut per revolution per conductor = $P\Phi$ Wb.

### Step 2 — Time Per Revolution
Speed $N$ in rpm → revolutions per second = $N/60$.
Time per revolution:
$$T = \frac{60}{N} \text{ seconds}$$

### Step 3 — Average EMF Per Conductor
By Faraday's Law:
$$\text{Average EMF per conductor} = \frac{P\Phi}{T} = \frac{P\Phi N}{60} \text{ V}$$

### Step 4 — Series Conductors Per Parallel Path
Total conductors = $Z$. They are distributed over $A$ parallel paths.
Conductors in series per path = $Z/A$.

### Step 5 — Total EMF
EMFs in series add; EMFs in different paths are in parallel (so total = series EMF in any one path):
$$E_b = \left(\frac{Z}{A}\right) \cdot \left(\frac{P\Phi N}{60}\right) = \frac{\Phi Z N P}{60 A}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Substitution Check:** If $N$ in rpm, $\omega = \frac{2\pi N}{60}$, then $E_b = \frac{\Phi Z P}{2\pi A} \cdot \omega$, defining the voltage constant $K_e = \frac{\Phi Z P}{2\pi A}$.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Optical Encoder Velocity Computation

**Problem Statement:** An incremental optical encoder has $N = 1024$ pulses per revolution and outputs a pulse-train frequency of $f = 25.6$ kHz. Calculate the shaft speed in (a) rad/s, (b) rpm.

### Step 1 — Establish the Frequency-Velocity Relationship
Each revolution of the disc produces $N$ pulses. So the time for one revolution is $T = N/f$ seconds.

### Step 2 — Compute the Rotational Period
$$T = \frac{1024}{25{,}600} = 0.04 \text{ s/rev}$$

### Step 3 — Convert to rad/s
One revolution = $2\pi$ rad.
$$\omega = \frac{2\pi}{T} = \frac{2\pi \times 25{,}600}{1024} = 50\pi \text{ rad/s} = 157.08 \text{ rad/s}$$

### Step 4 — Convert to rpm
$$N_{\text{rpm}} = \frac{60}{T} = 60 \times \frac{25{,}600}{1024} = 1500 \text{ rpm}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Verification:** $\omega = \frac{2\pi N}{60} = \frac{2\pi \times 1500}{60} = 50\pi$ rad/s ✓

---

## 3.4 Derivation: Accelerometer Steady-State Displacement

**Problem Statement:** A MEMS accelerometer has a proof mass $m = 1.2 \times 10^{-9}$ kg suspended by four identical flexure beams, each of stiffness $k_{\text{beam}} = 0.5$ N/m. Compute (a) the total spring constant, (b) the steady-state displacement under an input acceleration of $a = 5g$ (where $g = 9.81$ m/s²), and (c) the natural frequency.

### Step 1 — Total Spring Constant
For four parallel beams:
$$k = 4 \cdot k_{\text{beam}} = 4 \times 0.5 = 2.0 \text{ N/m}$$

### Step 2 — Steady-State Displacement
At static equilibrium, spring force = inertial force:
$$kx_0 = ma$$
$$x_0 = \frac{ma}{k} = \frac{(1.2 \times 10^{-9}) \times 5 \times 9.81}{2.0} = \frac{5.886 \times 10^{-8}}{2.0} = 2.943 \times 10^{-8} \text{ m}$$

Converting to nanometers:
$$x_0 \approx 29.43 \text{ nm}$$

### Step 3 — Natural Frequency
$$\omega_n = \sqrt{\frac{k}{m}} = \sqrt{\frac{2.0}{1.2 \times 10^{-9}}} = \sqrt{1.667 \times 10^{9}} = 40{,}824.83 \text{ rad/s}$$

Converting to Hz:
$$f_n = \frac{\omega_n}{2\pi} = \frac{40{,}824.83}{6.2832} \approx 6{,}496.6 \text{ Hz} \approx 6.5 \text{ kHz}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** Such a sensor can faithfully measure accelerations with frequency content up to ~6.5 kHz. For high-frequency shock testing (e.g., ballistics), stiffer beams and smaller masses are used to push $f_n$ to 100s of kHz.

---

## 3.5 Derivation: GPS Receiver Position from 4 Satellites

**Problem Statement:** A GPS receiver observes pseudoranges to four satellites with known positions. Set up the linearized system of equations to solve for the receiver's $(x, y, z)$ coordinates and clock bias $\delta t$.

### Step 1 — Write Pseudorange Equation for Satellite $i$
$$\rho_i = \sqrt{(x-x_i)^2 + (y-y_i)^2 + (z-z_i)^2} + c\delta t + \varepsilon_i$$
where $i = 1, 2, 3, 4$.

### Step 2 — Linearization (Taylor Expansion)
Let the receiver's approximate position be $(x_0, y_0, z_0)$. Define corrections $\Delta x, \Delta y, \Delta z, \Delta b = c\delta t$.

The geometric range from approximate position to satellite $i$:
$$r_{i,0} = \sqrt{(x_0-x_i)^2 + (y_0-y_i)^2 + (z_0-z_i)^2}$$

The first-order Taylor expansion gives:
$$\rho_i \approx r_{i,0} - \frac{(x_0-x_i)}{r_{i,0}}\Delta x - \frac{(y_0-y_i)}{r_{i,0}}\Delta y - \frac{(z_0-z_i)}{r_{i,0}}\Delta z + c\Delta t$$

### Step 3 — Direction Cosines (Line-of-Sight Unit Vector)
Define the line-of-sight unit vector components:
$$u_{xi} = -\frac{(x_0-x_i)}{r_{i,0}}, \quad u_{yi} = -\frac{(y_0-y_i)}{r_{i,0}}, \quad u_{zi} = -\frac{(z_0-z_i)}{r_{i,0}}$$

Then:
$$\rho_i - r_{i,0} = u_{xi}\Delta x + u_{yi}\Delta y + u_{zi}\Delta z - c\Delta t$$

### Step 4 — Matrix Form (4 equations, 4 unknowns)
$$\begin{bmatrix}
u_{x1} & u_{y1} & u_{z1} & -1 \\
u_{x2} & u_{y2} & u_{z2} & -1 \\
u_{x3} & u_{y3} & u_{z3} & -1 \\
u_{x4} & u_{y4} & u_{z4} & -1
\end{bmatrix}
\begin{bmatrix}
\Delta x \\ \Delta y \\ \Delta z \\ c\Delta t
\end{bmatrix}
=
\begin{bmatrix}
\rho_1 - r_{1,0} \\ \rho_2 - r_{2,0} \\ \rho_3 - r_{3,0} \\ \rho_4 - r_{4,0}
\end{bmatrix}$$

Solving the linear system gives the receiver position correction. Iteration converges to a sub-meter accuracy in standalone GPS (further improved to cm-level in RTK).

---

## 3.6 Worked Example: DGPS Pseudorange Correction

**Problem Statement:** A base GPS receiver at known position $(x_0, y_0, z_0) = (1{,}589{,}231.4, 4{,}872{,}341.7, 4{,}102{,}345.2)$ m observes a pseudorange of $\rho_0 = 22{,}543{,}210.5$ m to Satellite 7. The true geometric range is $r_0 = 22{,}543{,}208.0$ m. A rover simultaneously measures a pseudorange of $\rho_{\text{rov}} = 21{,}892{,}455.3$ m to the same satellite. Compute the corrected rover pseudorange.

### Step 1 — Compute the Pseudorange Correction (PRC)
$$\text{PRC} = r_0 - \rho_0 = 22{,}543{,}208.0 - 22{,}543{,}210.5 = -2.5 \text{ m}$$

The negative sign indicates that the measured pseudorange is 2.5 m longer than the true range (atmospheric delay).

### Step 2 — Apply Correction to Rover
$$\rho_{\text{rov,corrected}} = \rho_{\text{rov}} + \text{PRC} = 21{,}892{,}455.3 + (-2.5) = 21{,}892{,}452.8 \text{ m}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Practical Insight:** A real DGPS system broadcasts a stream of PRCs and RRCs (range-rate corrections) every 1–10 seconds. The rover applies them in real time via RTCM SC-104 protocol, achieving sub-meter to meter-level accuracy in agricultural autosteer, surveying, and drone mapping.

# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Block Diagram: Incremental Optical Encoder

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    sourceA[GaAs IR LED Light Source] --> optA[Collimating Lens]
    optA --> discA[Slotted Code Disc N PPR]
    discA -->|intermittent light| pdA[Photodetector Photodiode]
    pdA --> ampA[Amplifier]
    ampA --> cmpA[Schmitt Trigger Comparator]
    cmpA --> chA[Channel A Output]
    discA -->|phase shift 90 deg| discB[Dual Slit Pattern]
    discB --> pdB[Photodetector B]
    pdB --> ampB[Amplifier B]
    ampB --> cmpB[Schmitt Trigger B]
    cmpB --> chB[Channel B Output]
    chA --> decode[Quadrature Decoder]
    chB --> decode
    decode -->|pulse count and direction| cpuA[Motor Controller or PLC]

    cpuA --> pos[Position theta rad]
```

    motor -.->|same shaft| tg[Tachogenerator]

    fb --> readout[Analog RPM Indicator]
```

    ocA --> ecuA[ABS or ECU Counter]

    calcA --> ctrlA[Braking Force Modulator]
```

    massA --> flexA[Folded Flexure Beam x4]

```

```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

---

## PART A — 3-MARK SHORT-ANSWER QUESTIONS

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Question]**
**"Differentiate between incremental and absolute optical encoders. State one application of each."**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Incremental Encoder | Absolute Encoder |
|---|---|---|
| Output | Pulse train (no absolute position) | Unique multi-bit code per position |
| Position recovery after power-off | Needs homing | Immediate on power-up |
| Wiring | 2–3 signals (A, B, Z) | Many parallel lines (e.g., 10–12 for 1024 CPR) |
| Cost | Low | Higher |
| Application | CNC spindle feedback, 3D-printer axes | Robotic joint absolute position, telescope antenna pointing |

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model Question]**
**"Explain the working principle of a Hall effect sensor. Write the expression for Hall voltage."**

**Model Answer:**
A current $I$ is passed through a semiconductor placed in a perpendicular magnetic field $B$. Charge carriers experience Lorentz force $F = qv \times B$, drifting to one side of the conductor. The accumulated charge creates a transverse Hall voltage:
$$V_H = \frac{I \cdot B}{n \cdot e \cdot t}$$
This voltage is proportional to $B$, enabling non-contact magnetic-field sensing. Used in BLDC commutation, ABS wheel-speed sensing, and proximity switches.

---

## PART B — 14-MARK QUESTIONS (ESE Module Internal Choice)

### QUESTION A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model — Module 1 ESE]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** With the aid of a neat block diagram, explain the construction and working of an incremental optical encoder. Derive the relationship between output pulse frequency and angular velocity of the shaft.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A DC tachogenerator with the following specifications is used in a speed-control loop:
- Flux per pole, $\Phi = 0.012$ Wb
- Number of conductors, $Z = 480$
- Number of poles, $P = 4$
- Number of parallel paths, $A = 2$
- Speed, $N = 1800$ rpm
Calculate (i) the generated EMF, (ii) the voltage constant $K_e$ in V·s/rad, (iii) the EMF when the speed drops to 1200 rpm.

#### Model Solution — Part (a)
**Block Diagram:** Refer to Section 4.1 (Block Diagram: Incremental Optical Encoder).

**Construction:**
- A GaAs infrared LED + lens → produces a collimated beam.
- A code disc with $N$ radial slots, mounted on the rotating shaft.
- A photodetector (photodiode) on the opposite side of the disc.
- Two such LED-detector pairs arranged at 90° offset for quadrature (Channel A and B).
- Signal-conditioning electronics (Schmitt trigger) to shape the waveform into clean TTL pulses.

**Working:**
The LED emits a continuous IR beam. The slotted disc rotates, alternately allowing and blocking the beam. The photodetector generates a sinusoidal voltage that is shaped into a square wave. Two channels 90° out of phase allow direction sensing.

**Derivation of Frequency-Velocity Relationship:**
Each revolution of the disc produces exactly $N$ pulses. If the rotational speed is $N_s$ in revolutions per second:
$$f = N \cdot N_s \quad \text{and} \quad \omega = 2\pi N_s$$
Therefore:
$$f = \frac{N \cdot \omega}{2\pi} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \omega = \frac{2\pi f}{N}$$

#### Model Solution — Part (b)
**Given:** $\Phi = 0.012$ Wb, $Z = 480$, $P = 4$, $A = 2$, $N = 1800$ rpm.

**(i) Generated EMF:**
$$E_b = \frac{\Phi Z N P}{60 A} = \frac{0.012 \times 480 \times 1800 \times 4}{60 \times 2}$$

Calculating numerator:
$$0.012 \times 480 = 5.76$$
$$5.76 \times 1800 = 10{,}368$$
$$10{,}368 \times 4 = 41{,}472$$

Denominator:
$$60 \times 2 = 120$$

$$E_b = \frac{41{,}472}{120} = 345.6 \text{ V}$$

**(ii) Voltage Constant $K_e$:**
Convert rpm to rad/s: $\omega = \frac{2\pi N}{60} = \frac{2\pi \times 1800}{60} = 60\pi = 188.496$ rad/s.

$$K_e = \frac{E_b}{\omega} = \frac{345.6}{188.496} = 1.8336 \text{ V·s/rad}$$

**(iii) EMF at 1200 rpm:**
Linear scaling: $E_b \propto N$.
$$E_{b,\text{new}} = 345.6 \times \frac{1200}{1800} = 230.4 \text{ V}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Question A:**
> - **Part (a):** Many students forget to explain *why* two channels (A and B) are used. Always state that Channel B is 90° phase-shifted to allow **direction discrimination** (quadrature decoding). This is a guaranteed 1–2 mark item.
> - **Part (b):** Unit conversion is the killer. Forgetting to convert rpm to rad/s (or vice-versa) in $K_e$ calculation results in a **full -1 mark deduction**. Also, $A = 2$ is for a *wave-wound* DC machine; if $A = P = 4$ (lap-wound), substitute correctly.

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model — Module 1 ESE — Alternative Choice]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Derive the expression for the Hall voltage in a semiconductor. Explain with a diagram how a Hall effect sensor can be used to measure the speed of a rotating wheel.

**(b) [7 Marks]** What is an Inertial Measurement Unit (IMU)? Describe the architecture of a 9-DoF IMU. Explain how GPS works using trilateration, and state the role of a fourth satellite in obtaining a 3D fix.

#### Model Solution — Part (a)
**Derivation:** Refer to Section 3.1 (Step-by-step derivation with all 5 steps reproduced in board exam form).

**Wheel Speed Application:**
A small permanent magnet is mounted on a wheel spoke. A Hall sensor is fixed to the stationary fork/housing. As the wheel rotates, the magnet passes the Hall sensor once per revolution. The Hall voltage spikes each time, producing a square wave whose frequency $f$ relates to wheel angular speed:
$$\omega_{\text{wheel}} = 2\pi f \quad \text{and} \quad v_{\text{vehicle}} = \omega_{\text{wheel}} \cdot r_{\text{wheel}}$$

This is the principle behind e-bike speedometers, ABS wheel-speed sensors, and BLDC motor commutation.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)
**IMU Definition:** An IMU is an electronic module that measures a body's specific force (via accelerometer), angular rate (via gyroscope), and magnetic heading (via magnetometer) along three orthogonal axes, providing a 9-DoF (Degrees of Freedom) motion solution.

**9-DoF IMU Architecture:** Refer to Section 4.5 (Block Diagram: 9-DoF IMU).
- 3-axis MEMS accelerometer: ADXL345, MPU-6050 family.
- 3-axis MEMS gyroscope: integrated in MPU-6050/9250.
- 3-axis magnetometer: AK09911, HMC5883L.
- Sensor-fusion processor running Madgwick / Mahony / Extended Kalman Filter.
- Outputs: roll $\phi$, pitch $\theta$, yaw $\psi$ (Euler angles or quaternion), linear acceleration, and gravity-compensated heading.

**GPS Trilateration:**
GPS uses a constellation of $\geq 24$ satellites in MEO at altitude 20,200 km. Each satellite continuously broadcasts its position and a precisely time-stamped ranging signal. A receiver measures the time-of-flight $t_i$ for each satellite $i$ and computes the pseudorange:
$$\rho_i = c \cdot t_i = \sqrt{(x-x_i)^2 + (y-y_i)^2 + (z-z_i)^2} + c\delta t$$

**Role of 4th Satellite:**
- 3 satellites give 3 equations in 3 unknowns $(x, y, z)$, but the receiver clock has an unknown bias $\delta t$.
- The 4th satellite provides the 4th equation required to solve for $(x, y, z, \delta t)$ simultaneously.
- Result: A fully-resolved 3D position fix with nanosecond-level clock synchronization.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Question B:**
> - **Part (a):** Students often write the Hall voltage formula but skip the Lorentz force derivation. The examiner expects **at least 4 derivation steps** (drift velocity, Lorentz balance, Hall field, final expression). Skipping steps costs 3–4 marks.
> - **Part (b):** A common error is to state that "3 satellites are enough" — this is wrong. The 4th satellite is required precisely because the receiver's quartz clock is not atomic-grade. Always mention the **clock bias** explicitly.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Memory Anchors for Last-Minute Revision:**

- **Velocity Sensor Outputs:** Encoder → digital pulse train; Tacho → analog voltage (DC or AC); Hall → switching pulse.
- **Encoder Resolution Formula:** $\theta_{\text{res}} = 360°/N$ where $N$ = pulses per revolution.
- **Encoder Velocity Formula:** $\omega = 2\pi f / N$.
- **Tacho Output:** $E_b = K_e \omega$ where $K_e$ is the voltage constant.
- **Hall Voltage:** $V_H = IB / (net)$ — units: $I$ in A, $B$ in T, $t$ in m, output in V.
- **Lorentz Force Direction:** Right-hand rule, $\vec{F} = q(\vec{v} \times \vec{B})$.
- **Hall Sensor Material:** High-mobility semiconductors (InSb, GaAs, InAs), not metals.
- **Accelerometer Transduction Options:** Piezoelectric, piezoresistive, capacitive (MEMS).
- **Accelerometer Static Deflection:** $x = ma/k$ (must-know for sensor sizing problems).
- **MEMS Gyro Principle:** Coriolis force on a vibrating proof mass.
- **RLG/FOG Gyro Principle:** Sagnac effect (counter-rotating laser beams / optical fibre).
- **Compass Output:** $\psi = \operatorname{atan2}(B_y, B_x)$ — must use **atan2**, not atan, to preserve quadrant.
- **IMU Degrees of Freedom:** 6-DoF (accel + gyro), 9-DoF (accel + gyro + mag), 10-DoF (+ barometer).
- **GPS Constellation:** $\geq 24$ satellites, 20,200 km altitude, 55° inclination, 6 planes.
- **GPS Trilateration:** Minimum 4 satellites for 3D fix with clock bias.
- **Pseudorange Equation:** $\rho_i = r_i + c\delta t + \varepsilon_i$ (note the + sign for clock bias).
- **DGPS PRC:** $\text{PRC}_i = r_{i,\text{true}} - \rho_{i,\text{meas}}$ (correction sign convention).
- **RTK vs RTD:** RTK uses carrier-phase (cm-level); RTD uses code/pseudorange (meter-level).
- **Standard Constants to Memorize:** $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s, $g = 9.81$ m/s², $\mu_\oplus = 3.986 \times 10^{14}$ m³/s².
- **GPS Errors Hierarchy (largest → smallest):** Ionospheric > Tropospheric > Ephemeris > Clock > Multipath.
- **Differential GPS Error Sources Removed:** All common-mode errors (atmospheric, ephemeris, clock) — but **not multipath** (site-specific).
- **Sensor Fusion:** Kalman Filter combines IMU (high rate, drifts) with GPS (low rate, absolute) to produce a smooth, drift-free navigation solution.
- **KTU Favourite Question Patterns:** (i) Derive Hall voltage — 7 marks. (ii) EMF equation of tachogenerator — 7 marks. (iii) Working of RTK GPS with block diagram — 14 marks. (iv) Compare incremental vs absolute encoder — table-format 7 marks.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Exam Strategy Tip (KTU 2024 Scheme):** In 14-mark questions, always start with a **neat block diagram** (carries 2–3 marks independently), then proceed with the working principle, then the derivation, then a numerical example. This structure mirrors the KTU board valuation key and maximizes partial credit even if your final numerical answer is incorrect.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBRT303 - SENSORS AND ACTUATORS

# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Force Sensors — Foundational Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> A **force sensor** (or force transducer) is a device that converts an applied mechanical force, load, or weight into a measurable electrical signal (voltage, current, or resistance change) proportional to the magnitude of the input force. The SI unit of force is the **Newton (N)**, and force sensors typically operate in ranges from **millinewtons (mN)** in biomedical applications up to **meganewtons (MN)** in heavy industrial load cells.

In the KTU 2024 scheme for *Sensors and Actuators*, force sensors are studied primarily through two transduction principles:
1. **Piezoresistive / Strain-Gauge Based Force Sensing** — relies on the change of electrical resistance of a conductor or semiconductor when mechanically strained.
2. **Piezoelectric Based Force Sensing** — relies on the generation of an electric charge in certain non-centrosymmetric crystals when subjected to mechanical stress.

Both belong to the broader class of **mechanical-to-electrical transducers**.

### Conceptual Analogy — Plain-English Intuition

Imagine you are stretching a rubber band between your fingers.
- As you pull harder, the rubber band becomes **thinner and longer**.
- If a thin wire were embedded inside this rubber band, it would also become **thinner and longer**, and its electrical resistance would **increase** because electrons have a harder time flowing through a narrower path. **This is exactly how a strain-gauge force sensor works.**
- Now imagine striking a crystal of quartz with a hammer. The moment the hammer hits, the crystal momentarily produces a tiny voltage spike across its faces — like the crystal "screams electrically" in response to the impact. **This is exactly how a piezoelectric force sensor works.**

## 1.2 Strain — The Underlying Physical Quantity

> [!NOTE]
> **Strain (ε)** is defined as the *ratio of change in length to the original length* of a material under stress. It is **dimensionless** (commonly expressed in **microstrain (µε)**, where 1 µε = 10⁻⁶).

$$
\varepsilon = \frac{\Delta L}{L_0}
$$

**Stress (σ)** is the internal restoring force per unit cross-sectional area:

$$
\sigma = \frac{F}{A} \quad \text{[N/m}^2\text{ or Pascal (Pa)]}
$$

These two are linked by **Hooke's Law** for linear elastic deformation:

$$
\sigma = E \cdot \varepsilon
$$

where **E** is the **Young's Modulus** (modulus of elasticity) of the material.

## 1.3 What is a Strain Gauge?

A **strain gauge** (or strain gage) is a thin foil or semiconductor pattern whose electrical resistance changes in a known, repeatable, and linear manner when the substrate on which it is bonded is strained.

| Property | Typical Value (Foil Gauge) | Typical Value (Semiconductor Gauge) |
|----------|----------------------------|--------------------------------------|
| Gauge Factor (Gf) | **2.0 – 2.2** | **±100 to ±200** |
| Resistance (R) | **120 Ω, 350 Ω, 1000 Ω** | **120 Ω – 1000 Ω** |
| Strain Range | **±0.1 % to ±3 %** | **±0.3 %** |
| Temperature Sensitivity | Low | High (requires compensation) |

## 1.4 Piezoelectric Effect — The Direct Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **direct piezoelectric effect** (discovered by Jacques and Pierre Curie in 1880) is the ability of certain non-centrosymmetric crystals (e.g., **Quartz, PZT — Lead Zirconate Titanate, PVDF polymer**) to generate an electric charge **Q** on their surfaces when subjected to mechanical stress.

The constitutive equation (simplified for the **d₃₃ mode** — force applied along the polarization axis):

$$
Q = d_{33} \cdot F
$$

where **d₃₃** is the **piezoelectric charge coefficient** (units: **Coulomb/Newton = pC/N**).

For typical PZT material: **d₃₃ ≈ 300 – 600 pC/N**.
For Quartz: **d₁₁ ≈ 2.3 pC/N**.

## 1.5 Torque Sensors — Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Torque (τ)** is the rotational equivalent of force, defined as **τ = F × r** (force times moment arm). The SI unit is **N·m**. A **torque sensor** measures the twisting moment applied to a rotating or stationary shaft without significantly impeding the rotation.

A torque sensor must respond to **shear strain** in the shaft (not axial strain), since twisting produces a characteristic **45° principal stress pattern** on the shaft surface.

> [!TIP]
> **Common industrial torque ranges**: Robotic joints (1–100 N·m), automotive drivetrains (100–2000 N·m), wind turbine shafts (10⁴–10⁶ N·m).
