# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Working Principle of a Resistive Strain Gauge

A metallic foil strain gauge consists of a thin conductive grid bonded to a flexible backing (usually polyimide). When the host structure deforms:

1. The grid wires stretch (or compress) along the sensitive axis.
2. Length increases → wire gets longer.
3. Cross-sectional area decreases (Poisson effect).
4. Resistivity changes slightly (piezoresistive contribution).

The governing change in resistance:

$$
\frac{\Delta R}{R} = G_f \cdot \varepsilon
$$

where **Gf** is the **Gauge Factor** (a dimensionless constant).

### Derivation of Gauge Factor from First Principles

Starting from the basic resistance formula for a uniform conductor:

$$
R = \rho \cdot \frac{L}{A}
$$

Taking the logarithmic differential:

$$
\frac{dR}{R} = \frac{d\rho}{\rho} + \frac{dL}{L} - \frac{dA}{A}
$$

Substituting strain definitions and using Poisson's ratio (ν):

$$
\frac{dL}{L} = \varepsilon \quad ; \quad \frac{dA}{A} = -2\nu \varepsilon \quad ; \quad \frac{d\rho}{\rho} = C \cdot \varepsilon
$$

(where C is the piezoresistive constant of the material)

Therefore:

$$
\frac{dR}{R} = \varepsilon (1 + 2\nu + C) = G_f \cdot \varepsilon
$$

Hence:

$$
\boxed{\,G_f = 1 + 2\nu + C\,}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> For constantan (a Cu-Ni alloy used in foil gauges), the piezoresistive term **C ≈ 0**, so Gf ≈ 1 + 2(0.33) ≈ **2.0**. For silicon semiconductors, the **piezoresistive term dominates** (C ≈ 100–200), giving very high gauge factors.

## 2.2 Wheatstone Bridge — The Signal Conditioning Core

A single strain gauge produces a very small resistance change (e.g., for 1000 µε at Gf = 2, ΔR/R = 0.002 = 0.2%). The **Wheatstone bridge** converts this tiny resistance change into a measurable voltage.

### Quarter, Half, and Full Bridge Configurations

| Configuration | Active Gauges | Output Voltage (for small ΔR/R) | Sensitivity Factor |
|--------------|---------------|----------------------------------|--------------------|
| Quarter Bridge | 1 | V_out = (V_ex · ΔR) / (4R) | **1×** |
| Half Bridge | 2 (one in tension, one in compression) | V_out = (V_ex · ΔR) / (2R) | **2×** |
| Full Bridge | 4 (two tension, two compression) | V_out = V_ex · (ΔR/R) | **4×** |

The full-bridge output:

$$
V_{out} = V_{ex} \left( \frac{R_1}{R_1+R_2} - \frac{R_4}{R_3+R_4} \right)
$$

For a balanced bridge (all R equal) under strain, with ΔR₁ = +ΔR, ΔR₂ = −ΔR, ΔR₃ = +ΔR, ΔR₄ = −ΔR:

$$
V_{out} = V_{ex} \cdot \frac{\Delta R}{R}
$$

## 2.3 Load Cells — Force Sensors Using Strain Gauges

A **load cell** is a force sensor where one or more strain gauges are bonded to a precisely machined elastic element (spring member). The most common geometries are:

1. **Cantilever Beam Load Cell** — strain gauge bonded to the top surface (tension) and bottom surface (compression). Used for **small to medium forces (1 N – 10 kN)**.
2. **S-type / S-beam Load Cell** — shaped like the letter 'S'; measures tension and compression. Used for **medium to high forces (100 N – 50 kN)**.
3. **Compression / Column Load Cell** — cylindrical element with multiple gauges arranged axially. Used for **very high forces (up to 1000 kN)**.
4. **Shear Beam Load Cell** — strain gauges measure shear stress; suitable for harsh environments.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Design Equation for a Cantilever Beam Force Sensor:**
> For a cantilever of length **L**, width **b**, and thickness **h**, with force **F** applied at the free end:
> $$\varepsilon_{max} = \frac{6 \cdot F \cdot L}{E \cdot b \cdot h^2}$$
> This equation is essential for KTU numerical problems.

## 2.4 Piezoelectric Force Sensor — Working Principle

When a force **F** is applied along the polarization axis (3-direction) of a piezoelectric crystal:

1. The crystal lattice deforms, shifting the centers of positive and negative charges.
2. This produces bound surface charge **Q** on electrodes plated on the crystal faces.
3. Charge is collected and converted to voltage via a **charge amplifier**.

The constitutive equations (IEEE Standard on Piezoelectricity):

$$
D_i = d_{ijk} \cdot \sigma_{jk} + \varepsilon^T_{ij} \cdot E_j
$$

In simplified scalar form for the **d₃₃ mode**:

$$
Q = d_{33} \cdot F
$$

The open-circuit voltage across the crystal of capacitance **C_p**:

$$
V_{oc} = \frac{Q}{C_p} = \frac{d_{33} \cdot F}{C_p} = \frac{d_{33} \cdot F \cdot h}{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}
$$

where **h** = crystal thickness, **A** = electrode area.

## 2.5 Equivalent Circuit of a Piezoelectric Sensor

The piezoelectric sensor can be modelled as:

> **A voltage source V = Q/C_p in series with the source capacitance C_p**, OR equivalently, **a current source i = dQ/dt in parallel with C_p**, with the whole combination shunted by the insulation/leakage resistance **R_a** (very high, typically 10¹⁰ Ω or more).

This is followed by a **charge amplifier** (op-amp with feedback capacitor **C_f** and feedback resistor **R_f**):

$$
V_{out} = -\frac{Q}{C_f} = -\frac{d_{33} \cdot F}{C_f}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Common Mistake:** Students often confuse **charge-mode** vs **voltage-mode** amplifiers. For static (DC) force measurement, piezoelectric sensors are unsuitable because the charge leaks away — they excel at **dynamic force measurement only**.

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning | Units |
|---|---------|---------|-------|
| 1 | $\varepsilon = \Delta L / L$ | Strain | dimensionless (µε) |
| 2 | $\sigma = E \cdot \varepsilon$ | Hooke's Law | Pa |
| 3 | $\Delta R / R = G_f \cdot \varepsilon$ | Gauge Equation | dimensionless |
| 4 | $G_f = 1 + 2\nu + C$ | Gauge Factor Derivation | dimensionless |
| 5 | $V_{out} = V_{ex} \cdot (\Delta R / R)$ | Full Bridge Output | V |
| 6 | $F = \sigma \cdot A$ | Force from Stress | N |
| 7 | $Q = d_{33} \cdot F$ | Piezoelectric Charge | C |
| 8 | $V_{oc} = Q / C_p$ | Open-circuit Voltage | V |
| 9 | $V_{out} = -Q / C_f$ | Charge Amplifier Output | V |
| 10 | $\tau = F \times r$ | Torque Definition | N·m |
| 11 | $\varepsilon_{45°} = \tau / (G \cdot r^3 \cdot 2\pi)$ | Shear Strain in Shaft | dimensionless |
| 12 | $G = E / [2(1+\nu)]$ | Shear Modulus | Pa |

### Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Strain-gauge load cells** are the industry standard for weighing scales, truck weighbridges, and process control.
- **Piezoelectric force sensors** are indispensable for **impact testing, engine cylinder pressure monitoring, dynamic force measurement in machining, and tactile sensors in robotics**.
- **Torque sensors** are critical in **automotive engine management (crankshaft torque), wind turbine monitoring, robotic joint control, and bolt-tightening systems**.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Force from Strain in a Cantilever Beam

**Problem Setup:** A cantilever beam of rectangular cross-section (width **b**, thickness **h**, length **L**, Young's modulus **E**) has a force **F** applied vertically at its free end. The beam is fixed at one end. A strain gauge is bonded to the top surface at distance **L** from the load application point (or at distance **x** from the fixed end).

**Goal:** Derive the expression for strain **ε** on the top surface as a function of applied force **F**.

### Step 1 — Bending Moment at the Strain Gauge Location

The bending moment at a section distance **x** from the fixed end:

$$
M(x) = F \cdot (L - x)
$$

### Step 2 — Bending Stress at the Outer Fiber

Using the flexure formula $\sigma = M y / I$, where **y = h/2** (distance from neutral axis to outer fiber) and **I = bh³/12** (second moment of area for rectangle):

$$
\sigma_{max} = \frac{M(x) \cdot (h/2)}{bh^3/12} = \frac{6 \cdot M(x)}{b \cdot h^2}
$$

### Step 3 — Substituting the Bending Moment

$$
\sigma_{max} = \frac{6 \cdot F \cdot (L - x)}{b \cdot h^2}
$$

### Step 4 — Applying Hooke's Law

$$
\varepsilon = \frac{\sigma_{max}}{E} = \frac{6 \cdot F \cdot (L - x)}{E \cdot b \cdot h^2}
$$

### Step 5 — Special Case: Gauge at the Fixed End (x = 0)

At the fixed end (where strain is maximum):

$$
\boxed{\,\varepsilon_{max} = \frac{6 \cdot F \cdot L}{E \cdot b \cdot h^2}\,}
$$

### Step 6 — Solving for Force from Measured Strain

Rearranging:

$$
F = \frac{\varepsilon \cdot E \cdot b \cdot h^2}{6 \cdot L}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Numerical Example:** If E = 200 GPa, b = 0.02 m, h = 0.005 m, L = 0.1 m, and measured strain ε = 1000 µε = 10⁻³, then F = (10⁻³ × 200×10⁹ × 0.02 × 25×10⁻⁶) / (6 × 0.1) = **166.67 N**. Verify this is below the elastic limit!

## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation: Piezoelectric Charge Amplifier Output

**Setup:** A piezoelectric force sensor with charge coefficient **d₃₃** is subjected to a sinusoidal force **F(t) = F₀ sin(ωt)**. The sensor is connected to an inverting op-amp charge amplifier with feedback capacitance **C_f** and feedback resistance **R_f**.

### Step 1 — Generated Charge

$$
Q(t) = d_{33} \cdot F(t) = d_{33} \cdot F_0 \sin(\omega t)
$$

### Step 2 — Current Through the Feedback Network

$$
i(t) = \frac{dQ}{dt} = d_{33} \cdot F_0 \cdot \omega \cos(\omega t)
$$

### Step 3 — Op-Amp Virtual Short Property

At the inverting input, the voltage is held at virtual ground (since non-inverting input is grounded). Therefore, the **entire piezoelectric current** must flow through the feedback impedance.

### Step 4 — Output Voltage in Time Domain

Integrating the current across **C_f**:

$$
V_{out}(t) = -\frac{1}{C_f} \int i(t) \, dt = -\frac{d_{33} \cdot F_0 \sin(\omega t)}{C_f}
$$

### Step 5 — Steady-State Frequency Response

For the complete feedback network (R_f in parallel with C_f):

$$
V_{out}(t) = -\frac{d_{33} \cdot F_0 \sin(\omega t)}{C_f} \cdot \frac{1}{\sqrt{1 + (1/(\omega R_f C_f))^2}}
$$

### Step 6 — Low-Frequency Cutoff

The −3 dB low-frequency cutoff:

$$
f_L = \frac{1}{2\pi R_f C_f}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> To measure static forces, R_f C_f must be infinite (impossible in practice), confirming that **piezoelectric sensors cannot measure static forces** — only dynamic forces above f_L.

## 3.3 Exhaustive Derivation: Torque-Induced Shear Strain on a Shaft

**Setup:** A solid circular shaft of radius **R**, length **L**, and shear modulus **G** is subjected to torque **T** at its free end.

### Step 1 — Torsional Shear Stress at the Surface

From torsion theory:

$$
\tau_{max} = \frac{T \cdot R}{J}
$$

where **J = π R⁴ / 2** is the polar moment of inertia for a solid shaft.

Therefore:

$$
\tau_{max} = \frac{2 T}{\pi R^3}
$$

### Step 2 — Principal Stresses at 45° to Shaft Axis

Pure shear is equivalent to equal-magnitude tension and compression stresses acting at **±45°** to the shaft axis. The principal stresses are:

$$
\sigma_1 = +\tau_{max} \quad ; \quad \sigma_2 = -\tau_{max}
$$

### Step 3 — Principal Strains on the Surface

$$
\varepsilon_{45°} = \frac{\sigma_1 - \nu \sigma_2}{E} = \frac{\tau_{max}(1+\nu)}{E}
$$

### Step 4 — Substituting τ_max

$$
\varepsilon_{45°} = \frac{2 T (1+\nu)}{\pi R^3 E}
$$

Using the relationship **G = E / [2(1+ν)]**, i.e., **(1+ν)/E = 1/(2G)**:

$$
\boxed{\,\varepsilon_{45°} = \frac{T}{\pi R^3 G}\,}
$$

### Step 5 — Twist Angle for Reference

The angle of twist over length L:

$$
\theta = \frac{T \cdot L}{J \cdot G} = \frac{2 T L}{\pi R^4 G}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Numerical Example:** A steel shaft of R = 0.02 m, G = 80 GPa, with torque T = 500 N·m:
> ε₄₅° = 500 / (π × (0.02)³ × 80×10⁹) = 500 / (π × 8×10⁻⁶ × 80×10⁹) = 500 / (2.01×10⁶) = **248.7 µε**.
> A strain gauge mounted at 45° to the shaft axis will detect this strain.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Force and Torque Sensors

| Parameter | Strain-Gauge Force Sensor | Piezoelectric Force Sensor | Strain-Gauge Torque Sensor |
|-----------|--------------------------|----------------------------|----------------------------|
| **Physical Principle** | Resistance change | Charge generation | Resistance change |
| **Output Signal** | ΔR (requires bridge) | Charge Q or Voltage V | ΔR (requires bridge) |
| **Static Force Measurement** | **Yes (excellent)** | **No** (only dynamic) | **Yes (excellent)** |
| **Dynamic Force Measurement** | Good (limited by bridge BW) | **Excellent (very high BW)** | Good |
| **Sensitivity** | Low (ΔR/R ~ 10⁻³) | Very High | Low (ΔR/R ~ 10⁻³) |
| **Linearity** | Excellent | Good (with charge amp) | Excellent |
| **Hysteresis** | < 0.05 % | < 1 % | < 0.05 % |
| **Temperature Drift** | Moderate (compensated) | Low | Moderate |
| **Cost** | Low – Medium | Medium – High | Medium |
| **Typical Application** | Weigh scales, load cells | Impact hammers, pressure sensors | Shaft torque, engine test rigs |

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Strain Gauge Bonded on a Cantilever Beam — Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Applied Force F] --> B[Cantilever Beam]
    B --> C[Top Surface Tension +epsilon]
    B --> D[Bottom Surface Compression -epsilon]
    C --> E[Strain Gauge 1 bonded - Active]
    D --> F[Strain Gauge 2 bonded - Active]
    E --> G[Wheatstone Bridge Arm R1]
    F --> H[Wheatstone Bridge Arm R2]
    G --> I[Excitation Voltage Vex]
    H --> I
    I --> J[Differential Output Vout]
    J --> K[Instrumentation Amplifier]
    K --> L[ADC - Microcontroller]
    L --> M[Digital Force Display Newton]
```

## 4.2 Wheatstone Bridge Configurations

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Wheatstone Bridge Configurations] --> B[Quarter Bridge - 1 Active Gauge]
    A --> C[Half Bridge - 2 Active Gauges]
    A --> D[Full Bridge - 4 Active Gauges]
    B --> B1[Output: Vex times deltaR divided by 4R]
    C --> C1[Output: Vex times deltaR divided by 2R]
    D --> D1[Output: Vex times deltaR divided by R]
    D1 --> E[Best Sensitivity, Auto Temperature Compensation]
```

## 4.3 Piezoelectric Force Sensor Signal Conditioning Chain

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Mechanical Force F] --> B[Piezoelectric Crystal]
    B --> C[Electrodes collect Charge Q]
    C --> D[Piezoelectric Capacitance Cp]
    D --> E[Charge Amplifier - Op-Amp]
    E --> F[Feedback Capacitor Cf]
    E --> G[Feedback Resistor Rf]
    F --> H[DC Blocking High Pass Filter]
    H --> I[ADC and Microcontroller]
    I --> J[Digital Force Display or Recorder]
```

## 4.4 Torque Sensor Architecture (Strain-Gauge Type)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Rotating Shaft with Applied Torque T] --> B[Twist Angle theta]
    B --> C[Shear Strain at 45 degrees]
    C --> D[Strain Gauge SG1 at plus 45 deg]
    C --> E[Strain Gauge SG2 at minus 45 deg]
    D --> F[Full Bridge Wheatstone]
    E --> F
    F --> G[Slip Ring or Wireless Telemetry]
    G --> H[Signal Amplifier]
    H --> I[Microcontroller]
    I --> J[Torque Display Newton metre]
```

## 4.5 Classification of Torque Sensors — Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Torque Sensors] --> B[Strain Gauge Based - Contact Type]
    A --> C[Magnetoelastic - Non Contact]
    A --> D[Optical - Non Contact]
    A --> E[Capacitive - Non Contact]
    B --> B1[High accuracy, requires slip rings or telemetry]
    C --> C1[Based on Villari effect, no slip rings needed]
    D --> D1[Uses polarised light, very high precision]
    E --> E1[Uses capacitive plates, sensitive to wear]
```

## 4.6 Force Sensor End-to-End System Block

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Physical Force Input] --> B[Sensing Element - Strain Gauge or Piezo]
    B --> C[Transduction - Mechanical to Electrical]
    C --> D[Signal Conditioning - Amplifier and Filter]
    D --> E[ADC Conversion]
    E --> F[Microcontroller DSP]
    F --> G[Display HMI]
    F --> H[Output - 4-20 mA or 0-10 V or Digital]
    F --> I[Feedback Control Loop Actuator]
```

> [!TIP]
> For KTU 2024 board examinations, **block diagrams of sensor signal chains** are frequently asked for **7-mark questions**. Memorise the chain: **Physical Quantity → Sensing Element → Transducer → Signal Conditioning → ADC → Microcontroller → Output**.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define the term "Gauge Factor" of a strain gauge. What is its typical value for a metallic foil strain gauge and for a semiconductor strain gauge?**

**Model Answer:**
The gauge factor (Gf) of a strain gauge is defined as the ratio of fractional change in electrical resistance (ΔR/R) to the fractional change in length, i.e., the mechanical strain (ε = ΔL/L). Mathematically,

$$
G_f = \frac{\Delta R / R}{\varepsilon}
$$

It is a dimensionless quantity that indicates the sensitivity of the strain gauge. **For metallic foil gauges (e.g., constantan), Gf ≈ 2.0 to 2.2**, while **for semiconductor (silicon) gauges, Gf ≈ ±100 to ±200** due to the dominant piezoresistive effect.

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**Explain why piezoelectric sensors cannot measure static forces.**

**Model Answer:**
Piezoelectric sensors generate charge **Q = d₃₃ · F** only when force is applied or varying. When a static force is applied, the generated charge begins to leak away through the finite insulation resistance of the sensor, the connecting cable, and the input impedance of the amplifier. Since no further charge is being generated, the output voltage decays exponentially with a time constant **τ = R_a · C_p**, where R_a is the insulation resistance and C_p is the sensor capacitance. Therefore, piezoelectric sensors are inherently **AC-coupled** and suitable only for **dynamic force measurement**, with a low-frequency cutoff determined by the charge amplifier circuit: **f_L = 1 / (2π R_f C_f)**.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

### Question A — Option 1 (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**(a)** Derive the expression for gauge factor of a strain gauge starting from the basic resistance formula. Mention the typical values for metallic and semiconductor gauges. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** With a neat block diagram, explain the working of a strain gauge based load cell. A cantilever beam type load cell has the following specifications: length L = 10 cm, width b = 2 cm, thickness h = 5 mm, Young's modulus E = 200 GPa. Four identical strain gauges (each of resistance 350 Ω and gauge factor 2.1) are bonded — two on top, two on bottom — and connected in a full Wheatstone bridge with an excitation voltage of 5 V. If the measured output voltage is 1.25 mV, calculate the applied force. **(7 Marks)**

### Model Solution for Question A

**Part (a) — Derivation of Gauge Factor:**

Starting from $R = \rho \frac{L}{A}$, take logarithmic differential:

$$
\frac{dR}{R} = \frac{d\rho}{\rho} + \frac{dL}{L} - \frac{dA}{A}
$$

Substituting strain and Poisson's ratio (with A = L²):

$$
\frac{dL}{L} = \varepsilon, \quad \frac{dA}{A} = -2\nu \varepsilon, \quad \frac{d\rho}{\rho} = C \varepsilon
$$

Therefore:

$$
\frac{dR}{R} = (1 + 2\nu + C) \varepsilon
$$

Hence:

$$
\boxed{\,G_f = 1 + 2\nu + C\,}
$$

For **metallic foil (constantan)**: C ≈ 0, ν ≈ 0.33 → **Gf ≈ 2.0**.
For **semiconductor (silicon)**: C dominates → **Gf ≈ ±100 to ±200**.

**Part (b) — Load Cell Working + Numerical:**

The strain gauge load cell consists of an elastic element (spring member) to which strain gauges are bonded. When force is applied, the elastic element deforms; the strain gauges change resistance; this change is converted into a measurable voltage by the Wheatstone bridge, amplified, and displayed as a force value.

**Numerical Solution:**

Strain at the fixed end of the cantilever:

$$
\varepsilon = \frac{6 F L}{E b h^2}
$$

For a full bridge with ΔR₁ = +ΔR, ΔR₂ = −ΔR, ΔR₃ = +ΔR, ΔR₄ = −ΔR:

$$
V_{out} = V_{ex} \cdot \frac{\Delta R}{R} = V_{ex} \cdot G_f \cdot \varepsilon
$$

Substituting ε:

$$
V_{out} = V_{ex} \cdot G_f \cdot \frac{6 F L}{E b h^2}
$$

Solving for F:

$$
F = \frac{V_{out} \cdot E \cdot b \cdot h^2}{V_{ex} \cdot G_f \cdot 6 L}
$$

Substituting values:
- V_out = 1.25 × 10⁻³ V
- E = 200 × 10⁹ Pa
- b = 0.02 m
- h = 0.005 m → h² = 25 × 10⁻⁶ m²
- V_ex = 5 V
- G_f = 2.1
- L = 0.1 m

Numerator: 1.25×10⁻³ × 200×10⁹ × 0.02 × 25×10⁻⁶ = 1.25×10⁻³ × 10⁵ = **125 N·m²**

Denominator: 5 × 2.1 × 6 × 0.1 = 5 × 2.1 × 0.6 = **6.3 V**

Therefore:

$$
F = \frac{125}{6.3} = \boxed{19.84 \text{ N}}
$$

---

### Question B — Option 2 (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**(a)** Explain the direct piezoelectric effect with relevant equations. Draw the equivalent circuit of a piezoelectric sensor and derive the expression for the output voltage of a charge amplifier. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A piezoelectric force sensor has a charge sensitivity of d₃₃ = 400 pC/N and capacitance C_p = 1 nF. It is connected to a charge amplifier with feedback capacitor C_f = 100 pF. A sinusoidal force of amplitude F₀ = 10 N at frequency 100 Hz is applied. Determine:
(i) the peak charge generated,
(ii) the peak output voltage of the charge amplifier,
(iii) the minimum feedback resistance R_f required to keep the low-frequency cutoff at or below 5 Hz. **(7 Marks)**

### Model Solution for Question B

**Part (a) — Theory:**

The direct piezoelectric effect is the generation of electric charge in certain non-centrosymmetric crystals when subjected to mechanical stress. The constitutive equations are:

$$
D_i = d_{ijk} \sigma_{jk} + \varepsilon^T_{ij} E_j
$$

For the d₃₃ mode (force along polarization):

$$
Q = d_{33} \cdot F \quad ; \quad V_{oc} = \frac{Q}{C_p}
$$

Equivalent circuit: A current source i = dQ/dt in parallel with C_p, shunted by insulation resistance R_a, followed by a charge amplifier.

The charge amplifier output:

$$
\boxed{\,V_{out} = -\frac{Q}{C_f} = -\frac{d_{33} \cdot F}{C_f}\,}
$$

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution:**

**(i) Peak charge generated:**

$$
Q_0 = d_{33} \cdot F_0 = 400 \times 10^{-12} \text{ C/N} \times 10 \text{ N} = 4 \times 10^{-9} \text{ C} = \boxed{4 \text{ nC}}
$$

**(ii) Peak output voltage:**

$$
V_{out,peak} = \frac{Q_0}{C_f} = \frac{4 \times 10^{-9}}{100 \times 10^{-12}} = \boxed{40 \text{ V}}
$$

**(iii) Minimum feedback resistance:**

$$
f_L = \frac{1}{2\pi R_f C_f} \leq 5 \text{ Hz}
$$

$$
R_f \geq \frac{1}{2\pi \cdot 5 \cdot 100 \times 10^{-12}} = \frac{1}{3.14 \times 10^{-9}} \approx \boxed{3.18 \times 10^{8} \, \Omega \approx 318 \, M\Omega}
$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting units:** Many students write "Gf = 2" without units (correct — dimensionless), but write "ε = 1000" instead of "ε = 1000 µε". Always show **explicit units**.
> 2. **Bridge configuration confusion:** A **quarter bridge** uses ONE active gauge and three fixed resistors; a **full bridge** uses four active gauges and gives 4× the sensitivity. Mixing them up costs 2–3 marks.
> 3. **Piezoelectric charge amplifier sign:** The inverting amplifier produces a **negative** output: V_out = −Q/Cf. Some students drop the negative sign.
> 4. **Strain gauge on 45° for torque:** Strain gauges for torque measurement MUST be bonded at **exactly 45°** to the shaft axis (along the principal stress directions). Bonding them axially or circumferentially will yield zero or very low sensitivity.
> 5. **Static vs Dynamic force:** Never claim that a piezoelectric sensor measures static forces — this is an automatic 2-mark deduction in KTU valuation.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Quick-Reference High-Density Summary — Memorise Before Exam**

### Strain Gauge Force Sensors
- [x] **Strain** ε = ΔL/L (dimensionless, often in µε)
- [x] **Gauge Factor** Gf = (ΔR/R) / ε ≈ **2.0 (metal)**, **±100 to 200 (semiconductor)**
- [x] **Wheatstone Bridge:** Quarter (1×), Half (2×), Full (4× sensitivity)
- [x] **Load cell types:** Cantilever, S-type, Column, Shear beam
- [x] **Cantilever max strain:** ε = 6FL/(Ebh²)
- [x] **Hooke's Law:** σ = Eε
- [x] **Poisson's ratio** ν relates lateral to axial strain

### Piezoelectric Force Sensors
- [x] **Direct piezoelectric effect:** Q = d₃₃·F
- [x] **Materials:** Quartz (d₁₁ ≈ 2.3 pC/N), PZT (d₃₃ ≈ 300–600 pC/N), PVDF (polymer)
- [x] **Equivalent circuit:** Current source ∥ Cp ∥ Ra
- [x] **Charge amplifier:** V_out = −Q/Cf
- [x] **Cannot measure static forces** — only dynamic
- [x] **Low-frequency cutoff:** f_L = 1/(2πRfCf)
- [x] **Open-circuit voltage:** V_oc = Q/Cp = d₃₃F·h/(ε₀εᵣA)

### Torque Sensors
- [x] **Torque** τ = F × r (N·m)
- [x] **Shear modulus** G = E/[2(1+ν)]
- [x] **Surface shear stress** τ_max = TR/J = 2T/(πR³)
- [x] **45° principal strain** ε₄₅° = T/(πR³G)
- [x] **Strain gauges mounted at ±45°** on shaft surface
- [x] **Signal transmission:** Slip rings, rotary transformers, or wireless telemetry
- [x] **Non-contact alternatives:** Magnetoelastic (Villari effect), optical (polarisation), capacitive

### Cross-Cutting KTU Points
- [x] Always convert ε from µε to dimensionless before substitution (1 µε = 10⁻⁶).
- [x] For numericals, verify the operating strain is below the gauge's rated strain limit.
- [x] Full-bridge configurations automatically compensate for temperature-induced resistance changes (all four gauges see the same temperature).
- [x] For KTU 14-mark questions, always draw a **neat labelled diagram** (5–10 % of marks are awarded for diagrams).
- [x] Mention **at least one real-world application** for each sensor type to gain "extra" appreciation marks.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Block Schematic Representations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBRT303 - SENSORS AND ACTUATORS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Block Schematic Representations

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Block Schematic Representation (Block Diagram):**
> A **block schematic representation** is a simplified, high-level graphical model used in instrumentation, control, and sensor-actuator engineering to depict the functional flow of signals/energy through a system. Each functional stage is represented as a **rectangular block** with an **input arrow**, an **output arrow**, and a **transfer function (gain)** labelled inside. The interconnections (lines/arrows) represent the direction of signal flow.

According to the **KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus (PCBRT303, Module 1)**, block schematic representations form the **foundation for understanding sensor signal-conditioning chains and actuator drive chains**. They allow a complex physical system (electromechanical, electronic, or mechatronic) to be abstracted into identifiable, cascadable functional units.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: A Restaurant Order Pipeline 🍽️**
> Imagine a restaurant kitchen. A customer places an order (**Input**) → a waiter notes it down (**Sensor**) → passes the slip to the chef (**Signal Conditioning**) → the chef prepares the dish (**Processing/Actuator**) → the dish is plated and served (**Output**). A **block diagram** is simply a flowchart of this pipeline. Each rectangle = one station; each arrow = "passing the work forward." A sensor-actuator system works the same way.

### 1.3 Why Use Block Diagrams?

Block schematics are used because they:

1. **Abstract complexity** — hide internal physics, expose function.
2. **Enable modular analysis** — each block can be designed/tested independently.
3. **Quantify signal flow** — each block has a mathematical **transfer function** $G(s)$.
4. **Predict system behaviour** — overall response is obtained by combining block gains algebraically.
5. **Standardize documentation** — universally accepted in IEEE, ISO, and KTU board examinations.

### 1.4 Standard Terminology in KTU Board Context

| Term | Meaning (KTU notation) |
|---|---|
| **Block** | Rectangle carrying a transfer function |
| **Summing Junction** | Circle ($\sum$) that adds/subtracts signals |
| **Take-off Point** | Branch point where one signal feeds multiple paths |
| **Forward Path** | Path from input to output (no feedback) |
| **Feedback Path** | Path returning output (or part of it) to summing junction |
| **Transfer Function $G(s)$** | Ratio of output to input: $G(s)=\dfrac{Y(s)}{X(s)}$ |
| **Open Loop** | System without feedback |
| **Closed Loop** | System with feedback (sensors in the loop) |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Anatomy of a Block

Every block in a sensor-actuator schematic has **three** essential elements:

1. **Input Signal** $X(s)$ — enters from the left.
2. **Transfer Function** $G(s)$ — the gain/impedance embedded inside the block.
3. **Output Signal** $Y(s)$ — exits from the right, where $Y(s) = G(s)\cdot X(s)$.

```
        X(s)  ─────► ┌───────────┐ ─────►  Y(s) = G(s)·X(s)
                    │    G(s)    │
                    └───────────┘
```

> [!NOTE]
> In a **sensor chain**, the input $X(s)$ is a *physical quantity* (temperature, pressure, displacement). In an **actuator chain**, the input is an *electrical control signal* and the output is *mechanical work*.

### 2.2 Building Blocks of a Sensor-Actuator System

A generic **sensor system** (per KTU Module 1 syllabus) consists of the following cascaded blocks:

1. **Primary Sensing Element** — converts physical quantity $\rightarrow$ electrical/secondary signal (e.g., thermocouple, strain gauge, LVDT).
2. **Variable Conversion Element** — modifies the signal (e.g., resistance-to-voltage).
3. **Variable Manipulation Element** — amplifies, filters, linearises.
4. **Signal Conditioning Stage** — noise filtering, A/D conversion.
5. **Data Transmission / Output Stage** — display, recording, or feedback.

A generic **actuator system** follows the reverse conceptual order:

1. **Input / Command Stage** — controller output.
2. **Signal Conditioning (Power Amplifier)** — boosts low-power signal.
3. **Conversion Element** — electrical $\rightarrow$ mechanical/thermal/fluidic.
4. **Actuating / Output Element** — performs physical work.

### 2.3 Mathematical Modelling of a Block

A block is fully described by its **transfer function** $G(s)$, the ratio of the Laplace-transformed output to the Laplace-transformed input under **zero initial conditions**:

$$G(s) = \frac{Y(s)}{X(s)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **KTU 2024 Scheme** emphasises that students must be able to (a) draw the block diagram of a given physical sensor/actuator system and (b) compute the *overall transfer function* by combining blocks.

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> Memorise the following table — these combinations are **frequently repeated** in KTU board examination problems.

| # | Block Combination | Resulting Transfer Function | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Cascade (Series)** of $G_1, G_2, G_3$ | $G(s) = G_1(s)\cdot G_2(s)\cdot G_3(s)$ | Order does not matter (associative) |
| 2 | **Parallel** (same input, summed output) | $G(s) = G_1(s) + G_2(s)$ | Additive |
| 3 | **Negative Feedback Closed Loop** with $G$ in forward, $H$ in feedback | $T(s) = \dfrac{G(s)}{1+G(s)H(s)}$ | Classic *control* formula |
| 4 | **Positive Feedback Closed Loop** | $T(s) = \dfrac{G(s)}{1-G(s)H(s)}$ | Unstable, rarely used |
| 5 | **Block-Shift (Pre-multiply)** — block after summing junction | Multiply block gain with the *added* signal | Use Mason's gain formula for complex graphs |
| 6 | **Sensitivity** of $T$ w.r.t. $G$ in closed loop | $S_G^T = \dfrac{1}{1+G(s)H(s)}$ | Low sensitivity if loop gain $\gg 1$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Absolute value caution (table syntax):** In KTU answer sheets, write $\vert 1 + G(s)H(s) \vert$ using **vertical bar notation** (never use `|x|` inside markdown tables as it breaks rendering). Use `\lvert` and `\rvert` in LaTeX.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Mechatronics**: Block diagrams model robot joint chains (sensor $\rightarrow$ controller $\rightarrow$ motor actuator).
- **Process Control**: A pressure transmitter, PID controller, and control valve form a textbook closed-loop block diagram.
- **Automotive**: Engine Control Unit (ECU) block: crankshaft position sensor $\rightarrow$ ECU $\rightarrow$ fuel injector actuator.
- **Biomedical**: ECG machine: electrode (sensor) $\rightarrow$ amplifier $\rightarrow$ filter $\rightarrow$ display.
- **IoT/Embedded**: Sensor $\rightarrow$ ADC $\rightarrow$ microcontroller $\rightarrow$ communication module $\rightarrow$ cloud.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Overall Transfer Function of a Cascaded System

Consider **three blocks** $G_1(s)$, $G_2(s)$, $G_3(s)$ connected in series (cascade), as in any sensor-actuator chain. Let the system input be $X(s)$ and the final output be $Y(s)$.

**Step 1 — Write the first block's output.**
The output of the first block is the input of the second. Denote the intermediate signal as $W(s)$:

$$W(s) = G_1(s)\cdot X(s)$$

**Step 2 — Cascade to the second block.**
The output of $G_1$ becomes the input of $G_2$. Let the next intermediate be $V(s)$:

$$V(s) = G_2(s)\cdot W(s)$$

Substitute the value of $W(s)$ from Step 1:

$$V(s) = G_2(s)\cdot G_1(s)\cdot X(s)$$

**Step 3 — Cascade to the third block.**
The output of $G_2$ becomes the input of $G_3$. The final output is $Y(s)$:

$$Y(s) = G_3(s)\cdot V(s)$$

Substitute the value of $V(s)$ from Step 2:

$$Y(s) = G_3(s)\cdot G_2(s)\cdot G_1(s)\cdot X(s)$$

**Step 4 — Identify the overall transfer function.**
By definition, $G(s) = \dfrac{Y(s)}{X(s)}$. Rearranging:

$$G(s) = \frac{Y(s)}{X(s)} = G_3(s)\cdot G_2(s)\cdot G_1(s)$$

Therefore, the overall transfer function of cascaded blocks is the **product of individual transfer functions**. The order of multiplication is **commutative** for linear time-invariant (LTI) systems:

$$G_{\text{overall}}(s) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} G_i(s)$$

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Negative Feedback Closed-Loop Transfer Function

This is the **most important derivation** in the KTU Module 1 syllabus and is directly applicable to *closed-loop sensor/actuator systems* such as servo motors and process controllers.

Consider the block schematic with forward-path transfer function $G(s)$ and feedback-path transfer function $H(s)$, with a **negative** summing junction.

**Step 1 — Identify signals at the summing junction.**
Let the external input be $R(s)$ (reference) and the feedback signal be $B(s)$. The error signal $E(s)$ at the summing junction is:

$$E(s) = R(s) - B(s)$$

(The subtraction occurs because the loop is *negative* feedback.)

**Step 2 — Forward path computation.**
The forward block $G(s)$ takes the error and produces the output $C(s)$:

$$C(s) = G(s)\cdot E(s)$$

**Step 3 — Feedback path computation.**
The feedback block $H(s)$ samples the output and produces the feedback signal:

$$B(s) = H(s)\cdot C(s)$$

**Step 4 — Substitute the feedback signal into Step 1.**
Replace $B(s)$ in the expression for $E(s)$:

$$E(s) = R(s) - H(s)\cdot C(s)$$

**Step 5 — Express $C(s)$ in terms of $R(s)$ only.**
From Step 2, $C(s) = G(s)\cdot E(s)$. Substitute the expression for $E(s)$:

$$C(s) = G(s)\cdot [R(s) - H(s)\cdot C(s)]$$

**Step 6 — Expand the bracket.**

$$C(s) = G(s)\cdot R(s) - G(s)\cdot H(s)\cdot C(s)$$

**Step 7 — Collect $C(s)$ terms on the left-hand side.**

$$C(s) + G(s)\cdot H(s)\cdot C(s) = G(s)\cdot R(s)$$

**Step 8 — Factor out $C(s)$.**

$$C(s)\cdot [1 + G(s)\cdot H(s)] = G(s)\cdot R(s)$$

**Step 9 — Solve for the closed-loop transfer function** $T(s) = \dfrac{C(s)}{R(s)}$:

$$T(s) = \frac{C(s)}{R(s)} = \frac{G(s)}{1 + G(s)\cdot H(s)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **canonical negative-feedback formula**. For *unity feedback* ($H(s)=1$), it simplifies to $T(s) = \dfrac{G(s)}{1+G(s)}$, which frequently appears in KTU numerical problems on servo systems and position-control loops.

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Sensitivity of Closed-Loop Gain to Forward-Block Variation

In a practical sensor-actuator system, the forward-path gain $G(s)$ may drift due to temperature, ageing, or component tolerance. We need to know how much the *closed-loop* output changes when $G$ changes.

**Definition.** The **sensitivity** of closed-loop transfer function $T$ with respect to forward gain $G$ is:

$$S_G^T = \frac{\partial T / T}{\partial G / G} = \frac{\partial \ln T}{\partial \ln G}$$

**Step 1 — Start with the closed-loop formula.**

$$T = \frac{G}{1 + GH}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the partial derivative of $T$ with respect to $G$.**
Treat $H$ as constant:

$$\frac{\partial T}{\partial G} = \frac{(1+GH)\cdot 1 - G\cdot (H)}{(1+GH)^2} = \frac{1+GH - GH}{(1+GH)^2} = \frac{1}{(1+GH)^2}$$

**Step 3 — Form the ratio $\dfrac{\partial T / T}{\partial G / G}$.**

$$S_G^T = \frac{\partial T}{\partial G}\cdot \frac{G}{T} = \frac{1}{(1+GH)^2}\cdot \frac{G}{\dfrac{G}{1+GH}}$$

**Step 4 — Simplify.**

$$S_G^T = \frac{1}{(1+GH)^2}\cdot \frac{G(1+GH)}{G} = \frac{1}{1+GH}$$

**Conclusion.** The sensitivity is **reduced by a factor of $(1+GH)$**. If the loop gain $GH \gg 1$, then $S_G^T \rightarrow 0$, meaning the closed-loop system is **insensitive to forward-gain variations** — a fundamental reason why feedback is used in sensor-actuator control.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Block Reduction Techniques (Exam Cheat Grid)

| Reduction Type | Before | After (Equivalent) | Used When |
|---|---|---|---|
| Series | $G_1 \rightarrow G_2$ | $G_1 G_2$ | Blocks one after another |
| Parallel | $G_1, G_2$ summed | $G_1 + G_2$ | Same input, outputs added |
| Feedback | $G$ forward, $H$ feedback | $\dfrac{G}{1+GH}$ | Closed loop |
| Block shift before summing | Block on input side of summer | Multiplies added signal | Simplify a complex loop |
| Take-off point move | Branch point relocated | Identity preserved (with inverse multiply) | When feedback path blocks impede reduction |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Generalised Sensor Block Schematic (KTU Module 1 Standard)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    PHY["Physical Quantity\n(measurand)"] --> S1["Primary Sensor\n(Transducer Element)"]
    S1 --> S2["Variable Conversion\n(R to V or I)"]
    S2 --> S3["Signal Conditioning\n(Amplifier, Filter)"]
    S3 --> S4["Data Acquisition\n(ADC, Display)"]
    S4 --> OUT["Output\n(Voltage / Digital)"]

    classDef block fill:#E8F1FF,stroke:#1E5AA8,stroke-width:2px,color:#0A2540;
    class S1,S2,S3,S4 block;
```

### 4.2 Generalised Actuator Block Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    IN["Input Command\n(Electrical Signal)"] --> A1["Signal Conditioning\n(Power Amplifier)"]
    A1 --> A2["Control Stage\n(PWM / Driver IC)"]
    A2 --> A3["Conversion Element\n(Electrical to Mechanical)"]
    A3 --> A4["Actuating Element\n(Motor / Valve / Heater)"]
    A4 --> OUT["Physical Output\n(Displacement / Force / Heat)"]

    classDef act fill:#FFF1E8,stroke:#C25A1E,stroke-width:2px,color:#5C2A0A;
    class A1,A2,A3,A4 act;
```

### 4.3 Canonical Negative-Feedback Closed-Loop (Sensor + Actuator)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    R["R(s)\nReference Input"] --> SUM{{"+/-"}}
    SUM --> E["E(s)\nError"]
    E --> G["G(s)\nController + Actuator"]
    G --> C["C(s)\nOutput"]
    C --> LOAD["Plant / Process"]
    LOAD --> C
    C --> H["H(s)\nSensor Feedback"]
    H --> SUM

    classDef fb fill:#E8FFE8,stroke:#1E8A1E,stroke-width:2px,color:#0A3A0A;
    class G,H,LOAD fb;
```

### 4.4 Block Reduction for a Typical Series Chain (Sensor Chain)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    X["X(s)\nPhysical Input"] --> G1["G1(s)\nPrimary Sensor\n(eg Thermocouple)"]
    G1 --> G2["G2(s)\nAmplifier\n(eg Instrumentation Amp)"]
    G2 --> G3["G3(s)\nFilter\n(eg Low-Pass)"]
    G3 --> Y["Y(s)\nConditioned Output"]

    classDef chain fill:#F0E8FF,stroke:#5A1E8A,stroke-width:2px,color:#2A0A4A;
    class G1,G2,G3 chain;
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Equivalent Reduced Form** of the above is a single block: $G_{\text{eq}}(s) = G_1(s)\cdot G_2(s)\cdot G_3(s)$.

### 4.5 Block Diagram of a Position Control System (KTU-Standard Closed-Loop Example)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    R["R(s)\nDesired Position"] --> SUM{{"+"}}
    SUM --> E["E(s)\nError Voltage"]
    E --> AMP["Kamp\nAmplifier"]
    AMP --> M["Km\nDC Motor"]
    M --> GEAR["Kg\nGear Train"]
    GEAR --> Y["Y(s)\nShaft Position"]
    Y --> FB["Kt\nPotentiometric Sensor"]
    FB --> SUM

    classDef loop fill:#FFE8F1,stroke:#A81E5A,stroke-width:2px,color:#4A0A2A;
    class AMP,M,GEAR,FB loop;
```

**Closed-loop transfer function** of the position control system:

$$T(s) = \frac{K_{amp}\cdot K_m\cdot K_g}{1 + K_{amp}\cdot K_m\cdot K_g\cdot K_t}$$

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Question]**

**(a)** Define a **block diagram** in the context of a sensor-actuator system. List its **four** essential elements.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **block diagram** is a graphical representation of a system in which the principal functional stages are represented as **rectangular blocks**, with arrows indicating the direction of signal flow and the **transfer function** of each stage written inside its block.

The four essential elements are:

1. **Block** — represents a functional stage with a transfer function $G(s)$.
2. **Summing junction** ($\sum$) — adds or subtracts two or more signals.
3. **Take-off point** — branches a signal into multiple paths.
4. **Signal flow lines (arrows)** — indicate the direction of signal flow.

---

**(b)** What is a **transfer function**? Write its mathematical expression.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **transfer function** is the ratio of the Laplace transform of the output to the Laplace transform of the input of a linear time-invariant (LTI) system, assuming **zero initial conditions**.

$$G(s) = \frac{Y(s)}{X(s)}$$

where $Y(s) = \mathcal{L}\{y(t)\}$ is the output and $X(s) = \mathcal{L}\{x(t)\}$ is the input. It contains complete information about the system's gain and dynamic behaviour.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> (1) Students commonly **forget to label the summing junction sign** ($+$ or $-$). Mark loss = 1 mark.
> (2) Forgetting to **mention zero initial conditions** when defining $G(s)$ leads to -1 mark.
> (3) In cascade problems, students often **add** gains instead of **multiplying** them. Mark loss = 2 marks.
> (4) Always write the **units** of each block's gain in derivation problems to get full marks.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Question]**

**(a)** Draw the **block schematic representation** of a **generalised sensor system**, clearly labelling each functional stage. State the function of **each block**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A measurement system has three cascaded blocks with transfer functions $G_1(s) = 10$, $G_2(s) = \dfrac{1}{1+0.1s}$, and $G_3(s) = 5$. Find the **overall transfer function** of the system and the **steady-state output** for a step input of magnitude **2 units**. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — 7 marks**

**Block schematic of a generalised sensor system:**

```
Physical Quantity ──► Primary Sensor ──► Variable Conversion ──► Variable Manipulation ──► Signal Conditioning ──► Data Transmission ──► Output
```

**Function of each block (1 mark each, plus 1 mark for diagram and 1 mark for arrow direction):**

1. **Primary Sensor (Transducer Element):** Converts the physical measurand (e.g., temperature, pressure, force) into an electrical signal.
2. **Variable Conversion Element:** Converts the signal into a more usable form, e.g., resistance change $\rightarrow$ voltage change (using a Wheatstone bridge).
3. **Variable Manipulation Element:** Performs signal modification such as amplification, attenuation, or linearisation.
4. **Signal Conditioning:** Includes filtering, A/D conversion, isolation, and noise suppression.
5. **Data Transmission / Output Stage:** Drives the display, recorder, or feedback network.

*(1 mark reserved for arrows / direction-of-flow convention.)*

---

**Part (b) — 7 marks**

**Step 1 — Identify the configuration.**
The three blocks are in **series (cascade)**. Therefore, the overall transfer function is the **product** of the three.

**Step 2 — Write the product.**

$$G(s) = G_1(s)\cdot G_2(s)\cdot G_3(s) = 10 \cdot \frac{1}{1+0.1s} \cdot 5$$

**Step 3 — Multiply the constant gains first.**

$$G(s) = \frac{50}{1+0.1s}$$

**Step 4 — Steady-state output for a unit-magnitude step input (Final Value Theorem).**
For a step input of magnitude 2, $X(s) = \dfrac{2}{s}$.

Output in Laplace domain:

$$Y(s) = G(s)\cdot X(s) = \frac{50}{1+0.1s}\cdot \frac{2}{s} = \frac{100}{s(1+0.1s)}$$

**Step 5 — Apply the Final Value Theorem** to find steady-state output $y_{ss}$:

$$y_{ss} = \lim_{s \to 0} s\cdot Y(s) = \lim_{s \to 0} s \cdot \frac{100}{s(1+0.1s)} = \lim_{s \to 0} \frac{100}{1+0.1s}$$

$$y_{ss} = \frac{100}{1+0} = 100 \text{ units}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Answer:** $G(s) = \dfrac{50}{1+0.1s}$ and $y_{ss} = 100$ units.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Internal Choice]**

**(a)** Derive the **closed-loop transfer function** of a **negative-feedback** system with forward-path transfer function $G(s)$ and feedback-path transfer function $H(s)$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A **temperature control system** has the following block parameters: amplifier gain $K_a = 5$, heater transfer function $G_h(s) = \dfrac{2}{1+5s}$, temperature sensor gain $K_t = 0.5$ V/°C. The system uses **unity feedback** ($H(s)=1$). Find the **closed-loop transfer function** and the **time constant** of the controlled response. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — 7 marks**

(Refer to the full derivation in **Section 3.2** above. Key steps to be written:)

1. Define error $E(s) = R(s) - B(s)$ — **1 mark**
2. $C(s) = G(s)\cdot E(s)$ — **1 mark**
3. $B(s) = H(s)\cdot C(s)$ — **1 mark**
4. Substitution and simplification — **2 marks**
5. Final result: $T(s) = \dfrac{G(s)}{1 + G(s)H(s)}$ — **2 marks**

---

**Part (b) — 7 marks**

**Step 1 — Identify the components of the forward path.**
The forward path consists of the amplifier $K_a$ and the heater $G_h(s)$ cascaded:

$$G(s) = K_a \cdot G_h(s) = 5 \cdot \frac{2}{1+5s} = \frac{10}{1+5s}$$

**Step 2 — Identify the feedback path.**
For unity feedback, $H(s) = 1$.

**Step 3 — Apply the closed-loop formula.**

$$T(s) = \frac{G(s)}{1 + G(s)\cdot H(s)} = \frac{\dfrac{10}{1+5s}}{1 + \dfrac{10}{1+5s}}$$

**Step 4 — Combine the denominator by bringing to a common form.**

$$T(s) = \frac{10}{1+5s} \cdot \frac{1+5s}{1+5s + 10} = \frac{10}{5s + 11}$$

**Step 5 — Normalize to standard first-order form** $T(s) = \dfrac{K}{\tau s + 1}$:

$$T(s) = \frac{10/11}{\frac{5}{11}s + 1} = \frac{0.909}{0.4545\,s + 1}$$

**Step 6 — Identify the time constant** $\tau$:

$$\tau = \frac{5}{11} \approx 0.4545 \text{ seconds}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Answer:** $T(s) = \dfrac{10}{5s+11}$, time constant $\tau \approx 0.4545$ s. Note that the **sensor gain $K_t$ does not enter the forward path** — it would enter if feedback were non-unity.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist**

- **Block diagram** = rectangle + arrow-in + arrow-out + transfer function inside.
- **Three core elements** of any block: input, transfer function $G(s)$, output.
- **Cascade rule:** $G_{\text{overall}} = G_1\cdot G_2\cdot G_3$ (multiplication, not addition).
- **Parallel rule:** $G_{\text{overall}} = G_1 + G_2$ (same input, summed output).
- **Closed-loop rule (negative feedback):** $T(s) = \dfrac{G}{1+GH}$ — **the single most-tested equation in Module 1.**
- **Unity feedback simplification:** set $H(s)=1$ in the closed-loop formula.
- **Transfer function** is defined only for **LTI systems** under **zero initial conditions**.
- **Summing junction** must be drawn as a circle with $\pm$ signs clearly marked.
- **Take-off point** has no gain — it is a passive branch.
- **Sensitivity** of closed-loop gain to $G$-variation is $\dfrac{1}{1+GH}$ — high loop gain ⇒ low sensitivity (key reason for using feedback in sensors/actuators).
- **Time constant** $\tau$ appears as the coefficient of $s$ in a normalized first-order denominator $1+\tau s$.
- **Always state units** of gains and outputs in KTU board answers.
- **Generalised sensor chain:** Primary Sensor → Variable Conversion → Variable Manipulation → Signal Conditioning → Output.
- **Generalised actuator chain:** Input → Power Amplifier → Driver → Conversion Element → Output.
- **Negative feedback** is used in actuator control to **reduce sensitivity**, **reject disturbances**, and **linearize** the response.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Interpreting Typical Manufacturer's Datasheet of Internal Sensors; Note: Block Schematic Representations, Interpretation of Typical Manufacturer's Datasheet, and Numerical Problems of the Main Sensors are to be Covered
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBRT303 - SENSORS AND ACTUATORS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Interpreting Typical Manufacturer's Datasheet of Internal Sensors; Note: Block Schematic Representations, Interpretation of Typical Manufacturer's Datasheet, and Numerical Problems of the Main Sensors are to be Covered

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a Manufacturer's Datasheet?

A **Manufacturer's Datasheet** (also called a *specification sheet*, *data bulletin*, or *product datasheet*) is an official, standardized technical document published by a sensor manufacturer that quantitatively describes every operational, electrical, mechanical, and environmental characteristic of a sensor. For a B.Tech engineer, it is the **single most authoritative reference** before designing, selecting, or interfacing any sensor in an instrumentation, automation, IoT, or embedded project.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (PCBRT303 – Module 1):**
> *Interpreting a typical manufacturer's datasheet of internal sensors* means systematically extracting the block schematic, pin/terminal configuration, electrical ratings, transfer function, error budget, environmental limits, and protection class from a real datasheet — and using those values to perform design calculations.

## 1.2 What are "Internal Sensors"?

In the KTU/industrial context, **Internal Sensors** are devices that measure the *intrinsic* state variables of a process, machine, or system — quantities that exist *inside* the equipment itself rather than external environmental properties.

| Category | Examples | Physical Variable Measured |
|----------|----------|----------------------------|
| **Temperature** | RTD, Thermocouple, Thermistor, LM35, PT100 | Heat / °C, K, °F |
| **Pressure** | Strain-gauge, Piezoelectric, Capacitive | Force per unit area (Pa, bar, psi) |
| **Flow** | Orifice + DP, Turbine, Coriolis, Electromagnetic | Volume/time (L/min, m³/h) |
| **Level** | Float, Ultrasonic, Capacitive, Hydrostatic | Height of liquid (m, mm) |
| **Force / Load** | Load cell, Strain gauge | Force (N, kgf) |
| **Humidity** | DHT11, DHT22, SHT31 | Relative Humidity (% RH) |
| **Proximity / Position** | Inductive, Capacitive, LVDT, Encoder | Displacement (mm) |
| **Speed / RPM** | Tachogenerator, Hall-effect sensor | Rotational speed (RPM) |
| **Vibration / Accelerometer** | MEMS ADXL345, Piezoelectric | Acceleration (g, m/s²) |

> [!NOTE]
> *Internal* in industrial instrumentation is distinguished from *external* (or *environmental*) sensors that measure weather/ambient parameters. Pressure, temperature, flow, level, force — the "big four" of process industries — are classic internal sensors.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Vehicle Specification Sheet"

Imagine you are buying a car. The showroom brochure (datasheet) tells you:

- **Engine type** = Sensor *operating principle* (e.g., strain gauge bridge)
- **Mileage** = Sensor *accuracy / efficiency*
- **Top speed** = Sensor *maximum range / bandwidth*
- **Fuel tank capacity** = Sensor *supply voltage tolerance*
- **Warranty** = Sensor *operating life / MTBF*
- **Working temperature range** (e.g., -20 °C to 50 °C) = Sensor's *environmental limits*

A car buyer who ignores the brochure may buy a vehicle that cannot climb a hill. Likewise, an engineer who skips the datasheet may design a circuit that burns the sensor on Day 1. **The datasheet is a contract between manufacturer and designer.**

## 1.4 The Three Pillars of a Sensor Datasheet

Every professional datasheet (Honeywell, Texas Instruments, Bosch, Omron, Endress+Hauser, etc.) is built on three structural pillars that KTU expects students to recognize:

1. **Block Schematic / Functional Diagram** — How the internal stages are connected (sensing element → signal conditioning → ADC → output).
2. **Electrical & Pin Description** — Supply, ground, signal-out, pin numbers, package type.
3. **Performance / Static & Dynamic Characteristics** — Range, sensitivity, accuracy, linearity, hysteresis, response time, drift.

```
   Pillar 1                Pillar 2                Pillar 3
┌──────────────┐      ┌──────────────┐      ┌──────────────────┐
│  BLOCK        │      │  ELECTRICAL  │      │  PERFORMANCE     │
│  SCHEMATIC    │ ───► │  INTERFACE   │ ───► │  SPECIFICATIONS  │
│  (Internal    │      │  (Pins,      │      │  (Accuracy,      │
│   structure)  │      │   supply)    │      │   range, drift)  │
└──────────────┘      └──────────────┘      └──────────────────────┘
```

## 1.5 Universal Symbols and Standards Used

KTU students must be familiar with these standard symbols/conventions appearing in datasheets:

| Symbol / Notation | Meaning |
|-------------------|---------|
| **V<sub>CC</sub> / V<sub>DD</sub>** | Positive supply voltage |
| **V<sub>SS</sub> / GND** | Ground / 0 V reference |
| **V<sub>out</sub>, V<sub>o</sub>** | Analog output voltage |
| **I<sub>out</sub>** | Current-mode output (e.g., 4–20 mA) |
| **ΔX / FS** | Change in X / Full-Scale value |
| **% FS** | Percent of Full Scale — common unit for accuracy |
| **T<sub>C</sub>, TC** | Temperature Coefficient (drift per °C) |
| **B, β** | Beta constant (thermistor) |
| **R<sub>0</sub>** | Resistance at reference temperature (e.g., 100 Ω at 0 °C for PT100) |
| **IPxx** | Ingress Protection rating (e.g., IP67) |
| **RoHS** | Restriction of Hazardous Substances (compliance) |
| **CE / UL / IEC** | Safety / regulatory compliance marks |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examiners' Hint:** Always convert every quantity to **SI base units** (V, A, °C, Pa, m) before any numerical calculation. KTU valuation scripts explicitly check for unit consistency — losing a full mark for missing units is extremely common.

## 1.6 Why Datasheet Reading is a High-Weightage Skill

KTU lists this topic under **Module 1 (Fundamentals of Sensors)** because every later module — temperature, pressure, flow, level, and actuators — requires you to read a datasheet. The skill is *transversal* across the entire syllabus. The university frequently asks 3-mark short questions such as *"With the help of a datasheet, explain the pin configuration of LM35"* or 14-mark design questions such as *"Design a signal conditioning circuit for a PT100 based on datasheet parameters."*

> [!TIP]
> Treat the datasheet not as documentation but as a **mathematical object**. Every parameter (sensitivity, range, error) is a *number with units and tolerances* that can be substituted directly into design equations.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomy of a Sensor Datasheet — Section-by-Section Walkthrough

A typical industrial sensor datasheet (e.g., Honeywell SS39ET, TI LM35, Omega PT100, Bosch BMP280) contains the following standardized sections. KTU students must be able to locate and interpret each.

### Section A — General Description
- Sensor type and operating principle (e.g., *"piezoresistive silicon pressure sensor"*, *"band-gap temperature sensor"*).
- Package style and mounting.
- Intended applications.

### Section B — Block Schematic / Functional Diagram
- Shows the internal signal flow: **Sensing Element → Signal Conditioning (amplifier) → Linearization → Output Driver (V or mA) → Protection/Regulation**.
- Highlights the user-accessible pins only; internal transistors, op-amps, and resistors are abstracted into functional blocks.

### Section C — Absolute Maximum Ratings
- These are the *hard destruction limits*, not the operating limits. Exceeding them **permanently damages** the sensor.
  - Maximum supply voltage (e.g., V<sub>CCmax</sub> = 6 V for LM35)
  - Operating/storage temperature (e.g., -55 °C to +150 °C)
  - Maximum load (for load cells)
  - ESD rating

> [!WARNING]
> Operating a sensor at *Absolute Maximum Ratings* may damage it *immediately or accelerate aging*. KTU will penalize confusion between *operating* and *absolute maximum* parameters.

### Section D — Recommended Operating Conditions
- The safe, calibrated, linear range over which the manufacturer *guarantees* specifications.
- Supply voltage, ambient temperature, humidity, vibration levels.

### Section E — Electrical Characteristics (Static Performance)
- **Range** (span)
- **Accuracy** (% FS, % reading, or ±X units)
- **Sensitivity** (mV/°C, mV/V, mV/kPa)
- **Linearity / Non-linearity** (% FS)
- **Hysteresis** (% FS)
- **Repeatability** (% FS)
- **Zero offset / Zero drift**
- **Output impedance / Load impedance**

### Section F — Dynamic Performance
- **Response time / Time constant (τ)**
- **Bandwidth** (Hz)
- **Settling time**
- **Self-resonant frequency**

### Section G — Environmental & Mechanical
- IP rating, mechanical shock, vibration, materials (SS316, Hastelloy), weight, dimensions.

### Section H — Connection / Wiring Diagram
- Pin-out, color codes, suggested PCB footprint, 2-wire / 3-wire / 4-wire hookups.

---

## 2.2 Core Static Characteristics — Definitions and Equations

The *static* behaviour describes the sensor at equilibrium. These parameters are universally tested in KTU numerical problems.

### 2.2.1 Span (Full-Scale Range, FS)

The algebraic difference between the maximum and minimum output values across the calibrated range.

$$ \text{Span} = y_{\max} - y_{\min} $$

For a 4–20 mA pressure transmitter calibrated for 0–10 bar:
$$ \text{Span} = 20\,\text{mA} - 4\,\text{mA} = 16\,\text{mA} $$

### 2.2.2 Sensitivity (K or S)

The slope of the ideal (straight-line) input–output transfer curve. It is the *first derivative* of output with respect to input at the operating point.

$$ S = K = \frac{\Delta y}{\Delta x} = \frac{y_{\max} - y_{\min}}{x_{\max} - x_{\min}} $$

**Unit:** V per unit of measurand (e.g., mV/°C, mV/kPa).

### 2.2.3 Transfer Function (Linear Sensor)

The straight-line equation relating output to input:

$$ y = K \cdot x + b $$

where:
- $y$ = sensor output (V or mA)
- $K$ = sensitivity (V/unit)
- $x$ = measurand (the physical input)
- $b$ = offset / intercept (output when $x = 0$)

### 2.2.4 Linearity / Non-linearity

Maximum deviation of the actual output curve from the *best-fit straight line*, expressed as a percentage of Full-Scale output (FSO).

$$ \text{Non-linearity } (\%\,FS) = \frac{\Delta y_{\max}}{y_{FSO}} \times 100 $$

### 2.2.5 Hysteresis

The maximum difference in output for the *same* input value approached from *opposite directions* (increasing vs. decreasing).

$$ \text{Hysteresis } (\%\,FS) = \frac{\vert y_{\uparrow} - y_{\downarrow} \vert_{\max}}{y_{FSO}} \times 100 $$

### 2.2.6 Repeatability

The ability to reproduce the same output for the same input under identical conditions (over multiple cycles). Often expressed as $\pm$ %FS or as 2σ/3σ standard deviation.

### 2.2.7 Accuracy (Total Error)

The closeness of the measured value to the *true* value. Accuracy *combines* linearity, hysteresis, repeatability, and zero/span errors. Datasheets commonly quote it as:

$$ \text{Accuracy } (\%\,FS) = \frac{\text{Maximum measured error}}{y_{FSO}} \times 100 $$

> [!NOTE]
> **Precision ≠ Accuracy.** Precision is *repeatability* (closeness of repeated measurements to each other). Accuracy is closeness to the true value. A sensor can be precise but inaccurate (systematic bias).

### 2.2.8 Resolution

The smallest change in input that produces a detectable change in output. For digital sensors, it is the **Least Significant Bit (LSB)** of the ADC:

$$ \text{Resolution} = \frac{\text{FS Range}}{2^{N}} $$

where $N$ = number of ADC bits.

### 2.2.9 Drift

Slow change of output with time (long-term drift) or with temperature (thermal drift / temperature coefficient). Drift is critical for industrial applications where recalibration cycles are long.

$$ \text{Thermal drift} = \frac{\Delta y}{\Delta T} \quad [\text{units per °C}] $$

---

## 2.3 Core Dynamic Characteristics

### 2.3.1 First-Order Time Constant (τ)

Many sensors (thermistors, thermometers, thermal-mass flowmeters) behave as first-order systems:

$$ \tau = \frac{C}{hA} \quad [\text{seconds}] $$

where $C$ = thermal capacity, $h$ = heat-transfer coefficient, $A$ = surface area.

Step response: the output reaches 63.2 % of final value in time $\tau$, 86.5 % in $2\tau$, 95 % in $3\tau$, 99.3 % in $5\tau$.

### 2.3.2 Bandwidth (f<sub>c</sub>)

Frequency at which output power drops to half (-3 dB) of DC value. For a first-order system:

$$ f_c = \frac{1}{2\pi\tau} \quad [\text{Hz}] $$

### 2.3.3 Step Response Equations

First-order transfer function in Laplace domain:

$$ \frac{Y(s)}{X(s)} = \frac{K}{\tau s + 1} $$

For a step input of magnitude $A$ applied at $t = 0$:

$$ y(t) = K A \left(1 - e^{-t/\tau}\right) $$

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Master Cheat-Table)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Memorize this table — KTU numericals in Module 1 are almost always based on one or more of these equations.

| # | Parameter | Symbol | Formula | Unit | Typical Datasheet Value (Example) |
|---|-----------|--------|---------|------|-----------------------------------|
| 1 | Span | FS | $y_{\max} - y_{\min}$ | Same as output | 16 mA (for 4–20 mA) |
| 2 | Sensitivity | $K$ or $S$ | $\dfrac{\Delta y}{\Delta x}$ | V/°C, V/kPa, V/N | 10 mV/°C (LM35) |
| 3 | Transfer (linear) | $y(x)$ | $Kx + b$ | V or mA | — |
| 4 | Linearity error | NL | $\dfrac{\Delta y_{\max}}{y_{FSO}} \cdot 100$ | % FS | $\pm$ 0.5 % FS |
| 5 | Hysteresis | Hy | $\dfrac{\vert y_{\uparrow} - y_{\downarrow} \vert_{\max}}{y_{FSO}} \cdot 100$ | % FS | $\pm$ 0.1 % FS |
| 6 | Repeatability | R | $2\sigma / \text{FS}$ | % FS | $\pm$ 0.05 % FS |
| 7 | Accuracy | Acc | $\dfrac{e_{\max}}{y_{FSO}} \cdot 100$ | % FS | $\pm$ 0.25 % FS |
| 8 | Resolution | Res | $\dfrac{\text{FS Range}}{2^{N}}$ | Same as input | 0.024 % (12-bit) |
| 9 | ADC step size | $q$ | $\dfrac{V_{\text{ref}}}{2^{N} - 1}$ | V/LSB | 0.8 mV (5 V ref, 12-bit) |
| 10 | Time constant | $\tau$ | $\dfrac{C}{hA}$ | s | 5 s (thermistor) |
| 11 | Bandwidth | $f_c$ | $\dfrac{1}{2\pi \tau}$ | Hz | 31.8 mHz |
| 12 | First-order step | $y(t)$ | $KA\left(1 - e^{-t/\tau}\right)$ | Output unit | — |
| 13 | Thermal drift | TC | $\dfrac{\Delta y}{\Delta T}$ | unit/°C | 0.05 % FS/°C |
| 14 | %FS conversion | %FS | $\dfrac{\text{Quantity}}{y_{FSO}} \cdot 100$ | % | — |
| 15 | Dead-band / Threshold | — | Smallest detectable $\Delta x$ | Input unit | 0.1 °C |

---

## 2.5 Real-World Significance — Why These Equations Matter

| Field | Application | Parameter Used |
|-------|-------------|----------------|
| **Industrial Process Control (Refineries, Power Plants)** | Selecting transmitters for SCADA/DCS | Accuracy, drift, IP rating |
| **Medical Electronics (Patient Monitoring)** | Body temperature, blood pressure | Resolution, linearity, biocompatibility |
| **Automotive (ECU, ADAS)** | MAP, MAF, oil pressure, coolant temperature | Range, response time, ESD rating |
| **Aerospace (Avionics)** | Altitude, cabin pressure, surface temperature | Repeatability, vibration, MTBF |
| **IoT / Smart Agriculture** | Soil moisture, ambient T/RH | Supply current, digital interface |
| **Robotics** | Force/torque, IMU, encoders | Bandwidth, resolution, output type |

> [!TIP]
> In viva voce and design questions, always begin with the datasheet parameter that *constrains* your design — typically either **range**, **supply voltage**, or **accuracy**. Identify the bottleneck first; the rest of the design follows from it.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Transfer Function from Two Datasheet Points

A common KTU problem: *"A sensor datasheet shows that an input of 0 °C gives an output of 0 V, and an input of 100 °C gives an output of 5 V. Find the sensitivity and the transfer function."*

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Identify the two calibration points from the datasheet:**

$$ (x_1,\, y_1) = (0\,°\text{C},\, 0\,\text{V}) $$

$$ (x_2,\, y_2) = (100\,°\text{C},\, 5\,\text{V}) $$

**Step 2 — Apply the linear transfer equation $y = Kx + b$. Use point 1 to find the offset $b$:**

$$ 0 = K(0) + b \;\Rightarrow\; b = 0 $$

**Step 3 — Substitute point 2 to solve for sensitivity $K$:**

$$ 5 = K(100) + 0 $$

$$ K = \frac{5\,\text{V}}{100\,°\text{C}} = 0.05\,\text{V/°C} = 50\,\text{mV/°C} $$

**Step 4 — Write the complete transfer function:**

$$ \boxed{\,y(\text{V}) = 0.05 \cdot x(°\text{C})\,} $$

**Step 5 — Verify by testing an intermediate point, e.g., $x = 50\,°\text{C}$:**

$$ y = 0.05 \times 50 = 2.5\,\text{V} \;\checkmark $$

### Worked Numerical — Extended KTU Style

> *A pressure sensor datasheet specifies the following calibration: at 0 bar the output is 4 mA, and at 10 bar the output is 20 mA. Find the sensitivity in mA/bar, the transfer equation, and the output at 3.5 bar.*

**Sensitivity:**

$$ K = \frac{20 - 4}{10 - 0} = \frac{16\,\text{mA}}{10\,\text{bar}} = 1.6\,\text{mA/bar} $$

**Offset (b):**

$$ b = y_1 - K x_1 = 4 - (1.6)(0) = 4\,\text{mA} $$

**Transfer equation:**

$$ y(\text{mA}) = 1.6 \cdot P(\text{bar}) + 4 $$

**Output at $P = 3.5$ bar:**

$$ y = 1.6 \times 3.5 + 4 = 5.6 + 4 = 9.6\,\text{mA} $$

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Reverse Calculation (Input from Output)

> *The same pressure transmitter outputs 12 mA. What is the measured pressure?*

Rearrange the transfer equation:

$$ x = \frac{y - b}{K} $$

$$ P = \frac{12 - 4}{1.6} = \frac{8}{1.6} = 5\,\text{bar} $$

**Verification:** Substitute back: $1.6 \times 5 + 4 = 12$ mA ✓

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Resolution and ADC Step Size

> *A load cell with range 0–500 N is connected to a 12-bit ADC with a 5 V reference and a gain stage producing exactly 5 V at 500 N. Find the resolution in Newtons.*

**Step 1 — Number of discrete levels for a 12-bit ADC:**

$$ L = 2^{12} = 4096 $$

**Step 2 — Voltage per LSB (quantization step):**

$$ q = \frac{V_{\text{ref}}}{2^{N} - 1} = \frac{5}{4095} = 1.221\,\text{mV/LSB} $$

**Step 3 — Resolution in Newtons (assuming uniform mapping of FS to V<sub>ref</sub>):**

$$ \text{Resolution} = \frac{500\,\text{N}}{4096} = 0.122\,\text{N/LSB} \approx 122\,\text{mN} $$

Or equivalently:

$$ \text{Resolution} = \frac{q}{K} = \frac{1.221\,\text{mV}}{10\,\text{mV/N}} = 0.1221\,\text{N} $$

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — First-Order Step Response

> *A thermistor with time constant $\tau = 8$ s is suddenly immersed from 25 °C air into 80 °C water. The sensitivity is 10 mV/°C, with zero offset. Find the output voltage at $t = 4$ s, 8 s, 16 s, and the steady-state value.*

**Step 1 — Identify parameters:**

$$ \tau = 8\,\text{s},\quad K = 10\,\text{mV/°C},\quad \Delta T = 80 - 25 = 55\,°\text{C} $$

**Step 2 — Steady-state output (final value):**

$$ y_{\infty} = K \cdot \Delta T = 10 \times 55 = 550\,\text{mV} = 0.55\,\text{V} $$

**Step 3 — First-order step response equation:**

$$ y(t) = y_{\infty}\left(1 - e^{-t/\tau}\right) $$

**Step 4 — Evaluate at the requested instants:**

At $t = 4\,\text{s}$:

$$ y(4) = 550 \times \left(1 - e^{-4/8}\right) = 550 \times (1 - e^{-0.5}) $$

$$ e^{-0.5} \approx 0.6065 $$

$$ y(4) = 550 \times (1 - 0.6065) = 550 \times 0.3935 = 216.4\,\text{mV} \approx 0.216\,\text{V} $$

At $t = 8\,\text{s} = \tau$:

$$ y(8) = 550 \times (1 - e^{-1}) = 550 \times (1 - 0.3679) = 550 \times 0.6321 = 347.7\,\text{mV} \approx 0.348\,\text{V} $$

At $t = 16\,\text{s} = 2\tau$:

$$ y(16) = 550 \times (1 - e^{-2}) = 550 \times (1 - 0.1353) = 550 \times 0.8647 = 475.6\,\text{mV} \approx 0.476\,\text{V} $$

At $t \to \infty$:

$$ y_{\infty} = 550\,\text{mV} = 0.55\,\text{V} \;\checkmark $$

**Observation:** At one time constant, the sensor reads 63.2 % of the final value — a *universal* KTU result worth remembering.

---

## 3.5 Derivation 5 — Total Error Budget (Accuracy from Multiple Sources)

> *A pressure sensor datasheet lists: Linearity = ± 0.3 %FS, Hysteresis = ± 0.2 %FS, Repeatability = ± 0.1 %FS, Zero drift = ± 0.1 %FS. Range = 0–10 bar. Find the worst-case total error in bar and the maximum likely error (RSS method).*

**Step 1 — Worst-case (arithmetic sum) error in %FS:**

$$ e_{\text{WC}} = 0.3 + 0.2 + 0.1 + 0.1 = 0.7\,\%\,\text{FS} $$

**Step 2 — Convert to absolute error in bar (FS = 10 bar):**

$$ e_{\text{WC,abs}} = \frac{0.7}{100} \times 10 = 0.07\,\text{bar} $$

**Step 3 — Root-Sum-Square (RSS) method assuming independent errors:**

$$ e_{\text{RSS}} = \sqrt{0.3^2 + 0.2^2 + 0.1^2 + 0.1^2} $$

$$ e_{\text{RSS}} = \sqrt{0.09 + 0.04 + 0.01 + 0.01} = \sqrt{0.15} = 0.387\,\%\,\text{FS} $$

**Step 4 — Convert RSS error to bar:**

$$ e_{\text{RSS,abs}} = \frac{0.387}{100} \times 10 = 0.0387\,\text{bar} $$

> [!NOTE]
> KTU typically asks for the **worst-case** sum method because it requires no assumption of independence. The RSS method is used when errors are statistically independent and Gaussian — also acceptable as an "engineering estimate."

---

## 3.6 Derivation 6 — Output from %FS Specification

> *A temperature transmitter has 0–200 °C range and 4–20 mA output. What current corresponds to 60 % FS input?*

**Step 1 — Input value at 60 % FS:**

$$ T = 0.60 \times 200 = 120\,°\text{C} $$

**Step 2 — Sensitivity (mA/°C):**

$$ K = \frac{20 - 4}{200 - 0} = \frac{16}{200} = 0.08\,\text{mA/°C} $$

**Step 3 — Output:**

$$ y = 0.08 \times 120 + 4 = 9.6 + 4 = 13.6\,\text{mA} $$

**Shortcut method using %FS:**

$$ y = 4 + (0.60) \times 16 = 4 + 9.6 = 13.6\,\text{mA} \;\checkmark $$

---

## 3.7 Derivation 7 — Temperature Compensation of Drift

> *A sensor with sensitivity $K = 2$ mV/kPa and zero offset $b = 1$ mV at 25 °C has a thermal drift of 0.05 %FS/°C. The full-scale output is 100 mV. What is the corrected output at 100 kPa and 65 °C?*

**Step 1 — Drift in mV/°C:**

$$ \text{Drift} = \frac{0.05}{100} \times 100\,\text{mV} = 0.05\,\text{mV/°C} $$

**Step 2 — Temperature rise from reference (25 °C):**

$$ \Delta T = 65 - 25 = 40\,°\text{C} $$

**Step 3 — Total drift in mV:**

$$ \Delta y_{\text{drift}} = 0.05 \times 40 = 2\,\text{mV} $$

**Step 4 — Ideal output at 100 kPa (uncorrected):**

$$ y_{\text{ideal}} = 2 \times 100 + 1 = 201\,\text{mV} $$

**Step 5 — Corrected output (subtract drift if it represents a positive bias, or add if negative — depends on sign convention in datasheet):**

$$ y_{\text{corrected}} = 201 - 2 = 199\,\text{mV} $$

---

## 3.8 Analytical Matrix — Common KTU Numerical Templates

| Problem Type | Given | Find | Governing Equation |
|--------------|-------|------|--------------------|
| Sensitivity from two points | $(x_1, y_1), (x_2, y_2)$ | $K$ | $K = (y_2 - y_1)/(x_2 - x_1)$ |
| Output for given input | $K, b, x$ | $y$ | $y = Kx + b$ |
| Input from output | $K, b, y$ | $x$ | $x = (y - b)/K$ |
| %FS to absolute | $y, y_{FSO}$ | $y_{\%FS}$ | $y_{\%FS} = (y/y_{FSO}) \times 100$ |
| Resolution of ADC | FS range, $N$ bits | Resolution | $\text{Res} = \text{FS}/2^N$ |
| Step response at $t$ | $K, A, \tau, t$ | $y(t)$ | $y(t) = KA(1 - e^{-t/\tau})$ |
| Time to reach 63.2 % | $\tau$ | $t_{63\%}$ | $t_{63\%} = \tau$ |
| Time to reach 95 % | $\tau$ | $t_{95\%}$ | $t_{95\%} = 3\tau$ |
| Total worst-case error | NL, Hy, R, Drift | $e_{WC}$ | Sum in %FS |
| RSS error | NL, Hy, R, Drift | $e_{RSS}$ | $\sqrt{\sum (\text{component})^2}$ |
| Bandwidth | $\tau$ | $f_c$ | $1/(2\pi\tau)$ |
| Sensitivity with offset | $y_{\min}, y_{\max}, x_{\min}, x_{\max}$ | $K, b$ | $K = \Delta y/\Delta x$; $b = y_{\min} - K x_{\min}$ |
| Reverse bias correction | $V_{\text{meas}}, \text{drift}, \Delta T$ | $V_{\text{true}}$ | $V_{\text{true}} = V_{\text{meas}} \pm \text{drift} \cdot \Delta T$ |

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!NOTE]
> Since the topic concerns *interpretation* of datasheet schematics rather than circuit design, the diagrams below are **functional block-level representations** of common internal sensors as they appear in datasheets. They are not physical drawings but logical architectures.

## 4.1 Generic Internal-Sensor Functional Block Schematic

This is the *universal* block diagram found in nearly every sensor datasheet. KTU frequently asks students to draw or label this structure.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Physical<br/>Measurand<br/>T, P, F, L]:::input --> B[Sensing<br/>Element<br/>Transducer]:::core
    B --> C[Signal<br/>Conditioning<br/>Amplifier Filter]:::core
    C --> D[Linearization<br/>and<br/>Compensation]:::core
    D --> E[Output<br/>Driver<br/>V or mA]:::core
    E --> F[Output<br/>Terminal<br/>to Controller]:::output
    G[Voltage<br/>Regulator<br/>and Protection]:::support --> B
    G --> C
    G --> D
    G --> E
    H[Reference<br/>Voltage Source]:::support --> D

    classDef input fill:#dbeafe,stroke:#1e3a8a,stroke-width:2px,color:#0f172a
    classDef core fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#a16207,stroke-width:2px,color:#0f172a
    classDef output fill:#dcfce7,stroke:#166534,stroke-width:2px,color:#0f172a
    classDef support fill:#f3e8ff,stroke:#6b21a8,stroke-width:2px,color:#0f172a
```

**Reading the diagram (KTU interpretation):**

| Block | Function | Datasheet Section |
|-------|----------|-------------------|
| Physical Measurand | Real-world variable to be measured | Application notes |
| Sensing Element | Converts physical quantity → electrical signal (e.g., strain gauge, thermistor) | Operating principle |
| Signal Conditioning | Amplification, filtering, bridge completion | Electrical characteristics |
| Linearization & Compensation | Corrects non-linearity and temperature drift | Performance table |
| Output Driver | Converts to industry-standard signal (0–10 V, 4–20 mA, I²C, SPI) | Output specification |
| Voltage Regulator | Provides stable internal supply; ESD/over-voltage protection | Absolute max ratings |
| Reference Voltage | Used for ADC and ratiometric measurements | Electrical characteristics |

## 4.2 LM35 Precision Centigrade Temperature Sensor — Datasheet Block

The LM35 (Texas Instruments) is the **most-frequently quoted** sensor in KTU papers. Its internal block schematic has three functional stages.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph LM35_Internal [LM35 INTERNAL BLOCK SCHEMATIC]
        direction TB
        BE[Bandgap<br/>Reference<br/>Cell] --> A1[Buffer<br/>Amplifier]
        A1 --> OUT[Output<br/>Pin Vout<br/>Pin 2]
    end
    PS[Supply<br/>+4V to +30V<br/>Pin 1] --> BE
    GND[Ground<br/>Pin 3] --> LM35_Internal

```

**Key datasheet parameters for LM35:**

| Parameter | Value |
|-----------|-------|
| Sensitivity | 10 mV/°C (linear) |
| Range | -55 °C to +150 °C |
| Supply V<sub>CC</sub> | 4 V to 30 V (basic), 4 V to 20 V (low-voltage) |
| Accuracy | ± 0.5 °C (typical, calibrated) |
| Output at 25 °C | 250 mV |
| Self-heating | 0.08 °C (still air) |
| Quiescent current | 60 µA typical |
| Package | TO-92, TO-220, SOIC-8 |

**Reading the transfer function directly from the datasheet:**

$$ V_{\text{out}}(\text{mV}) = 10 \cdot T(°\text{C}) $$

For $T = 100\,°\text{C}$: $V_{\text{out}} = 1000$ mV = 1.0 V.
