## 4.3 PT100 RTD — Typical Datasheet Block and Wiring

PT100 = Platinum RTD with nominal 100 Ω at 0 °C. Two-, three-, and four-wire configurations appear in datasheets.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    PT[PT100 RTD<br/>100 ohm at 0 C<br/>Alpha 0.00385]:::rt
    PT --> W1[Lead Wire 1<br/>L1]:::wire
    PT --> W2[Lead Wire 2<br/>L2]:::wire
    PT --> W3[Lead Wire 3<br/>L3]:::wire
    PT --> W4[Lead Wire 4<br/>L4]:::wire
    W1 --> BR[Kelvin Bridge<br/>Measurement<br/>Circuit]:::bridge
    W2 --> BR
    W3 --> BR
    W4 --> BR
    BR --> SIG[Conditioned<br/>Output Vout<br/>or 4-20 mA]:::output

    classDef rt fill:#ffe4e6,stroke:#9f1239,stroke-width:2px,color:#0f172a
    classDef wire fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#a16207,stroke-width:1.5px,color:#0f172a
    classDef bridge fill:#e0e7ff,stroke:#3730a3,stroke-width:2px,color:#0f172a
    classDef output fill:#dcfce7,stroke:#166534,stroke-width:2px,color:#0f172a

    class PT rt
    class W1,W2,W3,W4 wire
    class BR bridge
    class SIG output
```

**Key PT100 datasheet parameters:**

| Parameter | Value |
|-----------|-------|
| Nominal R at 0 °C | 100.00 Ω |
| Temperature coefficient $\alpha$ | 0.00385 Ω/Ω/°C (IEC 60751) |
| Range | -200 °C to +850 °C |
| Class A accuracy | $\pm (0.15 + 0.002 \vert T \vert)$ °C |
| Class B accuracy | $\pm (0.30 + 0.005 \vert T \vert)$ °C |
| Self-heating | 0.4 °C/mW (in still water) |
| Lead configurations | 2-wire, 3-wire, 4-wire |

**Callendar-Van Dusen equation (datasheet-derived transfer):**

For $T \ge 0\,°\text{C}$:

$$ R_T = R_0 \left[1 + A T + B T^2\right] $$

For $T < 0\,°\text{C}$ (full form):

$$ R_T = R_0 \left[1 + A T + B T^2 + C (T - 100)\, T^3\right] $$

with standard constants: $A = 3.9083 \times 10^{-3}$, $B = -5.775 \times 10^{-7}$, $C = -4.183 \times 10^{-12}$.

---

## 4.4 Generic 4–20 mA Current-Loop Transmitter — Datasheet Block

This is the industry standard for analog signal transmission. The datasheet always shows a *block schematic* with sensor, signal conditioner, V-to-I converter, and loop supply.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SENSOR[Sensing<br/>Element] --> COND[Signal<br/>Conditioner<br/>and Linearizer] 
    COND --> V2I[Voltage to<br/>Current<br/>Converter]
    V2I --> LOOP[Current Loop<br/>4 to 20 mA<br/>2 Wire]
    LOOP --> RX[Receiver<br/>250 ohm<br/>Shunt]
    RX --> ADC[ADC or<br/>PLC Input]
    VLOOP[Loop Supply<br/>12 to 36 V DC] --> V2I
    VLOOP --> LOOP

    style SENSOR fill:#dbeafe,stroke:#1e3a8a,stroke-width:2px
    style COND fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#a16207,stroke-width:2px
    style V2I fill:#fce7f3,stroke:#9d174d,stroke-width:2px
    style LOOP fill:#dcfce7,stroke:#166534,stroke-width:2px
    style RX fill:#e0e7ff,stroke:#3730a3,stroke-width:2px
    style ADC fill:#ffe4e6,stroke:#9f1239,stroke-width:2px
    style VLOOP fill:#f3e8ff,stroke:#6b21a8,stroke-width:2px
```

**Standard 4–20 mA loop interpretation (the heart of industrial datasheets):**

| Loop Current | Meaning | % of Span |
|--------------|---------|-----------|
| 4 mA | Live Zero (0 % of range) | 0 % |
| 8 mA | 25 % of range | 25 % |
| 12 mA | 50 % of range | 50 % |
| 16 mA | 75 % of range | 75 % |
| 20 mA | Full Scale (100 %) | 100 % |
| < 4 mA | Under-range / fault (NAMUR NE43) | — |
| > 20 mA | Over-range / fault | — |

**Why 4 mA and not 0 mA?** The 4 mA "live zero" allows the loop to power a 2-wire transmitter from the signal current itself, and lets < 4 mA be unambiguously detected as a *broken wire* fault.

---

## 4.5 Strain-Gauge Load Cell — Wheatstone Bridge Datasheet Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph BRIDGE [Wheatstone Bridge]
        R1[R1]:::r --- N1[Node A]:::node
        R2[R2]:::r --- N1
        N1 --- N2[Node B]:::node
        R3[R3]:::r --- N2
        R4[R4 Active<br/>Strain Gauge]:::rgauge --- N2
    end
    VIN[Excitation<br/>Voltage<br/>5 V to 10 V DC]:::vin --- R1
    VIN --- R4
    N1 --- AMP[Instrumentation<br/>Amplifier]:::amp
    N2 --- AMP
    AMP --> FILTER[Low Pass<br/>Filter]:::filter
    FILTER --> OUT[Vout<br/>mV per V per unit strain]:::out

    classDef r fill:#fef9c3,stroke:#854d0e,stroke-width:1.5px
    classDef rgauge fill:#fecaca,stroke:#b91c1c,stroke-width:2.5px
    classDef node fill:#1f2937,stroke:#0f172a,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef vin fill:#dbeafe,stroke:#1e3a8a,stroke-width:2px
    classDef amp fill:#dcfce7,stroke:#166534,stroke-width:2px
    classDef filter fill:#e0e7ff,stroke:#3730a3,stroke-width:2px
    classDef out fill:#f3e8ff,stroke:#6b21a8,stroke-width:2px
```

**Key load-cell datasheet parameters:**

| Parameter | Typical Value |
|-----------|---------------|
| Capacity (FS) | 10 kg, 50 kg, 1000 kg |
| Output at FS (sensitivity) | 2 mV/V nominal |
| Excitation | 5 V to 15 V DC |
| Combined error | 0.02 % to 0.1 % FS |
| Zero balance | $\pm$ 1 % FS |
| Creep (30 min) | $\pm$ 0.05 % FS |
| Temperature effect on zero | 0.002 % FS/°C |
| Input resistance | 400 $\Omega \pm 20$ |
| Output resistance | 350 $\Omega \pm 5$ |
| Protection | IP67 (typical) |

**Output voltage calculation from datasheet (worked formula):**

$$ V_{\text{out}} = V_{\text{exc}} \times S \times \frac{W}{W_{\text{FS}}} $$

where $V_{\text{exc}}$ = excitation, $S$ = sensitivity (mV/V), $W$ = applied load, $W_{\text{FS}}$ = full-scale capacity.

> Example: 100 kg FS, 2 mV/V sensitivity, 10 V excitation, 50 kg load:
> $$ V_{\text{out}} = 10 \times 0.002 \times (50/100) = 0.010 \times 0.5 = 0.005\,\text{V} = 5\,\text{mV} $$

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All questions below strictly follow the **KTU 2024 Scheme** pattern: Part A (3-mark short answer) and Part B (14-mark with internal choice, sub-parts of 7 + 7 marks). Model answers include every step of the calculation.

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 (Model)]
**With reference to a sensor datasheet, differentiate between *accuracy*, *precision*, *resolution*, and *sensitivity*. List the units in which each is typically expressed.**

**Model Answer:**

| Term | Definition | Typical Unit |
|------|------------|--------------|
| **Accuracy** | Closeness of measured value to the true value | % FS, ±°C, ± mV |
| **Precision** | Closeness of repeated measurements to each other (repeatability) | % FS, ± 2σ |
| **Resolution** | Smallest input change producing a detectable output change | input unit, LSB |
| **Sensitivity** | Slope of the transfer function (Δy/Δx) | V/°C, mV/kPa, mV/V |

> **Exam Tip (3-mark scoring):** Defining all four terms correctly = 2 marks; listing units correctly = 1 mark. Add one example (e.g., LM35) to be safe.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024 (Model)]
**Explain the significance of "4 mA live zero" in a 4–20 mA current-loop transmitter datasheet. Why is the loop not designed as 0–20 mA?**

**Model Answer:**

1. The 4 mA is the *live zero* — it provides power to operate the transmitter's internal electronics from the loop current itself, enabling the 2-wire operation.
2. The 4 mA offset lets the receiver detect a *broken wire* (current < 4 mA) as a fault condition, per **NAMUR NE43** recommendation.
3. A 0–20 mA loop would be unable to distinguish between "0 % measurement" and "no signal / broken wire."
4. Additional benefits: power harvesting, loop integrity check, and inherent short-circuit detection. (Any 3 points = 3 marks)

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions with Internal Choice (14 Marks)

> [!NOTE]
> KTU Module-1 style: each long answer has internal choice. **Both alternatives are given in full** with complete model solutions. Pick whichever carries more marks you can defend.

---

### QUESTION A — Set 1 (14 Marks)

> **Q A(a).** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]
> A temperature sensor datasheet specifies: output = 10 mV/°C, range = 0–150 °C, supply = +5 V, non-linearity = ± 0.5 %FS, hysteresis = ± 0.2 %FS, repeatability = ± 0.1 %FS, zero drift = 0.05 %FS/°C. **(7 marks)**

> **(i)** Find the transfer function and the output at 75 °C.
> **(ii)** Calculate the worst-case total error in °C at the operating temperature of 50 °C.

**Model Solution — Part A(a)(i): Transfer Function & Output at 75 °C**

**Step 1 — Sensitivity from datasheet:**

$$ K = 10\,\text{mV/°C} $$

**Step 2 — Transfer function (assuming zero offset, since not mentioned, so $b = 0$):**

$$ V_{\text{out}}(\text{mV}) = 10 \cdot T(°\text{C}) $$

**Step 3 — Output at 75 °C:**

$$ V_{\text{out}} = 10 \times 75 = 750\,\text{mV} = 0.75\,\text{V} $$

**Model Solution — Part A(a)(ii): Worst-Case Error in °C at 50 °C**

**Step 1 — Full-scale output:**

$$ y_{FSO} = 10\,\text{mV/°C} \times 150\,°\text{C} = 1500\,\text{mV} $$

**Step 2 — Sum of static errors (in %FS):**

$$ e_{\text{static}} = 0.5 + 0.2 + 0.1 = 0.8\,\%\,\text{FS} $$

**Step 3 — Convert to mV:**

$$ e_{\text{static,mV}} = \frac{0.8}{100} \times 1500 = 12\,\text{mV} $$

**Step 4 — Thermal drift from 25 °C reference (assume 25 °C as cal. ref.):**

$$ \Delta T = 50 - 25 = 25\,°\text{C} $$

$$ e_{\text{drift}} = 0.05 \times 25 = 1.25\,\%\,\text{FS} $$

In mV: $\dfrac{1.25}{100} \times 1500 = 18.75$ mV

**Step 5 — Total worst-case error (mV):**

$$ e_{\text{total}} = 12 + 18.75 = 30.75\,\text{mV} $$

**Step 6 — Convert to °C using sensitivity:**

$$ e_{°C} = \frac{30.75\,\text{mV}}{10\,\text{mV/°C}} = 3.075\,°\text{C} \approx \pm 3.08\,°\text{C} $$

**Final Answer:** Transfer function $V_{\text{out}} = 10\,T$ mV, output at 75 °C is **750 mV**, and the worst-case error at 50 °C is **± 3.08 °C**. ✅

---

> **Q A(b).** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]
> A pressure transmitter with range 0–10 bar and 4–20 mA output is calibrated with a 3-point test: at 0 bar → 4.0 mA, at 5 bar → 11.9 mA, at 10 bar → 20.0 mA. **(7 marks)**

> **(i)** Calculate the sensitivity, the offset, and the transfer function.
> **(ii)** Find the non-linearity in %FS and the maximum possible pressure error in mbar.

**Model Solution — Part A(b)(i): Sensitivity, Offset, Transfer Function**

**Step 1 — Sensitivity from end points (best-fit line):**

$$ K = \frac{20.0 - 4.0}{10 - 0} = \frac{16}{10} = 1.6\,\text{mA/bar} $$

**Step 2 — Offset using the 0 bar point:**

$$ b = y_1 - K x_1 = 4.0 - (1.6)(0) = 4.0\,\text{mA} $$

**Step 3 — Transfer function:**

$$ y(\text{mA}) = 1.6 \cdot P(\text{bar}) + 4.0 $$

**Model Solution — Part A(b)(ii): Non-linearity and Maximum Error**

**Step 1 — Predicted output at 5 bar (mid-point) using the best-fit line:**

$$ y_{\text{ideal}}(5) = 1.6 \times 5 + 4.0 = 8.0 + 4.0 = 12.0\,\text{mA} $$

**Step 2 — Actual output at 5 bar (from datasheet):** 11.9 mA

**Step 3 — Deviation (Δy):**

$$ \Delta y = 12.0 - 11.9 = 0.1\,\text{mA} $$

**Step 4 — Full-scale output in mA:**

$$ y_{FSO} = 20 - 4 = 16\,\text{mA} $$

**Step 5 — Non-linearity in %FS:**

$$ \text{NL} = \frac{0.1}{16} \times 100 = 0.625\,\%\,\text{FS} $$

**Step 6 — Maximum possible pressure error (reverse calculation):**

$$ \Delta P = \frac{\Delta y}{K} = \frac{0.1\,\text{mA}}{1.6\,\text{mA/bar}} = 0.0625\,\text{bar} = 62.5\,\text{mbar} $$

**Final Answer:** $K = 1.6$ mA/bar, $b = 4$ mA, transfer function $y = 1.6P + 4$; non-linearity = **0.625 %FS**, max pressure error = **62.5 mbar**. ✅

---

### QUESTION B — Alternative Choice (14 Marks)

> **Q B(a).** [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Modified]
> A thermistor datasheet specifies: $R_{25} = 10\,\text{k}\Omega$, $\beta = 3950$ K. Calculate: **(7 marks)**

> **(i)** The resistance at 80 °C.
> **(ii)** The resistance at -10 °C, and comment on the sensor's behaviour.

**Model Solution — Part B(a)(i): Resistance at 80 °C**

**Step 1 — Convert temperatures to Kelvin:**

$$ T_{25} = 25 + 273.15 = 298.15\,\text{K} $$

$$ T_{80} = 80 + 273.15 = 353.15\,\text{K} $$

**Step 2 — Apply the β-parameter (Steinhart-Hart simplified) equation:**

$$ R_T = R_{25} \cdot \exp\!\left[\beta \left(\frac{1}{T} - \frac{1}{T_{25}}\right)\right] $$

$$ \frac{1}{T} - \frac{1}{T_{25}} = \frac{1}{353.15} - \frac{1}{298.15} $$

$$ = 2.8316 \times 10^{-3} - 3.3539 \times 10^{-3} = -5.2223 \times 10^{-4}\,\text{K}^{-1} $$

**Step 3 — Exponent:**

$$ \beta \times (-5.2223 \times 10^{-4}) = 3950 \times (-5.2223 \times 10^{-4}) = -2.0628 $$

**Step 4 — Exponential:**

$$ e^{-2.0628} \approx 0.1270 $$

**Step 5 — Final resistance:**

$$ R_{80} = 10\,000 \times 0.1270 = 1270\,\Omega \approx 1.27\,\text{k}\Omega $$

**Model Solution — Part B(a)(ii): Resistance at -10 °C**

**Step 1 — Convert to Kelvin:**

$$ T_{-10} = -10 + 273.15 = 263.15\,\text{K} $$

**Step 2 — Inverse-difference term:**

$$ \frac{1}{263.15} - \frac{1}{298.15} = 3.8001 \times 10^{-3} - 3.3539 \times 10^{-3} = 4.4615 \times 10^{-4}\,\text{K}^{-1} $$

**Step 3 — Exponent:**

$$ 3950 \times 4.4615 \times 10^{-4} = 1.7623 $$

**Step 4 — Exponential:**

$$ e^{1.7623} \approx 5.825 $$

**Step 5 — Final resistance:**

$$ R_{-10} = 10\,000 \times 5.825 = 58\,250\,\Omega \approx 58.25\,\text{k}\Omega $$

**Comment (1 mark):** The thermistor exhibits **negative temperature coefficient (NTC)** behaviour — resistance decreases as temperature rises. The relationship is highly non-linear (exponential), requiring linearization (e.g., parallel resistor or Steinhart-Hart equation) for precision applications. ✅

---

> **Q B(b).** [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Modified]
> A load cell datasheet specifies: capacity 200 kg, sensitivity 2 mV/V, excitation 10 V, non-linearity 0.05 %FS, hysteresis 0.03 %FS, repeatability 0.02 %FS, zero balance ± 1 %FS, creep (30 min) 0.05 %FS. The cell is used in a weighing scale. **(7 marks)**

> **(i)** Find the output voltage at full scale and at 75 kg load (assume zero balance is zero for simplicity).
> **(ii)** Calculate the worst-case combined error in grams.

**Model Solution — Part B(b)(i): Output Voltage at FS and at 75 kg**

**Step 1 — Output at full scale (200 kg):**

$$ V_{\text{out,FS}} = V_{\text{exc}} \times S = 10\,\text{V} \times 2\,\text{mV/V} = 20\,\text{mV} $$

**Step 2 — Output at 75 kg (linear assumption):**

$$ V_{\text{out}}(75) = 20\,\text{mV} \times \frac{75}{200} = 20 \times 0.375 = 7.5\,\text{mV} $$

**Model Solution — Part B(b)(ii): Worst-Case Combined Error in Grams**

**Step 1 — Sum of error components in %FS:**

$$ e_{\text{WC}} = 0.05 + 0.03 + 0.02 + 0.05 = 0.15\,\%\,\text{FS} $$

(Ignoring zero balance as instructed.)

**Step 2 — Convert to kg:**

$$ e_{\text{abs}} = \frac{0.15}{100} \times 200\,\text{kg} = 0.30\,\text{kg} = 300\,\text{g} $$

**Step 3 — Justification (KTU expects an interpretation):** A worst-case weighing error of ± 300 g on a 200 kg capacity scale is equivalent to ± 0.15 % of reading, acceptable for commercial/industrial weighing (typically ≤ 0.1 % for trade-approved scales) but tight for pharmaceutical/precise batching. ✅

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning & Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students most commonly lose marks in datasheet-based questions (Module 1):**
>
> 1. **Confusing %FS with absolute values.** If FS = 10 bar, ± 0.5 %FS = ± 0.05 bar, NOT ± 0.5 bar. Marks lost: 1–2 per question.
> 2. **Forgetting to include the offset $b$.** For 4–20 mA loops, $b = 4$ mA must be added; students often write $y = Kx$ only.
> 3. **Mixing up operating limits and absolute maximum.** A V<sub>CC,max</sub> of 30 V is the *destruction* limit, not the *operating* range.
> 4. **Unit errors.** Sensitivity 10 mV/°C must be used as 0.01 V/°C when output is in volts — keep dimensions consistent.
> 5. **Linearizing a non-linear sensor incorrectly.** A thermistor's β-equation is *exponential*, not linear. Forcing $y = Kx + b$ on it loses all marks.
> 6. **Not reading the question carefully.** "Find the *output*" and "Find the *input* that produces …" require different rearrangements of the same equation.
> 7. **Skipping the schematic label question.** KTU frequently shows a sensor block diagram with empty boxes and asks students to fill them. Always mention *every* block (sensor, amplifier, filter, ADC, MCU) with arrow directions.
> 8. **RSS vs worst-case confusion.** Worst-case = sum (always safe upper bound); RSS = statistical estimate (valid only for independent errors). State the assumption.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> Read this section *the night before* the exam. It is the consolidated high-yield summary of this entire Module-1 topic.

**A. Datasheet Anatomy (must-know sections)**
- ☐ General description and operating principle
- ☐ Block schematic / functional diagram
- ☐ Absolute maximum ratings
- ☐ Recommended operating conditions
- ☐ Electrical characteristics (static)
- ☐ Dynamic characteristics
- ☐ Environmental/mechanical
- ☐ Connection/wiring diagram

**B. Core Static Performance Parameters**
- ☐ **Span / Full Scale (FS)** = $y_{\max} - y_{\min}$
- ☐ **Sensitivity** $K = \Delta y / \Delta x$
- ☐ **Transfer function** $y = Kx + b$ (linear sensor)
- ☐ **Linearity** = max deviation / FSO × 100 %
- ☐ **Hysteresis** = max $\vert y_{\uparrow} - y_{\downarrow}\vert$ / FSO × 100 %
- ☐ **Repeatability** = closeness of repeated readings (often 2σ)
- ☐ **Accuracy** = total error / FSO × 100 % (or ±X units)
- ☐ **Resolution** = smallest detectable Δx; for ADC = FS/2<sup>N</sup>
- ☐ **Drift** = slow change with time or temperature (TC in unit/°C)

**C. Core Dynamic Performance Parameters**
- ☐ **Time constant** $\tau = C / (hA)$
- ☐ **Step response** $y(t) = KA(1 - e^{-t/\tau})$
- ☐ At $t = \tau$: $y = 0.632\,y_{\infty}$ **(63.2 % rule)**
- ☐ At $t = 3\tau$: $y = 0.950\,y_{\infty}$ **(95 % settling)**
- ☐ At $t = 5\tau$: $y = 0.993\,y_{\infty}$ **(practical steady state)**
- ☐ **Bandwidth** $f_c = 1 / (2\pi \tau)$

**D. Numerical-Problem Formulas (Memorize)**
- ☐ Sensitivity: $K = (y_2 - y_1)/(x_2 - x_1)$
- ☐ Output: $y = Kx + b$
- ☐ Input: $x = (y - b)/K$
- ☐ %FS to absolute: $\text{Abs} = (\% \text{FS}/100) \times \text{FS}$
- ☐ Resolution: $\text{Res} = \text{FS} / 2^{N}$
- ☐ Worst-case error: $e_{WC} = \sum \text{components}$ (%FS)
- ☐ RSS error: $e_{RSS} = \sqrt{\sum (\text{components})^2}$ (%FS)
- ☐ Thermistor: $R_T = R_{25} \exp[\beta(1/T - 1/T_{25})]$
- ☐ PT100: $R_T = R_0[1 + AT + BT^2]$
- ☐ Load cell: $V_{\text{out}} = V_{\text{exc}} \times S \times (W/W_{\text{FS}})$

**E. Block Schematic Order (Universal)**
- ☐ Measurand → **Sensing element** → **Signal conditioner** → **Linearizer** → **Output driver** → **Output terminal**; with parallel **voltage regulator** and **reference** feeding the active stages.

**F. 4–20 mA Loop Rules**
- ☐ Live zero = 4 mA
- ☐ FS = 20 mA
- ☐ Span = 16 mA
- ☐ < 4 mA = broken wire / fault (NAMUR NE43)
- ☐ %FS input = (I − 4)/16 × 100

**G. Common Sensors in KTU Module 1**
- ☐ **LM35** — 10 mV/°C, range −55 to +150 °C
- ☐ **PT100** — 100 Ω at 0 °C, α = 0.00385
- ☐ **Thermistor** — exponential R–T, β ≈ 3950 K
- ☐ **Load cell** — Wheatstone bridge, 2 mV/V typical
- ☐ **Strain gauge** — gauge factor GF = (ΔR/R)/(ΔL/L)

**H. Vocabulary Traps**
- ☐ **Accuracy ≠ Precision** (precision = repeatability)
- ☐ **Sensitivity ≠ Resolution** (slope vs smallest step)
- ☐ **Linearity ≠ Accuracy** (linearity is *one component* of total accuracy)
- ☐ **Span ≠ Range** (range = absolute; span = algebraic difference of outputs)

**I. Exam-Boost Mnemonics**
- ☐ *"S**T**andard **P**erformance **P**arameters"*: **S**ensitivity, **T**ransfer, **P**recision, **P**ercent-FS
- ☐ *Order of block diagram*: **M–S–C–L–O** → **M**easurand, **S**ensor, **C**onditioner, **L**inearizer, **O**utput
- ☐ *4–20 mA fault signs*: **4 - 20 = LIVE**, anything < 4 or > 20 = DEAD (broken or out-of-range)

**J. Pre-Exam Mental Checklist**
- ☐ Did I read the *full* question stem? (look for hidden "input" vs "output" hint)
- ☐ Are my units SI-converted?
- ☐ Did I include the offset (b) for current-loop and differential sensors?
- ☐ Did I justify whether to use sum or RSS for error combination?
- ☐ Did I draw a labelled block diagram (KTU rewards schematics with 2–3 extra marks)?

---

> **End of Module 1 Notes — "Interpreting Typical Manufacturer's Datasheet of Internal Sensors."**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Exteroceptive or External Sensors - Contact Type, Non-contact Type
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBRT303 - SENSORS AND ACTUATORS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Exteroceptive or External Sensors - Contact Type, Non-contact Type

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exteroceptive Sensors (External Sensors)** are a class of measurement transducers designed to acquire information about the **external environment** surrounding a robotic system, automated machine, or mechatronic device. These sensors detect physical phenomena (force, light, sound, electromagnetic radiation, distance, texture) that originate **outside** the host system, enabling the machine to perceive, interpret, and interact with its surroundings.

The prefix *"extero-"* is derived from the Latin word *exterus*, meaning *external* or *outward*. In the KTU 2024 Scheme taxonomy of sensors, exteroceptive sensors stand in direct contrast to **proprioceptive sensors** (which measure internal states like motor shaft position, battery voltage, or joint angles) and **interoceptive sensors** (which monitor the system's own internal health parameters such as temperature and vibration).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Classification Snapshot (Module 2 Focus Area):**
> - **Exteroceptive (External) Sensors** ← *This Module*
> - Proprioceptive (Internal State) Sensors
> - Interoceptive (Self-Monitoring) Sensors

---

## 1.2 The Two-Fold KTU Classification of Exteroceptive Sensors

According to the **physical interaction mechanism** with the target, the KTU 2024 Scheme categorizes exteroceptive sensors into two principal sub-classes:

### A. Contact-Type Exteroceptive Sensors
Contact-type sensors require **direct physical touch** between the sensing element and the object being measured. The transduction mechanism typically relies on:
- Mechanical deformation (strain, pressure)
- Electrical contact closure (switches, conductive elastomers)
- Triboelectric or piezoelectric effects
- Thermal conduction (temperature touch probes)

**Common Examples:** Tactile sensor arrays, limit switches, bump sensors, whisker/fibre-optic contact probes, force-sensitive resistors (FSR), conductive rubber sensors.

### B. Non-Contact-Type Exteroceptive Sensors
Non-contact sensors acquire information about an object **without any physical touch**, using wave propagation (acoustic, optical, electromagnetic) or field interaction. The transduction mechanism is based on:
- Electromagnetic wave reflection/refraction (Light, IR, Laser)
- Acoustic wave time-of-flight (Ultrasonic, Sonar)
- Inductive coupling (Eddy current, Capacitive proximity)
- Magnetic field perturbation (Hall effect proximity)
- Radiometric emission (Thermal IR cameras)

**Common Examples:** Ultrasonic rangefinders, IR proximity sensors, Laser range finders, vision cameras (CCD/CMOS), LiDAR, capacitive proximity switches, inductive proximity sensors.

---

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

> [!TIP]
> **Human Sensory Analogy:** Think of how a blind person navigates a room.
> - **Contact sense** is like using a **white cane** — you must physically tap the floor and walls to know what is there. The cane only "sees" what it touches. *(This is a Contact-Type Sensor)*
> - **Non-contact sense** is like **listening to echoes** (clapping hands and hearing reflections) or **using echolocation** — you can detect objects, walls, and their distance without ever touching them. *(This is a Non-Contact Sensor)*

### Why Two Types Exist — The Engineering Trade-off

| Requirement | Best Sensor Type | Engineering Reason |
|---|---|---|
| Detect a **switched button press** | Contact (limit switch) | Absolute reliability, zero false triggers |
| Measure **distance while moving fast** | Non-contact (ultrasonic/LiDAR) | Avoid collision damage, no wear and tear |
| Measure **surface texture/roughness** | Contact (tactile array) | Requires mechanical coupling to surface |
| Detect **transparent glass** | Non-contact (capacitive/laser) | Optical/ultrasonic may fail, capacitive works |
| **High-speed obstacle avoidance** | Non-contact (IR/Ultrasonic) | Sub-millisecond response, no moving parts |

The choice between contact and non-contact is **not about superiority** but about **application suitability**, response time, and the **physical state** of the target object.

---

## 1.4 Key Physical Constants and Standard Metrics (Bold for Recall)

The following constants and metrics are **routinely used** in KTU numerical problems on this topic:

- **Speed of sound in air at 20°C:** $v_{sound} = 343 \text{ m/s}$
- **Speed of light in vacuum:** $c = 3 \times 10^{8} \text{ m/s}$
- **Speed of light in optical fibre (approx.):** $c_{fibre} \approx 2 \times 10^{8} \text{ m/s}$
- **Standard ultrasonic operating frequency:** $f = 40 \text{ kHz}$
- **Wavelength of 40 kHz ultrasound in air:** $\lambda \approx 8.5 \text{ mm}$
- **Standard IR proximity range:** $2 \text{ cm to } 80 \text{ cm}$
- **Standard LiDAR accuracy:** $\pm 2 \text{ cm at } 25 \text{ m}$

---

## 1.5 Module 2 Positioning in the KTU Syllabus Flow

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Context:** Module 2 of PCBRT303 transitions from internal state monitoring (Module 1: Proprioception) to **environmental perception**. The contact vs. non-contact classification introduced here is a **foundational pillar** that recurs in robotics, autonomous vehicles, industrial automation, and biomedical instrumentation. Every sensor described in this module will be tagged with its sub-class (C for Contact, NC for Non-Contact) in your exam paper.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Contact-Type Exteroceptive Sensors — Theoretical Breakdown

### 2.1.1 Tactile Sensors (Force/Pressure Sensitive)

Tactile sensors convert **mechanical pressure or force** into an electrical signal. They are the robotic equivalent of human skin.

**Working Principle (Step-by-Step):**
1. An external force $F$ is applied normal to the sensor surface.
2. The active sensing material (e.g., conductive polymer, FSR, piezoelectric film, capacitive elastomer) **deforms** mechanically.
3. This deformation alters an internal electrical property — either **resistance** (piezoresistive), **capacitance** (capacitive), **voltage generation** (piezoelectric), or **charge accumulation** (triboelectric).
4. The change is measured, conditioned, and converted to a digital pressure map.

**Common Tactile Transduction Technologies:**

| Technology | Physical Change | Output |
|---|---|---|
| **FSR (Force Sensitive Resistor)** | Resistance $\downarrow$ as force $\uparrow$ | Analog voltage |
| **Piezoelectric** | Generates charge $Q = d \cdot F$ | Voltage pulse |
| **Capacitive** | $C = \varepsilon A/d$ changes with $d$ | Capacitance change |
| **Conductive Elastomer (Quantum Tunneling Composite)** | Resistance $\downarrow$ under compression | Resistance drop |
| **Optical Tactile (GelSight type)** | Visual deformation of gel surface | Image-based force |

**Constitutive Equation for FSR (Highly Tested in KTU):**

The resistance of a typical FSR follows an inverse-power law with applied force:

$$R_{FSR}(F) = R_0 \cdot \left(\frac{F}{F_0}\right)^{-\alpha}$$

Where:
- $R_{FSR}(F)$ = Resistance under applied force $F$ (in Ohms, $\Omega$)
- $R_0$ = Reference resistance at force $F_0$ (in $\Omega$)
- $F$ = Applied normal force (in Newtons, N)
- $F_0$ = Reference force (in N)
- $\alpha$ = Sensitivity exponent (typically $0.5 \le \alpha \le 1.0$)

### 2.1.2 Limit Switches and Bump Sensors

These are **binary (digital)** contact sensors. They change state (ON/OFF) when a mechanical actuator is physically displaced by contact with an obstacle.

- **Limit Switch:** Used for end-of-travel detection. Housed in rugged enclosures for industrial use.
- **Bump Sensor:** Spring-loaded contact ring around a mobile robot's chassis. Closes a circuit upon collision.
- **Whisker Sensor:** Flexible fibre or wire that deflects upon contact; the deflection is detected by a strain gauge or optical encoder.

---

## 2.2 Non-Contact-Type Exteroceptive Sensors — Theoretical Breakdown

### 2.2.1 Ultrasonic Sensors (Time-of-Flight Principle)

The most commonly tested non-contact sensor in KTU papers. It works on the principle of **acoustic time-of-flight (ToF)**.

**Operational Steps:**
1. A piezoelectric transducer emits a **burst of ultrasound** (typically at $f = 40 \text{ kHz}$) into the air.
2. The sound wave propagates through the medium at speed $v_{sound} = 343 \text{ m/s}$ (at 20°C).
3. Upon striking an object, a portion of the wave is **reflected back** (echo) to the receiver.
4. The receiver detects the echo, and the time elapsed $\Delta t$ is measured.
5. Distance is computed using the round-trip time formula (derived in Section 3).

### 2.2.2 Infrared (IR) Proximity Sensors

IR sensors use **triangulation** or **reflected intensity** to estimate distance.

**Reflective IR Sensor (Simple Proximity):**
- An IR LED emits infrared light at $\lambda \approx 940 \text{ nm}$.
- A photodiode (receiver) detects the reflected IR from the target.
- Distance is **inversely proportional to the reflected intensity** (qualitative).

**IR Triangulation Sensor (Quantitative Distance):**
- A laser/IR beam is projected at an angle onto the target.
- The reflected beam strikes a position-sensitive detector (PSD) at a different angle.
- The **position of the spot on the PSD** depends on the target's distance.
- This forms a triangle, hence the name.

### 2.2.3 Vision Sensors (CCD/CMOS Cameras)

A 2D array of photosensitive pixels that convert incident light intensity into electrical charge.

**Key Equation (Pixel Intensity):**

$$I_{pixel} = \int_{\lambda_1}^{\lambda_2} S(\lambda) \cdot R(\lambda) \cdot d\lambda$$

Where $S(\lambda)$ is the spectral power distribution of the scene and $R(\lambda)$ is the sensor responsivity.

### 2.2.4 Capacitive Proximity Sensors

These detect changes in **capacitance** caused by the approach of a target (any material, including non-metals).

$$C = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{d}$$

When target approaches, effective $d$ decreases → $C$ increases → detected.

### 2.2.5 Inductive Proximity Sensors

Detects only **ferrous/metallic** targets via eddy current losses in an LC oscillator. When metal enters the magnetic field, eddy currents are induced, dampening the oscillator amplitude — this triggers the output.

### 2.2.6 LiDAR (Light Detection and Ranging)

Uses pulsed laser beams and measures the time-of-flight of light to construct 2D/3D point clouds of the environment.

---

## 2.3 KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA CHEAT SHEET

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorize this table — it covers ~80% of KTU numerical problems on this topic.**

| # | Formula | Description | Units | Applicable Sensor |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $d = \dfrac{v \cdot \Delta t}{2}$ | Distance from round-trip time-of-flight | m | Ultrasonic, Sonar, LiDAR |
| 2 | $v_{sound} = 331 + 0.6 \cdot T$ | Speed of sound in air at temperature $T$°C | m/s | Ultrasonic (temp correction) |
| 3 | $\Delta t = \dfrac{2d}{v}$ | Time delay for echo return | s | Ultrasonic |
| 4 | $R_{FSR}(F) = R_0 \left(\dfrac{F}{F_0}\right)^{-\alpha}$ | Resistance vs. applied force | $\Omega$ | FSR / Tactile |
| 5 | $C = \dfrac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{d}$ | Parallel-plate capacitance | Farads | Capacitive proximity |
| 6 | $C_{new} = \dfrac{\varepsilon_0 A}{d - \Delta d}$ | Capacitance with target approach | F | Capacitive sensor |
| 7 | $f = \dfrac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{LC}}$ | Resonant frequency of LC tank | Hz | Inductive proximity |
| 8 | $X = \dfrac{f \cdot B}{Z}$ | Camera imaging (pinhole) | m or px | Vision (CCD/CMOS) |
| 9 | $\lambda = \dfrac{v_{sound}}{f}$ | Wavelength of ultrasound | m | Ultrasonic |
| 10 | $I_{reflected} \propto \dfrac{1}{d^2}$ | Inverse-square intensity fall-off | W/m² | IR / Optical (qualitative) |
| 11 | $V_{out} = k \cdot \varepsilon$ | Strain gauge output (Wheatstone) | V | Tactile / Strain |
| 12 | $Q = d \cdot F$ | Piezoelectric charge generation | C | Piezo tactile |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Tip:** The **factor of 2** in Formula #1 is the single most common source of mark deduction. Examiners report that **~30% of students** forget to halve the round-trip distance. **Always halve the product** $v \cdot \Delta t$ because the sound travels *out and back*.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Sensor Type | Industry Application | Why It Is Chosen |
|---|---|---|
| **Tactile (FSR)** | Prosthetic hands, robotic grippers, smartphone touchscreens | Mimics human skin for delicate grasping |
| **Limit Switch** | CNC machine tool end-stops, elevator doors, conveyor gates | Absolute reliability, failsafe operation |
| **Ultrasonic** | Car reversing sensors, drone altitude hold, liquid level in tanks | Cheap, weather-immune, works in dark/fog |
| **IR Proximity** | Mobile robot wall-following, paper-edge detection in printers | Fast response, low power, cheap |
| **Vision (CMOS)** | Autonomous vehicles, pick-and-place, facial recognition, OCR | Rich information, non-contact |
| **Capacitive** | Touchscreens, fuel level gauges, plastic part detection | Detects non-metals (unlike inductive) |
| **LiDAR** | Self-driving cars, terrain mapping, archaeology | Sub-cm accuracy, 360° scanning |
| **Inductive** | Metal object counting, position sensing in CNC | Robust, oil/dirt-immune, contactless metal detection |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Ultrasonic Distance Measurement Formula

### Problem Context
A KTU-class mobile robot uses a forward-mounted ultrasonic transducer (HC-SR04 type) operating at $40 \text{ kHz}$ in air at $20°\text{C}$. An echo is received after $\Delta t = 12 \text{ ms}$ from emission. Calculate the distance to the obstacle.

### Detailed Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Identify the Physical Principle**

The ultrasonic sensor operates on the **time-of-flight (ToF)** principle. The transducer emits a pulse of ultrasound, which travels to the obstacle, reflects, and returns to the receiver. The time taken for the **complete round trip** is measured.

**Step 2 — Set up the Kinematic Equation**

For a wave travelling at constant speed $v$ over a time interval $\Delta t$, the distance covered is:

$$d_{round} = v \cdot \Delta t$$

**Step 3 — Account for the Round Trip**

The measured $\Delta t$ corresponds to the wave travelling **outward** to the obstacle AND **backward** to the receiver. Therefore, the obstacle is only at **half** the round-trip distance:

$$d_{obstacle} = \frac{d_{round}}{2} = \frac{v \cdot \Delta t}{2}$$

**Step 4 — Identify the Wave Speed**

In dry air at standard temperature ($T = 20°\text{C}$), the speed of sound is:

$$v_{sound}(20°C) = 343 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 5 — Convert the Time to SI Units**

$$\Delta t = 12 \text{ ms} = 12 \times 10^{-3} \text{ s} = 0.012 \text{ s}$$

**Step 6 — Substitute into the Derived Formula**

$$d_{obstacle} = \frac{343 \times 0.012}{2}$$

**Step 7 — Perform the Arithmetic**

$$343 \times 0.012 = 4.116 \text{ m}$$

$$d_{obstacle} = \frac{4.116}{2} = 2.058 \text{ m}$$

**Step 8 — Final Result with Significant Figures**

$$\boxed{d_{obstacle} \approx 2.06 \text{ m}}$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Speed of Sound with Temperature Correction

The speed of sound in air is **not a constant** — it varies with temperature because the bulk modulus and density of air both change.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Start with the Thermodynamic Relation**

For an ideal gas, the speed of sound is given by:

$$v_{sound} = \sqrt{\frac{\gamma \cdot R \cdot T_{abs}}{M}}$$

Where:
- $\gamma$ = Adiabatic index (ratio of specific heats) = 1.4 for air
- $R$ = Universal gas constant = $8.314 \text{ J/(mol·K)}$
- $T_{abs}$ = Absolute temperature in Kelvin
- $M$ = Molar mass of air = $0.029 \text{ kg/mol}$

**Step 2 — Convert Temperature to Kelvin**

$$T_{abs} = T_{°C} + 273.15$$

**Step 3 — Linearize Around Reference Temperature (20°C)**

It can be shown empirically (and verified by Taylor expansion of the above) that:

$$v_{sound}(T) \approx 331 \text{ m/s} + 0.6 \cdot T_{°C}$$

**Step 4 — Numerical Example: At $T = 30°C$**

$$v_{sound}(30°C) = 331 + 0.6 \times 30$$

$$v_{sound}(30°C) = 331 + 18 = 349 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 5 — Use the Corrected Value in Distance Formula**

If $\Delta t = 10 \text{ ms}$ at 30°C:

$$d = \frac{349 \times 0.010}{2} = \frac{3.49}{2} = 1.745 \text{ m}$$

Compare to assuming $v = 343$ m/s:

$$d_{wrong} = \frac{343 \times 0.010}{2} = 1.715 \text{ m}$$

$$\text{Error} = \frac{1.745 - 1.715}{1.745} \times 100\% \approx 1.72\%$$

This $1.72\%$ error is why **temperature correction matters** in precision applications.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Capacitive Proximity Sensor — Distance Computation

### Problem
A capacitive proximity sensor has plate area $A = 10 \text{ cm}^2 = 10 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2$. The dielectric is air ($\varepsilon_r = 1$). The initial plate separation is $d_0 = 5 \text{ mm}$. A target object approaches, reducing the gap to $d = 3 \text{ mm}$. Calculate the change in capacitance.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Initial Capacitance**

$$C_0 = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{d_0}$$

Where $\varepsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12} \text{ F/m}$.

Substituting values:

$$C_0 = \frac{(8.854 \times 10^{-12}) \times 1 \times (10 \times 10^{-4})}{5 \times 10^{-3}}$$

$$C_0 = \frac{8.854 \times 10^{-15}}{5 \times 10^{-3}} = 1.7708 \times 10^{-12} \text{ F} = 1.77 \text{ pF}$$

**Step 2 — New Capacitance (after target approach)**

$$C_{new} = \frac{\varepsilon_0 A}{d_{new}} = \frac{8.854 \times 10^{-12} \times 10 \times 10^{-4}}{3 \times 10^{-3}}$$

$$C_{new} = \frac{8.854 \times 10^{-15}}{3 \times 10^{-3}} = 2.951 \times 10^{-12} \text{ F} = 2.95 \text{ pF}$$

**Step 3 — Change in Capacitance**

$$\Delta C = C_{new} - C_0 = 2.95 - 1.77 = 1.18 \text{ pF}$$

**Step 4 — Percentage Change**

$$\% \Delta C = \frac{1.18}{1.77} \times 100\% \approx 66.7\%$$

**Step 5 — Final Result**

$$\boxed{\Delta C = 1.18 \text{ pF} \quad (\text{or } 66.7\% \text{ increase})}$$

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: FSR Resistance — Worked Numerical

### Problem
An FSR sensor has $R_0 = 10 \text{ k}\Omega$ at reference force $F_0 = 1 \text{ N}$ with sensitivity exponent $\alpha = 0.75$. Calculate the resistance when a force of $F = 4 \text{ N}$ is applied.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Identify the Governing Equation**

$$R_{FSR}(F) = R_0 \cdot \left(\frac{F}{F_0}\right)^{-\alpha}$$

**Step 2 — Form the Force Ratio**

$$\frac{F}{F_0} = \frac{4}{1} = 4$$

**Step 3 — Apply the Exponent**

$$\left(\frac{F}{F_0}\right)^{-\alpha} = 4^{-0.75}$$

Computing $4^{-0.75}$:

$$4^{-0.75} = \frac{1}{4^{0.75}} = \frac{1}{4^{3/4}} = \frac{1}{(2^2)^{3/4}} = \frac{1}{2^{3/2}} = \frac{1}{2\sqrt{2}}$$

$$\frac{1}{2\sqrt{2}} = \frac{1}{2.8284} \approx 0.3536$$

**Step 4 — Multiply by $R_0$**

$$R_{FSR}(4N) = 10{,}000 \times 0.3536 = 3536 \text{ }\Omega$$

**Step 5 — Final Result**

$$\boxed{R_{FSR}(4N) \approx 3.54 \text{ k}\Omega}$$

This shows the **inverse-power decrease** — applying 4× the force reduces the resistance to ~35% of the reference.

---

## 3.5 Derivation 5: Piezoelectric Touch Sensor Output

### Problem
A piezoelectric tactile sensor has a charge sensitivity $d = 250 \text{ pC/N}$ (typical for PVDF film). A finger presses with force $F = 2 \text{ N}$. Calculate the generated charge and the open-circuit voltage if the sensor capacitance is $C_p = 1.2 \text{ nF}$.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Charge Generation Equation**

$$Q = d \cdot F$$

**Step 2 — Substitute**

$$Q = (250 \times 10^{-12}) \times 2 = 500 \times 10^{-12} \text{ C} = 500 \text{ pC}$$

**Step 3 — Open-Circuit Voltage**

For a capacitor, $Q = C_p \cdot V$, therefore:

$$V = \frac{Q}{C_p} = \frac{500 \times 10^{-12}}{1.2 \times 10^{-9}}$$

$$V = \frac{500}{1200} = 0.4167 \text{ V}$$

**Step 4 — Final Result**

$$\boxed{Q = 500 \text{ pC}, \quad V_{oc} \approx 417 \text{ mV}}$$

---

## 3.6 Analytical Comparison Matrix: Contact vs. Non-Contact

| Parameter | Contact-Type Sensor | Non-Contact Sensor |
|---|---|---|
| **Physical Interaction** | Required | Not required |
| **Wear and Tear** | Yes (mechanical) | Negligible |
| **Response Speed** | Slower (mechanical inertia) | Very fast (wave propagation) |
| **Range** | Very short (mm) | Short to very long (m to km for LiDAR) |
| **Sensitivity to Surface** | High (texture-dependent) | Variable |
| **Calibration Drift** | Moderate | Low (solid-state) |
| **Cost (basic)** | Very low | Low to high |
| **Examples** | Limit switch, FSR, tactile array | Ultrasonic, IR, Vision, LiDAR |
| **Failure Mode** | Mechanical wear | Lens contamination, ambient interference |
| **Target Material Dependency** | Low | High (e.g., inductive needs metal) |
| **Hazardous Environment Suitability** | Poor (contact risk) | Excellent (sealed, remote) |
| **Data Richness** | Low (1D force/contact) | High (2D image, 3D point cloud) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

## 4.4 Mermaid Block Diagram 4: IR Triangulation Working Principle

```mermaid
graph LR
    LED[IR Laser Diode Emitter] --> BEAM[Beam at Angle Theta1]
    BEAM --> TARGET[Target Object Surface]
    TARGET --> REFL[Reflected Beam at Angle Theta2]
    REFL --> PSD[Position Sensitive Detector PSD]
    PSD --> COND[Signal Conditioning Circuit]
    COND --> PROC[Microcontroller ADC Read]
    PROC --> DISP[Distance d Displayed]

    style LED fill:#FFA500
    style PSD fill:#9370DB
    style DISP fill:#32CD32
```

**Operating Principle of IR Triangulation (Geometric Setup):**

A right triangle is formed by the emitter–target–receiver geometry. The distance $d$ to the target is related to the position $x$ of the spot on the PSD by:

$$d = \frac{f \cdot B}{x}$$

Where:
- $f$ = Focal length of the receiver lens
- $B$ = Baseline distance between emitter and receiver
- $x$ = Spot position on the PSD

As the target moves **closer**, the spot on the PSD moves **further** (or further, depending on geometry), providing a monotonic distance mapping.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Differentiate between contact-type and non-contact-type exteroceptive sensors. Give two examples of each.**

**Model Answer (Mark-Wise Distribution):**

| Mark Component | Content |
|---|---|
| **1 Mark** | Definition: Contact-type sensors require physical touch with the target object for measurement, whereas non-contact-type sensors measure without any physical contact using waves or fields. |
| **1 Mark** | Two examples of **Contact-type**: (i) Tactile sensor (FSR), (ii) Limit switch. |
| **1 Mark** | Two examples of **Non-contact type**: (i) Ultrasonic rangefinder, (ii) IR proximity sensor. |

**Tabular Comparison (Bonus point for full marks):**

| Aspect | Contact Type | Non-Contact Type |
|---|---|---|
| Physical contact | Required | Not required |
| Working principle | Mechanical/electrical change on touch | Wave/field-based transduction |
| Wear | Yes | Negligible |
| Speed | Moderate | High |

---

### Question A2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Explain the working principle of an ultrasonic distance sensor with the help of a suitable equation.**

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> **Working Principle:** An ultrasonic sensor operates on the **Time-of-Flight (ToF)** principle. A piezoelectric transducer emits a **40 kHz** ultrasonic pulse that travels through air, reflects off the target, and returns to the receiver. The time delay $\Delta t$ between emission and echo reception is measured. Distance is computed using:
> 
> $$d = \frac{v \cdot \Delta t}{2}$$
> 
> where $v$ is the speed of sound in air (343 m/s at 20°C). The factor 2 accounts for the **round-trip** travel of the sound wave.

**Mark Distribution:**
- **1 Mark** — Time-of-Flight principle
- **1 Mark** — Distance formula with factor of 2
- **1 Mark** — Identification of speed of sound and units

---

## 5.2 PART B — Full-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

---

### 📝 Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]

**Q. (a)** With the aid of a neat block diagram, explain the working of an **ultrasonic sensor** for distance measurement in a robotic system. Derive the distance formula and discuss the effect of temperature on measurement accuracy. **(7 Marks)**

**Q. (b)** Describe the construction and working of a **piezoelectric tactile sensor**. Derive the relationship between the applied force and the generated charge. List two industrial applications. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Q. (a) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 — Introduction (1 Mark)**
Ultrasonic sensors are non-contact exteroceptive sensors that use acoustic waves above the human hearing range (typically 40 kHz) to determine the distance to an object.

**Step 2 — Block Diagram Description (2 Marks)**
The system consists of:
- **Transmitter (Piezoelectric TX)** — emits ultrasonic pulse
- **Air medium** — wave propagation channel
- **Target object** — reflects the wave
- **Receiver (Piezoelectric RX)** — detects the echo
- **Timer / Microcontroller** — measures time-of-flight $\Delta t$
- **Computation block** — calculates distance

**Step 3 — Derivation of Distance Formula (2 Marks)**

For a round-trip travel:

$$d_{round} = v \cdot \Delta t$$

The actual one-way distance to the object is:

$$d = \frac{v \cdot \Delta t}{2}$$

**Step 4 — Effect of Temperature (2 Marks)**

The speed of sound in air depends on temperature:

$$v_{sound}(T) = 331 + 0.6 \cdot T_{°C} \quad \text{(m/s)}$$

At $T = 20°C$, $v = 343$ m/s. At $T = 40°C$, $v = 355$ m/s. An error in $v$ by $\pm 10$ m/s gives a $\pm 2.9\%$ distance error. For precision applications, temperature compensation is **mandatory**.

---

#### Model Solution for Q. (b) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 — Construction (2 Marks)**
A piezoelectric tactile sensor is constructed from a thin film of piezoelectric material (commonly **PVDF — Polyvinylidene Fluoride**, ~28 μm thick) sandwiched between two metallic electrodes. The whole assembly is mounted on a flexible substrate. Electrical leads are connected to the electrodes for signal extraction.

**Step 2 — Working Principle (2 Marks)**
When an external force $F$ is applied, the piezoelectric film deforms mechanically. This mechanical deformation causes a **re-alignment of dipoles** within the PVDF crystalline structure, generating a **charge $Q$** on the electrodes (**direct piezoelectric effect**). The charge is proportional to the applied force.

**Step 3 — Derivation (2 Marks)**
The constitutive equation of the direct piezoelectric effect is:

$$Q = d \cdot F$$

Where:
- $Q$ = Generated charge (Coulombs)
- $d$ = Piezoelectric charge sensitivity (C/N) — for PVDF, $d \approx 20$ to $30$ pC/N
- $F$ = Applied force (Newtons)

The open-circuit voltage is:

$$V = \frac{Q}{C_p}$$

where $C_p$ is the capacitance of the piezoelectric element.

**Step 4 — Applications (1 Mark)**
1. Robotic gripper fingertip sensing for slip detection.
2. Biomedical pulse/heartbeat monitoring sensors.
3. Keyboard and touch-sensitive input devices.

---

### 📝 Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]

**Q. (a)** Explain the principle of operation of an **IR triangulation sensor** for distance measurement. Derive the geometric relation between target distance and spot position on the PSD. **(7 Marks)**

**Q. (b)** Compare and contrast **capacitive and inductive proximity sensors** based on their working principle, target material suitability, sensing range, and typical applications. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Q. (a) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 — Introduction (1 Mark)**
An IR triangulation sensor is a non-contact exteroceptive sensor that uses the **geometric principle of triangulation** to determine the distance to a target.

**Step 2 — Construction (1 Mark)**
It consists of:
- An **IR laser diode emitter** (or LED) — projects a beam
- A **receiver lens** — focuses the reflected beam
- A **Position Sensitive Detector (PSD)** — a linear photodiode that reports the position of the light spot
- A baseline separation $B$ between emitter and receiver lens

**Step 3 — Working Principle (2 Marks)**
The IR beam strikes the target at an angle and is reflected. The reflected beam enters the receiver lens and is focused onto the PSD. The **position $x$** of the focused spot on the PSD depends on the **target distance $d$** — closer targets produce spots at different positions than farther ones, due to the changing angle of incidence.

**Step 4 — Geometric Derivation (3 Marks)**

Using similar triangles in the optical geometry:

$$\frac{x}{f} = \frac{B}{d}$$

Rearranging:

$$d = \frac{f \cdot B}{x}$$

Where:
- $d$ = Distance to the target
- $f$ = Focal length of the receiver lens
- $B$ = Baseline distance between emitter and receiver
- $x$ = Position of the spot on the PSD (measured from optical axis)

This relation is **monotonically decreasing** in $x$ — as the target moves closer, the spot moves to a different PSD position, providing a one-to-one mapping.

---

#### Model Solution for Q. (b) — 7 Marks

**Comparison Table (7 Marks — 1.75 per row):**

| Parameter | Capacitive Proximity Sensor | Inductive Proximity Sensor |
|---|---|---|
| **Working Principle** | Detects change in capacitance $C = \varepsilon A/d$ as target approaches and alters the electric field between sensor plates. | Detects eddy current losses induced in a metallic target by the sensor's high-frequency electromagnetic field, damping an LC oscillator. |
| **Target Material** | Detects **both metallic and non-metallic** targets (plastic, wood, liquid, glass). | Detects only **ferrous metals** (and limited non-ferrous metals with reduced range). |
| **Sensing Range** | Typically 1 mm to 40 mm. | Typically 1 mm to 50 mm. |
| **Typical Applications** | Liquid level sensing, plastic part detection on conveyor, smartphone touchscreens, paper-edge detection. | CNC tool position sensing, metal object counting, gear tooth detection, end-of-travel limits in machine tools. |

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **🚨 TOP REASONS STUDENTS LOSE MARKS IN THIS TOPIC (Based on KTU Valuation Patterns):**
> 
> 1. **Forgetting the Factor of 2 (Most Common, ~30% of papers):** In the ultrasonic distance formula, students often write $d = v \cdot \Delta t$ instead of $d = v \cdot \Delta t / 2$. This single error **costs 2 to 3 marks** instantly.
> 
> 2. **Confusing Contact and Non-Contact Examples:** Writing "thermocouple" or "RTD" (which are *temperature* sensors, not exteroceptive in the spatial sense) as contact exteroceptive sensors. **Always classify by spatial perception.**
> 
> 3. **Wrong Units in Final Answer:** Computing distance in mm when the formula expects meters, or vice versa. Always convert $\Delta t$ from ms to seconds and length from mm to m **before** substitution.
> 
> 4. **Piezoelectric Confusion:** Confusing the *direct* effect (force → charge, used in sensors) with the *converse* effect (voltage → strain, used in actuators). The exam expects you to state **direct** effect for sensors.
> 
> 5. **Missing Temperature Compensation:** In ultrasonic problems, not mentioning the $v_{sound}(T) = 331 + 0.6T$ correction when the temperature differs from 20°C. Examiners **deduct 1 mark** for ignoring this.
> 
> 6. **No Block Diagram in Long Answers:** For 7-mark questions, a missing block diagram = **loss of 2 marks**. Always draw a labelled functional block diagram.
> 
> 7. **Conflating "Tactile" with "Touchscreen":** Tactile sensors provide *force/pressure* data, not just contact detection. Mentioning both spatial resolution and force sensitivity scores extra credit.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **🚀 RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST — Print This and Glance 5 Minutes Before Exam**

### 🔹 Definitions to Memorize
- ✅ **Exteroceptive Sensor** = Sensor that perceives the **external environment** of a system.
- ✅ **Contact-type** = Requires **physical touch** with the target.
- ✅ **Non-contact type** = Uses **waves/fields**, no physical contact.

### 🔹 Top 5 Contact-Type Sensors (in order of KTU frequency)
1. **Tactile sensor array** (FSR / Piezo / Capacitive)
2. **Limit switch** (binary digital)
3. **Bump sensor** (collision detection)
4. **Whisker sensor** (strain-gauge based deflection)
5. **Force/Torque sensor** (wrist-mounted)

### 🔹 Top 5 Non-Contact Sensors (in order of KTU frequency)
1. **Ultrasonic sensor** (40 kHz, ToF principle)
2. **IR proximity sensor** (intensity or triangulation)
3. **Vision sensor** (CCD/CMOS camera)
4. **Capacitive proximity** (detects everything)
5. **Inductive proximity** (detects metals only)
6. **LiDAR** (laser ToF, 2D/3D)

### 🔹 Formula Shortlist (Tattoo on Your Brain)
- ✅ $d = \dfrac{v \cdot \Delta t}{2}$ — **Ultrasonic / LiDAR distance**
- ✅ $v_{sound}(T) = 331 + 0.6T$ — **Temperature-corrected speed of sound**
- ✅ $R_{FSR}(F) = R_0 \left(\dfrac{F}{F_0}\right)^{-\alpha}$ — **FSR resistance**
- ✅ $C = \dfrac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{d}$ — **Capacitive sensor**
- ✅ $Q = d \cdot F$ — **Piezoelectric charge**
- ✅ $d = \dfrac{f \cdot B}{x}$ — **IR triangulation**

### 🔹 Numerical Constants to Remember
- ✅ $v_{sound}(20°C) = 343 \text{ m/s}$
- ✅ $c = 3 \times 10^8 \text{ m/s}$ (light)
- ✅ $f_{ultrasonic} = 40 \text{ kHz}$
- ✅ $\varepsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12} \text{ F/m}$

### 🔹 Decision Tree — Which Sensor to Choose?
- Need **distance only, cheap** → **Ultrasonic**
- Need **distance + colour/shape info** → **Vision**
- Need to detect **any object regardless of material** → **Capacitive**
- Need to detect **only metals** in harsh environment → **Inductive**
- Need **force/pressure profile** → **Tactile array**
- Need **collision detection** (simple, failsafe) → **Bump / Limit switch**

### 🔹 Common Exam Traps
- ❌ Don't write $d = v \cdot \Delta t$ (forgot the /2)
- ❌ Don't use $v = 340$ or $v = 330$ — use $v = 343$ at 20°C unless temperature is given
- ❌ Don't classify RTD/thermocouple as exteroceptive **spatial** sensors (they measure temperature, not environment geometry)
- ❌ Don't confuse piezoelectric **direct** effect (sensors) with **converse** effect (actuators)

### 🔹 Block Diagrams You Must Be Able to Draw from Memory
1. ✅ Ultrasonic sensor with TX → air → object → RX → amplifier → timer → display
2. ✅ IR triangulation with LED → target → lens → PSD → signal conditioning → MCU
3. ✅ Tactile matrix with surface → 16×16 grid → MUX → ADC → pressure map
4. ✅ Mobile robot with multi-sensor fusion (this is a frequent 14-mark full question)

> 🎯 **Final Exam Tip:** In any 14-mark question, **always start with a labelled block diagram** before any text or formula. This immediately secures 2 to 3 marks and gives structure to the rest of your answer. Examiners love well-organized answers — a diagram-first approach is the **single highest ROI study habit** for KTU exams.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBRT303 - SENSORS AND ACTUATORS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Tactile; Proximity - Detection of Physical Contact or Closeness, Contact Switches, Bumpers, Inductive Proximity, Capacitive Proximity

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Tactile Sensors — The "Sense of Touch" for Machines

A **tactile sensor** is a non-visual sensor that detects physical contact, contact force, pressure, or surface characteristics between the sensor and an object. It functions as the **"skin"** of a machine, analogous to the mechanoreceptors (touch corpuscles) in human skin.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Syllabus-aligned):** A tactile sensor is a device that responds to mechanical stimulation such as pressure, force, vibration, or touch by producing a measurable electrical or optical signal. It converts a *physical interaction* into a *transducible variable* (resistance, capacitance, voltage, or frequency shift).

**Sub-classification (KTU Module-2 framework):**
- **Contact type** — Requires direct mechanical contact (e.g., contact switches, bumpers, micro-switches).
- **Force/Pressure type** — Measures the magnitude of contact force (e.g., piezoresistive, piezoelectric, capacitive tactile arrays).
- **Slip/Torque type** — Detects incipient slip or shear forces on a gripped object.

### Conceptual Analogy
Imagine you, blindfolded, trying to recognize a coin on a table. You use the *moment of contact* (touch), the *pressure* (weight), and the *texture* (surface). That is exactly what tactile sensors give a robot:
- **Contact Switch** = "Did I touch it?" (binary yes/no)
- **Bumper** = "Did I crash into it?" (collision detection with localization)
- **Force/Pressure Array** = "How hard am I gripping it and where is it slipping?" (continuous force feedback)

---

## 1.2 Proximity Sensors — The "Sense of Nearness" Without Touching

A **proximity sensor** is a non-contact device that detects the *presence, absence, or distance* of a target object within a defined sensing zone, without any physical contact. It is the machine's equivalent of the human ability to feel someone's hand approaching your back without them touching you.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus High-Yield Point:** Proximity sensors do NOT require mechanical contact. They use *fields* (electric, magnetic, electromagnetic, light, sound) and detect *disturbances* in those fields caused by the target.

**The two principal proximity-sensing families covered in KTU Module 2 are:**

| Family | Field Used | Detects | Standard Range |
|--------|------------|---------|----------------|
| **Inductive** | Electromagnetic field | Only **ferrous & non-ferrous metals** | **1 mm – 60 mm** |
| **Capacitive** | Electrostatic field | **Metals, plastics, wood, liquids, powders, humans** | **1 mm – 40 mm** |

---

## 1.3 Contact Switches

A **contact switch** (also called a *limit switch*, *microswitch*, or *mechanical contact sensor*) is the simplest and oldest form of tactile sensor. It is a binary (ON/OFF) electromechanical device whose internal contacts physically open or close when an external mechanical force is applied through an *actuator* (lever, plunger, roller, or whisker).

> [!NOTE]
> **Operating Principle:** When an external object physically depresses the actuator → internal metal contacts *make* (NO contact closes) or *break* (NC contact opens) → the circuit changes state → the controller detects a logic-level transition.

**Key Terminology:**
- **NO (Normally Open)** — Current flows only when pressed.
- **NC (Normally Closed)** — Current flows until pressed.
- **COM (Common)** — The shared terminal.
- **Pole & Throw** — SPST, DPDT, etc.

### Real-World Analogy
Think of a doorbell button. You press it → contacts touch → the bell rings. Release → contacts separate → silence. A contact switch is exactly this principle, industrialised and ruggedised.

---

## 1.4 Bumpers

A **bumper** (also known as a *collision sensor* or *whisker sensor* in mobile robotics) is a specialised tactile sensor used to detect *collision events* in mobile robots, AGVs (Automated Guided Vehicles), and robotic arms. It typically consists of a strip of conductive material mounted on a compliant substrate that, on impact, completes a circuit or triggers a switch.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Bumpers vs. Contact Switches:** A *single contact switch* is a point sensor; a *bumper* is a **distributed (line or area) tactile sensor** that tells the controller *where* along the robot's perimeter the collision occurred, enabling intelligent reaction (reverse, turn, re-plan path).

**Common Bumper Constructions:**
1. **Conductive-rubber strip on conductive backing** — pressure short-circuits two layers.
2. **Micro-switch array behind a compliant pad** — multiple microswitches under a foam/rubber skin.
3. **Optical/IR break-beam bumper** — collision interrupts an infrared beam.

---

## 1.5 Inductive Proximity Sensors

An **inductive proximity sensor** (IPS) is a non-contact sensor that detects the presence of a *metallic target* by generating an alternating electromagnetic field via an LC oscillator coil and sensing the **eddy-current losses** induced in the target.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Inductive proximity sensors are **ferrous-metal detectors**. They will NOT detect wood, plastic, water, or humans. They are the workhorse of factory automation for detecting metal parts on conveyors, end-of-stroke on pneumatic cylinders, and tool-position verification on CNC machines.

### Conceptual Analogy
Imagine throwing a stone into a still pond. The water ripples (the oscillating EM field). Now imagine placing a *metal object* near the ripples — it disturbs and dampens them. The sensor's electronics detect this "dampening" and conclude: *a metal object is near.* That disturbance is the **eddy-current loss**.

---

## 1.6 Capacitive Proximity Sensors

A **capacitive proximity sensor** (CPS) is a non-contact sensor that detects the presence of *any material* (metal, plastic, wood, glass, liquid, powder, even a human body) by sensing a change in the **electrical capacitance** between its active electrode and a reference electrode when a target enters the electrostatic field.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Distinction from Inductive:** Capacitive sensors respond to the **dielectric constant (εᵣ)** of the target, not to its conductivity. Because every material has a dielectric constant different from air (ε_air = 1.0006), capacitive sensors are *universal target* sensors — but they require careful *sensitivity adjustment* to avoid false triggering.

### Conceptual Analogy
Think of a capacitor as two metal plates separated by an insulator (air). If you bring your hand close to the plates, your body acts as a new "dielectric" because water in your tissues has εᵣ ≈ 80. The capacitance jumps, the oscillator frequency changes, the trigger circuit fires — and your touchless water tap turns ON. That is a capacitive proximity sensor in action.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operating Principles — Step-by-Step Logic

### 2.1.1 Contact Switch — Logic Chain

1. **Idle state** — Actuator is at rest. Internal contacts hold their default position (NO = open, NC = closed).
2. **External contact** — A target object physically depresses the actuator with a force exceeding the *operating force (F_op)*.
3. **Contact transition** — A spring-loaded mechanism snaps the contacts to the new state (snap-action gives crisp digital output, eliminating contact bounce concerns in quality switches).
4. **Electrical signal** — Voltage across the output terminals changes from HIGH→LOW or LOW→HIGH.
5. **Controller input** — PLC / microcontroller reads the logic level and executes the programmed action.
6. **Release** — When force falls below the *release force (F_rel)*, the actuator returns, restoring the original state.

> **Why snap-action?** It ensures the contacts move *fully* in one motion — preventing the indeterminate "mid-state" where arcing and chattering could occur, which would damage contacts and confuse the controller.

### 2.1.2 Bumper — Distributed Logic

1. Robot moves forward along a planned trajectory.
2. Bumper strip is energised (e.g., pull-up resistor maintains HIGH).
3. On collision → conductive layers touch → current flows → voltage drops to LOW.
4. Multiple parallel segments allow the controller to identify **which zone** was hit (left, right, front-left, etc.).
5. Robot controller executes escape manoeuvre (reverse + rotate away from the impacted zone).

### 2.1.3 Inductive Proximity Sensor — The Eddy-Current Mechanism

1. **Oscillator stage** — An LC tank circuit (colpitts/Hartley oscillator) generates a high-frequency alternating current, typically **100 kHz – 1 MHz**, in a ferrite-cored coil. This produces an alternating electromagnetic field at the sensor's *active face*.
2. **Idle state (no target)** — Oscillation amplitude is stable at a reference level. A detector/demodulator stage reads "no target" and keeps the output transistor OFF (or ON, depending on NO/NC logic).
3. **Target enters field** — Eddy currents are induced in the target's surface (Faraday's Law). These eddy currents *consume energy* from the sensor's field, which appears as a *load* on the LC tank.
4. **Amplitude collapse** — The oscillation amplitude drops sharply once the target is within the **rated operating distance (S_n)**.
5. **Trigger threshold** — A Schmitt-trigger comparator detects the amplitude drop and switches the output stage (transistor) — output goes LOW (NPN sinking) or HIGH (PNP sourcing).
6. **Target leaves** — Amplitude recovers → hysteresis prevents chattering near the boundary.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "1 Sn" vs. "Sr" vs. "Sa" Convention (KTU-Exam Favourite):**
> - **S_n** = *Rated operating distance* (theoretical, measured in ideal lab with a square mild-steel target of side = 1×S_n, thickness = 1 mm).
> - **S_r** = *Real operating distance* (0.81 × S_n typical, the actual useable distance per IEC 60947-5-2).
> - **S_a** = *Assured operating distance* (0 ≤ S_a ≤ 0.81 × S_n — guaranteed boundary within which the sensor *must* trigger).

### 2.1.4 Capacitive Proximity Sensor — The Capacitance-Change Mechanism

1. **Active electrode + reference electrode** form an open-faced capacitor. A relaxation oscillator (typically 50 kHz – 1 MHz) drives the active electrode.
2. **Idle capacitance (C₀)** — Set by the air gap, sensor geometry, and the dielectric constant of air (ε_air ≈ 1.0006).
3. **Target enters field** — Target's dielectric constant εᵣ replaces part of the air dielectric. Since *C ∝ εᵣ*, the capacitance **increases** by ΔC.
4. **Oscillator frequency shift** — The relaxation oscillator's frequency f = 1/(R·C·ln 2) drops as C rises.
5. **Threshold detect** — When f falls below the trip point, the output transistor switches state.
6. **Sensitivity adjustment** — A potentiometer sets the trip point so the sensor ignores background but detects the intended target. **Higher εᵣ → longer sensing range** (water εᵣ ≈ 80 can be detected 5–10× farther than plastic εᵣ ≈ 2–4).

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

### Table 2.A — Contact Switch & Bumper

| Quantity | Symbol | Formula / Value | Unit |
|----------|--------|------------------|------|
| Operating force | $F_{op}$ | 0.1 – 3 N (typical microswitch) | **N** |
| Release force | $F_{rel}$ | 0.05 – 1.5 N | **N** |
| Differential travel (pretravel – overtravel) | $\Delta x$ | 0.4 – 1.6 mm | **mm** |
| Mechanical life | $L_m$ | $10^6 – 10^7$ cycles | **cycles** |
| Bumper contact resistance | $R_c$ | < **5 Ω** (typ.) | **Ω** |
| Response time | $t_r$ | < **5 ms** | **ms** |

### Table 2.B — Inductive Proximity Sensor

| Quantity | Symbol | Formula | Unit |
|----------|--------|---------|------|
| Coil inductance (ideal solenoid) | $L$ | $L = \dfrac{\mu_0 \mu_r N^2 A}{\ell}$ | **H** |
| Resonant frequency of LC tank | $f_0$ | $f_0 = \dfrac{1}{2\pi \sqrt{LC}}$ | **Hz** |
| Induced EMF in target (Faraday) | $\varepsilon$ | $\varepsilon = -N \dfrac{d\Phi_B}{dt}$ | **V** |
| Eddy-current power loss (per unit volume, in target) | $P_e$ | $P_e = \dfrac{\pi^2 B^2 d^2 f^2}{6 \rho}$ | **W/m³** |
| Correction factor (target material) | $K_{mat}$ | 1.0 (steel) / 0.5 (aluminium) / 0.4 (brass) | — |
| Real operating distance | $S_r$ | $S_r = 0.81 \cdot S_n \cdot K_{mat}$ | **mm** |
| Assured operating distance | $S_a$ | $0 \le S_a \le 0.81 \cdot S_n$ | **mm** |
| Hysteresis H (% of S_r) | $H$ | $H = \dfrac{S_r - S_d}{S_r} \cdot 100\%$ (typ. **1–15%**) | **%** |
| Switching frequency | $f_{sw}$ | 100 – 5000 Hz | **Hz** |
| Operating temperature | $T$ | −25 to +85 | **°C** |

### Table 2.C — Capacitive Proximity Sensor

| Quantity | Symbol | Formula | Unit |
|----------|--------|---------|------|
| Capacitance (parallel plate) | $C$ | $C = \dfrac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{d}$ | **F** |
| Capacitance (cylindrical) | $C$ | $C = \dfrac{2\pi\varepsilon_0\varepsilon_r L}{\ln(R_2/R_1)}$ | **F** |
| Vacuum permittivity | $\varepsilon_0$ | $8.854 \times 10^{-12}$ | **F/m** |
| Effective ΔC (with target) | $\Delta C$ | $\Delta C = C_0(\varepsilon_{r,t} - 1)$ | **F** |
| RC oscillator frequency | $f_{osc}$ | $f_{osc} = \dfrac{1}{RC \ln 2}$ | **Hz** |
| Effective sensing range (vs. εᵣ) | $S_{eff}$ | $S_{eff} \propto (\varepsilon_{r,t} - 1)$ | **mm** |
| Dielectric constant of water | $\varepsilon_{r,H_2O}$ | **≈ 80** | — |
| Dielectric constant of typical plastic | $\varepsilon_{r,pl}$ | **2 – 4** | — |
| Dielectric constant of wood (dry) | $\varepsilon_{r,wood}$ | **2 – 6** | — |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard metric you must memorise:** $\varepsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12}$ F/m is the **vacuum permittivity** (also called the *permittivity of free space*). It appears in **every** capacitance-based sensor equation.

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Sensor Type | Real-World Use |
|-------------|----------------|
| **Contact switch** | Elevator door interlocks, washing-machine lid locks, conveyor end-stops, safety gates on machinery. |
| **Bumper** | AGV (Automated Guided Vehicle) perimeter safety, robotic vacuum cleaners (e.g., Roomba), car parking sensors (mechanical), Mars-rover collision avoidance. |
| **Inductive proximity** | CNC tool-presence detection, pneumatic-cylinder end-of-stroke, gear-tooth counting (tachometry), coin validation in vending machines, weld-nut detection on sheet metal. |
| **Capacitive proximity** | Touchless soap dispensers, liquid-level sensing through glass walls, tablet-counting in pharmaceutical packaging, paper-stack-height detection in printers, ice/frost detection on aircraft wings, smartphone touchscreens (projected capacitive). |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Selection Rule (KTU Lab-viva favourite):**
> - Target is **metal only** + clean industrial environment → **Inductive** (cheaper, more repeatable, immune to dust/humidity).
> - Target is **plastic / liquid / organic / human** OR target is **behind a non-metallic wall** → **Capacitive**.
> - Need to detect *collision force / location* on a robot → **Bumper**.
> - Need a *simple, dirt-cheap binary* "is it there?" without electronics → **Contact switch / microswitch**.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Inductive Sensor: Operating Distance Reduction Factor for Non-Ferrous Targets

**Problem statement:** A datasheet specifies a rated operating distance $S_n = 8$ mm for a standard *mild-steel square target*. An aluminium target is to be detected. The correction factor for aluminium is $K_{mat} = 0.5$. Compute the real operating distance $S_r$ for the aluminium target.

**Step 1 — Identify the governing relationship.**
By IEC 60947-5-2 (the *de facto* international standard for proximity sensors), the real operating distance is

$$S_r = 0.81 \cdot S_n \cdot K_{mat}$$

where the 0.81 factor accounts for manufacturing tolerances and the standard 1-mm-thick mild-steel square target of side 1×S_n.

**Step 2 — Substitute the given numerical values.**

$$S_r = 0.81 \times S_n \times K_{mat} = 0.81 \times 8 \text{ mm} \times 0.5$$

**Step 3 — Multiply the scalar factors first.**

$$0.81 \times 0.5 = 0.405$$

**Step 4 — Multiply by the operating distance.**

$$S_r = 0.405 \times 8 \text{ mm} = 3.24 \text{ mm}$$

**Final Answer:**

$$S_r = 3.24 \text{ mm}$$

> **Engineering interpretation:** The aluminium target will only be detected reliably up to 3.24 mm, not 8 mm. If the application requires detection at 5 mm, the engineer must either choose a *larger sensor* (e.g., 12 mm $S_n$) or use a *ferrous target bracket* to mount the aluminium part.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Eddy-Current Loss Density in a Conductive Target

**Problem statement:** A $d = 1$ mm thick steel plate ($\rho = 1.6 \times 10^{-7}$ Ω·m) is exposed to an AC magnetic field of flux density $B = 0.05$ T at $f = 500$ kHz. Compute the eddy-current power loss per unit volume.

**Step 1 — Write the governing equation (for a thin plate where skin depth $\delta \gg d$):**

$$P_e = \frac{\pi^2 B^2 d^2 f^2}{6\rho}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the given values.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_e &= \frac{\pi^2 \times (0.05)^2 \times (1\times 10^{-3})^2 \times (5\times 10^{5})^2}{6 \times 1.6\times 10^{-7}} \\[6pt]
&= \frac{9.8696 \times 2.5\times 10^{-3} \times 1\times 10^{-6} \times 2.5\times 10^{11}}{9.6 \times 10^{-7}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Multiply the numerator terms in sequence.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
9.8696 \times 2.5\times 10^{-3} &= 2.4674 \times 10^{-2} \\
2.4674\times 10^{-2} \times 1\times 10^{-6} &= 2.4674 \times 10^{-8} \\
2.4674\times 10^{-8} \times 2.5\times 10^{11} &= 6.1685 \times 10^{3}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Divide by the denominator.**

$$P_e = \frac{6.1685 \times 10^{3}}{9.6 \times 10^{-7}} = 6.43 \times 10^{9} \text{ W/m}^{3}$$

**Final Answer:**

$$P_e \approx 6.43 \times 10^{9} \text{ W/m}^{3}$$

> **Engineering interpretation:** This enormous volumetric power loss is what *dampens* the LC tank of the inductive sensor, causing the oscillation amplitude to collapse and trigger the output. The equation also reveals *why higher frequencies give longer range*: doubling $f$ quadruples $P_e$, sharply increasing sensitivity.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Capacitive Sensor: Change in Capacitance with a Plastic Target

**Problem statement:** A capacitive proximity sensor has an active face of area $A = 4 \text{ cm}^2 = 4 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2$ and a sensing-gap of $d = 2$ mm. Compute the change in capacitance $\Delta C$ when a plastic target with $\varepsilon_{r,t} = 3.0$ enters the field, compared to free air ($\varepsilon_{r,air} \approx 1$).

**Step 1 — Capacitance in air (no target, baseline):**

$$C_0 = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_{r,air} A}{d} = \frac{8.854\times 10^{-12} \times 1 \times 4\times 10^{-4}}{2\times 10^{-3}}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the numerator.**

$$8.854 \times 10^{-12} \times 4 \times 10^{-4} = 3.5416 \times 10^{-15} \text{ F·m}$$

**Step 3 — Divide by the denominator.**

$$C_0 = \frac{3.5416 \times 10^{-15}}{2 \times 10^{-3}} = 1.7708 \times 10^{-12} \text{ F} \approx 1.77 \text{ pF}$$

**Step 4 — Capacitance with target present:**

$$C_t = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_{r,t} A}{d} = 3.0 \times C_0 = 3.0 \times 1.77 \text{ pF} = 5.31 \text{ pF}$$

**Step 5 — Change in capacitance:**

$$\Delta C = C_t - C_0 = 5.31 - 1.77 = 3.54 \text{ pF}$$

**Final Answer:**

$$\Delta C \approx 3.54 \text{ pF}$$

> **Engineering interpretation:** The oscillator frequency drops in proportion to this ΔC. The threshold detector is set to fire when ΔC exceeds ~3 pF. Plastic, with $\varepsilon_r = 3$, gives a measurable signal; air gives nothing. The sensor's *sensitivity knob* effectively sets the minimum ΔC that will fire the output.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Capacitive Oscillator Frequency and Trigger Threshold

**Problem statement:** Given $R = 10$ kΩ and the baseline capacitance from §3.3 ($C_0 = 1.77$ pF), compute the natural frequency of the relaxation oscillator. Then compute the *new* frequency when the plastic target is present.

**Step 1 — Governing formula:**

$$f_{osc} = \frac{1}{R C \ln 2}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the no-target case.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
f_0 &= \frac{1}{10^4 \times 1.77\times 10^{-12} \times 0.6931} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{1}{1.2268 \times 10^{-8}} \\[4pt]
&= 8.15 \times 10^{7} \text{ Hz} = 81.5 \text{ MHz}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the with-target case.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
f_t &= \frac{1}{10^4 \times 5.31\times 10^{-12} \times 0.6931} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{1}{3.68 \times 10^{-8}} \\[4pt]
&= 2.72 \times 10^{7} \text{ Hz} = 27.2 \text{ MHz}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Frequency shift (the "signal"):**

$$\Delta f = f_0 - f_t = 81.5 - 27.2 = 54.3 \text{ MHz}$$

> **Engineering interpretation:** A *huge* frequency shift (54 MHz) is produced by a tiny capacitance change (3.54 pF). The trigger threshold is set anywhere within this range; e.g., setting it at 60 MHz means the output fires when frequency drops below 60 MHz, giving a precise detection event.

---

## 3.5 Comparison Matrix — Inductive vs. Capacitive (Most Important KTU Table)

| Parameter | **Inductive Proximity** | **Capacitive Proximity** |
|-----------|--------------------------|----------------------------|
| Operating principle | Eddy-current loss in target | Change in capacitance |
| Field type | Electromagnetic (H-field) | Electrostatic (E-field) |
| Detects | **Only metals** (ferrous best) | **All materials** (metals, plastics, liquids) |
| Typical range | **1 – 60 mm** | **1 – 40 mm** |
| Sensitivity to εᵣ | None | Strong (proportional) |
| Sensitivity to conductivity | Strong | Weak |
| Sealed against dust/liquid | Easy (no front opening) | Difficult (front is open to field) |
| Cost | Lower | Higher (more complex compensation) |
| Mounting — flush allowed? | Yes (most models) | Often not (requires side-clearance) |
| Hysteresis | **1 – 15%** of S_r | **1 – 20%** of S_r |
| Switching frequency | Up to **5 kHz** | Up to **100 Hz** (slower) |
| Output stages | NPN / PNP / NO / NC | Same |
| IP rating (typical) | **IP67** | **IP65** (because of open face) |
| Typical KTU application | Pneumatic-cylinder end stroke | Liquid-level through glass wall |

---

## 3.6 Comparison Matrix — Contact Switch vs. Bumper

| Parameter | **Contact Switch** | **Bumper** |
|-----------|--------------------|--------------|
| Sensing geometry | **Point** (1 contact location) | **Line / Area** (distributed) |
| Output type | Binary (mechanical) | Binary (multi-zone) |
| Spatial resolution | None | Yes (left/centre/right) |
| Cost | Very low (₹10 – ₹100) | Moderate (₹200 – ₹2000) |
| Mechanical life | $10^6 – 10^7$ cycles | $10^5 – 10^6$ impacts |
| Typical use | Door interlocks, end-stops | Mobile-robot collision avoidance |
| Requires electronics? | No (pure switch) | Usually requires pull-up resistors |
| Hysteresis (pretravel vs release) | Built into spring design | Defined by elastomer properties |
