# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question A1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Differentiate between tactile sensors and proximity sensors with one example each.**

**Model Answer:**

A **tactile sensor** requires **physical contact** between the sensor and the target to produce a measurable response. The simplest example is a *microswitch / contact switch* whose contacts close only when mechanically pressed.

A **proximity sensor** detects the **presence of a target without any physical contact**, by sensing changes in an electromagnetic or electrostatic field. Examples include the *inductive proximity sensor* (detects metal via eddy currents) and the *capacitive proximity sensor* (detects any material via change in capacitance).

> **Key contrast:** Tactile = needs *touch*; Proximity = *touchless*. Tactile gives collision/force information; Proximity gives presence/absence within a defined sensing range.

---

### **Question A2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Why does a capacitive proximity sensor require a sensitivity adjustment, but an inductive proximity sensor generally does not?**

**Model Answer:**

A **capacitive sensor** must distinguish between many different *target dielectric constants* (εᵣ ranging from ~2 for plastic to ~80 for water) and may also be influenced by environmental factors such as humidity, mounting surface, and nearby objects. The sensitivity potentiometer is used to **calibrate the trip threshold** so the sensor ignores the background (mounting plate, condensation) and fires only on the intended target.

An **inductive sensor**, on the other hand, is *only* affected by conductive (metallic) targets. Since the target set is restricted to metals and the sensor face can be fully sealed in metal and resin, **environmental influence is negligible** — the factory-calibrated threshold works across all standard metal targets, and correction factors (e.g., $K_{mat} = 0.5$ for aluminium) are baked into the datasheet rather than needing a knob.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice Pattern

> **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern:** Module-internal choice. Student answers **either** Question A **or** Question B, with sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks. Provide full step-by-step model solutions.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**With a neat block diagram, explain the construction and working of an inductive proximity sensor. State any four industrial applications.**

**Model Solution:**

**1. Construction (Block diagram elements):**
- **Oscillator stage** — Colpitts or Hartley LC oscillator generating a high-frequency signal (typically 100 kHz – 1 MHz).
- **Sensing coil** — Wound on a ferrite core, open on the active face; produces an alternating electromagnetic field.
- **Detector / Demodulator stage** — Rectifies the oscillator amplitude to a DC level.
- **Schmitt trigger** — Compares the rectified level to a fixed reference; provides a clean, bounce-free digital transition.
- **Output stage** — NPN sinking or PNP sourcing transistor (often with short-circuit and reverse-polarity protection).
- **Power supply** — 10–30 V DC, two-wire or three-wire configuration.

**2. Working Principle:**
When a **metallic target** approaches the active face, eddy currents are induced in the target surface (Faraday's Law). These eddy currents draw energy from the sensor's field, **damping the LC oscillator's amplitude**. The detector circuit senses this amplitude drop. When the target is closer than the rated operating distance $S_n$, the amplitude falls below the Schmitt trigger threshold, switching the output transistor (and hence the load).

**3. Four Industrial Applications:**
1. End-of-stroke detection on pneumatic and hydraulic cylinders.
2. Tool-presence verification in CNC machining centres.
3. Gear-tooth counting and speed measurement (tachometry).
4. Detection of metal parts on conveyor belts in assembly lines.
5. (Bonus) Coin validation in vending machines; weld-nut presence on sheet-metal assemblies.

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**A proximity sensor has rated operating distance $S_n = 10$ mm. The target material has a correction factor $K_{mat} = 0.4$. Compute the real operating distance. If the hysteresis is 10% of $S_r$, compute the release distance $S_d$.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Real operating distance (per IEC 60947-5-2):**

$$S_r = 0.81 \times S_n \times K_{mat}$$

$$S_r = 0.81 \times 10 \text{ mm} \times 0.4 = 3.24 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 2 — Hysteresis and release distance:**

$$H = \frac{S_r - S_d}{S_r} \times 100\% = 10\%$$

$$S_r - S_d = 0.10 \times S_r \Rightarrow S_d = 0.90 \times S_r$$

$$S_d = 0.90 \times 3.24 \text{ mm} = 2.916 \text{ mm}$$

**Final Answers:**

$$S_r = 3.24 \text{ mm}, \qquad S_d = 2.92 \text{ mm}$$

> **Engineering interpretation:** The sensor *triggers* at 3.24 mm as the target approaches, but only *releases* at 2.92 mm as the target moves away — a hysteresis band of 0.32 mm that prevents output chattering at the boundary.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Explain the working principle of a capacitive proximity sensor with a neat block diagram. Discuss how the dielectric constant of the target affects the sensing range.**

**Model Solution:**

**1. Working Principle:**
A capacitive proximity sensor consists of two electrodes — an **active electrode** (on the sensor face) and a **reference electrode** (connected to the sensor body / earth). Together they form a capacitor whose dielectric is the surrounding air (εᵣ ≈ 1). A relaxation oscillator drives the active electrode at a frequency $f = 1/(RC \ln 2)$.

When a target enters the electrostatic field, the **dielectric constant of the target material** replaces part of the air gap. Since capacitance $C = \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A / d$, the capacitance **rises in proportion to $\varepsilon_r$**. This rise in C causes the oscillator's frequency to **fall**, and when the frequency drops below a preset threshold, the output transistor is switched.

**2. Effect of Dielectric Constant on Sensing Range:**

- The change in capacitance $\Delta C = C_0(\varepsilon_{r,t} - 1)$ is **directly proportional to** the difference between the target's dielectric constant and air's.
- Therefore, the **effective sensing range** scales approximately as $(\varepsilon_{r,t} - 1)$.
- A high-$\varepsilon_r$ target (e.g., water, $\varepsilon_r \approx 80$) can be detected at **5–10× the range** of a low-$\varepsilon_r$ target (e.g., dry wood, $\varepsilon_r \approx 2$).
- A *sensitivity potentiometer* allows the user to set the threshold so that only the intended target triggers the output, rejecting low-$\varepsilon_r$ backgrounds.

**3. Block Diagram (textual):**

```
Power Supply → Relaxation Oscillator → Active Electrode ↔ Target (dielectric)
                                          ↕
                              Reference Electrode
                                          ↕
                               Frequency Shift → Threshold Detector → Output
```

**4. Two Key Applications:**
- Liquid-level detection through a glass or plastic wall.
- Tablet / pill counting in pharmaceutical packaging.

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**A capacitive proximity sensor has active area $A = 6 \text{ cm}^2$ and gap $d = 1.5$ mm. Compute the capacitance with (i) no target, and (ii) a glass target of $\varepsilon_r = 6$. What is the percentage change in capacitance?**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Capacitance with no target (air, $\varepsilon_r \approx 1$):**

$$C_0 = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{d} = \frac{8.854 \times 10^{-12} \times 1 \times 6 \times 10^{-4}}{1.5 \times 10^{-3}}$$

$$C_0 = \frac{8.854 \times 6}{1.5} \times 10^{-12-4+3} = \frac{53.124}{1.5} \times 10^{-13}$$

$$C_0 = 35.42 \times 10^{-13} \text{ F} = 3.542 \text{ pF}$$

**Step 2 — Capacitance with glass target ($\varepsilon_r = 6$):**

$$C_t = 6 \times C_0 = 6 \times 3.542 = 21.25 \text{ pF}$$

**Step 3 — Percentage change:**

$$\%\Delta C = \frac{C_t - C_0}{C_0} \times 100 = \frac{21.25 - 3.542}{3.542} \times 100$$

$$= \frac{17.71}{3.542} \times 100 = 500\%$$

**Final Answers:**

$$C_0 = 3.54 \text{ pF}, \quad C_t = 21.25 \text{ pF}, \quad \%\Delta C = 500\%$$

> **Engineering interpretation:** A 500% (5×) increase in capacitance is enormous and easily detected. Even low-$\varepsilon_r$ targets like dry plastic (εᵣ ≈ 2) give a 100% rise — still ample signal for detection with proper sensitivity calibration.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes students make in KTU Board Examinations on this topic (causing loss of 1–4 marks per question):**
>
> 1. **Confusing $S_n$ with $S_r$:** Students often use $S_n$ (rated) directly in problems, forgetting the 0.81 correction factor. **Always use $S_r = 0.81 \times S_n \times K_{mat}$** unless the question explicitly says "rated" distance.
>
> 2. **Forgetting the unit conversion for $A$ and $d$:** $A$ is in m², $d$ in m. Converting cm² → m² requires $\times 10^{-4}$ and mm → m requires $\times 10^{-3}$. A wrong conversion gives 7 wrong orders of magnitude in C.
>
> 3. **Confusing "inductive detects all materials":** Inductive sensors are **metal-only**. A student writing "inductive sensor detects plastic" will lose full marks on the question.
>
> 4. **Omitting the "0.81" factor in $S_r$:** This single missing factor is the #1 mark-loser across all KTU past papers. The 0.81 comes from the IEC standard's mild-steel-target calibration and is *not* a "fudge factor" — it has physical meaning.
>
> 5. **Drawing the wrong block diagram:** Many students draw a *capacitive* block diagram (with the relaxation oscillator and electrode pair) but label it *inductive*. Examiners dock 1–2 marks for this. **Memorise the two diagrams distinctly.**
>
> 6. **Mixing up NO and NC output:** In KTU, the *Normally Open* contact is open at rest; *Normally Closed* is closed at rest. A wrong labelling in a microswitch diagram costs 1 mark.
>
> 7. **Forgetting $\varepsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12}$ F/m:** Examiner expects this constant *with its value* on the formula sheet. Always quote the value explicitly.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> **Must Memorise for KTU 2024 ESE — Module 2 — Tactile & Proximity Sensors:**

- [x] **Tactile sensor** = contact-required; **Proximity sensor** = non-contact.
- [x] **Three tactile sub-types:** contact switch, bumper, force/pressure array.
- [x] **Contact switch terminology:** NO, NC, COM, snap-action, operating force, release force, differential travel.
- [x] **Bumper = distributed tactile** — used in mobile robots and AGVs.
- [x] **Inductive proximity** = metal-only detection; uses **eddy-current losses** at 100 kHz–1 MHz.
- [x] **Capacitive proximity** = universal target detection; uses **change in capacitance**; sensitivity to dielectric constant.
- [x] **Rated operating distance** $S_n$ is the *lab value*. **Real operating distance** $S_r = 0.81 \times S_n \times K_{mat}$.
- [x] **Assured operating distance** $0 \le S_a \le 0.81 \times S_n$ — guaranteed switching range.
- [x] **Hysteresis** $H = (S_r - S_d)/S_r \times 100\%$, typ. 1–15%.
- [x] **Capacitance formula** $C = \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A / d$ (parallel plate).
- [x] **Permittivity of free space** $\varepsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12}$ F/m — quote with value.
- [x] **Dielectric constant of water** $\varepsilon_r \approx 80$; **plastic** $\varepsilon_r \approx 2$–4; **air** $\varepsilon_r \approx 1$.
- [x] **Oscillator frequency** $f = 1/(RC \ln 2)$ — *C increases, f decreases* → threshold trigger.
- [x] **Inductive sensor ranges:** 1–60 mm; **Capacitive sensor ranges:** 1–40 mm.
- [x] **Selection rule:** Metal target → inductive; non-metal / liquid / human → capacitive.
- [x] **IP rating:** Inductive typically IP67 (sealed); Capacitive typically IP65 (open face).
- [x] **Switching frequency:** Inductive up to 5 kHz; Capacitive up to 100 Hz.
- [x] **Block-diagram labels must be distinct** between inductive (oscillator + coil + eddy) and capacitive (relaxation oscillator + electrode pair + frequency shift).
- [x] **Two-wire vs three-wire** sensor wiring; PNP (sourcing) vs NPN (sinking) output — *match to PLC input card*.
- [x] **Real-world use cases:** door interlocks (switch), AGV collision (bumper), CNC tool detect (inductive), touchless tap (capacitive).

> **Last-Line Mnemonic (for viva):**
> *"Inductives Ignore Insulators; Capacitives Care for εᵣ."* 🧠
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Semiconductor Displacement Sensor
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBRT303 - SENSORS AND ACTUATORS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Semiconductor Displacement Sensor

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Defining the Semiconductor Displacement Sensor

A **Semiconductor Displacement Sensor** is a solid-state, contactless (or contact-based) transducer that converts a physical change in position, distance, or mechanical deformation into a measurable electrical signal, exploiting the **electromagnetic and quantum transport properties of doped semiconductor materials** (typically Silicon, Germanium, GaAs, InSb, or InAs).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (PCBRT303 – Module 2):**
> Semiconductor Displacement Sensors form the foundational class of non-optical, non-inductive transducers that use the **Hall Effect**, **Magnetoresistance**, **Piezoresistive Effect**, and **Photoconductive / Lateral Photoelectric Effect** in semiconductor crystals to deliver an output voltage, current, or resistance change proportional to a displacement, position, or mechanical strain.

The class typically includes the following sub-families:

| Sub-Family | Working Principle | Typical Output |
|---|---|---|
| **Hall Effect Sensor** | Lorentz force on charge carriers | Hall Voltage $V_H$ |
| **Magnetoresistive (MR / GMR / TMR) Sensor** | Field-dependent carrier mobility | Resistance change $\Delta R$ |
| **Piezoresistive Sensor** | Strain-induced band-gap shift | Resistance change $\Delta R$ |
| **Position Sensitive Detector (PSD)** | Lateral photoeffect | Position-dependent photocurrents $I_1, I_2$ |

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — "The River in a Magnetic Field":**
> Imagine a stream of leaves (charge carriers) flowing through a straight channel. When you place a strong magnet across the channel, the leaves are pushed sideways. If you place collecting plates on the sides, you can measure how many leaves land on the left versus the right plate. The number of "pushed" leaves depends directly on **how strong the magnet is** AND **how far the magnet is from the channel**. Move the magnet a few millimeters closer — more leaves get pushed. That sideways push is the **Hall Effect**, and the voltage on the plates is the **Hall Voltage**. A **Semiconductor Displacement Sensor** is essentially this river-of-leaves circuit, miniaturized to micron scale and packaged in a tiny chip.

A more functional analogy: a Hall sensor used for throttle position in an e-bike is like a tiny invisible ruler — as the thumb throttle rotates, a tiny magnet moves closer to a silicon chip, and the chip reports the new position as a voltage that the controller reads in **real time**.

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

The following physical constants are essential for semiconductor displacement sensor analysis:

- **Electron charge:** $q = 1.602 \times 10^{-19}$ **C**
- **Free electron mass:** $m_e = 9.109 \times 10^{-31}$ **kg**
- **Vacuum permeability:** $\mu_0 = 4\pi \times 10^{-7}$ **H/m**
- **Boltzmann constant:** $k_B = 1.381 \times 10^{-23}$ **J/K**
- **Room-temperature thermal voltage:** $V_T = k_B T / q \approx 25.85$ **mV** at **T = 300 K**

> [!NOTE]
> KTU examiners frequently assume $V_T \approx 26$ mV and $T = 300$ K unless stated otherwise. Always carry units — losing a unit in a derivation is a **guaranteed 1-mark deduction** in ESE valuation.

## 1.4 Relevance in the B.Tech Engineering Curriculum

Semiconductor displacement sensors are the **silent workhorses** of modern industry:

- **Automotive:** Crankshaft position, throttle position, ABS wheel speed, gear-shift detection
- **Robotics:** Brushless DC motor commutation, robotic-arm joint encoders
- **Consumer Electronics:** Smartphone lid/flip detection, joystick modules, camera autofocus
- **Industrial Automation:** Pneumatic cylinder end-position feedback, conveyor belt tracking
- **Medical Devices:** Infusion pump plunger position, surgical instrument tip tracking

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Hall Effect — Theoretical Foundation

The Hall Effect is the **transverse deflection of charge carriers** in a current-carrying conductor or semiconductor placed in a perpendicular magnetic field. The phenomenon was discovered by **Edwin H. Hall in 1879** and is the most commercially exploited semiconductor displacement-sensing principle.

### 2.1.1 Operational Logic (Step by Step)

1. **Step 1 — Current Injection:** A control current $I_x$ is forced along the length (x-direction) of a rectangular semiconductor strip (the "Hall plate").
2. **Step 2 — Magnetic Biasing:** An external magnetic field $B_z$ is applied perpendicular to the strip surface (z-direction). This field is the *measurand*-coupled input — it changes with the position of a nearby magnet.
3. **Step 3 — Lorentz Force on Carriers:** Each carrier of charge $q$ moving with drift velocity $v_d$ experiences a magnetic Lorentz force
   $F_B = q \, v_d \times B$
   This pushes electrons to one lateral face and holes (if any) to the other, creating charge accumulation.
4. **Step 4 — Hall Voltage Buildup:** The accumulated surface charge creates a transverse electric field $E_H$ that produces an opposing electrostatic force $F_E = q E_H$. Equilibrium is reached when $F_E = F_B$, yielding the **Hall field** $E_H = v_d B_z$.
5. **Step 5 — Measurable Output:** The transverse Hall voltage across the width $w$ of the strip is
   $V_H = E_H \cdot w = v_d B_z w$

> [!TIP]
> **Why is a semiconductor used instead of a metal?** In a metal, the carrier concentration $n$ is enormous ($\sim 10^{28}$ m$^{-3}$), making $V_H$ vanishingly small. In a doped semiconductor, $n \sim 10^{21}$–$10^{24}$ m$^{-3}$, which is **4–7 orders of magnitude lower**, producing a Hall voltage that is easily measured (mV range).

### 2.1.2 Why the Hall Effect Senses Displacement

In a displacement-sensing application, the magnetic field $B_z$ seen by the Hall plate is **not constant** — it varies as a function of the gap or lateral position between the magnet and the sensor:

$B_z = f(x)$ where $x$ is the displacement.

A permanent magnet's on-axis field at distance $d$ follows the dipole approximation:

$B_z(d) \approx \frac{\mu_0 \, M \, V}{2\pi d^3}$

where $M$ is the magnetization and $V$ the magnet volume. Hence $V_H \propto B_z \propto 1/d^3$, and a calibrated Hall sensor effectively becomes a **non-contact distance gauge**.

## 2.2 Magnetoresistance (MR) Family

### 2.2.1 Ordinary Magnetoresistance (OMR)

In non-magnetic semiconductors, the carrier mobility $\mu$ depends weakly on $B$ due to the Lorentz force altering the carrier trajectory. The fractional resistance change is approximately:

$\frac{\Delta \rho}{\rho_0} \approx (\mu B)^2 \quad \text{for } \mu B \ll 1$

This is the **physical (or ordinary) magnetoresistance**.

### 2.2.2 Giant Magnetoresistance (GMR)

GMR occurs in **multilayer thin-film structures** alternating ferromagnetic and non-magnetic layers. The relative resistance change can reach **10–80%** at room temperature, with a saturating behavior at fields of a few mT. The origin is **spin-dependent scattering** of conduction electrons at the ferromagnet/normal-metal interfaces.

### 2.2.3 Tunneling Magnetoresistance (TMR)

TMR uses a **thin insulating tunnel barrier** (typically MgO, 1–2 nm thick) sandwiched between two ferromagnetic electrodes. The tunneling probability depends on the relative magnetization direction, yielding MR ratios exceeding **100–600%** in modern MgO-based junctions.

## 2.3 Piezoresistive Displacement Sensors

When a semiconductor crystal is mechanically stressed, the band structure changes, altering the effective mass and mobility of carriers. This **piezoresistive effect** (discovered by **C.S. Smith in 1954**) produces a large, predictable change in resistivity.

The fractional resistance change is given by:

$\frac{\Delta R}{R_0} = G \cdot \varepsilon$

where $G$ is the **gauge factor** and $\varepsilon = \Delta L / L$ is the longitudinal strain.

- For metal strain gauges: $G \approx 2$ (geometric effect dominates)
- For semiconductor strain gauges: $G = 50$ to $200$ (piezoresistive effect dominates)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The piezoresistive gauge factor for silicon is **highly anisotropic and doping-dependent**. For p-type silicon along the $\langle 111 \rangle$ direction, $G$ can exceed **170**. KTU problems often simplify to $G \approx 120$ for a generic silicon gauge — read the question stem carefully.

## 2.4 Position Sensitive Detector (PSD)

A PSD is a **large-area lateral photodiode** in which a focused light spot generates a photocurrent that divides between two (1-D) or four (2-D) electrodes. The relative current division is a linear function of the spot's position on the active area.

For a 1-D PSD of length $L$:

$x = \frac{L}{2} \cdot \frac{I_2 - I_1}{I_1 + I_2}$

This is a **non-segmented, analog, continuous-position** measurement, unlike discrete photodiode arrays.

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Standard Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $F_L = q \, v_d B_z$ | Lorentz force on a carrier | N |
| 2 | $V_H = \dfrac{I_x B_z}{q \, n \, d}$ | Hall voltage (thin-strip form) | V |
| 3 | $R_H = \dfrac{1}{q n}$ | Hall coefficient | m³/C |
| 4 | $\mu_H = \dfrac{\sigma}{q n} = \sigma R_H$ | Hall mobility | m²/(V·s) |
| 5 | $K_H = \dfrac{V_H}{I_x B_z} = \dfrac{1}{q n d}$ | Hall sensor sensitivity (product sensitivity) | V/AT |
| 6 | $V_H = \dfrac{R_H}{d} \, I_x B_z$ | Hall voltage (compact form) | V |
| 7 | $\rho(B) = \rho_0 (1 + \mu^2 B^2)$ | OMR in a semiconductor (low-field) | Ω·m |
| 8 | $MR\% = \dfrac{\Delta R}{R_{sat}} \times 100$ | GMR / TMR ratio definition | % |
| 9 | $\dfrac{\Delta R}{R_0} = G \cdot \varepsilon$ | Piezoresistive relation | dimensionless |
| 10 | $G = 1 + 2\nu + \dfrac{\Delta \rho / \rho}{\varepsilon}$ | Gauge factor with Poisson term | dimensionless |
| 11 | $x = \dfrac{L}{2} \cdot \dfrac{I_2 - I_1}{I_1 + I_2}$ | 1-D PSD position formula | m |
| 12 | $B_z(d) \approx \dfrac{\mu_0 M V}{2\pi d^3}$ | On-axis field of a dipole magnet | T |
| 13 | $V_T = \dfrac{k_B T}{q} \approx 25.85$ mV | Thermal voltage at 300 K | V |
| 14 | $\sigma = n q \mu$ | Conductivity relation | S/m |
| 15 | $\omega_c = qB/m^*$ | Cyclotron frequency | rad/s |

> [!WARNING]
> **Memorization tip:** The "**Big Five**" for KTU Hall-effect problems are (1) Hall voltage, (2) Hall coefficient, (3) Hall mobility, (4) sensitivity, and (5) carrier concentration. If you memorize these and their units, you solve ~80% of numerical Part B questions.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation — Hall Voltage in a Rectangular Semiconductor Strip

Consider a rectangular semiconductor strip of length $L$, width $w$, and thickness $d$, oriented along the x-axis. A control current $I_x$ flows from $x = 0$ to $x = L$, and a magnetic field $B_z$ is applied along the z-axis. The Hall contacts are on the lateral y-faces.

### Step 1 — Drift Velocity from Current Density

The current density along x is

$J_x = \dfrac{I_x}{w \cdot d}$

The drift velocity of the carriers (assumed electrons for an n-type semiconductor) is related to the current density by

$J_x = n \, q \, v_d$

Solving for the drift velocity:

$v_d = \dfrac{J_x}{n q} = \dfrac{I_x}{n q w d}$

**Logic:** The current density equation is essentially a count of charges passing per unit time per unit area. The number density $n$ tells us how many carriers are available, and $q$ tells us the charge per carrier. Dividing current density by $nq$ gives the average carrier velocity.

### Step 2 — Lorentz Force Per Carrier

A carrier moving with $v_d$ in field $B_z$ experiences the magnetic component of the Lorentz force

$F_y = q \, v_d \times B_z = q \, v_d B_z$ (taking the magnitude in the transverse direction)

Substituting the expression for $v_d$ from Step 1:

$F_y = q \cdot \dfrac{I_x}{n q w d} \cdot B_z = \dfrac{I_x B_z}{n w d}$

**Logic:** The Lorentz force in a Hall plate is exactly what pushes carriers sideways; the product $q v_d$ combines into $J_x$ from Step 1, giving a clean force expression in terms of measurable quantities.

### Step 3 — Transverse Electric Field at Equilibrium

The deflected carriers accumulate on the lateral y-face, producing a transverse Hall electric field $E_H$ that grows until the electrostatic force on a carrier balances the magnetic force:

$q E_H = q v_d B_z \quad \Rightarrow \quad E_H = v_d B_z$

**Logic:** This is a force-balance condition. Equilibrium is reached dynamically: the more carriers that pile up, the stronger the opposing field, until the carrier no longer accelerates sideways.

### Step 4 — Hall Voltage Across the Width

The Hall voltage is the integral of $E_H$ across the strip width $w$:

$V_H = E_H \cdot w = v_d B_z w$

Substituting the drift velocity from Step 1:

$V_H = \dfrac{I_x}{n q w d} \cdot B_z \cdot w = \dfrac{I_x B_z}{n q d}$

**Logic:** The width $w$ cancels out beautifully — the Hall voltage is **independent of the strip width** but is **inversely proportional to thickness $d$ and carrier concentration $n$**. This is why KTU problems often vary $n$ and $d$ to test student understanding.

### Step 5 — Hall Coefficient Definition

Define the **Hall coefficient**:

$R_H = \dfrac{1}{n q}$ (for n-type, with sign convention that $R_H$ is negative for electrons)

The Hall voltage becomes:

$V_H = \dfrac{R_H \, I_x B_z}{d}$

**Logic:** $R_H$ is a **material property** of the semiconductor that captures the carrier-density information. High-purity semiconductors have a large $R_H$ and hence a large $V_H$ — that is why intrinsic or lightly-doped semiconductors are preferred for Hall sensors.

### Step 6 — Hall Mobility

Using the conductivity relation $\sigma = n q \mu$, we can write:

$\mu_H = \sigma R_H = \dfrac{\sigma}{n q}$

This defines the **Hall mobility**, which is a key figure of merit for a Hall-sensor material.

**Logic:** Mobility and Hall coefficient together characterize how easily carriers flow and how strongly they respond to a magnetic field. For displacement sensing, a high $\mu_H$ is desirable because it gives a large $V_H$ for a small $B_z$.

### Step 7 — Sensitivity

The product sensitivity of a Hall sensor is:

$K_H = \dfrac{V_H}{I_x B_z} = \dfrac{R_H}{d} = \dfrac{1}{n q d}$

Typical values for InSb, InAs, and GaAs Hall sensors are:

- **InSb:** $K_H \approx 100$–$300$ V/AT (highest, narrow bandgap)
- **InAs:** $K_H \approx 50$–$100$ V/AT
- **GaAs:** $K_H \approx 10$–$30$ V/AT (lowest but best temperature stability)

**Logic:** KTU problems often ask the student to compare materials — InSb is best for room-temperature sensitivity, GaAs is best for industrial-grade temperature stability from $-40$°C to $+150$°C.

## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation — Magnetoresistance in a Hall Geometry

For a semiconductor with carriers of mobility $\mu$ in a magnetic field $B$, the conductivity becomes a tensor. For an isotropic medium with $\vec{B} \parallel \hat{z}$:

$\sigma_{xx} = \dfrac{\sigma_0}{1 + (\mu B)^2}$

$\sigma_{xy} = \dfrac{\sigma_0 \mu B}{1 + (\mu B)^2}$

The Hall angle is $\theta_H = \tan^{-1}(\mu B)$, also called $\omega_c \tau$ where $\omega_c = qB/m^*$ is the cyclotron frequency and $\tau$ is the scattering time.

The fractional change in longitudinal resistivity is:

$\dfrac{\Delta \rho}{\rho_0} = \dfrac{\rho_{xx}(B) - \rho_{xx}(0)}{\rho_{xx}(0)} = (\mu B)^2$

**Logic:** The MR effect is **quadratic in $B$** and **quadratic in mobility**. Hence, high-mobility semiconductors (InSb, InAs) show strong OMR. In contrast, GMR and TMR (multilayer structures) have a *linear* response at low fields and saturate, making them ideal for digital switching applications.

## 3.3 Exhaustive Derivation — Piezoresistive Gauge Factor

For a uniformly doped semiconductor bar of length $L$ and cross-section $A = w \cdot d$:

$R_0 = \dfrac{\rho_0 L}{A}$

Under axial strain $\varepsilon = \Delta L / L$, three changes occur:

1. Length change: $L \rightarrow L + \Delta L = L(1 + \varepsilon)$
2. Cross-sectional change due to Poisson contraction: $A \rightarrow A(1 - \nu \varepsilon)^2$, where $\nu$ is Poisson's ratio
3. Resistivity change due to piezoresistive effect: $\rho \rightarrow \rho_0 (1 + \pi_l \sigma)$, where $\pi_l$ is the longitudinal piezoresistive coefficient and $\sigma = E \varepsilon$ is the axial stress

The new resistance is:

$R = \dfrac{\rho_0 (1 + \pi_l E \varepsilon) \cdot L(1+\varepsilon)}{A(1-\nu \varepsilon)^2}$

For small strains, expand using the binomial theorem:

$R \approx R_0 (1 + \varepsilon)(1 + \pi_l E \varepsilon)(1 + 2\nu \varepsilon)$

$R \approx R_0 [1 + (1 + 2\nu + \pi_l E)\varepsilon]$ to first order in $\varepsilon$

Thus:

$\dfrac{\Delta R}{R_0} = (1 + 2\nu + \pi_l E)\varepsilon$

The gauge factor is:

$G = 1 + 2\nu + \pi_l E$

**Logic:** The three terms represent (1) length, (2) area, and (3) resistivity contributions. For metals, $\pi_l E$ is negligible and $G \approx 1 + 2\nu \approx 2$. For silicon, $\pi_l E \approx 100$ to $170$, dominating the gauge factor.

## 3.4 PSD Position Derivation

For a 1-D PSD of length $L$ with electrodes at $x = 0$ and $x = L$, the total photocurrent is $I_0 = I_1 + I_2$. The current to each electrode is inversely proportional to the distance from the spot:

$I_1 = \dfrac{I_0 (L - x)}{L}, \quad I_2 = \dfrac{I_0 \, x}{L}$

Solving for $x$:

$x = \dfrac{L}{2} \cdot \dfrac{I_2 - I_1}{I_1 + I_2}$

**Logic:** This linearized position formula is independent of the total light intensity $I_0$ — a key advantage of PSDs over photodiode arrays. Even if the source intensity fluctuates, the ratio $(I_2 - I_1)/(I_1 + I_2)$ remains constant.

## 3.5 Numerical Worked Example (KTU-Standard)

> **Problem:** A Hall-effect displacement sensor uses an n-type silicon strip with thickness $d = 100\ \mu\text{m}$, donor concentration $N_D = 10^{21}\ \{m}^{-3}$, and electron mobility $\mu_n = 0.135\ \text{m}^2/(\text{V}\cdot\text{s})$. A control current $I_x = 5\ \text{mA}$ is applied, and a small permanent magnet produces a magnetic field $B_z = 0.2\ \text{T}$ at the active region. Find:
> (a) The Hall coefficient $R_H$.
> (b) The Hall voltage $V_H$.
> (c) The product sensitivity $K_H$.

**Solution:**

**Part (a) — Hall coefficient:**

$$R_H = -\frac{1}{n \, q} = -\frac{1}{(10^{21})(1.602 \times 10^{-19})}$$

$$R_H = -\frac{1}{1.602 \times 10^{2}} = -6.243 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{m}^3/\text{C}$$

**Part (b) — Hall voltage:**

$$V_H = \frac{|R_H| \, I_x \, B_z}{d} = \frac{(6.243 \times 10^{-3})(5 \times 10^{-3})(0.2)}{100 \times 10^{-6}}$$

$$V_H = \frac{6.243 \times 10^{-6}}{10^{-4}} = 6.243 \times 10^{-2}\ \text{V} = 62.43\ \text{mV}$$

**Part (c) — Product sensitivity:**

$$K_H = \frac{V_H}{I_x B_z} = \frac{62.43 \times 10^{-3}}{(5 \times 10^{-3})(0.2)} = \frac{62.43 \times 10^{-3}}{10^{-3}} = 62.43\ \text{V/AT}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Verification by direct substitution:** $K_H = 1/(nqd) = 1/((10^{21})(1.602 \times 10^{-19})(100 \times 10^{-6})) = 1/(1.602 \times 10^{-2}) = 62.43$ V/AT. ✓

## 3.6 Comparative Analytical Matrix — Semiconductor Displacement Sensor Families

| Parameter | Hall Effect | OMR (Semiconductor) | GMR | TMR | Piezoresistive | PSD |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Output type** | Voltage | Resistance | Resistance | Resistance | Resistance | Currents $I_1, I_2$ |
| **Active principle** | Lorentz force | Mobility change | Spin scattering | Quantum tunneling | Band structure | Lateral photo-effect |
| **Range (typical)** | 0.1–10 mm | 0.1–5 mm | 0.1–3 mm | 0.05–2 mm | 0–0.5 mm (contact) | 1–100 mm (optical) |
| **Resolution** | ~1 µm | ~10 µm | ~0.1 µm | ~0.01 µm | ~0.1 µm | ~0.5 µm |
| **Linearity** | Good (with magnet shaping) | Quadratic in $B$ | Hysteretic, non-linear | Hysteretic, non-linear | Linear (small strain) | Excellent (linear) |
| **Temperature drift** | Moderate | High | Low | Low | Moderate | Low |
| **Cost (relative)** | Low | Low | Medium | High | Low | Medium |
| **KTU weightage** | ★★★★★ | ★★★ | ★★★ | ★★ | ★★★★ | ★★★ |

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`
**Define the Hall Effect and write the expression for the Hall voltage in a semiconductor strip. Mention the significance of the Hall coefficient.**

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> The **Hall Effect** is the generation of a transverse electromotive force (Hall voltage) across a current-carrying conductor or semiconductor when it is placed in a perpendicular magnetic field. It arises due to the Lorentz force deflecting charge carriers to one side of the conductor.
>
> The Hall voltage is given by:
>
> $$V_H = \frac{R_H \, I_x \, B_z}{d} = \frac{I_x B_z}{n q d}$$
>
> where $R_H = 1/(nq)$ is the **Hall coefficient**. The Hall coefficient is significant because (i) its sign indicates the **type of charge carrier** (negative for electrons, positive for holes), (ii) its magnitude gives the **carrier concentration**, and (iii) combined with conductivity, it yields the **Hall mobility**, a key material figure of merit for sensor applications.

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`
**Differentiate between Giant Magnetoresistance (GMR) and Ordinary Magnetoresistance (OMR) in semiconductors.**

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
>
> | Aspect | OMR | GMR |
> |---|---|---|
> | **Origin** | Lorentz-force-altered carrier trajectory | Spin-dependent scattering at ferromagnet/normal-metal interfaces |
> | **Magnitude** | Typically $< 1\%$ at room temperature | $10$–$80\%$ |
> | **Field response** | Quadratic in $B$ ($\Delta \rho / \rho \propto B^2$) | Saturating, quasi-linear at low $B$ |
> | **Material** | Bulk non-magnetic semiconductor | Multilayer thin film (Fe/Cr/Co/Cu) |
> | **Application** | Magnetic field probes, current sensing | Hard-disk read heads, position encoders |
>
> In short, GMR is a **quantum-mechanical, spin-dependent** effect engineered via thin-film layering, whereas OMR is a **classical, semiclassical transport** effect in bulk semiconductors.

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 Pattern Reminder:** Each Part B question has internal choice — answer **either** Question A **or** Question B. Each is split into two sub-parts, usually 7 + 7 marks.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]`

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the working of a **Hall Effect displacement sensor**. Define the Hall coefficient and derive the expression for the Hall voltage. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An n-type germanium Hall sensor has the following specifications: thickness $d = 200\ \mu\text{m}$, donor concentration $n = 5 \times 10^{20}\ \text{m}^{-3}$, electron mobility $\mu = 0.39\ \text{m}^2/(\text{V}\cdot\text{s})$, control current $I_x = 10\ \text{mA}$, and applied magnetic field $B = 0.15\ \text{T}$.
**Find:**
(i) The Hall coefficient $R_H$
(ii) The Hall voltage $V_H$
(iii) The Hall mobility $\mu_H$
(iv) The sensitivity $K_H$ in V/AT. **(7 marks)**

> **Model Solution (Question A):**
>
> **(a) Working and Derivation (7 marks):**
>
> **Working principle:** A Hall Effect displacement sensor uses a thin semiconductor strip (Hall plate) carrying a control current $I_x$. A small permanent magnet is mechanically coupled to the object whose displacement is to be measured. As the object moves, the magnet's position relative to the Hall plate changes, altering the perpendicular magnetic flux density $B_z$ at the sensor. The Hall voltage $V_H$ generated is therefore a function of the displacement.
>
> **Block diagram (described):**
> Moving Object → Attached Magnet → Variable Air Gap → Hall Plate → Differential Amplifier → Signal Conditioner → ADC → Microcontroller → Displacement Display.
>
> **Derivation (Step-by-Step, full 7 marks):**
>
> Let the Hall plate have length $L$ (x-direction), width $w$ (y-direction), and thickness $d$ (z-direction). A control current $I_x$ flows along x, and $B_z$ is applied along z.
>
> Current density along x:
>
> $$J_x = \frac{I_x}{w \, d}$$
>
> Drift velocity:
>
> $$v_d = \frac{J_x}{n q} = \frac{I_x}{n q w d}$$
>
> Lorentz force on a carrier:
>
> $$F_y = q \, v_d B_z$$
>
> At equilibrium, the transverse electric field satisfies $q E_H = q v_d B_z$, hence
>
> $$E_H = v_d B_z$$
>
> Hall voltage across width $w$:
>
> $$V_H = E_H \, w = v_d B_z w = \frac{I_x B_z}{n q d}$$
>
> Defining the Hall coefficient $R_H = 1/(nq)$:
>
> $$V_H = \frac{R_H \, I_x B_z}{d}$$
>
> **(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**
>
> **(i) Hall coefficient:**
>
> $$R_H = -\frac{1}{n q} = -\frac{1}{(5 \times 10^{20})(1.602 \times 10^{-19})}$$
>
> $$R_H = -\frac{1}{80.1} = -1.248 \times 10^{-2}\ \text{m}^3/\text{C}$$
>
> **(ii) Hall voltage:**
>
> $$V_H = \frac{|R_H| \, I_x \, B_z}{d} = \frac{(1.248 \times 10^{-2})(10 \times 10^{-3})(0.15)}{200 \times 10^{-6}}$$
>
> $$V_H = \frac{1.872 \times 10^{-5}}{2 \times 10^{-4}} = 9.36 \times 10^{-2}\ \text{V} = 93.6\ \text{mV}$$
>
> **(iii) Hall mobility:**
>
> $$\sigma = n q \mu = (5 \times 10^{20})(1.602 \times 10^{-19})(0.39) = 31.24\ \text{S/m}$$
>
> $$\mu_H = \sigma |R_H| = (31.24)(1.248 \times 10^{-2}) = 0.3899\ \text{m}^2/(\text{V}\cdot\text{s})$$
>
> Note: $\mu_H \approx \mu$ (drift mobility) in the absence of magnetic-field-dependent scattering differences.
>
> **(iv) Sensitivity:**
>
> $$K_H = \frac{V_H}{I_x B_z} = \frac{93.6 \times 10^{-3}}{(10 \times 10^{-3})(0.15)} = \frac{93.6 \times 10^{-3}}{1.5 \times 10^{-3}} = 62.4\ \text{V/AT}$$
>
> **Verification:** $K_H = 1/(n q d) = 1/[(5 \times 10^{20})(1.602 \times 10^{-19})(200 \times 10^{-6})] = 1/(1.602 \times 10^{-2}) = 62.4$ V/AT. ✓

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the **principle of piezoresistive displacement sensing** in semiconductors. Derive the expression for the gauge factor of a semiconductor strain gauge, and compare its magnitude with that of a metal strain gauge. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A silicon piezoresistive sensor is used to measure the deflection of a cantilever beam. The gauge factor of the doped silicon resistor is $G = 120$, and its nominal resistance is $R_0 = 1\ \text{k}\Omega$. The beam experiences a strain $\varepsilon = 500\ \mu\varepsilon$ (i.e., $500 \times 10^{-6}$).
**Find:**
(i) The change in resistance $\Delta R$
(ii) The change in voltage across the gauge when it is supplied by a constant current source of $I = 5\ \text{mA}$
(iii) If the strain is doubled, what is the new $\Delta R$? **(7 marks)**

> **Model Solution (Question B):**
>
> **(a) Piezoresistive Principle and Gauge Factor Derivation (7 marks):**
>
> **Principle:** When a semiconductor crystal is subjected to mechanical stress, the band structure is altered (deformation potential). This shifts the effective mass and scattering rates of charge carriers, producing a large change in resistivity — the **piezoresistive effect**. The fractional change in resistance is given by
>
> $$\frac{\Delta R}{R_0} = G \cdot \varepsilon$$
>
> **Derivation:** Under axial strain $\varepsilon$, the geometric change in a bar of original resistance $R_0 = \rho_0 L / A$ gives:
>
> $$R = \frac{\rho_0 (1 + \pi_l E \varepsilon) \cdot L(1 + \varepsilon)}{A(1 - \nu \varepsilon)^2} \approx R_0[1 + (1 + 2\nu + \pi_l E)\varepsilon]$$
>
> Therefore, the gauge factor is
>
> $$G = 1 + 2\nu + \pi_l E$$
>
> where $\pi_l$ is the longitudinal piezoresistive coefficient and $E$ is Young's modulus.
>
> **Comparison:**
>
> | Property | Metal | Semiconductor |
> |---|---|---|
> | $G$ (typical) | $2$ | $50$–$200$ |
> | Dominant term | $1 + 2\nu \approx 2$ (geometric) | $\pi_l E \approx 100$–$170$ (resistive) |
> | Temperature sensitivity | Low | Higher, but compensable |
> | Hysteresis | Negligible | Low for monocrystalline Si |
>
> **Conclusion:** Semiconductor gauges are **50–100× more sensitive** than metal gauges, making them ideal for micro-scale strain measurements (MEMS pressure sensors, accelerometers, flow meters).
>
> **(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**
>
> **(i) Change in resistance:**
>
> $$\Delta R = R_0 \cdot G \cdot \varepsilon = (1000)(120)(500 \times 10^{-6}) = (1000)(0.06) = 60\ \Omega$$
>
> **(ii) Change in voltage (constant current):**
>
> $$\Delta V = I \cdot \Delta R = (5 \times 10^{-3})(60) = 0.3\ \text{V} = 300\ \text{mV}$$
>
> **(iii) New $\Delta R$ when strain is doubled:**
>
> $$\Delta R_{new} = R_0 \cdot G \cdot (2\varepsilon) = (1000)(120)(1000 \times 10^{-6}) = (1000)(0.12) = 120\ \Omega$$
>
> So the change in resistance also doubles — a key advantage of the **linear** piezoresistive response for small strains.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls (Hall Effect & Piezoresistive Problems):**
> 1. **Sign of $R_H$:** Many students drop the negative sign for n-type semiconductors. The Hall coefficient is **negative for electrons** and **positive for holes**. While the magnitude is often what matters numerically, examiners **deduct 0.5–1 mark** for forgetting the sign convention.
> 2. **Unit mismatch in $d$:** Thickness $d$ is almost always given in µm. Failing to convert to **meters** before substituting in the formula is the single most common error. Always write the unit-conversion step explicitly.
> 3. **Sensitivity formula confusion:** $K_H = V_H / (I_x B_z)$ is the **product sensitivity**. Some textbooks define $K_H$ as $V_H / B_z$ with a specified $I_x$. Read the question's definition carefully.
> 4. **Gauge factor terms:** Forgetting the $1 + 2\nu$ contribution and writing $G = \pi_l E$ only is **partial credit territory** — the geometric term must appear in the derivation.
> 5. **Strain unit trap:** $500\ \mu\varepsilon = 500 \times 10^{-6}$, **not** $500 \times 10^{-3}$. KTU problems deliberately use micro-strain notation to test this.
> 6. **Forgetting the current direction:** A Hall sensor reverses polarity if the magnet is flipped. Always specify the **sign convention** of $V_H$ with reference to the magnet orientation.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist — Semiconductor Displacement Sensor**

- ⭐ The **Hall Effect** is the **transverse deflection** of charge carriers in a magnetic field, producing a measurable **Hall voltage** $V_H = R_H I_x B_z / d$.
- ⭐ The **Hall coefficient** $R_H = 1/(nq)$ reveals the **sign** (carrier type) and **density** of charge carriers — a fundamental materials diagnostic.
- ⭐ **Hall mobility** $\mu_H = \sigma R_H$ is the key figure of merit for sensor-grade semiconductors like InSb, InAs, and GaAs.
- ⭐ **Ordinary Magnetoresistance (OMR)** is **quadratic in $B$** and dominant in high-mobility bulk semiconductors.
- ⭐ **GMR** arises from **spin-dependent scattering** in ferromagnetic multilayers — resistance changes up to **80%** at room temperature.
- ⭐ **TMR** uses a **MgO tunnel barrier** between ferromagnets — modern TMR junctions exceed **100% MR** at room temperature.
- ⭐ The **piezoresistive gauge factor** $G = 1 + 2\nu + \pi_l E$ has a dominant term $\pi_l E$ in semiconductors ($G \approx 100$–$200$), versus $G \approx 2$ in metals.
- ⭐ The **Position Sensitive Detector (PSD)** output is $x = (L/2) \cdot (I_2 - I_1)/(I_1 + I_2)$ — independent of source intensity, highly linear.
- ⭐ Material selection rule of thumb: **InSb for highest sensitivity**, **GaAs for temperature stability**, **Si for monolithic integration with electronics**.
- ⭐ Always convert $d$ from µm to **meters**; always convert $\mu\varepsilon$ to dimensionless strain; always state the **sign of $R_H$**.
- ⭐ KTU's "Big Five" formulas: **$V_H$, $R_H$, $\mu_H$, $K_H$, $G$** — internalize these and you solve 80% of numericals.
- ⭐ A semiconductor displacement sensor is **non-contact** (Hall, MR, PSD) or **contact** (piezoresistive) — match the **range, resolution, environment, and linearity** requirements of the application.
- ⭐ The **gauge factor formula** $G = 1 + 2\nu + \pi_l E$ must be derived in full, with each term physically interpreted (geometric vs. resistive).
- ⭐ KTU examiners value **clear diagrams**: a block-level functional flow with labeled **measurand input**, **transduction stage**, **signal conditioning**, and **output display** is worth **2–3 marks** in Part B.
- ⭐ For displacement sensing with a magnet, the field falls off as $1/d^3$ along the magnet axis — use this to design a **linearizing magnet profile** (e.g., a tapered pole piece) if the application demands linear output.
- ⭐ Modern **integrated Hall ICs** (e.g., Allegro, Honeywell, Melexis, TDK) package the Hall element, amplifier, ADC, and DSP in a single chip — the **circuit-level understanding** of these is now within the KTU 2024 syllabus scope.

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBRT303 - SENSORS AND ACTUATORS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Range Sensors - IR, Sonar, Laser Range Finder, Optical Triangulation (1D), Structured Light (2D), Performance Comparison of Range Sensors

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Introduction to Range Sensors

A **Range Sensor** (also called a *Distance Sensor* or *Proximity Sensor with Distance Output*) is a non-contact electro-optical or acoustic transducer that quantitatively measures the spatial separation between the sensor head and a target object in its field of view. The output is typically a continuous analog voltage/current or a digital word proportional to the absolute distance $d$.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Definition:** A range sensor is defined as a device that emits a known signal (electromagnetic wave or acoustic wave) toward a target and converts the reflected/returned energy into a measurable distance parameter using **Time-of-Flight (ToF)**, **Triangulation**, or **Phase-Shift** principles.

### 1.2 Classification of Range Sensing Principles

| Principle | Physical Carrier | Typical Range | Resolution |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Time-of-Flight (ToF)** | Sound, Light | cm → km | mm to cm |
| **Triangulation** | Light (LASER/LED) | mm → meters | µm to mm |
| **Phase-Shift Detection** | Modulated Light | cm → 100 m | sub-mm |
| **Interferometry** | Coherent Light | nm → mm | nm (sub-wavelength) |
| **Structured Light / Stereo** | Projected Pattern | cm → meters | mm to cm |

---

## 1.3 Infrared (IR) Range Sensor

### Formal Definition
An **Infrared Range Sensor** is an optoelectronic device that operates by emitting a beam of infrared electromagnetic radiation (typically $\lambda = 850 \text{ nm}$ to $940 \text{ nm}$) using an IR LED. The reflected radiation from the target is captured by a position-sensitive photodetector (PSD) or a linear CCD array. The displacement of the spot on the detector is geometrically converted into a distance value.

### Conceptual Analogy
> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Intuitive Analogy — "The Blind Man's Stick":** Imagine a person standing in a dark room holding a flashlight in one hand and a camera in the other. The flashlight shines a light spot on a nearby wall. If the wall is **near**, the light spot on the wall (seen by the camera) appears **low and to one side**. If the wall is **far**, the light spot appears **higher and more centered**. The brain translates "where the spot appears" into "how far the wall is." An IR range sensor does exactly the same thing — the IR LED is the flashlight, the PSD is the camera, and a built-in op-amp circuit does the brain's math.

### Key Physical Constants
- **Speed of light in vacuum:** $c = 3 \times 10^8 \text{ m/s}$
- **Speed of sound in air (20°C):** $v_s = 343 \text{ m/s}$
- **Refractive index of air:** $n = 1.00029$

### Operating Principle
1. An IR LED emits a modulated IR beam toward the target.
2. Light diffuses off the target surface (Lambertian reflection for most surfaces).
3. A lens focuses the reflected beam onto a Position-Sensitive Detector (PSD).
4. The spot position $x$ on the PSD varies inversely with the target distance $z$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Common Commercial Example:** **Sharp GP2Y0A21YK0F** — a popular IR range sensor used in robotics, with an analog output of 0.4 V (30 cm) to 3.1 V (4 cm), range 4–30 cm.

---

## 1.4 Sonar (Ultrasonic) Range Sensor

### Formal Definition
A **Sonar (Sound Navigation and Ranging) Range Sensor** is an active acoustic transducer that emits a high-frequency pressure wave (typically $f = 20 \text{ kHz}$ to $200 \text{ kHz}$, well above human hearing) and measures the elapsed time for the echo to return from a target. The distance is calculated as $d = \frac{v_s \cdot t}{2}$, where $v_s$ is the speed of sound in the propagation medium.

### Conceptual Analogy
> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Intuitive Analogy — "Bats and Whistles in a Foggy Canyon":** Stand at one end of a long canyon and shout loudly. Listen carefully. The time gap between your shout and the returning echo tells you how far the canyon wall is. If the echo comes back in 1 second, the wall is roughly 170 meters away (sound travels ~340 m/s). A sonar sensor is a tiny, electronic version of this — it "shouts" with inaudible ultrasound and times the echo with a microcontroller timer.

### Operating Principle
1. The piezo-electric transducer is excited with a burst of $\sim 10$–$16$ cycles of $40 \text{ kHz}$ waveform (trigger pulse, typically $10 \mu s$).
2. The same transducer (or a separate receiver) switches to "listen" mode.
3. A comparator detects the envelope of the returning echo.
4. A timer (often built-in, e.g., HC-SR04) measures the round-trip time $t$.
5. Distance is computed: $d = \frac{v_s \cdot t}{2}$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Common Commercial Example:** **HC-SR04** — widely used in KTU lab experiments. Range: 2 cm to 400 cm, accuracy: 3 mm, supply: 5 V DC.

---

## 1.5 Laser Range Finder (LRF)

### Formal Definition
A **Laser Range Finder (LRF)** is an active electro-optical instrument that determines distance to a target by measuring the **Time-of-Flight of a collimated, monochromatic laser pulse** (pulsed LRF) or by measuring the **phase difference** between a sinusoidally modulated CW laser and its reflected return (phase-shift LRF). The high directionality and speed of light ($c = 3 \times 10^8 \text{ m/s}$) enable millimeter-level accuracy over hundreds of meters.

### Conceptual Analogy
> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Intuitive Analogy — "A Stopwatch Between Two Flashes of Lightning":** Imagine you are exactly between two mountains. Both flash lightning at exactly the same instant. If you time how long the light takes to reach you from each mountain, you can tell which one is closer. A pulsed LRF is like firing a flashbulb at a wall and using a stopwatch triggered by a fast photodiode to measure the time until the reflection arrives. Since light is so fast ($\sim 1 \text{ ns}$ per 30 cm), a tiny timing error means a big distance error — so very high-speed electronics (TDCs) are used.

### Two Sub-Types of LRF
| Sub-Type | Operating Variable | Typical Range | Typical Use |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Pulsed LRF** | Time-of-Flight $\Delta t$ | 1 m → 20 km | Surveying, LIDAR |
| **Phase-Shift LRF** | Phase $\phi$ of modulated signal | 0.1 m → 100 m | Industrial metrology |

### Critical Formulas
- Pulsed LRF: $d = \frac{c \cdot \Delta t}{2}$
- Phase-shift LRF: $d = \frac{c \cdot \phi}{4 \pi f_{mod}}$

---

## 1.6 Optical Triangulation (1D)

### Formal Definition
**Optical Triangulation** is a passive-active hybrid range-sensing method in which a collimated light beam (usually from a laser diode) is projected onto a target, and the position of the reflected spot is imaged through a converging lens onto a linear photodetector array (linear CCD or CMOS). The distance from the lens to the target is then computed from the **lens equation** combined with **similar triangles** in the optical geometry.

### Conceptual Analogy
> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Intuitive Analogy — "The Sun's Shadow in a Sundial":** Hold a stick vertically in the sun. Walk closer to a wall. The shadow's tip on the wall moves. The angle between the stick and the line to the shadow's tip is a triangle. By knowing the stick's length and measuring the shadow's length and angle, you can compute the wall's distance. Optical triangulation builds a fixed triangle between the laser, the lens, and the detector — and the target's position closes the triangle.

### Geometric Layout
The classic triangulation sensor forms a triangle with vertices at:
- **L:** Laser emitter
- **O:** Imaging lens center
- **P:** Target point on object
- **I:** Image of P on the detector

---

## 1.7 Structured Light (2D)

### Formal Definition
**Structured Light** is a 3D active range-sensing technique in which a known 2D pattern (e.g., parallel stripes, a checkerboard, a sinusoidal fringe, or a pseudo-random dot array) is projected onto a target surface. A 2D camera captures the distortion of the pattern caused by the surface geometry. By analyzing how each line/point is displaced and bent, the depth $z(x, y)$ at every pixel can be reconstructed — yielding a full **3D point cloud** of the surface.

### Conceptual Analogy
> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Intuitive Analogy — "The Movie Projector and the Funhouse Mirror":** Imagine projecting a perfect grid of straight lines onto a perfectly flat wall. Now push a thumb into the wall — the lines around your thumb get bent and curved. By measuring exactly *how much* each line is bent, you can mathematically reconstruct the depth of the thumb. Structured light does this with one projector (the slide projector) and one camera (the observer), and a computer solves the depth per pixel.

### Typical Pattern Types
- **Binary coded patterns** (Gray-code)
- **Sinusoidal fringe patterns** (phase-shift profilometry)
- **Pseudo-random dot arrays** (used in Apple Face ID)
- **Moiré fringe patterns**

---

## 1.8 Performance Comparison Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** KTU examiners frequently ask a **comparison table** between these sensor families. Mastering the trade-offs (range vs. accuracy vs. cost vs. environmental robustness) is a 14-mark high-yield topic.

| Feature | IR | Sonar | Laser (ToF) | Triangulation | Structured Light |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Range | 4–80 cm | 2 cm – 10 m | 1 m – 20 km | 1 cm – 1 m | 10 cm – 10 m |
| Accuracy | ±1–3 cm | ±2–5 cm | ±1–5 mm | ±0.01–0.1 mm | ±0.1–2 mm |
| Cost | Very Low | Very Low | High | Medium | Medium–High |
| Beam Divergence | Wide | Very Wide | Very Narrow | Narrow | N/A (wide pattern) |
| Surface Dependence | High (color) | Low (acoustic) | Medium | High (specular) | Low |
| Works in Smoke/Dust | No | Yes (good) | No (poor) | No | No |
| Update Rate | Fast (kHz) | Slow (~20 Hz) | Very Fast (MHz) | Fast (kHz) | Slow (camera-limited) |

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 IR Range Sensor — Working Logic

### Step-by-Step Operational Logic

1. **Modulated IR Emission:** The IR LED is driven by a modulated current (typically at 1 kHz–10 kHz) to reject ambient light interference.
2. **Beam Projection:** A collimating lens (often a molded plastic aspheric) projects the beam to a known angle $\alpha$ (typically 5°–15° from the optical axis).
3. **Diffuse Reflection:** The IR light scatters from the target. For most non-shiny surfaces, this follows **Lambert's Cosine Law**:
$$I_{reflected} = I_0 \cos\theta$$
4. **Reception Lens:** A second lens (often co-axial or offset by baseline $b$) focuses the reflected light onto a PSD.
5. **Spot Position Detection:** The PSD produces two photocurrents $I_A$ and $I_B$ from its two ends. The position of the spot is:
$$x = \frac{L_{PSD}}{2} \cdot \frac{I_A - I_B}{I_A + I_B}$$
where $L_{PSD}$ is the active length of the PSD.
6. **Triangulation Computation:** The distance $z$ is inversely proportional to $x$.

### The Core "Why"
The PSD output current difference is *fundamentally* a geometric property — independent of the target's reflectivity (to first order) because the ratio $(I_A - I_B)/(I_A + I_B)$ is normalized. This makes IR triangulation robust against surface color variations.

### KTU Formula Cheat Sheet — IR Range Sensor

| Formula | Description | Units | Validity |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $z = \frac{f \cdot b}{x}$ | Triangulation depth | m, m, m, m | Triangulation geometry |
| $I_{PSD} = I_A + I_B$ | Total reflected intensity | A | PSD model |
| $x = \frac{L}{2} \cdot \frac{I_A - I_B}{I_A + I_B}$ | Spot position on PSD | m, m, A, A | Linear PSD |
| $SNR \propto \frac{P_{reflected}}{\sqrt{P_{ambient}}}$ | Signal quality | dimensionless | Modulated systems |

---

## 2.2 Sonar (Ultrasonic) Range Sensor — Working Logic

### Step-by-Step Operational Logic

1. **Trigger Pulse:** A 10 µs TTL pulse is sent to the sensor's "Trig" pin.
2. **Piezoelectric Burst:** The transducer emits 8 cycles of 40 kHz sound.
3. **Switch to Receive:** The transducer is electronically switched from TX to RX mode.
4. **Echo Detection:** A comparator thresholds the received envelope.
5. **Time Measurement:** A timer (HC-SR04) outputs a TTL pulse on the "Echo" pin with width equal to the round-trip time $t$.
6. **Distance Computation:**
$$d = \frac{v_s \cdot t}{2}$$
7. **Speed of Sound Compensation:** $v_s$ depends on air temperature $T$ (in °C):
$$v_s(T) = 331.3 + 0.606 \cdot T \;\; \text{m/s}$$

### Engineering Realities
- **Crosstalk:** Multiple sonars in close proximity interfere. Use staggered firing or different $f$.
- **Specular reflection:** At $\theta > 30°$, sound bounces away (no echo).
- **Beam cone:** A typical 40 kHz transducer has a $15°$ beam width.
- **Soft surfaces:** Foam/cloth absorb ultrasound → no echo.

### KTU Formula Cheat Sheet — Sonar

| Formula | Description | Units |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $d = \frac{v_s \cdot t}{2}$ | Distance via round-trip ToF | m, m/s, s |
| $v_s(T) = 331.3 + 0.606T$ | Temperature-corrected speed | m/s, °C |
| $\lambda = \frac{v_s}{f}$ | Acoustic wavelength | m, m/s, Hz |
| $R_{min} = \frac{c_{ring} \cdot t_{burst}}{2}$ | Minimum range (ring-down) | m |
| $\theta_{beam} \approx \frac{\lambda}{D_{transducer}}$ | Beam divergence (rad) | rad, m, m |

---

## 2.3 Laser Range Finder (LRF) — Working Logic

### Step-by-Step: Pulsed ToF LRF

1. **Laser Pulse Generation:** A pulsed laser diode (e.g., 905 nm) emits a 5–10 ns pulse with peak power ~50 W.
2. **Start Pulse:** A silicon avalanche photodiode (APD) generates a "start" signal for a **Time-to-Digital Converter (TDC)**.
3. **Beam Projection:** A lens collimates the pulse toward the target.
4. **Reflection/Scattering:** The target scatters a tiny fraction ($\sim 10^{-9}$ for 1 km).
5. **Return Detection:** A receiver APD detects the weak return → "stop" signal.
6. **TDC Measurement:** The TDC measures $\Delta t$ with picosecond resolution.
7. **Distance Calculation:**
$$d = \frac{c \cdot \Delta t}{2}$$

### Step-by-Step: Phase-Shift LRF

1. **CW Laser Modulation:** A laser diode is amplitude-modulated with $f_{mod}$ (e.g., 10 MHz).
2. **Reference Channel:** A fraction of the emitted light goes to a reference photodiode.
3. **Return Channel:** Reflected light hits a measurement photodiode.
4. **Phase Comparison:** The phase $\phi$ between the two signals is measured.
5. **Distance Calculation:**
$$d = \frac{c \cdot \phi}{4 \pi f_{mod}}$$

### Why Phase-Shift LRF is more Accurate (for short range)
The phase $\phi$ can be measured to $0.1°$ precision.
$$\Delta d = \frac{c \cdot \Delta\phi}{4\pi f_{mod}} = \frac{3 \times 10^8 \cdot 0.1 \cdot \pi/180}{4 \pi \cdot 10^7} = 0.42 \text{ mm}$$
This is why handheld "laser tape measures" (Bosch, Leica) use phase-shift ToF.

### KTU Formula Cheat Sheet — LRF

| Formula | Description | Units |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $d = \frac{c \cdot \Delta t}{2}$ | Pulsed ToF distance | m, m/s, s |
| $d = \frac{c \cdot \phi}{4 \pi f_{mod}}$ | Phase-shift distance | m, m/s, rad, Hz |
| $E_p = \frac{P_{peak} \cdot \tau_{pulse}}{2}$ | Pulse energy | J, W, s |
| $P_{received} = P_{transmitted} \cdot \frac{A_{rx} \cdot \rho}{\pi \cdot \theta^2 \cdot d^4} \cdot e^{-2\sigma d}$ | LIDAR range equation | W |
| $R_{ambiguity} = \frac{c}{2 f_{mod}}$ | Maximum unambiguous range | m |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Utility:** Phase-shift LRF is the technology inside every handheld Bosch/Stanley laser distance meter used by civil engineers and interior designers. Pulsed ToF LRF powers military rangefinders, autonomous vehicle LIDAR (e.g., Velodyne), and satellite laser altimetry (ICESat-2).

---

## 2.4 Optical Triangulation (1D) — Working Logic

### Step-by-Step Operational Logic

1. **Laser Projection:** A visible (650 nm) or IR (780 nm) laser diode projects a spot onto the target.
2. **Imaging:** A converging lens (focal length $f$) images the laser spot onto a linear detector (linear CCD or PSD).
3. **Spot Position:** The spot lands at position $x$ on the detector.
4. **Geometric Computation:** Using similar triangles:
$$z = \frac{f \cdot b}{x}$$
where:
- $z$ = distance from lens to target
- $f$ = focal length of imaging lens
- $b$ = baseline (distance from laser to lens)
- $x$ = spot position on detector (relative to center)

5. **Nonlinearity:** The relation is *inverse*, not linear. At $x \to 0$ (target at infinity), $z \to \infty$. Near the sensor, $z$ changes very rapidly with $x$.

### The "Schief" Problem (Scheimpflug Condition)
For sharp focus across the full measurement range, the laser plane, lens plane, and detector plane must intersect at a single line. This is the **Scheimpflug principle**, essential for high-quality triangulation sensors (e.g., Keyence LK-G series).

### KTU Formula Cheat Sheet — Triangulation

| Formula | Description | Units |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $z = \frac{f \cdot b}{x}$ | Triangulation depth | m |
| $\frac{1}{s_o} + \frac{1}{s_i} = \frac{1}{f}$ | Thin-lens equation | 1/m |
| $\tan\alpha = \frac{x}{f}$ | Receiver angle | rad |
| $\tan\beta = \frac{b}{z}$ | Emitter angle | rad |
| $\Delta z = \frac{f \cdot b}{x} - \frac{f \cdot b}{x + \Delta x}$ | Resolution in $z$ for detector pixel $\Delta x$ | m |

---

## 2.5 Structured Light (2D) — Working Logic

### Step-by-Step Operational Logic

1. **Pattern Projection:** A projector (DLP, LCD, or laser-based) casts a known pattern onto the target.
2. **Camera Capture:** A 2D camera (offset by baseline $b$ from the projector) captures the deformed pattern.
3. **Feature Matching:** Each projected feature (line, dot, fringe) is matched between projector and camera — this is **stereo correspondence** using the projector as a "smart illuminator."
4. **Triangulation Per Pixel:** For each matched pair, the 3D coordinate $(X, Y, Z)$ is computed using the same triangulation equation, applied to *every* pixel.
5. **Point Cloud Assembly:** The result is a 3D point cloud $\{(X_i, Y_i, Z_i)\}_{i=1}^{N}$.

### Why It's Called "Structured Light"
- *Unstructured* light = ambient lighting (gives no depth).
- *Structured* light = a deliberately encoded spatial pattern (gives depth by triangulation).

### Common Encoding Strategies

| Strategy | Principle | Pros | Cons |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Binary stripes (Gray code)** | Sequential on/off stripes encode column index | Simple, robust | Slow (needs N frames) |
| **Sinusoidal fringes** | Phase-shift of sinusoid between frames | High accuracy | Needs multiple frames |
| **Single-shot random dots** | One frame, dot pattern matched to reference | Real-time | Less accurate |
| **Color-coded stripes** | Each stripe has unique color | One frame | Color-dependent |

### KTU Formula Cheat Sheet — Structured Light

| Formula | Description |
| :--- | :--- |
| $Z = \frac{f \cdot b}{d + (x_p - x_c)}$ | Depth at pixel $(x_p, x_c)$ |
| $\phi(x,y) = \arctan\left(\frac{I_4 - I_2}{I_1 - I_3}\right)$ | 4-step phase-shift extraction |
| $Z(x,y) = \frac{L_0 \cdot f}{L_0 + f - \Delta\phi \cdot (L_0/f) \cdot (p/\pi)}$ | Fringe-to-depth map |
| $d_{disparity} = x_{left} - x_{right}$ | Stereo disparity (for explanation only) |

### Real-World Utility
- **Apple Face ID:** 30,000 infrared dots, single-shot, ~30 fps.
- **Microsoft Kinect v1:** Structured-light IR pattern, range 0.8–4 m.
- **3D scanning (Artec, EinScan):** Handheld, turntable, full-body 3D models.
- **Quality control:** Solder paste inspection (SPI), PCB 3D AOI.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Optical Triangulation Depth Equation

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this derivation is high-yield:** This is a guaranteed question in KTU Module 2 — typically 5–7 marks. Memorize the geometry.

### Setup
Consider a 1D triangulation sensor with:
- Laser emitter at point $L$
- Imaging lens of focal length $f$ at point $O$
- Baseline (horizontal separation) $= b$
- Target surface at perpendicular distance $z$ from the lens
- Laser spot on target at $P = (b, z)$ measured from the lens
- Image of $P$ formed at $I$ on a detector located at distance $s_i$ behind the lens, with lateral position $x$ from the optical axis

### Derivation

**Step 1 — Thin lens equation:**
For the imaging lens to focus point $P$ onto the detector:
$$\frac{1}{z} + \frac{1}{s_i} = \frac{1}{f}$$

**Step 2 — Optical axis definition:**
Choose coordinates such that the lens is at origin $(0, 0)$, the laser is at $(-b, 0)$, the target is along $y$-axis at $(0, z)$, and the detector is along the y-axis at $(0, -s_i)$ (behind the lens).

**Step 3 — Image point location:**
The object point $P$ is at $(b, z)$. Through the lens, its image $I$ is at lateral position:
$$x = -\frac{s_i}{z} \cdot b$$

This comes from the property of a thin lens: lateral magnification is $m_T = -s_i / z$.

**Step 4 — Eliminate $s_i$:**
From the thin lens equation:
$$\frac{1}{s_i} = \frac{1}{f} - \frac{1}{z} = \frac{z - f}{fz}$$
$$s_i = \frac{fz}{z - f}$$

**Step 5 — Substitute into the image position equation:**
$$x = -\frac{b}{z} \cdot \frac{fz}{z - f} = -\frac{fb}{z - f}$$

**Step 6 — Solve for $z$:**
$$x(z - f) = -fb$$
$$xz - xf = -fb$$
$$xz = xf - fb$$
$$xz = f(x - b)$$
$$\boxed{z = \frac{f(x - b)}{x}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Common error:** Students often write $z = fb/x$. The correct form is $z = f(x-b)/x$ if $x$ is measured from the position of the laser image at infinity, or simply $z = f b / x$ if $x$ is measured from the *center* of the detector (which is the *image of the optical axis at infinity*). Be **explicit about your coordinate origin** in the exam.

### Numerical Example (KTU-style)

> **Problem:** A triangulation sensor has $f = 20$ mm and $b = 15$ mm. A target produces a spot at $x = 5$ mm from the detector center. Find the distance $z$.

**Solution:**
$$z = \frac{f \cdot b}{x} = \frac{20 \times 10^{-3} \times 15 \times 10^{-3}}{5 \times 10^{-3}}$$
$$z = \frac{300 \times 10^{-6}}{5 \times 10^{-3}} = 60 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}$$
$$\boxed{z = 60 \text{ mm}}$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Sonar Distance from Time-of-Flight

### Setup
An ultrasonic pulse is emitted at $t = 0$ from a transducer. It travels through air at speed $v_s$ to the target, reflects, and returns. The total elapsed time is $t$.

### Derivation

**Step 1 — Define total path length:**
The wave travels from sensor → target → sensor, a total round-trip distance of $2d$.

**Step 2 — Apply the kinematic equation:**
For constant velocity:
$$\text{distance} = \text{velocity} \times \text{time}$$
$$2d = v_s \cdot t$$

**Step 3 — Solve for $d$:**
$$\boxed{d = \frac{v_s \cdot t}{2}}$$

### Numerical Example

> **Problem:** An HC-SR04 measures an echo pulse width of $t = 5800 \mu s$. Air temperature is $25°$C. Find the distance.

**Step 1 — Compute speed of sound at 25°C:**
$$v_s = 331.3 + 0.606 \cdot 25 = 331.3 + 15.15 = 346.45 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the formula:**
$$d = \frac{346.45 \times 5800 \times 10^{-6}}{2}$$
$$d = \frac{346.45 \times 5.8 \times 10^{-3}}{2}$$
$$d = \frac{2.0094}{2}$$
$$\boxed{d \approx 1.0047 \text{ m}}$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Laser Range Finder (Pulsed ToF) Distance

### Setup
A laser pulse is emitted at $t = 0$, hits target at distance $d$, returns at $t = \Delta t$. The pulse propagates in air at speed of light $c$.

### Derivation

**Step 1 — One-way travel time:**
$$t_{one-way} = \frac{d}{c}$$

**Step 2 — Round-trip time:**
$$\Delta t = 2 t_{one-way} = \frac{2d}{c}$$

**Step 3 — Solve for $d$:**
$$\boxed{d = \frac{c \cdot \Delta t}{2}}$$

### Numerical Example

> **Problem:** A pulsed laser rangefinder measures $\Delta t = 66.67$ ns. Find the distance.

**Solution:**
$$d = \frac{3 \times 10^8 \times 66.67 \times 10^{-9}}{2}$$
$$d = \frac{20}{2}$$
$$\boxed{d = 10 \text{ m}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Useful rule of thumb:** A round-trip of 1 ns corresponds to **15 cm** of one-way distance. So if you ever see a TDC value in nanoseconds, just multiply by 0.15 to get meters.

---

## 3.4 Derivation: Phase-Shift LRF Distance

### Setup
A CW laser is amplitude-modulated at frequency $f_{mod}$. The emitted wave is $E(t) = E_0 \cos(2\pi f_{mod} t)$. After traveling distance $d$ to target and back, the wave picks up a phase delay $\phi$ corresponding to the round-trip.

### Derivation

**Step 1 — Phase of one-way trip:**
A wave of frequency $f_{mod}$ has period $T = 1/f_{mod}$. A round-trip time of $\Delta t$ corresponds to a phase:
$$\phi = 2\pi f_{mod} \cdot \Delta t$$

**Step 2 — Substitute $\Delta t = 2d/c$:**
$$\phi = 2\pi f_{mod} \cdot \frac{2d}{c} = \frac{4\pi f_{mod} \cdot d}{c}$$

**Step 3 — Solve for $d$:**
$$\boxed{d = \frac{c \cdot \phi}{4 \pi f_{mod}}}$$

### Validity (Ambiguity Range)
The phase $\phi$ is only unambiguous within $[0, 2\pi)$. When $\phi = 2\pi$, the wave has completed one full cycle — this corresponds to a distance increase of:
$$d_{unambiguous} = \frac{c}{2 f_{mod}}$$
This is the **maximum unambiguous range**. For $f_{mod} = 10$ MHz:
$$d_{unambiguous} = \frac{3 \times 10^8}{2 \times 10^7} = 15 \text{ m}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Higher $f_{mod}$ = better resolution, but smaller range.** Lower $f_{mod}$ = larger range, but coarser resolution. Commercial LRFs use multiple $f_{mod}$ values to span both metrics.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Resolution vs. Range Trade-off

| Sensor Type | Resolution (best) | Range (max) | Update Rate | Surface Required |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **IR Triangulation** | 0.1 mm | 1 m | 1 kHz | Matte, diffuse |
| **Sonar (HC-SR04)** | 3 mm | 4 m | 20 Hz | Any (acoustic) |
| **Pulsed LRF** | 1 mm | 20 km | 100 kHz | Most surfaces |
| **Phase-Shift LRF** | 0.1 mm | 100 m | 10 Hz | Most surfaces |
| **Structured Light** | 0.05 mm (close) | 10 m | 30 Hz | Diffuse, non-mirror |
| **Interferometry** | 0.001 µm | 1 m | 1 MHz | Mirror-like |

### The Fundamental Trade-off Equation (LIDAR)
For all optical range sensors, the **maximum range** is governed by:
$$R_{max} = \left( \frac{P_t \cdot D^2 \cdot \rho \cdot \eta_{rx}}{P_{min} \cdot \theta^2} \right)^{1/4}$$
where:
- $P_t$ = transmitted peak power
- $D$ = receiver aperture diameter
- $\rho$ = target reflectivity
- $\eta_{rx}$ = receiver optical efficiency
- $P_{min}$ = minimum detectable power
- $\theta$ = beam divergence

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight for KTU exams:** Doubling the aperture diameter increases range by a factor of $\sqrt{2} \approx 1.41$, but doubling the power also increases range by $\sqrt[4]{2} \approx 1.19$. So **optics matter more than power** in LIDAR design.

        nodeB -->|Reflected ray| nodeC[Imaging Lens focal f]
        nodeC -->|Image formation| nodeD[Linear CCD Detector]
        nodeD -->|Spot position x| nodeE[Signal Processor]
        nodeE -->|Compute z equals f times b divided by x| nodeF[Distance Output]
    end
    nodeG[Baseline b] -.->|Separation| nodeA
    nodeG -.->|Separation| nodeC
```

## 4.5 Structured Light — 3D Scanning System

graph LR
    nodeA[Pattern Generator DLP or LCD] -->|Projected fringe or dot pattern| nodeB[Target Object 3D]
    nodeB -->|Deformed pattern image| nodeC[Calibrated 2D Camera]
    nodeC -->|Raw image| nodeD[Feature Extraction DSP]
    nodeE[Reference Pattern Memory] -->|Known pattern| nodeD
    nodeD -->|Matched features| nodeF[Triangulation Solver]
    nodeF -->|X Y Z per pixel| nodeG[3D Point Cloud]
    nodeG -->|Mesh generation| nodeH[STL or OBJ Output]
```
