# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**Differentiate between an active transducer and a passive transducer. Give two examples of each.**

**Model Answer (Keywords for 3 marks):**

| Aspect | Active Transducer | Passive Transducer |
|--------|-------------------|--------------------|
| Power source | Self-generating; uses measurand energy | Needs external excitation |
| Auxiliary power | Not required | Required |
| Output energy | Derived from measurand | Derived from external supply |
| Examples | Thermocouple, Piezoelectric crystal, Photovoltaic cell | RTD, LVDT, Strain gauge, Potentiometer |

> **Marking scheme:** 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for distinguishing feature, 1 mark for examples.

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**What is a digital transducer? How is it different from an analog transducer? Mention two applications.**

**Model Answer:**

A **digital transducer** produces an output that is **discrete (stepwise)** in nature — typically binary, BCD, or a pulse train — as a function of the input measurand.

**Differences:**

| Aspect | Analog | Digital |
|--------|--------|---------|
| Output | Continuous function of input | Discrete steps / pulses |
| Resolution | Infinite (in theory) | Limited by bit count / pulse resolution |
| ADC required | Yes, before digital processing | No |
| Examples | Thermocouple, RTD, LVDT | Shaft encoder, incremental encoder, digital tachometer |
| Noise immunity | Lower | Higher |

**Applications:** Shaft encoders in CNC machines, digital tachometers in motor speed monitoring, absolute encoders in robotics.

> **Marking scheme:** 1 mark definition, 1.5 marks differences, 0.5 mark applications.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A

**[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Module 3]**
**(a) [7 marks]** Classify transducers based on the source of energy. Explain the principle and working of any one active transducer in detail.

**(b) [7 marks]** With a neat block diagram, distinguish between primary and secondary transducers using a suitable example. State two advantages and two limitations of each.

---

### Question A — Model Solution

#### (a) [7 marks] — Classification by Source of Energy

**Step 1:** Define source of energy as a basis of classification.

The classification of transducers based on the **source of energy** divides them into two broad categories:
1. **Active Transducers**
2. **Passive Transducers**

**Step 2:** Define Active Transducer (2 marks).

> An **active transducer** is one that does not require an external auxiliary power source for its operation. The output electrical signal is generated directly from the energy of the input measurand using an appropriate physical principle (e.g., Seebeck effect, piezoelectric effect, photovoltaic effect).

**Examples (1 mark):** Thermocouple, piezoelectric crystal, photovoltaic cell, electromagnetic tachogenerator, Hall effect sensor.

**Step 3:** Define Passive Transducer (2 marks).

> A **passive transducer** requires an external auxiliary power source (DC or AC excitation) to operate. The measurand modulates the externally supplied energy (resistance, inductance, capacitance) to produce the output signal.

**Examples (1 mark):** RTD, thermistor, strain gauge, LVDT, potentiometer, capacitive displacement sensor.

**Step 4:** Detailed working of an active transducer — Thermocouple (1 mark for selection + explanation).

**Principle:** Seebeck Effect (Thermoelectric Effect).
**Construction:** Two dissimilar metal wires (e.g., Chromel and Alumel for Type K) joined at one end to form the **hot (measuring) junction**. The other ends are kept at a reference temperature (cold junction) and connected to the measuring instrument.

**Working:** When the hot junction is heated, an EMF is generated due to the temperature difference between the two junctions:

$$V = S \cdot \Delta T = S \cdot (T_{hot} - T_{cold})$$

where $S$ is the **Seebeck coefficient** (in µV/°C) and $\Delta T$ is the temperature difference.

The generated EMF (typically µV to mV) is measured by a millivoltmeter or amplified and digitized. Cold-junction compensation is required for absolute temperature measurement.

---

#### (b) [7 marks] — Primary vs. Secondary Transducer

**Step 1:** Define Primary Transducer (1.5 marks).

> A **primary transducer** (or sensing element) is the first stage in a measurement chain that comes into **direct contact with the measurand** and converts it into a more usable form, typically a mechanical displacement, force, or another physical variable.

**Step 2:** Define Secondary Transducer (1.5 marks).

> A **secondary transducer** (or variable-conversion element) accepts the output of the primary transducer and converts it into an **electrical signal** suitable for further processing, display, or recording.

**Step 3:** Block diagram (2 marks).

```
+----------+      +-----------+      +-------------+      +-----------+
| Measurand| ---> | Primary   | ---> | Intermediate| ---> | Secondary | ---> Electrical
| (P, T, F)|      | Transducer|      | Signal      |      | Transducer|     Output
+----------+      +-----------+      +-------------+      +-----------+
                          (mechanical / hydraulic)        (electrical)
```

**Step 4:** Suitable example — Bourdon Tube + LVDT Pressure Gauge (1.5 marks).

- The **Bourdon tube** is the **primary transducer** — it converts the applied pressure into mechanical displacement (tip travel).
- The **LVDT core**, mechanically coupled to the Bourdon tip, is the **secondary transducer** — it converts the displacement into an AC voltage proportional to pressure.

**Step 5:** Advantages and Limitations (1.5 marks).

| Aspect | Primary Transducer | Secondary Transducer |
|--------|--------------------|----------------------|
| Advantages | Direct measurand contact, simple, robust | Enables electrical readout, signal conditioning, telemetry |
| Limitations | Often produces non-electrical output; not directly readable | Adds error sources (offset, drift, nonlinearity); requires primary to be functional |

---

### Question B (Alternative Choice)

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 3]**
**(a) [7 marks]** Explain the various principles of transduction. Discuss in detail the principle of operation of a piezoelectric transducer and its applications.

**(b) [7 marks]** Classify transducers based on the physical quantity (measurand) they sense. With the help of a neat sketch, explain the working of an LVDT and state two merits and two demerits.

---

### Question B — Model Solution

#### (a) [7 marks] — Principles of Transduction

**Step 1:** Definition (1 mark).

> The **principle of transduction** is the underlying physical law or effect that the transducer exploits to convert input measurand energy into output electrical energy.

**Step 2:** List the major principles (3 marks).

| S.No. | Principle | Measurand | Transducer |
|------|-----------|-----------|-----------|
| 1 | Variable Resistance | Temperature, strain, light | RTD, Strain gauge, LDR |
| 2 | Variable Inductance | Displacement | LVDT |
| 3 | Variable Capacitance | Displacement, level, humidity | Capacitive sensor |
| 4 | Piezoelectric Effect | Force, pressure, accel | Piezo crystal |
| 5 | Photoelectric Effect | Light | Photodiode, phototransistor |
| 6 | Photovoltaic Effect | Light → Voltage | Solar cell |
| 7 | Thermoelectric (Seebeck) | Temperature | Thermocouple |
| 8 | Hall Effect | Magnetic field, current | Hall sensor |
| 9 | Electromagnetic Induction | Velocity, vibration | Tachogenerator, pickup coil |

**Step 3:** Piezoelectric transducer — working (2 marks).

Certain crystals (quartz, Rochelle salt, PZT — lead zirconate titanate) are **anisotropic** and generate an electrical charge on their faces when subjected to mechanical stress. The governing relation is:

$$q = d \cdot F$$

where $d$ is the piezoelectric charge constant (C/N), and $F$ is the applied force. The output voltage is:

$$V = \frac{q}{C_p} = \frac{d \cdot F}{C_p}$$

**Construction:** A piezoelectric crystal sandwiched between two metallic electrodes. A seismic mass is mounted on the crystal so that vibrations produce alternating stress.

**Working:** When an external force/vibration compresses the crystal, charge appears on the electrodes. The charge is proportional to the applied force. A charge amplifier converts this high-impedance charge into a low-impedance voltage signal.

**Step 4:** Applications (1 mark).
- Accelerometers and vibration sensors in industrial machines.
- Pressure sensors in engines, hydraulic systems.
- Ultrasound transducers (medical imaging, SONAR, NDT).
- Microphones and piezo buzzers.
- Quartz watches and oscillators.

---

#### (b) [7 marks] — Classification by Physical Quantity + LVDT

**Step 1:** Classification table (2 marks).

| Physical Quantity | Transducer Examples |
|------------------|---------------------|
| Temperature | RTD, Thermocouple, Thermistor, Bimetallic strip |
| Pressure | Bourdon tube + LVDT, Piezo, Strain gauge diaphragm |
| Displacement | LVDT, Potentiometer, Capacitive sensor |
| Force / Torque | Load cell (strain), Piezoelectric force sensor |
| Flow | Orifice plate + DP cell, Turbine flow meter, Magnetic flow meter |
| Level | Float, Ultrasonic, Capacitive, Conductivity |
| Velocity | Tachogenerator, Doppler sensor |
| Acceleration | Seismometer, Piezo accelerometer |
| Light | LDR, Photodiode, Phototransistor, Solar cell |
| Humidity | Capacitive / Resistive humidity sensor |
| Chemical | pH electrode, Gas sensor |

**Step 2:** LVDT construction and working sketch (2.5 marks).

**Construction (description):** An LVDT (Linear Variable Differential Transformer) consists of:
- One **primary coil** $P$ at the center.
- Two identical **secondary coils** $S_1$ and $S_2$ wound on either side of the primary, connected in **series opposition**.
- A movable **ferromagnetic core** (armature) that slides along the axis of the coils.
- An **AC excitation source** (typically 1–10 kHz, 5–24 V) applied to the primary.

**Working:**
- The primary is energized by AC excitation.
- The core couples the flux from the primary to the two secondaries.
- The two secondaries are connected in **opposite polarity**, so the output is the **difference**:
$$V_{out} = V_{S1} - V_{S2}$$
- When the core is **centered**, $V_{S1} = V_{S2}$, so $V_{out} = 0$ (null position).
- When the core moves **toward $S_1$**, $V_{S1} > V_{S2}$ and $V_{out}$ is positive and proportional to displacement.
- When the core moves **toward $S_2$**, the output is negative.
- Magnitude of $V_{out}$ is proportional to displacement; **phase indicates direction**.

$$V_{out} = K \cdot x \cdot V_{ex} \cdot \sin(\omega t)$$

where $K$ is the sensitivity (V/V/mm), $x$ is the displacement, and $V_{ex}$ is the excitation amplitude.

**Step 3:** Merits and Demerits (2.5 marks).

**Merits:**
1. **Frictionless operation** — no mechanical contact between core and coils, so no wear and infinite resolution.
2. **High linearity** over a wide range (typically ±0.5% of full scale).
3. **Infinite resolution** — output is a smooth continuous function of displacement.
4. **Robust and reliable** — sealed construction, suitable for harsh environments.

**Demerits:**
1. Requires **AC excitation and signal conditioning** (demodulator, filter) — adds complexity.
2. **Dynamic response is limited** by the excitation frequency and mass of the core.
3. Affected by **external magnetic fields** and temperature.
4. **Limited range** — core must remain within the coil assembly.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks**
>
> 1. **Conflating "active/passive" with "analog/digital".** A *digital* transducer can still be *passive* (e.g., a shaft encoder needs DC power). Marks are lost when students write "digital = active".
> 2. **Missing the "principle of transduction" link.** The KTU 2024 marking scheme requires the student to **name the physical effect** (e.g., "Seebeck effect", "piezoelectric effect"). Writing only "transducer" without naming the principle loses 1–2 marks.
> 3. **Confusing primary and secondary transducer roles.** A Bourdon tube alone is *primary*; adding an LVDT makes a *secondary* stage. Students often wrongly call the LVDT "primary" because it gives the electrical output.
> 4. **No block diagram or sketch in 14-mark answers.** KTU examiners deduct up to 2 marks if a labeled diagram/schematic is missing.
> 5. **Units and equations missing.** Any formula (e.g., $V = S \cdot \Delta T$, $q = d \cdot F$) must be quoted with **units** of the coefficients to get full credit.
> 6. **Application list missing.** KTU 2024 ESE expects at least 2–3 real-world applications per long answer.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

### 5.3.1 One-Line Definitions
- **Transducer:** Device that converts one form of energy to another.
- **Active Transducer:** Self-generating; no external power needed.
- **Passive Transducer:** Requires external excitation.
- **Analog Transducer:** Continuous output.
- **Digital Transducer:** Discrete (binary / pulse / BCD) output.
- **Primary Transducer:** First stage; directly contacts measurand.
- **Secondary Transducer:** Second stage; converts primary's output to electrical signal.
- **Principle of Transduction:** The physical law that the transducer exploits.

### 5.3.2 Quick-Fire Examples for Exam Revision

| Transducer | Energy Source | Principle | Measurand | Output |
|------------|---------------|-----------|-----------|--------|
| Thermocouple | Active | Seebeck | Temperature | Analog mV |
| RTD | Passive | Resistive | Temperature | Analog resistance |
| Thermistor | Passive | Resistive | Temperature | Analog resistance |
| LVDT | Passive | Inductive | Displacement | Analog V |
| Strain Gauge | Passive | Resistive | Strain / Force | Analog mV |
| Piezo Crystal | Active | Piezoelectric | Force / Accel | Analog charge |
| Photovoltaic Cell | Active | Photovoltaic | Light | Analog V / A |
| Photodiode | Active* | Photoelectric | Light | Analog current |
| Solar Cell | Active | Photovoltaic | Light | Analog V |
| Hall Sensor | Active | Hall Effect | Magnetic field | Analog V |
| Tachogenerator | Active | Electromagnetic | Speed | Analog V |
| Shaft Encoder | Passive | Optical | Position | Digital binary |
| Bourdon Tube | (Mechanical) | Elastic | Pressure | (Mech.) |
| Capacitive Sensor | Passive | Capacitive | Displacement / Level | Analog F |
| Eddy Current | Passive | Inductive | Displacement | Analog V |

### 5.3.3 Common Pitfalls
- **Photodiode vs. Solar cell:** Both are light sensors, but the photodiode needs a bias and is typically used in reverse-bias (passive mode) while the solar cell self-generates a voltage (active mode).
- **Thermistor vs. RTD:** Both are resistive, but the thermistor has a **non-linear, exponential** $R$–$T$ relationship, while the RTD is approximately **linear** for platinum.
- **Bourdon tube:** Converts pressure → mechanical displacement; **does not** produce an electrical output by itself. Pair with LVDT or strain gauge for electrical readout.
- **Piezoelectric effect is reversible:** Direct effect (mechanical → electrical, used in sensors) and converse effect (electrical → mechanical, used in actuators/buzzers).

### 5.3.4 KTU 2024 Module 3 — Top Memory Hooks
1. **"Active = No Aux Power"** — Mnemonic: **"A**ctive = **A**utonomous."
2. **"Passive = Powered"** — Mnemonic: **"P**assive = **P**lugged in."
3. **"Primary = Physical contact"** — Mnemonic: **"P**rimary = **P**hysical."
4. **"Secondary = Signal conversion"** — Mnemonic: **"S**econdary = **S**ignals."
5. **"Analog = A is for Ampere (continuous)"** — Mnemonic: **"A**nalog = **A**ll values."
6. **"Digital = D is for Discreet"** — Mnemonic: **"D**igital = **D**iscrete."

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Transducer and Inverse Transducer
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCBRT303 - SENSORS AND ACTUATORS > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Transducer and Inverse Transducer # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition A **Transducer** is defined as a device that converts energy from one form to another. In the context of measurement systems and instrumentation, a transducer is a device that converts a physical quantity (the measurand) into a corresponding electrical signal (an output) that can be measured, recorded, and processed. Mathematically, the general transducer relationship is expressed as: $$y = f(x)$$ Where: - $x$ = Input physical quantity (measurand) - $y$ = Output electrical signal - $f$ = Transfer function of the transducer > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Definition (Board-Exam Standard):** *"A transducer is a device that converts one form of energy into another form. In instrumentation, it specifically converts a non-electrical physical quantity into an electrical quantity (or vice versa)."* An **Inverse Transducer** is defined as a device that performs the reverse operation of a transducer. It converts an electrical signal (input) back into a non-electrical physical quantity (output). In other words, while a transducer transforms physical energy into electrical energy, an inverse transducer transforms electrical energy into physical (or non-electrical) energy. $$x = f^{-1}(y)$$ ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy & Intuition Think of a **transducer as a multilingual translator** sitting at the border between two different countries: - **Country A** (the physical world) speaks the language of *heat, pressure, light, force, displacement*. - **Country B** (the electronic world) speaks the language of *voltage, current, and resistance*. A **transducer** is like a translator who listens to Country A and speaks in Country B's language. For example, a microphone "listens" to sound waves (mechanical pressure variations) and "speaks" in electrical voltage signals. An **inverse transducer** is the reverse translator — it takes the electrical "language" from Country B and converts it into a physical phenomenon in Country A. For example, a **loudspeaker** takes an electrical audio signal and converts it back into sound waves that we can hear. > [!NOTE] > **The Senses Analogy:** Human senses are natural transducers! Your **eye** is a transducer (light energy → electrical nerve signals to the brain), and your **muscles** act as inverse transducers (electrical brain signals → physical movement). ## 1.3 Energy Domain Classification A transducer always bridges **two different energy domains**. The most common input domains encountered in KTU examinations are: | Energy Domain | Example Measurand | Typical Transducer Element | |---|---|---| | Mechanical | Force, Pressure, Displacement | Strain Gauge, Bourdon Tube | | Thermal | Temperature, Heat Flow | Thermocouple, RTD, Thermistor | | Electrical | Voltage, Current, Charge | Current Transformer, Hall Sensor | | Magnetic | Magnetic Field Strength | Hall Effect Sensor, Search Coil | | Chemical | Gas Concentration, pH | Electrochemical Cell | | Radiant | Light Intensity | Photodiode, LDR, Phototransistor | > [!TIP] > **KTU Examiner's Tip:** Always identify the **input energy domain** and the **output energy domain** when classifying a transducer in an exam. This is the most common marking scheme breakdown for 3-mark questions. ## 1.4 Need for Transducers in Engineering Modern engineering systems, especially those based on NEP 2020 outcome-based learning, require real-time monitoring and control. The block of any measurement or control system can be summarized as: $$\text{Physical Phenomenon} \xrightarrow{\text{Transducer}} \text{Electrical Signal} \xrightarrow{\text{Processing}} \text{Output / Control Action} \xrightarrow{\text{Inverse Transducer}} \text{Physical Action}$$ Without transducers, we cannot: - **Sense** the environment (smartphones, IoT devices, automobiles, medical equipment). - **Automate** industrial processes (robotics, CNC machines, SCADA systems). - **Monitor** safety-critical systems (aerospace, nuclear plants). # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Operational Breakdown — How a Transducer Works A transducer operates in **three sequential stages**: 1. **Sensing Element (Detector):** The physical quantity is applied to the sensing element, which responds with a measurable change in some property (resistance, capacitance, inductance, voltage, etc.). 2. **Transduction Element:** This intermediate change is converted into an electrical signal by an appropriate transduction mechanism (piezoelectric, electromagnetic, electrochemical, etc.). 3. **Output Stage:** The signal is conditioned, amplified, and presented as a usable output (voltage, current, or digital form). ## 2.2 Comprehensive Classification of Transducers ### A. Based on the Method of Energy Conversion - **Active Transducers (Self-Generating):** Do not require an external power source for operation. They generate their own electrical output (e.g., a thermocouple generates a voltage from a temperature gradient using the Seebeck effect). - **Passive Transducers (Externally Powered):** Require an external excitation signal (typically DC or AC voltage). They operate by changing an electrical parameter such as resistance, capacitance, or inductance in response to the measurand (e.g., a strain gauge changes resistance when stretched). ### B. Based on the Output Signal Type - **Analog Transducers:** Produce a continuous output signal proportional to the input (e.g., RTD for temperature). - **Digital Transducers:** Produce a discrete output, often in the form of pulses or binary codes (e.g., rotary encoders, digital temperature sensors like DS18B20). ### C. Based on the Physical Principle - **Resistive Transducers:** Resistance changes (strain gauge, RTD, LDR, thermistor, potentiometer). - **Capacitive Transducers:** Capacitance changes (capacitive displacement sensor, capacitive level sensor, capacitive humidity sensor). - **Inductive Transducers:** Inductance changes (LVDT, eddy current sensor, variable reluctance sensor). - **Piezoelectric Transducers:** Voltage generated by mechanical stress (piezoelectric accelerometer, piezoelectric force sensor, piezoelectric microphone). - **Photoelectric Transducers:** Light converted to electricity (photodiode, phototransistor, solar cell). - **Thermoelectric Transducers:** Temperature converted to voltage (thermocouple). - **Electrochemical Transducers:** Chemical concentration to voltage (pH electrode, gas sensor). ### D. Based on Application Domain - **Primary Transducer:** Directly responds to the physical stimulus (e.g., the Bourdon tube in a pressure gauge). - **Secondary Transducer:** Converts the output of the primary transducer into an electrical signal (e.g., the LVDT attached to the Bourdon tube that converts its displacement into voltage). ## 2.3 Inverse Transducer — Detailed Analysis An **inverse transducer** converts electrical energy into a non-electrical, physical form. Common examples include: - **Loudspeaker:** Electrical audio signal → Sound waves (mechanical pressure variations in air). - **DC Motor:** Electrical DC input → Mechanical rotational motion (torque and angular velocity). - **Solenoid:** Electrical current → Linear mechanical force and displacement. - **Heater / Resistive Element:** Electrical current → Heat (thermal energy). - **LED / Display:** Electrical current → Light (radiant energy). - **Piezoelectric Buzzer:** Electrical AC signal → Sound waves. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Distinction (Frequently Tested in KTU):** > - A **Thermocouple** is a **transducer** (Temperature → Voltage). > - A **Heater Coil** is an **inverse transducer** (Voltage/Current → Heat). > - A **Microphone** is a **transducer** (Sound → Voltage). > - A **Loudspeaker** is an **inverse transducer** (Voltage → Sound). ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet & Performance Metrics The following equations describe the static and dynamic behavior of a transducer: | Parameter | Formula / Expression | Definition / Unit | Physical Significance | |---|---|---|---| | **Sensitivity** | $K = \dfrac{dy}{dx}$ | Output per unit input (e.g., V/°C, mV/kPa) | Higher sensitivity = larger output for a given input change | | **Span** | $S = x_{max} - x_{min}$ | Range of input values (units of measurand) | Defines operating limits of the transducer | | **Linearity (Static Error)** | $e_L = \dfrac{(y_{actual} - y_{ideal})}{y_{FS}} \times 100\%$ | Percentage of Full-Scale output | Measure of how close the response is to a straight line | | **Hysteresis Error** | $e_H = \dfrac{(y_{up} - y_{down})}{y_{FS}} \times 100\%$ | Percentage of Full-Scale output | Difference in output for increasing vs. decreasing input | | **Repeatability Error** | $e_R = \dfrac{\Delta y_{max}}{y_{FS}} \times 100\%$ | Percentage of Full-Scale output | Ability to reproduce the same output under identical conditions | | **Dead Zone** | Smallest $x$ that produces detectable $y$ | Input units (e.g., °C, kPa) | Insensitivity at very low input magnitudes | | **Resolution** | Smallest detectable change in $x$ | Input units or output units | Finest granularity the transducer can resolve | | **Dynamic Transfer Function** | $\dfrac{Y(s)}{X(s)} = \dfrac{K}{\tau s + 1}$ (1st order) | Dimensionless / s | Describes transient response; $\tau$ = time constant | | **Resonant Frequency** | $f_n = \dfrac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{LC}}$ (for LVDT-like systems) | Hertz (Hz) | Frequency at which the output peaks | > [!NOTE] > **Important for KTU Numericals:** The most commonly tested performance parameter is **Sensitivity**. Always remember that sensitivity is the **slope of the calibration curve** at a given operating point, not necessarily a constant across the full span. ## 2.5 Selection Criteria for Transducers (Engineering Utility) When selecting a transducer for a real engineering application, the following factors must be considered: - **Operating Range:** Must be greater than or equal to the expected input span. - **Sensitivity:** High enough to produce a usable output, but not so high that it saturates. - **Accuracy & Precision:** Should match the tolerance requirements of the application. - **Linearity & Hysteresis:** Affects calibration complexity and measurement reliability. - **Dynamic Response:** Bandwidth must accommodate the frequency content of the measurand. - **Environmental Compatibility:** Temperature, humidity, vibration, EMI, and chemical exposure. - **Size, Weight, and Cost:** Critical in aerospace, biomedical, and consumer electronics applications. ## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Applications | Field | Transducer Used | Inverse Transducer Used | Application | |---|---|---|---| | **Automotive** | Oxygen sensor, MAP sensor, knock sensor | Fuel injector solenoid, EGR valve motor | Engine management & emission control | | **Medical** | ECG electrode, blood pressure sensor, pulse oximeter | Insulin pump actuator, defibrillator pads | Patient monitoring & therapy | | **Aerospace** | Strain gauge, gyroscope, accelerometer | Flight control surface actuator, thrust reverser | Aircraft control & health monitoring | | **Industrial Automation** | Load cell, proximity sensor, flow meter | Servo motor, pneumatic cylinder, solenoid valve | Robotic assembly lines, SCADA | | **Consumer Electronics** | Touchscreen (capacitive), accelerometer, microphone | Vibration motor, speaker, display panel | Smartphones, smartwatches, IoT devices | # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of Transducer Sensitivity from a Linear Calibration Curve Consider a transducer whose output $y$ varies linearly with the input $x$ as shown by its calibration characteristic. The general linear model is: $$y = a + bx$$ Where: - $a$ = Output when input is zero (offset or zero-drift) - $b$ = Slope of the calibration line **Step 1:** Identify two known operating points $(x_1, y_1)$ and $(x_2, y_2)$ from the calibration data. **Step 2:** Substitute both points into the linear equation. $$y_1 = a + b \cdot x_1$$ $$y_2 = a + b \cdot x_2$$ **Step 3:** Subtract the two equations to eliminate $a$. $$y_2 - y_1 = b \cdot (x_2 - x_1)$$ **Step 4:** Solve for the slope $b$, which is the sensitivity $K$. $$b = \frac{y_2 - y_1}{x_2 - x_1}$$ **Step 5:** Express the final sensitivity equation in KTU board-exam notation. $$K = \frac{\Delta y}{\Delta x} \quad \text{(units: output units per input unit)}$$ **Step 6:** Substitute back to find the offset $a$ if required. $$a = y_1 - b \cdot x_1$$ **Step 7:** The complete linear input-output relationship of the transducer becomes: $$y = Kx + a$$ > [!NOTE] > **Logic Explanation:** Steps 1–2 capture the empirical calibration behavior. Step 3 is a standard algebraic technique (elimination) used to find an unknown constant. Step 4 isolates the sensitivity, which is the key performance parameter. Steps 5–7 convert the result into the standard form expected in KTU answer sheets. --- ## 3.2 Derivation of Linearity (Static) Error **Step 1:** Define the ideal linear output of the transducer based on the full-scale (FS) operating point. $$y_{ideal} = K \cdot x$$ **Step 2:** At any given input $x$, measure the actual output of the transducer $y_{actual}$. This may deviate from the ideal straight line due to non-linearity in the sensing mechanism. **Step 3:** Compute the deviation between the actual and ideal outputs at the operating point. $$\Delta y = y_{actual} - y_{ideal}$$ **Step 4:** Express the deviation as a percentage of the full-scale output $y_{FS}$ (which corresponds to $x_{max}$). $$e_{L}\% = \frac{\Delta y}{y_{FS}} \times 100\%$$ **Step 5:** Substitute the expressions for $y_{ideal}$ and $y_{FS}$. $$e_{L}\% = \frac{y_{actual} - K \cdot x}{K \cdot x_{max}} \times 100\%$$ > [!NOTE] > **Logic Explanation:** This derivation quantifies how far the transducer's actual response deviates from the ideal straight-line behavior. Step 1 establishes the benchmark, Step 2 captures the real-world non-ideality, and Step 3–4 normalize the error for fair comparison across different transducers. Step 5 puts the formula in the form most often asked in KTU 14-mark problems. --- ## 3.3 Worked Example: Sensitivity and Linearity Error Calculation **Problem:** A pressure transducer is calibrated using two known pressure standards: - At $P_1 = 2$ bar, the output is $V_1 = 1.0$ V. - At $P_2 = 10$ bar, the output is $V_2 = 5.0$ V. - The full-scale input is $P_{max} = 10$ bar. - At an input of $P_3 = 6$ bar, the actual output is measured as $V_{actual} = 2.85$ V (due to slight non-linearity). **Find:** (a) Sensitivity $K$ in V/bar, (b) The ideal output at $P_3$, (c) The linearity error as a percentage of full-scale output. **Solution:** **Step 1 (Sensitivity):** Using the formula derived in Section 3.1: $$K = \frac{V_2 - V_1}{P_2 - P_1} = \frac{5.0 - 1.0}{10 - 2} = \frac{4.0}{8} = 0.5 \text{ V/bar}$$ **Step 2 (Ideal Output at $P_3$):** Using the ideal linear model: $$V_{ideal} = K \cdot P_3 = 0.5 \times 6 = 3.0 \text{ V}$$ **Step 3 (Full-Scale Output):** $$V_{FS} = K \cdot P_{max} = 0.5 \times 10 = 5.0 \text{ V}$$ **Step 4 (Linearity Error):** Using the formula from Section 3.2: $$e_{L}\% = \frac{V_{actual} - V_{ideal}}{V_{FS}} \times 100\% = \frac{2.85 - 3.0}{5.0} \times 100\% = \frac{-0.15}{5.0} \times 100\% = -3.0\%$$ **Step 5 (Interpretation):** The negative sign indicates the actual output is below the ideal line. The magnitude of linearity error is **3 % of full-scale output**, which is within typical industrial tolerance. --- ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Transducer vs. Inverse Transducer | Property | Transducer | Inverse Transducer | |---|---|---| | **Energy Flow Direction** | Physical (non-electrical) $\rightarrow$ Electrical | Electrical $\rightarrow$ Physical (non-electrical) | | **Power Source Required** | Sometimes (passive type) | Always (active energy conversion required) | | **Output Domain** | Electrical (V, mA, Hz, $\Omega$) | Physical (sound, light, motion, heat) | | **Typical Use Case** | Sensing / Measurement | Actuation / Control | | **Example #1** | Microphone (Sound $\rightarrow$ Voltage) | Loudspeaker (Voltage $\rightarrow$ Sound) | | **Example #2** | Thermocouple (Temperature $\rightarrow$ mV) | Heating Coil (Current $\rightarrow$ Heat) | | **Example #3** | Photodiode (Light $\rightarrow$ Current) | LED (Current $\rightarrow$ Light) | | **Example #4** | Piezoelectric Sensor (Force $\rightarrow$ Voltage) | DC Motor (Current $\rightarrow$ Rotation) | | **Position in Measurement Chain** | Input / Primary stage | Output / Final stage | | **KTU Exam Memory Trick** | "**Trans**ducer — **Trans**lates **f**rom physical to electrical" | "**Inverse** — **I**nverts the conversion direction" | > [!TIP] > **The Energy Triangle:** A unified view is the concept of a **transducer pair** — for every transducer, there exists a corresponding inverse transducer that performs the reverse operation. This symmetry is the basis of **transceivers, transducers-as-actuators, and the duality in IoT devices**. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Functional Block Diagram: Position of a Transducer in a Measurement System The following block diagram maps how a transducer is positioned in a complete measurement and control system. It illustrates the dual role of a transducer (sensor side) and an inverse transducer (actuator side). ```mermaid flowchart LR A["PHYSICAL PHENOMENON
Temperature, Pressure,
Force, Light, Sound"] B["SENSING ELEMENT
Detects the measurand
e.g., bimetallic strip"] C["TRANSDUCTION ELEMENT
Converts to electrical signal
e.g., strain gauge bridge"] D["SIGNAL CONDITIONING
Amplification, Filtering,
Linearization, A/D conversion"] E["DISPLAY OR PROCESSOR
Microcontroller, PLC,
Computer, Cloud"] F["CONTROL SIGNAL
Decisions based on input"] G["INVERSE TRANSDUCER
Electrical to physical form
e.g., heater, motor, speaker"] H["ACTUATED PHYSICAL ACTION
Heat, Motion, Sound, Light"] A --> B B --> C C --> D D --> E E --> F F --> G G --> H H -.feedback loop.-> A ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the Diagram:** Blocks A → D represent the **measurement chain** (transducer side). Block G represents the **inverse transducer**. Block H is the final physical actuation. The dashed feedback line from H back to A closes the **control loop**, which is the foundation of all automated systems. --- ## 4.2 Classification Hierarchy Diagram The following diagram provides a high-level taxonomy of transducers, mapped according to the energy conversion principle. This is a frequent KTU short-answer requirement. ```mermaid flowchart TD T["TRANSDUCER CLASSIFICATION"] A1["ACTIVE TRANSDUCER
Self-generating, no external power
Examples: Thermocouple, Piezoelectric sensor"] A2["PASSIVE TRANSDUCER
Requires external excitation
Examples: Strain gauge, RTD, LVDT"] A3["ANALOG TRANSDUCER
Continuous output
Examples: Thermocouple, LVDT"] A4["DIGITAL TRANSDUCER
Discrete output
Examples: Encoder, digital temperature IC"] A5["RESISTIVE
Strain gauge, RTD, LDR"] A6["CAPACITIVE
Capacitive displacement sensor"] A7["INDUCTIVE
LVDT, variable reluctance"] A8["PIEZOELECTRIC
Piezo accelerometer, force sensor"] A9["PHOTOELECTRIC
Photodiode, solar cell"] A10["THERMOELECTRIC
Thermocouple, thermopile"] A11["ELECTROCHEMICAL
pH probe, gas sensor"] T --> A1 T --> A2 T --> A3 T --> A4 T --> A5 T --> A6 T --> A7 T --> A8 T --> A9 T --> A10 T --> A11 ``` --- ## 4.3 Transducer vs. Inverse Transducer — Directional Energy Flow The following schematic explicitly compares the **direction of energy conversion** between a transducer and an inverse transducer, with concrete engineering examples. ```mermaid flowchart LR P["PHYSICAL DOMAIN
Heat, Force, Light, Sound, Motion"] E["ELECTRICAL DOMAIN
Voltage, Current, Charge, Frequency"] subgraph TRANSDUCER_ZONE ["TRANSDUCER (Sensing Mode)"] T1["Microphone
Sound to Voltage"] T2["Thermocouple
Temperature to mV"] T3["Photodiode
Light to Current"] end subgraph INVERSE_ZONE ["INVERSE TRANSDUCER (Actuation Mode)"] I1["Loudspeaker
Voltage to Sound"] I2["Heater Coil
Current to Heat"] I3["LED
Current to Light"] end P --> T1 T1 --> E P --> T2 T2 --> E P --> T3 T3 --> E E --> I1 I1 --> P E --> I2 I2 --> P E --> I3 I3 --> P ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **Diagram Interpretation:** Notice the symmetry of the arrows. In the **Transducer Zone**, arrows flow from Physical → Electrical. In the **Inverse Transducer Zone**, arrows flow from Electrical → Physical. This visual symmetry is the easiest way to remember the difference in any KTU board exam. --- ## 4.4 Closed-Loop Control System — Full Block Diagram The following block diagram represents the **complete closed-loop system** where a transducer senses and an inverse transducer actuates, forming the backbone of industrial automation. ```mermaid flowchart LR REF["SETPOINT
Reference input"] CMP["COMPARATOR
Error detector"] ERR["ERROR SIGNAL
e = Setpoint - Feedback"] CTRL["CONTROLLER
PID or fuzzy logic"] DAC["DAC / POWER AMP
Signal conditioning"] INV["INVERSE TRANSDUCER
Actuator: motor, heater, valve"] PROC["PROCESS / PLANT
Physical system under control"] SENS["TRANSDUCER
Sensor: LVDT, RTD, strain gauge"] FBK["FEEDBACK SIGNAL
Measured output"] OUT["CONTROLLED OUTPUT
Desired physical quantity"] REF --> CMP FBK --> CMP CMP --> ERR ERR --> CTRL CTRL --> DAC DAC --> INV INV --> PROC PROC --> OUT OUT --> SENS SENS --> FBK FBK --> CMP ``` > [!NOTE] > **Why This Matters for KTU Examinations:** This diagram combines both a **transducer** (sensing branch) and an **inverse transducer** (actuation branch) in a single closed loop. KTU 14-mark questions frequently ask students to "explain with a block diagram" the role of both types in a control system. Mastering this diagram guarantees full marks on such questions. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > [!NOTE] > **KTU Pattern:** Part A questions test direct recall and concise conceptual understanding. Each answer should be 4–6 lines, with a clear definition, classification, and at least one example. ### Question 1. Define a transducer. Classify transducers based on the energy conversion principle, with one example for each class. [3 Marks] **[KTU University Exam - May 2024 / Model Question Paper]** **Model Answer:** A transducer is a device that converts energy from one form to another. In instrumentation, it converts a non-electrical physical quantity (the measurand) into an electrical signal that can be measured, processed, and recorded. Classification based on the principle of energy conversion: - **Resistive Transducer:** Resistance changes with the measurand. *Example:* Strain gauge (resistance changes with applied force). - **Inductive Transducer:** Inductance changes with the measurand. *Example:* Linear Variable Differential Transformer (LVDT) for displacement. - **Capacitive Transducer:** Capacitance changes with the measurand. *Example:* Capacitive displacement sensor. - **Piezoelectric Transducer:** Voltage is generated when subjected to mechanical stress. *Example:* Piezoelectric accelerometer. - **Thermoelectric Transducer:** Voltage is generated from a temperature gradient. *Example:* Thermocouple. --- ### Question 2. Differentiate between a transducer and an inverse transducer. Give two examples of each. [3 Marks] **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]** **Model Answer:** | Sl. No. | Transducer | Inverse Transducer | |---|---|---| | 1 | Converts physical (non-electrical) energy into electrical energy. | Converts electrical energy into physical (non-electrical) energy. | | 2 | Used for sensing and measurement. | Used for actuation and control. | | 3 | Example 1: **Microphone** — Sound waves → Electrical voltage. | Example 1: **Loudspeaker** — Electrical voltage → Sound waves. | | 4 | Example 2: **Thermocouple** — Temperature → Millivolts. | Example 2: **DC Motor** — Electrical current → Mechanical rotation. | > [!WARNING] > **Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Part A):** Many students write generic examples like "a sensor" or "an actuator" without naming a specific device. **This costs 1 to 2 marks.** Always name the specific device (e.g., *thermocouple*, *LVDT*, *piezoelectric crystal*, *photodiode*). --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice) > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Part B questions in the End Semester Examination carry 14 marks and offer an internal choice between two complete questions. Each question typically has two sub-parts (a) and (b), with 7 marks each. The sub-parts test both conceptual understanding and mathematical problem-solving ability. --- ### 🔹 Question A (14 Marks) **Question A (a):** With a neat block diagram, explain the working of a measurement system. Clearly identify the role of the transducer and the inverse transducer. State any four static characteristics of a transducer. [7 Marks] **Question A (b):** A thermocouple-based temperature transducer has the following calibration data: - At $T_1 = 100^\circ C$, output $V_1 = 4.2$ mV - At $T_2 = 300^\circ C$, output $V_2 = 12.6$ mV Find: (i) The sensitivity of the transducer in mV/°C. (ii) The output voltage at $T = 250^\circ C$. (iii) The offset voltage $a$ in the equation $V = a + KT$. [7 Marks] **[KTU University Exam - May 2024]** --- #### Model Solution to Question A (a) **Step 1:** Draw and label the block diagram of a general measurement system. ``` Physical Quantity → Sensor / Sensing Element → Transducer → Signal Conditioning → Display / Output ``` Refer to Section 4.1 of these notes for the detailed functional block diagram. **Step 2:** Role of the **transducer** in the measurement system: - The transducer converts the non-electrical physical input quantity (e.g., temperature, pressure, force) into a corresponding electrical signal (e.g., mV, V, mA). - It is positioned at the **input stage** of the measurement chain. - *Example:* A thermocouple converts temperature into millivolts. **Step 3:** Role of the **inverse transducer** in the measurement system: - The inverse transducer converts the processed electrical signal back into a physical quantity, used to actuate or control the process. - It is positioned at the **output stage** of the measurement chain. - *Example:* A heater coil converts electrical current into heat. **Step 4:** List the four static characteristics: - **Accuracy:** Closeness of the measured value to the true value. - **Sensitivity:** Ratio of change in output to change in input. - **Linearity:** Degree to which the output is proportional to the input. - **Drift:** Change in output over time for a constant input. *(Additional acceptable characteristics: Hysteresis, Repeatability, Resolution, Span, Dead Zone.)* --- #### Model Solution to Question A (b) **Step 1 — Sensitivity Calculation:** Using the sensitivity formula: $$K = \frac{V_2 - V_1}{T_2 - T_1} = \frac{12.6 - 4.2}{300 - 100} = \frac{8.4}{200} = 0.042 \text{ mV/°C}$$ **Step 2 — Output Voltage at $T = 250^\circ C$:** Using the ideal linear model $V = KT$ (assuming the line passes through the origin for simplicity): $$V_{250} = K \cdot T = 0.042 \times 250 = 10.5 \text{ mV}$$ **Step 3 — Offset Voltage $a$:** Use the linear model with offset $V = a + KT$. Substituting point $(T_1, V_1) = (100, 4.2)$: $$4.2 = a + (0.042)(100)$$ $$4.2 = a + 4.2$$ $$a = 0 \text{ mV}$$ **Verification** at $(T_2, V_2) = (300, 12.6)$: $$V_2 = 0 + (0.042)(300) = 12.6 \text{ mV} \checkmark$$ Therefore, the calibration line passes through the origin, and the offset is **0 mV**. The transducer equation simplifies to: $$V = 0.042 \cdot T \quad \text{(mV, with T in °C)}$$ > [!WARNING] > **Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Question A):** Many students forget to include the **units** in the final sensitivity answer. KTU examiners deduct **0.5 to 1 mark** for missing units. Always write "$K = 0.042$ mV/°C" — not just "$0.042$". Also, in the diagram, every block must be labeled. An unlabeled block diagram loses at least 1 mark. --- ### 🔹 Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice **Question B (a):** What is an inverse transducer? Describe with a neat diagram the working of any two inverse transducers. State one application of each. [7 Marks] **Question B (b):** A pressure transducer has a sensitivity of $0.8$ V/bar and an offset of $0.5$ V. If the full-scale input pressure is $20$ bar, calculate: (i) The output voltage at $P = 12$ bar. (ii) The full-scale output voltage. (iii) The percentage linearity error if the actual output at $P = 12$ bar is measured to be $9.8$ V. [7 Marks] **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]** --- #### Model Solution to Question B (a) **Step 1 — Definition:** An inverse transducer is a device that converts an electrical quantity (input) into a non-electrical physical quantity (output). It performs the reverse operation of a transducer and is used in the **actuation stage** of a control or measurement system. **Step 2 — Working of a DC Motor (Inverse Transducer #1):** - **Construction:** Armature winding, field winding, commutator, brushes. - **Working:** When DC voltage is applied across the armature terminals, current flows through the conductors. The interaction between the current-carrying conductors and the magnetic field of the field winding produces a **mechanical torque** ($T = K_t \cdot I_a$) on the armature. - **Output:** Rotational mechanical motion (torque and angular velocity). - **Application:** Used in robotic arms, electric vehicles, CNC machine tools, and household appliances. **Step 3 — Working of a Piezoelectric Buzzer / Loudspeaker (Inverse Transducer #2):** - **Construction:** Piezoelectric crystal disc (often PZT ceramic) bonded to a metal diaphragm, enclosed in a plastic housing. - **Working:** When an AC electrical signal is applied across the crystal electrodes, the crystal undergoes **inverse piezoelectric effect** — it deforms mechanically in proportion to the applied voltage. The mechanical vibration is transferred to the diaphragm, which pushes the surrounding air and produces sound waves. - **Output:** Audible sound waves. - **Application:** Used in alarm systems, smoke detectors, electronic doorbells, and computer motherboards. > [!NOTE] > **Diagram Requirement:** Include a labeled cross-sectional diagram of both devices, identifying the electrical input terminals and the physical output (rotation / sound). Block diagrams are acceptable for full marks if clear and well-labeled. --- #### Model Solution to Question B (b) **Given Data:** - Sensitivity $K = 0.8$ V/bar - Offset $a = 0.5$ V - Full-scale input $P_{max} = 20$ bar - Operating point: $P = 12$ bar - Actual output at $P = 12$ bar: $V_{actual} = 9.8$ V **Step 1 (Output Voltage at $P = 12$ bar):** The transducer equation is $V = a + K \cdot P$. $$V_{ideal} = 0.5 + (0.8)(12) = 0.5 + 9.6 = 10.1 \text{ V}$$ **Step 2 (Full-Scale Output Voltage):** $$V_{FS} = a + K \cdot P_{max} = 0.5 + (0.8)(20) = 0.5 + 16 = 16.5 \text{ V}$$ **Step 3 (Percentage Linearity Error):** $$e_{L}\% = \frac{V_{actual} - V_{ideal}}{V_{FS}} \times 100\% = \frac{9.8 - 10.1}{16.5} \times 100\%$$ $$e_{L}\% = \frac{-0.3}{16.5} \times 100\% \approx -1.818\%$$ The magnitude of linearity error is approximately **1.82 % of full-scale output**, with the negative sign indicating the actual output is below the ideal straight-line response. > [!WARNING] > **Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Question B):** A common mistake is to compute the full-scale output as **only** $K \cdot P_{max}$ (i.e., $16$ V) and **ignore the offset** $0.5$ V. The full-scale output is the output at the **maximum rated input**, which **must include the offset** if the calibration equation has a non-zero offset. This is a 1-mark deduction. Also, students sometimes forget the **percentage sign** in the final linearity error — a costly omission. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 Board Examination** ### 🔑 Key Definitions - **Transducer:** Converts physical (non-electrical) energy → electrical energy. - **Inverse Transducer:** Converts electrical energy → physical (non-electrical) energy. - **Active Transducer:** Self-generating, no external power required. - **Passive Transducer:** Requires external excitation (DC or AC). ### 🔑 Critical Concepts - A transducer is **always** an energy converter — never just a signal amplifier. - A transducer is placed in the **input stage** of a measurement system. - An inverse transducer is placed in the **output stage** of a control system. - Sensitivity $K = \dfrac{dy}{dx}$ is the slope of the calibration curve. - The output equation of a linear transducer: $y = a + Kx$. - Full-scale output $V_{FS} = a + K \cdot x_{max}$. - Linearity error is **always** expressed as a **percentage of full-scale output**. ### 🔑 Common Transducer Examples (Memorize These) - *Sound to voltage:* Microphone, piezoelectric sensor. - *Temperature to voltage:* Thermocouple, RTD, thermistor. - *Light to current:* Photodiode, phototransistor, solar cell. - *Force to voltage:* Piezoelectric force sensor, load cell. - *Displacement to voltage:* LVDT, strain gauge, capacitive sensor. ### 🔑 Common Inverse Transducer Examples (Memorize These) - *Voltage to sound:* Loudspeaker, piezoelectric buzzer. - *Voltage/Current to heat:* Heater coil, resistive heating element. - *Current to light:* LED, OLED display, incandescent lamp. - *Current to motion:* DC motor, stepper motor, solenoid. - *Current to pressure/vibration:* Solenoid valve, vibration motor. ### 🔑 Formulae to Memorize (One-Liners for Last-Minute Revision) - $K = \dfrac{V_2 - V_1}{T_2 - T_1}$ - $V_{ideal} = a + K \cdot x$ - $V_{FS} = a + K \cdot x_{max}$ - $e_{L}\% = \dfrac{V_{actual} - V_{ideal}}{V_{FS}} \times 100\%$ - $e_{H}\% = \dfrac{V_{up} - V_{down}}{V_{FS}} \times 100\%$ ### 🔑 Frequently Asked Exam Traps - Confusing a **transducer** with a **sensor** — every sensor is a transducer, but not every transducer is a sensor. - Confusing a **thermocouple** (transducer) with a **thermistor** (passive transducer) — they work on **different principles** (Seebeck effect vs. resistance change). - Forgetting to include the **offset $a$** when calculating the full-scale output. - Writing linearity error as a decimal instead of a percentage. - Forgetting to state **units** for sensitivity (e.g., V/°C, mV/kPa, mA/N). ### 🔑 High-Yield Sentence Starters (For 14-Mark Answers) - *"A transducer is a device that converts one form of energy into another…"* - *"An inverse transducer performs the reverse operation of a transducer by converting…"* - *"The sensitivity of a transducer is defined as the ratio of change in output to change in input…"* - *"A thermocouple is an active transducer that generates a millivolt-level emf using the Seebeck effect…"* - *"The full-scale output is obtained by substituting the maximum rated input into the calibration equation…"* > [!TIP] > **Final KTU Exam Strategy:** Always start any transducer-related answer with a **one-sentence definition** in bold or underlined. Examiners use a "definition-first" evaluation pattern. After the definition, follow up with a **labeled block diagram** and a **real-world example**. This three-part structure is the most reliable way to secure full marks.

topics.title: Position Transducers; Displacement Transducer - LVDTs, Captive Armatures, Unguided Armatures, Force-Extended Armatures
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBRT303 - SENSORS AND ACTUATORS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Position Transducers; Displacement Transducer - LVDTs, Captive Armatures, Unguided Armatures, Force-Extended Armatures

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Position Transducers — Formal Definition

A **Position Transducer** is a sensing device that converts the spatial position (linear displacement or angular rotation) of a measured object into a proportional, measurable electrical signal (voltage, current, or digital count). According to the KTU 2024 Scheme (PCBRT303, Module 4), position transducers form the foundational category from which displacement, proximity, and level sensors are derived.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Spirit):**
> *A position transducer is a device used for measuring the position of an object, usually in terms of linear or angular displacement. The most commonly used position transducer for linear displacement measurement is the LVDT.*

Position transducers are broadly classified as:

| Class | Operating Principle | Example |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Resistive** | Resistance change with wiper movement | Potentiometer |
| **Inductive** | Mutual inductance variation | **LVDT** |
| **Capacitive** | Capacitance variation with plate gap | Capacitive displacement sensor |
| **Optical** | Light interruption/encoding | Optical encoder |
| **Magnetic** | Hall effect / magnetostriction | Hall sensor |

## 1.2 Displacement Transducers — Conceptual Anchor

A **displacement transducer** is a sub-class of position transducer dedicated specifically to quantifying the magnitude of motion (in mm, μm, or m) of a body from a reference point. Two key metrics govern all displacement transducers:

- **Range** — the maximum measurable travel (typically **0.1 mm to 500 mm** for industrial LVDTs).
- **Resolution** — the smallest detectable change (LVDTs achieve sub-μm resolution).

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy — The "Telescope Ruler":**
> Imagine a telescopic ruler that lengthens as you pull it. If a tiny generator were attached to this ruler, pulling it would produce a voltage. An LVDT works exactly like this — a magnetic "ruler" (iron core) slides through a transformer, and the *electrical balance* of that transformer tells you *exactly* how far the ruler has been pulled — **without ever touching the moving part**.

## 1.3 The LVDT — Linear Variable Differential Transformer

The **Linear Variable Differential Transformer (LVDT)** is a passive inductive transducer that converts linear displacement into a differential AC voltage. The KTU 2024 Module 4 syllabus designates it as the **principal displacement transducer** for board examination purposes.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **LVDT — Core Definition (Board-Ready):**
> *An LVDT is an electromechanical transducer used to convert linear motion (displacement) of an object into a corresponding electrical signal. It consists of a movable soft-iron core and a transformer with one primary winding and two identical secondary windings connected in series opposition.*

### Constructional Anatomy

1. **Primary Winding (P):** Wound at the centre of a hollow cylindrical former. Energized by a stable AC source (typically **1 V to 24 V RMS** at **50 Hz to 20 kHz**).
2. **Two Secondary Windings (S₁ and S₂):** Identical in number of turns, wound symmetrically on either side of the primary.
3. **Movable Core (Armature):** A high-permeability soft-iron (or nickel-iron) cylinder that slides axially inside the former bore.
4. **Former/Housing:** Non-magnetic stainless steel casing that shields the assembly and provides mechanical rigidity.
5. **Shielding:** Mu-metal canisters to block external magnetic interference (stray field rejection ≥ **100 dB** in premium grades).

### Physical Constants & Standards (KTU High-Yield)

| Parameter | Typical Value / Standard |
| :--- | :--- |
| Excitation Frequency | **50 Hz to 20 kHz** (audio range preferred) |
| Excitation Voltage | **1 V to 24 V RMS** |
| Operating Range | **±0.1 mm to ±500 mm** |
| Linearity | **±0.1 % to ±0.5 % of Full Scale (FS)** |
| Resolution | **Infinite (theoretically)** — limited by detector noise |
| Operating Temperature | **−55 °C to +150 °C** (military grade) |
| Output at Null | **0 V (theoretically)** — residual voltage ≤ **0.5 % FS** |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Working Principle — The Heart of the LVDT

The LVDT operates on the principle of **mutual inductance variation** between the primary winding and the two secondaries, governed by the position of a movable ferromagnetic core.

### Step-by-Step Operating Logic

1. The primary winding is energized by a stable sinusoidal AC voltage $e_p = E_p \sin(\omega t)$.
2. This produces an alternating primary current $i_p$, which generates an alternating magnetic flux $\Phi_p$ in the core region.
3. The flux links the two secondary windings $S_1$ and $S_2$ through the soft-iron movable core.
4. The induced EMFs in the secondaries are $e_{s1}$ and $e_{s2}$.
5. The secondaries are connected in **series opposition** (anti-series), so the net output is:
   $$e_{out} = e_{s1} - e_{s2}$$
6. **At Null Position (Core Centred):** Flux linkage with $S_1$ = flux linkage with $S_2$, hence $e_{s1} = e_{s2}$ and $e_{out} = 0$.
7. **Core Displaced Towards S₁:** Flux coupling with $S_1$ increases → $e_{s1}$ grows; flux coupling with $S_2$ decreases → $e_{s2}$ shrinks. The net $e_{out}$ is in phase with the primary.
8. **Core Displaced Towards S₂:** Reverse phenomenon → $e_{out}$ is 180° out of phase with the primary.

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Why" Behind Differential Output:**
> Using *differential* (not absolute) output cancels common-mode noise, eliminates the DC offset problem, and provides **directional information** (sign of displacement) through phase reversal — a single elegant trick that makes the LVDT superior to single-coil inductors.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet — The Cheat-Sheet Table

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $e_p = E_p \sin(\omega t)$ | Primary excitation (AC source) | Volts |
| 2 | $e_{s1} = -M_1 \dfrac{di_p}{dt}$ | Induced EMF in secondary 1 | Volts |
| 3 | $e_{s2} = -M_2 \dfrac{di_p}{dt}$ | Induced EMF in secondary 2 | Volts |
| 4 | $e_{out} = e_{s1} - e_{s2} = -\left(M_1 - M_2\right) \dfrac{di_p}{dt}$ | Net differential output | Volts |
| 5 | $M_1 = M_2$ at null | Null condition | Henry |
| 6 | $E_{out,rms} = K_s \cdot x$ | Linear region output (RMS) | Volts |
| 7 | $K_s = \dfrac{dE_{out}}{dx}$ | Sensitivity of LVDT | V/mm |
| 8 | $S = \dfrac{\Delta E_{out}}{\Delta x}$ | Static sensitivity (alternate notation) | V/mm |
| 9 | $e_{residual} = \sqrt{e_{s1}^2 + e_{s2}^2} \neq 0$ | Residual voltage (non-zero even at null) | Volts |
| 10 | $L \% = \dfrac{\text{Actual Output} - \text{Ideal Output}}{\text{FS Output}} \times 100$ | Per cent linearity (KTU favourite) | % |
| 11 | $\phi = \tan^{-1}\left(\dfrac{\omega L}{R}\right)$ | Phase angle of primary winding | radians |
| 12 | $f = \dfrac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{LC}}$ | Natural frequency of LVDT tank | Hz |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL KTU Notation Rule:** In your answer scripts, **always** draw a Mermaid-style block diagram of the LVDT showing primary in the centre and secondaries on either side. Examiners specifically award **2 marks for the diagram** in the 14-mark question.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

LVDTs are the **workhorse of industrial metrology** and are used as the sensing element in:

- **Aircraft flight-control surface position feedback** (Boeing, Airbus).
- **Hydraulic / pneumatic cylinder piston-position monitoring** in CNC machines.
- **Turbine blade-tip clearance measurement** in jet engines.
- **Civil-structural health monitoring** (bridge deflection, dam tilt).
- **Medical devices:** MRI-compatible patient table positioning.
- **Process control:** Bourdon-tube pressure gauges, float-level sensors in fuel tanks.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why LVDT beats Potentiometer (frequently asked KTU question):**
> - Infinite resolution (potentiometer has wire-wound granularity).
> - Frictionless operation (no wiper wear).
> - Infinite mechanical life (potentiometer: ~10⁶ cycles).
> - Operates in harsh environments (high temperature, vibration, radiation).

# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation I — Output Voltage Expression of LVDT

**Objective:** Derive an explicit, position-dependent expression for the differential output voltage of the LVDT.

**Given / Assumed:**
- Primary excitation: $e_p = E_p \sin(\omega t)$ where $\omega = 2\pi f$.
- Primary current: $i_p = I_p \sin(\omega t - \phi)$, where $\phi$ is the phase angle introduced by the inductive primary winding.
- Number of primary turns: $N_p$.
- Number of turns in each secondary (assumed equal): $N_s$.
- Mutual inductance between primary and $S_1$: $M_1$.
- Mutual inductance between primary and $S_2$: $M_2$.

**Step 1 — Express the induced EMFs in the two secondaries.**

By Faraday's law of electromagnetic induction, the EMF induced in a coil is the negative rate of change of flux linkage, equivalently expressed through mutual inductance:

$$e_{s1} = -M_1 \cdot \frac{di_p}{dt}$$

$$e_{s2} = -M_2 \cdot \frac{di_p}{dt}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the time derivative of primary current.**

$$i_p = I_p \sin(\omega t - \phi)$$

Differentiating with respect to time:

$$\frac{di_p}{dt} = \omega I_p \cos(\omega t - \phi)$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the derivative back into the EMF expressions.**

$$e_{s1} = -M_1 \cdot \omega I_p \cos(\omega t - \phi)$$

$$e_{s2} = -M_2 \cdot \omega I_p \cos(\omega t - \phi)$$

**Step 4 — Apply the series-opposition connection rule.**

The two secondary windings are connected in **series opposition**, meaning their polarities are arranged such that the resultant output is their algebraic *difference*:

$$e_{out} = e_{s1} - e_{s2}$$

Substituting:

$$e_{out} = -\omega I_p \cos(\omega t - \phi) \cdot (M_1 - M_2)$$

**Step 5 — Analyse the null position (core centred).**

When the movable core is at the geometric centre of the former, by symmetry the flux coupling with $S_1$ exactly equals the flux coupling with $S_2$:

$$M_1 = M_2 = M_0$$

Substituting:

$$e_{out} = -\omega I_p \cos(\omega t - \phi) \cdot (M_0 - M_0)$$

$$\boxed{e_{out} = 0 \quad \text{(at null position)}}$$

This is the **null position** — the LVDT's reference zero.

**Step 6 — Express the differential mutual inductance for small displacement.**

For a small axial displacement $x$ of the core from the null, the change in mutual inductance is approximately linear in $x$ (within the linear operating range of the LVDT):

$$M_1 - M_2 = K_m \cdot x$$

where $K_m$ is the **mutual-inductance sensitivity** (in Henry per mm).

**Step 7 — Final expression for output voltage (general form).**

$$\boxed{e_{out} = -\omega I_p K_m \cdot x \cdot \cos(\omega t - \phi)}$$

**Step 8 — Convert to RMS form for engineering use.**

Since the output is sinusoidal with peak amplitude $\hat{E}_{out} = \omega I_p K_m x$:

$$E_{out,rms} = \frac{\omega I_p K_m x}{\sqrt{2}}$$

Combining all constant terms (excitation, frequency, coil geometry) into a single sensitivity constant $K_s$:

$$\boxed{E_{out,rms} = K_s \cdot x}$$

This is the **defining linear transfer function** of the LVDT and is the most-quoted equation in KTU board exams.

**Step 9 — Directional (phase) information.**

- If $x > 0$ (core moves towards $S_1$): $e_{out}$ is **in phase** with primary voltage.
- If $x < 0$ (core moves towards $S_2$): $e_{out}$ is **180° out of phase** with primary voltage.

A **Phase-Sensitive Detector (PSD)** decodes this phase to determine displacement direction and produce a bipolar DC output.

## 3.2 Derivation II — Sensitivity and Linearity of LVDT

**Static Sensitivity** is the slope of the linear region of the $E_{out}$-vs-$x$ curve:

$$S = \frac{dE_{out}}{dx} = K_s \quad \text{(units: V/mm)}$$

**Per-cent Linearity** (KTU favourite 3-mark question) is defined as:

$$L \% = \frac{\text{Maximum Deviation from Best-Fit Straight Line}}{\text{Full-Scale Output}} \times 100$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Tip:** Always quote the linearity in the form **"±0.5 % of FS"** rather than absolute voltages, because the full-scale output depends on the specific LVDT model.

## 3.3 Analytical Worked Example

**Problem (KTU Style):**
> An LVDT has the following specifications:
> - Excitation voltage: $E_p = 5$ V RMS at $f = 1000$ Hz
> - Sensitivity: $K_s = 0.4$ V/mm
> - Core displacement: $x = 3$ mm
> Find (a) the RMS output voltage, (b) the peak output voltage, and (c) the direction of displacement if the output is in phase with primary.

**Solution:**

**(a) RMS output voltage:**

$$E_{out,rms} = K_s \cdot x = 0.4 \, \frac{\text{V}}{\text{mm}} \times 3 \, \text{mm}$$

$$E_{out,rms} = 1.2 \, \text{V (RMS)}$$

**(b) Peak output voltage:**

$$E_{out,peak} = \sqrt{2} \cdot E_{out,rms} = \sqrt{2} \times 1.2$$

$$E_{out,peak} = 1.697 \, \text{V (peak)}$$

**(c) Direction:**

Output is in phase → the core has moved **towards the secondary winding $S_1$** (the "positive" side).

Since Mermaid is a 2-D graph renderer, the above functional-architecture diagrams provide a **board-acceptable equivalent** that examiners recognise as legitimate schematic representation, especially when supplemented by hand-drawn diagrams in the answer booklet.

## 4.5 Detailed Description of the Three Armature Types

### 4.5.1 Captive Armature (Guided Armature)

- **Construction:** The soft-iron core is permanently attached to the measured object via a non-magnetic push-rod that passes through a sealed bearing at one end of the LVDT housing.
- **Working:** As the object moves linearly, the rod drags the core through the bore. Since the rod is constrained by the bearing, motion is purely **axial** — no radial wander.
- **Advantages:**
  - Eliminates side-load and eccentricity errors.
  - Provides excellent linearity (±0.1 % FS achievable).
  - Robust for industrial environments.
- **Disadvantages:**
  - Seal friction introduces a small stiction (≈ 0.5 N typical).
  - Requires alignment of the rod with the LVDT axis.
- **Applications:** CNC machine-tool positioning, hydraulic-cylinder piston-position feedback, materials-testing rigs (extensometers).

### 4.5.2 Unguided Armature (Free-Core / Unattached Armature)

- **Construction:** The core is **not** mechanically linked to the measured object. It sits free inside the bore and is driven by a separate mechanical coupling (e.g., a spring-mass system).
- **Working:** Often used in **seismic-mass accelerometers** — the core is attached to the seismic mass, and a relative displacement occurs between the core and the LVDT coil assembly when the case experiences acceleration.
- **Advantages:**
  - Extremely low moving mass → high natural frequency (up to **500 Hz** typical).
  - No friction, no stiction.
  - Infinite resolution.
- **Disadvantages:**
  - Susceptible to external shock and vibration pickup.
  - Limited to short ranges (±1 mm typical).
- **Applications:** Seismic accelerometers, vibration sensors, tilt sensors.

### 4.5.3 Force-Extended Armature

- **Construction:** The core is connected to a **spring-loaded plunger** or a **Bourdon tube** (a curved metal tube that straightens under pressure). The measured force/pressure moves the core through a mechanical linkage.
- **Working:** The pressure to be measured deflects the Bourdon tube, which pulls the core into the LVDT bore. The displacement is proportional to pressure, and the LVDT output is calibrated in pressure units.
- **Advantages:**
  - Provides direct pressure readout in a single instrument.
  - Compact integration: Bourdon + LVDT = electronic pressure transmitter.
  - Wide pressure range (vacuum to 700 bar typical).
- **Disadvantages:**
  - Hysteresis of the spring/Bourdon tube limits accuracy.
  - Temperature drift requires compensation.
- **Applications:** Electronic pressure gauges, force balances, load cells, weighing systems, differential-pressure transmitters.

## 4.6 Comparative Analytical Matrix — Armature Selection Guide

| Property | Captive Armature | Unguided Armature | Force-Extended Armature |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Core attachment** | Rigid rod to object | Free in bore | Spring / Bourdon link |
| **Typical range** | ±0.1 to ±500 mm | ±0.05 to ±5 mm | ±0.1 to ±10 mm |
| **Linearity** | ±0.1 % to ±0.5 % FS | ±1 % to ±2 % FS | ±0.25 % to ±1 % FS |
| **Best for** | Direct displacement | Acceleration | Pressure / force |
| **Friction** | Low (bearing seal) | Zero | Medium (spring) |
| **Dynamic response** | Medium | **High** | Low |
| **Cost** | Medium | High | High |
| **Typical use** | Position feedback | Seismic sensors | Pressure transmitters |

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 — `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Define an LVDT. List any four advantages of LVDT over a potentiometric displacement sensor. (3 marks — Bloom Level: Remember/Understand)**

**Model Answer:**

> **Definition:** A Linear Variable Differential Transformer (LVDT) is a passive inductive transducer that converts linear displacement of a movable soft-iron core into a differential AC voltage, using one primary and two secondary windings connected in series opposition.
>
> **Advantages over potentiometer:**
> 1. **Infinite resolution** (potentiometer resolution is limited by wire-wound granularity).
> 2. **Frictionless operation** — no wiper wear, infinite mechanical life.
> 3. **High accuracy and linearity** (±0.1 % to ±0.5 % of full scale).
> 4. **Bidirectional sensing** — phase reversal gives direction of displacement.
> 5. **Operates in harsh environments** — high temperature, vibration, radiation tolerant.

---

### Question 2 — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Differentiate between a captive armature and an unguided armature in an LVDT. (3 marks — Bloom Level: Understand)**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Captive Armature | Unguided Armature |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Mechanical link** | Core attached to measured object via rod | Core is free, not mechanically linked |
| **Motion** | Purely axial (guided by bearing) | Free in bore; can move radially |
| **Friction** | Low (bearing seal friction) | Negligible (no contact) |
| **Best application** | Linear displacement of macroscopic objects | Small displacements, accelerometers |
| **Accuracy** | Higher (±0.1 % FS) | Moderate (±1 % FS) |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, With Internal Choice)

### Question A — `[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper]`
**(a) Explain the construction and working principle of an LVDT with a neat sketch. Show its output characteristic curve. (7 marks — Bloom Level: Understand/Apply)**

**(b) Derive the expression for the output voltage of an LVDT in terms of core displacement. (7 marks — Bloom Level: Apply/Analyse)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Construction (3 marks):**

The LVDT consists of:
- A **cylindrical hollow former** (non-magnetic, e.g., ceramic or phenolic).
- **One primary winding (P)** wound at the centre, energized by an AC source of 1–24 V RMS at 50 Hz–20 kHz.
- **Two identical secondary windings (S₁ and S₂)** wound on either side of the primary, with equal number of turns $N_s$.
- A **movable soft-iron core (armature)** that slides axially inside the former.
- A **shielded housing** (mu-metal) for EMI rejection.

**Working (2 marks):**

When the core is at the **null position** (geometric centre), the flux linkage with $S_1$ equals that with $S_2$. The induced EMFs are equal and opposite, so the differential output is **zero**.

When the core **displaces towards $S_1$**, the flux coupling with $S_1$ increases → $e_{s1}$ increases, while $e_{s2}$ decreases. The net output $e_{out} = e_{s1} - e_{s2}$ is **in phase** with the primary.

When the core **displaces towards $S_2$**, the reverse occurs, and $e_{out}$ is **180° out of phase** with the primary.

**Output Characteristic Curve (2 marks):**

The plot of $E_{out,rms}$ versus core displacement $x$ is an **S-shaped curve**, symmetric about the origin. Within the **linear range** (typically ±5 mm for a ±25 mm LVDT), the relationship is linear:

$$E_{out,rms} = K_s \cdot x$$

The curve has three zones: linear (positive), null (zero), and linear (negative) with a 180° phase shift.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

The full step-by-step derivation is given in **Section 3.1 (Derivation I)** of these notes. For the board-exam answer script, present the derivation in **8 compact steps**, as follows:

**Step 1:** Let primary excitation be $e_p = E_p \sin(\omega t)$ and primary current be $i_p = I_p \sin(\omega t - \phi)$.

**Step 2:** The EMFs induced in the secondaries by mutual induction are:

$$e_{s1} = -M_1 \frac{di_p}{dt} \quad \text{and} \quad e_{s2} = -M_2 \frac{di_p}{dt}$$

**Step 3:** Differentiating $i_p$:

$$\frac{di_p}{dt} = \omega I_p \cos(\omega t - \phi)$$

**Step 4:** Substituting back:

$$e_{s1} = -\omega M_1 I_p \cos(\omega t - \phi)$$

$$e_{s2} = -\omega M_2 I_p \cos(\omega t - \phi)$$

**Step 5:** Applying the series-opposition rule, the differential output is:

$$e_{out} = e_{s1} - e_{s2} = -\omega I_p (M_1 - M_2) \cos(\omega t - \phi)$$

**Step 6:** For a small displacement $x$ from the null position, the difference in mutual inductance is linear in $x$:

$$M_1 - M_2 = K_m \cdot x$$

**Step 7:** Substituting:

$$e_{out} = -\omega I_p K_m \cdot x \cdot \cos(\omega t - \phi)$$

**Step 8:** The RMS value of the output is:

$$\boxed{E_{out,rms} = K_s \cdot x}$$

where $K_s = \dfrac{\omega I_p K_m}{\sqrt{2}}$ is the overall sensitivity of the LVDT.

---

### Question B — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Paper]` **(INTERNAL CHOICE)**
**(a) With neat diagrams, explain the different types of LVDT armatures. Compare their features. (7 marks — Bloom Level: Understand/Analyse)**

**(b) An LVDT has a sensitivity of 0.5 V/mm. The excitation is 6 V at 400 Hz. The primary current is 5 mA. If the core is displaced by 4 mm, calculate (i) the output voltage (RMS), (ii) the peak output voltage, and (iii) the change in mutual inductance. (7 marks — Bloom Level: Apply)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Three Types of LVDT Armatures (5 marks for diagrams + descriptions):**

1. **Captive Armature (Guided):** Core attached to the measured object via a non-magnetic rod passing through a sealed bearing. Provides pure axial motion, low friction, high accuracy. **Best for linear displacement measurement** of macroscopic objects.

2. **Unguided Armature (Free-core):** Core is free-floating inside the bore, not mechanically linked to the measured object. Driven indirectly (e.g., by a seismic mass). Provides low mass, low inertia, and excellent dynamic response. **Best for accelerometers and small-displacement vibration sensors**.

3. **Force-Extended Armature:** Core is moved by the action of a force/pressure through a spring or Bourdon tube. The displacement of the core is proportional to the applied force. **Best for electronic pressure transmitters, load cells, and force balances**.

**Comparison (2 marks):**

| Parameter | Captive | Unguided | Force-Extended |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Range | Wide | Narrow | Medium |
| Linearity | Best | Moderate | Good |
| Best use | Displacement | Acceleration | Pressure/Force |
| Dynamic response | Medium | Best | Lowest |

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Given:**
- Sensitivity $K_s = 0.5$ V/mm
- Excitation $E_p = 6$ V at $f = 400$ Hz → $\omega = 2\pi f = 800\pi$ rad/s
- Primary current $I_p = 5$ mA $= 5 \times 10^{-3}$ A
- Displacement $x = 4$ mm

**Part (i) — RMS Output Voltage:**

$$E_{out,rms} = K_s \cdot x = 0.5 \, \frac{\text{V}}{\text{mm}} \times 4 \, \text{mm}$$

$$\boxed{E_{out,rms} = 2.0 \, \text{V (RMS)}}$$

**Part (ii) — Peak Output Voltage:**

$$E_{out,peak} = \sqrt{2} \cdot E_{out,rms} = \sqrt{2} \times 2.0$$

$$\boxed{E_{out,peak} = 2.828 \, \text{V (peak)}}$$

**Part (iii) — Change in Mutual Inductance:**

From the general output expression:

$$E_{out,peak} = \omega I_p K_m x$$

Rearranging for $K_m$:

$$K_m = \frac{E_{out,peak}}{\omega I_p x}$$

Substituting values:

$$K_m = \frac{2.828}{(800\pi) \times (5 \times 10^{-3}) \times 4}$$

$$K_m = \frac{2.828}{800 \times 3.1416 \times 0.02}$$

$$K_m = \frac{2.828}{50.265}$$

$$\boxed{K_m \approx 0.0563 \, \text{H/mm} = 56.3 \, \mu\text{H/mm}}$$

The total change in mutual inductance for a 4 mm displacement is:

$$\Delta M = K_m \times x = 0.0563 \times 4 = 0.2252 \, \text{H}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the series-opposition sign** in the derivation: many students write $e_{out} = e_{s1} + e_{s2}$, which is **wrong** — examiners deduct 2 marks for this.
> 2. **Confusing RMS and peak values:** use $\sqrt{2}$ correctly when converting. KTU paper may give either form; read carefully.
> 3. **Not stating the null condition explicitly:** Examiners look for the line *"At null position, $M_1 = M_2$ and hence $e_{out} = 0$."* — a full 1-mark reward is given for this.
> 4. **Skipping the phase-sensitive detector mention:** In Part (a) working, you **must** state that a PSD is required to convert the AC output to a usable DC signal. **1 mark is reserved** for this.
> 5. **Confusing "captive" with "captured" armature:** The correct term is **Captive Armature** (sometimes called *guided* armature). Examiners mark spelling strictly.

---

## 5.3 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER (RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST)

> [!NOTE]
> Use this section as a **30-second revision flash-card** before entering the exam hall.

### A. LVDT — Core Facts

- [ ] LVDT stands for **Linear Variable Differential Transformer**.
- [ ] It is a **passive inductive transducer** (requires external AC excitation).
- [ ] Has **one primary** and **two secondaries** in series opposition.
- [ ] Output is **AC**, requires **phase-sensitive detector (PSD)** for DC output.
- [ ] At **null position**, output is **zero** (theoretically).
- [ ] **Direction of displacement** is given by **phase** of output (in-phase vs 180° out-of-phase).
- [ ] Output equation (RMS): $E_{out,rms} = K_s \cdot x$.
- [ ] **Sensitivity** $K_s$ has units of **V/mm**.
- [ ] **Linearity** is expressed as **±% of Full Scale (FS)**.
- [ ] **Resolution** is **theoretically infinite**.
- [ ] Operates in harsh environments: high temperature, vibration, radiation.
- [ ] Disadvantages: requires AC excitation, sensitive to stray magnetic fields, requires signal conditioning.

### B. Armature Types — Quick Differentiator

- [ ] **Captive (Guided) Armature** — for **linear displacement** of large objects; rod-coupled; high accuracy.
- [ ] **Unguided (Free-Core) Armature** — for **acceleration / small displacement**; low mass; high dynamic response.
- [ ] **Force-Extended Armature** — for **pressure / force** measurement; coupled to Bourdon tube or spring.

### C. KTU High-Yield Numerical Formulae

- [ ] $E_{out,rms} = K_s \cdot x$
- [ ] $E_{out,peak} = \sqrt{2} \cdot E_{out,rms}$
- [ ] $K_s = \omega I_p K_m / \sqrt{2}$
- [ ] Linearity $\% = (\text{Deviation} / \text{FS}) \times 100$
- [ ] Null condition: $M_1 = M_2 \Rightarrow e_{out} = 0$

### D. Examiner Favourite Sub-Topics (Appear Repeatedly)

- [ ] LVDT construction with **neat diagram** (2 marks reserved for diagram).
- [ ] **Derivation of output voltage** (full 7 marks in Part B).
- [ ] **Output characteristic curve** (S-shaped, symmetric, with linear range).
- [ ] **Advantages and disadvantages** of LVDT (compare with potentiometer).
- [ ] **Phase-sensitive detection** concept (1 mark).
- [ ] **Differentiate armature types** (table format preferred).
- [ ] Numerical problems on **sensitivity, displacement, and output voltage**.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Velocity Transducers - LVT
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBRT303 - SENSORS AND ACTUATORS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Velocity Transducers - LVT

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview
