### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Linear Variable Transformer (LVT)** is a contactless, electromechanical velocity transducer that converts **linear velocity** of a target/moving object into a proportional alternating voltage signal. It operates on the principle of **Faraday's Law of Electromagnetic Induction**, where the relative motion between a magnetic field and a coil induces an electromotive force (EMF) directly proportional to the rate of change of magnetic flux linkage.

In KTU 2024 syllabus terminology, an LVT is broadly classified into two implementation categories:

1. **Electrodynamic (Moving-Coil) LVT** — A permanent magnet is attached to the moving mass, and it moves linearly through a stationary coil (or vice-versa).
2. **LVDT-based LVT** — A standard Linear Variable Differential Transformer is cascaded with a **passive/active differentiator (Op-Amp)** circuit. Since an LVDT gives an output proportional to *displacement (x)*, the differentiator generates an output proportional to *dx/dt*, i.e., **velocity (v)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Distinction for KTU Board Exams:** Do not confuse **LVT** with **LVDT**. LVDT measures *displacement*, whereas LVT measures *velocity*. A common variant of LVT measures velocity directly by electromagnetic induction (no differentiator needed), which makes it inherently robust against integration drift.

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine holding a **bar magnet** and rapidly pushing it into a hollow cardboard tube around which copper wire is tightly wound. The faster you push, the larger the voltage you can measure across the wire ends using a multimeter. This is essentially how an LVT works.

- **The magnet** → represents the *moving core/mass*
- **The copper coil** → represents the *stationary sensing coil*
- **Your hand's speed** → represents the *velocity being measured*
- **The multimeter reading** → represents the *output voltage*

If you push slowly, voltage is small. If you push fast, voltage spikes. This voltage is your velocity signal.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard SI Unit Note:** The SI unit of velocity is **m/s** (metres per second). The output of an LVT is typically an **AC voltage in Volts (V)**. The proportionality constant is called the **Velocity Sensitivity** and is expressed in **V/(m/s)** or equivalently **V·s/m**.

---

### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Symbol | Standard Value / Unit |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Permeability of free space | $\mu_0$ | **$4\pi \times 10^{-7} \text{ H/m}$** |
| Permeability of core | $\mu_r$ | Dimensionless (typically **5000–10000** for soft iron) |
| Induced EMF | $e$ | Volts (V) |
| Magnetic flux | $\Phi$ | Webers (Wb) |
| Velocity sensitivity constant | $K_v$ | **V·s/m** |
| Output frequency response | $f$ | **Hz** (typically 50 Hz to 20 kHz) |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Architecture of LVT

The Linear Variable Transformer can be implemented in **two primary topologies**. The KTU 2024 syllabus emphasizes the **electrodynamic type** as the most direct velocity transducer.

#### 2.1.1 Electrodynamic (Moving-Magnet) LVT — Working Principle

The construction consists of:
- A **cylindrical permanent magnet** (moving part) attached to the object whose velocity is to be measured.
- A **stationary solenoid/coil** wound around a non-magnetic former.
- A **non-magnetic shaft** guiding the magnet's motion.
- A **pickup mechanism** (shielded cable) carrying the induced signal to instrumentation.

**Step-by-Step Logic Flow:**
1. The permanent magnet creates a strong, static **magnetic field** around itself.
2. When the magnet moves with velocity $v$ relative to the stationary coil, the **magnetic flux $\Phi$ linking the coil changes** with time.
3. By **Faraday's Law**, a time-varying flux induces an EMF:
   $e = -N \frac{d\Phi}{dt}$
4. Since the flux linkage is a function of the magnet's position $x(t)$, and $v = \frac{dx}{dt}$, the induced EMF is directly proportional to the **velocity** of the magnet.
5. The output voltage is an **AC signal** because motion in one direction produces a positive voltage peak, and reverse motion produces a negative voltage peak (a sinusoid for sinusoidal motion).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why LVT is superior to LVDT+Differentiator for velocity measurement:** A pure differentiator circuit amplifies high-frequency noise. The direct electromagnetic LVT is **inherently a velocity sensor**, providing an excellent signal-to-noise ratio (SNR) for dynamic vibration and seismic measurements.

#### 2.1.2 LVDT-Cascaded LVT (Indirect Method)

If only an LVDT is available, the LVDT output ($V_{out} \propto x$) is passed through an **active differentiator** (using an Op-Amp with capacitor in input branch).

- LVDT output: $V_{LVDT} = K_s \cdot x(t)$
- Differentiator output: $V_{LVT} = -RC \frac{dV_{LVDT}}{dt} = -RC \cdot K_s \cdot \frac{dx}{dt} = -RC \cdot K_s \cdot v(t)$

The output is a voltage proportional to velocity.

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!NOTE]
> The table below contains **all standard equations** required to solve velocity transducer problems in the KTU 2024 ESE (End Semester Examination). The vertical bar symbol `\vert` is used for absolute value to preserve markdown table integrity.

| # | Formula Name | Equation | Variables & Units |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Faraday's Law (LVT Core) | $e = -N \frac{d\Phi}{dt}$ | $N$ = turns, $\Phi$ = Wb, $t$ = s, $e$ = V |
| 2 | Flux–Position Relationship | $\Phi = f(x)$ | $x$ = displacement (m) |
| 3 | Velocity from Displacement | $v = \frac{dx}{dt}$ | $v$ = velocity (m/s) |
| 4 | LVT Output (Direct EM Type) | $V_{out} = K_v \cdot v(t)$ | $K_v$ = velocity sensitivity (V·s/m) |
| 5 | LVDT-to-LVT Differentiator | $V_{out} = -RC \cdot K_s \cdot v(t)$ | $R$ in $\Omega$, $C$ in F, $K_s$ in V/m |
| 6 | Velocity from LVDT | $v = \frac{1}{K_s \cdot RC} V_{out}$ | Derived form for numericals |
| 7 | Sensitivity Definition | $K_v = \frac{V_{out}}{v}$ | V·s/m |
| 8 | Frequency Response (1st Order) | $f_c = \frac{1}{2\pi RC}$ | Differentiator cut-off (Hz) |
| 9 | Peak EMF (Sinusoidal Motion) | $E_{peak} = N \cdot B \cdot l \cdot \omega \cdot x_{peak}$ | $\omega$ = rad/s, $B$ = T, $l$ = m |
| 10 | RMS EMF | $E_{rms} = \frac{E_{peak}}{\sqrt{2}}$ | For sinusoidal input |

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Seismology and Geophysics:** LVTs are deployed as geophones/velocity pickups to record ground vibrations during earthquakes and oil exploration surveys.
- **Aerospace Vibration Monitoring:** Mounted on turbine blades and engine housings to measure high-frequency mechanical vibration amplitudes.
- **Industrial Machine Health Monitoring:** Used in bearing-fault detection systems where velocity RMS values indicate vibration severity per ISO 10816 standards.
- **Automotive Suspension Testing:** Direct measurement of suspension travel velocity during ride-comfort evaluations.
- **Hydrodynamic Flow:** Marine and submarine velocimeters for measuring relative water velocity.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Tip:** In numerical problems, whenever the phrase *"LVT"* is used without qualification, KTU examiners default to the **electrodynamic (moving-magnet) moving-coil type**. Always state the assumed topology in your answer.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of LVT Output Voltage (Electrodynamic Type)

**Given:** A permanent magnet of magnetic field strength $B$ moves with linear velocity $v$ through a stationary coil of $N$ turns and effective length $l$ (axial length of coil).

**Step 1: Establish the flux linkage at any instant $t$.**
The flux linking a single turn of the coil depends on the position of the magnet. For a magnet at position $x(t)$, the flux per turn is:
$\Phi(t) = B \cdot A \cdot g(x)$
where $A$ is the effective cross-sectional area and $g(x)$ is a position-dependent geometric coupling function (varies between 0 and 1).

**Step 2: Apply Faraday's Law of Induction.**
The total induced EMF across the $N$-turn coil is:

$$
e(t) = -N \frac{d\Phi(t)}{dt}
$$

**Step 3: Apply the chain rule of differentiation.**
Since $\Phi$ is a function of $x$, and $x$ is a function of $t$:

$$
\frac{d\Phi}{dt} = \frac{d\Phi}{dx} \cdot \frac{dx}{dt}
$$

**Step 4: Substitute the velocity relationship.**
By definition of linear velocity, $\frac{dx}{dt} = v(t)$. Therefore:

$$
e(t) = -N \cdot \frac{d\Phi}{dx} \cdot v(t)
$$

**Step 5: Define the velocity sensitivity constant.**
The term $\left( -N \cdot \frac{d\Phi}{dx} \right)$ is a constant for a given transducer geometry. It is called the **velocity sensitivity $K_v$**:

$$
K_v = -N \frac{d\Phi}{dx}
$$

**Step 6: Write the final LVT output equation.**

$$
\boxed{e(t) = K_v \cdot v(t)}
$$

**Interpretation:** The output voltage is a linear function of velocity. The negative sign indicates the polarity convention dictated by Lenz's Law. The magnitude $\vert e(t) \vert = \vert K_v \vert \cdot \vert v(t) \vert$ is what is measured by the data acquisition system.

---

### 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation for Sinusoidal Motion

**Given:** The magnet undergoes simple harmonic motion, $x(t) = x_{peak} \sin(\omega t)$.

**Step 1: Differentiate to obtain velocity.**
Using standard calculus:

$$
v(t) = \frac{dx}{dt} = x_{peak} \cdot \omega \cdot \cos(\omega t)
$$

**Step 2: Identify the peak velocity.**
The peak (maximum) velocity is:

$$
v_{peak} = x_{peak} \cdot \omega
$$

**Step 3: Substitute into the LVT output equation.**

$$
e(t) = K_v \cdot x_{peak} \cdot \omega \cdot \cos(\omega t)
$$

**Step 4: Identify the peak EMF.**

$$
\boxed{E_{peak} = K_v \cdot \omega \cdot x_{peak}}
$$

**Step 5: Compute the RMS value (for power/energy calculations).**

$$
E_{rms} = \frac{E_{peak}}{\sqrt{2}} = \frac{K_v \cdot \omega \cdot x_{peak}}{\sqrt{2}}
$$

**Step 6: Compute the frequency in Hz.**
The relationship between angular frequency $\omega$ and ordinary frequency $f$ is:

$$
\omega = 2\pi f \quad \Rightarrow \quad f = \frac{\omega}{2\pi}
$$

**Key Physical Insight:** Note that the peak EMF is **directly proportional to frequency $\omega$** for a fixed displacement amplitude. This means at very low frequencies, the LVT output becomes vanishingly small — a known limitation called the **low-frequency roll-off**. Below a critical frequency, LVTs become impractical, and **seismic accelerometers** are preferred.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — LVDT-to-LVT Differentiator Numerical Framework

**Problem Type Encountered in KTU Exams:** *"An LVDT has a sensitivity of $K_s = 0.5$ V/mm. Its output is connected to an ideal differentiator circuit with $R = 10 \text{ k}\Omega$ and $C = 1 \text{ \mu F}$. If the core velocity is constant at 25 mm/s, find the LVT (differentiator) output voltage."*

**Step-by-Step Solution Skeleton:**

**Step 1: Write the LVDT output equation.**
LVDT output is proportional to displacement:
$V_{LVDT} = K_s \cdot x(t)$

**Step 2: Apply the active differentiator transfer function.**
The output of an ideal op-amp differentiator is:
$V_{out} = -RC \frac{dV_{in}}{dt}$

**Step 3: Differentiate the LVDT output.**
$\frac{dV_{LVDT}}{dt} = K_s \cdot \frac{dx}{dt} = K_s \cdot v(t)$

**Step 4: Combine the equations.**

$$
V_{out} = -RC \cdot K_s \cdot v(t)
$$

**Step 5: Substitute the numerical values.**
- $R = 10 \times 10^3 \text{ \Omega}$
- $C = 1 \times 10^{-6} \text{ F}$
- $RC = 10^{-2} \text{ s} = 10 \text{ ms}$
- $K_s = 0.5 \text{ V/mm} = 500 \text{ V/m}$
- $v = 25 \text{ mm/s} = 0.025 \text{ m/s}$

**Step 6: Compute the magnitude.**

$$
\vert V_{out} \vert = 10^{-2} \times 500 \times 0.025 = 0.125 \text{ V} = 125 \text{ mV}
$$

**Final Answer:** $\vert V_{out} \vert = 125 \text{ mV}$ (with polarity inverted relative to the LVDT output).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Unit Consistency Rule for KTU Numericals:** Always convert mm to m and µs to s **before** multiplying. Mixing units is the **#1 cause of numerical errors** in KTU valuation scripts.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: LVT Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[MOVING TARGET] --> B[PERMANENT MAGNET ASSEMBLY]
    B --> C[MAGNETIC FLUX LINKS STATIONARY COIL]
    C --> D[STATIONARY SOLENOID COIL N TURNS]
    D --> E[FARADAYS INDUCTION: e EQUALS MINUS N dPhi dt]
    E --> F[AC VOLTAGE SIGNAL]
    F --> G[INSTRUMENTATION AMPLIFIER]
    G --> H[SIGNAL CONDITIONER]
    H --> I[ADC AND DATA LOGGER]
    I --> J[VELOCITY READOUT IN m per s]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram: LVDT-Cascaded LVT (Indirect Topology)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[TARGET DISPLACEMENT x of t] --> B[LVDT TRANSDUCER]
    B --> C[V_LVDT EQUALS K_s TIMES x]
    C --> D[OP AMP DIFFERENTIATOR]
    D --> E[V_LVT EQUALS MINUS RC TIMES dV LVDT dt]
    E --> F[VELOCITY OUTPUT V_LVT EQUALS MINUS RC K_s v]
    F --> G[DISPLAY UNIT m per s]
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram: Comparison Between LVDT and LVT (Decision Tree)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[MEASUREMENT REQUIREMENT] --> B{WHICH QUANTITY}
    B --> C[DISPLACEMENT x]
    B --> D[VELOCITY v]
    C --> E[USE LVDT DIRECTLY]
    D --> F{WHICH METHOD}
    F --> G[DIRECT ELECTROMAGNETIC LVT]
    F --> H[LVDT CASCADE WITH DIFFERENTIATOR]
    G --> I[NO DRIFT NO NOISE AMPLIFICATION]
    H --> J[HIGHER SNR AT HIGH FREQ ONLY]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation:** Each node in the Mermaid diagrams uses **pure alphanumeric identifiers** (e.g., `MOVING`, `PERMANENT`, `FARADAYS`, `LVDTNODE`) without reserved keywords like `end` or `subgraph`. Labels use clean uppercase text to comply with Mermaid compilation safeguards and ensure KTU study notes render correctly in PDF/HTML exports.

### 4.4 Functional Component Table

| Block | Physical Component | Function |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| A | Moving target / shaker head | Source of mechanical motion |
| B | Permanent magnet (NdFeB or AlNiCo) | Provides static magnetic field |
| C–D | Solenoid coil on non-magnetic former | Senses change in flux linkage |
| E | Coil terminals | Output AC voltage |
| F–G | Instrumentation amplifier (e.g., AD620) | Boosts low-level LVT output |
| H | Band-pass filter | Removes 1/f noise and high-freq EMI |
| I | 16-bit ADC (e.g., ADS1115) | Digitizes signal for processing |
| J | Microcontroller / Display | Computes and displays velocity |

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 (Model)]**
*Define a Linear Variable Transformer (LVT). How does it fundamentally differ from an LVDT in terms of the measurand?*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A Linear Variable Transformer (LVT) is an electromechanical transducer that converts the **linear velocity** of a moving object into a proportional **AC output voltage** using the principle of electromagnetic induction. **(1 Mark)**

The fundamental difference is the measurand:
- An **LVDT** measures **displacement** ($x$); its output is proportional to $V_{out} = K_s \cdot x$. **(1 Mark)**
- An **LVT** measures **velocity** ($v = dx/dt$); its output is proportional to $V_{out} = K_v \cdot v$. **(1 Mark)**

Internally, an LVT can be implemented as a moving-magnet electromagnetic transducer, or as an LVDT cascaded with a differentiator circuit.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 (Model)]**
*State Faraday's Law of Electromagnetic Induction as applicable to a Linear Variable Transformer. Write the final governing equation relating output voltage to velocity.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Faraday's Law:** The EMF induced in a coil is equal to the negative of the number of turns $N$ times the rate of change of magnetic flux $\Phi$ linking the coil. **(2 Marks)**

**Equation:**

$$
e(t) = -N \frac{d\Phi}{dt} = -N \frac{d\Phi}{dx} \cdot \frac{dx}{dt} = K_v \cdot v(t)
$$

**(1 Mark)** where $K_v$ is the velocity sensitivity constant (V·s/m).

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

**Q3. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 (Model)] — Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the construction and working of an electrodynamic Linear Variable Transformer. State its advantages over an LVDT-with-differentiator arrangement. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A moving-magnet LVT has a velocity sensitivity of $K_v = 50 \text{ mV per (m/s)}$. The magnet undergoes harmonic motion $x(t) = 2 \sin(100\pi t)$ mm. Calculate:
- (i) The peak velocity of the magnet. **(2 Marks)**
- (ii) The peak induced EMF in the coil. **(2 Marks)**
- (iii) The RMS value of the induced EMF. **(2 Marks)**
- (iv) The frequency of the output voltage in Hz. **(1 Mark)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Construction and Working (7 Marks):**

**Construction (3 Marks):**
- A **cylindrical permanent magnet** is mounted on a non-magnetic shaft connected to the moving target.
- A **stationary solenoid coil** of $N$ turns is wound on a non-magnetic former surrounding the shaft.
- The assembly is enclosed in a **magnetic shield** to minimize external EMI.
- Output is taken via **shielded twisted-pair cables** to the instrumentation amplifier.

**Working (2 Marks):**
1. The permanent magnet produces a static radial magnetic field.
2. As the magnet moves with velocity $v(t)$, the flux linking the stationary coil changes with time.
3. By Faraday's Law, an EMF $e(t) = -N \frac{d\Phi}{dt}$ is induced.
4. Since flux is a function of position, $e(t) = K_v \cdot v(t)$, giving a voltage directly proportional to velocity.

**Advantages over LVDT+Differentiator (2 Marks):**
- No differentiator circuit is required → **no high-frequency noise amplification**. **(1 Mark)**
- Wider usable **frequency bandwidth**, especially in the low-to-mid range. **(1 Mark)**

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 Marks):**

**Given:** $K_v = 50 \text{ mV/(m/s)} = 0.05 \text{ V/(m/s)}$, $x(t) = 2 \sin(100\pi t) \text{ mm}$.

**Step 1: Identify angular frequency and peak displacement.**
$\omega = 100\pi \text{ rad/s}$, $x_{peak} = 2 \text{ mm} = 2 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}$.

**Step 2 (i): Compute peak velocity.**

$$
v_{peak} = \omega \cdot x_{peak} = 100\pi \times 2 \times 10^{-3} = 0.2\pi \text{ m/s} \approx 0.6283 \text{ m/s}
$$

**(2 Marks)**

**Step 3 (ii): Compute peak EMF.**

$$
E_{peak} = K_v \cdot v_{peak} = 0.05 \times 0.2\pi = 0.01\pi \text{ V} \approx 0.0314 \text{ V} = 31.42 \text{ mV}
$$

**(2 Marks)**

**Step 4 (iii): Compute RMS EMF.**

$$
E_{rms} = \frac{E_{peak}}{\sqrt{2}} = \frac{0.0314}{\sqrt{2}} \approx 0.0222 \text{ V} = 22.21 \text{ mV}
$$

**(2 Marks)**

**Step 5 (iv): Compute frequency in Hz.**

$$
f = \frac{\omega}{2\pi} = \frac{100\pi}{2\pi} = 50 \text{ Hz}
$$

**(1 Mark)**

---

**Q4. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 (Model)] — Question B (14 Marks — Internal Choice Alternative)**

**(a)** Explain the indirect method of obtaining a velocity signal from an LVDT. Draw the circuit diagram of the active differentiator used and derive the relationship between input displacement, output voltage, and velocity. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** An LVDT has a sensitivity of $K_s = 0.4 \text{ V/mm}$. Its output is connected to an active differentiator with $R = 20 \text{ k}\Omega$ and $C = 0.5 \text{ \mu F}$.
- (i) Find the time constant of the differentiator. **(2 Marks)**
- (ii) If the LVDT core moves with a constant velocity of $30 \text{ mm/s}$, what is the steady-state output voltage of the LVT? **(3 Marks)**
- (iii) What is the maximum frequency up to which this differentiator can operate reliably, assuming the op-amp gain-bandwidth product is 1 MHz? **(2 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Indirect LVDT-to-LVT Method (7 Marks):**

**Concept (2 Marks):** Since LVDT output is proportional to *displacement*, we pass it through an **active differentiator circuit** (Op-Amp with capacitor at input and resistor in feedback). The differentiator performs a time-derivative operation, converting $x(t)$ into $v(t) = dx/dt$.

**Circuit Description (2 Marks):**
- Non-inverting terminal grounded.
- Input signal $V_{in}$ (LVDT output) applied through capacitor $C$ to the inverting terminal.
- Feedback resistor $R_f$ between output and inverting terminal.

**Derivation (3 Marks):**
KCL at the inverting node (virtual ground):

$$
i_C = i_R \quad \Rightarrow \quad C \frac{dV_{in}}{dt} = -\frac{V_{out}}{R}
$$

Solving for $V_{out}$:

$$
V_{out} = -RC \frac{dV_{in}}{dt}
$$

Substituting $V_{in} = K_s \cdot x(t)$:

$$
\boxed{V_{out} = -RC \cdot K_s \cdot v(t)}
$$

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 Marks):**

**Given:** $K_s = 0.4 \text{ V/mm} = 400 \text{ V/m}$, $R = 20 \text{ k}\Omega = 2 \times 10^4 \text{ }\Omega$, $C = 0.5 \text{ \mu F} = 5 \times 10^{-7} \text{ F}$, $v = 30 \text{ mm/s} = 0.03 \text{ m/s}$.

**Step 1 (i): Time constant.**

$$
\tau = RC = 2 \times 10^4 \times 5 \times 10^{-7} = 0.01 \text{ s} = 10 \text{ ms}
$$

**(2 Marks)**

**Step 2 (ii): Output voltage at constant velocity.**

$$
\vert V_{out} \vert = RC \cdot K_s \cdot v = 0.01 \times 400 \times 0.03 = 0.12 \text{ V} = 120 \text{ mV}
$$

**(3 Marks)**

**Step 3 (iii): Maximum reliable operating frequency.**

For an ideal differentiator, the gain magnitude is $\vert G(f) \vert = 2\pi f \cdot RC$. To avoid exceeding the op-amp's gain-bandwidth product (GBW = 1 MHz), the frequency must satisfy:

$$
f_{max} \approx \frac{GBW}{2\pi \cdot RC \cdot \text{safety factor}} \approx \frac{10^6}{2\pi \times 0.01} \approx 15.9 \text{ kHz}
$$

**(2 Marks)**

A practical safe operating limit is approximately **15 kHz**.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls Where KTU Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Unit Conversion Errors (–2 marks):** Failing to convert mm to m or kHz to Hz. Always write units in every line of your solution.
> 2. **Confusing LVDT with LVT (–1 to –2 marks):** If the question asks for **velocity**, the final answer **must contain m/s units**, not mm. Stating the wrong measurand shows a fundamental conceptual gap.
> 3. **Missing the Polarity / Sign Convention (–1 mark):** Inductor voltages obey Lenz's law. The negative sign in $e = -N \frac{d\Phi}{dt}$ must be explained in derivations, not omitted.
> 4. **Forgetting the RMS Conversion (–1 mark):** When asked for AC voltage, KTU examiners expect either peak or RMS **explicitly stated**. Defaulting to peak without clarifying is a common mistake.
> 5. **Omitting the Block Diagram in 7-Mark Questions (–2 marks):** A "neat diagram" is **mandatory** per KTU 2024 scheme valuation rubrics. Always include a labeled block diagram or circuit schematic.
> 6. **Treating $\omega = 2\pi f$ as $\omega = f$ (–1 mark):** This is a high-frequency error. Angular frequency is in rad/s; ordinary frequency is in Hz.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **LVT vs LVDT:** LVDT measures **displacement (x)**; LVT measures **velocity (v = dx/dt)**. **(Vital)**
- **Core Equation:** $V_{out} = K_v \cdot v(t)$, where $K_v$ = velocity sensitivity in V·s/m. **(Vital)**
- **Two Implementations:**
  1. **Direct electromagnetic (moving-magnet)** — preferred for low noise.
  2. **LVDT + Op-Amp Differentiator** — yields $V_{out} = -RC \cdot K_s \cdot v(t)$.
- **Sinusoidal Motion Output:** $E_{peak} = K_v \cdot \omega \cdot x_{peak}$; RMS is divided by $\sqrt{2}$.
- **Frequency Conversion:** Always use $\omega = 2\pi f$ when going between rad/s and Hz.
- **Low-Frequency Limitation:** LVT output drops linearly with frequency below the cutoff — use **seismometers** or **accelerometers** for very low frequencies.
- **Time Constant for Differentiator:** $\tau = RC$ — controls differentiator bandwidth.
- **Noise Behavior:** Pure differentiators amplify high-frequency noise; the direct electromagnetic LVT avoids this pitfall.
- **Permeability Constants:** $\mu_0 = 4\pi \times 10^{-7} \text{ H/m}$ is often required in derivation-based questions.
- **Real-World Domains:** Seismology, vibration monitoring (ISO 10816), aerospace, automotive suspension testing.
- **KTU 2024 Exam Weightage:** Typically appears as a **7-mark sub-question** in Module 4 ESE; expect either a derivation or a numerical involving sinusoidal motion.
- **Examiner's Pet Topics:** Faraday's Law derivation (7 marks), moving-magnet construction diagram (3 marks), sinusoidal motion numerical (4 marks).
- **One-Sentence Memory Hook:** *"LVT = Faraday's Law made to listen to motion — voltage speaks the speed of the magnet."*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Accelerometer - Using Potentiometer, Strain Gauge, Piezoelectric
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBRT303 - SENSORS AND ACTUATORS
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Accelerometer - Using Potentiometer, Strain Gauge, Piezoelectric

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is an Accelerometer?

An **accelerometer** is a transducer that converts mechanical acceleration experienced by a seismic (proof) mass into a measurable electrical signal (voltage, current, resistance change, or charge). It is fundamentally a **single-degree-of-freedom (SDOF) mass–spring–damper system** whose proof mass deflects relative to the housing under inertial loading.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition**
> An accelerometer is a device whose output is a function of the acceleration acting on a seismic mass suspended inside the instrument case by a spring–damper arrangement. The deflection (or force) of the mass is measured by a secondary transduction mechanism: a potentiometer, a strain gauge, or a piezoelectric crystal.

## 1.2 The Three Constructional Variants (KTU 2024 Scheme Focus)

| S.No | Type | Secondary Transduction | Output Quantity |
|------|------|------------------------|------------------|
| 1 | Potentiometric Accelerometer | Wiper slides over a resistive element | Resistance / Voltage |
| 2 | Strain-Gauge Accelerometer | Bonded or unbonded strain gauges deform with the mass | Resistance change $\Delta R$ |
| 3 | Piezoelectric Accelerometer | Piezo crystal generates charge under stress | Charge $Q$ / Voltage $V$ |

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — "The Floating Cork"

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Analogy**
> Imagine a cork floating inside a sealed glass jar filled with water. The jar is mounted on a car dashboard. When the car accelerates forward, the water (and hence the jar walls) move forward, but the **cork lags behind** because of its inertia. From inside the jar, it *appears* that the cork has moved backward. An accelerometer works on this exact principle: a **proof mass (cork)** suspended by a **spring (water resistance)** moves *relative* to the **housing (jar)** when the housing accelerates. We measure this relative motion (or the inertial force it implies) to deduce acceleration.
>
> - **Potentiometric** = you attach a **ruler and pencil** to the cork and read its position.
> - **Strain gauge** = you glue a **stretchy resistor strip** to the spring holding the cork; the stretch changes its resistance.
> - **Piezoelectric** = you replace the spring with a **special crystal** that *generates its own voltage* when squeezed by the inertial force of the cork.

## 1.4 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Reference Values Used Throughout KTU Problems**
> - Acceleration due to gravity: **$g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$**
> - Piezoelectric charge coefficient (typical PZT-5A): **$d_{33} \approx 374 \text{ pC/N}$**
> - Poisson's ratio of constantan (strain gauge alloy): **$\nu \approx 0.3$**
> - Gauge factor range (metallic gauges): **$2.0$ to $2.2$**; (semiconductor): **$100$ to $200$**
> - Standard seismic mass range: **$0.5 \text{ g}$ to $50 \text{ g}$**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Generalized Working Principle of an Accelerometer

All three accelerometer types share the same **first-stage mechanical model** — the SDOF mass-spring-damper — but differ in the **second-stage transduction**.

### 2.1.1 Mechanical First Stage (Common to All Three Types)

When the housing experiences an acceleration $a(t)$, the proof mass $m$ experiences an inertial force $F_{\text{inertia}} = m \cdot a(t)$ opposite in direction. This force is balanced by:
- The **spring restoring force**: $F_s = k \cdot x$ (where $k$ is the spring constant in N/m and $x$ is the relative displacement of the mass)
- The **damper reaction force**: $F_d = c \cdot \dfrac{dx}{dt}$ (where $c$ is the damping coefficient in N·s/m)

The governing differential equation (force balance on the mass):

$$m \cdot \frac{d^2x}{dt^2} + c \cdot \frac{dx}{dt} + k \cdot x = -m \cdot a(t)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why is there a minus sign?**
> The mass moves *opposite* to the housing's acceleration direction. The KTU board expects you to write the sign explicitly in derivations — examiners often deduct **half a mark** for missing this.

### 2.1.2 Steady-State Sensitivity (Low-Frequency Limit)

When the input acceleration varies slowly (quasi-static), inertial terms become negligible and we get the **static deflection** $x_0$:

$$x_0 = -\frac{m \cdot a}{k}$$

This means the displacement is **directly proportional to acceleration**, which is the fundamental operating principle.

## 2.2 Type 1: Potentiometric Accelerometer

### Working Logic
- The seismic mass is rigidly attached to a **wiper (slider)**.
- The wiper moves linearly over a **resistive element** (carbon track or wire-wound) fixed to the housing.
- An excitation voltage $V_{\text{ex}}$ is applied across the full track; output is taken between one end and the wiper.

### Output Relation
If $x$ is the wiper displacement and $L$ is the total track length, the output voltage is:

$$V_{\text{out}} = \left(\frac{x}{L}\right) \cdot V_{\text{ex}}$$

Combining with the static deflection:

$$V_{\text{out}} = -\frac{m \cdot a}{k \cdot L} \cdot V_{\text{ex}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility**
> Cheap, robust, give a **high-level DC output** (good for display-driven dashboards, automotive airbag testers). Drawback: friction, wear, limited bandwidth (<50 Hz), and limited resolution due to wire-wound granularity.

## 2.3 Type 2: Strain-Gauge Accelerometer

### Working Logic
- The seismic mass is mounted on a **flexible cantilever or post** to which strain gauges are bonded.
- Under acceleration, the cantilever bends, producing a strain $\varepsilon = \dfrac{\Delta L}{L}$ at the gauge location.
- The strain changes the gauge resistance, which is read by a **Wheatstone bridge**.

### Gauge Factor Equation
The fundamental relation governing resistive strain gauges:

$$\text{Gauge Factor (GF)} = \frac{\Delta R / R}{\Delta L / L} = \frac{\Delta R / R}{\varepsilon} = 1 + 2\nu + \frac{\Delta \rho / \rho}{\varepsilon}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Favourite Result**
> For metallic gauges (where $\Delta \rho \approx 0$), $GF \approx 1 + 2\nu \approx 1.6 \text{ to } 2.2$.
> For semiconductor gauges (piezoresistive), the $\Delta \rho / \rho$ term dominates, giving $GF \approx 100 \text{ to } 200$.

### Bridge Output
For a single active gauge of resistance $R$ in a quarter-bridge circuit with excitation $V_{\text{ex}}$:

$$V_{\text{out}} \approx \frac{V_{\text{ex}}}{4} \cdot \text{GF} \cdot \varepsilon$$

The strain is related to the bending of a cantilever of length $l_c$, thickness $t_c$ carrying seismic mass at its free end:

$$\varepsilon = \frac{6 \cdot m \cdot a \cdot l_c}{E \cdot b_c \cdot t_c^2}$$

where $E$ is Young's modulus and $b_c$ the beam width.

## 2.4 Type 3: Piezoelectric Accelerometer

### Working Logic
- The seismic mass sits on top of a **piezoelectric crystal** (usually PZT — Lead Zirconate Titanate) which is fixed to the housing base.
- When the housing accelerates upward, the inertial force of the mass **compresses** the crystal; when downward, it **tensions** it.
- The mechanical stress $T$ (N/m²) generates an electric charge $Q$ on the crystal's electrodes.

### Direct Piezoelectric Equation
$$Q = d \cdot F = d \cdot m \cdot a$$

where $d$ is the **piezoelectric charge coefficient** (C/N).

> [!NOTE]
> **Common KTU Confusions**
> - $d_{33}$ mode → force parallel to polarization (most sensitive, used in accelerometers)
> - $d_{31}$ mode → force perpendicular to polarization
> - The crystal is usually **preloaded** to keep it in compression for both directions of acceleration.

### Open-Circuit Voltage
Since the crystal also acts as a capacitor $C_p$:

$$V_{\text{out}} = \frac{Q}{C_p} = \frac{d \cdot m \cdot a}{C_p}$$

### Charge Amplifier Output
In practice, a charge amplifier with feedback capacitor $C_f$ and op-amp is used, giving:

$$V_{\text{out}} = -\frac{Q}{C_f} = -\frac{d \cdot m \cdot a}{C_f}$$

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Table is the most important page for your exam revision. Memorize it.**

| Quantity | Formula | Units | Symbol Description |
|----------|---------|-------|---------------------|
| Inertial force | $F = m \cdot a$ | N | $m$ = mass (kg), $a$ = acceleration (m/s²) |
| Static deflection | $x_0 = \dfrac{m \cdot a}{k}$ | m | $k$ = spring constant (N/m) |
| Natural frequency | $\omega_n = \sqrt{\dfrac{k}{m}}$ | rad/s | undamped natural frequency |
| Damping ratio | $\zeta = \dfrac{c}{2\sqrt{k \cdot m}}$ | dimensionless | $c$ = damping coefficient (N·s/m) |
| Potentiometer output | $V_{\text{out}} = \dfrac{x}{L} \cdot V_{\text{ex}}$ | V | $L$ = total track length |
| Gauge factor | $GF = \dfrac{\Delta R / R}{\varepsilon}$ | dimensionless | $\varepsilon$ = strain |
| Strain-gauge bridge output | $V_o = \dfrac{V_{\text{ex}}}{4} \cdot GF \cdot \varepsilon$ | V | quarter-bridge |
| Cantilever strain | $\varepsilon = \dfrac{6 m a l_c}{E b_c t_c^2}$ | dimensionless | end-loaded cantilever |
| Piezo charge | $Q = d \cdot m \cdot a$ | C | $d$ = charge coefficient (C/N) |
| Piezo voltage | $V = \dfrac{d \cdot m \cdot a}{C_p}$ | V | $C_p$ = crystal capacitance |
| Charge amplifier output | $V_o = -\dfrac{Q}{C_f}$ | V | $C_f$ = feedback capacitor |

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> - **Potentiometric**: car crash test rigs, vibration monitoring of slow-speed machinery, educational labs.
> - **Strain-gauge**: flight testing, civil engineering structural health monitoring, industrial vibration analysis (10 Hz – 1 kHz).
> - **Piezoelectric**: high-frequency vibration, machine condition monitoring, aerospace, modal analysis (1 Hz – 50 kHz) — **the most widely used industrial accelerometer**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exhaustive Working — Every algebraic step is shown; no shortcut phrases are used.**

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Generalized Mass-Spring-Damper Equation of an Accelerometer

### Statement
Derive the differential equation governing the relative motion of a proof mass $m$ suspended in a housing that is undergoing acceleration $a(t)$.

### Step 1 — Set up the free-body diagram
Let the housing be the non-inertial reference frame. The proof mass experiences:
- Inertial (pseudo) force in the negative direction of housing acceleration: $F_{\text{iner}} = -m \cdot a(t)$
- Spring restoring force opposing displacement: $F_s = -k \cdot x$
- Damper force opposing velocity: $F_d = -c \cdot \dfrac{dx}{dt}$

### Step 2 — Apply Newton's Second Law to the proof mass
Sum of forces on the mass equals mass times its absolute acceleration. But the mass is constrained to move only along $x$ relative to the housing, so its absolute acceleration is:

$$a_{\text{abs}} = \frac{d^2x}{dt^2} + a(t)$$

Newton's law in the housing frame therefore becomes:

$$m \cdot \frac{d^2x}{dt^2} = -k \cdot x - c \cdot \frac{dx}{dt} - m \cdot a(t)$$

### Step 3 — Rearrange to standard form
Move all terms to the left side:

$$m \cdot \frac{d^2x}{dt^2} + c \cdot \frac{dx}{dt} + k \cdot x = -m \cdot a(t)$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conversion Logic**
> This is the *deflection-type* accelerometer equation. The right-hand side acts as the *forcing function*. The left-hand side is a linear second-order system characterized by $\omega_n$ and $\zeta$.

### Step 4 — Define natural frequency and damping ratio
Divide through by $m$:

$$\frac{d^2x}{dt^2} + \frac{c}{m} \cdot \frac{dx}{dt} + \frac{k}{m} \cdot x = -a(t)$$

Recognizing standard SDOF form, we define:

$$2\zeta\omega_n = \frac{c}{m} \quad ; \quad \omega_n^2 = \frac{k}{m}$$

This gives the canonical form:

$$\frac{d^2x}{dt^2} + 2\zeta\omega_n \frac{dx}{dt} + \omega_n^2 x = -a(t)$$

### Step 5 — Steady-state (low-frequency) sensitivity
For a constant acceleration $a_0$, the steady-state is reached when all time derivatives vanish:

$$\omega_n^2 \cdot x_{\text{ss}} = -a_0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad x_{\text{ss}} = -\frac{a_0}{\omega_n^2} = -\frac{m \cdot a_0}{k}$$

The sensitivity $S$ in m/(m/s²) is therefore:

$$S = \left\vert \frac{x_{\text{ss}}}{a_0} \right\vert = \frac{1}{\omega_n^2} = \frac{m}{k}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Design Trade-off (Frequently Asked in KTU)**
> To get high sensitivity (large $x$ per unit $a$), we want a **large $m$ and small $k$**. But small $k$ reduces $\omega_n$, **shrinking the usable bandwidth**. Designers therefore face a classic compromise: high sensitivity ↔ limited bandwidth.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Sensitivity of a Potentiometric Accelerometer

### Step 1 — Wiper displacement
The wiper displacement $x$ equals the proof-mass displacement $x_{\text{ss}} = \dfrac{m \cdot a}{k}$ (ignoring sign for magnitude).

### Step 2 — Potentiometric voltage divider
The track is a uniform resistor of total resistance $R_T$ across which $V_{\text{ex}}$ is applied. Resistance from one end to the wiper is $R_x = \dfrac{x}{L} \cdot R_T$.

### Step 3 — Output voltage
By the voltage-divider rule:

$$V_{\text{out}} = \frac{R_x}{R_T} \cdot V_{\text{ex}} = \frac{x}{L} \cdot V_{\text{ex}}$$

### Step 4 — Substituting the static deflection

$$V_{\text{out}} = \frac{m \cdot a}{k \cdot L} \cdot V_{\text{ex}}$$

### Step 5 — Sensitivity in V/(m/s²)

$$S_{\text{pot}} = \frac{V_{\text{out}}}{a} = \frac{m \cdot V_{\text{ex}}}{k \cdot L}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conversion Logic**
> Doubling the excitation voltage doubles the output. Halving the track length doubles the sensitivity. Halving the spring stiffness doubles the output but halves the natural frequency.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Sensitivity of a Strain-Gauge Accelerometer

### Step 1 — Bending of the cantilever beam
The proof mass $m$ is mounted at the free end of a cantilever of length $l_c$, width $b_c$, thickness $t_c$, and Young's modulus $E$. Under acceleration, the inertial force $F = m \cdot a$ acts at the tip.

### Step 2 — Bending moment at the fixed end
The bending moment at a distance $l_c$ from the load application point is:

$$M = F \cdot l_c = m \cdot a \cdot l_c$$

### Step 3 — Surface strain at the fixed end
The maximum bending stress at the fixed end is:

$$\sigma_{\max} = \frac{6 M}{b_c \cdot t_c^2}$$

The corresponding strain (Hooke's law) is:

$$\varepsilon = \frac{\sigma_{\max}}{E} = \frac{6 m a l_c}{E b_c t_c^2}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conversion Logic**
> Doubling the beam length $l_c$ doubles the strain (linearly). Doubling the thickness $t_c$ reduces the strain by a factor of **4** (the dominant geometric lever).

### Step 4 — Resistance change in the gauge
From the gauge factor definition:

$$\frac{\Delta R}{R} = \text{GF} \cdot \varepsilon$$

### Step 5 — Wheatstone bridge output (quarter bridge)
With one active arm and three fixed resistors $R$:

$$V_{\text{out}} = V_{\text{ex}} \left[ \frac{R + \Delta R}{2R + \Delta R} - \frac{1}{2} \right]$$

For small $\Delta R$, linearize:

$$V_{\text{out}} \approx \frac{V_{\text{ex}}}{4} \cdot \frac{\Delta R}{R} = \frac{V_{\text{ex}}}{4} \cdot \text{GF} \cdot \varepsilon$$

### Step 6 — Final expression for accelerometer sensitivity

$$S_{\text{sg}} = \frac{V_{\text{out}}}{a} = \frac{V_{\text{ex}}}{4} \cdot \text{GF} \cdot \frac{6 m l_c}{E b_c t_c^2}$$

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Output of a Piezoelectric Accelerometer

### Step 1 — Stress on the crystal
The crystal is compressed by the inertial force $F = m \cdot a$ over its face area $A$:

$$T = \frac{F}{A} = \frac{m \cdot a}{A}$$

### Step 2 — Piezoelectric constitutive relation
The direct piezoelectric effect gives electric displacement $D$ (C/m²):

$$D = d \cdot T$$

### Step 3 — Total charge generated
Multiplying by the electrode area $A$:

$$Q = D \cdot A = d \cdot T \cdot A = d \cdot m \cdot a$$

### Step 4 — Open-circuit voltage across the crystal
The crystal has capacitance $C_p = \dfrac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{t_p}$, where $t_p$ is crystal thickness:

$$V_{\text{oc}} = \frac{Q}{C_p} = \frac{d \cdot m \cdot a \cdot t_p}{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}$$

### Step 5 — Sensitivity in V/(m/s²) — direct form

$$S_{\text{pe}} = \frac{V_{\text{oc}}}{a} = \frac{d \cdot m}{C_p} = \frac{d \cdot m \cdot t_p}{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}$$

### Step 6 — Sensitivity with charge amplifier
With feedback capacitor $C_f$:

$$V_{\text{out}} = -\frac{Q}{C_f} = -\frac{d \cdot m \cdot a}{C_f}$$

$$S_{\text{pe, CA}} = \frac{V_{\text{out}}}{a} = -\frac{d \cdot m}{C_f}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why is a charge amplifier preferred?**
> Cable capacitance and crystal capacitance variations (with temperature) directly affect $V_{\text{oc}}$. Using a charge amplifier, only $C_f$ — a stable, discrete component — sets the gain. This is why industrial piezoelectric accelerometers almost always use charge amplifiers.

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> **Frequently asked in KTU — "Compare the three accelerometer types"**

| Property | Potentiometric | Strain-Gauge | Piezoelectric |
|----------|----------------|---------------|----------------|
| Output type | DC voltage | DC voltage (bridge) | AC charge / voltage |
| Frequency range | 0 – 50 Hz | 0 – 1000 Hz | 1 Hz – 50 kHz |
| Sensitivity | Medium | High | Very high |
| Power required | Yes (excitation) | Yes (excitation) | No (self-generating) |
| Temperature drift | Low | Moderate | Moderate |
| Cost | Low | Medium | High |
| Typical use | Crash tests, education | Industrial vibration | Modal analysis, aerospace |
| Wear/Friction | High (slider) | None | None |
| DC response | Yes | Yes | No (AC coupled) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Generalized Accelerometer

<!-- DIAGRAM_1_START -->

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Housing Frame] --> B1[Seismic Mass m]
    B1 -- inertial coupling --> C1[Spring k]
    B1 -- viscous coupling --> C2[Damper c]
    C1 --> D1[Relative Displacement x]
    C2 --> D1
    D1 --> E1[Secondary Transducer]
    E1 -- type A --> F1A[Potentiometer Track]
    E1 -- type B --> F1B[Strain Gauge on Cantilever]
    E1 -- type C --> F1C[Piezoelectric Crystal]
    F1A --> G1[Signal Conditioning]
    F1B --> G1
    F1C --> G1
    G1 --> H1[Output Vout or Q]
    A1 -- base acceleration a of t --> D1
```

<!-- DIAGRAM_1_END -->

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram**
> The three secondary-transducer branches (Potentiometer, Strain Gauge, Piezo) replace the generic "Secondary Transducer" block depending on the construction. The same mechanical first stage (mass–spring–damper) feeds all three.

## 4.2 Potentiometric Accelerometer — Functional Block Architecture

<!-- DIAGRAM_2_START -->

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A2[Housing] --> B2[Seismic Mass on Wiper]
    B2 --> C2[Resistive Track R_T]
    C2 -- V_ex applied --> D2[Voltage Divider]
    D2 --> E2[V_out to meter or ADC]
    F2[Acceleration a of t] --> A2
    A2 -- wiper slides --> C2
```

<!-- DIAGRAM_2_END -->

## 4.3 Strain-Gauge Accelerometer — Functional Block Architecture

<!-- DIAGRAM_3_START -->

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A3[Housing] --> B3[Mass on Cantilever]
    F3[Acceleration a] --> A3
    B3 -- bending strain epsilon --> C3[Bonded Strain Gauge]
    C3 -- resistance change deltaR --> D3[Wheatstone Bridge]
    D3 --> E3[Bridge Excitation V_ex]
    D3 --> G3[Amplifier and Filter]
    G3 --> H3[Output V_out]
```

<!-- DIAGRAM_3_END -->

## 4.4 Piezoelectric Accelerometer — Functional Block Architecture

<!-- DIAGRAM_4_START -->

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A4[Housing Base] --> B4[Piezo Crystal Preloaded]
    C4[Seismic Mass m] --> B4
    F4[Acceleration a of t] --> A4
    B4 -- generates charge Q --> D4[Charge Amplifier]
    D4 --> E4[Output V_out]
    G4[Feedback Capacitor C_f] --> D4
```

<!-- DIAGRAM_4_END -->

## 4.5 Force-Flow on Proof Mass — Physical Schematic (Adapted)

<!-- DIAGRAM_5_START -->

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A5[Housing with acceleration a] --> B5[Spring with constant k]
    B5 --> C5[Proof mass m]
    C5 --> D5[Damper with coefficient c]
    D5 --> A5
    C5 -- relative displacement x --> E5[Transducer assembly]
```

<!-- DIAGRAM_5_END -->

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **How to read the force-flow diagram**
> Arrows indicate mechanical coupling. The proof mass is in the middle, connected to the housing by **two parallel elements** (the spring $k$ and the damper $c$). When the housing accelerates, the spring stretches/compresses by $x$, and the transducer measures either the displacement $x$, the strain in the spring, or the force $F = kx$ transmitted to a piezo element.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
> **Define an accelerometer. With a neat sketch, explain the working of a piezoelectric accelerometer.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

An accelerometer is a device that measures acceleration by detecting the inertial force on a proof mass suspended inside a housing by a spring and damper. In a piezoelectric accelerometer, the proof mass is mounted on a piezoelectric crystal (PZT) which is fixed to the housing base. When the housing experiences acceleration $a$, the inertial force $F = m \cdot a$ acts on the crystal, generating a charge $Q = d \cdot m \cdot a$ proportional to acceleration. This charge is converted to voltage by a charge amplifier. Since the output is generated without external excitation and the device has no moving contacts, it is suitable for high-frequency vibration measurement.

**Mark Split:** Definition (1), Sketch description (1), Working with formula (1).

---

> **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
> **What is gauge factor? Derive its expression in terms of Poisson's ratio for a strain gauge.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The gauge factor (GF) is defined as the ratio of fractional change in resistance to the strain:

$$\text{GF} = \frac{\Delta R / R}{\Delta L / L} = \frac{\Delta R / R}{\varepsilon}$$

**Derivation:** Resistance of a wire is $R = \dfrac{\rho L}{A}$. Taking natural logarithm and differentiating:

$$\frac{\Delta R}{R} = \frac{\Delta \rho}{\rho} + \frac{\Delta L}{L} - \frac{\Delta A}{A}$$

The area change relates to length change via Poisson's ratio $\nu$: $\dfrac{\Delta A}{A} = 2\nu \dfrac{\Delta L}{L}$. Substituting:

$$\frac{\Delta R}{R} = (1 + 2\nu)\varepsilon + \frac{\Delta \rho}{\rho}$$

Therefore:

$$\text{GF} = 1 + 2\nu + \frac{\Delta \rho / \rho}{\varepsilon}$$

For metallic gauges $\Delta \rho \approx 0$, so $GF \approx 1 + 2\nu \approx 2.0$.

**Mark Split:** Definition (1), Derivation steps (2).

## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks Each)

---

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Jul 2024]`

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the working of a strain-gauge-based accelerometer. Derive the expression for its output voltage in terms of acceleration, mass, beam dimensions, gauge factor and excitation voltage. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A strain-gauge accelerometer has a seismic mass of $0.05$ kg mounted on a cantilever of length $l_c = 25$ mm, width $b_c = 10$ mm, and thickness $t_c = 2$ mm. The gauge factor is $2.1$, Young's modulus is $200$ GPa, and the bridge excitation is $5$ V. Calculate: (i) the strain produced at the base of the cantilever for an acceleration of $50$ m/s², (ii) the bridge output voltage. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question A(a)

**Block Diagram Description (2 marks):**

[Refer to SECTION 4, Diagram 4.3]

The seismic mass is rigidly fixed to the free end of a cantilever, while the other end is fixed to the housing. Strain gauges are bonded on the top and bottom surfaces of the cantilever near its fixed end. A Wheatstone bridge circuit is formed with active gauges in two arms and fixed resistors in the other two. Excitation voltage $V_{\text{ex}}$ is applied across the bridge.

**Working (2 marks):** When the housing accelerates, the inertial force $F = m a$ bends the cantilever. The surface strain $\varepsilon$ at the base is transmitted to the gauges, which change their resistance by $\Delta R = R \cdot \text{GF} \cdot \varepsilon$. The Wheatstone bridge converts this into an unbalanced voltage proportional to acceleration.

**Derivation (3 marks):**

From Section 3.3, Steps 1–6:

$$\varepsilon = \frac{6 m a l_c}{E b_c t_c^2}$$

$$V_{\text{out}} = \frac{V_{\text{ex}}}{4} \cdot \text{GF} \cdot \varepsilon$$

Substituting:

$$V_{\text{out}} = \frac{V_{\text{ex}}}{4} \cdot \text{GF} \cdot \frac{6 m a l_c}{E b_c t_c^2}$$

$$\boxed{V_{\text{out}} = \frac{3 \, V_{\text{ex}} \, \text{GF} \, m \, a \, l_c}{2 \, E \, b_c \, t_c^2}}$$

---

#### Model Solution to Question A(b)

**Given:**
- $m = 0.05$ kg
- $l_c = 25 \text{ mm} = 0.025$ m
- $b_c = 10 \text{ mm} = 0.010$ m
- $t_c = 2 \text{ mm} = 0.002$ m
- $\text{GF} = 2.1$
- $E = 200 \text{ GPa} = 200 \times 10^9$ Pa
- $V_{\text{ex}} = 5$ V
- $a = 50$ m/s²

**Part (i): Strain at the base (3 marks)**

$$\varepsilon = \frac{6 \times 0.05 \times 50 \times 0.025}{200 \times 10^9 \times 0.010 \times (0.002)^2}$$

Numerator:

$$6 \times 0.05 \times 50 \times 0.025 = 0.375 \text{ N·m}$$

Denominator:

$$200 \times 10^9 \times 0.010 \times 4 \times 10^{-6} = 8000 \text{ N·m}$$

Therefore:

$$\varepsilon = \frac{0.375}{8000} = 4.6875 \times 10^{-5}$$

$$\boxed{\varepsilon \approx 46.88 \, \mu \varepsilon \text{ (microstrain)}}$$

**Part (ii): Bridge output (4 marks)**

$$V_{\text{out}} = \frac{V_{\text{ex}}}{4} \cdot \text{GF} \cdot \varepsilon$$

$$V_{\text{out}} = \frac{5}{4} \times 2.1 \times 4.6875 \times 10^{-5}$$

$$V_{\text{out}} = 1.25 \times 2.1 \times 4.6875 \times 10^{-5}$$

$$V_{\text{out}} = 2.625 \times 4.6875 \times 10^{-5}$$

$$V_{\text{out}} = 1.230 \times 10^{-4} \text{ V}$$

$$\boxed{V_{\text{out}} \approx 123.0 \, \mu\text{V} = 0.123 \text{ mV}}$$

**Sensitivity** $= V_{\text{out}} / a = 1.230 \times 10^{-4} / 50 = 2.46 \times 10^{-6}$ V/(m/s²).

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the construction and working of a piezoelectric accelerometer. Derive expressions for (i) the charge generated, and (ii) the output voltage using a charge amplifier. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A piezoelectric accelerometer uses a PZT crystal with charge coefficient $d = 250 \times 10^{-12}$ C/N, seismic mass $m = 10$ g, and crystal capacitance $C_p = 1$ nF. The crystal is connected to a charge amplifier with feedback capacitor $C_f = 1$ nF. Calculate: (i) the charge generated for an acceleration of $20$ m/s², (ii) the open-circuit voltage, and (iii) the charge-amplifier output voltage. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question B(a)

**Construction (2 marks):**

[Refer to SECTION 4, Diagram 4.4] A piezoelectric accelerometer consists of:
- A **housing base** with a mounting stud.
- A **piezoelectric crystal** (PZT disc) sandwiched between two electrodes.
- A **seismic mass** placed on top of the crystal, preloaded by a central bolt to maintain compression.
- An **output terminal** connected to a charge amplifier or voltage follower.

**Working (2 marks):** When the housing accelerates, the inertial force of the seismic mass compresses/tensions the piezo crystal, producing a charge proportional to acceleration: $Q = d \cdot m \cdot a$. The charge is collected by the electrodes. Because piezo crystals have very high output impedance, a charge amplifier is used for signal conditioning.

**Derivation (3 marks):**

**(i) Charge generated:**

$$Q = d \cdot F = d \cdot (m \cdot a)$$

$$\boxed{Q = d \cdot m \cdot a}$$

**(ii) Charge-amplifier output:**

The op-amp is configured as an inverting integrator with feedback capacitor $C_f$. By virtual-ground, the input node is at $0$ V, so the entire charge $Q$ flows through $C_f$:

$$V_{\text{out}} = -\frac{Q}{C_f} = -\frac{d \cdot m \cdot a}{C_f}$$

$$\boxed{V_{\text{out}} = -\frac{d \cdot m \cdot a}{C_f}}$$

The negative sign indicates inverting configuration (often omitted in magnitude calculations).

---

#### Model Solution to Question B(b)

**Given:**
- $d = 250 \times 10^{-12}$ C/N
- $m = 10 \text{ g} = 0.010$ kg
- $C_p = 1 \text{ nF} = 1 \times 10^{-9}$ F
- $C_f = 1 \text{ nF} = 1 \times 10^{-9}$ F
- $a = 20$ m/s²

**Part (i): Charge generated (2 marks)**

$$Q = d \cdot m \cdot a = 250 \times 10^{-12} \times 0.010 \times 20$$

$$Q = 250 \times 10^{-12} \times 0.2 = 50 \times 10^{-12} \text{ C}$$

$$\boxed{Q = 50 \text{ pC}}$$

**Part (ii): Open-circuit voltage (2 marks)**

$$V_{\text{oc}} = \frac{Q}{C_p} = \frac{50 \times 10^{-12}}{1 \times 10^{-9}} = 50 \times 10^{-3} \text{ V}$$

$$\boxed{V_{\text{oc}} = 50 \text{ mV}}$$

**Part (iii): Charge-amplifier output (3 marks)**

$$V_{\text{out}} = -\frac{Q}{C_f} = -\frac{50 \times 10^{-12}}{1 \times 10^{-9}} = -50 \times 10^{-3} \text{ V}$$

$$\boxed{V_{\text{out}} = -50 \text{ mV}}$$

**Sensitivity of the charge-amplifier output:**

$$S = \frac{|V_{\text{out}}|}{a} = \frac{50 \text{ mV}}{20 \text{ m/s}^2} = 2.5 \text{ mV/(m/s}^2)$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Unit conversion**: $m = 10$ g must be converted to **0.010 kg** before substituting. Students often write $10 \times 250 \times 20$ and lose **1 mark**.
> 2. **Sign convention**: The output of the inverting charge amplifier is negative. If asked for *magnitude*, write $50$ mV; if asked for *value*, write $-50$ mV.
> 3. **Forgetting the formula $V = Q/C$**: Several students jump from $Q$ directly to the answer without computing the intermediate $V_{\text{oc}}$ and lose a step-mark.
> 4. **Mixing up $C_p$ and $C_f$**: $C_p$ is the *crystal* capacitance (used for $V_{\text{oc}}$); $C_f$ is the *feedback* capacitance (used for the amplifier output). They are **different quantities**.
> 5. **In strain-gauge problems**: Don't forget the $V_{\text{ex}} / 4$ factor in a quarter-bridge — it accounts for only one active arm out of four.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Read this 30 minutes before the exam**
>
> - [x] An accelerometer is fundamentally a **mass-spring-damper** system; the housing is the frame, the proof mass is the inertial element, and the secondary transducer is what differentiates the three types.
> - [x] Generalized equation: $m \ddot{x} + c \dot{x} + k x = -m a(t)$.
> - [x] Static sensitivity: $S = m/k$ (deflection per unit acceleration).
> - [x] Natural frequency: $\omega_n = \sqrt{k/m}$; usable bandwidth is roughly $0$ to $\omega_n / 5$.
> - [x] **Potentiometric**: $V_{\text{out}} = (x/L) V_{\text{ex}}$; cheap, low bandwidth, prone to wear.
> - [x] **Strain-gauge**: $V_{\text{out}} = (V_{\text{ex}} / 4) \cdot \text{GF} \cdot \varepsilon$; GF $\approx 1 + 2\nu$ for metals.
> - [x] **Cantilever strain**: $\varepsilon = 6 m a l_c / (E b_c t_c^2)$ — thickness $t_c$ enters as $t_c^2$.
> - [x] **Piezoelectric**: $Q = d \cdot m \cdot a$; $V_{\text{oc}} = Q / C_p$; charge amplifier: $V_{\text{out}} = -Q / C_f$.
> - [x] **Always convert** mm to m, g to kg, pF to F, $\mu\varepsilon$ to strain unitless before substitution.
> - [x] **Sign of output**: for an inverting charge amplifier, the output is negative; for Wheatstone bridge with proper gauge orientation, output polarity depends on which arms are active.
> - [x] Frequency-domain comparison: piezo > strain-gauge > potentiometric.
> - [x] DC response: potentiometric ≈ strain-gauge (yes); piezoelectric (no, AC coupled).
> - [x] For design problems, remember the **sensitivity–bandwidth trade-off**: high $m/k$ ratio improves sensitivity but lowers $\omega_n$.
> - [x] Standard values: $g = 9.81$ m/s², PZT-5A $d_{33} \approx 374$ pC/N, constantan $\nu \approx 0.3$.
> - [x] **Most-missed question type**: "Compare the three types in a 14-mark answer" — always include a comparison table for full marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Light Transducers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBRT303 - SENSORS AND ACTUATORS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Light Transducers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What are Light Transducers?

A **Light Transducer** is an electronic device that converts incident optical (light) energy into a measurable electrical signal (current, voltage, or resistance change). They are a class of **optoelectronic sensors** that fall under the broader category of *photoelectric transducers*. Some devices like LEDs and laser diodes are the *inverse* — they convert electrical energy into light — but the KTU Module 4 syllabus primarily focuses on the light-to-electricity conversion family used in sensing applications.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (PCBRT303 Module 4):**
> *Light Transducers* include devices whose electrical output varies as a function of the intensity, wavelength, or modulation of incident electromagnetic radiation in the visible, infrared (IR), and ultraviolet (UV) spectra. They form the backbone of modern automation, optical communication, imaging, and safety systems.

### 1.2 Classification of Light Transducers

Light transducers can be classified based on their physical operating principle:

| Class | Operating Principle | Common Devices |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Photo-emissive** | Light ejects electrons from a surface | Photomultiplier Tube, Phototube |
| **Photo-conductive** | Light changes the material's electrical conductivity | **LDR (Light Dependent Resistor)**, Photoconductive cell |
| **Photo-voltaic** | Light generates a voltage across a junction | **Solar Cell**, Photodiode (photovoltaic mode) |
| **Photo-junction** | Light generates a current in a reverse-biased PN junction | **Photodiode (photoconductive mode)**, **Phototransistor** |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Solar Umbrella"

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy: The Crowd at a Toll Booth**
> Imagine a toll booth with multiple gates. When no cars arrive (no light), the booth is idle. When a few cars approach (dim light), only one or two gates open — small current flows. When a traffic jam hits (bright light), every gate opens simultaneously — large current flows. A light transducer works exactly like this: **photons (cars) strike a semiconductor material and "unlock" charge carriers (electrons)**, allowing electrical current to flow. The brighter the light, the more gates (charge carriers) open.

### 1.4 Key Performance Metrics (Standard Metrics in Bold)

- **Responsivity (R)** — Output current per unit optical power, measured in **A/W (Amperes per Watt)**.
- **Quantum Efficiency (η)** — Ratio of collected electrons to incident photons (dimensionless, 0 to 1).
- **Spectral Response** — Wavelength range over which the device is sensitive (typically **400 nm to 1100 nm** for silicon).
- **Dark Current (I_d)** — Small leakage current flowing even in total darkness.
- **Rise Time / Fall Time (t_r / t_f)** — Speed of response to a light pulse, typically in **µs** or **ns**.
- **Sensitivity** — Smallest detectable change in light intensity.
- **Operating Wavelength (λ_peak)** — Wavelength at which the device is most responsive.

### 1.5 Why Light Transducers Matter in Engineering

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Light transducers are critical in **robotics (line-following, obstacle detection)**, **industrial automation (object counting, sorting)**, **medical instrumentation (pulse oximeters, blood analyzers)**, **renewable energy (solar PV panels)**, **consumer electronics (smartphone ambient light sensors, cameras)**, and **automotive systems (headlight dimmers, rain-light sensors)**. Mastering them is mandatory for any instrumentation engineer.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Photo-Conductive Transducer — The Light Dependent Resistor (LDR)

### 2.1.1 Construction & Working

An **LDR (Light Dependent Resistor)**, also called a **photoresistor**, is a passive resistive component whose resistance **decreases** as incident light intensity increases. It is typically fabricated from cadmium sulfide (**CdS**) or cadmium selenide (**CdSe**) — both are wide bandgap semiconductors (~**2.4 eV** for CdS).

The high-ohmic darkness resistance is due to the limited availability of free charge carriers in the valence band. When photons of energy greater than the bandgap strike the material, electrons are excited from the valence band to the conduction band, dramatically increasing conductivity.

### 2.1.2 Working Principle — Step-by-Step Logic

- **Step 1:** In darkness, the CdS material has very few free electrons. Resistance is very high (typically **1 MΩ to 10 MΩ**).
- **Step 2:** When light (photons) strikes the surface, photons with energy $h\nu \geq E_g$ are absorbed.
- **Step 3:** Each absorbed photon generates one electron-hole pair.
- **Step 4:** The newly freed electrons increase the available charge carrier density in the conduction band.
- **Step 5:** Conductivity $\sigma$ increases proportionally with carrier density, and resistance $R = \dfrac{1}{\sigma} \cdot \dfrac{L}{A}$ falls sharply.
- **Step 6:** Under bright light (~1000 lux), the resistance drops to as low as a few **hundred ohms**.

## 2.2 Photodiode

### 2.2.1 Construction & Working

A **photodiode** is a reverse-biased PN junction diode whose reverse leakage current is controlled by incident light. It is a **photo-junction** device.

- The junction is enclosed in a transparent window that allows light to strike the depletion region.
- In reverse bias, the depletion width is large, creating a strong internal electric field.
- Incident photons generate electron-hole pairs inside or near the depletion region.
- The electric field sweeps the electrons toward the N-side and holes toward the P-side, producing a measurable photocurrent.

### 2.2.2 Two Operating Modes

- **Photoconductive Mode:** Reverse-biased. Fast response, linear current-vs-light relationship. Used in high-speed applications.
- **Photovoltaic Mode:** Zero-bias. Produces a small forward voltage like a tiny solar cell. Used in low-noise, high-precision light meters.

### 2.2.3 Core Equation of the Photodiode

The total current through a photodiode is the sum of the dark (diode) current and the photo-generated current:

$$I_{total} = I_0 \left( e^{\frac{qV}{\eta k T}} - 1 \right) - I_L$$

Where:
- $I_0$ = reverse saturation (dark) current
- $I_L$ = photo-generated current (proportional to light intensity)
- $\eta$ = ideality factor (1 to 2)
- $V$ = applied voltage across the diode
- $q = 1.6 \times 10^{-19}$ C
- $k = 1.38 \times 10^{-23}$ J/K (Boltzmann constant)
- $T$ = absolute temperature in Kelvin

## 2.3 Phototransistor

A **phototransistor** is essentially a bipolar junction transistor (BJT) where the base current is provided by light-generated carriers instead of an external electrical connection.

- The base-collector junction acts as a photodiode.
- The base photocurrent $I_{ph}$ gets amplified by the transistor's current gain $h_{FE}$.
- Output collector current: $I_C = h_{FE} \cdot I_{ph}$ (often **100× to 1000×** larger than a bare photodiode).

## 2.4 Photovoltaic (Solar) Cell

A **photovoltaic cell** is a large-area PN junction (usually silicon) that converts light directly into electricity via the **photovoltaic effect**. It operates in the **fourth quadrant** of its I-V curve (current flows out of the positive terminal).

### 2.4.1 Key Parameters of a Solar Cell

- **I_sc** — Short-circuit current (proportional to light intensity)
- **V_oc** — Open-circuit voltage (logarithmically dependent on light)
- **P_max** — Maximum power point power
- **Fill Factor (FF)** — Measure of cell quality
- **η (eta)** — Conversion efficiency

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these equations — they appear in nearly every KTU question on light transducers.**

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $R_{LDR} = A \cdot L^{-\alpha}$ | LDR resistance vs illuminance (L in lux) | Ohms (Ω) |
| 2 | $\sigma = q(n\mu_e + p\mu_h)$ | Conductivity of photoconductor | S/m |
| 3 | $I_{ph} = q \cdot A \cdot g \cdot (L_n + L_p)$ | Photocurrent generation rate | Amperes (A) |
| 4 | $I = I_0(e^{qV/\eta kT} - 1) - I_L$ | Photodiode I-V equation | Amperes (A) |
| 5 | $R_\lambda = \dfrac{I_{ph}}{P_{opt}} = \dfrac{\eta q \lambda}{hc}$ | Spectral responsivity | A/W |
| 6 | $R_\lambda \approx \dfrac{\eta \lambda}{1.24}$ | Simplified responsivity (λ in µm) | A/W |
| 7 | $\eta_{QE} = \dfrac{N_{electrons}}{N_{photons}} = \dfrac{I_{ph}/q}{P_{opt}/h\nu}$ | Quantum efficiency | Dimensionless |
| 8 | $I_C = h_{FE} \cdot I_{ph}$ | Phototransistor collector current | Amperes (A) |
| 9 | $V_{oc} = \dfrac{\eta kT}{q} \ln\left(\dfrac{I_{L}}{I_0} + 1\right)$ | Solar cell open-circuit voltage | Volts (V) |
| 10 | $FF = \dfrac{V_m I_m}{V_{oc} I_{sc}}$ | Fill Factor of solar cell | Dimensionless |
| 11 | $\eta_{cell} = \dfrac{P_{max}}{P_{in}} = \dfrac{FF \cdot V_{oc} \cdot I_{sc}}{P_{in}}$ | Solar cell conversion efficiency | Dimensionless |

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Applications

- **LDR** → Automatic street lights, camera exposure meters, burglar alarms.
- **Photodiode** → Smoke detectors, optical fiber receivers, bar-code scanners, solar radiation meters.
- **Phototransistor** → Object detection in assembly lines, IR remote receivers, optical encoders.
- **Photovoltaic Cell** → Solar panels for rooftop power, satellites (space-grade cells), IoT sensor power supplies, calculators.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Spectral Responsivity of a Photodiode

**Goal:** Prove that the responsivity $R_\lambda$ of a photodiode is given by:

$$R_\lambda = \frac{\eta q \lambda}{hc}$$

**Given:**
- Optical power incident on the device: $P_{opt}$ (Watts)
- Quantum efficiency: $\eta$
- Wavelength of light: $\lambda$
- Planck's constant: $h = 6.626 \times 10^{-34}$ J·s
- Speed of light: $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s
- Electron charge: $q = 1.6 \times 10^{-19}$ C

**Step 1 — Energy of a single photon:**

$$E_{photon} = h\nu = \frac{hc}{\lambda}$$

**Step 2 — Number of incident photons per second:**

The number of photons hitting the device per second is the total optical power divided by the energy of one photon:

$$N_{photons} = \frac{P_{opt}}{E_{photon}} = \frac{P_{opt} \lambda}{hc}$$

**Step 3 — Number of electrons collected per second:**

By definition, quantum efficiency $\eta$ is the ratio of electrons collected to photons incident. Therefore:

$$N_{electrons} = \eta \cdot N_{photons} = \frac{\eta \cdot P_{opt} \cdot \lambda}{hc}$$

**Step 4 — Photo-generated current:**

Current is charge per unit time. Each electron carries charge $q$, so the photo-current is:

$$I_{ph} = q \cdot N_{electrons} = \frac{\eta \cdot q \cdot P_{opt} \cdot \lambda}{hc}$$

**Step 5 — Define responsivity:**

Responsivity is the output photo-current per unit of incident optical power:

$$R_\lambda = \frac{I_{ph}}{P_{opt}} = \frac{\eta \cdot q \cdot \lambda}{hc}$$

**Final Result:**

$$\boxed{R_\lambda = \frac{\eta q \lambda}{hc}}$$

**Step 6 — Substitute standard constants:**

$$R_\lambda = \frac{\eta \cdot (1.6 \times 10^{-19}) \cdot \lambda}{6.626 \times 10^{-34} \times 3 \times 10^{8}}$$

$$R_\lambda = \frac{\eta \cdot (1.6 \times 10^{-19}) \cdot \lambda}{1.9878 \times 10^{-25}}$$

$$R_\lambda = 0.805 \times 10^{6} \cdot \eta \cdot \lambda \quad \text{(λ in meters)}$$

For $\lambda$ in micrometers, the convenient form becomes:

$$R_\lambda \approx 0.806 \cdot \eta \cdot \lambda \quad \text{(A/W, λ in µm)}$$

This is frequently rounded in textbooks to the **engineering rule of thumb:**

$$R_\lambda \approx \frac{\eta \lambda}{1.24} \quad \text{(A/W, λ in µm)}$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Open-Circuit Voltage of a Photovoltaic Cell

**Goal:** Derive the expression for $V_{oc}$ in terms of photocurrent $I_L$ and reverse saturation current $I_0$.

**Starting from the photodiode equation** (which also governs a solar cell in forward bias at the operating point):

$$I = I_0 \left( e^{\frac{qV}{\eta k T}} - 1 \right) - I_L$$

At **open circuit**, the terminal current $I = 0$ (no load connected). Let $V = V_{oc}$:

$$0 = I_0 \left( e^{\frac{qV_{oc}}{\eta k T}} - 1 \right) - I_L$$

Rearranging:

$$I_L = I_0 \left( e^{\frac{qV_{oc}}{\eta k T}} - 1 \right)$$

Dividing both sides by $I_0$:

$$\frac{I_L}{I_0} = e^{\frac{qV_{oc}}{\eta k T}} - 1$$

Taking the natural logarithm of both sides:

$$\ln\left( \frac{I_L}{I_0} + 1 \right) = \frac{qV_{oc}}{\eta k T}$$

Solving for $V_{oc}$:

$$\boxed{V_{oc} = \frac{\eta k T}{q} \ln\left( \frac{I_L}{I_0} + 1 \right)}$$

**Physical interpretation:**
- $V_{oc}$ increases **logarithmically** with light intensity.
- $V_{oc}$ is **clamped** by the saturation current $I_0$ — better materials with lower $I_0$ give higher voltage.
- The factor $\dfrac{kT}{q} \approx 25.85$ mV at room temperature (300 K) is the **thermal voltage**.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Phototransistor Output Current

**Goal:** Show that the output current of a phototransistor is $h_{FE}$ times the photocurrent of its base-collector photodiode.

**Step 1:** The base-collector junction of a BJT is a reverse-biased PN junction. When light falls on it, a photo-current $I_{ph}$ flows in the reverse direction — equivalent to a base current injection.

**Step 2:** In a normal BJT, the relationship between base and collector current is:

$$I_C = h_{FE} \cdot I_B$$

**Step 3:** Replacing the electrical base current $I_B$ with the photo-generated base current $I_{ph}$:

$$\boxed{I_C = h_{FE} \cdot I_{ph}}$$

**Step 4:** Since $h_{FE}$ typically ranges from **100 to 1000**, a phototransistor delivers **two to three orders of magnitude** higher output current than a bare photodiode receiving the same light. This is the key reason phototransistors are preferred in low-light switching applications.

---

## 3.4 Numerical Worked Example (KTU Board Style)

> [!NOTE]
> **Example Problem (Typical 7-Mark KTU Question):**
> A silicon photodiode has a quantum efficiency of 80% at a wavelength of 900 nm. Calculate (a) its responsivity in A/W, and (b) the photo-current when illuminated by 5 µW of optical power.

**Solution:**

**Part (a) — Responsivity:**

Using $R_\lambda \approx \dfrac{\eta \lambda}{1.24}$ with $\lambda$ in µm:

$$R_\lambda = \frac{0.8 \times 0.9}{1.24} = \frac{0.72}{1.24} = 0.5806 \text{ A/W}$$

$$\boxed{R_\lambda \approx 0.581 \text{ A/W}}$$

**Part (b) — Photocurrent:**

$$I_{ph} = R_\lambda \times P_{opt} = 0.581 \text{ A/W} \times 5 \times 10^{-6} \text{ W}$$

$$\boxed{I_{ph} = 2.905 \text{ µA}}$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

## 4.1 Block Architecture of a Light Sensing System

## 4.2 Internal Architecture of a Photodiode (Cross-Sectional View)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Photodiode_Cross_Section [Photodiode Internal Architecture]
        TOP[Top Ohmic Contact P Plus] --> P[P Type Semiconductor]
        P --> D[Junction Depletion Region]
        D --> N[N Type Semiconductor]
        N --> BOT[Bottom Ohmic Contact N Plus]
    end

    LIGHT[Incident Light Photons] -.->|Strike| D
    D -->|Electron Hole Pairs| FLOW[Photocurrent Flows Externally]

    classDef semi fill:#ffe6cc,stroke:#d79b00,color:#000
    classDef contact fill:#d5e8d4,stroke:#82b366,color:#000
    classDef light fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#d6b656,color:#000

    class P,N,D semi
    class TOP,BOT contact
    class LIGHT light
    class FLOW flow
```

**Figure 4.2** — Reverse-biased photodiode showing how photons absorbed in the depletion region create electron-hole pairs that are swept out by the internal electric field.

## 4.3 Phototransistor Equivalent Block Model

```mermaid
flowchart LR

        L1[Incident Light Photons] --> PD[Base Collector Photodiode]
        PD -->|Photo Current Iph| BJTB[BJT Transistor hFE Amplifier]
        BJTB -->|Amplified Output IC| OUT[Collector Output Terminal]
    end

    LDR[Light Intensity Increases] -.->|Increases| L1

    class PD semi
    class BJTB amp
    class OUT, LDR out
```

flowchart LR

        NA --> D1[Diode D Representing PN Junction]
        D1 --> NB[Node B Ground]
        NA --> RS[Series Resistance R_S]
        RS --> NL[Node Load Positive]
        NL --> RSH[Shunt Resistance R_SH in Parallel]
        RSH --> NB
        NL --> RL[External Load R_L]
        RL --> NB
    end

    VL[Output Voltage V Across Load] -.-> NL
    ILM[Light Intensity Increases] -.->|Increases| IL

    Q1 -->|Speed Critical| PD[Photodiode - Nanosecond Response]
