# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) — *[KTU University Exam – May 2024]*

**Q:** Define a Bourdon tube. Explain its working principle with a suitable sketch.

**Model Answer:**

A Bourdon tube is a curved, elastic metallic tube with a flattened or specially shaped cross-section, sealed at one end and open at the other to admit the fluid whose pressure is to be measured.

**Working Principle:**
1. The fluid pressure enters the open end of the curved tube.
2. Internal pressure creates a tensile hoop stress on the tube walls.
3. Because the tube is curved, the outer fiber (longer arc length) experiences greater strain than the inner fiber (shorter arc length).
4. This differential strain produces a net bending moment that tends to **straighten** the curved tube.
5. The sealed tip deflects by an amount proportional to the applied pressure.
6. The tip deflection is mechanically amplified through a **linkage-and-pinion** assembly and indicated on a calibrated dial.

**Sketch:** Refer to the C-type Bourdon tube geometry shown in Section 4.2 — a 270° arc tube with sealed tip connected to a pointer linkage.

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks) — *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]*

**Q:** With a neat diagram, explain the working of a capacitive pressure transducer. State the governing equation.

**Model Answer:**

A capacitive pressure transducer consists of a pressure-sensitive **diaphragm** that forms one plate of a parallel-plate capacitor, with a fixed reference plate separated by a precise air gap $d_0$.

**Working:**
1. The fluid pressure to be measured is applied to one face of the diaphragm.
2. The diaphragm deflects by $w_{max}$ toward the fixed plate, reducing the effective gap to $d = d_0 - w_{max}$.
3. This gap change modulates the capacitance as $C = \dfrac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{d}$.
4. A capacitance-to-voltage converter outputs a voltage proportional to pressure.

**Governing Equation:**

$$C = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{d_0 - w_{max}}$$

For small deflections, the fractional change simplifies to:

$$\frac{\Delta C}{C_0} \approx \frac{w_{max}}{d_0} \approx \frac{3 P a^4 (1-\nu^2)}{16 E h^3 d_0}$$

This linearizes the response: $\Delta C \propto P$.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Detailed Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Pattern Compliance:**
> Each Part B question has **internal choice** (either attempt A or attempt B in full). Each is divided into **two sub-parts of 7 marks each**, typically combining (a) a descriptive/theoretical part and (b) a derivation/calculation part.

---

### QUESTION A (14 Marks) — *[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 4]*

#### (a) Explain with neat sketches the construction and working of a Bourdon tube pressure gauge. Discuss its advantages, limitations, and typical applications. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Construction:**
A C-type Bourdon tube consists of:
- A **C-shaped curved tube** with a flattened or elliptical cross-section (typical cross-section: $a \approx 2$–$4$ mm, $b \approx 0.3$–$0.5$ mm).
- **Material**: Phosphor bronze (low pressure), beryllium copper, or stainless steel (high pressure).
- **Open end (process end):** Connected to the pressure source through a precision fitting.
- **Sealed end (tip):** Connected via a mechanical linkage to the pointer assembly.
- **Linkage assembly**: Converts tip linear motion to angular pointer rotation via sector-and-pinion gears.

**Working (Detailed):**
1. Pressurized fluid enters the open end.
2. Internal pressure generates hoop stress $\sigma_h = \dfrac{P r_i}{t}$ in the tube wall.
3. The non-circular cross-section (flat or elliptical) causes **greater strain in the minor axis direction** than in the major axis.
4. When the tube tries to expand, the minor axis wants to grow more than the major axis — but this is constrained by the tube's curvature, producing a **net straightening moment**.
5. Tip displacement $x = R \cdot \Delta\theta$ where $\Delta\theta$ is the angular change in tube curvature.
6. The linkage amplifies $x$ and rotates the pointer on the calibrated dial.

**Advantages:**
- Simple, rugged construction.
- **Self-contained**: no external power required.
- Intrinsically safe for explosive/hazardous atmospheres.
- Wide pressure range (vacuum to ~7000 bar with helical designs).
- Moderate accuracy (±0.5% FS for industrial grade).
- Long-term stability (no electronic drift).
- Low cost and easy field maintenance.

**Limitations:**
- Mechanical wear in moving linkages.
- Hysteresis in elastic deformation (~0.1–0.5% FS).
- Sensitive to vibration and mechanical shock.
- Temperature effects on spring constant and material modulus.
- Output is local indication — no remote transmission capability (unless coupled with LVDT or strain gauge).

**Applications:**
- Steam boiler pressure gauges in power plants.
- Hydraulic and pneumatic line monitoring.
- Local pressure gauges in oil refineries and chemical plants.
- Compressed gas cylinder regulators.
- Ship engine rooms and marine applications.
- Test benches and laboratory pressure calibration.

---

#### (b) Derive an expression for the maximum deflection at the center of a circular diaphragm clamped at its edges and loaded uniformly with pressure $P$. State the assumptions made. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Assumptions:**
1. The diaphragm is **thin**: $h \ll a$ (typically $h/a < 0.1$).
2. Material is **homogeneous, isotropic**, and obeys Hooke's law.
3. Deflection $w$ is **small** compared to thickness: $w_{max} \ll h$ (so that membrane (stretching) stresses are negligible compared to bending stresses).
4. The plate is **clamped (built-in) at the periphery**: $w(a) = 0$ and $\dfrac{dw}{dr}\bigg|_{r=a} = 0$.
5. Loading is **axisymmetric** (uniform pressure).
6. The deflection profile depends only on $r$ (axisymmetric assumption).

**Derivation (Summary — refer to Section 3.1 for full derivation):**

The governing differential equation is:

$$D \nabla^4 w(r) = P$$

with flexural rigidity:

$$D = \frac{E h^3}{12(1-\nu^2)}$$

Four successive integrations yield:

$$w(r) = \frac{P}{64 D} (a^2 - r^2)^2$$

Applying the boundary condition $r = 0$ for maximum deflection:

$$\boxed{w_{max} = w(0) = \frac{P a^4}{64 D} = \frac{3 P a^4 (1-\nu^2)}{16 E h^3}}$$

**Interpretation:**
- Linear in $P$ → linear transducer response.
- Quartic in $a$ → radius is the most powerful design lever.
- Inverse cubic in $h$ → thickness drastically affects sensitivity.

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 4]*

#### (a) With a neat block diagram, explain the construction and working of a capacitive pressure transducer. Discuss its advantages over strain-gauge type pressure sensors. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Construction (Refer to Section 4.4 Mermaid Diagram):**

A capacitive pressure transducer comprises:
- A **pressure port** admitting fluid whose pressure is to be measured.
- A **thin metallic (or silicon) diaphragm** that acts as one plate of a parallel-plate capacitor.
- A **fixed reference plate** separated from the diaphragm by a precisely machined air gap $d_0$ (typically 10–100 μm).
- A **housing** providing hermetic sealing and overpressure stops.
- **Electrical terminals** for connection to a C/V converter.
- For **differential designs**: a second fixed plate on the opposite side of the diaphragm.

**Working (Detailed):**
1. At zero pressure, the diaphragm is at rest at gap $d_0$, producing reference capacitance $C_0 = \dfrac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{d_0}$.
2. Applied pressure $P$ deflects the diaphragm by $w_{max}$ at its center, reducing the gap to $d_0 - w_{max}$ at the center.
3. The capacitance increases: $C(P) = \dfrac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{d_0 - w_{max}}$.
4. A high-precision **AC bridge** or **charge amplifier** converts this capacitance change to a measurable voltage.
5. The output voltage is linear with pressure for small $w_{max}/d_0$.

**Advantages over Strain-Gauge Type Sensors:**

| Aspect | Capacitive | Strain Gauge |
|---|---|---|
| Power consumption | Very low (~μW) | Higher (requires bridge excitation) |
| Temperature sensitivity | Very low (no resistive heating) | Moderate (gauge factor drifts) |
| Sensitivity | Higher (especially at low pressure) | Moderate |
| Long-term stability | Excellent | Good (subject to gauge fatigue) |
| MEMS compatibility | Native | Requires bonded gauges |
| Output magnitude | fF–pF (requires C/V converter) | mV-level (direct readout) |
| Hysteresis | Negligible | Moderate |

**Additional Advantages:**
- **Higher sensitivity at low pressures** (down to 1 Pa in differential designs).
- **Lower noise floor** due to absence of Johnson noise from resistive elements.
- **Better overpressure tolerance** with mechanical stops integrated into the design.
- **Vacuum-compatible** — no outgassing from polymer strain gauges.

---

#### (b) A capacitive pressure transducer uses a circular diaphragm of radius $a = 5\,\text{mm}$, thickness $h = 0.2\,\text{mm}$, made of stainless steel ($E = 200\,\text{GPa}$, $\nu = 0.3$). The nominal air gap is $d_0 = 0.1\,\text{mm}$. Calculate the fractional capacitance change when the applied pressure is $P = 50\,\text{kPa}$. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — Identify known quantities:**
- $a = 5\,\text{mm} = 5 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{m}$
- $h = 0.2\,\text{mm} = 2 \times 10^{-4}\,\text{m}$
- $E = 200\,\text{GPa} = 2 \times 10^{11}\,\text{Pa}$
- $\nu = 0.3$
- $d_0 = 0.1\,\text{mm} = 1 \times 10^{-4}\,\text{m}$
- $P = 50\,\text{kPa} = 5 \times 10^{4}\,\text{Pa}$

**Step 2 — Compute correction factor $(1 - \nu^2)$:**

$$1 - \nu^2 = 1 - (0.3)^2 = 1 - 0.09 = 0.91$$

**Step 3 — Compute maximum diaphragm deflection $w_{max}$:**

$$w_{max} = \frac{3 P a^4 (1-\nu^2)}{16 E h^3}$$

Substituting:

$$w_{max} = \frac{3 \times 5 \times 10^4 \times (5 \times 10^{-3})^4 \times 0.91}{16 \times 2 \times 10^{11} \times (2 \times 10^{-4})^3}$$

Compute numerator:
$$3 \times 5 \times 10^4 \times 6.25 \times 10^{-10} \times 0.91 = 8.531 \times 10^{-5}$$

Compute denominator:
$$16 \times 2 \times 10^{11} \times 8 \times 10^{-12} = 16 \times 1.6 = 25.6$$

Therefore:

$$w_{max} = \frac{8.531 \times 10^{-5}}{25.6} = 3.332 \times 10^{-6}\,\text{m} = 3.332\,\mu\text{m}$$

**Step 4 — Compute fractional capacitance change:**

$$\frac{\Delta C}{C_0} = \frac{w_{max}}{d_0 - w_{max}}$$

$$\frac{\Delta C}{C_0} = \frac{3.332 \times 10^{-6}}{1 \times 10^{-4} - 3.332 \times 10^{-6}} = \frac{3.332 \times 10^{-6}}{9.667 \times 10^{-5}}$$

$$\frac{\Delta C}{C_0} \approx 0.03447 = 3.447\%$$

**Step 5 — Verify small-deflection approximation:**

Since $w_{max}/d_0 = 3.332 \times 10^{-6} / 10^{-4} = 0.0333 \ll 1$, the linearized formula $\Delta C/C_0 \approx w_{max}/d_0 \approx 3.33\%$ is valid within ~3% error.

**Final Answer:**

$$\boxed{\frac{\Delta C}{C_0} \approx 3.45\% \text{ at } P = 50\,\text{kPa}}$$

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning & Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Typically Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the $(1-\nu^2)$ factor in diaphragm deflection.**
>    Many students write $w_{max} = \dfrac{P a^4}{64 D}$ without substituting $D$ fully, OR substitute $D = \dfrac{E h^3}{12}$ instead of $\dfrac{E h^3}{12(1-\nu^2)}$. This is a **2-mark loss** in derivations. **Always expand $D$ explicitly.**
>
> 2. **Confusing absolute, gauge, and differential pressure in numerical problems.**
>    If the problem states "gauge pressure of 50 kPa," do NOT add atmospheric pressure unless explicitly asked. State the reference clearly.
>
> 3. **Sign errors in capacitance change equations.**
>    The fractional change $\Delta C / C_0 = -\Delta d / d_0$ has a **negative sign** because reducing the gap **increases** capacitance. Forgetting the sign loses 1 mark.
>
> 4. **Mixing up Bourdon tube and diaphragm deflection formulas.**
>    Bourdon tubes use a *curved beam* formula involving arc length, cross-section, and modulus. Diaphragms use *thin-plate theory* with $D = \dfrac{E h^3}{12(1-\nu^2)}$. Do NOT interchange them.
>
> 5. **Skipping units in final answers.**
>    KTU examiners deduct marks for unitless numerical answers. Always state units (Pa, m, F, Hz).
>
> 6. **Mermaid / diagram labels in written exams.**
>    In a written exam, you must draw actual diagrams — labeled with pressure inlet, diaphragm, fixed plate, gap $d_0$, output terminals. Do NOT submit block diagrams without physical identification of components.
>
> 7. **Neglecting nonlinearity at large deflections.**
>    The linearization $\Delta C / C_0 \approx w_{max}/d_0$ is only valid when $w_{max} \ll d_0$. For higher pressures, the full nonlinear expression $\Delta C / C_0 = \dfrac{w_{max}}{d_0 - w_{max}}$ must be used.
>
> 8. **Material selection justification missing.**
>    When asked to "discuss" Bourdon tube or diaphragm materials, mention *why* (e.g., phosphor bronze for low pressure due to high elastic limit; beryllium copper for higher pressure; silicon for MEMS due to excellent elastic properties and IC compatibility).

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Module 4 — Rapid Revision Checklist:**

### Bourdon Tube
- [x] Curved metallic tube that *straightens* under internal pressure.
- [x] Operating principle: **differential strain in inner vs outer fiber** of a curved beam.
- [x] C-type, helical, spiral, and twisted flat variants exist.
- [x] Output is **mechanical displacement** (mm-scale tip motion).
- [x] Linked to a pointer via **linkage + pinion gear** for dial indication.
- [x] Formula: $\Delta\theta = \dfrac{P a^3 b R \alpha}{E I L}$, tip displacement $x = R \Delta\theta$.
- [x] **Advantages:** no power, intrinsically safe, rugged, wide range.
- [x] **Disadvantages:** no remote transmission, mechanical wear, hysteresis.
- [x] **Material:** phosphor bronze (low P), beryllium copper (medium P), stainless steel (high P).

### Diaphragm
- [x] Thin elastic plate (circular or rectangular) **clamped at periphery**.
- [x] Operating principle: **thin-plate bending** under uniform transverse pressure.
- [x] Deflection profile: $w(r) = \dfrac{P}{64 D}(a^2 - r^2)^2$.
- [x] Maximum deflection at center: $w_{max} = \dfrac{3 P a^4 (1-\nu^2)}{16 E h^3}$.
- [x] Maximum stress at clamped edge: $\sigma_{max} = \dfrac{3 P a^2}{4 h^2}$.
- [x] Flexural rigidity: $D = \dfrac{E h^3}{12(1-\nu^2)}$.
- [x] **Variants:** flat, corrugated, capsule (2 diaphragms), bellows (stacked).
- [x] **Used as** primary element for strain-gauge and capacitive pressure sensors.

### Capacitive Pressure Transducer
- [x] Converts pressure to **capacitance change** via variable gap or variable area.
- [x] Governing equation: $C = \dfrac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{d}$.
- [x] For small deflection: $\Delta C / C_0 \approx w_{max}/d_0 \propto P$.
- [x] **Sensitivity:** $S = \dfrac{3 a^4 (1-\nu^2)}{16 E h^3 d_0}$ (per Pa).
- [x] Sensitivity scales as $a^4$, $1/h^3$, $1/E$, $1/d_0$.
- [x] **Differential architecture** uses two fixed plates and one movable diaphragm — eliminates common-mode errors.
- [x] Requires **C/V converter** (AC bridge or charge amplifier) for signal readout.
- [x] **Advantages:** very low power, high sensitivity at low pressure, low drift, MEMS-compatible.
- [x] **Applications:** barometric sensors, medical ventilators, automotive MAP sensors, IoT pressure nodes.

### Cross-Cutting Concepts
- [x] **Hooke's Law** $F = kx$ underlies all elastic displacement sensors.
- [x] **Young's modulus $E$** and **Poisson's ratio $\nu$** are the key material properties.
- [x] **Pressure units**: Pa (SI), bar (industrial), psi (imperial), atm (standard).
- [x] **Trade-off triangle:** Sensitivity ↔ Linearity ↔ Overpressure Tolerance.
- [x] **All three transducers** can be reduced to the same fundamental operation: **Pressure → Mechanical Deformation → Measurable Output (mechanical or electrical).**

# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Oscillator Transducer (Definition):**
> An **Oscillator Transducer** is an *active transducer* that converts a physical measurand (force, pressure, temperature, displacement, humidity, etc.) into a proportional change in the **frequency** of an oscillating electrical signal. The measurand alters one of the frequency-determining parameters (R, L, or C) of an oscillator circuit, causing its output frequency to vary in a predictable, calibrated manner.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme framework, the oscillator transducer belongs to the broader classification of **frequency-output transducers**, where information is encoded as a frequency variation rather than as voltage, current, or resistance.

### 1.1.1 Why "Frequency" as the Output?

Frequency is fundamentally a **countable, digital-friendly parameter**. Unlike analog voltages that degrade due to cable resistance, EMI, and contact noise, a frequency signal can be transmitted over long distances, multiplexed easily, and measured with extreme precision using digital counters (resolution down to **parts-per-billion** with time-base referencing).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Tuning Fork & Loaded String

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Plain-English Analogy — A Guitar String Under Tension:**
> Imagine plucking a guitar string. The pitch (frequency) you hear depends on how tightly the string is stretched. If you increase tension → pitch rises (frequency increases). If you loosen the string → pitch falls (frequency decreases). An **oscillator transducer** works exactly the same way: the measurand (force, pressure, temperature) acts like the "finger tightening/loosening the string," and the output frequency tells us how much the string has been stretched.

In a vibrating-wire force transducer, pulling on the wire increases its tension, raising its resonant frequency. By counting how fast it vibrates, we can deduce the unknown force with exquisite precision.

## 1.3 The Underlying Physical Principle

Every oscillator transducer relies on **one of three energy-domain conversions**:

| Mechanism | Energy Domain Change | Output Domain |
|---|---|---|
| **Parametric Modulation** | Measurand → R, L, or C | Frequency change |
| **Resonant Frequency Shift** | Measurand → Stiffness/Mass of resonator | Frequency change |
| **Piezoelectric Coupling** | Mechanical stress → Crystal deformation | Frequency change |

## 1.4 Classification Tree of Oscillator Transducers

```
Oscillator Transducers
├── 1. RC Oscillator Transducer
│      ├── Resistance-varying type (RDT)
│      └── Capacitance-varying type (CDT)
├── 2. LC Oscillator Transducer
│      ├── Inductance-varying type
│      └── Capacitance-varying type (most common)
├── 3. Mechanical Oscillator Transducer
│      ├── Vibrating-wire type
│      ├── Vibrating-cylinder type
│      └── Tuning-fork type
└── 4. Crystal Oscillator Transducer
       ├── Quartz crystal microbalance (QCM)
       └── SAW (Surface Acoustic Wave) device
```

## 1.5 Typical Frequency Ranges in Practice

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Reference:** Most industrial oscillator transducers operate between **1 kHz** and **100 MHz**. Crystal oscillators typically lie in the **1–50 MHz** band, vibrating-wire types in the **1–5 kHz** band, and LC oscillators in the **100 kHz–10 MHz** band. The choice is dictated by the desired resolution, sensor size, and frequency-counter availability.

# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Operational Architecture — How an Oscillator Transducer Works

Every oscillator transducer consists of **three functional blocks**:

1. **Sensing Element (Frequency-Determining Network):** A passive R, L, or C element whose value changes with the measurand.
2. **Active Amplifier / Sustaining Circuit:** Provides loop gain ≥ 1 and 0° / 360° phase shift to maintain oscillation (Barkhausen criterion).
3. **Output / Counting Stage:** A frequency counter or phase-locked loop (PLL) that translates the frequency into a digital reading.

### 2.1.1 The Barkhausen Criterion (Recall from Network Theory)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For sustained oscillation, the loop gain must be unity and the total phase shift around the loop must be zero (or an integer multiple of 2π):
>
> $$\vert A\beta \vert = 1 \quad \text{and} \quad \angle A\beta = 2n\pi, \quad n = 0, 1, 2, \ldots$$
>
> Any small perturbation of R, L, or C that shifts the resonant condition *changes the operating frequency* — this is precisely the transduction mechanism.

## 2.2 Type-1: RC Oscillator Transducer

### 2.2.1 Operating Principle

In an RC oscillator (Wien-bridge or phase-shift topology), the oscillation frequency is set by an R-C network. If the measurand alters R or C, the frequency shifts accordingly.

### 2.2.2 Frequency Formula

For a **Wien-bridge oscillator**:

$$f_o = \frac{1}{2\pi RC}$$

For a **phase-shift oscillator** (three identical RC sections):

$$f_o = \frac{1}{2\pi RC\sqrt{6}}$$

### 2.2.3 Sensitivity Derivation

Sensitivity is defined as $\displaystyle S = \frac{df_o}{dx}$, where $x$ is the measurand. For $f_o = \frac{1}{2\pi RC}$:

$$\frac{df_o}{dC} = -\frac{1}{2\pi RC^2} = -\frac{f_o}{C}$$

$$\frac{df_o}{dR} = -\frac{1}{2\pi R^2 C} = -\frac{f_o}{R}$$

### 2.2.4 Application Examples

| Measurand | Element Changed | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|
| Humidity | Hygroscopic dielectric → C changes | Humidity sensors |
| Temperature | Thermistor → R changes | Digital thermometers |
| Gas concentration | Gas-sensitive polymer → C changes | Electronic noses |

## 2.3 Type-2: LC Oscillator Transducer

### 2.3.1 Operating Principle

An LC tank circuit (Hartley or Colpitts topology) oscillates at its resonant frequency. The measurand alters L or C, changing the resonant frequency.

### 2.3.2 Frequency Formula

$$f_o = \frac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{LC}}$$

### 2.3.3 Sensitivity

$$S_C = \frac{df_o}{dC} = -\frac{1}{4\pi\sqrt{L}} \cdot C^{-3/2} = -\frac{f_o}{2C}$$

$$S_L = \frac{df_o}{dL} = -\frac{f_o}{2L}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the factor of 1/2?** Because $f_o \propto (LC)^{-1/2}$, and differentiating $C^{-1/2}$ yields $-\frac{1}{2}C^{-3/2}$. This halving shows up everywhere in oscillator-transducer sensitivity calculations.

### 2.3.4 Application Examples

- **Differential-capacitor pressure sensor** in a Colpitts oscillator — diaphragm deflection changes $C_1$ and $C_2$, shifting $f_o$.
- **LVDT with carrier excitation** — the secondary voltage modulates an LC oscillator indirectly.

## 2.4 Type-3: Mechanical Oscillator Transducer

### 2.4.1 Operating Principle — The Vibrating Wire

A taut wire (typically tungsten) is placed in a magnetic field. The measurand (force, pressure, or load) changes the wire's tension $T$, which alters its natural mechanical frequency.

### 2.4.2 Frequency Formula — Vibrating String

The fundamental transverse vibration frequency of a string under tension $T$ is:

$$f_o = \frac{1}{2L}\sqrt{\frac{T}{\rho A}} = \frac{1}{2L}\sqrt{\frac{\sigma}{\rho}}$$

where:
- $L$ = length of wire (m)
- $T$ = tension (N)
- $\rho$ = density of wire material ($\text{kg/m}^3$)
- $A$ = cross-sectional area ($\text{m}^2$)
- $\sigma = T/A$ = tensile stress ($\text{N/m}^2$)

### 2.4.3 Sensitivity to Tension

$$S_T = \frac{df_o}{dT} = \frac{1}{4L\sqrt{\rho A}} \cdot T^{-1/2} = \frac{f_o}{2T}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Square-root dependence:** Notice that $f_o$ varies as $\sqrt{T}$, not linearly. This is a **characteristic nonlinearity** that must be linearized either mathematically (via square-root extraction) or by electronic compensation. This is a classic exam-favourite pitfall!

### 2.4.4 Vibrating-Cylinder Type

A hollow cylinder vibrates in radial modes. Pressure applied externally compresses the cylinder, raising hoop stress and hence frequency. Frequency is approximately:

$$f_o \approx \frac{1}{2\pi R}\sqrt{\frac{E}{\rho}}$$

where $E$ is Young's modulus and $R$ the mean radius. External pressure modifies the effective stiffness.

## 2.5 Type-4: Crystal Oscillator Transducer (QCM / Piezoelectric)

### 2.5.1 Operating Principle

An AT-cut quartz crystal has a sharply defined mechanical resonance at:

$$f_o = \frac{N}{t}\sqrt{\frac{c_{qq}}{\rho_q}}$$

where:
- $N$ = harmonic mode number (1, 3, 5, ...)
- $t$ = crystal thickness (m)
- $c_{qq}$ = elastic stiffness of quartz ($\text{N/m}^2$)
- $\rho_q$ = density of quartz ($\text{kg/m}^3$)

### 2.5.2 Mass-Loading Effect (Sauerbrey Equation)

When a thin film of mass $\Delta m$ is deposited on the crystal face, the frequency *decreases*:

$$\Delta f = -\frac{2f_o^2}{A\sqrt{\rho_q c_{qq}}} \cdot \Delta m$$

This is the **Sauerbrey equation**, central to quartz crystal microbalances used to measure nanogram-level mass changes in chemistry and biosensing.

## 2.6 ⭐ KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA CHEAT SHEET ⭐

| # | Transducer Type | Frequency Formula | Sensitivity $\displaystyle df_o/dx$ | Typical Measurand |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **RC (Wien-bridge)** | $f_o = \dfrac{1}{2\pi RC}$ | $-\dfrac{f_o}{C}$ or $-\dfrac{f_o}{R}$ | Humidity, Temperature |
| 2 | **RC (Phase-shift)** | $f_o = \dfrac{1}{2\pi RC\sqrt{6}}$ | $-\dfrac{f_o}{2C}$ | Same as above |
| 3 | **LC (Hartley/Colpitts)** | $f_o = \dfrac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{LC}}$ | $-\dfrac{f_o}{2C}$ or $-\dfrac{f_o}{2L}$ | Pressure, Displacement |
| 4 | **Vibrating Wire** | $f_o = \dfrac{1}{2L}\sqrt{\dfrac{T}{\rho A}}$ | $+\dfrac{f_o}{2T}$ | Force, Load, Pressure |
| 5 | **Vibrating Cylinder** | $f_o \approx \dfrac{1}{2\pi R}\sqrt{\dfrac{E}{\rho}}$ | Pressure-dependent $E_{\text{eff}}$ | Pressure |
| 6 | **Quartz Crystal (QCM)** | $f_o = \dfrac{N}{t}\sqrt{\dfrac{c_{qq}}{\rho_q}}$ | $\Delta f = -\dfrac{2f_o^2}{A\sqrt{\rho_q c_{qq}}}\Delta m$ | Mass, Thickness, Gas sorption |

> [!NOTE]
> **Quick Mnemonic — "The Square-Root Club":** Every resonant transducer's frequency depends on a square root of a stiffness-to-mass ratio. *LC, vibrating wire, quartz, vibrating cylinder — all obey the same generic rule:* $f_o \propto \sqrt{\dfrac{\text{Stiffness}}{\text{Effective Mass}}}$. Increase stiffness → frequency rises. Increase mass → frequency falls.

## 2.7 Engineering Utility & Real-World Applications

| Industry | Oscillator Transducer Used | Measurand |
|---|---|---|
| Oil & Gas (down-hole) | Vibrating-wire pressure sensor | Formation pressure |
| Civil (structural health) | Vibrating-wire strain gauge | Load in bridges, dams |
| Process control | LC oscillator + capacitive probe | Level, pressure |
| Analytical chemistry | Quartz crystal microbalance | Nanogram mass |
| HVAC | RC humidity oscillator | Relative humidity |
| Aerospace | Crystal oscillator pressure sensor | Altitude, airspeed |
| Automotive | LC torque sensor | Engine torque |

## 2.8 Advantages vs Disadvantages (KTU Frequently Asked)

### Advantages

- **Digital output** → directly compatible with microcontrollers and counters.
- **High noise immunity** → frequency is unaffected by amplitude noise, cable resistance, or stray capacitance (unlike voltage signals).
- **Long-distance transmission** → no degradation over kilometers (twisted pair suffices).
- **High accuracy** → frequency can be measured to 1 part in $10^8$ with crystal time-base.
- **Multiplexing friendly** → many sensors share one cable using FDM (Frequency-Division Multiplexing).

### Disadvantages

- **Square-root nonlinearity** in resonant types → requires linearization.
- **Temperature sensitivity** of $L$, $C$, and crystal frequency → requires compensation.
- **Reference oscillator needed** → adds cost and complexity.
- **Limited dynamic range** in some topologies (e.g., Wien-bridge saturates for large $R$ or $C$ variation).

# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Frequency Sensitivity of an RC Wien-Bridge Oscillator

### Problem Setup

A Wien-bridge oscillator transducer uses a capacitive humidity sensor with nominal capacitance $C_0 = 470$ pF and a fixed resistor $R = 10\text{ k}\Omega$. The capacitance changes by $\Delta C = +5$ pF due to humidity. Determine the percentage change in output frequency.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Write the Wien-bridge frequency formula.**

$$f_o = \frac{1}{2\pi RC_0}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute numerical values to find the nominal frequency.**

$$f_{o,\text{nom}} = \frac{1}{2\pi \times (10 \times 10^3) \times (470 \times 10^{-12})}$$

$$f_{o,\text{nom}} = \frac{1}{2\pi \times 4.7 \times 10^{-6}}$$

$$f_{o,\text{nom}} = \frac{1}{2.9531 \times 10^{-5}}$$

$$f_{o,\text{nom}} \approx 33{,}863 \text{ Hz} \approx 33.86 \text{ kHz}$$

**Step 3 — Write the frequency with the changed capacitance.**

$$f_{o,\text{new}} = \frac{1}{2\pi R(C_0 + \Delta C)} = \frac{1}{2\pi R \cdot 475\text{ pF}}$$

$$f_{o,\text{new}} = \frac{1}{2\pi \times 10^4 \times 4.75 \times 10^{-10}}$$

$$f_{o,\text{new}} = \frac{1}{2.9845 \times 10^{-5}}$$

$$f_{o,\text{new}} \approx 33{,}506 \text{ Hz} \approx 33.51 \text{ kHz}$$

**Step 4 — Compute the absolute change.**

$$\Delta f_o = f_{o,\text{new}} - f_{o,\text{nom}} = 33{,}506 - 33{,}863 = -357 \text{ Hz}$$

**Step 5 — Compute the percentage change.**

$$\frac{\Delta f_o}{f_{o,\text{nom}}} \times 100\% = \frac{-357}{33{,}863} \times 100\% \approx -1.054\%$$

**Step 6 — Cross-check using the sensitivity formula.**

Using $\dfrac{df_o}{dC} = -\dfrac{f_o}{C}$:

$$\Delta f_o \approx -\frac{f_o}{C_0} \cdot \Delta C = -\frac{33{,}863}{470 \times 10^{-12}} \times 5 \times 10^{-12}$$

$$\Delta f_o = -\frac{33{,}863 \times 5}{470} \approx -360.2 \text{ Hz}$$

The small discrepancy (357 vs 360) is the linearization error of the differential approximation. **For exam purposes, both are acceptable**, but the *exact ratio* method is preferred when $\Delta C / C_0$ exceeds 1 %.

$$\boxed{\frac{\Delta f_o}{f_{o,\text{nom}}} \approx -1.05\%}$$

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Tension Measurement Using a Vibrating-Wire Transducer

### Problem Setup

A vibrating-wire load cell uses a tungsten wire of length $L = 50$ mm, diameter $d = 0.25$ mm, and density $\rho = 19{,}300 \text{ kg/m}^3$. With no load, the wire tension is $T_0 = 5$ N. A load increases the tension to $T_1 = 8$ N. Compute the initial and final frequencies.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Compute the cross-sectional area.**

$$A = \frac{\pi d^2}{4} = \frac{\pi \times (0.25 \times 10^{-3})^2}{4}$$

$$A = \frac{\pi \times 6.25 \times 10^{-8}}{4} = 4.909 \times 10^{-8} \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 2 — Compute the initial frequency $f_1$ at $T_0 = 5$ N.**

$$f_1 = \frac{1}{2L}\sqrt{\frac{T_0}{\rho A}}$$

$$f_1 = \frac{1}{2 \times 50 \times 10^{-3}} \sqrt{\frac{5}{19{,}300 \times 4.909 \times 10^{-8}}}$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate the inner radical.**

$$\frac{T_0}{\rho A} = \frac{5}{19{,}300 \times 4.909 \times 10^{-8}} = \frac{5}{9.474 \times 10^{-4}}$$

$$\frac{T_0}{\rho A} = 5277.5 \text{ m}^2/\text{s}^2$$

**Step 4 — Take the square root.**

$$\sqrt{5277.5} = 72.65 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 5 — Divide by $2L$.**

$$f_1 = \frac{72.65}{2 \times 0.050} = \frac{72.65}{0.100} = 726.5 \text{ Hz}$$

**Step 6 — Compute the new frequency $f_2$ at $T_1 = 8$ N.**

$$\frac{T_1}{\rho A} = \frac{8}{9.474 \times 10^{-4}} = 8444.0 \text{ m}^2/\text{s}^2$$

$$\sqrt{8444.0} = 91.89 \text{ m/s}$$

$$f_2 = \frac{91.89}{0.100} = 918.9 \text{ Hz}$$

**Step 7 — Compute the frequency shift.**

$$\Delta f = f_2 - f_1 = 918.9 - 726.5 = 192.4 \text{ Hz}$$

**Step 8 — Verify using the sensitivity formula $S_T = f_o / (2T)$.**

At nominal tension $T_0 = 5$ N:

$$S_T = \frac{726.5}{2 \times 5} = 72.65 \text{ Hz/N}$$

$$\Delta f \approx S_T \cdot \Delta T = 72.65 \times (8 - 5) = 217.9 \text{ Hz}$$

The linearized estimate (217.9 Hz) differs from the exact (192.4 Hz) by about 13 %, which is **expected because of the square-root law** — small changes must be treated exactly when high accuracy is required.

$$\boxed{f_1 = 726.5 \text{ Hz}, \quad f_2 = 918.9 \text{ Hz}, \quad \Delta f = 192.4 \text{ Hz}}$$

## 3.3 Derivation 3: QCM Mass Loading — Sauerbrey Equation

### Problem Setup

A 5 MHz AT-cut quartz crystal microbalance (QCM) has electrode area $A = 0.196 \text{ cm}^2$. After deposition of a thin film, the frequency shifts by $\Delta f = -200 \text{ Hz}$. Compute the deposited mass. Use standard QCM constants: for AT-cut quartz, $\rho_q = 2648 \text{ kg/m}^3$ and $\mu_q = c_{qq}\rho_q = 2.947 \times 10^{10} \text{ kg/(m·s}^2)$.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Recall the Sauerbrey equation.**

$$\Delta f = -\frac{2f_o^2}{A\sqrt{\rho_q \mu_q}} \cdot \Delta m$$

**Step 2 — Solve for $\Delta m$.**

$$\Delta m = -\frac{A\sqrt{\rho_q \mu_q}}{2f_o^2} \cdot \Delta f$$

**Step 3 — Compute $\sqrt{\rho_q \mu_q}$.**

$$\rho_q \mu_q = 2648 \times 2.947 \times 10^{10} = 7.804 \times 10^{13}$$

$$\sqrt{\rho_q \mu_q} = \sqrt{7.804 \times 10^{13}} = 8.834 \times 10^{6} \text{ kg/(m}^2 \cdot \text{s)}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute numerical values.**

- $A = 0.196 \text{ cm}^2 = 0.196 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2 = 1.96 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^2$
- $f_o = 5 \times 10^6 \text{ Hz}$

$$\Delta m = -\frac{(1.96 \times 10^{-5}) \times (8.834 \times 10^6)}{2 \times (5 \times 10^6)^2} \times (-200)$$

**Step 5 — Compute the numerator.**

$$1.96 \times 10^{-5} \times 8.834 \times 10^6 = 1.96 \times 8.834 \times 10^{1} = 173.15$$

**Step 6 — Compute the denominator.**

$$2 \times 25 \times 10^{12} = 5 \times 10^{13}$$

**Step 7 — Combine.**

$$\Delta m = -\frac{173.15}{5 \times 10^{13}} \times (-200)$$

$$\Delta m = \frac{173.15 \times 200}{5 \times 10^{13}} = \frac{34{,}630}{5 \times 10^{13}}$$

$$\Delta m = 6.926 \times 10^{-10} \text{ kg} \approx 693 \text{ ng}$$

$$\boxed{\Delta m \approx 6.93 \times 10^{-10} \text{ kg} = 693 \text{ ng}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** This astonishing sensitivity — detecting less than a microgram of mass — is why QCMs are used in atmospheric pollution monitors, biosensors, and thin-film deposition controllers.

## 3.4 Derivation 4: LC Oscillator with Differential Capacitive Pressure Sensor

### Problem Setup

A pressure sensor uses a differential capacitor $C_1 = C_0 + \Delta C$ and $C_2 = C_0 - \Delta C$ inside a Colpitts oscillator. Derive the expression for frequency change in terms of $\Delta C$.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Identify the effective Colpitts tank capacitance.**

For a Colpitts oscillator with two capacitors in series:

$$C_{\text{eff}} = \frac{C_1 C_2}{C_1 + C_2}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the differential values.**

$$C_1 C_2 = (C_0 + \Delta C)(C_0 - \Delta C) = C_0^2 - \Delta C^2$$

$$C_1 + C_2 = 2C_0$$

Therefore:

$$C_{\text{eff}} = \frac{C_0^2 - \Delta C^2}{2C_0} = \frac{C_0}{2}\left(1 - \left(\frac{\Delta C}{C_0}\right)^2\right)$$

**Step 3 — Write the oscillation frequency.**

$$f_o = \frac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{L \, C_{\text{eff}}}}$$

**Step 4 — Compare with the balanced (no-pressure) frequency.**

At $\Delta C = 0$:

$$f_{o,0} = \frac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{L \cdot C_0/2}} = \frac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\frac{2}{LC_0}}$$

**Step 5 — Express the frequency ratio.**

$$\frac{f_o}{f_{o,0}} = \sqrt{\frac{C_0/2}{C_{\text{eff}}}} = \sqrt{\frac{C_0/2}{(C_0/2)(1 - (\Delta C/C_0)^2)}}$$

$$\frac{f_o}{f_{o,0}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{1 - (\Delta C/C_0)^2}}$$

**Step 6 — Series expand for small $\Delta C/C_0$ (Taylor series).**

Using $\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{1-x}} \approx 1 + \dfrac{x}{2} + \dfrac{3x^2}{8} + \ldots$ with $x = (\Delta C/C_0)^2$:

$$\frac{f_o}{f_{o,0}} \approx 1 + \frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{\Delta C}{C_0}\right)^2 + \frac{3}{8}\left(\frac{\Delta C}{C_0}\right)^4 + \ldots$$

**Step 7 — Linearize (small signal).**

$$\Delta f_o = f_o - f_{o,0} \approx f_{o,0} \cdot \frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{\Delta C}{C_0}\right)^2$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Crucial Pitfall:** Notice that the frequency change is **second-order** in $\Delta C$! This is *why differential capacitive oscillators are nearly insensitive to common-mode pressure variations* — a powerful feature for noise rejection in industrial pressure transmitters.

$$\boxed{\frac{\Delta f_o}{f_{o,0}} \approx \frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{\Delta C}{C_0}\right)^2 \text{ (for small signals)}}$$

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Choosing the Right Oscillator Transducer

| Design Goal | Best Choice | Why |
|---|---|---|
| Highest mass sensitivity | **QCM (Crystal)** | Sauerbrey: ng-level mass shifts kHz-scale $f$ |
| Highest force/load accuracy | **Vibrating Wire** | Pure mechanical resonance, $\sigma$ is very stable |
| Lowest cost | **RC Oscillator** | Few components, IC-compatible |
| Best common-mode rejection | **Differential LC** | $\Delta f \propto (\Delta C/C_0)^2$ |
| High-temperature operation | **Vibrating-wire (tungsten)** | Tungsten stable up to 2000 °C |
| Smallest physical size | **SAW / Crystal** | mm-scale resonators |
| Multiplexing on one cable | **Any with FDM** | Each sensor assigned a different frequency band |

```mermaid
    CREF[CAPACITOR C1<br>Fixed 470 pF]
    WB[WIEN BRIDGE<br>NETWORK<br>Series and Parallel]
    OP[OP AMP<br>Non inverting input]
    OUT[SINE WAVE<br>OUTPUT at f out]
    CT[FREQUENCY<br>COUNTER]
    DSP[DIGITAL DISPLAY<br>RH percentage]

    H --> HS
    HS --> CV
    CV --> WB
    RB --> WB
    RA --> WB
    CREF --> WB
    WB --> OP
    OP --> OUT
    OUT --> CT
    CT --> DSP

    classDef humidStyle fill:#AFEEEE,stroke:#008B8B,color:#000000
    classDef circuitStyle fill:#FFFACD,stroke:#B8860B,color:#000000
    classDef outputStyle fill:#E6E6FA,stroke:#4B0082,color:#FFFFFF

    class H,HS,CV humidStyle
    class RB,RA,CREF,WB,OP circuitStyle
    class OUT,CT,DSP outputStyle
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** The hygroscopic dielectric absorbs water vapour, changing its permittivity $\varepsilon_r$ and therefore $C_x$. This shifts the Wien-bridge null frequency. The op-amp sustains oscillation exactly at the new frequency, and a counter decodes the humidity.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1
**`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`**
**Define an oscillator transducer. Mention two advantages of using frequency as the output variable.**

**Model Answer:**

An **oscillator transducer** is an active transducer that converts a physical measurand into a proportional change in the **frequency** of an oscillating electrical signal. The measurand alters one of the frequency-determining parameters (R, L, or C) of an oscillator circuit.

**Two advantages of frequency output:**
1. **High noise immunity:** Amplitude noise and cable attenuation do not affect the frequency measurement.
2. **Long-distance transmission without degradation:** A frequency signal can travel over twisted pairs for hundreds of metres without loss of accuracy, and it is naturally compatible with digital counters.

*(Any two of: digital output, easy multiplexing, high accuracy, time-base referencing possible)*

---

### Question A2
**`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`**
**State the Sauerbrey equation for a quartz crystal microbalance and explain the significance of the negative sign.**

**Model Answer:**

$$\Delta f = -\frac{2 f_o^2}{A \sqrt{\rho_q \mu_q}} \cdot \Delta m$$

where $f_o$ is the resonant frequency, $A$ is the electrode area, $\rho_q$ is the density of quartz, and $\mu_q = \rho_q c_{qq}$ is the shear modulus of quartz.

**Significance of the negative sign:** Mass loading *reduces* the resonant frequency. Adding mass to the crystal face increases the effective inertia of the vibrating system, lowering its natural frequency — exactly as adding a small mass to a tuning fork lowers its pitch.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Always state units** in the final answer — examiners routinely deduct 0.5 mark for missing units.
> 2. **For square-root-law transducers** (vibrating wire, LC), do *not* use linear approximations blindly; show both exact and linearized forms when $\Delta x/x_0 > 5\%$.
> 3. **Common-mode vs differential** confusion is a frequent error in Colpitts/Hartley oscillator-transducer problems. Read the question carefully.
> 4. Always draw or describe the circuit diagram — full marks require it.

---

### ⭐ QUESTION A — 14 Marks (CHOICE 1)

**`[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 4]`**

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the working principle of a **vibrating-wire transducer** for force measurement. Derive the expression for its natural frequency. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A vibrating-wire force transducer has the following specifications:
- Wire material: Tungsten, $\rho = 19{,}300 \text{ kg/m}^3$
- Length $L = 40$ mm, diameter $d = 0.2$ mm
- Initial tension $T_1 = 4$ N (zero-load)
- Final tension $T_2 = 9$ N (full-scale load)

Calculate (i) the initial frequency, (ii) the full-scale frequency, and (iii) the frequency sensitivity in Hz/N at mid-scale. **(7 marks)**

---

#### 📝 Model Solution to Question A

### Part (a) — Working Principle and Derivation (7 Marks)

**Block Diagram (Verbal Description for Solution Sheet):**

The vibrating-wire transducer consists of:

1. A **tungsten wire** clamped between two end fixtures.
2. A **permanent magnet** placed perpendicular to the wire, providing a steady magnetic field.
3. An **excitation coil** that sends a current pulse through the wire.
4. A **pick-up coil** that senses the EMF induced when the wire vibrates in the magnetic field.
5. A **force-application mechanism** (e.g., a diaphragm or load button) that pulls the wire, increasing its tension.

**Working Sequence:**
1. A short current pulse is passed through the wire from the excitation coil.
2. The interaction between the current and the magnetic field produces a transverse force, plucking the wire.
3. The wire vibrates at its natural transverse frequency.
4. As it vibrates in the magnetic field, an alternating EMF is induced in the pick-up coil.
5. This EMF is amplified and used to sustain oscillation (the wire is part of a positive-feedback loop).
6. When an external force is applied, the wire tension $T$ changes, altering the natural frequency $f_o$.
7. The frequency is counted and converted to force.

**Derivation:**

Consider a uniform wire of length $L$, mass per unit length $m = \rho A$, under tension $T$. The wave equation for transverse displacement $y(x,t)$ is:

$$\frac{\partial^2 y}{\partial t^2} = \frac{T}{m} \cdot \frac{\partial^2 y}{\partial x^2}$$

Assume separation of variables $y(x,t) = Y(x)\cos(\omega t)$. Substituting:

$$-\omega^2 Y = \frac{T}{m} Y''$$

This is a Sturm–Liouville problem with solution:

$$Y_n(x) = A_n \sin\left(\frac{n\pi x}{L}\right)$$

Applying the boundary conditions $Y(0) = Y(L) = 0$ (fixed ends):

$$\omega_n = \frac{n\pi}{L}\sqrt{\frac{T}{m}}$$

The fundamental mode ($n = 1$) gives:

$$\omega_o = \frac{\pi}{L}\sqrt{\frac{T}{\rho A}} \quad \Rightarrow \quad f_o = \frac{\omega_o}{2\pi} = \frac{1}{2L}\sqrt{\frac{T}{\rho A}}$$

$$\boxed{f_o = \frac{1}{2L}\sqrt{\frac{T}{\rho A}}}$$

---

### Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 Marks)

**Given:**
- $\rho = 19{,}300 \text{ kg/m}^3$
- $L = 40 \text{ mm} = 0.040 \text{ m}$
- $d = 0.2 \text{ mm} = 2 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}$
- $T_1 = 4$ N, $T_2 = 9$ N

**Step 1 — Compute cross-sectional area.**

$$A = \frac{\pi d^2}{4} = \frac{\pi \times (2 \times 10^{-4})^2}{4} = \frac{\pi \times 4 \times 10^{-8}}{4} = \pi \times 10^{-8}$$

$$A = 3.1416 \times 10^{-8} \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 2 — Compute $\rho A$.**

$$\rho A = 19{,}300 \times 3.1416 \times 10^{-8} = 6.063 \times 10^{-4} \text{ kg/m}$$

**Step 3 — Compute initial frequency at $T_1 = 4$ N.**

$$f_{o1} = \frac{1}{2L}\sqrt{\frac{T_1}{\rho A}} = \frac{1}{0.080}\sqrt{\frac{4}{6.063 \times 10^{-4}}}$$

$$f_{o1} = 12.5 \times \sqrt{6597.6}$$

$$f_{o1} = 12.5 \times 81.23 = 1015.4 \text{ Hz}$$

**Step 4 — Compute full-scale frequency at $T_2 = 9$ N.**

$$f_{o2} = \frac{1}{0.080}\sqrt{\frac{9}{6.063 \times 10^{-4}}}$$

$$f_{o2} = 12.5 \times \sqrt{14{,}844.6}$$

$$f_{o2} = 12.5 \times 121.84 = 1523.0 \text{ Hz}$$

**Step 5 — Compute mid-scale tension.**

$$T_{\text{mid}} = \frac{T_1 + T_2}{2} = \frac{4 + 9}{2} = 6.5 \text{ N}$$

**Step 6 — Compute mid-scale frequency.**

$$f_{o,\text{mid}} = 12.5 \times \sqrt{\frac{6.5}{6.063 \times 10^{-4}}} = 12.5 \times \sqrt{10{,}721.4}$$

$$f_{o,\text{mid}} = 12.5 \times 103.54 = 1294.3 \text{ Hz}$$

**Step 7 — Compute mid-scale sensitivity.**

$$S_T = \frac{f_{o,\text{mid}}}{2 \, T_{\text{mid}}} = \frac{1294.3}{2 \times 6.5} = \frac{1294.3}{13.0}$$

$$\boxed{S_T \approx 99.6 \text{ Hz/N at mid-scale}}$$

**Final Answers:**

| Quantity | Value |
|---|---|
| Initial frequency $f_{o1}$ | **1015.4 Hz** |
| Full-scale frequency $f_{o2}$ | **1523.0 Hz** |
| Frequency span $\Delta f$ | **507.6 Hz** |
| Mid-scale sensitivity | **99.6 Hz/N** |

---

### ⭐ QUESTION B — 14 Marks (CHOICE 2 / ALTERNATIVE)

**`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024, Module 4]`**

**(a)** Describe with a circuit diagram, the operation of a **Wien-bridge oscillator used as a humidity transducer**. Derive the expression for the oscillation frequency. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A Wien-bridge humidity transducer uses a capacitive sensor whose capacitance varies as $C = 470\,(1 + 0.01\,\text{RH})$ pF, where RH is the relative humidity in percent. The fixed resistor is $R = 10 \text{ k}\Omega$. Determine the frequency at RH = 0 %, RH = 50 %, and RH = 100 %. Also calculate the sensitivity in Hz/(%RH) at RH = 50 %. **(7 marks)**

---

#### 📝 Model Solution to Question B

### Part (a) — Wien-Bridge Humidity Transducer (7 Marks)

**Circuit Diagram (Verbal Description):**

The Wien-bridge oscillator consists of:
- A **series RC arm** ($R$ and $C_x$ in series, where $C_x$ is the humidity-dependent capacitor).
- A **parallel RC arm** ($R$ and $C$ in parallel).
- These two arms form a frequency-selective feedback network.
- An **op-amp** with non-inverting input connected to the bridge mid-point and the inverting input providing gain control via two resistors $R_f$ and $R_1$.
- The op-amp output is fed back to the bridge input.

**Operating Principle:**

1. The bridge provides zero phase shift at only one frequency — the oscillation frequency.
2. At this frequency, the positive feedback exactly equals the negative feedback, satisfying the Barkhausen criterion.
3. The capacitive humidity sensor (typically a hygroscopic polymer dielectric between two electrodes) absorbs water vapour as humidity rises.
4. This increases the dielectric constant $\varepsilon_r$ of the sensor, raising $C_x$.
5. Since $f_o \propto 1/C_x$, the oscillation frequency decreases as humidity increases.
6. The frequency is counted by a digital counter and converted to RH.

**Derivation of Oscillation Frequency:**

The transfer function of the series arm is:

$$Z_s = R + \frac{1}{j\omega C_x}$$

The transfer function of the parallel arm is:

$$Z_p = \frac{R \cdot \dfrac{1}{j\omega C}}{R + \dfrac{1}{j\omega C}} = \frac{R}{1 + j\omega RC}$$

For zero phase shift (oscillation condition), the bridge output $\beta$ must be purely real:

$$\beta = \frac{Z_p}{Z_s + Z_p}$$

Setting the imaginary part of $\beta$ to zero, after algebraic manipulation:

$$\omega RC_x = 1 \quad \text{and} \quad \omega RC = 1$$

For both conditions to hold simultaneously in the standard symmetric Wien bridge (taking $C_x = C$):

$$\omega_o = \frac{1}{RC_x} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \boxed{f_o = \frac{1}{2\pi R C_x}}$$

The amplitude condition requires the non-inverting gain to be exactly 3:

$$A = 1 + \frac{R_f}{R_1} = 3 \quad \Rightarrow \quad R_f = 2R_1$$

In practice, an automatic gain control (AGC) circuit or a non-linear element (lamp, JFET, or diode) replaces $R_f$ to stabilize amplitude.

---

### Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 Marks)

**Given:**
- $C = 470(1 + 0.01\,\text{RH})$ pF
- $R = 10 \text{ k}\Omega$

**Step 1 — Frequency at RH = 0 %.**

$$C_0 = 470(1 + 0) = 470 \text{ pF} = 470 \times 10^{-12} \text{ F}$$

$$f_{o}(0\%) = \frac{1}{2\pi \times 10^4 \times 470 \times 10^{-12}} = \frac{1}{2\pi \times 4.7 \times 10^{-6}}$$

$$f_{o}(0\%) = \frac{1}{2.953 \times 10^{-5}} = 33{,}864 \text{ Hz} \approx 33.86 \text{ kHz}$$

**Step 2 — Frequency at RH = 50 %.**

$$C_{50} = 470(1 + 0.01 \times 50) = 470(1.50) = 705 \text{ pF}$$

$$f_{o}(50\%) = \frac{1}{2\pi \times 10^4 \times 705 \times 10^{-12}} = \frac{1}{2\pi \times 7.05 \times 10^{-6}}$$

$$f_{o}(50\%) = \frac{1}{4.429 \times 10^{-5}} = 22{,}578 \text{ Hz} \approx 22.58 \text{ kHz}$$

**Step 3 — Frequency at RH = 100 %.**

$$C_{100} = 470(1 + 0.01 \times 100) = 470 \times 2 = 940 \text{ pF}$$

$$f_{o}(100\%) = \frac{1}{2\pi \times 10^4 \times 940 \times 10^{-12}} = \frac{1}{2\pi \times 9.4 \times 10^{-6}}$$

$$f_{o}(100\%) = \frac{1}{5.906 \times 10^{-5}} = 16{,}932 \text{ Hz} \approx 16.93 \text{ kHz}$$

**Step 4 — Sensitivity at RH = 50 %.**

Differentiate $f_o = \dfrac{1}{2\pi R \cdot 470(1 + 0.01\,\text{RH}) \times 10^{-12}}$ with respect to RH:

$$\frac{df_o}{d(\text{RH})} = -\frac{1}{2\pi R} \cdot \frac{0.01 \times 470 \times 10^{-12}}{\left[470(1 + 0.01\,\text{RH}) \times 10^{-12}\right]^2}$$

At RH = 50 %:

$$\frac{df_o}{d(\text{RH})}\bigg|_{50\%} = -\frac{f_o(50\%)}{C_{50}} \cdot \frac{dC_{50}}{d(\text{RH})}$$

$$\frac{dC}{d(\text{RH})} = 470 \times 0.01 = 4.7 \text{ pF}/(\% \text{RH})$$

$$\frac{df_o}{d(\text{RH})}\bigg|_{50\%} = -\frac{22{,}578}{705 \times 10^{-12}} \times (4.7 \times 10^{-12})$$

$$= -\frac{22{,}578 \times 4.7}{705} = -\frac{106{,}117}{705} \approx -150.5 \text{ Hz}/(\%\text{RH})$$

$$\boxed{S_{\text{RH}}\big|_{50\%} \approx -150.5 \text{ Hz}/(\% \text{RH})}$$

**Summary Table:**

| RH (%) | $C$ (pF) | $f_o$ (Hz) |
|---|---|---|
| 0 | 470 | **33,864** |
| 50 | 705 | **22,578** |
| 100 | 940 | **16,932** |

**Frequency span:** $33{,}864 - 16{,}932 = 16{,}932$ Hz over 0–100 % RH.

---

## 5.3 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Oscillator Transducers (Module 4)**

- ✅ **Core idea:** Measurand → change in R, L, C, tension, or mass → change in oscillation frequency $f_o$.
- ✅ **Four families:** RC, LC, Mechanical (vibrating wire/cylinder), Crystal (QCM).
- ✅ **Master formula for RC:** $\displaystyle f_o = \frac{1}{2\pi RC}$ (Wien-bridge).
- ✅ **Master formula for LC:** $\displaystyle f_o = \frac{1}{2\pi \sqrt{LC}}$ (Colpitts / Hartley).
- ✅ **Master formula for vibrating wire:** $\displaystyle f_o = \frac{1}{2L}\sqrt{\frac{T}{\rho A}}$.
- ✅ **Master formula for QCM:** $\displaystyle f_o = \frac{N}{t}\sqrt{\frac{c_{qq}}{\rho_q}}$; mass shift: $\displaystyle \Delta f = -\frac{2 f_o^2}{A\sqrt{\rho_q \mu_q}} \Delta m$.
- ✅ **Sensitivity generic rule:** $S_x = \dfrac{df_o}{dx} = \dfrac{k \cdot f_o}{x}$, where $k$ depends on the power-law exponent (e.g., $k = -1$ for RC, $k = -1/2$ for LC).
- ✅ **Square-root nonlinearity** is the single most important source of error in resonant transducers — always check if linearization is valid.
- ✅ **Differential capacitive Colpitts:** $\Delta f_o \propto (\Delta C/C_0)^2$ — common-mode rejection.
- ✅ **Barkhausen criterion:** $|A\beta| = 1$, phase = $2n\pi$ — must hold for sustained oscillation.
- ✅ **Advantages:** Digital output, noise immunity, long-distance transmission, multiplexing.
- ✅ **Disadvantages:** Square-root nonlinearity, temperature drift, reference oscillator required.
- ✅ **QCM sensitivity:** Sub-nanogram mass changes are routinely measurable — used in biosensors, pollution monitors, thin-film deposition.
- ✅ **Industrial favourite:** Vibrating-wire load cells are the gold standard for civil-structural and down-hole pressure monitoring due to extreme long-term stability.
- ✅ **Units check:** Always write Hz, N, m, kg/m³ in the final answer. Marks deducted for missing units.
- ✅ **Formula-derivation:** Examiners award full marks only when both the working principle *and* the formula are derived step-by-step — never write a formula without justification.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Flow Transducer - Orifice Plate, Venturi, Defective Type Flow Sensor, Spin Type Flow Sensor, Electromagnetic Flow Sensor
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBRT303 - SENSORS AND ACTUATORS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Flow Transducer - Orifice Plate, Venturi, Defective Type Flow Sensor, Spin Type Flow Sensor, Electromagnetic Flow Sensor

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Introduction to Flow Transducers

A **Flow Transducer** (or flow meter) is an instrument used to measure the volumetric or mass flow rate of a fluid (liquid or gas) flowing through a pipe or open channel. In industrial automation, flow measurement is critical for process control, billing, safety, and efficiency monitoring.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** Flow transducers are devices that convert fluid flow rate information into a measurable electrical or pneumatic signal. They are classified based on the physical principle employed: differential pressure, velocity, mass, positive displacement, and electromagnetic methods.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Garden Hose Test

Imagine you press your thumb over the end of a garden hose. What happens?
- The water shoots out **faster** (velocity increases)
- The pressure on the **upstream** side of your thumb rises
- The pressure on the **downstream** side (the nozzle) drops

This is exactly how an **orifice plate works** — it creates a deliberate constriction in the pipe, and by measuring the pressure difference across the constriction, we can deduce the flow rate. Similarly, a **Venturi tube** does the same thing but with a smoother, gradual contraction, like the nozzle of a rocket engine. A **magnetic flow meter** works on a different principle — like a generator where the fluid itself acts as the moving conductor cutting through magnetic field lines.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why measure flow?** Because in almost every process industry (refineries, water treatment plants, chemical plants, power plants, food & beverage), knowing *how much* fluid moves per unit time is the basis of revenue, quality, and safety.

## 1.3 Classification of Flow Transducers

| Category | Operating Principle | Example |
|----------|--------------------|---------| 
| Differential Pressure (Obstruction-type) | Bernoulli's equation | Orifice Plate, Venturi, Flow Nozzle |
| Deflection (Variable Area) | Force on a body | Target/Impact meter, Rotameter |
| Velocity-based (Spin/Turbine) | Rotational speed proportional to flow | Turbine flow meter, Spin meter |
| Electromagnetic | Faraday's Law of Induction | Magmeter |
| Ultrasonic | Doppler shift / Transit time | Ultrasonic flow meter |
| Coriolis | Coriolis force on vibrating tubes | Coriolis mass flow meter |
| Vortex | Von Karman vortex shedding | Vortex shedding meter |

> [!NOTE]
> This module focuses on the **five** types: **Orifice Plate, Venturi, Deflection (Defective) Type, Spin Type, and Electromagnetic Flow Sensor**.

## 1.4 Quick Glossary of Key Terms

- **Reynolds Number (Re):** $\text{Re} = \dfrac{\rho v D}{\mu}$ — Dimensionless number indicating flow regime (laminar vs. turbulent).
- **Discharge Coefficient ($C_d$):** Ratio of actual to theoretical flow, accounting for energy losses and vena contracta. Typically **0.6 to 0.65** for orifice plates.
- **Vena Contracta:** The point just downstream of the orifice where the flow cross-section is minimum and the velocity is maximum.
- **Beta Ratio ($\beta$):** $\beta = \dfrac{d}{D}$ — Ratio of orifice (or throat) diameter to pipe diameter.
- **Permanent Pressure Loss:** Unrecoverable pressure drop downstream of the device due to friction and turbulence.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Orifice Plate

### 2.1.1 Construction

An **orifice plate** is the simplest and most widely used differential pressure flow meter. It consists of a thin, flat circular plate (usually stainless steel) with a precisely machined concentric hole (orifice) clamped between two pipe flanges.

- **Concentric Orifice:** Most common, used for clean liquids/gases.
- **Eccentric Orifice:** Hole offset to bottom — for fluids with suspended solids.
- **Segmental Orifice:** Partial circle — for slurry flows.
- **Quadrant Edge Orifice:** Used for viscous / low Reynolds number flow (Re < 10,000).

### 2.1.2 Working Principle

When fluid flows through the pipe, the orifice constriction forces the fluid to accelerate through the smaller opening, then expand again downstream. According to **Bernoulli's principle**, this velocity increase must be accompanied by a pressure drop. The pressure difference is measured using a **differential pressure transmitter** connected to pressure tappings.

**Pressure Tappings** (locations where pressure is measured):
- **Flange Taps:** 1 inch upstream and downstream of the orifice plate.
- **D and D/2 Taps (Corner taps):** At the orifice plate face itself.
- **Vena Contracta Taps:** At the vena contracta location (gives the highest ΔP).

### 2.1.3 Why the Orifice Plate Creates Measurable Signal

- Fluid velocity increases through the orifice → **kinetic energy up, pressure energy down**
- Pressure recovers partially downstream but **not fully** (some energy is lost to turbulence)
- The difference between upstream and downstream pressure → proportional to (flow rate)²
- This quadratic relationship is a defining characteristic of all DP flow meters

## 2.2 Venturi Tube

### 2.2.1 Construction

A **Venturi tube** is a converging-diverging pipe section, invented by **Giovanni Venturi** in 1797. It has three sections:

1. **Inlet (Converging) Section:** Smoothly contracts to a throat, angle of convergence ≈ 20°.
2. **Throat:** A short parallel section of minimum cross-section where flow is most uniform.
3. **Outlet (Diverging) Section:** Gradually expands back to pipe diameter, angle ≈ 5–7° to prevent flow separation.

### 2.2.2 Working Principle

Similar to orifice plate, but with **gradual contractions and expansions** that minimize turbulence and energy loss. The pressure drop is measured between the upstream section and the throat. The Venturi is more accurate than an orifice plate and has much **lower permanent pressure loss** (typically only **10–15%** of the differential pressure, compared to **40–60%** for an orifice plate).

### 2.2.3 Advantages over Orifice Plate
- Higher accuracy (Cd closer to 0.98)
- Lower permanent pressure loss → **less pumping cost**
- Handles slurries and dirty fluids (no sharp edges to clog)
- Self-cleaning due to high velocity at throat

### 2.2.4 Disadvantages
- Large physical size (length ≈ 5–8 times pipe diameter)
- Expensive to manufacture and install
- Heavy

## 2.3 Deflection Type Flow Sensor

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note on Terminology:** The term "Defective Type" in the KTU syllabus appears to be a typographical rendering of "**Deflection Type**" flow sensor. This class includes the **Target (Impact) Type Flow Meter** and the **Variable Area (Rotameter / Float) Flow Meter**, both of which operate by measuring the physical deflection or displacement of a body inserted in the flow.

### 2.3.1 Target (Impact / Annular) Type Flow Meter

**Construction:** A circular disc (the "target") is suspended centrally in the pipe by a rigid bar attached to a force-measuring transducer (strain gauge or force balance) on the opposite wall.

**Working Principle:** When fluid strikes the target, it exerts a **drag force** $F_D$ given by:

$$F_D = C_D \cdot \frac{\rho v^2}{2} \cdot A_t$$

where $A_t$ is the target area and $C_D$ is the drag coefficient. The force is proportional to **v²** (square of velocity), and hence to the **square of flow rate**.

**Applications:** Used for dirty fluids, slurries, and applications where insertion-type flow measurement is preferred (steam, pulp, chemicals).

### 2.3.2 Rotameter (Variable Area Flow Meter)

**Construction:** A **tapered tube** (larger at top, smaller at bottom) made of glass, metal, or plastic, with a **float** inside. The float rises to a height where the upward drag force balances the float's weight minus buoyancy.

**Working Principle:** As flow increases, the float rises higher, exposing more annular area. The equilibrium height is a direct visual or magnetic indication of flow.

**Advantages:** Simple, no external power needed, linear scale, low cost.
**Disadvantages:** Must be installed vertically; accuracy ±2–5%; not suitable for opaque or very high-pressure fluids.

## 2.4 Spin Type Flow Sensor (Turbine Flow Meter)

### 2.4.1 Construction

A **multi-bladed rotor (turbine)** is mounted coaxially inside the pipe on a shaft supported by bearings. A **magnetic pickup coil** (or reluctance pickup) is mounted externally, sensing the passage of each blade tip.

### 2.4.2 Working Principle

The flowing fluid impinges on the angled blades, causing the rotor to spin. The **rotational speed N is directly proportional to the fluid velocity v**:

$$N = K \cdot v$$

where $K$ is the meter's K-factor (pulses per unit volume, e.g., pulses/liter). The pickup coil produces a **pulse train**; frequency is measured and converted to flow rate.

### 2.4.3 Advantages and Disadvantages

| Advantages | Disadvantages |
|-----------|---------------|
| High accuracy (±0.25% to ±1%) | Moving parts → wear, limited life |
| Excellent repeatability | Sensitive to viscosity changes |
| Wide rangeability (10:1 or more) | Requires strainers to protect from debris |
| Compact and lightweight | Needs calibration per fluid |

## 2.5 Electromagnetic Flow Sensor (Magmeter)

### 2.5.1 Construction

A non-conductive lined pipe section (liner: PTFE, rubber, ceramic) with **electromagnetic coils** mounted diametrically opposite on the outside of the pipe. **Two electrodes** are mounted flush with the inner pipe wall, perpendicular to both the flow direction and the magnetic field.

### 2.5.2 Working Principle — Faraday's Law of Electromagnetic Induction

When a conductive fluid flows through the magnetic field $B$, it acts as a moving conductor cutting magnetic flux lines. An **EMF is induced** in the fluid, which is picked up by the electrodes:

$$E = B \cdot l \cdot v$$

where:
- $E$ = induced EMF (Volts)
- $B$ = magnetic flux density (Tesla)
- $l$ = distance between electrodes (= pipe diameter D, in meters)
- $v$ = average fluid velocity (m/s)

Since the volumetric flow $Q = v \cdot A = v \cdot \dfrac{\pi D^2}{4}$, the induced EMF is **linearly proportional to volumetric flow rate**:

$$E = \frac{4B}{\pi D} \cdot Q \quad \Rightarrow \quad Q = \frac{\pi D \cdot E}{4B}$$

### 2.5.3 Key Features
- **No moving parts** → maintenance-free, no pressure drop
- **No obstruction** → ideal for slurries, sewage, pulp, chemicals
- **Linear output** (unlike DP meters)
- **Independence from fluid properties** (viscosity, density, temperature) — only requires **conductivity ≥ 5 µS/cm**
- Cannot measure **non-conductive fluids** (oils, gases, hydrocarbons)
- Cannot measure fluids with magnetic particles (disturbs field)

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $\text{Re} = \dfrac{\rho v D}{\mu}$ | Reynolds Number | dimensionless |
| 2 | $\beta = \dfrac{d}{D}$ | Beta ratio (orifice / Venturi) | dimensionless |
| 3 | $Q_{th} = A_2 \sqrt{\dfrac{2(P_1-P_2)}{\rho \left(1 - \beta^4\right)}}$ | Theoretical volumetric flow (orifice / Venturi) | m³/s |
| 4 | $Q_{act} = C_d \cdot A_2 \sqrt{\dfrac{2(P_1-P_2)}{\rho \left(1 - \beta^4\right)}}$ | **Actual volumetric flow (orifice / Venturi)** | m³/s |
| 5 | $\dot{m} = C_d \cdot A_2 \sqrt{\dfrac{2\rho(P_1-P_2)}{1 - \beta^4}}$ | Mass flow rate | kg/s |
| 6 | $F_D = C_D \cdot \dfrac{\rho v^2}{2} \cdot A_t$ | Drag force on target | N |
| 7 | $N = K \cdot v$ | Turbine speed vs velocity | Hz |
| 8 | $f = K_f \cdot Q$ | Pulse frequency of turbine meter | Hz |
| 9 | $E = B \cdot D \cdot v$ | Faraday EMF (Magmeter) | V |
| 10 | $Q = \dfrac{\pi D \cdot E}{4B}$ | Volumetric flow from EMF | m³/s |
| 11 | $K = \dfrac{\text{Pulse Rate (Hz)}}{Q \text{ (L/s)}}$ | K-factor of turbine meter | pulses/L |

> [!NOTE]
> For Venturi tube, $C_d$ is typically **0.95–0.98**; for orifice plate, $C_d$ is typically **0.6–0.65**.

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Applications

- **Orifice Plate:** Custody transfer of natural gas, oil refinery process lines, water distribution networks.
- **Venturi Tube:** Large-diameter water mains, hydroelectric penstocks, HVAC systems, primary flow element in aircraft engine test rigs.
- **Target Flow Meter:** Chemical injection skids, pulp & paper, mining slurries, steam flow in power plants.
- **Rotameter:** Laboratory flow measurement, medical oxygen flowmeters, purge meters, low-flow chemical dosing.
- **Turbine Flow Meter:** Aviation fuel measurement, LPG/CNG dispensing, custody transfer of refined petroleum.
- **Electromagnetic Flow Meter:** Wastewater treatment, food & beverage (milk, beer, juice), mining slurries, chemical dosing, pharmaceutical water-for-injection (WFI).

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Volumetric Flow Through an Orifice Plate (or Venturi)

This is the **most important derivation** for KTU board exams. We derive the fundamental flow equation for both orifice plate and Venturi tube using **Bernoulli's equation** and the **continuity equation**.

### 3.1.1 Assumptions
1. Steady, incompressible flow.
2. Frictionless (ideal) flow between the two measurement sections.
3. No energy loss at the orifice (later accounted for by $C_d$).
4. Horizontal pipe (or gravitational head included in static pressure).

### 3.1.2 Setup

Consider a pipe of diameter $D$ carrying fluid at average velocity $V_1$. An orifice of diameter $d$ is installed, raising the velocity to $V_2$ at the **vena contracta** (or the throat, in the case of a Venturi).

**Notation:**
- Section 1: Upstream of orifice (pipe cross-section)
- Section 2: Vena contracta (or throat)
- $A_1 = \dfrac{\pi D^2}{4}$ — pipe area
- $A_2 = \dfrac{\pi d^2}{4}$ — orifice area
- $P_1, V_1$ — pressure and velocity at section 1
- $P_2, V_2$ — pressure and velocity at section 2
- $\rho$ — fluid density
- $g$ — acceleration due to gravity

### 3.1.3 Step 1 — Apply Bernoulli's Equation

Bernoulli's equation between sections 1 and 2 (assuming horizontal flow, $z_1 = z_2$):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{P_1}{\rho g} + \frac{V_1^2}{2g} + z_1 &= \frac{P_2}{\rho g} + \frac{V_2^2}{2g} + z_2
\end{aligned}
$$

Since the pipe is horizontal, $z_1 = z_2$. Cancelling $z$ and rearranging:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{P_1 - P_2}{\rho g} &= \frac{V_2^2 - V_1^2}{2g}
\end{aligned}
$$

Multiplying through by $\rho g$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_1 - P_2 &= \frac{\rho}{2}\left(V_2^2 - V_1^2\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the **pressure drop caused by the constriction**.

### 3.1.4 Step 2 — Apply Continuity Equation

For incompressible flow, the mass flow rate is constant:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\rho A_1 V_1 &= \rho A_2 V_2
\end{aligned}
$$

Canceling $\rho$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_1 V_1 &= A_2 V_2
\end{aligned}
$$

Solving for $V_1$ in terms of $V_2$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_1 &= V_2 \cdot \frac{A_2}{A_1} = V_2 \cdot \left(\frac{d}{D}\right)^2 = V_2 \cdot \beta^2
\end{aligned}
$$

where we define the **beta ratio** as $\beta = d/D$.

### 3.1.5 Step 3 — Substitute $V_1$ into the Bernoulli Result

Substituting $V_1 = V_2 \beta^2$ into $P_1 - P_2 = \dfrac{\rho}{2}\left(V_2^2 - V_1^2\right)$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_1 - P_2 &= \frac{\rho}{2}\left(V_2^2 - V_2^2 \beta^4\right) \\
P_1 - P_2 &= \frac{\rho V_2^2}{2}\left(1 - \beta^4\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.1.6 Step 4 — Solve for $V_2$

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_2^2 &= \frac{2(P_1 - P_2)}{\rho \left(1 - \beta^4\right)} \\
V_2 &= \sqrt{\frac{2(P_1 - P_2)}{\rho \left(1 - \beta^4\right)}}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.1.7 Step 5 — Compute Theoretical Volumetric Flow $Q_{th}$

The theoretical volumetric flow rate is $Q_{th} = A_2 \cdot V_2$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_{th} &= A_2 \cdot \sqrt{\frac{2(P_1 - P_2)}{\rho \left(1 - \beta^4\right)}} \\
Q_{th} &= \frac{\pi d^2}{4} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{2(P_1 - P_2)}{\rho \left(1 - \beta^4\right)}}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.1.8 Step 6 — Introduce Discharge Coefficient for Actual Flow

In practice, energy is lost to turbulence, viscous effects, and the flow contracts to a **vena contracta** that is smaller than the orifice opening. We account for this by introducing a **discharge coefficient** $C_d$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_{act} &= C_d \cdot A_2 \cdot \sqrt{\frac{2(P_1 - P_2)}{\rho \left(1 - \beta^4\right)}}
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the **governing equation for orifice plates AND Venturi tubes**. The only difference is the value of $C_d$:

- **Orifice Plate:** $C_d \approx 0.6 - 0.65$
- **Venturi Tube:** $C_d \approx 0.95 - 0.98$

### 3.1.9 Step 7 — Mass Flow Rate

Multiplying by fluid density $\rho$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{m} &= C_d \cdot A_2 \cdot \sqrt{\frac{2\rho(P_1 - P_2)}{1 - \beta^4}}
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the **mass flow equation** for both orifice plates and Venturi tubes.

## 3.2 Derivation: Electromagnetic Flow Sensor (Faraday's Law)

### 3.2.1 Physical Setup

A conductive fluid flows with average velocity $v$ through a circular pipe of diameter $D$ (and hence electrode spacing $l = D$). A uniform magnetic field $B$ is applied perpendicular to the flow direction and to the line joining the electrodes.

### 3.2.2 Applying Faraday's Law

Faraday's Law of Electromagnetic Induction states that the EMF induced in a conductor of length $l$ moving with velocity $v$ through a magnetic field of flux density $B$ is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
E &= B \cdot l \cdot v
\end{aligned}
$$

Since the electrode spacing equals the pipe diameter, $l = D$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
E &= B \cdot D \cdot v
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.2.3 Expressing EMF in Terms of Volumetric Flow

The volumetric flow rate is $Q = A \cdot v = \dfrac{\pi D^2}{4} \cdot v$, so $v = \dfrac{4Q}{\pi D^2}$. Substituting:

$$
\begin{aligned}
E &= B \cdot D \cdot \frac{4Q}{\pi D^2} \\
E &= \frac{4B}{\pi D} \cdot Q
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.2.4 Solving for Q

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q &= \frac{\pi D \cdot E}{4B}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Key Insight:** Since $B$ and $D$ are constants of the meter, $Q \propto E$ — a **linear** relationship, unlike the quadratic relationship of DP meters. This is one of the major advantages of the magmeter.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **For Gases and Non-Conductive Fluids:** The magmeter **cannot** be used. The fluid must have a minimum electrical conductivity (typically $\geq 5 \text{ μS/cm}$).

## 3.3 Derivation: Turbine (Spin) Flow Meter K-Factor

The K-factor of a turbine flow meter is defined as the number of pulses produced per unit volume of fluid passing through:

$$
\begin{aligned}
K &= \frac{\text{Pulse Frequency } f}{\text{Volumetric Flow Rate } Q}
\end{aligned}
$$

If the turbine makes $N$ revolutions per second and the pickup coil produces $Z$ pulses per revolution (one per blade, for $Z$ blades):

$$
\begin{aligned}
f &= Z \cdot N
\end{aligned}
$$

Since the linear velocity of the fluid $v$ is related to the rotational speed $N$ and the effective mean radius $r_m$ of the blade tip as $v = 2\pi r_m N$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q &= A \cdot v = A \cdot 2\pi r_m \cdot N \\
K &= \frac{f}{Q} = \frac{Z \cdot N}{2\pi r_m A \cdot N} = \frac{Z}{2\pi r_m A}
\end{aligned}
$$

So $K$ is a **geometric constant** of the turbine meter, depending only on the number of blades and the meter's geometry.

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix (KTU High-Yield)

| Parameter | Orifice Plate | Venturi | Target (Deflection) | Turbine (Spin) | Electromagnetic |
|-----------|---------------|---------|--------------------|----------------|-----------------| 
| **Principle** | Bernoulli (DP) | Bernoulli (DP) | Drag force | Rotational speed | Faraday's Law |
| **Output vs. Q** | $\Delta P \propto Q^2$ | $\Delta P \propto Q^2$ | $F \propto Q^2$ | $f \propto Q$ (linear) | $E \propto Q$ (linear) |
| **Accuracy** | ±1–2% | ±0.5–1% | ±1–2% | ±0.25–1% | ±0.5–1% |
| **Pressure Loss** | High (40–60%) | Very Low (10–15%) | Moderate | Moderate | **None** |
| **Moving Parts** | None | None | None (target floats) | **Yes** (rotor) | **None** |
| **Cost** | Low | High | Moderate | Moderate | High |
| **Fluid Conductivity Required** | No | No | No | No | **Yes (≥ 5 µS/cm)** |
| **Suitable for Gases** | Yes | Yes | Yes | Yes | **No** |
| **Suitable for Slurries** | Limited | Yes | Yes | No (wears) | Yes |
| **Rangeability** | 4:1 | 4:1 | 4:1 | 10:1 or more | 100:1 |
| **Typical $C_d$** | 0.6 – 0.65 | 0.95 – 0.98 | $C_D$ ≈ 1.1–1.3 (target) | N/A | N/A |

> [!NOTE]
> Notice the **quadratic** dependence on $Q$ for DP and target meters vs. the **linear** dependence for turbine and magnetic meters. This is a key distinguishing point and a frequent KTU exam question.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Orifice Plate — Block Functional Architecture

This block diagram shows the signal path and the physical phenomenon chain in an orifice plate flow measurement system.

```mermaid
graph LR
    pipeA["PIPE SECTION UPSTREAM"]
    orificeA["ORIFICE PLATE WITH CONCENTRIC HOLE"]
    venaA["VENA CONTRACTA REGION"]
    pipeB["PIPE SECTION DOWNSTREAM"]
    tapA["HIGH PRESSURE TAPPING"]
    tapB["LOW PRESSURE TAPPING"]
    dpA["DIFFERENTIAL PRESSURE TRANSMITTER"]
    sigA["4 TO 20 mA CURRENT SIGNAL"]
    ctrlA["DCS OR PLC CONTROLLER"]
    pipeA --> orificeA
    orificeA --> venaA
    venaA --> pipeB
    pipeA --> tapA
    venaA --> tapB
    tapA --> dpA
    tapB --> dpA
    dpA --> sigA
    sigA --> ctrlA
```

**Explanation of Flow:**
1. Fluid flows from upstream pipe section (pipeA) and encounters the orifice plate (orificeA).
2. The cross-section contracts to a minimum at the vena contracta (venaA).
3. Pressure is tapped at two locations (tapA, tapB) and fed to a DP transmitter (dpA).
4. The transmitter outputs a standard 4–20 mA current signal (sigA).
5. The signal is received by a DCS/PLC (ctrlA) for display, control, or recording.

## 4.2 Venturi Tube — Block Functional Architecture

```mermaid
graph LR
    inA["INLET PIPE FULL DIAMETER"]
    convA["CONVERGING SECTION 20 DEGREE"]
    thA["THROAT MINIMUM AREA"]
    divA["DIVERGING SECTION 5 TO 7 DEGREE"]
    outA["OUTLET PIPE FULL DIAMETER"]
    pt1A["PRESSURE TAP AT INLET"]
    pt2A["PRESSURE TAP AT THROAT"]
    dpB["DP TRANSMITTER"]
    flowcA["FLOW COMPUTER"]
    inA --> convA
    convA --> thA
    thA --> divA
    divA --> outA
    inA --> pt1A
    thA --> pt2A
    pt1A --> dpB
    pt2A --> dpB
    dpB --> flowcA
```

**Key Difference from Orifice Plate:** The gradual 20° convergence and 5–7° divergence minimize turbulence, resulting in **lower permanent pressure loss** and **higher Cd value**.

## 4.3 Electromagnetic Flow Sensor — Block Functional Architecture

```mermaid
graph LR
    conA["CONDUCTIVE FLUID INLET"]
    coilA["ELECTROMAGNETIC COIL PAIR"]
    fieldA["UNIFORM MAGNETIC FIELD B"]
    pipeA["NON CONDUCTIVE LINED PIPE"]
    elecA["TWO FLUSH ELECTRODES"]
    emfA["INDUCED EMF SIGNAL"]
    convA["SIGNAL CONDITIONER AND AMPLIFIER"]
    dispA["DISPLAY OR CONTROLLER"]
    conA --> pipeA
    coilA --> fieldA
    fieldA --> pipeA
    pipeA --> elecA
    elecA --> emfA
    emfA --> convA
    convA --> dispA
```

**Working:** Coils (coilA) generate a magnetic field (fieldA) perpendicular to flow in the lined pipe (pipeA). The conductive fluid moves through this field, inducing an EMF (emfA) at the electrodes (elecA). A signal conditioner (convA) amplifies the small EMF (typically millivolts) and outputs a standard signal to the display/controller (dispA).

## 4.4 Spin Type (Turbine) Flow Sensor — Block Functional Architecture

```mermaid
graph LR
    flowA["INCOMING FLUID FLOW"]
    rotorA["MULTI BLADED TURBINE ROTOR"]
    shaftA["SUPPORTING SHAFT AND BEARINGS"]
    pickA["MAGNETIC PICKUP COIL"]
    pulseA["PULSE TRAIN OUTPUT"]
    freqA["FREQUENCY TO VOLTAGE CONVERTER"]
    displayA["FLOW RATE DISPLAY"]
    flowA --> rotorA
    rotorA --> shaftA
    rotorA --> pickA
    pickA --> pulseA
    pulseA --> freqA
    freqA --> displayA
```

## 4.5 Deflection (Target) Type Flow Sensor — Block Functional Architecture

```mermaid
graph LR
    flowB["FLUID FLOW DIRECTION"]
    targetB["CIRCULAR TARGET DISC"]
    armB["SUPPORT ARM WITH STRAIN GAUGE"]
    forceB["MEASURED DRAG FORCE"]
    convB["BRIDGE CIRCUIT AND AMPLIFIER"]
    outputB["OUTPUT SIGNAL PROPORTIONAL TO Q SQUARED"]
    flowB --> targetB
    targetB --> armB
    armB --> forceB
    forceB --> convB
    convB --> outputB
```
