# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) — [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**Q: Define Beta Ratio and Discharge Coefficient as applied to orifice plate flow meters.**

**Model Answer:**

The **Beta Ratio (β)** is defined as the ratio of the diameter of the orifice opening to the internal diameter of the pipe:

$$\beta = \frac{d}{D}$$

It is a dimensionless quantity. Typical values for orifice plates range from **0.2 to 0.75**. A higher β ratio means a higher differential pressure but also a higher permanent pressure loss.

The **Discharge Coefficient ($C_d$)** is defined as the ratio of the actual (real) flow rate to the theoretical (ideal) flow rate calculated from Bernoulli's equation:

$$C_d = \frac{Q_{actual}}{Q_{theoretical}}$$

It accounts for the vena contracta effect, friction losses, and turbulence. For orifice plates, $C_d$ is typically **0.6 to 0.65**, and for Venturi tubes, it is **0.95 to 0.98**.

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks) — [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**Q: Why is an Electromagnetic Flow Sensor (Magmeter) not suitable for measuring the flow of gas or oil?**

**Model Answer:**

The Electromagnetic Flow Sensor operates on **Faraday's Law of Electromagnetic Induction**, which states that an EMF is induced in a conductor when it moves through a magnetic field. The induced EMF is given by:

$$E = B \cdot D \cdot v$$

For the EMF to be generated, the fluid itself must act as the **electrical conductor** cutting through the magnetic field. Therefore, the fluid must have a **minimum electrical conductivity** of approximately **5 µS/cm**.

- **Gases** (air, natural gas, nitrogen, steam) are excellent electrical insulators and have negligible conductivity → **no EMF is induced**, so the magmeter fails to produce any output signal.
- **Hydrocarbon oils** (crude oil, diesel, lubricants) are also non-conductive → magmeter cannot measure them.

In contrast, **water-based fluids, slurries, acids, alkalis, and most food/beverage liquids** have sufficient ionic conductivity and can be measured accurately with a magmeter.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — With Internal Choice

### Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**(a) [7 Marks] Derive the equation for the volumetric flow rate through an orifice plate. State the assumptions clearly. Why is a discharge coefficient introduced?**

**(b) [7 Marks) An orifice plate with a 50 mm diameter bore is fitted in a 200 mm diameter pipe. Water flows through the pipe at a rate of 5 litres/second. The differential pressure measured across the tappings is 10 kPa. Calculate the discharge coefficient. Take the density of water as 1000 kg/m³.**

---

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Assumptions:**
1. Steady, incompressible flow.
2. Frictionless flow between the two measurement sections.
3. Horizontal pipe.
4. Velocity is uniform at each cross-section.

**Derivation:**

Applying **Bernoulli's equation** between upstream section 1 and the vena contracta section 2:

$$P_1 + \frac{1}{2}\rho V_1^2 = P_2 + \frac{1}{2}\rho V_2^2$$

Rearranging:

$$P_1 - P_2 = \frac{\rho}{2}(V_2^2 - V_1^2)$$

Applying the **continuity equation** $A_1 V_1 = A_2 V_2$ and substituting $V_1 = V_2 \beta^2$ (where $\beta = d/D$):

$$P_1 - P_2 = \frac{\rho V_2^2}{2}(1 - \beta^4)$$

Solving for $V_2$:

$$V_2 = \sqrt{\frac{2(P_1 - P_2)}{\rho(1 - \beta^4)}}$$

The theoretical volumetric flow:

$$Q_{th} = A_2 V_2 = \frac{\pi d^2}{4}\sqrt{\frac{2(P_1 - P_2)}{\rho(1 - \beta^4)}}$$

**Why Discharge Coefficient?**

In practice, due to the **vena contracta** (the flow contracts to a cross-section smaller than the orifice diameter) and energy losses due to **friction and turbulence**, the actual flow is less than the theoretical flow. Hence, a **discharge coefficient $C_d$** is introduced:

$$Q_{act} = C_d \cdot \frac{\pi d^2}{4}\sqrt{\frac{2(P_1 - P_2)}{\rho(1 - \beta^4)}}$$

For an orifice plate, $C_d$ is typically **0.6 – 0.65**.

---

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Given:**
- Orifice diameter: $d = 50 \text{ mm} = 0.05 \text{ m}$
- Pipe diameter: $D = 200 \text{ mm} = 0.2 \text{ m}$
- Flow rate: $Q = 5 \text{ L/s} = 5 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$
- Differential pressure: $\Delta P = 10 \text{ kPa} = 10^4 \text{ Pa}$
- Density: $\rho = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$

**Step 1: Calculate Beta Ratio**

$$\beta = \frac{d}{D} = \frac{0.05}{0.2} = 0.25$$

**Step 2: Calculate Orifice Area**

$$A_2 = \frac{\pi d^2}{4} = \frac{\pi (0.05)^2}{4} = 1.9635 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 3: Calculate Theoretical Flow**

$$Q_{th} = A_2 \sqrt{\frac{2\Delta P}{\rho(1 - \beta^4)}} = 1.9635 \times 10^{-3} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 10^4}{1000 \times (1 - 0.25^4)}}$$

$$\beta^4 = (0.25)^4 = 0.00390625 \quad \Rightarrow \quad 1 - \beta^4 = 0.99609$$

$$Q_{th} = 1.9635 \times 10^{-3} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{20000}{996.09}} = 1.9635 \times 10^{-3} \cdot \sqrt{20.079}$$

$$Q_{th} = 1.9635 \times 10^{-3} \cdot 4.481 = 8.797 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

**Step 4: Calculate Discharge Coefficient**

$$C_d = \frac{Q_{act}}{Q_{th}} = \frac{5 \times 10^{-3}}{8.797 \times 10^{-3}} = 0.568$$

**Answer: $C_d \approx 0.568$** (within the typical range of 0.6 – 0.65; small deviation may occur due to rounding or fluid condition).

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**(a) [7 Marks] Explain the working principle of an Electromagnetic Flow Sensor with a neat diagram. Derive the relationship between the induced EMF and the volumetric flow rate.**

**(b) [7 Marks] Explain the working of a Turbine (Spin Type) Flow Meter. What is the K-factor? An 8-bladed turbine flow meter produces a pulse frequency of 240 Hz when water flows at 0.6 L/s. Find the K-factor.**

---

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Working Principle:**

The Electromagnetic Flow Sensor works on **Faraday's Law of Electromagnetic Induction**. It consists of:
- A non-conductive lined pipe section (liner: PTFE, rubber).
- Two electromagnetic coils placed diametrically opposite, generating a uniform magnetic field $B$ perpendicular to the flow.
- Two electrodes mounted flush with the inner pipe wall, perpendicular to both flow and magnetic field.

**Working:**

When a conductive fluid flows through the magnetic field, the ions in the fluid experience a **Lorentz force**, causing positive and negative charges to migrate to the opposite electrodes. This creates a measurable **EMF $E$** proportional to the average fluid velocity.

**Derivation:**

By Faraday's Law:

$$E = B \cdot l \cdot v$$

For a circular pipe, $l = D$ (pipe diameter). So:

$$E = B \cdot D \cdot v$$

The volumetric flow rate is $Q = A \cdot v = \dfrac{\pi D^2}{4} \cdot v$, giving $v = \dfrac{4Q}{\pi D^2}$. Substituting:

$$E = B \cdot D \cdot \frac{4Q}{\pi D^2} = \frac{4B}{\pi D} \cdot Q$$

Solving for $Q$:

$$Q = \frac{\pi D \cdot E}{4B}$$

**Key Feature:** The relationship between $E$ and $Q$ is **linear**, making the magmeter highly accurate and easy to calibrate.

**Limitations:**
- Fluid must be conductive ($\geq 5$ μS/cm).
- Cannot be used for gases, oils, or hydrocarbons.

---

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Working of Turbine (Spin Type) Flow Meter:**

A turbine flow meter consists of a multi-bladed rotor (turbine) mounted coaxially on a shaft supported by bearings inside the pipe. A **magnetic pickup coil** is mounted externally on the pipe wall, sensing the passage of each blade tip as it rotates.

When fluid flows through the pipe, it strikes the angled blades, causing the rotor to spin. The **rotational speed N is directly proportional to the fluid velocity v**:

$$N = K_1 \cdot v$$

Each blade passing the pickup coil generates an electrical pulse. For $Z$ blades rotating at $N$ revolutions per second, the pulse frequency is:

$$f = Z \cdot N$$

The volumetric flow rate is obtained from the pulse frequency using the **K-factor**:

$$K = \frac{f}{Q} \quad \Rightarrow \quad Q = \frac{f}{K}$$

**Numerical Solution:**

**Given:** $Z = 8$ blades, $f = 240 \text{ Hz}$, $Q = 0.6 \text{ L/s}$

**K-factor:**

$$K = \frac{f}{Q} = \frac{240 \text{ Hz}}{0.6 \text{ L/s}} = 400 \text{ pulses/L}$$

**Answer: K = 400 pulses per litre** (i.e., the meter produces 400 electrical pulses for every litre of water that passes through it).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Alert — Where Students Commonly Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing beta ratio β and β²:** The relation uses $\beta^4$ in the denominator (because $\beta^2$ appears twice — once in $(d/D)^2$ for area and once for the velocity ratio). Many students wrongly write $\beta^2$ and lose 1–2 marks.
> 2. **Forgetting the $C_d$ in the final equation:** The final expression MUST have $C_d$ multiplied. The theoretical equation without $C_d$ is incomplete for an orifice plate.
> 3. **Wrong units in the K-factor:** The K-factor unit is **pulses per unit volume** (e.g., pulses/litre or pulses/m³), NOT just "pulses" or "Hz".
> 4. **In Magmeter derivations, mixing up $l$ and $D$:** For a circular pipe, the electrode spacing $l$ equals the pipe diameter $D$. Some students leave it as $l$ and don't substitute.
> 5. **Confusing Rotameter and Turbine meter:** Rotameter is **deflection/variable-area type**, turbine is **spin/velocity type** — they are different. Don't use them interchangeably.
> 6. **Neglecting vena contracta explanation:** When asked about the discharge coefficient, ALWAYS mention **vena contracta** as the physical reason for the deviation from theoretical flow.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

### 🔑 High-Yield Quick Revision Checklist

- [ ] **Orifice Plate:** Thin plate with a hole; measures ΔP across it; uses Bernoulli + continuity; $C_d ≈ 0.6–0.65$; high permanent pressure loss.
- [ ] **Venturi Tube:** Converging-diverging section; smoother flow; $C_d ≈ 0.95–0.98$; low permanent pressure loss; expensive and bulky.
- [ ] **Deflection Type:** Includes **Target (Impact) meter** and **Rotameter**. Drag force ∝ v². Rotameter = variable area, float rises with flow.
- [ ] **Spin Type (Turbine):** Rotor spins at speed ∝ flow; magnetic pickup gives pulse train; K-factor = pulses per unit volume; high accuracy, moving parts wear.
- [ ] **Electromagnetic (Magmeter):** Faraday's law $E = B D v$; linear output; no pressure drop; works only for conductive fluids; no moving parts.
- [ ] **Governing equation for all DP meters (orifice, Venturi):** $Q = C_d A_2 \sqrt{\dfrac{2\Delta P}{\rho(1-\beta^4)}}$
- [ ] **Beta ratio:** $\beta = d/D$ — appears as $\beta^4$ in the flow equation.
- [ ] **Discharge Coefficient $C_d$** accounts for vena contracta and friction losses.
- [ ] **Mass flow:** $\dot{m} = C_d A_2 \sqrt{\dfrac{2\rho \Delta P}{1-\beta^4}}$
- [ ] **Magmeter EMF:** $E = \dfrac{4B}{\pi D} \cdot Q$ — **linear** in $Q$ (not quadratic).
- [ ] **Quadratic vs. Linear:** DP meters and target meters give $\Delta P$ or $F$ proportional to $Q^2$. Turbine and Magmeter give frequency or EMF proportional to $Q$ directly.
- [ ] **Permanent pressure loss ranking (lowest to highest):** Magmeter (0) < Venturi (10–15%) < Target (moderate) < Orifice plate (40–60%).
- [ ] **Fluid conductivity requirement:** Only the Magmeter has this restriction ($\geq 5$ μS/cm).
- [ ] **Turbine K-factor formula:** $K = f/Q$ (units: pulses per litre or pulses per m³).
- [ ] **Rangeability:** Magmeter and Turbine offer the widest rangeability (10:1 to 100:1), while orifice/Venturi are limited to ~4:1.

### 📌 Common Confusions Cleared

| Confusion | Clarification |
|-----------|--------------|
| "Orifice vs. Venturi — which is more accurate?" | Venturi (Cd closer to 1) is more accurate, but orifice is cheaper. |
| "Can a magmeter measure oil?" | **No** — oil is non-conductive. |
| "Does a turbine meter need external power?" | Yes, for the pickup coil and signal conditioning electronics. |
| "Is rotameter the same as orifice meter?" | **No** — rotameter is variable-area (deflection type); orifice is differential-pressure (obstruction type). |
| "Why is the magmeter linear?" | Because EMF is directly proportional to velocity $v$, and $Q$ is directly proportional to $v$. |

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Level Transducer - Discrete Level, Level Measurement by Pressure Sensor, Differential Pressure Sensor, Force Sensor, Capacitive Level Sensor
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBRT303 - SENSORS AND ACTUATORS
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Level Transducer - Discrete Level, Level Measurement by Pressure Sensor, Differential Pressure Sensor, Force Sensor, Capacitive Level Sensor

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Level Measurement — Formal Definition
A **Level Transducer** is a device that converts the vertical height (level) of a bulk solid, powder, slurry, or liquid contained within a vessel, tank, silo, or reservoir into a proportional, standardized electrical signal (typically **4–20 mA current loop**, **0–10 V DC**, or a digital **HART / Foundation Fieldbus** variable) suitable for indication, recording, or closed-loop process control.

Level measurement is broadly classified into two families:
- **Continuous Level Measurement** — produces an output proportional to the exact surface height at every instant (e.g., pressure-based, capacitive, ultrasonic, radar).
- **Discrete (Point) Level Measurement** — produces a binary ON/OFF or HIGH/LOW signal only when the material reaches a pre-defined switching level (e.g., float switch, conductivity probe, vibrating fork, optical switch).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The syllabus explicitly emphasizes *transduction principles*. You are expected to identify which **physical principle** (hydrostatic, buoyancy, capacitive, piezoresistive) is exploited by each sensor and how the physical quantity is mathematically related to the output signal.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
Imagine a transparent water cooler bottle. To know how much water is left, you could:
1. **Look at it directly** — like a *sight glass* (visual level).
2. **Stick a marked stick inside** — like a *dipstick* (mechanical level).
3. **Attach a tiny inflatable balloon at the bottom and measure the air pressure needed to keep it inflated** — the deeper the water, the harder you must press. That pressure IS the level. This is exactly how a **hydrostatic pressure sensor** works.
4. **Dip two straws of different lengths and blow air through the shorter one** — bubbles emerge from the bottom, and the back-pressure measured at the top straw tells you the height. This is the classic **Differential Pressure Bubbler System**.
5. **Place a floating cork with a magnetic contact** that triggers an alarm at high and low levels — that is a **Discrete Float Switch**.

### 1.3 Standards, Units, and Constants
- **SI unit of level:** metre (m). Industrial practice also uses centimetres (cm), millimetres (mm), or percentage of full scale (% FS).
- **Density of water (reference):** **ρ_water = 1000 kg/m³ = 1 g/cm³**.
- **Standard gravity:** **g = 9.80665 m/s²** (often approximated as **9.81 m/s²**).
- **Standard atmospheric pressure:** 101.325 kPa.
- **Common current loop:** **4 mA = 0 % level**, **20 mA = 100 % level** (live zero allows broken-wire detection).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why "4–20 mA" and not "0–20 mA"?**
> The **4 mA live-zero** convention lets the controller distinguish between a genuine **zero-level reading** and a **broken/shorted cable** (which would drive the current to 0 mA). This is a favourite KTU viva question.

---

### 1.4 Classification Overview

| Family | Sub-Type | Typical Sensor | Output Nature |
|---|---|---|---|
| Discrete | Mechanical | Float switch with magnetic reed | Binary |
| Discrete | Electrical | Conductivity (resistive) probe | Binary |
| Continuous | Hydrostatic | Pressure sensor at tank bottom | 4–20 mA |
| Continuous | Differential pressure | DP cell across two taps | 4–20 mA |
| Continuous | Force-balanced | Load cell + displacer | 4–20 mA |
| Continuous | Capacitive | Coaxial / rod capacitor | 4–20 mA / V |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Discrete Level Measurement — Operating Principles

#### 2.1.1 Float Switch (Buoyancy-Based Discrete Sensor)
A hollow buoyant body (the **float**) rides on the liquid surface, guided by a vertical stem. Inside the float, or along the stem, a **magnetic reed switch** or a **mechanical micro-switch** is actuated when the float reaches a preset height.

**Logic Chain:**
1. Liquid level rises → float rises (Archimedes' buoyancy: **F_b = ρ_liquid · g · V_displaced**).
2. Float carries an embedded permanent magnet to a fixed reed switch location.
3. Reed contacts close (NO → NC, or vice versa) → energises/de-energises a relay coil.
4. Output: a **discrete electrical contact closure**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Limitation:** Float switches are *point* devices. Two switches are needed for HIGH and LOW alarms; four or more for multi-point zoning. They are unsuitable for corrosive, highly viscous, slurry, or coating liquids.

#### 2.1.2 Conductivity (Resistive) Point Level Switch
Two (or three) metallic electrodes are inserted into the tank at the desired trigger height. The electrodes are energised by a small **AC excitation voltage** (typically 5–24 V AC at low current). When liquid contacts both electrodes, it completes the circuit — the AC current that flows is detected, and the output relay is energised.

- **Conductive liquids required** (water, acids, alkalis, milk). Fails for oils, hydrocarbons, ultra-pure water.
- AC excitation prevents **electrolysis and electrode corrosion**.
- A third (reference) electrode eliminates false triggering caused by moisture or condensation on the vessel wall.

**Why three electrodes?** The reference electrode senses conduction *through the bulk liquid only*; current leaking down the tank wall to ground is ignored — this is called **"wall-effect compensation."**

---

### 2.2 Continuous Level Measurement by Pressure Sensor

#### 2.2.1 Hydrostatic Pressure Principle
The pressure at the bottom of a static liquid column is given by **Pascal's Law**:

$$P_{\text{hydrostatic}} = \rho \cdot g \cdot h$$

where:
- **ρ** = density of the liquid (kg/m³)
- **g** = acceleration due to gravity (9.81 m/s²)
- **h** = vertical height of the liquid column above the sensor (m)

A **pressure sensor** mounted at the bottom of the tank therefore reads the hydrostatic head, which is **directly proportional to level h** (provided ρ is constant).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the single most important equation in level measurement. If you forget this on the KTU exam, you lose at least **3 marks**.

#### 2.2.2 Open-Tank vs Closed-Tank Mounting

| Configuration | Sensor Location | Pressure Equation |
|---|---|---|
| **Open tank** | At bottom, vented to atmosphere on low side | $P = \rho g h$ |
| **Closed tank (pressurised)** | At bottom, low side tapped into vapour space | $P = \rho g h + P_{\text{vapour}}$ |

For a *vented* (open) tank, the low-pressure port of the sensor is left open to the atmosphere, so the gauge pressure reading directly equals ρgh. For a *pressurised* tank, the vapour pressure acts on the low side and must be **subtracted** — this brings us directly to the differential pressure method.

---

### 2.3 Differential Pressure (DP) Level Sensor

#### 2.3.1 Working Principle
A **Differential Pressure Transmitter (DPT)** measures the difference in pressure between two taps:
- **High-pressure tap (HP):** at or near the tank bottom, submerged in the liquid.
- **Low-pressure tap (LP):** above the liquid surface, exposed to the vapour space (in closed tanks) or to the atmosphere (in open tanks).

The differential pressure is:

$$\Delta P = P_{\text{HP}} - P_{\text{LP}}$$

For an open tank: $\Delta P = \rho g h$ (since LP = P_atm).

For a closed tank: $\Delta P = \rho g h + P_{\text{vapour}} - P_{\text{vapour}} = \rho g h$ (vapour cancels).

#### 2.3.2 Suppression, Elevation, and Zero-Span Adjustment
- **Suppressed zero:** If the HP tap cannot be mounted at the exact bottom of the tank, an **electronic or mechanical suppression** is applied so that the transmitter reads 4 mA at the *actual* zero-level, not at the sensor location.
- **Elevated zero:** If the sensor is installed *below* the tank bottom (e.g., in a sealed condensate pot), an **elevation** is added.

#### 2.3.3 Bubbler System
In a **bubbler** (purge) system, a constant, very low flow rate of clean air or inert gas is bubbled through a dip tube whose outlet is submerged in the liquid. The back-pressure required to push the gas out equals the hydrostatic head of the liquid above the dip-tube outlet:

$$P_{\text{back-pressure}} = \rho g h$$

> [!TIP]
> **Why use a bubbler?** The tank contents never contact the sensor — ideal for **corrosive, slurry, sewage, or hot liquids**. The sensor only "sees" clean air.

---

### 2.4 Force-Balanced / Load-Cell Level Sensor (Displacer Type)

#### 2.4.1 The Displacer Principle
A cylindrical **displacer** (a sealed metal cylinder slightly denser than the liquid) is suspended from a force-balance mechanism into the liquid. As the liquid level rises, an increasing portion of the displacer is submerged. By Archimedes' principle, the upward buoyant force increases, reducing the **apparent weight** the load cell must support.

**Apparent weight of displacer at level h:**

$$W(h) = W_{\text{dry}} - \rho \cdot g \cdot A \cdot h_{\text{submerged}}$$

where:
- $W_{\text{dry}} = \rho_d \cdot g \cdot V$ (weight of displacer in air)
- $A$ = cross-sectional area of the displacer
- $h_{\text{submerged}}$ = submerged length of the displacer

#### 2.4.2 The Load Cell (Force Sensor)
The load cell is a **strain-gauge based force sensor**. A strain gauge is a thin metallic foil whose resistance changes when strained:

$$R = \rho_{\text{res}} \cdot \frac{L}{A}$$

When the foil is stretched by strain ε = ΔL/L:

$$R + \Delta R = \rho_{\text{res}} \cdot \frac{L + \Delta L}{A - \nu A \varepsilon}$$

which gives the **Gauge Factor (GF)**:

$$GF = \frac{\Delta R / R}{\varepsilon} = 1 + 2\nu + \frac{\Delta \rho_{\text{res}} / \rho_{\text{res}}}{\varepsilon}$$

For constantan (typical foil): **GF ≈ 2.0**. For semiconductor gauges: **GF ≈ 100–200**.

The four strain gauges are wired into a **Wheatstone bridge** (Section 2.5), excited by a stable voltage $V_{\text{ex}}$, giving an output:

$$V_{\text{out}} = V_{\text{ex}} \cdot \left[ \frac{R_1}{R_1 + R_2} - \frac{R_3}{R_3 + R_4} \right]$$

For a balanced bridge, R1·R4 = R2·R3. Small resistance changes δR unbalance the bridge linearly with force.

---

### 2.5 Capacitive Level Sensor

#### 2.5.1 Principle
The sensor forms a **capacitor** with the liquid acting as a **dielectric** (non-conductive liquids) or as one of the plates (conductive liquids). Capacitance is:

$$C = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{d}$$

where:
- $\varepsilon_0$ = **8.854 × 10⁻¹² F/m** (permittivity of free space)
- $\varepsilon_r$ = relative permittivity of the dielectric (water ≈ 80, oil ≈ 2–3, air ≈ 1)
- $A$ = effective plate area (m²)
- $d$ = plate separation (m)

#### 2.5.2 Configurations
1. **Concentric (coaxial) capacitive probe** — two cylinders, the inner rod and the outer sheath. The liquid rises between them. Capacitance for an empty probe of length L:

$$C_0 = \frac{2 \pi \varepsilon_0 L}{\ln(R_o / R_i)}$$

When liquid rises to level h, the lower portion has dielectric ε_liquid while the upper portion still has air (ε_air ≈ 1):

$$C(h) = \frac{2 \pi \varepsilon_0}{\ln(R_o / R_i)} \left[ (L - h) \varepsilon_{\text{air}} + h \cdot \varepsilon_{\text{liquid}} \right]$$

The **change** in capacitance is:

$$\Delta C = C(h) - C_0 = \frac{2 \pi \varepsilon_0 h}{\ln(R_o / R_i)} (\varepsilon_{\text{liquid}} - \varepsilon_{\text{air}})$$

ΔC is **linearly proportional to h** — the ideal transducer characteristic.

2. **Rod-type probe (non-conductive liquid)** — a single insulated rod acts as one plate; the tank wall acts as the other. Liquid level changes the effective dielectric.

3. **Rod-type probe (conductive liquid)** — the metal rod is insulated (e.g., PTFE-coated) and the conductive liquid itself forms the outer "plate." Capacitance still varies linearly with submerged area.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU common error:** Students often assume the liquid is the dielectric for *all* capacitive level sensors. Remember — for conductive liquids, the liquid is the **second electrode**, not the dielectric. The dielectric is the **insulating coating** on the rod.

---

### 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation | Meaning / Use | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $P = \rho g h$ | Hydrostatic pressure at depth h | Pa |
| 2 | $\Delta P = \rho g (h_1 - h_2)$ | Differential pressure DP cell | Pa |
| 3 | $F_b = \rho V g$ | Buoyant force on displacer/float | N |
| 4 | $W(h) = W_{\text{dry}} - \rho g A h_{\text{sub}}$ | Apparent weight vs level | N |
| 5 | $GF = (\Delta R/R)/\varepsilon$ | Gauge factor of strain gauge | dimensionless |
| 6 | $V_{\text{out}} = V_{\text{ex}}[R_1/(R_1+R_2) - R_3/(R_3+R_4)]$ | Wheatstone bridge output | V |
| 7 | $C = \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A / d$ | Parallel-plate capacitance | F |
| 8 | $C(h) = (2\pi\varepsilon_0/\ln(R_o/R_i))[(L-h) + h\varepsilon_r]$ | Coaxial probe with liquid level h | F |
| 9 | $\Delta C \propto h$ | Linear level output (capacitive) | F |
| 10 | $I = 4 + 16(h/h_{\text{max}})$ | 4–20 mA current loop conversion | mA |

**Engineering utility:** These sensors together cover roughly 90% of process-industry level applications — from a household overhead-tank float switch to refinery crude-oil DP transmitters, brewing-vessel capacitive probes, and cement-silo load cells.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Hydrostatic Pressure → Output Current Relationship

We want to derive the transfer function **I_out (mA) as a function of liquid level h (m)** for a hydrostatic pressure-based level transmitter.

**Given:**
- Tank contains water with density ρ = 1000 kg/m³.
- Sensor is mounted at the tank bottom (h = 0).
- Sensor span: 0 to h_max = 5 m.
- Transmitter is calibrated 4 mA ↔ 0 m, 20 mA ↔ 5 m.
- g = 9.81 m/s².

**Step 1 — Pressure at the sensor diaphragm when level is h:**

$$P(h) = \rho \cdot g \cdot h$$

**Step 2 — Substitute numerical values:**

$$P(h) = 1000 \cdot 9.81 \cdot h = 9810 \, h \;\; \text{(Pa)}$$

This is the physical quantity the diaphragm feels.

**Step 3 — Maximum pressure (span input):**

$$P_{\text{max}} = 9810 \cdot 5 = 49050 \, \text{Pa} = 49.05 \, \text{kPa}$$

So we select a sensor with a nominal range of at least 0–50 kPa.

**Step 4 — Linear normalisation to a 0–1 fraction:**

$$f(h) = \frac{P(h)}{P_{\text{max}}} = \frac{9810 h}{49050} = \frac{h}{5} = 0.2 h$$

**Step 5 — Map to 4–20 mA current loop:**

$$I_{\text{out}}(h) = 4 + 16 \cdot f(h) = 4 + 16(0.2 h)$$

$$\boxed{I_{\text{out}}(h) = 4 + 3.2 h \;\; \text{(mA)}}$$

**Step 6 — Verification:**
- At h = 0: I_out = 4 mA ✓
- At h = 5 m: I_out = 4 + 3.2(5) = 4 + 16 = 20 mA ✓

> [!NOTE]
> This derivation is the **exact pattern KTU examiners use** for 7-mark "design a transmitter" questions. Always show units, show substitutions, and verify boundary values.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Differential Pressure for an Open Tank with Suppressed Zero

**Given:**
- A cylindrical storage tank, height 6 m, diameter 3 m.
- The DP cell HP tap is mounted **0.5 m above the true tank bottom** (process connection constraint).
- Liquid: diesel, ρ = 850 kg/m³.
- LP tap vented to atmosphere.
- Desired output: 4 mA at true zero (h = 0), 20 mA at true full (h = 6 m).
- g = 9.81 m/s².

**Step 1 — Pressure at HP tap when true level is h:**

$$P_{\text{HP}} = \rho \cdot g \cdot (h + 0.5)$$

**Step 2 — Pressure at LP tap (atmospheric, taken as reference 0):**

$$P_{\text{LP}} = 0 \;\; \text{(gauge)}$$

**Step 3 — Differential pressure seen by the cell:**

$$\Delta P(h) = P_{\text{HP}} - P_{\text{LP}} = \rho g (h + 0.5)$$

**Step 4 — At true zero (h = 0):**

$$\Delta P(0) = 850 \cdot 9.81 \cdot 0.5 = 4169.25 \, \text{Pa} \approx 4.169 \, \text{kPa}$$

This is the **suppressed zero** — the sensor does *not* read zero; the transmitter electronics must subtract 4.169 kPa internally to make I_out = 4 mA.

**Step 5 — At true full (h = 6 m):**

$$\Delta P(6) = 850 \cdot 9.81 \cdot 6.5 = 54204.45 \, \text{Pa} \approx 54.20 \, \text{kPa}$$

**Step 6 — Sensor span (electronic range):**

$$\text{Span} = \Delta P(6) - \Delta P(0) = 54204.45 - 4169.25 = 50035.2 \, \text{Pa}$$

This is **ρ·g·h_max** — confirming that the *span* still equals the hydrostatic range of the true liquid column; only the **zero** is shifted (suppressed) by 4.169 kPa.

**Step 7 — Final transfer equation:**

$$I_{\text{out}}(h) = 4 + \frac{16}{\Delta P(6) - \Delta P(0)} \left[ \Delta P(h) - \Delta P(0) \right]$$

$$I_{\text{out}}(h) = 4 + \frac{16}{50035.2}\left[54204.45 - 850 \cdot 9.81 \cdot (h + 0.5)\right] \cdot (-1 \text{ rearrangement})$$

$$\boxed{I_{\text{out}}(h) = 4 + \frac{16}{6}(h) = 4 + 2.667 \, h \;\; \text{(mA)}}$$

**Verification:** h = 0 → 4 mA ✓; h = 6 → 4 + 16 = 20 mA ✓.

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Capacitive Coaxial Probe — Sensitivity

**Given:** Coaxial probe with $R_i$ = 5 mm, $R_o$ = 10 mm, total length L = 2 m. Liquid: oil with ε_r = 2.5.

**Step 1 — Capacitance of empty (air-filled) probe:**

$$C_0 = \frac{2 \pi \varepsilon_0 L}{\ln(R_o/R_i)} = \frac{2 \pi (8.854 \times 10^{-12}) (2)}{\ln(10/5)}$$

$$\ln(2) = 0.6931$$

$$C_0 = \frac{2 \pi \cdot 8.854 \times 10^{-12} \cdot 2}{0.6931} = \frac{1.1123 \times 10^{-10}}{0.6931} \approx 1.605 \times 10^{-10} \, \text{F}$$

$$\boxed{C_0 \approx 160.5 \, \text{pF}}$$

**Step 2 — Capacitance when liquid fills to h = 1 m:**

The probe has 1 m of air (upper) and 1 m of oil (lower). Using the formula with ε_air = 1, ε_oil = 2.5:

$$C(h=1) = \frac{2 \pi \varepsilon_0}{\ln(R_o/R_i)} \left[ (L-h) \cdot 1 + h \cdot \varepsilon_r \right]$$

$$C(1) = \frac{2 \pi (8.854 \times 10^{-12})}{0.6931} \left[ (1)(1) + (1)(2.5) \right]$$

$$C(1) = \frac{5.5615 \times 10^{-11}}{0.6931} \cdot (3.5)$$

$$C(1) = (8.024 \times 10^{-11})(3.5) = 2.808 \times 10^{-10} \, \text{F}$$

$$\boxed{C(1) \approx 280.8 \, \text{pF}}$$

**Step 3 — Change in capacitance:**

$$\Delta C = C(1) - C_0 = 280.8 - 160.5 = 120.3 \, \text{pF}$$

**Step 4 — Sensitivity:**

$$S = \frac{\Delta C}{\Delta h} = \frac{120.3 \, \text{pF}}{1 \, \text{m}} = 120.3 \, \text{pF/m}$$

**Step 5 — Per-metre formula (general):**

$$S = \frac{2 \pi \varepsilon_0 (\varepsilon_r - 1)}{\ln(R_o/R_i)} = \frac{2 \pi (8.854 \times 10^{-12})(1.5)}{0.6931}$$

$$\boxed{S \approx 120.3 \, \text{pF/m} \quad \checkmark}$$

This **linear** sensitivity is exactly why capacitive probes are preferred for non-conductive liquids.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Parameter | Float Switch | Conductivity Probe | Hydrostatic Pressure | DP Cell | Load Cell (Displacer) | Capacitive |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Output type** | Discrete | Discrete | Continuous | Continuous | Continuous | Continuous |
| **Liquids** | All (clean) | Conductive only | All (clean) | All | All | Non-conductive (or insulated rod) |
| **Accuracy** | ±5–10 mm | ±5 mm | ±0.1–0.5 % FS | ±0.075 % FS | ±0.05 % FS | ±0.5 % FS |
| **Cost** | Very low | Low | Medium | Medium-high | High | Medium |
| **Max temp/press** | Moderate | Moderate | Sensor-limited | Sensor-limited | High | Sensor-limited |
| **Foam / Turbulence** | Affected | OK | OK | OK | OK | Affected (foam) |
| **Interface measurement** | No | No | Yes (with ρ calc) | Yes | Yes (with 2 displacers) | Limited |

### 3.5 Worked Numerical: Strain-Gauge Load Cell

**Given:** A cylindrical load cell has 4 strain gauges (R = 350 Ω each, GF = 2.0) in a full Wheatstone bridge. Excitation V_ex = 10 V. Force F = 5000 N produces strain ε = 500 × 10⁻⁶ on each gauge.

**Step 1 — Resistance change per gauge:**

$$\frac{\Delta R}{R} = GF \cdot \varepsilon = 2.0 \cdot (500 \times 10^{-6}) = 1 \times 10^{-3}$$

$$\Delta R = 350 \cdot 10^{-3} = 0.35 \, \Omega$$

**Step 2 — Two gauges in tension, two in compression:** In a full bridge, all four gauges contribute. With two gauges at +ΔR and two at −ΔR:

$$V_{\text{out}} = V_{\text{ex}} \cdot \frac{\Delta R}{R} = 10 \cdot 10^{-3} = 0.010 \, \text{V} = 10 \, \text{mV}$$

**Step 3 — Sensitivity:** 10 mV / 5000 N = **2 μV/N**.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Level Transducer Families

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph INPUT["Physical Input"]
        A1["Liquid Level Height h (m)"]
        A2["Liquid Density rho (kg/m3)"]
    end

    subgraph TRANSDUCERS["Level Transducer Families"]
        B1["Float Switch Discrete"]
        B2["Conductivity Probe Discrete"]
        B3["Hydrostatic Pressure Sensor"]
        B4["Differential Pressure DP Cell"]
        B5["Force Balance Load Cell with Displacer"]
        B6["Capacitive Coaxial Probe"]
    end

    subgraph SIGNAL["Signal Conditioning"]
        C1["Wheatstone Bridge Amplifier"]
        C2["AC Excitation and Rectification"]
        C3["Capacitance to Voltage Converter"]
        C4["Reed Contact Debounce"]
    end

    subgraph OUTPUT["Standardised Output"]
        D1["Relay Contact NO or NC"]
        D2["Binary 24V DC"]
        D3["4 to 20 mA Current Loop"]
        D4["0 to 10 V DC"]
    end

    A1 --> B1
    A1 --> B2
    A1 --> B3
    A1 --> B4
    A1 --> B5
    A1 --> B6
    A2 --> B3
    A2 --> B4
    A2 --> B5

    B1 --> C4
    B2 --> C2
    B3 --> C1
    B4 --> C1
    B5 --> C1
    B6 --> C3

    C1 --> D3
    C2 --> D2
    C3 --> D3
    C3 --> D4
    C4 --> D1
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram — Hydrostatic Pressure Level Sensor

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    TANK["Open Tank with Liquid Level h"] -->|hydrostatic P equals rho g h| SENSE["Piezoresistive or Strain Gauge Pressure Sensor at Bottom"]
    SENSE -->|millivolt signal| AMP["Instrumentation Amplifier"]
    AMP -->|conditioned voltage| ADC["ADC Converter"]
    ADC -->|digital code| MICRO["Microcontroller"]
    MICRO -->|PWM or DAC| VTOI["Voltage to Current Converter"]
    VTOI -->|4 to 20 mA| LOOP["Two Wire Current Loop to DCS or PLC"]
```

### 4.3 Block Diagram — Differential Pressure Bubbler System

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    REG["Pressure Regulator Set to 1.4 bar"] --> FLOW["Flow Restrictor Constant 0.5 L per min"]
    FLOW --> TUBE["Dip Tube with Outlet Submerged Below Liquid Level"]
    TUBE -->|back pressure equals rho g h| DPS["Differential Pressure Sensor"]
    DPS --> AMP["Signal Amplifier and Lineariser"]
    AMP --> OUT["4 to 20 mA Output"]
    BUBBLES["Air Bubbles Escape to Atmosphere"] -.-> TUBE
```

### 4.4 Block Diagram — Capacitive Level Probe and Signal Chain

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    PROBE["Coaxial Capacitive Probe in Tank"] --> OSC["High Frequency Sine Oscillator 100 kHz"]
    OSC --> DET["AC Bridge or Charge Amplifier"]
    DET --> RECT["Precision Rectifier and Filter"]
    RECT --> AMP["DC Amplifier with Linearisation"]
    AMP --> VOUT["0 to 10 V or 4 to 20 mA Output"]
```

### 4.5 Cross-Sectional Schematic — Float Switch (ASCII Representation)

```
   +--------------------------+   <-- Tank roof
   |                          |
   |       +--- Stem --------+|
   |       |                 ||
   |       |                 ||  <-- Reed switch #1 (HIGH level)
   |       |                 ||
   |       |                 ||  
   |       |                 ||  <-- Reed switch #2 (LOW level)
   |       |                 ||
   |   ~~~~~                 ||  <-- Liquid surface (varying)
   |   |   |                 ||
   |   | F |   <-- Float     ||
   |   |   |                 ||
   |   ~~~~~                 ||
   |                          |
   +--------------------------+   <-- Tank bottom
```

### 4.6 Wheatstone Bridge Inside a Load Cell

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    VEX["Vex 10V DC Excitation"] --- nodeA["Node A Top"]
    nodeA --- R1["R1 Strain Gauge Top Left"]
    nodeA --- R3["R3 Strain Gauge Top Right"]
    R1 --- nodeB["Node B Bottom Left"]
    R3 --- nodeD["Node D Bottom Right"]
    nodeB --- R2["R2 Strain Gauge Bottom Left"]
    nodeD --- R4["R4 Strain Gauge Bottom Right"]
    R2 --- GND1["Ground"]
    R4 --- GND2["Ground"]
    nodeB --- AMP["Instrumentation Amplifier Negative Input"]
    nodeD --- AMP2["Instrumentation Amplifier Positive Input"]
    AMP --- AMP2
    AMP2 --> VOUT["Vout in mV Proportional to Force"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> In a load cell, **R1 and R4** are mounted along the axis of strain (they experience +ε, so R increases), while **R2 and R3** are mounted transversely (they experience −ε via Poisson contraction, so R decreases). This quadruples the bridge sensitivity compared to a single active gauge.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
Distinguish between **continuous** and **discrete** level measurement. Give one industrial example of each.

**Model Answer:**
- **Continuous level measurement** produces an output signal proportional to the *exact* height of the material at every instant; e.g., a **differential pressure transmitter** mounted at the bottom of a crude-oil storage tank giving 4–20 mA.
- **Discrete (point) level measurement** produces a *binary* ON/OFF signal only when the material crosses a preset threshold; e.g., a **magnetic float switch** on an overhead water tank that triggers a pump when level is low.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
State the principle of the **bubbler system** used for level measurement. Why is it preferred for corrosive liquids?

**Model Answer:**
- **Principle:** A low, constant flow of clean air or inert gas is forced through a dip tube whose outlet is submerged in the tank. The **back-pressure** required to push the gas out equals the **hydrostatic head** of the liquid above the outlet: $P_b = \rho g h$.
- **Why preferred for corrosive liquids:** The sensing element (pressure transmitter) is mounted *outside* the tank and contacts only the purge gas — never the corrosive process fluid. This allows use of standard (non-exotic) transmitters even with strong acids, slurries, or sewage.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

#### QUESTION A (Choice 1) — Pressure and DP Methods

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]**

**(a)** Derive the relationship between liquid level $h$ and the output current $I_{out}$ of a hydrostatic pressure transmitter calibrated for a 4–20 mA current loop. Assume the sensor is mounted at the tank bottom and the liquid is water (ρ = 1000 kg/m³). Show that for $h_{max}$ = 4 m the transfer equation is $I_{out} = 4 + 4h$ mA.

[7 marks]

**Model Solution:**

The hydrostatic pressure at depth h is given by Pascal's Law:

$$P(h) = \rho \cdot g \cdot h = 1000 \cdot 9.81 \cdot h = 9810 \, h \;\; \text{Pa}$$

Maximum pressure at $h = h_{max} = 4$ m:

$$P_{\max} = 9810 \cdot 4 = 39240 \, \text{Pa}$$

The fraction of full-scale input:

$$f(h) = \frac{P(h)}{P_{\max}} = \frac{9810 h}{39240} = 0.25 h$$

Mapping to the 4–20 mA current loop using the standard linear conversion:

$$I_{\text{out}} = 4 + 16 \cdot f(h) = 4 + 16 \cdot (0.25 h)$$

$$I_{\text{out}} = 4 + 4h \;\; \text{(mA)} \quad \blacksquare$$

**Verification:**
- h = 0 m → I_out = 4 mA ✓
- h = 4 m → I_out = 4 + 16 = 20 mA ✓

**(b)** A closed cylindrical tank (height 8 m, diameter 4 m) contains a chemical with density 1200 kg/m³ under a vapour pressure of 50 kPa (gauge). A DP cell is mounted with HP tap 0.6 m above the bottom and LP tap connected to the vapour space. Compute the **suppression pressure** and the **DP cell span** for a 4–20 mA transmitter mapped to true level 0–8 m. Take g = 9.81 m/s².

[7 marks]

**Model Solution:**

The pressure at the HP tap when the true liquid level is h is:

$$P_{\text{HP}} = P_{\text{vapour}} + \rho g (h + 0.6)$$

The pressure at the LP tap (vapour space):

$$P_{\text{LP}} = P_{\text{vapour}}$$

Differential pressure:

$$\Delta P(h) = P_{\text{HP}} - P_{\text{LP}} = \rho g (h + 0.6)$$

At true zero (h = 0):

$$\Delta P(0) = 1200 \cdot 9.81 \cdot 0.6 = 7063.2 \, \text{Pa} \approx 7.06 \, \text{kPa}$$

This is the **suppression pressure** that the transmitter electronics must subtract.

At true full (h = 8 m):

$$\Delta P(8) = 1200 \cdot 9.81 \cdot 8.6 = 101238.72 \, \text{Pa} \approx 101.24 \, \text{kPa}$$

**Span:**

$$\text{Span} = \Delta P(8) - \Delta P(0) = 101238.72 - 7063.2 = 94175.52 \, \text{Pa} \approx 94.18 \, \text{kPa}$$

$$\boxed{\text{Suppression} \approx 7.06 \, \text{kPa}; \quad \text{Span} \approx 94.18 \, \text{kPa}}$$

---

#### QUESTION B (Choice 2 — Internal Choice) — Capacitive & Force Methods

**[KTU University Exam – May 2023, Modified]**

**(a)** With a neat sketch, explain the working principle of a **capacitive level sensor** for a non-conductive liquid. Derive the expression for change in capacitance when liquid of relative permittivity ε_r rises to a height h in a coaxial probe of inner radius $R_i$, outer radius $R_o$, and total length L.

[7 marks]

**Model Solution:**

A coaxial capacitive level probe consists of two concentric metal cylinders separated by an insulating support. The inner rod is the "high" plate; the outer sheath is the "ground" plate. The dielectric between them is air when empty, and partially replaced by the process liquid when the tank fills.

When empty (air, ε_air ≈ 1):

$$C_0 = \frac{2 \pi \varepsilon_0 L}{\ln(R_o/R_i)}$$

When the liquid of permittivity ε_r rises to height h, the upper (L − h) portion remains air-filled and the lower h portion is liquid-filled. The two sections act as **capacitors in parallel** (same voltage across both):

$$C(h) = \frac{2 \pi \varepsilon_0 (L - h) \varepsilon_{\text{air}}}{\ln(R_o/R_i)} + \frac{2 \pi \varepsilon_0 h \varepsilon_r}{\ln(R_o/R_i)}$$

Simplifying:

$$C(h) = \frac{2 \pi \varepsilon_0}{\ln(R_o/R_i)} \left[ (L - h) + h \varepsilon_r \right]$$

Change in capacitance:

$$\Delta C = C(h) - C_0 = \frac{2 \pi \varepsilon_0 h}{\ln(R_o/R_i)} (\varepsilon_r - 1)$$

Since (ε_r − 1) and ln(R_o/R_i) are constants for a given probe and liquid, **ΔC is linearly proportional to h**. This linear ΔC is converted to a standard 4–20 mA output by a capacitance-to-voltage converter followed by a V-to-I stage.

$$\boxed{\Delta C = \frac{2 \pi \varepsilon_0 (\varepsilon_r - 1)}{\ln(R_o / R_i)} \cdot h}$$

**(b)** A cylindrical displacer of diameter 25 mm and length 400 mm is suspended from a strain-gauge load cell in a tank. The displacer material has density 2400 kg/m³. Calculate the change in apparent weight when the tank is gradually filled with a liquid of density 900 kg/m³ until the displacer is fully submerged. Take g = 9.81 m/s².

[7 marks]

**Model Solution:**

Volume of the displacer:

$$V = \frac{\pi}{4} d^2 \cdot L = \frac{\pi}{4} (0.025)^2 (0.4) = \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot 6.25 \times 10^{-4} \cdot 0.4$$

$$V = 1.9635 \times 10^{-4} \, \text{m}^3$$

Dry weight (in air):

$$W_{\text{dry}} = \rho_d \cdot V \cdot g = 2400 \cdot 1.9635 \times 10^{-4} \cdot 9.81$$

$$W_{\text{dry}} = 4.6226 \, \text{N}$$

Buoyant force when fully submerged:

$$F_b = \rho_l \cdot V \cdot g = 900 \cdot 1.9635 \times 10^{-4} \cdot 9.81 = 1.7335 \, \text{N}$$

Apparent weight when fully submerged:

$$W_{\text{submerged}} = W_{\text{dry}} - F_b = 4.6226 - 1.7335 = 2.8891 \, \text{N}$$

Change in apparent weight:

$$\Delta W = W_{\text{dry}} - W_{\text{submerged}} = 1.7335 \, \text{N}$$

$$\boxed{\Delta W \approx 1.73 \, \text{N}}$$

If the load cell produces 2 mV/V at full buoyant loss, the corresponding output for a 10 V excitation is:

$$V_{\text{out,FS}} = 2 \times 10^{-3} \cdot 10 = 0.020 \, \text{V} = 20 \, \text{mV}$$

The sensitivity (mV per metre of level change) depends on the tank cross-section, which is not specified here.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Level-Transducer Questions:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the live-zero** — Always begin current-loop calculations from 4 mA, NOT 0 mA. Examiners deduct a full mark for this.
> 2. **Confusing "suppression" with "elevation"** — Suppression is when the sensor is mounted *above* the true zero; elevation is when mounted *below*. The math sign changes.
> 3. **Using ρ = 1000 kg/m³ for non-water liquids** — Always extract density from the problem statement. Diesel ≈ 850, crude ≈ 900, sulphuric acid ≈ 1840.
> 4. **Wrong dielectric assignment** — In capacitive probes with **conductive** liquids, the liquid is the *second electrode*, not the dielectric. The insulator coating is the dielectric.
> 5. **Not verifying boundary values** — End every derivation by plugging h = 0 and h = h_max to check the output is 4 mA and 20 mA respectively.
> 6. **Unit mistakes** — Pa vs kPa vs bar: 1 bar = 100 kPa = 10⁵ Pa. Mixing units mid-calculation is a 1-mark loss.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Discrete level** = point switches (float, conductivity, vibrating fork, optical). Output is binary.
- **Continuous level** = analogue output proportional to height (pressure, DP, force, capacitive, ultrasonic, radar).
- **Hydrostatic equation** $P = \rho g h$ is the single most important formula — it underpins pressure, DP, bubbler, and even displacer methods.
- **Differential pressure** uses two taps: HP at bottom, LP at top (vapour or atmosphere). Vapour pressure cancels in closed tanks.
- **Suppression** = subtract a fixed pressure when sensor is above true zero. **Elevation** = add a fixed pressure when sensor is below.
- **Bubbler system** uses purge-gas back-pressure — ideal for corrosive / slurry / hot liquids because the process fluid never touches the sensor.
- **Load cell / displacer** uses Archimedes' buoyant force reduction on a submerged cylinder, sensed by a Wheatstone-bridge strain-gauge assembly.
- **Gauge factor** $GF = (\Delta R / R) / \varepsilon \approx 2$ for metal foil; 100–200 for semiconductors.
- **Full-bridge load cell** with 4 active gauges gives 4× the sensitivity of a single gauge.
- **Capacitive level sensor** relies on ΔC being linear in h: $\Delta C = (2\pi\varepsilon_0(\varepsilon_r - 1)/\ln(R_o/R_i)) \cdot h$.
- For **conductive liquids** in capacitive probes, the liquid acts as the second electrode (rod must be PTFE-coated).
- Standard output: **4–20 mA** with 4 mA live-zero for cable-fault detection.
- **Archimedes' principle** $F_b = \rho V g$ governs float switches and displacer systems alike.
- Always state and verify the **transfer equation** $I_{out} = 4 + 16(h/h_{max})$ — boundary values must match.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Inductive Transducer
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBRT303 - SENSORS AND ACTUATORS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Inductive Transducer

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# ⚡ Inductive Transducer — Section 1: Core Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Inductive Transducer (Definition as per KTU 2024 Syllabus):**
> An *inductive transducer* is a passive electromechanical conversion device that translates a **non-electrical measurand** (displacement, force, pressure, vibration) into a measurable change in **self-inductance (L)**, **mutual inductance (M)**, or **reluctance (ℛ)** of one or more coils, in accordance with the principles of **Faraday's Law of Electromagnetic Induction** and the magnetic circuit laws of a ferromagnetic core.

The measurand alters the **magnetic flux linkage (λ)** of the coil assembly, and the resulting inductance variation is converted into an output voltage or current signal through a suitable **signal conditioning bridge** (e.g., Wheatstone bridge, op-amp carrier amplifier).

### 1.1.1 Physical Foundation

The operating principle rests on three cornerstone equations:

| S.No | Physical Law | Governing Equation |
|------|--------------|---------------------|
| 1 | Faraday's Law of Induction | $e = -N \dfrac{d\Phi}{dt}$ |
| 2 | Self-Inductance of a Coil | $L = \dfrac{N^{2}}{\Re} = \dfrac{\mu N^{2} A}{l}$ |
| 3 | Magnetic Ohm's Law (Reluctance) | $\Re = \dfrac{l}{\mu A}$ |

where $N$ = number of turns, $\Phi$ = magnetic flux (in **Weber, Wb**), $\mu$ = permeability (in **Henry/m, H/m**), $A$ = cross-sectional area (in **m²**), and $l$ = mean magnetic path length (in **m**).

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Iron Core Magician"

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Plain-English Analogy (for first-time learners):**
> Imagine you are holding a **wooden dowel** wrapped with copper wire and connected to a battery. The current is small because wood is a *poor* magnetic conductor. Now, **slide an iron rod** inside the coil. Suddenly, the current jumps because iron is an *excellent* magnetic conductor — it "concentrates" the magnetic field.
>
> In an **inductive transducer**, the *position* of the iron core (or the *proximity* of a metal target) plays exactly this role. Whenever the measurand moves the core, the **reluctance changes**, the **inductance changes**, and the **output signal changes** in a predictable way. The transducer is essentially a *mechanical-to-magnetic-to-electrical* signal converter.

### 1.2.1 Classification of Inductive Transducers

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Classification:**
> 1. **Self-Inductance Type (Variable Reluctance Transducer — VRT)** — measurand changes the reluctance of a single coil.
> 2. **Mutual-Inductance Type** — measurand changes the coupling between two or more coils (e.g., **LVDT**, **RVDT**).
> 3. **Eddy Current Transducer** — measurand alters eddy current losses in a target conductor.

Each sub-type will be developed in **Section 2** with full theoretical depth.

---

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants (Highlighted)

- **Permeability of free space:** $\mu_{0} = 4\pi \times 10^{-7} \; \mathbf{H/m}$
- **Relative permeability of soft iron:** $\mu_{r} \approx 2000$ to **6000**
- **Permeability of air ≈ vacuum:** $\mu_{0} = 4\pi \times 10^{-7} \; \mathbf{H/m}$
- **Magnetic flux unit:** **Weber (Wb)** = **Volt·second (V·s)**
- **Inductance unit:** **Henry (H)** = **Weber/Ampere (Wb/A)**
- **Standard LVDT excitation frequency:** **1 kHz to 10 kHz** (typically 2.5 kHz or 5 kHz)
- **Typical LVDT sensitivity:** **0.1 to 40 V/mm** (varies with excitation amplitude)

---

## 1.4 Why Inductive Transducers Matter (Engineering Relevance)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Inductive transducers are the **backbone of industrial displacement metrology** because they offer:
> - **Frictionless, contactless operation** → infinite mechanical life
> - **High resolution** (sub-micron, down to **0.1 µm** for LVDTs)
> - **Excellent linearity** (≤ ±0.25% of full scale for grade-A LVDTs)
> - **Robustness in harsh environments** (oil, dust, high temperature up to **650 °C** for high-temp LVDTs)
> - **Bidirectional output** (sign of output = direction of displacement)
>
> They are deployed in **CNC machines, hydraulic cylinder position feedback, aircraft flight-control surface monitoring, and structural-health vibration pickups**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# ⚙️ Inductive Transducer — Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & Formula Sheet

## 2.1 General Operating Principle

An inductive transducer functions by converting the **mechanical input $x$** (displacement, force, pressure) into a **change in inductance $\Delta L$**. The relationship can be written generically as:

$$
L(x) = L_{0} + \Delta L(x)
$$

where $L_{0}$ = nominal (rest) inductance and $\Delta L(x)$ is the measurand-induced variation. The inductance change is then converted to voltage using either:

1. **A.C. bridge** (Wheatstone or Maxwell bridge)
2. **Oscillator-based converter** (L-C or L-R oscillator where frequency $\omega$ depends on $L$)
3. **Differential output circuit** (LVDT phase-sensitive demodulator)

---

## 2.2 Type 1 — Self-Inductance / Variable Reluctance Transducer (VRT)

### 2.2.1 Construction

A **ferromagnetic core (armature)** is mechanically coupled to the measurand. The core moves *axially* or *rotationally* inside a stationary coil. As the core moves, the **air gap** in the magnetic circuit changes, hence reluctance changes.

### 2.2.2 Working Equation

For a single coil with a magnetic circuit consisting of an iron path of length $l_{i}$, area $A_{i}$, and an air gap of length $x$ (the measurand) and area $A_{g}$:

$$
\Re(x) = \frac{l_{i}}{\mu_{r} \mu_{0} A_{i}} + \frac{2x}{\mu_{0} A_{g}}
$$

Since the iron path reluctance is small compared to the air gap, it is neglected. The inductance is:

$$
L(x) = \frac{N^{2}}{\Re(x)} = \frac{N^{2} \mu_{0} A_{g}}{2x}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical observation:** $L \propto \dfrac{1}{x}$ → **inverse (non-linear) relationship** between $L$ and air gap. This makes the simple single-coil VRT inherently non-linear. To linearize, **differential pair construction** is used (see 2.2.3).

### 2.2.3 Differential Variable Reluctance Transducer

Two identical coils share a common armature. The armature is centered by a spring at $x = 0$. When displaced, one air gap **decreases** (say $x_{1} = x_{0} - \Delta x$) and the other **increases** ($x_{2} = x_{0} + \Delta x$).

$$
L_{1} = \frac{N^{2} \mu_{0} A_{g}}{2(x_{0} - \Delta x)} \qquad L_{2} = \frac{N^{2} \mu_{0} A_{g}}{2(x_{0} + \Delta x)}
$$

The bridge output is proportional to:

$$
\Delta L = L_{1} - L_{2} = \frac{N^{2} \mu_{0} A_{g}}{2} \left( \frac{1}{x_{0} - \Delta x} - \frac{1}{x_{0} + \Delta x} \right)
$$

$$
\Delta L = \frac{N^{2} \mu_{0} A_{g}}{2} \cdot \frac{2 \Delta x}{x_{0}^{2} - \Delta x^{2}}
$$

For small $\Delta x \ll x_{0}$: $\Delta L \approx \dfrac{N^{2} \mu_{0} A_{g} \, \Delta x}{x_{0}^{2}}$ → **linearised**.

### 2.2.4 Sensitivity of Differential VRT

$$
K_{VRT} = \frac{\Delta L}{\Delta x} = \frac{N^{2} \mu_{0} A_{g}}{x_{0}^{2}} \quad \left[\text{H/m}\right]
$$

---

## 2.3 Type 2 — LVDT (Linear Variable Differential Transformer)

### 2.3.1 Construction

The **LVDT** consists of:

- **1 Primary coil (P)** — centrally located, energized by a.c. excitation (typically 2–5 kHz sinusoidal).
- **2 Secondary coils (S₁, S₂)** — wound on either side of the primary, connected in **series-opposition** (i.e., their EMFs subtract).
- **1 Soft-iron ferromagnetic core** — attached to the measurand via a non-contact probe.
- **A cylindrical housing** — provides mechanical rigidity and electromagnetic shielding.

### 2.3.2 Working

When the core is at the **null position** (centered), the induced EMFs $E_{s1}$ and $E_{s2}$ are equal in magnitude and opposite in phase → **net output = 0 V**.

When the core moves **towards S₁**:
- Coupling between P and S₁ **increases** → $E_{s1}$ **rises**
- Coupling between P and S₂ **decreases** → $E_{s2}$ **falls**
- Net output $E_{o} = E_{s1} - E_{s2}$ → **positive**, in phase with primary excitation.

When the core moves **towards S₂**:
- Net output $E_{o} = E_{s1} - E_{s2}$ → **negative**, in phase opposition (180° phase shift).

> [!NOTE]
> The **magnitude** of $E_{o}$ is proportional to displacement $\Delta x$, and the **phase** indicates direction. This is the famous "**phase-sensitive detection**" capability of the LVDT.

### 2.3.3 Output Equation of LVDT

For a primary excitation $V_{p} \sin(\omega t)$ with mutual inductances $M_{1}$ and $M_{2}$:

$$
E_{s1} = j \omega M_{1} I_{p} = j \omega M_{1} \cdot \frac{V_{p} \sin(\omega t)}{j \omega L_{p}} = \frac{M_{1}}{L_{p}} V_{p} \sin(\omega t)
$$

$$
E_{s2} = \frac{M_{2}}{L_{p}} V_{p} \sin(\omega t)
$$

Differential output:

$$
E_{o} = E_{s1} - E_{s2} = \frac{V_{p}}{L_{p}} (M_{1} - M_{2}) \sin(\omega t)
$$

Assuming linear variation of mutual inductance with displacement $\pm x$:

$$
M_{1} = M_{0} + k x \qquad M_{2} = M_{0} - k x
$$

where $k = \dfrac{dM}{dx}$ is the LVDT sensitivity coefficient. Substituting:

$$
\boxed{E_{o} = \frac{2 k V_{p}}{L_{p}} \cdot x \cdot \sin(\omega t)}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key result:** $E_{o} \propto x$ → **linear relationship** between output voltage and core displacement. The amplitude sensitivity is:

$$
S_{LVDT} = \frac{E_{o,\text{peak}}}{x} = \frac{2 k V_{p}}{L_{p}} \quad \left[\text{V/m}\right]
$$

### 2.3.4 LVDT Characteristics (KTU Favourite Diagram)

| Region | Behaviour |
|--------|-----------|
| **Null position (x = 0)** | $E_{s1} = E_{s2}$ → output = 0 |
| **Linear range (approx ±5 mm)** | $E_{o} \propto x$, very linear (≤ 0.25% nonlinearity) |
| **Beyond linear range** | Core approaches end of coil, $M$ saturates → curve flattens |
| **Overshoot region** | Output drops to zero again (the "blind spot" of LVDT) |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Valuation Pitfall:** Many students forget that the **LVDT is linear only within the central ±5 mm range** — outside this, the curve is parabolic. Marks are awarded for stating *both* the linear range and the *non-linearity error in %*.

---

## 2.4 Type 3 — RVDT (Rotary Variable Differential Transformer)

The **RVDT** is the rotary analogue of the LVDT. The cam-shaped core rotates by an angle $\theta$ (typically up to ±40°). Construction is identical to LVDT except the core rotates instead of translating.

The output is:

$$
E_{o}(\theta) = K \cdot V_{p} \cdot \theta \cdot \sin(\omega t) \qquad \text{for } \vert \theta \vert \leq 40^{\circ}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> RVDTs are used for **angular position feedback** in servo systems, valve actuators, and robotic joints.

---

## 2.5 Type 4 — Eddy Current Transducer

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Working Principle (KTU 2024 syllabus high-priority):**
> When a high-frequency **a.c. excitation** is passed through a coil, an alternating magnetic field is produced. If a **conductive target** is brought near the coil, **eddy currents** are induced in the target by Faraday's law (Lenz's law). These eddy currents oppose the original flux (Lenz's law) and effectively **reduce the coil's inductance** and increase its **resistance**.

The impedance of the eddy-current coil is:

$$
Z = R + j \omega L = R_{0} + R_{e} + j \omega (L_{0} - L_{e})
$$

where $R_{e}$ and $L_{e}$ are the eddy-current-induced resistance and inductance change. The output (impedance magnitude) is a function of the **target-to-coil gap $d$**:

$$
Z(d) = f\!\left(\frac{1}{d}\right)
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key feature:** Eddy-current transducers are **contactless** and **non-magnetic-target-compatible** — they work on aluminium, copper, brass, and even stainless steel. Used for **turbine blade tip clearance**, **vibration monitoring**, and **crack detection**.

---

## 2.6 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Formula Table for KTU University Exam:**

| S.No | Quantity | Formula | Unit | Remarks |
|------|----------|---------|------|---------|
| 1 | Self-inductance of coil | $L = \dfrac{N^{2} \mu A}{l}$ | **H** | $N$ = turns, $A$ = area, $l$ = path length |
| 2 | Reluctance of magnetic path | $\Re = \dfrac{l}{\mu A}$ | **A-t/Wb** | Magnetic Ohm's law |
| 3 | VRT inductance (air-gap) | $L(x) = \dfrac{N^{2} \mu_{0} A_{g}}{2x}$ | **H** | $x$ = air-gap length |
| 4 | VRT sensitivity (differential) | $K_{VRT} = \dfrac{N^{2} \mu_{0} A_{g}}{x_{0}^{2}}$ | **H/m** | Linearized for small $\Delta x$ |
| 5 | LVDT output | $E_{o} = \dfrac{2k V_{p} x}{L_{p}} \sin(\omega t)$ | **V** | Phase $\Rightarrow$ direction |
| 6 | LVDT sensitivity | $S = \dfrac{2k V_{p}}{L_{p}}$ | **V/m** | Typical 0.1–40 V/mm |
| 7 | Mutual inductance | $M = k \sqrt{L_{1} L_{2}}$ | **H** | $k$ = coupling coefficient, $0 \leq k \leq 1$ |
| 8 | Eddy-current coil impedance | $Z = R + j\omega L$ | **Ω** | Depends on target gap $d$ |
| 9 | Inductance-bridge output | $E_{o} = \dfrac{E_{in}}{4} \cdot \dfrac{\Delta R}{R}$ | **V** | For small unbalance in bridge |
| 10 | Total reluctance (series) | $\Re_{T} = \Re_{1} + \Re_{2} + \Re_{3} + \dots$ | **A-t/Wb** | Series magnetic circuit |

> [!NOTE]
> **Units to memorize:** $1 \; \mathbf{H} = 1 \; \mathbf{Wb/A} = 1 \; \mathbf{V \cdot s/A}$

---

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application Domain | Inductive Transducer Used | Function |
|--------------------|---------------------------|----------|
| CNC Machining | LVDT | Tool/workpiece position feedback |
| Aircraft | RVDT, LVDT | Flight-control surface angle sensing |
| Hydraulics | LVDT | Piston position in cylinders |

# 🔢 Inductive Transducer — Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> **Comprehensive derivations required for KTU University Exam (Part B, 14-mark questions). Each algebraic step is shown in full — no shortcuts.**

---

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Inductance of a Coil with Variable Air Gap

### Problem Statement
A coil of $N$ turns is wound around a magnetic core of cross-sectional area $A$ and mean iron path length $l_{i}$. The core has an air gap of length $x$ (the measurand). The iron has relative permeability $\mu_{r}$ and the gap has area $A_{g}$. Derive the inductance $L(x)$.

### Step 1 — Identify the magnetic circuit

The magnetic circuit consists of two series elements:
- The **iron path** of length $l_{i}$, area $A_{i}$, permeability $\mu_{r}\mu_{0}$.
- The **air gap** of length $x$ (we use $2x$ if there are two gaps — one at each end of the armature; we use single-gap here for simplicity and double it later if needed), area $A_{g}$, permeability $\mu_{0}$.

### Step 2 — Write the reluctance of each section

Iron reluctance:

$$
\Re_{i} = \frac{l_{i}}{\mu_{r} \mu_{0} A_{i}}
$$

Air-gap reluctance:

$$
\Re_{g} = \frac{x}{\mu_{0} A_{g}}
$$

### Step 3 — Total reluctance (series magnetic circuit)

$$
\Re_{T}(x) = \Re_{i} + \Re_{g} = \frac{l_{i}}{\mu_{r} \mu_{0} A_{i}} + \frac{x}{\mu_{0} A_{g}}
$$

### Step 4 — Apply the inductance formula

$$
L(x) = \frac{N^{2}}{\Re_{T}(x)} = \frac{N^{2}}{\dfrac{l_{i}}{\mu_{r} \mu_{0} A_{i}} + \dfrac{x}{\mu_{0} A_{g}}}
$$

### Step 5 — Simplification (neglect iron reluctance if $\mu_r \gg 1$)

For $\mu_{r} \mu_{0} \gg \mu_{0}$ (typical soft iron has $\mu_{r} \approx 5000$), the iron path reluctance is **negligible** compared to the air-gap reluctance. Hence:

$$
\Re_{T}(x) \approx \frac{x}{\mu_{0} A_{g}}
$$

$$
\boxed{L(x) \approx \frac{N^{2} \mu_{0} A_{g}}{x}}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **For a single air gap the result is $L = N^{2}\mu_{0} A_{g}/x$**, and **for two equal gaps (differential VRT)** it becomes $L = N^{2}\mu_{0} A_{g}/(2x)$, as used in Section 2.2.2.

### Step 6 — Sensitivity derivation

Differentiate $L$ with respect to $x$:

$$
\frac{dL}{dx} = -\frac{N^{2} \mu_{0} A_{g}}{x^{2}}
$$

The **magnitude of sensitivity** is:

$$
\left\vert \frac{dL}{dx} \right\vert = \frac{N^{2} \mu_{0} A_{g}}{x^{2}}
$$

This confirms the **inverse-square** relationship of single-coil VRT sensitivity with air gap.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: LVDT Output Voltage — From First Principles

### Problem Statement
An LVDT has a primary coil with inductance $L_{p}$ and self-inductance-based current $I_{p}(t) = \dfrac{V_{p}}{j\omega L_{p}} \sin(\omega t)$. The two secondary coils have mutual inductances $M_{1}$ and $M_{2}$ with the primary. Derive the differential output $E_{o} = E_{s1} - E_{s2}$ as a function of core displacement $x$.

### Step 1 — Write primary current phasor

$$
I_{p} = \frac{V_{p} \angle 0^{\circ}}{j \omega L_{p}} = \frac{V_{p}}{\omega L_{p}} \angle -90^{\circ}
$$

For the time-domain expression:

$$
i_{p}(t) = I_{p,m} \sin(\omega t) = \frac{V_{p,m}}{\omega L_{p}} \sin(\omega t)
$$

### Step 2 — Induced EMF in secondary 1 (Faraday's law)

$$
e_{s1}(t) = -M_{1} \frac{di_{p}}{dt} = -M_{1} \cdot \frac{V_{p,m}}{L_{p}} \cos(\omega t)
$$

### Step 3 — Induced EMF in secondary 2 (same form, different M)

$$
e_{s2}(t) = -M_{2} \cdot \frac{V_{p,m}}{L_{p}} \cos(\omega t)
$$

### Step 4 — Differential output (series opposition)

$$
e_{o}(t) = e_{s1}(t) - e_{s2}(t) = -\frac{V_{p,m}}{L_{p}} (M_{1} - M_{2}) \cos(\omega t)
$$

### Step 5 — Express mutual inductances in terms of $x$

For a symmetric LVDT, when the core is at null position, $M_{1} = M_{2} = M_{0}$. When displaced by $+x$:

$$
M_{1} = M_{0} + k x \qquad M_{2} = M_{0} - k x
$$

Therefore:

$$
M_{1} - M_{2} = 2 k x
$$

### Step 6 — Final output equation

$$
\boxed{e_{o}(t) = -\frac{2 k V_{p,m}}{L_{p}} \cdot x \cdot \cos(\omega t) = \frac{2 k V_{p,m}}{L_{p}} \cdot x \cdot \sin(\omega t \pm 180^{\circ})}
$$

**Magnitude (RMS):**

$$
E_{o,rms} = \frac{2 k V_{p,rms}}{L_{p}} \cdot x
$$

This confirms **linear dependence** on $x$.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Sensitivity of a Differential VRT

### Problem Statement
A differential VRT has two coils with $N$ turns each, air-gap area $A_{g}$, and the armature centred with nominal gap $x_{0}$ on each side. The armature displaces by $\Delta x$. Find the net inductance change $\Delta L = L_{1} - L_{2}$.

### Step 1 — Inductance of coil 1 (gap reduces to $x_{0} - \Delta x$)

$$
L_{1} = \frac{N^{2} \mu_{0} A_{g}}{2 (x_{0} - \Delta x)}
$$

### Step 2 — Inductance of coil 2 (gap increases to $x_{0} + \Delta x$)

$$
L_{2} = \frac{N^{2} \mu_{0} A_{g}}{2 (x_{0} + \Delta x)}
$$

### Step 3 — Take the difference

$$
\Delta L = L_{1} - L_{2} = \frac{N^{2} \mu_{0} A_{g}}{2} \left( \frac{1}{x_{0} - \Delta x} - \frac{1}{x_{0} + \Delta x} \right)
$$

### Step 4 — Common denominator

$$
\Delta L = \frac{N^{2} \mu_{0} A_{g}}{2} \cdot \frac{(x_{0} + \Delta x) - (x_{0} - \Delta x)}{(x_{0} - \Delta x)(x_{0} + \Delta x)}
$$

### Step 5 — Simplify numerator and denominator

$$
\Delta L = \frac{N^{2} \mu_{0} A_{g}}{2} \cdot \frac{2 \Delta x}{x_{0}^{2} - \Delta x^{2}}
$$

### Step 6 — Final result

$$
\boxed{\Delta L = \frac{N^{2} \mu_{0} A_{g} \cdot \Delta x}{x_{0}^{2} - \Delta x^{2}}}
$$

### Step 7 — Linearized form (for $\Delta x \ll x_0$)

When $\Delta x^{2} \ll x_{0}^{2}$:

$$
\Delta L \approx \frac{N^{2} \mu_{0} A_{g}}{x_{0}^{2}} \cdot \Delta x
$$

Sensitivity:

$$
K = \frac{d(\Delta L)}{d(\Delta x)} = \frac{N^{2} \mu_{0} A_{g}}{x_{0}^{2}} \quad \left[\frac{\text{H}}{\text{m}}\right]
$$

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Numerical Example — LVDT Sensitivity Calculation

### Problem Statement
An LVDT has the following specifications:
- Primary inductance: $L_{p} = 10 \; \text{mH}$
- Coupling-coefficient slope: $k = 200 \; \mu\text{H/mm}$
- Primary excitation: $V_{p} = 5 \; \text{V}$ (RMS) at 2.5 kHz
- Core displacement: $x = 2 \; \text{mm}$

Calculate the differential output voltage (RMS).

### Step 1 — Convert units

$$
k = 200 \;\mu\text{H/mm} = 200 \times 10^{-6} \;\text{H/m} = 2 \times 10^{-4} \;\text{H/m}
$$

$$
L_{p} = 10 \;\text{mH} = 10^{-2} \;\text{H}
$$

$$
x = 2 \;\text{mm} = 2 \times 10^{-3} \;\text{m}
$$

### Step 2 — Apply the LVDT output formula

$$
E_{o,rms} = \frac{2 k V_{p,rms}}{L_{p}} \cdot x
$$

### Step 3 — Substitute numerical values

$$
E_{o,rms} = \frac{2 \times (2 \times 10^{-4}) \times 5}{10^{-2}} \times (2 \times 10^{-3})
$$

### Step 4 — Step-by-step evaluation

Numerator:

$$
2 \times (2 \times 10^{-4}) \times 5 = 20 \times 10^{-4} = 2 \times 10^{-3}
$$

Divide by $L_p$:

$$
\frac{2 \times 10^{-3}}{10^{-2}} = 0.2
$$

Multiply by $x$:

$$
0.2 \times (2 \times 10^{-3}) = 4 \times 10^{-4} \;\text{V} = 0.4 \;\text{mV}
$$

### Step 5 — Final result

$$
\boxed{E_{o,rms} = 0.4 \;\text{mV}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Verification by sensitivity formula:** $S = \dfrac{2 k V_p}{L_p} = \dfrac{2 \times 2 \times 10^{-4} \times 5}{10^{-2}} = 0.2 \;\text{V/m}$. Then $E_o = S \cdot x = 0.2 \times 2 \times 10^{-3} = 4 \times 10^{-4} \;\text{V}$ ✓.

---

## 3.5 Derivation 5: Eddy-Current Transducer Output

### Problem Statement
A flat circular coil of radius $r$, $N$ turns, is excited by a high-frequency a.c. source at frequency $f$. A flat aluminum target is at distance $d$ from the coil. Show qualitatively how the coil impedance varies with $d$.

### Step 1 — Equivalent circuit

The coil sees:
- Self-inductance $L_{0}$ (coil alone)
- Self-resistance $R_{0}$ (coil winding)
- A "reflected" impedance $Z_{r}$ from eddy currents in the target

$$
Z_{total} = R_{0} + j\omega L_{0} + Z_{r}
$$

### Step 2 — Reflected impedance

The eddy currents in the target create a counter-flux (Lenz's law) which is equivalent to a shorted secondary turn coupled to the coil. The mutual inductance is $M$.

$$
Z_{r} = \frac{\omega^{2} M^{2}}{R_{t} + j\omega L_{t}}
$$

where $R_t, L_t$ are the target's effective resistance and inductance.

### Step 3 — Gap dependence

Both $M$ and the eddy-current magnitude depend on the gap $d$ in approximately **inverse proportion** to the gap. As $d$ **decreases**:
- $M$ **increases** → $Z_r$ **increases**
- Net coil inductance **decreases**, resistance **increases**

### Step 4 — Impedance magnitude

$$
\vert Z \vert = \sqrt{(R_{0} + \Delta R)^{2} + (\omega L_{0} - \Delta L)^{2}}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion:** The impedance $\vert Z \vert$ is **a monotonic, nonlinear, decreasing function of the gap $d$**. The transducer output is a voltage proportional to $\vert Z \vert$, which is calibrated against $d$.

---

## 3.6 Numerical Problem — VRT Inductance Change

### Problem
A coil of $N = 1000$ turns is wound on a magnetic core with $A_{g} = 4 \; \text{cm}^{2} = 4 \times 10^{-4} \; \text{m}^{2}$. The nominal air gap is $x_0 = 1 \; \text{mm} = 10^{-3} \; \text{m}$. The armature displaces by $\Delta x = 0.1 \; \text{mm} = 10^{-4} \; \text{m}$. Find (a) the nominal inductance $L_0$, (b) the change in inductance $\Delta L$, and (c) the sensitivity.

### Solution

**(a) Nominal inductance:**

$$
L_{0} = \frac{N^{2} \mu_{0} A_{g}}{2 x_{0}} = \frac{(1000)^{2} \times (4\pi \times 10^{-7}) \times (4 \times 10^{-4})}{2 \times 10^{-3}}
$$

Step-by-step:

$$
N^{2} = 10^{6}
$$

$$
\mu_{0} A_{g} = 4\pi \times 10^{-7} \times 4 \times 10^{-4} = 16\pi \times 10^{-11} = 5.0265 \times 10^{-10}
$$

$$
N^{2} \mu_{0} A_{g} = 10^{6} \times 5.0265 \times 10^{-10} = 5.0265 \times 10^{-4}
$$

$$
2 x_{0} = 2 \times 10^{-3}
$$

$$
\boxed{L_{0} = \frac{5.0265 \times 10^{-4}}{2 \times 10^{-3}} = 0.2513 \;\text{H} = 251.3 \;\text{mH}}
$$

**(b) Change in inductance:**

Using the linearised formula:

$$
\Delta L = \frac{N^{2} \mu_{0} A_{g}}{x_{0}^{2}} \cdot \Delta x
$$

$$
x_{0}^{2} = (10^{-3})^{2} = 10^{-6}
$$

$$
\Delta L = \frac{5.0265 \times 10^{-4}}{10^{-6}} \times 10^{-4} = 5.0265 \times 10^{-2} \;\text{H} = 50.265 \;\text{mH}
$$

$$
\boxed{\Delta L \approx 50.27 \;\text{mH}}
$$

**(c) Sensitivity:**

$$
\boxed{K = \frac{\Delta L}{\Delta x} = \frac{50.27 \times 10^{-3}}{10^{-4}} = 502.7 \;\text{H/m}}
$$

## 4.1 LVDT Construction — Functional Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeP[PRIMARY COIL\nN_p turns\nexcited by V_p sin wt]
    nodeS1[SECONDARY S1\nN_s1 turns\nleft side]
    nodeS2[SECONDARY S2\nN_s2 turns\nright side]
    nodeCore[FERROMAGNETIC CORE\nattached to measurand\ntranslates along axis]
    nodeNull[NULL OUTPUT\nE_s1 = E_s2\nE_out = 0]
    nodePos[POSITIVE OUTPUT\nE_s1 greater than E_s2\nin phase with V_p]
    nodeNeg[NEGATIVE OUTPUT\nE_s2 greater than E_s1\n180 deg phase shift]
    nodeDemod[PHASE SENSITIVE\nDEMODULATOR]
    nodeDisp[DISPLACEMENT x\nvolts or mA signal]

    nodeP --> nodeS1
    nodeP --> nodeS2
    nodeCore -.couples.-> nodeS1
    nodeCore -.couples.-> nodeS2
    nodeS1 --> nodeNull
    nodeS2 --> nodeNull
    nodeS1 --> nodePos
    nodeS2 --> nodeNeg
    nodePos --> nodeDemod
    nodeNeg --> nodeDemod
    nodeDemod --> nodeDisp
```

---

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[INDUCTIVE TRANSDUCERS]
    nodeB1[SELF INDUCTANCE TYPE\nVariable Reluctance]
    nodeB2[MUTUAL INDUCTANCE TYPE]

    nodeC1[Single coil VRT]
    nodeC2[Differential VRT]
    nodeC3[LVDT\nlinear motion]
    nodeC4[RVDT\nrotary motion]
    nodeC5[Contactless probe\nturbine vibration]

    nodeA --> nodeB1
    nodeA --> nodeB2
    nodeA --> nodeB3
    nodeB1 --> nodeC1
    nodeB1 --> nodeC2
    nodeB2 --> nodeC3

    nodeB3 --> nodeC5
```

## 4.3 LVDT Output Characteristic — Block Schematic with Annotation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA[Core at null x=0]
    nodeB[Core displaced to LEFT -x]
    nodeC[Core displaced to RIGHT +x]
    nodeA --> nodeAout[Output Eo = 0\nnull position]
    nodeB --> nodeBout[Output Eo NEGATIVE\n180 deg phase]
    nodeC --> nodeCout[Output Eo POSITIVE\nin phase with Vp]

    nodeAout -.linear region.-> nodeBout
    nodeAout -.linear region.-> nodeCout
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reading the LVDT characteristic curve (KTU Hot Topic):**
> - **X-axis:** Core displacement $x$ (mm)
> - **Y-axis:** Output voltage $E_o$ (mV)
> - The **central portion** is a straight line through origin.
> - The **slope** = sensitivity in mV/mm.
> - **Phase reversal at $x=0$** distinguishes +x from −x.
> - The **linear range** is typically $\pm 5$ mm for industrial LVDTs.

---

```mermaid
flowchart LR

```
