# 📝 Inductive Transducer — Section 5: KTU 2024 Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pattern:** Part A questions test **definitions, working principles, and qualitative understanding**. Each answer must be **concise (3-4 sentences)** with a labelled diagram or formula where possible.

---

### **Q1. Define an inductive transducer. List any two advantages of LVDT.** [KTU University Exam – May 2023] [3 Marks]

**Model Answer:**

> An **inductive transducer** is a device that converts a non-electrical quantity such as displacement, force, or pressure into a change in the **self-inductance, mutual inductance, or reluctance** of an electrical circuit, which is then measured as an output voltage or current.
>
> **Two advantages of LVDT:**
> 1. **Infinite resolution** — since output is analog, limited only by the detector's noise floor.
> 2. **Frictionless operation** — the core does not touch the coils, giving effectively infinite mechanical life.
> 3. **High sensitivity** (0.1 to 40 V/mm) and excellent linearity (±0.25 % of FS).
> 4. **Bidirectional output** — the phase of $E_o$ indicates direction of displacement.
> 5. **Immune to environmental contamination** — works in oil, dust, and high temperature.

*(Any two well-explained advantages earn 3 marks.)*

---

### **Q2. Distinguish between LVDT and RVDT.** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022] [3 Marks]

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | LVDT | RVDT |
|---------|------|------|
| **Full form** | Linear Variable Differential Transformer | Rotary Variable Differential Transformer |
| **Core motion** | Translational (linear) along the coil axis | Rotational (angular) about the coil axis |
| **Measurand** | Linear displacement (mm) | Angular displacement (degrees) |
| **Core shape** | Cylindrical / rectangular slug | Cam-shaped or eccentric rotor |
| **Operating range** | ±0.1 mm to ±250 mm (typical) | ±40° (typical) |
| **Application** | Hydraulic cylinder position, CNC tool position | Servo valves, robotic joint angle, flight surface feedback |
| **Output equation** | $E_o \propto x$ | $E_o \propto \theta$ |
| **Construction** | Three coils on a straight bobbin | Three coils on a circular bobbin |

> Both use the **same principle** of differential mutual-inductance variation with **phase-sensitive detection**.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pattern:** Part B questions test **derivation, application, and design**. The question has two sub-parts — typically **7 + 7 marks** — and offers an **internal choice** between two alternative full questions (Question A OR Question B).

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — LVDT Detailed Analysis** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

#### **Part (a)** — With the help of a neat diagram, explain the construction and working principle of an LVDT. Derive the expression for its output voltage. [7 Marks]

**Model Answer:**

**1. Diagram (1.5 marks):**

```
        S1 (secondary 1)        S2 (secondary 2)
        |‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾|          |‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾|
        |    (left)             |    (right)
   [Ferromagnetic core ↔ attached to measurand, moves along axis]
   [Primary coil P — centrally placed, fed by V_p sin(ωt)]
```

**2. Construction (1.5 marks):**

- **Primary coil P:** central, $N_p$ turns, energized by a.c. excitation $V_p \sin(\omega t)$ at 2–10 kHz.
- **Two secondary coils S₁ and S₂:** identical, $N_s$ turns each, placed symmetrically on either side of the primary.
- **S₁ and S₂ are connected in series-opposition** — their EMFs subtract.
- **Soft-iron core:** high $\mu_r$ (≥ 5000), attached to the measurand via a non-contact probe.
- **Housing:** mu-metal shield for EMI protection.

**3. Working (2 marks):**

- **Null position (x = 0):** Core is centred → equal flux linking S₁ and S₂ → $E_{s1} = E_{s2}$ in magnitude but opposite in phase (due to series-opposition) → $E_o = 0$.
- **Displaced to S₁ side (x > 0):** Coupling P-S₁ **increases**, P-S₂ **decreases** → $E_{s1} > E_{s2}$ → $E_o > 0$, in phase with $V_p$.
- **Displaced to S₂ side (x < 0):** Reverse → $E_o < 0$, 180° out of phase with $V_p$.
- **Magnitude** of $E_o$ is proportional to $|x|$; **phase** indicates direction.

**4. Derivation (2 marks):**

Primary current:

$$
i_p(t) = \frac{V_{p,m}}{j \omega L_p} \sin(\omega t)
$$

Induced EMFs:

$$
e_{s1}(t) = -M_1 \frac{di_p}{dt}, \quad e_{s2}(t) = -M_2 \frac{di_p}{dt}
$$

Differential output:

$$
e_o(t) = e_{s1} - e_{s2} = -(M_1 - M_2)\frac{V_{p,m}}{L_p}\cos(\omega t)
$$

With $M_1 = M_0 + kx$ and $M_2 = M_0 - kx$:

$$
\boxed{e_o(t) = -\frac{2 k V_{p,m}}{L_p} \, x \, \cos(\omega t)}
$$

**5. Key features (1 mark):**
- Linear in $x$ over the central range.
- Output saturates when core moves beyond the coil length.
- Phase-sensitive demodulator recovers DC signal with polarity indicating direction.

---

#### **Part (b)** — An LVDT has a primary inductance of 5 mH. The mutual-inductance slope is $k = 250 \; \mu\text{H/mm}$. The primary is excited by 6 V RMS at 3 kHz. Calculate (i) the voltage sensitivity in V/mm, and (ii) the output voltage when the core is displaced by 3 mm. [7 Marks]

**Model Answer:**

**Given data:**

- $L_p = 5 \;\text{mH} = 5 \times 10^{-3} \;\text{H}$
- $k = 250 \;\mu\text{H/mm} = 250 \times 10^{-6} \;\text{H/m} = 2.5 \times 10^{-4} \;\text{H/m}$
- $V_{p,rms} = 6 \;\text{V}$
- $f = 3 \;\text{kHz}$ (used only to confirm carrier frequency; not needed in formula)
- $x = 3 \;\text{mm} = 3 \times 10^{-3} \;\text{m}$

**(i) Voltage sensitivity (3.5 marks):**

$$
S = \frac{2 k V_{p,rms}}{L_p}
$$

Substitute:

$$
S = \frac{2 \times (2.5 \times 10^{-4}) \times 6}{5 \times 10^{-3}}
$$

Numerator:

$$
2 \times 2.5 \times 10^{-4} \times 6 = 30 \times 10^{-4} = 3 \times 10^{-3}
$$

Divide by $L_p$:

$$
S = \frac{3 \times 10^{-3}}{5 \times 10^{-3}} = 0.6 \;\text{V/m}
$$

Convert to V/mm:

$$
S = 0.6 \times 10^{-3} \;\text{V/mm} = 0.6 \;\text{mV/mm}
$$

$$
\boxed{S = 0.6 \;\text{mV/mm}}
$$

**(ii) Output voltage at x = 3 mm (3.5 marks):**

$$
E_{o,rms} = S \times x
$$

$$
E_{o,rms} = 0.6 \;\text{mV/mm} \times 3 \;\text{mm} = 1.8 \;\text{mV}
$$

$$
\boxed{E_{o,rms} = 1.8 \;\text{mV}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Verification using the LVDT formula directly:**

$$
E_o = \frac{2 k V_p x}{L_p} = \frac{2 \times 2.5 \times 10^{-4} \times 6 \times 3 \times 10^{-3}}{5 \times 10^{-3}}
$$

$$
= \frac{2 \times 2.5 \times 6 \times 3}{5} \times 10^{-4-3+3} = \frac{90}{5} \times 10^{-4} = 18 \times 10^{-4} \;\text{V} = 1.8 \;\text{mV} \;\checkmark
$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Variable Reluctance and Eddy-Current** [Alternative Internal Choice]

#### **Part (a)** — With the help of a neat diagram, explain the construction and working of a **differential variable reluctance transducer (DVRT)**. Derive the expression for its sensitivity. [7 Marks]

**Model Answer:**

**1. Diagram (1.5 marks):**

A typical DVRT has **two identical E-shaped cores** with two coils $L_1$ and $L_2$ wound on the outer limbs. A **ferromagnetic armature** is suspended between them and is mechanically linked to the measurand. The armature is held in the null position by a **spring**.

**2. Construction (1 mark):**

- Two E-cores with centre limbs carrying the air gap.
- Armature pivots about a flexure or spring suspension.
- The two air gaps $x_1$ and $x_2$ change in **opposite directions** when the armature deflects.

**3. Working (1.5 marks):**

- When the measurand deflects the armature **downwards**, gap $x_1$ **decreases** (reluctance decreases, $L_1$ increases) and gap $x_2$ **increases** (reluctance increases, $L_2$ decreases).
- The two coils form adjacent arms of an **a.c. bridge** (Wheatstone / Owen bridge).
- The bridge becomes unbalanced → output voltage $E_o \propto (L_1 - L_2)$.
- For small displacements, this output is **linear** in $\Delta x$.

**4. Derivation (2.5 marks):**

Each coil's inductance is:

$$
L_i = \frac{N^2 \mu_0 A_g}{2 x_i} \quad (i = 1, 2)
$$

Let nominal gap be $x_0$. After displacement $\Delta x$:

$$
x_1 = x_0 - \Delta x, \quad x_2 = x_0 + \Delta x
$$

$$
L_1 = \frac{N^2 \mu_0 A_g}{2(x_0 - \Delta x)}, \quad L_2 = \frac{N^2 \mu_0 A_g}{2(x_0 + \Delta x)}
$$

Differential inductance:

$$
\Delta L = L_1 - L_2 = \frac{N^2 \mu_0 A_g}{2} \left[ \frac{1}{x_0 - \Delta x} - \frac{1}{x_0 + \Delta x} \right]
$$

After common denominator:

$$
\Delta L = \frac{N^2 \mu_0 A_g \cdot \Delta x}{x_0^2 - \Delta x^2}
$$

Linearized for $\Delta x \ll x_0$:

$$
\boxed{\Delta L \approx \frac{N^2 \mu_0 A_g}{x_0^2} \cdot \Delta x}
$$

**5. Sensitivity (0.5 marks):**

$$
K = \frac{d(\Delta L)}{d(\Delta x)} = \frac{N^2 \mu_0 A_g}{x_0^2} \quad \left[\frac{\text{H}}{\text{m}}\right]
$$

---

#### **Part (b)** — Explain the **working principle of an eddy-current transducer**. List two of its advantages and two applications. [7 Marks]

**Model Answer:**

**1. Working Principle (3 marks):**

- An **eddy-current transducer** consists of an excitation coil energized by a high-frequency a.c. source (typically 1 to 100 MHz).
- The coil produces an **alternating magnetic field** that extends into the space in front of it.
- When a **conductive target** (aluminum, copper, steel, etc.) is brought within the field region, **eddy currents** are induced in the target by **Faraday's law**.
- By **Lenz's law**, these eddy currents produce their own magnetic field that **opposes the original coil field**.
- The net effect is a **reduction in coil inductance** and an **increase in coil resistance** (the coil's impedance $Z$ changes).
- The change $\Delta Z$ is a **monotonic, non-linear function of the gap $d$** between the coil and the target.
- A bridge or oscillator-based detector converts $\Delta Z$ to a measurable output voltage.

**2. Mathematical expression (2 marks):**

The total coil impedance is:

$$
Z(d) = R_0 + R_e(d) + j\omega [L_0 - L_e(d)]
$$

where $R_e(d), L_e(d)$ are eddy-current-induced components, both functions of gap $d$. The output:

$$
V_o(d) = I_{exc} \cdot \vert Z(d) \vert
$$

**3. Advantages (1 mark):**
1. **Contactless measurement** → no wear, no friction, infinite life.
2. **Immune to dirt, oil, dust, water** → ideal for harsh industrial environments.
3. **High frequency response** → can measure dynamic vibrations up to **100 kHz**.
4. **Works on non-magnetic conductive targets** (aluminium, copper, brass).
5. **High resolution** → sub-micron position changes detectable.

**4. Applications (1 mark):**
1. **Turbine blade tip clearance measurement** in jet engines and steam turbines.
2. **Vibration monitoring** of rotating shafts.
3. **Non-destructive testing (NDT)** — surface crack detection.
4. **Proximity switches** in automation.
5. **Thickness measurement** of conductive coatings and sheets.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ KTU 2024 Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the direction (phase) of LVDT output:** Many students compute only the magnitude and lose 2 marks. Always mention that **phase indicates direction**.
> 2. **Confusing self-inductance and mutual-inductance types:** VRT = self-inductance; LVDT = mutual-inductance. Examiners specifically look for this distinction.
> 3. **Single-coil VRT is non-linear:** The relation is $L \propto 1/x$, NOT linear. Only **differential VRT** is linearized.
> 4. **Unit confusion:** Sensitivity is in **H/m** (VRT) or **V/m** (LVDT) — not just H or V.
> 5. **Not drawing the LVDT characteristic curve:** Even a rough sketch of the **S-shaped curve** with the linear region and the two null points earns easy 1-2 marks.
> 6. **Forgetting the formula for $L = N^2 \mu_0 A / (2x)$** for two air gaps — many students write $L = N^2 \mu_0 A / x$ (one gap), which is wrong for differential VRT.
> 7. **Eddy-current target must be conductive but can be non-magnetic** — many students incorrectly say it must be ferromagnetic.
> 8. **In numerical problems, ALWAYS show unit conversion steps** (mm → m, μH → H). Skipping this loses 1 mark.
> 9. **LVDT excitation frequency is 2–10 kHz, NOT 50 Hz** — mixing up with power line frequency is a common error.
> 10. **RVDT has the SAME principle as LVDT** but rotary core. State this clearly in Q1 (Part A) for full marks.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **🎯 Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU Exam:**
>
> ✅ **Three types of inductive transducers:**
> - **Self-inductance (VRT):** $L = \dfrac{N^2 \mu_0 A_g}{2x}$, $L \propto 1/x$, non-linear single-coil.
> - **Mutual-inductance (LVDT / RVDT):** $E_o = \dfrac{2kV_p x}{L_p} \sin(\omega t)$, linear.
> - **Eddy-current:** $Z(d) = f(1/d)$, contactless, works on conductive targets.
>
> ✅ **LVDT key facts:**
> - Three coils — **P, S₁, S₂** — series-opposed secondaries.
> - Excitation: **2–10 kHz** sinusoidal, 1–24 V amplitude.
> - Sensitivity: **0.1 to 40 V/mm**; linear range: **±5 mm** (typical industrial grade).
> - Phase-sensitive detection: **magnitude = |x|, phase = direction**.
> - Resolution: **sub-micron**; life: **infinite** (no contact).
>
> ✅ **Differential VRT key facts:**
> - Two coils with common armature → $L_1 - L_2$ output.
> - Sensitivity: $K = \dfrac{N^2 \mu_0 A_g}{x_0^2}$ H/m.
> - Used in **pressure transmitters** (Bourdon tube + DVRT).
>
> ✅ **Eddy-current key facts:**
> - Frequency: **1 to 100 MHz**.
> - Works on **non-magnetic** conductive targets (aluminium, copper).
> - Applications: **tip clearance, vibration, NDT, thickness**.
>
> ✅ **Formulas to memorize by heart:**
> - $L = \dfrac{N^2 \mu A}{l}$
> - $\Re = \dfrac{l}{\mu A}$
> - $L(x) = \dfrac{N^2 \mu_0 A_g}{2x}$ (VRT)
> - $E_o = \dfrac{2k V_p x}{L_p} \sin(\omega t)$ (LVDT)
> - $\Delta L = \dfrac{N^2 \mu_0 A_g \cdot \Delta x}{x_0^2 - \Delta x^2}$ (differential VRT)
>
> ✅ **Common exam traps:**
> - Phase reversal at null (LVDT) — must be mentioned.
> - Single-coil VRT non-linearity (vs. differential VRT linearity).
> - Eddy-current transducer detects **conductive** (not necessarily magnetic) targets.
> - RVDT = rotary analogue of LVDT (same principle, different motion).
>
> ✅ **Key constants:**
> - $\mu_0 = 4\pi \times 10^{-7}$ H/m
> - $\mu_r$ (soft iron) ≈ 2000 to 6000
> - Standard LVDT excitation ≈ 2.5 kHz or 5 kHz
> - 1 H = 1 Wb/A = 1 V·s/A

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Ultrasonic Transducer
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCBRT303 - SENSORS AND ACTUATORS > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Ultrasonic Transducer # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Ultrasonic Transducer (KTU 2024 Definition):** > An **ultrasonic transducer** is an electroacoustic device that converts electrical energy into high-frequency mechanical (acoustic) vibrations — typically in the frequency range **above 20 kHz (the human audible threshold)** — and conversely, converts received ultrasonic mechanical waves back into measurable electrical signals, based fundamentally on the **direct and inverse piezoelectric effect** exhibited by certain anisotropic crystalline and ceramic materials. The boundary of human hearing, **20 kHz**, separates the *sonic* from the *ultrasonic* spectrum. Practical engineering ultrasonic transducers operate in a much narrower band, generally between **20 kHz and 10 MHz**, with industrial non-destructive testing (NDT) clusters centered around **1 MHz to 10 MHz** and medical imaging favoring **2 MHz to 15 MHz**. | Spectrum Band | Frequency Range | Typical Source | |---|---|---| | Infrasonic | < 20 Hz | Earthquakes, machinery | | Audible | 20 Hz – 20 kHz | Human voice, speakers | | **Ultrasonic** | **20 kHz – 10 GHz** | **This transducer's domain** | | Hypersonic | > 10 GHz | Quantum phonons | ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Whispering Bat" > [!NOTE] > **The Bat Echolocation Analogy (Why Ultrasonic?):** > Imagine you are standing in a pitch-dark forest. You clap your hands sharply and listen for the echo bouncing off a tree. The time between the clap and the echo tells you how far the tree is. A **bat does exactly this**, but it shouts at frequencies far above what your ear can detect — using **ultrasonic chirps**. The **ultrasonic transducer is the engineering version of a bat's mouth and ears combined**: > - **Transmit mode** → it *screams* an ultrasonic pulse into a medium. > - **Receive mode** → it *listens* for the returning echo. > The key reason nature (and engineers) chose ultrasonic frequencies is that **higher frequency = shorter wavelength = finer spatial resolution**, allowing detection of tiny defects, precise distance measurement, and high-resolution imaging. ## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics - **Speed of sound in air (at 20 °C):** $c_{air} = \mathbf{343 \ m/s}$ - **Speed of sound in water (at 20 °C):** $c_{water} = \mathbf{1482 \ m/s}$ - **Speed of sound in steel:** $c_{steel} \approx \mathbf{5960 \ m/s}$ - **Acoustic impedance of air:** $Z_{air} \approx \mathbf{0.0004 \ MRayl}$ - **Acoustic impedance of water:** $Z_{water} \approx \mathbf{1.5 \ MRayl}$ - **Standard KTU reference frequency for NDT:** $f_0 = \mathbf{1 \ MHz}$ to $\mathbf{5 \ MHz}$ ## 1.4 Classification of Ultrasonic Transducers (KTU Syllabus Snapshot) > [!IMPORTANT] > KTU 2024 Module 4 categorizes ultrasonic transducers into the following families: > 1. **Piezoelectric Transducer** (PZT, Quartz, PVDF, Lithium Niobate) > 2. **Magnetostrictive Transducer** (Nickel, Terfenol-D) > 3. **Capacitive / Electrostatic Transducer** (cMUT) > 4. **Electromagnetic Acoustic Transducer (EMAT)** The **piezoelectric type dominates KTU board questions** due to its superior electromechanical coupling, compactness, and broadband response. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Piezoelectric Effect — The Heart of the Ultrasonic Transducer > [!NOTE] > **Discovered by Jacques and Pierre Curie in 1880**, the piezoelectric effect exists in two complementary forms that the transducer exploits symmetrically. ### 2.1.1 Direct Piezoelectric Effect (Sensing / Receiver Mode) When mechanical stress $\sigma$ is applied across certain non-centrosymmetric crystals (quartz, Rochelle salt, tourmaline), the internal charge centers separate, generating a measurable surface charge density $D$ on the crystal faces: $$D = d \cdot \sigma + \varepsilon^{T} \cdot E$$ where: - $d$ = **piezoelectric charge coefficient** (units: C/N or m/V) - $\varepsilon^{T}$ = **permittivity at constant stress** - $E$ = applied electric field ### 2.1.2 Inverse (Converse) Piezoelectric Effect (Actuator / Transmitter Mode) When an alternating electric field $E$ is applied across a piezoelectric crystal, it undergoes a mechanical strain $S$ proportional to the field: $$S = s^{E} \cdot \sigma + d \cdot E$$ where $s^{E}$ = **elastic compliance at constant electric field**. > [!TIP] > **Exam Tip:** The direct effect produces a voltage (sensor); the inverse effect produces displacement (actuator). The *same coefficient $d$* governs both — this is what makes the piezoelectric crystal a **reversible transducer**. ## 2.2 Operating Modes of a Piezoelectric Disc A piezoelectric disc can vibrate in several modes, each suited to a specific application. The KTU board frequently asks students to identify the mode from a diagram. | Mode | Vibration Direction | Typical Frequency | Application | |---|---|---|---| | **Thickness mode** ($k_t$) | Along thickness (z-axis) | **1 – 15 MHz** | NDT, medical imaging | | **Radial mode** ($k_p$) | Along disc radius | 50 – 500 kHz | Hydrophones, low-freq. sonar | | **Length mode** ($k_{31}$) | Along length | 20 – 200 kHz | Industrial cleaning | | **Shear mode** ($k_{15}$) | Shear strain | 5 – 50 MHz | SAW devices | ## 2.3 Resonance and Anti-Resonance (KTU Favourite Topic) When driven by an AC source, the piezoelectric crystal exhibits a frequency-dependent impedance with two critical points: > [!IMPORTANT] > - **Resonance frequency $f_r$**: the frequency at which the *mechanical* reactance cancels the *inertial* reactance, leaving the impedance **minimum (and real)**. Maximum mechanical displacement. > - **Anti-resonance frequency $f_a$**: the frequency at which the *elastic* reactance dominates, leaving impedance **maximum (and real)**. Minimum mechanical displacement. The separation between these two frequencies defines the **effective coupling coefficient** $k_{eff}$ — a key figure of merit. ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!WARNING] > **The following table contains the high-yield equations you MUST memorize for KTU 2024 ESE questions on this topic.** All absolute value notation uses `\vert` to ensure clean rendering in PDF/markdown pipelines. | # | Quantity | Formula | Description | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Resonance frequency (thickness mode) | $f_r = \dfrac{v_p}{2 t}$ | $v_p$ = velocity of sound in piezo material, $t$ = thickness | | 2 | Wavelength relation | $\lambda = \dfrac{v_p}{f}$ | Fundamental acoustic relation | | 3 | Acoustic impedance | $Z = \rho \cdot c$ | $\rho$ = density, $c$ = sound velocity | | 4 | Reflection coefficient (pressure) | $R = \dfrac{Z_2 - Z_1}{Z_2 + Z_1}$ | Between two media | | 5 | Transmission coefficient | $T = \dfrac{2 Z_2}{Z_2 + Z_1}$ | Pressure transmission | | 6 | Intensity transmission | $T_I = \dfrac{4 Z_1 Z_2}{(Z_1 + Z_2)^2}$ | Energy transmission | | 7 | Coupling coefficient (planar) | $k_{31}^2 = \dfrac{f_a^2 - f_r^2}{f_a^2}$ | From resonance/anti-resonance | | 8 | Coupling coefficient (thickness) | $k_t^2 = \dfrac{\pi}{2} \cdot \dfrac{f_r}{f_a} \cdot \tan\!\left(\dfrac{\pi}{2} \cdot \dfrac{f_a - f_r}{f_a}\right)$ | For thickness mode | | 9 | Quality factor | $Q = \dfrac{f_r}{\Delta f}$ | $\Delta f$ = −3 dB bandwidth | | 10 | Time-of-flight distance | $d = \dfrac{c \cdot t_{TOF}}{2}$ | Round-trip echo ranging | | 11 | Doppler frequency shift | $\Delta f = \dfrac{2 f_0 v \cos\theta}{c}$ | Flow measurement | | 12 | Piezo constitutive (direct) | $D = d \sigma + \varepsilon^{T} E$ | Sensor equation | | 13 | Piezo constitutive (inverse) | $S = s^{E} \sigma + d E$ | Actuator equation | ## 2.5 Common Piezoelectric Materials Used in KTU-Standard Transducers | Material | $d_{33}$ (pC/N) | Curie Temp (°C) | Typical Use | |---|---|---|---| | Quartz (SiO₂) | 2.3 | 573 | High-temp stability, oscillators | | PZT (Lead Zirconate Titanate) | 300–600 | 200–350 | General purpose, NDT probes | | PVDF (Polymer film) | 33 | 80 | Flexible sensors, wideband | | Lithium Niobate (LiNbO₃) | 6 | 1210 | SAW, high-temp | | Barium Titanate | 190 | 120 | Early transducers | ## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility > [!NOTE] > Ultrasonic transducers are the silent workhorses of modern industry: > - **Medical:** fetal imaging, tumour detection, physiotherapy (deep tissue healing at 1 MHz, 1 W/cm²). > - **Industrial NDT:** crack detection in pipelines, welds, and aerospace structures. > - **Cleaning:** ultrasonic baths at 40 kHz agitate cavitation bubbles to scrub micro-scale contaminants from PCBs and surgical tools. > - **Ranging/Automotive:** parking sensors operate at 40–58 kHz; LiDAR-equivalent sonar for blind-spot detection. > - **Flow metering:** transit-time and Doppler flowmeters measure fluid velocity in pipes. > - **Acoustic levitation / HIFU:** high-intensity focused ultrasound for non-invasive surgery (HIFU) at 1–4 MHz. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation I — Resonance Frequency of a Piezoelectric Disc in Thickness Mode > [!NOTE] > **Problem context:** A PZT disc of thickness $t$ is driven by an AC source across its major faces. Determine the thickness-mode resonance frequency $f_r$ assuming the lateral dimensions are much larger than $t$ (so radial modes are negligible — plane-wave approximation). ### Step 1 — Establish the mechanical boundary conditions The two flat faces of the disc are mechanically free surfaces (stress-free: $\sigma_{zz} = 0$). This creates a standing longitudinal wave trapped between the two free boundaries. ### Step 2 — Apply the 1-D wave equation The 1-D longitudinal acoustic wave equation in a piezo rod under zero applied field is: $$\frac{\partial^2 u}{\partial t^2} = v_p^2 \cdot \frac{\partial^2 u}{\partial z^2}$$ where $u(z, t)$ is the particle displacement and $v_p$ is the velocity of longitudinal acoustic waves in the piezoelectric material, given by: $$v_p = \sqrt{\frac{1}{\rho \, s_{33}^{E}}}$$ Here, $\rho$ is the material density and $s_{33}^{E}$ is the elastic compliance at constant electric field along the poling axis. ### Step 3 — Solve using separation of variables Assume a separable solution of the form: $$u(z, t) = U(z) \cdot e^{j \omega t}$$ Substituting into the wave equation gives: $$\frac{d^2 U}{dz^2} + \left(\frac{\omega}{v_p}\right)^2 U = 0$$ Let $k = \omega / v_p$ be the acoustic wavenumber. The general solution is: $$U(z) = A \cos(k z) + B \sin(k z)$$ ### Step 4 — Apply the stress-free boundary conditions The mechanical stress $\sigma_{zz}$ in a 1-D piezo slab is given by $\sigma_{zz} = (1/s_{33}^{E}) \cdot \partial u / \partial z - e_{33} E$, and the boundaries $z = 0$ and $z = t$ must have $\sigma_{zz} = 0$. Under open-circuit / driven conditions, the dominant constraint becomes the free-displacement condition: $$\left. \frac{dU}{dz} \right|_{z=0} = 0 \quad \text{and} \quad \left. \frac{dU}{dz} \right|_{z=t} = 0$$ Differentiating the general solution: $$\frac{dU}{dz} = -A k \sin(k z) + B k \cos(k z)$$ Applying the first boundary ($z = 0$): $$B k = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad B = 0$$ Applying the second boundary ($z = t$): $$-A k \sin(k t) = 0$$ For a non-trivial solution ($A \neq 0$), we require: $$\sin(k t) = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad k t = n \pi, \quad n = 1, 2, 3, \dots$$ ### Step 5 — Extract the fundamental resonance frequency The smallest non-trivial root corresponds to $n = 1$: $$k = \frac{\pi}{t} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{\omega_r}{v_p} = \frac{\pi}{t}$$ Converting $\omega_r = 2 \pi f_r$: $$\boxed{\,f_r = \frac{v_p}{2 t} = \frac{1}{2 t} \sqrt{\frac{1}{\rho \, s_{33}^{E}}}\,}$$ > [!TIP] > **Physical interpretation:** Halving the disc thickness *doubles* the resonance frequency. This is why high-frequency medical imaging probes (10 MHz) are physically very thin (∼200 μm for PZT). ## 3.2 Derivation II — Acoustic Impedance & Reflection at a Boundary > [!NOTE] > **Problem context:** An ultrasonic wave traveling in medium 1 strikes a planar interface with medium 2 at normal incidence. Find the pressure reflection coefficient. ### Step 1 — Write the forward and reflected pressure waves Let the incident pressure wave be $p_i = P_i e^{j(\omega t - k_1 x)}$ and the reflected wave be $p_r = P_r e^{j(\omega t + k_1 x)}$. The transmitted wave in medium 2 is $p_t = P_t e^{j(\omega t - k_2 x)}$. ### Step 2 — Apply continuity of pressure at the boundary ($x = 0$) The total pressure must be continuous across the interface: $$P_i + P_r = P_t$$ ### Step 3 — Apply continuity of particle velocity Particle velocity in a plane acoustic wave is $v = p / (\rho c) = p / Z$. Continuity of normal particle velocity at the interface gives: $$\frac{P_i}{Z_1} - \frac{P_r}{Z_1} = \frac{P_t}{Z_2}$$ ### Step 4 — Solve the simultaneous equations Dividing the second equation by the first: $$\frac{Z_2 (P_i - P_r)}{Z_1 (P_i + P_r)} = 1$$ Rearranging for $P_r / P_i$: $$P_r \, Z_2 \, Z_1^{-1} - P_r \, Z_2 \, Z_1^{-1} \cdot \frac{P_r}{P_i} \cdot \dots \quad \text{(full algebra)}$$ After clean algebra: $$\boxed{\,R = \frac{P_r}{P_i} = \frac{Z_2 - Z_1}{Z_2 + Z_1}\,}$$ ### Step 5 — Special cases and engineering insight - **Matching layer condition:** When $Z_1 = Z_2$, $R = 0$ (perfect transmission). This is why **matching layers** (e.g., epoxy between PZT $Z \approx 30$ MRayl and skin/water $Z \approx 1.5$ MRayl) are essential in medical probes. - **Complete reflection (rigid wall):** $Z_2 \gg Z_1$ gives $R \to +1$. - **Free surface (air-backed):** $Z_2 \ll Z_1$ gives $R \to -1$ (pressure node). ## 3.3 Derivation III — Electromechanical Coupling Coefficient from Resonance Data Starting from the relation between resonance and anti-resonance for a lightly damped piezo resonator, the IEEE Standard piezoelectric equivalent gives: $$k^2 = \frac{f_a^2 - f_r^2}{f_a^2}$$ ### Step 1 — Square both sides and rearrange $$k^2 f_a^2 = f_a^2 - f_r^2$$ $$f_r^2 = f_a^2 (1 - k^2)$$ ### Step 2 — Solve for $k$ $$\boxed{\,k = \sqrt{1 - \left(\frac{f_r}{f_a}\right)^2}\,}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Numerical Insight:** A high $k$ (close to 1) means a large bandwidth between $f_r$ and $f_a$, and therefore a *broadband*, *efficient* transducer. PZT-5H has $k_{33} \approx 0.75$, the highest of common piezo ceramics. ## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board Pattern) > [!NOTE] > **Question (Mock):** A PZT-5A disc has thickness $t = 2$ mm, density $\rho = 7750 \ \text{kg/m}^3$, and longitudinal wave velocity $v_p = 4350 \ \text{m/s}$. Compute (a) the fundamental thickness-mode resonance frequency, (b) the wavelength in the material, and (c) the acoustic impedance. **Solution:** **(a)** Using $f_r = v_p / (2 t)$: $$f_r = \frac{4350}{2 \times 2 \times 10^{-3}} = \frac{4350}{4 \times 10^{-3}} = 1.0875 \times 10^{6} \ \text{Hz}$$ $$\boxed{f_r \approx 1.09 \ \text{MHz}}$$ **(b)** $\lambda = v_p / f_r = 2 t = 4$ mm (a direct geometric consequence of the half-wavelength resonance). **(c)** $Z = \rho \cdot v_p = 7750 \times 4350$: $$Z = 3.37 \times 10^{7} \ \text{kg/(m}^2\cdot\text{s)} = 33.7 \ \text{MRayl}$$ This is the standard value for PZT — far higher than water (1.5 MRayl), confirming the **necessity of acoustic matching layers** in immersion probes. # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block Diagram — Ultrasonic Transducer System Architecture ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Pulser / Function Generator
0 to 500 V pp AC] --> B[Impedance Matching Network
50 ohm to Z piezo] B --> C[Piezoelectric Element
PZT Disc or PVDF Film] C --> D[Acoustic Backing Layer
High Damping Material] C --> E[Acoustic Matching Layer
Quarter Wave Couplant] E --> F[Test Medium
Metal / Water / Tissue] F --> G[Reflected Echo / Scattered Wave] G --> E E --> C C --> H[Low Noise Amplifier
40 to 80 dB Gain] H --> I[Time Gain Compensation
TGC Amplifier] I --> J[Signal Processor
Envelope Detection] J --> K[Display / Data Logger
OSC A Scan B Scan C Scan] ``` ## 4.2 Piezoelectric Transducer Internal Construction ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph X[Ultrasonic Probe Assembly] A1[Housing / Case
Stainless Steel] --> A2[Connector / Coaxial Lead] A1 --> A3[Backing Material
Tungsten Epoxy Composite] A3 --> A4[Piezoelectric Disc
PZT 5A or Quartz] A4 --> A5[Electrodes
Silver or Nickel Film] A5 --> A6[Matching Layer
Epoxy or Polymer] A6 --> A7[Protective Wear Plate
or Acoustic Window] end ``` ## 4.3 Operating Principle Flow — Pulse-Echo Mode ```mermaid flowchart TD S1[Start: HV Pulser Triggered] --> S2[PZT Disc Excited by HV Pulse] S2 --> S3[Inverse Piezoelectric Effect: PZT Vibrates at f r] S3 --> S4[Ultrasonic Wave Radiated into Couplant and Medium] S4 --> S5[Wave Propagates at c medium] S5 --> S6[Encounter Flaw or Backwall] S6 --> S7[Partial Reflection at Acoustic Impedance Mismatch] S7 --> S8[Echo Returns to PZT Disc] S8 --> S9[Direct Piezoelectric Effect: Mechanical Stress Generates Charge] S9 --> S10[Small Voltage Signal of order micro to milli Volts] S10 --> S11[Amplified and Time Resolved] S11 --> S12[Display Flaw Position: d equals c times t TOF divided by 2] ``` ## 4.4 Functional Architecture of a Pulse-Echo Ultrasonic Inspection System ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Operator Console] --> B[Pulser Receiver Unit] B --> C[Transducer Probe] C --> D[Test Specimen] D --> C C --> B B --> E[Signal Conditioner] E --> F[ADC and DSP] F --> G[Image Reconstruction Module] G --> H[Display and Alarm Module] H --> A ``` > [!NOTE] > **Why this architecture matters in KTU 2024 boards:** Examiners often ask students to *label* the functional blocks of an ultrasonic NDT system. The probe is the only part that physically contacts the specimen; all other blocks are electronic. The **backing layer** in the probe determines pulse length (damping), and the **matching layer** determines energy transfer efficiency into the medium. ## 4.5 Coupling Coefficient vs Frequency Response (Conceptual) ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Drive Frequency f] --> B{Is f equal to f r ?} B -- Yes --> C[Impedance Minimum
Max Current
Max Displacement] B -- No --> D[Move toward f a] D --> E{Is f equal to f a ?} E -- Yes --> F[Impedance Maximum
Min Current
Min Displacement] E -- No --> G[Intermediate Reactance Dominated] G --> H[Inductive below f r] G --> I[Capacitive above f r] ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **`[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Style]`** ### Question A.1 (3 Marks) **"Define the term 'ultrasonic transducer' and state the principle on which it works. List two common piezoelectric materials used in such transducers."** **Model Answer (3 marks allocation breakdown):** **Definition (1 mark):** An ultrasonic transducer is a device that converts electrical energy into high-frequency mechanical vibrations (above 20 kHz) and vice versa, used for generation and detection of ultrasonic waves. **Principle (1 mark):** It works on the **piezoelectric effect** — the *direct* effect for receiver (mechanical stress → electric charge) and the *inverse* effect for transmitter (electric field → mechanical strain). **Materials (1 mark):** Any two of: PZT (Lead Zirconate Titanate), Quartz, PVDF, Lithium Niobate, Barium Titanate. --- ### Question A.2 (3 Marks) **"Distinguish between the resonance frequency $f_r$ and anti-resonance frequency $f_a$ of a piezoelectric crystal. What is the significance of the difference $(f_a - f_r)$?"** **Model Answer:** | Feature | $f_r$ (Resonance) | $f_a$ (Anti-Resonance) | |---|---|---| | Impedance | Minimum (real) | Maximum (real) | | Mechanical displacement | Maximum | Minimum | | Phase | Zero | Zero (180° relative) | | Physical cause | Mass reactance cancels stiffness reactance | Stiffness dominates | **Significance (1 mark):** The difference $(f_a - f_r)$ determines the **electromechanical coupling coefficient** $k$, which quantifies the efficiency of energy conversion between electrical and mechanical domains: $$k^2 = \frac{f_a^2 - f_r^2}{f_a^2}$$ A larger separation implies a higher $k$ and a more efficient transducer. --- ## PART B — Full-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) > **`[KTU University Exam – December 2023 Module 4 Pattern]`** --- ### ❖ Question B.1 (14 Marks) — Option A **"With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the construction and working of a piezoelectric ultrasonic transducer. Derive the expression for the fundamental thickness-mode resonance frequency of a piezoelectric disc. A PZT disc has a thickness of 1.5 mm and longitudinal wave velocity of 4500 m/s. Determine the fundamental resonance frequency and the acoustic impedance if the density is 7800 kg/m³."** #### Part (a) — Construction, Working, and Block Diagram (7 marks) **Construction (3 marks):** A typical piezoelectric ultrasonic transducer consists of the following layers stacked along the acoustic axis: 1. **Backing layer** — a highly damped material (e.g., tungsten-epoxy composite) that absorbs the backward-radiated wave and shortens the pulse. 2. **Piezoelectric element** — PZT disc, typically 5–25 mm in diameter and 0.2–5 mm thick, with silver electrodes on both faces, polarized along the thickness. 3. **Matching layer** — quarter-wavelength-thick epoxy or polymer that acoustically couples the high-impedance piezo to the lower-impedance test medium. 4. **Wear plate / protective window** — guards against mechanical damage. 5. **Housing** — metal case providing EMI shielding and mechanical support; coaxial connector for the electrical drive. **Working Principle (3 marks):** > *Transmit mode:* A high-voltage AC pulse (e.g., 100–400 V) is applied across the PZT electrodes. The inverse piezoelectric effect causes the disc to vibrate at its fundamental thickness-mode resonance $f_r = v_p / (2t)$. This vibration launches an ultrasonic wave through the matching layer into the test medium. > *Receive mode:* Returning echoes impinge on the disc. The direct piezoelectric effect converts the mechanical stress into a measurable surface charge, which is read as a voltage across the electrodes. **Block Diagram (1 mark):** Refer to **Section 4.2** of these notes for the layered construction. The examiner awards the diagram mark if the student clearly shows: Housing → Backing → PZT → Electrodes → Matching layer → Test medium. #### Part (b) — Derivation and Numerical (7 marks) **Derivation (4 marks):** See **Section 3.1** of these notes — the complete derivation of $f_r = v_p / (2t)$ using the 1-D wave equation, separation of variables, and free-stress boundary conditions. **Numerical Computation (3 marks):** Given: $t = 1.5$ mm $= 1.5 \times 10^{-3}$ m, $v_p = 4500$ m/s, $\rho = 7800$ kg/m³. **(i) Fundamental resonance frequency:** $$f_r = \frac{v_p}{2 t} = \frac{4500}{2 \times 1.5 \times 10^{-3}} = \frac{4500}{3 \times 10^{-3}} = 1.5 \times 10^{6} \ \text{Hz}$$ $$\boxed{f_r = 1.5 \ \text{MHz}}$$ **(ii) Acoustic impedance:** $$Z = \rho \cdot v_p = 7800 \times 4500 = 3.51 \times 10^{7} \ \text{kg/(m}^2\cdot\text{s)}$$ $$\boxed{Z = 35.1 \ \text{MRayl}}$$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:** > - **Common Error 1 (1-mark loss):** Students frequently forget to convert mm to m before substituting into the formula, leading to a frequency answer that is off by $10^3$. Always write units in SI form before computing. > - **Common Error 2 (1-mark loss):** Confusing the *electrical* resonance (where impedance is minimum) with the *mechanical* resonance (where displacement is maximum). In a *lightly damped* piezo, these coincide at $f_r$, but in real systems with high $Q$, they may differ slightly. > - **Common Error 3 (2-mark loss):** Failing to mention the role of the *backing layer* and *matching layer* explicitly. KTU 2024 scheme explicitly tests understanding of these layers because they determine axial resolution and energy transfer. > - **Common Error 4 (1-mark loss):** Using density in g/cm³ without conversion. Always convert to kg/m³. --- ### ❖ Question B.2 (14 Marks) — Option B **"Explain the phenomenon of piezoelectricity. Derive the relation between resonance and anti-resonance frequencies with the electromechanical coupling coefficient. A piezoelectric crystal has $f_r = 1.0$ MHz and $f_a = 1.05$ MHz. Calculate the coupling coefficient. Mention two applications of ultrasonic transducers in medical and industrial fields."** #### Part (a) — Piezoelectricity and Resonance Relation (7 marks) **Piezoelectricity (3 marks):** The phenomenon, discovered by the Curie brothers in 1880, exists in two forms: - **Direct effect:** Certain non-centrosymmetric crystals (quartz, PZT, Rochelle salt) develop surface electric charge when subjected to mechanical stress. - **Inverse (converse) effect:** These same crystals undergo mechanical deformation (strain) when placed in an external electric field. > *Constitutive equations (1 mark):* $D = d\sigma + \varepsilon^{T} E$ and $S = s^{E}\sigma + d E$ (full equations in Section 2.1). **Resonance – Anti-resonance Derivation (4 marks):** The electrical admittance $Y$ of a piezoelectric resonator near its fundamental mode can be modeled by the **Van Dyke equivalent circuit** (a series RLC in parallel with the static capacitance $C_0$). The series branch resonates at $f_s = f_r$ (zero series reactance, minimum impedance). The parallel combination of $C_0$ with the series resonance creates a second null in admittance at $f_a > f_r$ (anti-resonance, maximum impedance). Setting the imaginary part of admittance to zero at $f_r$ and $f_a$ and applying the standard result: $$k^2 = \frac{f_a^2 - f_r^2}{f_a^2}$$ Rearranging: $$\boxed{k = \sqrt{1 - \left(\frac{f_r}{f_a}\right)^2}}$$ > [!TIP] > **Derivation in brief for the answer sheet:** From the Van Dyke model, the ratio $C/C_0 = k^2/(1-k^2)$, and the resonance condition gives $f_a/f_r = \sqrt{1 + C/C_0}$. Substituting yields $k^2 = (f_a^2 - f_r^2)/f_a^2$. (3 algebraic lines suffice for 4 marks.) #### Part (b) — Numerical and Applications (7 marks) **Numerical (3 marks):** Given: $f_r = 1.0$ MHz, $f_a = 1.05$ MHz. $$k = \sqrt{1 - \left(\frac{1.0}{1.05}\right)^2} = \sqrt{1 - (0.9524)^2}$$ $$k = \sqrt{1 - 0.9070} = \sqrt{0.0930} = 0.305$$ $$\boxed{k \approx 0.305 \quad \text{(or } k^2 = 0.093 \text{)}}$$ **Applications (4 marks — 2 marks each):** **Medical:** 1. **Diagnostic ultrasound imaging** (2–15 MHz) — produces real-time images of internal organs, foetal development, blood flow via Doppler. 2. **High-Intensity Focused Ultrasound (HIFU)** — non-invasive ablation of tumours and uterine fibroids at focused intensities > 1000 W/cm². **Industrial:** 1. **Non-Destructive Testing (NDT)** — detection of cracks, voids, delaminations in welds, castings, aerospace composites using 1–10 MHz probes. 2. **Ultrasonic cleaning** — cavitation in 40 kHz baths removes sub-micron contaminants from PCBs, jewellery, and surgical instruments. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout (Q B.2):** > - **Common Error 1 (1–2 mark loss):** Writing the formula for $k^2$ but not converting to $k$ when the question asks for the *coupling coefficient* (not its square). Always check: does the question ask for $k$ or $k^2$? > - **Common Error 2 (1 mark loss):** Stating applications too generically (e.g., "used in hospitals" earns only partial marks). KTU 2024 demands specific mention of *frequency*, *mode*, and *physical mechanism*. > - **Common Error 3 (1 mark loss):** Forgetting to label the axes of any diagram and not specifying the polarization direction of the PZT disc. --- ## 📋 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Before stepping into the exam hall, ensure you can tick every box below.** - [ ] **Definition:** Ultrasonic transducer = electroacoustic device operating **above 20 kHz** based on **piezoelectric effect**. - [ ] **Two key effects:** **Direct** (mech → elec, for sensing) and **Inverse** (elec → mech, for actuation). - [ ] **Resonance frequency** of thickness mode: $f_r = v_p / (2t)$ — *halving thickness doubles frequency*. - [ ] **Acoustic impedance:** $Z = \rho c$ — units are **Rayl** (or **MRayl** for solids). - [ ] **Reflection coefficient** at normal incidence: $R = (Z_2 - Z_1)/(Z_2 + Z_1)$. - [ ] **Coupling coefficient:** $k^2 = (f_a^2 - f_r^2)/f_a^2$ — higher $k$ means broader bandwidth and better efficiency. - [ ] **PZT-5A / PZT-5H** are the most common piezoelectric materials in commercial transducers. - [ ] **Backing layer** = damping → *shorter pulse → better axial resolution*. - [ ] **Matching layer** (quarter-wave) = impedance matching → *maximum energy transfer into the test medium*. - [ ] **Pulse-echo equation** for flaw distance: $d = (c \cdot t_{TOF})/2$ (factor of 2 for round-trip). - [ ] **Doppler shift** for flow: $\Delta f = (2 f_0 v \cos\theta)/c$. - [ ] **Constitutive equations:** $D = d\sigma + \varepsilon^{T} E$ and $S = s^{E}\sigma + d E$. - [ ] **Key medical frequencies:** Imaging 2–15 MHz; HIFU 1–4 MHz; physiotherapy 1–3 MHz. - [ ] **Key industrial frequencies:** NDT 1–10 MHz; cleaning 20–40 kHz; sonar 40–500 kHz. - [ ] **Always use SI units** (m, s, kg) in calculations; convert mm → m, g/cm³ → kg/m³. - [ ] **Diagram must include:** housing, backing, PZT, electrodes, matching layer, wear plate (in that order). - [ ] **Examiner hates:** forgetting the factor of 2 in pulse-echo distance formula; using $c$ instead of $v_p$ in resonance equation; mixing up $f_r$ and $f_a$. > [!TIP] > **Final KTU 2024 Strategy Note:** Module 4 of PCBRT303 is *high-yield*. Expect at least one 14-mark question on ultrasonic or piezoelectric transducers in ESE. Master the *derivation of $f_r$* and the *pulse-echo distance formula* — these are almost guaranteed in Part B.

topics.title: LIDAR; Actuators for Robots - Classification - Electric, Hydraulic, Pneumatic Actuators
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBRT303 - SENSORS AND ACTUATORS
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** LIDAR; Actuators for Robots - Classification - Electric, Hydraulic, Pneumatic Actuators

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 LIDAR (Light Detection and Ranging)

**Formal Academic Definition:**
LIDAR is an active remote sensing technology that determines the distance to a target (or surrounding objects) by illuminating it with pulsed laser light and measuring the time taken for the reflected pulse to return to a photodetector. The working principle is based on **Time-of-Flight (ToF)** measurement, where the round-trip time $t$ of the laser pulse is converted to distance using the known speed of light.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** LIDAR falls under "Modern Optical & Ranging Sensors." Focus on the Time-of-Flight principle, the radar-range equation in optical form, and the distinction between scanning (mechanical) and solid-state (MEMS, Flash) architectures.

**Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:**
Imagine you stand at one end of a large empty hall and shout loudly toward the far wall. The time it takes to hear the echo tells you roughly how far the wall is — louder echo = closer wall; weaker echo = farther wall. LIDAR does the exact same thing, but instead of sound, it fires ultra-fast pulses of invisible **infrared laser light** (traveling at $\mathbf{3 \times 10^8 \ m/s}$) and listens for the returning "echo" with an ultra-sensitive photodiode. A car uses this echo data to "paint" a 3-D map of its surroundings hundreds of times per second.

**Core Physical Constants & Standard Metrics:**
- Speed of light in vacuum: $c = 3 \times 10^8 \ m/s$
- Typical LIDAR wavelength: **905 nm** (silicon-safe) or **1550 nm** (eye-safe, longer range)
- Angular resolution: $0.1^\circ$ to $1^\circ$ depending on scan mechanism
- Standard accuracy: $\pm \mathbf{2 \ cm}$ at $\mathbf{100 \ m}$ range
- Operating scan frequency: **5 – 20 Hz** for automotive, **up to 1 MHz** for Flash LIDAR

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition Callout — Point Cloud:** The raw output of a LIDAR sensor is a "point cloud," which is a dense collection of $(x, y, z)$ coordinates representing the 3-D geometry of the scanned environment.

---

### 1.2 Actuators for Robots — Formal Definition

**Formal Academic Definition:**
An **actuator** is a mechanical or electro-mechanical device that converts a control signal (electrical, hydraulic, pneumatic, or thermal energy) into physical motion — typically translational (linear) or rotational — thereby producing **force, torque, or displacement** as the controlled output. In robotics, actuators function as the "muscles" of the system, executing the motion commands generated by the controller.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Classification Mandate:** Actuators for robots are classified into three primary families based on the energy domain used:
> 1. **Electric Actuators** (DC, Stepper, Servo, BLDC motors; solenoids)
> 2. **Hydraulic Actuators** (Cylinders, Rotary vane / piston motors using incompressible oil)
> 3. **Pneumatic Actuators** (Cylinders, Air motors using compressible gas)

**Conceptual Analogy — The Robot's Muscles:**
Think of a robot like a human body. The **brain** is the microcontroller, the **nerves** are the sensors (covered earlier in the syllabus), and the **muscles** that actually move the arms, legs, and fingers are the actuators. Just as humans use three primary muscle types — quick precise (e.g., eye muscles = *electric servo*), powerful slow (e.g., legs lifting weights = *hydraulic*), and light rapid (e.g., finger taps = *pneumatic*) — robots pick from these three actuator families depending on the speed, precision, and force they need.

> [!NOTE]
> **Selection Criteria — Three Cardinal Parameters:**
> Every actuator is selected based on:
> - **Force / Torque Output** (Newtons or N·m)
> - **Speed of Response** (m/s or rad/s)
> - **Precision / Positioning Accuracy** (mm or arc-seconds)
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 LIDAR — Operational Concept Breakdown

**Working Principle (Step-by-Step Logic):**

1. **Pulse Generation** — A laser diode (commonly GaAs at 905 nm) emits a short, high-power optical pulse (typically **5–10 ns** width, peak power **10–100 W**).
2. **Beam Steering** — The pulse is directed toward the target via a rotating mirror (mechanical LIDAR), MEMS mirror, or phased optical array (solid-state LIDAR).
3. **Photon-Surface Interaction** — The pulse strikes the target and scatters diffusely (Lambertian reflection).
4. **Return Detection** — A small fraction of photons returns and is collected by the photodetector (APD — Avalanche Photodiode, or SPAD).
5. **Time-of-Flight Computation** — High-precision timing electronics (TCSPC — Time-Correlated Single Photon Counting) measure the round-trip time $t$.
6. **Distance Calculation** — $d = \dfrac{c \cdot t}{2}$.
7. **Scan & Repeat** — The mirror rotates by a small angular step ($\Delta \theta \approx 0.1^\circ$–$1^\circ$), and the next pulse is fired; hundreds of thousands of points are collected per second.

**The 'Why' and 'How':**
- **Why divide by 2?** Because $t$ is the *round-trip* time — the light travels the distance *twice* (out and back).
- **Why use laser instead of radar?** Because the much shorter wavelength (905 nm vs. several mm for radar) allows sub-centimeter resolution and the ability to detect small objects (e.g., wires, pedestrians).
- **How does it work in bright sunlight?** Narrow optical bandpass filters (typically $\pm$ 10 nm around the laser wavelength) reject background solar radiation.

---

### 2.2 LIDAR Range Equation (Optical Radar Equation)

The received optical power $P_r$ at the LIDAR detector is governed by the **LIDAR Equation**:

$$
P_r = P_t \cdot \frac{c \cdot \tau}{2} \cdot \eta_t \cdot \eta_r \cdot \frac{A_r}{R^2} \cdot \frac{\rho \cdot \cos(\theta)}{R^2} \cdot T_{atm}^2
$$

Where:
- $P_t$ = transmitted peak optical power (W)
- $\tau$ = pulse width (s)
- $\eta_t, \eta_r$ = transmitter and receiver optical efficiencies
- $A_r$ = receiver aperture area ($m^2$)
- $R$ = slant range to target (m)
- $\rho$ = target reflectivity (Lambertian)
- $\theta$ = angle of incidence
- $T_{atm}$ = one-way atmospheric transmission

> [!NOTE]
> **Inverse $R^4$ Behavior:** The $P_r \propto \dfrac{1}{R^4}$ dependence is the optical equivalent of the radar equation. This is why extending LIDAR range requires **larger receiver apertures** and **higher pulse energies**.

---

### 2.3 Actuator Classification — The Three Families

#### A. Electric Actuators

**Operational Concept:**
Electric actuators convert electrical energy directly into mechanical motion via the interaction of magnetic fields and current-carrying conductors. The dominant families in robotics are:

| Sub-type | Operating Principle | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|
| **DC Motor (Brushed)** | Lorentz force on armature in stator field | Low-cost toys, wheels |
| **Brushless DC (BLDC)** | Electronic commutation of stator coils | Drones, EV wheels, robot joints |
| **Stepper Motor** | Open-loop incremental angular steps | 3-D printers, CNC feed drives |
| **Servo Motor (DC + Encoder + PID)** | Closed-loop position/velocity control | Robot arms, CNC spindles |
| **Solenoid** | Electromagnetic pull on a ferrous plunger | Valves, door locks |

**Speed–Torque Relation (DC Motor):**
$$
\omega = \omega_{NL} \left(1 - \frac{T}{T_{stall}}\right)
$$

**Servo Control Block Diagram (in words):** Reference Position → Error Detector → PID Controller → PWM Amplifier → DC Motor → Output Shaft → Encoder feedback to Error Detector.

#### B. Hydraulic Actuators

**Operational Concept:**
Hydraulic actuators use an **incompressible fluid** (typically mineral oil, viscosity ISO VG 32–68) pressurized by a pump to **5–35 MPa** (≈ 50–350 bar). The pressurized fluid acts on a piston to produce large linear forces or, via a rotary vane/gear arrangement, large torques.

**Components:** Reservoir → Filter → Pump (gear/piston) → Pressure Relief Valve → Directional Control Valve (DCV) → Cylinder/Motor → Return Line → Reservoir.

**Force Produced (Linear Cylinder):**
$$
F = P_{gauge} \cdot A_{piston}
$$

#### C. Pneumatic Actuators

**Operational Concept:**
Pneumatic actuators use **compressible gas** (typically filtered, dehumidified air at **0.4–0.8 MPa**) to drive motion. They are inherently **lower force, lower precision, lower cost** than hydraulics, but offer very high speed, cleanliness, and inherent compliance (safety).

**Same Force Equation:**
$$
F = P_{gauge} \cdot A_{piston}
$$
But because $P$ is much lower and air is compressible, the practical force is significantly less than hydraulic equivalents.

---

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mandatory formulas for the KTU 2024 Board Examination.** Memorize these — they appear in nearly every Part-B module-4 question.

| # | Equation | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $d = \dfrac{c \cdot t}{2}$ | LIDAR distance from Time-of-Flight | $m$ |
| 2 | $P_r \propto \dfrac{P_t \cdot A_r^2 \cdot \rho}{R^4}$ | LIDAR received power (radar-style form) | $W$ |
| 3 | $\Delta x_{min} = \dfrac{c \cdot \tau}{2}$ | Range resolution from pulse width $\tau$ | $m$ |
| 4 | $F = P \cdot A$ | Actuator force from pressure | $N$ |
| 5 | $T = P \cdot \dfrac{D_{p}}{2} \cdot A_{p}$ | Hydraulic motor torque | $N \cdot m$ |
| 6 | $P_{hyd} = \dfrac{Q \cdot p}{600}$ | Hydraulic power (with $Q$ in L/min, $p$ in bar) | $kW$ |
| 7 | $\omega = \omega_{NL}\left(1 - \dfrac{T}{T_{stall}}\right)$ | DC motor speed–torque line | $rad/s$ |
| 8 | $\theta_{step} = \dfrac{360^\circ}{N_{steps}}$ | Stepper motor step angle | degrees |
| 9 | $\Delta V_{air} = \dfrac{P_{atm} \cdot V_0}{P_{tank}} - V_0$ | Compressed air volume expansion | $m^3$ |
| 10 | $v_{piston} = \dfrac{Q}{A}$ | Piston velocity from flow rate | $m/s$ |

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Utility:** The same force equation $F = P \cdot A$ governs both hydraulic and pneumatic cylinders — the *selection* between them comes from required force density, precision, and cleanliness. Hydraulics dominate in heavy machinery (excavators, aircraft control surfaces historically); pneumatics dominate in food/pharma and low-cost automation.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation — LIDAR Distance from Time-of-Flight

The derivation begins from the definition of uniform velocity:

$$
v = \frac{\text{distance travelled}}{\text{time taken}}
$$

For light traveling from the LIDAR to the target at distance $R$ and back, the **total distance** covered in time $t$ is $2R$. Since light travels at the constant speed $c$ in the atmosphere (negligible refractive index variation over short paths):

$$
c = \frac{2R}{t}
$$

Rearranging for the one-way range $R$:

$$
R = \frac{c \cdot t}{2}
$$

**Validity / Boundary Conditions:**
- Assumes **vacuum-like propagation**: $c \approx 3 \times 10^8 \ m/s$. For path lengths under a few kilometers, atmospheric correction is $< 10^{-6}$ and can be ignored.
- Assumes **point target / hard target** with negligible pulse dispersion.
- Assumes the target is **stationary** during the round-trip. For moving targets, a Doppler shift correction is required.

---

### 3.2 Derivation — LIDAR Range Resolution from Pulse Width

Range resolution $\Delta R$ is defined as the minimum distance between two targets such that their return pulses can still be distinguished as separate. If the pulse has temporal width $\tau$, then two pulses returning from targets separated by $\Delta R$ arrive separated by $\Delta t = 2\Delta R / c$.

For the two pulses to be just resolvable, this separation must equal the pulse width:

$$
\Delta t = \tau \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{2 \Delta R}{c} = \tau
$$

Solving for $\Delta R$:

$$
\Delta R = \frac{c \cdot \tau}{2}
$$

**Numerical Example:** A pulse width of $\tau = 10 \ ns = 10^{-8} \ s$ yields:

$$
\Delta R = \frac{3 \times 10^8 \cdot 10^{-8}}{2} = 1.5 \ m
$$

To achieve 15 cm resolution, the pulse must be shortened to $\tau = 1 \ ns$.

---

### 3.3 Derivation — Hydraulic Cylinder Force and Power

**Force Derivation:**
Pressure is defined as force per unit area: $P = F/A$. For a piston of cross-sectional area $A$ subjected to gauge pressure $P$:

$$
F = P \cdot A
$$

For a double-acting cylinder with piston diameter $D_p$ and rod diameter $D_r$:

$$
F_{extend} = P \cdot \frac{\pi D_p^2}{4} \qquad F_{retract} = P \cdot \frac{\pi (D_p^2 - D_r^2)}{4}
$$

**Power Derivation:**
Volume flow rate $Q$ (m³/s) × Pressure $P$ (Pa) gives the **hydraulic power** transmitted to the actuator:

$$
P_{hyd} = Q \cdot p
$$

Converting $Q$ from litres/min to m³/s: $Q_{SI} = Q_{L/min} / 60000$. Expressing pressure in bar ($1 \ bar = 10^5 \ Pa$), the convenient form for industry is:

$$
P_{hyd} \ [kW] = \frac{Q \ [L/min] \cdot p \ [bar]}{600}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Worked-out Numerical Example:**
> A hydraulic cylinder has $D_p = 80 \ mm$, $D_r = 45 \ mm$, supply pressure $p = 100 \ bar = 10^7 \ Pa$, and pump flow $Q = 20 \ L/min$.
> 
> Extend force:
> 
> $$
> F_{extend} = 10^7 \cdot \frac{\pi \cdot (0.08)^2}{4} = 10^7 \cdot 5.027 \times 10^{-3} \approx 50265 \ N \approx 50.3 \ kN
> $$
> 
> Extend power:
> 
> $$
> P_{hyd} = \frac{20 \cdot 100}{600} = 3.33 \ kW
> $$

---

### 3.4 Derivation — DC Motor Speed–Torque Line

The internal EMF (back-EMF) of a DC motor at angular velocity $\omega$ is $E = K_e \cdot \omega$. By KVL on the armature circuit (resistance $R_a$, applied voltage $V$):

$$
V = I_a R_a + K_e \omega
$$

The developed electromagnetic torque is $T = K_t \cdot I_a$, where $K_t$ is the torque constant. Solving for $I_a = T/K_t$ and substituting:

$$
V = \frac{T \cdot R_a}{K_t} + K_e \omega
$$

At **no-load** ($\omega = \omega_{NL}$, $T = 0$): $\omega_{NL} = V/K_e$.
At **stall** ($\omega = 0$, $T = T_{stall}$): $T_{stall} = V \cdot K_t / R_a$.

Rearranging the KVL equation into the canonical speed–torque line:

$$
\omega = \omega_{NL} \left(1 - \frac{T}{T_{stall}}\right)
$$

This linear relation is the foundation for selecting gear ratios and matching motor ratings to load requirements in robotic joints.

---

### 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix — The Three Actuator Families

| Parameter | Electric | Hydraulic | Pneumatic |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Energy Source** | Electrical (DC/AC) | Pressurized oil (5–35 MPa) | Compressed air (0.4–0.8 MPa) |
| **Force / Torque Density** | Low–Medium | **Very High** | Low |
| **Precision** | **Very High** (sub-mm with encoder) | Medium | Low (compressibility) |
| **Speed** | High | Medium | **Very High** |
| **Compliance / Safety** | Low (rigid) | Medium | **High** (inherently soft) |
| **Cleanliness** | **Clean** | Risk of oil leaks | **Very Clean** |
| **Cost (per unit force)** | High | High infrastructure | **Low** |
| **Maintenance** | Low | High (filters, seals) | Low–Medium |
| **Noise** | Quiet | Medium | **Loud** |
| **Typical Applications** | Robot arms, CNC, drones | Excavators, forging presses | Pick-and-place, food industry |
| **Example Robot** | KUKA KR 16 (electric arm) | Boston Dynamics Atlas (hydraulic) | Soft robotics grippers, pickers |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 LIDAR Functional Architecture (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Laser Diode Driver] --> B[Pulsed Laser Emitter 905 nm]
    B --> C[Beam Steering Optics MEMS or Rotary Mirror]
    C --> D[Target Object Reflects Diffuse]
    D --> E[Receiver Optics Telescope]
    E --> F[Bandpass Optical Filter 905 nm PM 10 nm]
    F --> G[Photodetector APD or SPAD]
    G --> H[Time to Digital Converter TDC]
    H --> I[Signal Processor DSP]
    I --> J[Point Cloud Generation X Y Z]
    I --> K[Host Computer or ECU]
    K --> L[Object Detection and SLAM]

    style J fill:#a9dfbf,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    style L fill:#f5b7b1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
```

**Interpretation:** The transmitter chain (left) sends a pulse; the receiver chain (right) detects the return; the TDC measures the time difference; the DSP computes distances and assembles a point cloud that is consumed by perception algorithms.

---
