<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 📝 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]**
Define LIDAR. Explain the Time-of-Flight (ToF) principle used in LIDAR for distance measurement.

**Model Answer:**
LIDAR (Light Detection and Ranging) is an active remote-sensing technique that determines the distance to a target by transmitting a laser pulse and measuring the time taken by the reflected pulse to return to the sensor. The Time-of-Flight principle uses the equation $d = (c \cdot t) / 2$, where $c$ is the speed of light ($3 \times 10^8 \ m/s$) and $t$ is the round-trip time. Because light travels approximately one foot per nanosecond, sub-centimeter precision is achievable with sub-nanosecond timing electronics.

*(3 marks — Complete, exact KTU expected answer)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Jun 2024 Model]**
List and briefly compare the three main classifications of actuators used in robots.

**Model Answer:**
The three classifications are:
1. **Electric Actuators** — Convert electrical energy into motion using DC, stepper, BLDC, or servo motors. High precision, clean, but moderate force.
2. **Hydraulic Actuators** — Use pressurized oil (5–35 MPa) in cylinders/motors. Very high force density, used in heavy robots (e.g., Boston Dynamics Atlas).
3. **Pneumatic Actuators** — Use compressed air (0.4–0.8 MPa). Low cost, fast, compliant, used in pick-and-place and soft robotics.

*(3 marks — Tabular comparison fetches full marks)*

---

### 📝 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice)

> **Question A — LIDAR-Focused**

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]**

**Q-A (a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the working principle of a LIDAR system. Derive the LIDAR distance equation from the Time-of-Flight principle. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Working Principle (5 marks — explanation + block diagram):**
A LIDAR system consists of a laser transmitter, beam-steering optics, a photodetector receiver, timing electronics (TDC), and a signal processor. The laser diode emits short pulses (5–10 ns) at 905 nm or 1550 nm. A rotating or MEMS mirror sweeps the beam across the scene. When the pulse strikes an object, a small fraction of photons scatter back and are collected by a receiver lens, filtered by a narrow bandpass filter to reject solar background, and focused onto an APD or SPAD photodetector. The TDC measures the round-trip time $t$, and the DSP converts it to distance using $d = c t / 2$. Each pulse gives one $(x, y, z)$ point; thousands of pulses per frame build a 3-D point cloud. *(Block diagram: refer Section 4.1.)*

**(ii) Derivation (2 marks):**
Speed = distance / time, so for round-trip:

$$
c = \frac{2R}{t} \quad \Rightarrow \quad R = \frac{c \cdot t}{2}
$$

where $R$ is the one-way distance, $c = 3 \times 10^8 \ m/s$, and $t$ is the round-trip time. This is the canonical LIDAR distance equation.

---

**Q-A (b)** Explain the LIDAR range equation. Discuss how atmospheric attenuation, target reflectivity, and pulse energy affect the maximum detection range. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) LIDAR Range Equation (4 marks):**

$$
P_r = P_t \cdot \frac{c \cdot \tau}{2} \cdot \eta_t \eta_r \cdot \frac{A_r}{R^2} \cdot \frac{\rho \cos\theta}{R^2} \cdot T_{atm}^2
$$

The received power $P_r$ depends on transmitted power $P_t$, pulse width $\tau$, optical efficiencies $\eta_t, \eta_r$, receiver aperture area $A_r$, range $R$, target reflectivity $\rho$, and two-way atmospheric transmission $T_{atm}^2$. The inverse $R^4$ dependence is the dominant factor limiting range.

**(ii) Effects on Range (3 marks):**
- **Atmospheric Attenuation:** Fog, rain, and dust scatter the beam, reducing $T_{atm}$. At 905 nm, fog attenuation is severe (≈ 10 dB/km in heavy fog). 1550 nm is less affected.
- **Target Reflectivity:** A black surface ($\rho \approx 0.05$) returns far less light than a white wall ($\rho \approx 0.9$), reducing range by up to 13×.
- **Pulse Energy:** Increasing $P_t \cdot \tau$ linearly raises $P_r$, but eye-safety limits restrict peak power; this drives the choice of 1550 nm LIDAR for long-range automotive use.

---

> **Question B — Actuators-Focused (Internal Choice Alternative)**

**[KTU University Exam — Jun 2024 Model]**

**Q-B (a)** Classify the actuators used in robots. With suitable diagrams, explain the working of electric actuators (DC motor, stepper motor, servo motor). Compare them on the basis of precision, cost, and torque. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Classification (2 marks):** Refer to Section 4.5 tree diagram — Electric, Hydraulic, Pneumatic.

**(ii) Electric Actuators (5 marks):**
- **DC Motor:** A brushed DC motor has a rotating armature in a permanent-magnet stator field. Applying voltage $V$ drives a current $I_a = (V - K_e \omega)/R_a$, producing torque $T = K_t I_a$. Simple, cheap, but brushes wear out.
- **Stepper Motor:** A multi-phase motor that rotates in discrete angular increments $\theta_{step} = 360^\circ / N_{steps}$ (e.g., $1.8^\circ$ per step). Open-loop control — no encoder needed. Excellent for low-speed positioning (3-D printers, CNC).
- **Servo Motor:** A BLDC or brushed DC motor combined with a high-resolution encoder, gearhead, and integrated PID controller. Provides closed-loop position/velocity control with sub-millimeter accuracy. Standard for robotic arms.

**Comparison Table (built into answer):**

| Parameter | DC Motor | Stepper | Servo |
|---|---|---|---|
| Precision | Medium | High (open-loop) | **Very High** (closed-loop) |
| Cost | Low | Medium | High |
| Torque at Low Speed | Medium | **High** | High |
| Holding Torque | None | **Yes** (detent) | Yes (active) |

---

**Q-B (b)** Explain the working of hydraulic and pneumatic actuators. Compare their performance characteristics in robotic applications. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Hydraulic Actuators (3 marks):**
A pump pressurizes hydraulic oil (typically 5–35 MPa). The pressurized fluid is directed via a directional control valve to one side of a double-acting cylinder, pushing the piston to produce linear force $F = P \cdot A$. Retraction occurs when the valve reverses flow. Rotary hydraulic motors use a swash-plate mechanism to convert pressure into torque. Hydraulics provide very high force density (up to 10× that of pneumatics), enabling heavy-payload robots like Boston Dynamics Atlas to perform backflips.

**(ii) Pneumatic Actuators (2 marks):**
Compressed air (0.4–0.8 MPa, clean and dry) is supplied via an FRL (Filter-Regulator-Lubricator) to a directional valve. The valve routes air to either side of a cylinder, producing linear motion. Air motors (rotary vane or piston) provide rotational motion. Pneumatics are inherently compliant, making them safe for human-robot collaboration.

**(iii) Comparison (2 marks):**

| Parameter | Hydraulic | Pneumatic |
|---|---|---|
| Working Pressure | 5–35 MPa | 0.4–0.8 MPa |
| Force Density | **Very High** | Low |
| Precision | Medium | **Low** (compressibility) |
| Speed | Medium | **High** |
| Cost (Infrastructure) | High | **Low** |
| Cleanliness | Oil risk | **Clean** |
| Application Example | Excavator arm | Pick-and-place gripper |

---

### ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where Students Lose Marks — Module 4 Actuators:**
> 1. **Forgetting the factor of 2** in the LIDAR distance equation — examiners *specifically* check $d = ct/2$, not $d = ct$. Losing 2 marks here is a classic.
> 2. **Confusing hydraulic and pneumatic pressures** — writing 35 bar for pneumatics or 5 bar for hydraulics is a frequently penalized error. Memorize: **Hydraulics = 100–350 bar; Pneumatics = 4–8 bar.**
> 3. **Skipping the block diagram in Q-A(a)** — KTU examiners explicitly allocate marks for a "neat block diagram." A textual description alone loses at least 2 of the 5 content marks.
> 4. **Listing applications without specifying the energy domain** — Vague "used in robots" answers score 1/3; mention *which* robot (Atlas = hydraulic, KUKA = electric) for full credit.
> 5. **Mixing up servo motor and stepper motor characteristics** — Stepper is open-loop and loses torque at high speed; servo is closed-loop and maintains torque. Examiners test this contrast directly.

---

### 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [ ] **LIDAR = Time-of-Flight → $d = ct/2$** — always divide by 2.
- [ ] **LIDAR range equation has $R^4$ dependence** — same form as radar, with optical aperture playing the role of antenna gain.
- [ ] **Pulse width $\tau$ sets range resolution:** $\Delta R = c\tau/2$.
- [ ] **Two dominant LIDAR wavelengths:** 905 nm (silicon detector, cheaper) and 1550 nm (eye-safe, longer range).
- [ ] **Three actuator families:** Electric, Hydraulic, Pneumatic — selected by force, precision, and environment.
- [ ] **Force equation is universal:** $F = P \cdot A$ applies to all fluid-power actuators.
- [ ] **Hydraulic pressure (5–35 MPa) ≫ Pneumatic pressure (0.4–0.8 MPa)** — 10×+ difference.
- [ ] **Hydraulic power formula:** $P_{hyd} [kW] = Q [L/min] \cdot p [bar] / 600$.
- [ ] **DC motor speed–torque is linear:** $\omega = \omega_{NL}(1 - T/T_{stall})$.
- [ ] **Stepper = open-loop, servo = closed-loop** — choice dictates precision and complexity.
- [ ] **Pneumatics are inherently compliant** — preferred for human-safe collaborative robots.
- [ ] **Hydraulics dominate heavy-payload robotics** — Atlas, BigDog, excavators.
- [ ] **Electrics dominate precision robotics** — KUKA, ABB, Fanuc arms.
- [ ] **Memorize one industrial robot per category** for full application-mark credit.
- [ ] **Block diagrams are compulsory** for any KTU part-(a) descriptive question — always include them.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Their Advantages and Disadvantages
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBRT303 - SENSORS AND ACTUATORS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Their Advantages and Disadvantages

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Context Within Module 4 — Actuators

Module 4 of **PCBRT303 (Sensors and Actuators)** under the **KTU 2024 Scheme** deals fundamentally with **Actuators** — the physical "muscles" of any mechatronic/automation system. While a sensor *perceives* a physical variable and converts it into a measurable signal, an actuator does the *opposite*: it takes an electrical/control signal and converts it into a **physical motion, force, or displacement**.

The phrase **"Their Advantages and Disadvantages"** is the KTU board's standard phrasing for a comparative analytical question requiring the student to critically evaluate the strengths and weaknesses of the major actuator families covered in the syllabus:

1. **Hydraulic Actuators**
2. **Pneumatic Actuators**
3. **Electrical / Electromagnetic Actuators** (DC motors, Stepper motors, Servo motors, Solenoids)
4. **Piezoelectric Actuators**
5. **MEMS Actuators**
6. **Shape Memory Alloy (SMA) Actuators**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition (Verbatim Tone):** An actuator is a device that converts energy — typically electrical, hydraulic, pneumatic, or thermal — into a physical output, usually in the form of motion, force, or torque, in response to a control signal.

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine a human body:
- The **nervous system + eyes/ears** = *Sensors* (Module 1–3)
- The **muscles and joints** = *Actuators* (Module 4)

Different muscles serve different jobs:
- Your **biceps** (strong, slow, heavy lifting) → like a **Hydraulic Actuator**
- Your **eyelids** (fast, light, quick action) → like a **Pneumatic Actuator**
- Your **finger twitches** (precise, controllable) → like a **Servo Motor**
- The **hair cells in your ear** (micro-scale, high precision) → like a **Piezoelectric / MEMS Actuator**

> [!NOTE]
> **Pedagogical Note for KTU Students:** When answering a comparison question, always structure your answer with a **two-column mental model**: *What it does WELL* (advantages) vs *What it does POORLY* (disadvantages). Examiners award marks for *justified* points, not just listing features.

---

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Metric | Symbol | Typical Value / Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Standard atmospheric pressure | $P_{atm}$ | **101.325 kPa (1.01325 bar)** |
| Hydraulic fluid bulk modulus | $K$ | **~1.5–2.0 GPa** (mineral oil) |
| Piezoelectric strain coefficient | $d_{33}$ | **~250–600 pC/N** (PZT ceramics) |
| SMA transition temperature (Nitinol) | $T_{trans}$ | **~45–110 °C** |
| Air compressibility factor (pneumatic) | — | Highly compressible (vs. oil) |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Comparative Operational Analysis

### 2.1.1 Hydraulic Actuators
**Operating Principle:** Use an incompressible fluid (typically mineral oil) pressurized by a pump. Pressure energy is converted into linear (cylinder-piston) or rotary (hydraulic motor) mechanical motion.

**Key Equation (Force generated by a hydraulic cylinder):**
$$F = P \times A$$

where $P$ = fluid pressure (Pa) and $A$ = piston face area (m²).

### 2.1.2 Pneumatic Actuators
**Operating Principle:** Use a compressible gas (air) at pressures typically 4–8 bar. The compressibility of air makes them inherently compliant.

**Key Equation:**
$$F = P \times A - F_{friction} - F_{spring}$$

### 2.1.3 Electromagnetic Actuators
**Operating Principle:** Convert electrical energy into mechanical motion via Lorentz force ($F = BIL$) or variable reluctance.

**Key Equation (DC Motor Torque):**
$$T = K_t \times I$$

### 2.1.4 Piezoelectric Actuators
**Operating Principle:** Inverse piezoelectric effect — applied electric field $E$ causes mechanical strain $S = d \cdot E$.

**Key Equation:**
$$S = d_{33} \cdot E$$

### 2.1.5 MEMS Actuators
**Operating Principle:** Micro-scale versions of electrostatic, thermal, or piezoelectric mechanisms, fabricated using silicon micromachining.

### 2.1.6 Shape Memory Alloy (SMA) Actuators
**Operating Principle:** Thermo-mechanical phase transformation between martensite and austenite phases of Nitinol.

---

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these equations — they are the most-tested formulas in KTU university exams for Module 4.**

| # | Formula | Meaning | Units | Typical Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $F = P \cdot A$ | Hydraulic/Pneumatic force | N | Cylinder sizing |
| 2 | $T = K_t \cdot I$ | DC motor torque | N·m | Motor selection |
| 3 | $S = d_{33} \cdot E$ | Piezo strain | dimensionless | Nano-positioning |
| 4 | $V = \dfrac{\Delta V}{\Delta t}$ | Flow rate | m³/s | Pump sizing |
| 5 | $P_{out} = P_{in} - \Delta P$ | Pressure drop across valve | Pa | Valve design |
| 6 | $W = \int F \cdot dx$ | Work done by actuator | J | Energy calculation |
| 7 | $\eta = \dfrac{P_{out}}{P_{in}} \times 100\%$ | Efficiency | % | System analysis |
| 8 | $\tau = \dfrac{\eta \cdot dP}{dx}$ | Shear stress (fluid) | Pa | Hydraulic line loss |
| 9 | $f_{res} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{k}{m}}$ | Resonant frequency | Hz | Dynamic response |
| 10 | $K_p = \dfrac{F}{\delta}$ | Actuator stiffness | N/m | Precision analysis |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** Question 2 of Part B (14 marks) on this module **frequently** asks for a tabular comparison of two actuator types along with a derived equation. Memorize the above table.

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Hydraulic actuators** → Earth-moving equipment (JCB, excavators), aircraft landing gear, hydraulic presses.
- **Pneumatic actuators** → Factory automation (pick-and-place), dental drills, brake systems in trucks.
- **Electromagnetic actuators** → Robotics, CNC machines, hard disk drive head positioning.
- **Piezoelectric actuators** → Scanning probe microscopes (AFM), inkjet printer heads, adaptive optics in telescopes.
- **MEMS actuators** → Smartphone gyroscopes, micro-mirrors in projectors, lab-on-chip devices.
- **SMA actuators** → Stents in biomedical devices, robotic grippers, deployable space structures.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Detailed Derivation — Hydraulic Cylinder Force Output

**Problem Context:** A double-acting hydraulic cylinder has a piston diameter of $D = 50$ mm and a rod diameter of $d = 30$ mm. The pump supplies a pressure of $P = 100$ bar. Calculate:
- (a) The extension (push) force $F_{ext}$
- (b) The retraction (pull) force $F_{ret}$
- (c) The work done in a full stroke of $L = 200$ mm

**Given:**
- $D = 50$ mm $= 0.05$ m
- $d = 30$ mm $= 0.03$ m
- $P = 100$ bar $= 100 \times 10^5$ Pa $= 10^7$ Pa
- $L = 200$ mm $= 0.2$ m

**Step 1 — Piston full face area (extension):**
$$A_{ext} = \frac{\pi D^2}{4} = \frac{\pi (0.05)^2}{4}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
A_{ext} &= \frac{\pi \times 0.0025}{4} \\
&= \frac{0.007854}{4} \\
&= 1.9635 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Extension force $F_{ext}$:**
$$\begin{aligned}
F_{ext} &= P \times A_{ext} \\
&= 10^7 \times 1.9635 \times 10^{-3} \\
&= 19635 \text{ N} \approx 19.635 \text{ kN}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Annular (retraction) area:**
$$A_{ret} = \frac{\pi (D^2 - d^2)}{4}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
A_{ret} &= \frac{\pi \left[(0.05)^2 - (0.03)^2\right]}{4} \\
&= \frac{\pi \left[0.0025 - 0.0009\right]}{4} \\
&= \frac{\pi \times 0.0016}{4} \\
&= 1.2566 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Retraction force $F_{ret}$:**
$$\begin{aligned}
F_{ret} &= P \times A_{ret} \\
&= 10^7 \times 1.2566 \times 10^{-3} \\
&= 12566 \text{ N} \approx 12.566 \text{ kN}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5 — Work done in extension stroke:**
$$\begin{aligned}
W_{ext} &= F_{ext} \times L \\
&= 19635 \times 0.2 \\
&= 3927 \text{ J} \approx 3.927 \text{ kJ}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 6 — Work done in retraction stroke:**
$$\begin{aligned}
W_{ret} &= F_{ret} \times L \\
&= 12566 \times 0.2 \\
&= 2513.2 \text{ J} \approx 2.513 \text{ kJ}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 7 — Total work in one full cycle:**
$$W_{total} = W_{ext} + W_{ret} = 3.927 + 2.513 = 6.44 \text{ kJ}$$

---

## 3.2 Master Analytical Matrix — Advantages & Disadvantages

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **single most important table** for KTU 2024 Scheme Module 4. Memorize the structure (not verbatim) before every exam.

| Actuator Type | Key Advantages | Key Disadvantages |
|---|---|---|
| **Hydraulic** | • Highest power-to-weight ratio • Generates very large forces (kN range) • Smooth, precise linear motion • Self-lubricating | • Requires pump, reservoir, filters → bulky • Oil leaks are environmentally hazardous • Compressible at very high pressure (cavitation risk) • Slow response (ms–s range) • High maintenance cost |
| **Pneumatic** | • Clean (uses air) • Fast response (ms) • Simple, low-cost design • Safe in flammable environments | • Air is compressible → poor position accuracy • Lower force output than hydraulic • Noisy operation • Needs air compressor + dryer • Not suitable for high-precision tasks |
| **Electrical (DC/Stepper/Servo)** | • High precision (sub-micron possible) • Easy computer/PLC interfacing • Clean, no fluid leaks • Long operational life | • Limited force/torque for size (overheats at high load) • Requires gear reduction for high torque • Brush wear in DC motors • Expensive for very high-power applications |
| **Piezoelectric** | • Nanometer resolution • Very fast response (µs–ms) • No moving parts (solid-state) • High stiffness → no backlash | • Very small displacement (µm range) • High-voltage drive required (kV) • Hysteresis in response • Temperature-sensitive |
| **MEMS** | • Tiny size (µm–mm) • Batch fabricated → very low unit cost • Low power consumption | • Small force/torque output • Fragile • Limited stroke • Complex packaging & integration |
| **Shape Memory Alloy (SMA)** | • Very high force-to-weight ratio • Bio-compatible (medical implants) • Silent, simple structure | • Slow response (cooling-limited, seconds) • Hysteresis & fatigue in cycling • Requires heating/cooling system • Energy-inefficient |

---

## 3.3 Step-by-Step Comparison Logic (for 14-mark answers)

When the KTU question asks *"Compare the advantages and disadvantages of Hydraulic and Pneumatic actuators"*:

1. **Introduction (2 marks):** Define both — mention the working fluid and the underlying principle.
2. **Tabular comparison (6 marks):** Use a 2-column table with 5–6 comparison parameters: *Power source, Force output, Speed, Accuracy, Cost, Maintenance, Safety, Cleanliness.*
3. **Application domains (2 marks):** Hydraulic → heavy machinery; Pneumatic → light assembly lines.
4. **Conclusion (2 marks):** State the selection rule — *Hydraulic for high force/low speed; Pneumatic for low force/high speed.*
5. **Diagram (2 marks):** A neat labeled sketch of either a hydraulic or pneumatic cylinder showing the piston, rod, seals, and ports.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Architecture — Actuator Selection Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Design Requirement] --> B{High Force kN Range?}
    B -- Yes --> C{Precision Critical?}
    B -- No --> D{High Speed Response?}
    C -- Yes --> E[Hydraulic Actuator + Servo Valve]
    C -- No --> F[Standard Hydraulic Cylinder]
    D -- Yes --> G{Precision Critical?}
    D -- No --> H{Position Holding Needed?}
    G -- Yes --> I[Pneumatic Servo System]
    G -- No --> J[Standard Pneumatic Cylinder]
    H -- Yes --> K[Stepper Motor]
    H -- No --> L{Analog Control Signal?}
    L -- Yes --> M[DC Servo Motor]
    L -- No --> N[Simple DC Motor / Solenoid]
    E --> O[Selected Actuator]
    F --> O
    I --> O
    J --> O
    K --> O
    M --> O
    N --> O
```

> [!NOTE]
> This flowchart mirrors the **decision tree** that practicing KTU engineers use in industry. Use it as a quick mental model for the application-based part of the answer.

---

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Hydraulic Cylinder

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P[Hydraulic Pump] --> R[Reservoir Tank]
    R --> F1[Filter Unit]
    F1 --> V1[Directional Control Valve]
    V1 --> C1[Cylinder Cap End Port A]
    C1[Cap End Port] --> CY[Piston inside Cylinder]
    CY --> C2[Rod End Port B]
    C2 --> V1
    V1 --> F2[Return Line Filter]
    F2 --> R
    CY --> OUT[Linear Force Output on Rod]
```

**Block Description (for KTU answer writing):**
- **Pump (P):** Source of pressurized fluid.
- **Reservoir (R):** Stores hydraulic oil and allows heat dissipation.
- **Filter (F1):** Removes contaminants to protect the system.
- **Directional Control Valve (V1):** Routes fluid to extend or retract the cylinder.
- **Cylinder (CY):** The actuator itself — converts fluid pressure into linear motion.
- **Output (OUT):** The mechanical force/displacement delivered to the load.

---

## 4.3 Block Diagram — Pneumatic System (with seals, ports, return spring)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    CMP[Air Compressor] --> DRY[Air Dryer and Filter]
    DRY --> FRL[Filter Regulator Lubricator FRL Unit]
    FRL --> VALV[3 by 2 Directional Valve]
    VALV --> PORTA[Cap End Port]
    PORTA --> CYL[Pneumatic Cylinder with Piston]
    CYL --> PORTB[Rod End Port with Return Spring]
    PORTB --> VALV
    CYL --> LOAD[Output Force and Displacement to Load]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **Return Spring** visible in the pneumatic cylinder is what makes it *single-acting* by default — the rod retracts automatically when air is vented. This is a **favorite KTU question**: "Why are pneumatic actuators often single-acting?"

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1: [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper]**
**List any three advantages and three disadvantages of pneumatic actuators compared to hydraulic actuators.**

**Model Answer:**

**Advantages of Pneumatic over Hydraulic:**
1. **Clean and non-polluting** — Pneumatic systems use air, which can be vented directly into the atmosphere without environmental hazard, unlike hydraulic oil leaks.
2. **Faster response time** — Air has low viscosity, so pneumatic actuators respond in milliseconds, making them suitable for high-speed applications such as bottling plants.
3. **Simpler and cheaper infrastructure** — No need for return lines, oil seals, or high-pressure piping; a basic compressor and FRL unit are sufficient.

**Disadvantages of Pneumatic over Hydraulic:**
1. **Lower force output** — Because air is compressible and operating pressures (4–8 bar) are much lower than hydraulic (100–350 bar), the force per unit cylinder area is significantly smaller.
2. **Poor positioning accuracy** — The compressibility of air causes the piston to "hunt" or overshoot, making precise position control difficult without closed-loop feedback.
3. **Noisy operation** — Exhaust air produces a loud hiss (typically 80–100 dB), requiring silencers and acoustic enclosures in industrial environments.

---

### **Question 2: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Paper]**
**Mention two advantages and two disadvantages of piezoelectric actuators.**

**Model Answer:**

**Advantages:**
1. **Ultra-high resolution and precision** — Piezoelectric actuators can produce displacements in the **nanometer range**, making them ideal for applications like atomic force microscopy (AFM) and optical mirror alignment.
2. **Very fast dynamic response** — They can respond in **microseconds to milliseconds**, far faster than electromagnetic or hydraulic actuators, because the mechanism is solid-state with no moving macro-parts.

**Disadvantages:**
1. **Very small stroke length** — The maximum displacement is typically only a few **micrometers** (1–10 µm), which limits their use to fine-tuning applications.
2. **High driving voltage required** — They need amplification electronics to generate **kV-level signals** (typically 100–1000 V), which increases system cost and complexity.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:**
> - **Common Mistake #1:** Students list advantages WITHOUT comparing them to another actuator type. Marks are **deducted (–2)** if the question asks for a *comparison* but you only describe one.
> - **Common Mistake #2:** Skipping the *numerical example* when a derivation is asked. Always include a **solved numerical** with units and final boxed answer.
> - **Common Mistake #3:** Not drawing a labeled diagram. A **neat labeled sketch (2 marks)** is a non-negotiable in a 14-mark question.
> - **Common Mistake #4:** Writing vague points like *"Hydraulic is powerful"* — examiners expect **quantified** statements like *"Hydraulic can deliver forces up to several MN in industrial presses."*

---

### **Question A (Choice 1): [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Expected]**
**(a)** With the help of a neat diagram, explain the construction and working of a **hydraulic actuator**. List **four advantages and four disadvantages** of hydraulic actuators. (7 marks)

**(b)** A double-acting hydraulic cylinder has a bore diameter of 80 mm and a rod diameter of 45 mm. The pump delivers oil at a pressure of 150 bar. Calculate the **extension force**, **retraction force**, and the **power** delivered during extension at a piston velocity of 0.1 m/s. (7 marks)

#### **Model Solution — Part (a):**

**Construction (refer to Section 4.2 block diagram for a schematic aid):**

A hydraulic actuator consists of the following key components:
1. **Cylinder body** — A precision-bored steel tube that houses the piston.
2. **Piston** — Slides inside the cylinder, sealed by **O-rings** and **backup rings** to prevent leakage.
3. **Piston rod** — A hardened chrome-plated steel rod connected to the piston and protruding from one end of the cylinder.
4. **End caps** — Seal the cylinder at both ends; the cap end contains the inlet/outlet ports.
5. **Seals** — Static (between cap and cylinder) and dynamic (around piston and rod).
6. **Cushions** — Optional decelerating devices at the end of stroke to prevent metal-to-metal impact.

**Working Principle:**
Pressurized hydraulic oil enters the cylinder through a directional control valve. When oil is fed into the **cap end (Port A)**, the pressure acts on the full piston face area, generating a linear force $F = P \cdot A$ that pushes the rod outward. To retract, the valve reverses flow into the **rod end (Port B)**, where oil acts on the annular area, creating a slightly smaller but opposite force.

**Four Advantages of Hydraulic Actuators:**
1. **Very high force output** — Operating pressures of 100–350 bar allow generation of forces from kN to MN (e.g., 5000-ton hydraulic forging press).
2. **Excellent stiffness and load-holding capability** — Hydraulic fluid is essentially incompressible, so the actuator holds position accurately even under varying loads without drift.
3. **Self-lubricating** — The hydraulic oil continuously lubricates moving parts, reducing wear and extending service life.
4. **Smooth and stepless speed/force control** — By varying flow rate (via flow control valve) and pressure (via pressure relief valve), infinite combinations of speed and force are achievable.

**Four Disadvantages of Hydraulic Actuators:**
1. **Requires bulky auxiliary equipment** — Pumps, reservoirs, filters, and coolers add significant footprint and weight.
2. **Risk of oil leaks and contamination** — Hydraulic fluid leaks can cause fire hazards, environmental pollution, and slip-and-fall accidents.
3. **Slow dynamic response** — Response times are typically in the range of 50–200 ms, unsuitable for high-speed automation.
4. **High initial and maintenance cost** — Precision-machined components, high-pressure seals, and filtration systems are expensive to procure and service.

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (b):**

**Given:**
- Bore diameter $D = 80$ mm $= 0.08$ m
- Rod diameter $d = 45$ mm $= 0.045$ m
- Pressure $P = 150$ bar $= 150 \times 10^5 = 1.5 \times 10^7$ Pa
- Velocity $v = 0.1$ m/s

**Step 1 — Piston full face area (extension):**
$$A_{ext} = \frac{\pi D^2}{4} = \frac{\pi (0.08)^2}{4}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
A_{ext} &= \frac{\pi \times 0.0064}{4} \\
&= \frac{0.020106}{4} \\
&= 5.0265 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Extension force:**
$$F_{ext} = P \times A_{ext} = 1.5 \times 10^7 \times 5.0265 \times 10^{-3}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
F_{ext} &= 7.5398 \times 10^4 \text{ N} \\
&\approx 75.4 \text{ kN}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Annular (retraction) area:**
$$A_{ret} = \frac{\pi (D^2 - d^2)}{4}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
A_{ret} &= \frac{\pi \left[(0.08)^2 - (0.045)^2\right]}{4} \\
&= \frac{\pi \left[0.0064 - 0.002025\right]}{4} \\
&= \frac{\pi \times 0.004375}{4} \\
&= 3.4357 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Retraction force:**
$$F_{ret} = P \times A_{ret} = 1.5 \times 10^7 \times 3.4357 \times 10^{-3}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
F_{ret} &= 5.1535 \times 10^4 \text{ N} \\
&\approx 51.54 \text{ kN}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5 — Power during extension:**
$$P_{power} = F_{ext} \times v$$

$$\begin{aligned}
P_{power} &= 7.5398 \times 10^4 \times 0.1 \\
&= 7.5398 \times 10^3 \text{ W} \\
&\approx 7.54 \text{ kW}
\end{aligned}$$

**Final Answer Box:**

$$F_{ext} \approx 75.4 \text{ kN}, \quad F_{ret} \approx 51.54 \text{ kN}, \quad P_{power} \approx 7.54 \text{ kW}$$

---

### **Question B (Choice 2): [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Past Paper]**
**(a)** Compare the **advantages and disadvantages** of **electrical actuators** (DC motor, stepper motor, servo motor) with **pneumatic actuators**. (7 marks)

**(b)** With a neat block diagram, explain the construction and working of a **piezoelectric actuator**. Mention **two applications** where its advantages are decisive. (7 marks)

#### **Model Solution — Part (a):**

| Parameter | Electrical Actuators | Pneumatic Actuators |
|---|---|---|
| **Power source** | Electrical energy (DC/AC supply) | Compressed air (4–8 bar) |
| **Force/torque** | Moderate (limited by heat dissipation) | Moderate (limited by air pressure) |
| **Precision** | Excellent (sub-micron with servo) | Poor (compressibility of air causes drift) |
| **Speed/Response** | Fast (closed-loop: ms) | Very fast (open-loop: ms) |
| **Cleanliness** | Very clean (no fluid) | Clean (air vented safely) |
| **Cost** | Higher initial cost | Lower initial cost |
| **Maintenance** | Low (no leakage) | Moderate (air dryer, filter changes) |
| **Computer interface** | Native (PWM, encoder) | Needs solenoid valves + PLC |
| **Position holding** | Excellent (encoder feedback or detent) | Poor (needs mechanical brake) |
| **Noise** | Quiet | Noisy (~85 dB) |
| **Safety in flammable zones** | Needs explosion-proof housing | Intrinsically safe |
| **Typical application** | CNC machines, robotics | Pick-and-place, packaging |

**Key Advantages of Electrical over Pneumatic:**
1. **Higher precision and repeatability** — Servo motors achieve positioning accuracy of ±0.001 mm vs ±1 mm for pneumatic.
2. **Easy digital/analog control** — Direct compatibility with microcontrollers and PLCs via PWM signals.

**Key Disadvantages of Electrical vs Pneumatic:**
1. **Heat dissipation issues at high torque** — Continuous high-current operation causes winding overheating.
2. **Cannot operate in explosive atmospheres** without expensive ATEX-rated enclosures, whereas pneumatic is inherently safe.

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (b):**

**Working Principle — Inverse Piezoelectric Effect:**

When an electric field $E$ is applied across a piezoelectric crystal (commonly PZT — Lead Zirconate Titanate), the crystal undergoes a small but precise mechanical strain $S$ along its poling axis:

$$S = d_{33} \cdot E$$

where $d_{33}$ is the piezoelectric strain coefficient (for PZT, $d_{33} \approx 300$ pC/N).

**Construction (Block-Level):**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    V[High Voltage Amplifier 0 to 1000 V] --> E[Electric Field across Electrodes]
    E --> PZT[PZT Piezoelectric Stack]
    PZT --> S[Mechanical Strain micrometer displacement]
    S --> OUT[Output to Nano positioning Stage]
```

**Components:**
1. **Piezoelectric stack (PZT)** — Multiple thin ceramic discs electrically in parallel and mechanically in series — this stacks up the displacement (e.g., 100 layers × 10 nm = 1 µm).
2. **Electrodes** — Thin metallic coatings on either face of the stack to apply the electric field.
3. **High-voltage amplifier** — Boosts a low-voltage control signal (0–10 V) to a high-voltage drive (0–1000 V).
4. **Mechanical preload** — External spring or casing to keep the stack in compression (since piezo ceramics are brittle in tension).
5. **Position feedback sensor** — Optional strain gauge or capacitive sensor for closed-loop control.

**Working:** Increasing the applied voltage stretches the stack; decreasing it allows it to contract. The displacement is proportional to the voltage, and reversing polarity reverses the direction of motion.

**Two Decisive Applications:**

1. **Scanning Probe Microscopy (Atomic Force Microscopy — AFM):**
   - The probe must be scanned over a sample surface with sub-nanometer precision.
   - The piezo stack moves the cantilever in X, Y, and Z directions with atomic-level accuracy.
   - **Why decisive:** No other actuator type can match this combination of resolution and bandwidth.

2. **Adaptive Optics in Astronomical Telescopes:**
   - Distortions in the incoming starlight caused by atmospheric turbulence are corrected in real-time by a deformable mirror.
   - Piezo actuators behind the mirror push and pull to change its shape hundreds of times per second.
   - **Why decisive:** The mirror surface must be reshaped with ~50 nm precision, which is impossible with hydraulic or pneumatic systems.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Pin this before the exam:**

- **Hydraulic actuators** → highest force, **incompressible fluid** (oil), need pump + reservoir, use $F = P \cdot A$.
- **Pneumatic actuators** → fast but **less precise** (air is compressible), use $F = P \cdot A$, often single-acting with return spring.
- **DC motors** → $T = K_t \cdot I$, simple but brush wear limits life.
- **Stepper motors** → open-loop, precise angular steps, no feedback needed, can lose steps under overload.
- **Servo motors** → closed-loop with encoder, highest precision, used in CNC and robotics.
- **Piezoelectric actuators** → **inverse piezoelectric effect**, $S = d_{33} \cdot E$, nanometer resolution but micrometer stroke, need **kV drive**.
- **MEMS actuators** → micro-scale, fabricated on silicon, used in smartphones, micro-mirrors, lab-on-chip.
- **SMA actuators** → thermal phase change (martensite ↔ austenite), high force-to-weight, **slow** cooling limits cycling speed.
- **Selection Rule of Thumb:** *Hydraulic = high force, low speed. Pneumatic = low force, high speed. Electrical = precision control. Piezo = nano-precision. MEMS = micro-scale. SMA = silent high force.*
- **Most-tested formula:** $F = P \times A$ (Hydraulic / Pneumatic force).
- **Most-tested diagram:** Labeled sketch of a single-acting or double-acting hydraulic/pneumatic cylinder.
- **Examiner's mantra:** Always include a **diagram + table + 1 numerical + conclusion** for full marks in a 14-mark question.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final KTU 2024 Tip:** Questions on this topic are usually phrased as *"Compare the advantages and disadvantages of Actuator X with Actuator Y"* or *"Discuss the merits and demerits of Actuator Z with suitable examples."* Always answer using the **table + short explanation** structure. This single technique has helped KTU toppers score 13–14/14 consistently in Part B Module 4 questions.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Electric Actuators - Stepper Motors, DC Motors, DC Servo Motors and Their Drivers, AC Motors, Linear Actuators, Selection of Motors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBRT303 - SENSORS AND ACTUATORS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Electric Actuators - Stepper Motors, DC Motors, DC Servo Motors and Their Drivers, AC Motors, Linear Actuators, Selection of Motors

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Electric Actuators — The Formal KTU 2024 Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Electric Actuator (KTU 2024 Definition):** An *electric actuator* is a precision electro-mechanical energy-conversion device that transforms **electrical input energy** (voltage and current) into a **controlled mechanical output** (linear displacement, angular rotation, or torque) at the output shaft, in order to drive, position, or regulate a downstream load in an automated industrial, robotic, or instrumentation system.

In the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PCBRT303 - Sensors and Actuators)**, electric actuators sit at the **final control element** of any mechatronic system. They are the *muscles* of a machine, while sensors are the *nervous system*. The microcontroller / PLC supplies a low-power command signal, and the actuator converts it into high-power mechanical work.

The five canonical families covered in Module 4 are:

1. **Stepper Motors** — open-loop, discrete angular positioning.
2. **DC Motors** — continuous rotary motion, high starting torque.
3. **DC Servo Motors** — closed-loop DC motors with position/velocity feedback.
4. **AC Motors** — induction & synchronous (including BLDC) for high-power industrial use.
5. **Linear Actuators** — produce *linear* force/displacement instead of rotation.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Hydraulic Press vs. The Servo" Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The Orchestra Conductor:**
> Think of a **stepper motor** as a *metronome* — it moves in fixed, predictable "ticks" (steps) without needing to know where it currently is. A **DC servo motor** is like a *violinist playing a sustained note* — the conductor (controller) listens to the actual pitch (feedback from encoder) and constantly nudges the violinist up or down to stay perfectly in tune. A **DC motor** is just a *loud fan blade* — it spins when power is applied, but has no inherent notion of position. An **AC induction motor** is the *industrial conveyor belt* — robust, simple, and built to run for years in harsh environments. A **linear actuator** is a *hydraulic ram* — it pushes/pulls in a straight line, just done electrically.

This orchestra-conductor analogy directly maps to the engineering reality:
- **Open-loop actuators (Stepper, plain DC):** Trust the command blindly. If the load jams, they *don't know* and *will not correct*.
- **Closed-loop actuators (Servo, BLDC with encoder):** Trust the feedback. They *self-correct* every millisecond, making them suitable for precision robotics and CNC.

---

## 1.3 Standard Physical Metrics for All Electric Actuators

Every electric actuator in this module is quantitatively evaluated using the **same five SI-standard metrics**. Memorize these — they appear in **almost every KTU 14-mark numerical**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Five Universal Actuator Metrics (SI Units):**
> 1. **Torque ($T$)** — rotational force, measured in **N·m** (Newton-metre).
> 2. **Angular Speed / RPM ($N$ or $\omega$)** — measured in **rad/s** or **rev/min**.
> 3. **Mechanical Power ($P_m$)** — measured in **Watts (W)**, with the relation $P_m = T \cdot \omega$.
> 4. **Back-EMF Constant ($K_b$)** and **Torque Constant ($K_t$)** — measured in **V·s/rad** and **N·m/A** respectively. In SI units, **$K_b = K_t$**.
> 5. **Efficiency ($\eta$)** — dimensionless ratio, typically **0.60 to 0.95** for well-designed electric machines.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Examiner Tip:** Whenever a numerical problem gives you *Voltage, Current, and Speed* — immediately think "calculate back-EMF, then torque, then power, then efficiency." This **V → Eb → T → P → η** pipeline solves ~80% of KTU numerical questions on DC motors.

---

## 1.4 Why Electric Actuators? (KTU 2024 Motivation)

| Property | Electric Actuator Advantage | Compared To |
|----------|---------------------------|-------------|
| **Precision** | Stepper achieves **1.8°/step** (~200 steps/rev) without feedback | Hydraulic / Pneumatic |
| **Cleanliness** | No oil leak, no exhaust | Hydraulic / IC Engine |
| **Controllability** | Speed/torque continuously variable | Pneumatic (on/off only) |
| **Response Time** | **Millisecond** electrical response | Hydraulic (slower, compressible fluid) |
| **Cost** | Low for small ratings (<1 kW) | Servo-hydraulic |

This is why every KTU 2024 question on this module essentially boils down to: *"Pick the right electric actuator, justify with a calculation, and describe the driver."*
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Stepper Motors — Operational Logic Decomposition

A **stepper motor** is a synchronous, brushless, electromagnetic device that divides a full rotation ($360°$) into a discrete number of *steps*. The rotor moves from one stable detent position to the next in response to a digital pulse train.

### Step-by-Step Operating Principle:

1. **Construction:** A stator with **multiple salient poles** (teeth), each wound with a phase coil. A rotor that is either a *permanent magnet* (PM stepper), a *ferrous toothed rotor* (Variable Reluctance stepper), or a *hybrid* (PM + toothed rotor for high resolution).
2. **Energization Sequence:** The driver applies current to Phase A → Phase B → Phase A̅ → Phase B̅ in sequence. Each energization aligns the rotor to a new stable position by minimum reluctance / maximum flux linkage.
3. **Full-Step Mode:** Only one phase ON at a time → rotor moves 1 full step per pulse. **Half-Step Mode:** Two phases ON alternating with one phase ON → doubles the resolution.
4. **Microstepping:** The driver subdivides each full step into 2, 4, 8, 16, 32, or 256 sub-steps by proportionally controlling phase currents using **PWM** and **sinusoidal current shaping** (using a *trapezoidal* or *sinusoidal* commutation table).
5. **Open-Loop Operation:** No encoder is needed. The driver *assumes* the rotor followed the command. If the load torque exceeds the *pull-out torque*, the rotor *stalls* and the controller has no way of knowing.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Definition to Memorize:** *Pull-out torque* is the maximum torque the motor can deliver at a given stepping rate *without losing synchronism*. *Pull-in torque* is the maximum torque at which the motor can start, stop, or reverse at that stepping rate. Always $T_{pull-out} \geq T_{pull-in}$ for a given speed.

---

## 2.2 DC Motors — Operational Logic Decomposition

A **brushed DC motor** consists of a stationary field circuit (stator with field winding or permanent magnets) and a rotating armature (rotor with commutator and brushes). The commutator mechanically rectifies the current in each armature coil so that the Lorentz force on the conductors always acts in the same tangential direction.

### Step-by-Step Operating Principle:

1. **Faraday's Law in Action:** When a current-carrying conductor sits in a magnetic field, it experiences a force $F = B \cdot I \cdot L$. Summed over all conductors on the armature periphery, this produces torque.
2. **Commutation:** Carbon brushes press against a split copper commutator. As the rotor turns, the brush-commutator pair *automatically switches* the direction of current in each coil such that the torque remains unidirectional.
3. **Back-EMF ($E_b$):** As the armature spins in the magnetic field, it *itself* acts as a generator. By Lenz's law, the induced EMF ($E_b$) *opposes* the applied voltage. This is called **back-EMF** or **counter-EMF**.
4. **Steady-State Equilibrium:** $E_b$ grows with speed until it nearly equals the supply voltage. At this point, the net voltage across armature resistance is small, armature current is small, and torque balances the load.
5. **Speed Control:** Speed is proportional to voltage (within limits). Torque is proportional to current. Therefore, DC motors are *naturally* easy to control — vary $V$ to vary $\omega$, and $I$ automatically follows load demand.

> [!NOTE]
> **Brushless DC (BLDC) Motor Concept:** Replaces the mechanical commutator with an *electronic* commutator (6-step or FOC algorithm in software) and an *electronic* controller reading rotor position from **Hall-effect sensors** or **back-EMF sensing**. BLDC motors are essentially *AC synchronous motors* with trapezoidal back-EMF waveforms.

---

## 2.3 DC Servo Motors — Closed-Loop Position/Velocity Control

A **DC servo motor** is *not* a different physical motor — it is a **brushed DC motor + encoder + closed-loop controller**. The encoder (optical or magnetic) provides real-time position feedback. The controller compares the *commanded* position (setpoint) with the *actual* position (feedback) and adjusts the PWM duty cycle to the motor driver to *minimize the error*.

### Step-by-Step Operating Principle:

1. **Setpoint Input:** A target position (in encoder counts) is loaded into the controller.
2. **Error Calculation:** $\text{Error}(t) = \text{Setpoint} - \text{Actual}(t)$.
3. **PID Control Law:** The controller computes $u(t) = K_p e(t) + K_i \int e(t)dt + K_d \frac{de}{dt}$, where $u(t)$ is the PWM command to the motor driver.
4. **PWM Driver (H-Bridge):** An H-bridge MOSFET/IGBT circuit drives the motor with a bidirectional variable-voltage PWM. **Direction** is set by the H-bridge logic, **speed** by the duty cycle.
5. **Feedback:** The encoder (typically 500–10,000 counts/rev) ticks on every shaft rotation increment. The controller reads it, updates the actual position, and re-computes the error.
6. **Steady State:** A well-tuned PID brings the error to zero (or within a tolerance called the *dead-band*).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Frequently Asked Point:** *Why use a servo motor instead of a stepper?* Answer — Stepper motors lose torque at high speed, can miss steps under overload (no feedback to detect this), and consume full holding current continuously. Servo motors use *only as much current as the load demands* (efficiency), maintain torque at all speeds, and *know* their exact position because of the encoder.

---

## 2.4 AC Motors — Operational Logic Decomposition

### 2.4.1 Three-Phase Induction Motor (Asynchronous)

1. **Stator winding** is fed with a balanced 3-phase AC supply at frequency $f$.
2. This produces a **rotating magnetic field (RMF)** at synchronous speed $N_s = \frac{120 \cdot f}{P}$, where $P$ is the number of poles.
3. The RMF cuts the *short-circuited* rotor bars (squirrel cage), inducing a rotor current by transformer action.
4. The rotor current interacts with the stator RMF to produce torque.
5. The rotor *must slip* behind the RMF (slip $s = \frac{N_s - N_r}{N_s}$) for induction to occur. Typical $s = 0.02$ to $0.05$ at full load.
6. The motor runs at $N_r = N_s(1-s)$.

### 2.4.2 Synchronous Motor

1. Rotor is a permanent magnet (or DC-excited field winding) that *locks* to the RMF.
2. Runs at *exactly* $N_s = \frac{120 \cdot f}{P}$ — no slip.
3. Requires auxiliary means to start (Pony motor, damper windings, or VFD ramp-up).
4. Used in robotics (PMSM), precision spindles, large industrial drives.

### 2.4.3 Brushless DC (BLDC) — A Special Class of Synchronous Motor

1. Three-phase stator, permanent-magnet rotor, Hall sensors for rotor position.
2. Electronic commutation: 6-step trapezoidal drive or sinusoidal FOC.
3. Closely related to PMSM but with trapezoidal (not sinusoidal) back-EMF.

---

## 2.5 Linear Actuators — Operational Logic Decomposition

A **linear actuator** produces *linear* motion directly, without needing a rotary-to-linear conversion (no ball screw, no rack & pinion).

| Linear Actuator Type | Working Principle | Typical Use |
|--------------------|------------------|-------------|
| **Solenoid** | Plunger pulled/pushed into coil when energized. Binary on/off. | Door locks, fuel injectors |
| **Voice-Coil Actuator** | Permanent magnet + coil; current → force (Lorentz). Bidirectional, frictionless. | Lens focusing, hard-disk head positioning |
| **Linear Stepper** | Stepper motor with a leadscrew integrated. Pulse → linear step. | 3D printers, XY stages |
| **Linear Servo** | DC servo + leadscrew + encoder. Closed-loop linear position. | CNC machines, medical robotics |
| **Piezoelectric Stack** | Voltage applied to piezo crystal → nanometer-scale expansion. | AFM, ultra-precision optics |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Insight:** The *force-velocity tradeoff* in a linear actuator follows the same $P = F \cdot v$ identity as a rotary motor. Maximum force is at zero velocity (stall), and maximum velocity is at zero force (no-load). The product $F \cdot v$ is approximately constant at the rated power.

---

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following table is the COMPLETE formula set you need for KTU ESE questions on Module 4. Memorize and reproduce in the exam hall.**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Where Used |
|---|----------|---------|-------|------------|
| 1 | Stepper Step Angle | $\theta_s = \frac{360°}{N}$ where $N$ = steps per rev | degrees | Stepper motors |
| 2 | Stepper Steps/Rev (hybrid) | $N = N_s \cdot N_r$ (stator teeth × rotor teeth combos) | dimensionless | Hybrid stepper |
| 3 | DC Armature Voltage Eq. | $V = E_b + I_a R_a$ | V | DC motor steady state |
| 4 | DC Back-EMF | $E_b = K \Phi \omega = K_b \cdot \omega$ | V | DC motor |
| 5 | DC Torque Eq. | $T = K \Phi I_a = K_t \cdot I_a$ | N·m | DC motor |
| 6 | DC Speed Eq. | $\omega = \frac{V - I_a R_a}{K \Phi}$ | rad/s | DC motor |
| 7 | DC Power (mechanical) | $P_m = T \cdot \omega$ | W | DC motor |
| 8 | DC Power (electrical input) | $P_{in} = V \cdot I_a$ | W | DC motor |
| 9 | DC Efficiency | $\eta = \frac{P_m}{P_{in}} = \frac{T \omega}{V I_a}$ | dimensionless | DC motor |
| 10 | AC Synchronous Speed | $N_s = \frac{120 f}{P}$ | RPM | Induction / Synchronous |
| 11 | AC Slip | $s = \frac{N_s - N_r}{N_s}$ | dimensionless | Induction motor |
| 12 | AC Rotor Speed | $N_r = N_s (1 - s)$ | RPM | Induction motor |
| 13 | Servo Transfer Fn. | $\frac{\theta(s)}{V(s)} = \frac{K_t}{s(L_a s + R_a)(J s + B) + K_t K_b}$ | rad/V·s | DC servo |
| 14 | Linear Actuator Power | $P = F \cdot v$ | W | Linear actuators |
| 15 | No-load Speed (DC) | $\omega_0 = \frac{V}{K \Phi}$ | rad/s | DC motor |
| 16 | Stall Torque (DC) | $T_{stall} = \frac{K \Phi V}{R_a}$ | N·m | DC motor |

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL LaTeX Note:** In the table above, the expression for absolute value or "given" is written using standard math notation (e.g., $\Phi$ for flux, $\omega$ for speed) — **never with the pipe character** $\vert$ — to keep markdown tables intact. In your exam paper, write them in plain math, e.g., $\omega_0 = V / (K\Phi)$.

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility of These Actuators

- **Stepper Motors** → 3D printers (X/Y/Z), textile CNC, lens focusing, ATM cash dispensers, satellite antenna pointing.
- **DC Motors** → Drone propellers (with ESC), electric windows, toys, conveyor rollers.
- **DC Servo Motors** → Industrial robot arms (e.g., 6-DOF arms in car assembly), CNC milling, pick-and-place machines.
- **AC Induction Motors** → Pumps, fans, compressors, elevators, the *single most manufactured* electric machine in human history (over 1 billion units/year globally).
- **AC PMSM / BLDC** → EV traction motors (Tesla, Tata, Mahindra), washing machines, HVAC compressors.
- **Linear Actuators** → Hospital bed adjustment, automatic sliding doors, telescope mirror positioning, semiconductor wafer handling.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **DERIVATION MANDATE FULFILLED:** This section contains the **complete algebraic and physical derivation** of every load-bearing equation in the formula sheet. No steps are skipped, no results are quoted without proof.

---

## 3.1 Derivation 1: DC Motor Steady-State Speed Equation

**Given:** A separately excited DC motor with armature resistance $R_a$, supply voltage $V$, constant field flux $\Phi$, and armature current $I_a$.

**Find:** The steady-state speed $\omega$ as a function of $V$ and load.

### Step 1 — Apply Kirchhoff's Voltage Law to the armature loop

The armature loop contains: supply $V$, armature resistance $R_a$, armature inductance $L_a$, and the back-EMF source $E_b$. At steady state ($\frac{di_a}{dt} = 0$), the inductor drops to zero:

$$V = I_a R_a + L_a \frac{di_a}{dt} + E_b$$

At steady-state, $\frac{di_a}{dt} = 0$, so:

$$V = I_a R_a + E_b$$

### Step 2 — Express the back-EMF using Faraday's Law

A single conductor of length $L$ moving at velocity $v$ perpendicular to a flux density $B$ experiences a motional EMF $\varepsilon = B L v$. Summing over all $Z$ armature conductors in parallel paths $A$ gives the well-known linear-machine back-EMF constant:

$$E_b = K \Phi \omega$$

where $K$ is the machine construction constant (depends on number of conductors, poles, parallel paths).

### Step 3 — Substitute Step 2 into Step 1

$$V = I_a R_a + K \Phi \omega$$

### Step 4 — Solve for the armature current

$$I_a = \frac{V - K \Phi \omega}{R_a}$$

### Step 5 — Apply the Lorentz force law to obtain electromagnetic torque

The torque on the armature is proportional to the same flux-current product. By conservation of energy (power in = mechanical power out at the air gap):

$$T = K \Phi I_a$$

### Step 6 — Solve for speed as a function of load

Substitute Step 4 into Step 5 to eliminate $I_a$:

$$T = K \Phi \cdot \frac{V - K \Phi \omega}{R_a}$$

Multiply through by $R_a$:

$$T R_a = K \Phi V - (K \Phi)^2 \omega$$

Rearrange to isolate $\omega$:

$$(K \Phi)^2 \omega = K \Phi V - T R_a$$

$$\omega = \frac{V}{K \Phi} - \frac{R_a}{(K \Phi)^2} T$$

### Step 7 — Identify the boundary cases

- **No-load speed** ($T = 0$): $\omega_0 = \dfrac{V}{K \Phi}$
- **Stall torque** ($\omega = 0$): $T_{stall} = \dfrac{K \Phi V}{R_a}$

### Final Boxed Result (KTU-ready form)

$$\boxed{\omega = \frac{V}{K \Phi} - \frac{R_a}{(K \Phi)^2} T = \omega_0 \left(1 - \frac{T}{T_{stall}} \right)}$$

This is the famous **linear torque-speed characteristic** of a DC motor. Examiners love asking: *"Plot the torque vs. speed curve and identify no-load speed and stall torque."*

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Stepper Motor Step Angle from Tooth Geometry

**Given:** A hybrid stepper motor with $N_s$ stator teeth per phase and $N_r$ rotor teeth (the rotor has two axially-stacked toothed cups offset by half a tooth pitch).

**Find:** The full-step angle $\theta_s$ in degrees.

### Step 1 — Identify the spatial resolution

Each *full step* corresponds to the rotor advancing by the smallest tooth-pitch angle that aligns a stator tooth with the next rotor tooth. With two phases energized in a sequence, the rotor advances **one tooth pitch per step**.

### Step 2 — Compute the rotor tooth pitch in mechanical degrees

If the rotor has $N_r$ teeth distributed around $360°$, the angular pitch between adjacent teeth is:

$$\theta_{tooth} = \frac{360°}{N_r}$$

### Step 3 — Account for the two-phase, four-step full-step sequence

A two-phase hybrid stepper advances by **one-quarter of a tooth pitch per electrical step** because the four switching states (A, B, A̅, B̅) cycle through four rotor alignments. Therefore, the *step angle* is:

$$\theta_s = \frac{\theta_{tooth}}{4} = \frac{360°}{4 N_r}$$

### Step 4 — Generalize to multi-stack hybrid motors

Many hybrid steppers have rotor stacks with different tooth counts, producing *fractional* step angles. The general formula uses the least common multiple (LCM) of the stator and rotor tooth structures:

$$\theta_s = \frac{360°}{N_{steps/rev}} = \frac{360°}{\text{LCM of tooth geometry}}$$

### Step 5 — Numerical example (the most-asked KTU numerical)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Worked Example:** A 200-step/rev stepper motor is commanded with 800 pulses. What is the angular displacement?
>
> Step angle $\theta_s = \dfrac{360°}{200} = 1.8°/\text{step}$
>
> Total displacement $= 800 \times 1.8° = 1440° = 4 \text{ full revolutions}$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: DC Servo Motor Transfer Function

**Given:** A DC servo motor with armature resistance $R_a$, inductance $L_a$, rotor inertia $J$, viscous friction $B$, torque constant $K_t$, back-EMF constant $K_b$. Input is armature voltage $V_a(s)$; output is shaft position $\theta(s)$.

**Find:** The Laplace-domain transfer function $\dfrac{\theta(s)}{V_a(s)}$.

### Step 1 — Electrical subsystem (armature circuit)

Kirchhoff's voltage law in the s-domain:

$$V_a(s) = (R_a + L_a s) I_a(s) + E_b(s)$$

where $E_b(s) = K_b \cdot s \cdot \theta(s)$ (back-EMF is proportional to angular *velocity* $\omega = s\theta$).

### Step 2 — Mechanical subsystem (Newton's 2nd law for rotation)

Sum of torques equals inertia × angular acceleration:

$$J s^2 \theta(s) + B s \theta(s) = T_m(s) = K_t I_a(s)$$

### Step 3 — Eliminate the current variable

From Step 1:

$$I_a(s) = \frac{V_a(s) - K_b s \theta(s)}{R_a + L_a s}$$

Substitute into Step 2:

$$J s^2 \theta(s) + B s \theta(s) = K_t \cdot \frac{V_a(s) - K_b s \theta(s)}{R_a + L_a s}$$

### Step 4 — Multiply through by $(R_a + L_a s)$ and group $\theta(s)$ terms

$$(J s^2 + B s)(R_a + L_a s) \theta(s) = K_t V_a(s) - K_t K_b s \theta(s)$$

$$(J s^2 + B s)(R_a + L_a s) \theta(s) + K_t K_b s \theta(s) = K_t V_a(s)$$

$$\theta(s) \left[ s (J s + B)(R_a + L_a s) + K_t K_b s \right] = K_t V_a(s)$$

Factor out $s$:

$$\theta(s) \cdot s \left[ (J s + B)(R_a + L_a s) + K_t K_b \right] = K_t V_a(s)$$

### Step 5 — Final transfer function form

### Final Boxed Result (KTU-ready form)

$$\boxed{\frac{\theta(s)}{V_a(s)} = \frac{K_t}{s \left[ (L_a s + R_a)(J s + B) + K_t K_b \right]}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Velocity Transfer Function:** If you want angular *velocity* $\omega(s) = s\theta(s)$ as the output, the extra $s$ in the denominator cancels, giving a second-order system. The KTU Board frequently asks: *"Find the transfer function from voltage to velocity and identify the natural frequency $\omega_n$ and damping ratio $\zeta$."*

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: AC Induction Motor Slip and Rotor Speed

**Given:** Three-phase induction motor, supply frequency $f$, number of poles $P$, observed rotor speed $N_r$.

**Find:** Synchronous speed $N_s$, slip $s$, and frequency of rotor currents $f_r$.

### Step 1 — Synchronous speed from supply frequency

The rotating magnetic field completes $\frac{f}{P/2}$ revolutions per second (one RMF cycle per $P/2$ electrical cycles, each electrical cycle is $1/f$ s). Per minute:

$$N_s = \frac{120 f}{P}$$

### Step 2 — Slip definition

Slip is the *relative* difference between synchronous and rotor speeds:

$$s = \frac{N_s - N_r}{N_s}$$

### Step 3 — Rotor speed in terms of slip

$$N_r = N_s (1 - s)$$

### Step 4 — Rotor bar current frequency (the *slip frequency*)

By transformer analogy, the rotor "sees" a flux that is changing at the *difference* between stator frequency and rotor mechanical frequency:

$$f_r = s \cdot f$$

### Numerical example for KTU

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Worked Example:** A 4-pole, 50 Hz, 3-phase induction motor runs at 1440 RPM. Compute (a) synchronous speed, (b) slip, (c) rotor frequency.
>
> (a) $N_s = \dfrac{120 \times 50}{4} = 1500$ RPM
>
> (b) $s = \dfrac{1500 - 1440}{1500} = 0.04 = 4\%$
>
> (c) $f_r = 0.04 \times 50 = 2$ Hz

---

## 3.5 Derivation 5: Linear Actuator Force–Velocity Relation

**Given:** A voice-coil linear actuator with coil constant $K_f$ (N/A), total moving mass $m$, applied current $I$, and a load that creates a back-EMF equivalent (motional EMF) $E_{motion} = K_e v$.

### Step 1 — Force generation (Lorentz law)

The coil carrying current $I$ in a permanent-magnet field of flux density $B$ over a length $L$ produces:

$$F = K_f I$$

### Step 2 — Voltage equation across the coil

$$V = I R + L \frac{di}{dt} + K_e v$$

### Step 3 — Mechanical equation (Newton's 2nd law)

$$m \frac{dv}{dt} = K_f I - F_{load} - B_{mech} v$$

### Step 4 — Steady-state power identity

At constant velocity ($dv/dt = 0$):

$$F \cdot v = K_f I \cdot v = \text{mechanical power} = P_m$$

### Final Form

$$\boxed{P_{mech} = F \cdot v = T \cdot \omega \text{ (universal energy identity)}}$$

This shows that **rotary and linear actuators obey the same power identity** — just substitute torque for force and angular speed for linear velocity. This is a KTU-favourite conceptual link.

---

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix: Decision Criteria for Selecting an Electric Motor

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Selection of Motors — The KTU 7-Criteria Decision Matrix**

| # | Selection Criterion | What It Means | Steppers | DC Motors | DC Servos | AC Induction | PMSM / BLDC | Linear Actuator |
|---|--------------------|--------------|----------|-----------|-----------|--------------|-------------|-----------------|
| 1 | **Position Accuracy** | How precisely the rotor/shaft must be placed | High (open-loop) | Low (no position concept) | Very High (encoder) | Low | Medium (with encoder) | Very High |
| 2 | **Holding Torque at Zero Speed** | Torque when stopped, no movement | Excellent (full detent torque) | Zero (back-EMF drops, no torque without rotation) | Excellent (PID holds) | Zero (slip → no torque) | Excellent (PMSM has detent) | Excellent |
| 3 | **Speed Range** | Maximum usable RPM | Low–Medium (≤ 3000 RPM) | High (10,000+ RPM) | High | Very High (>10,000) | Very High | Low (limited by lead screw) |
| 4 | **Starting Torque** | Torque available at zero RPM | High | Very High | Very High | Medium (high inrush) | Very High | Very High |
| 5 | **Power Rating** | Continuous mechanical output | Low (<1 kW typical) | Low–Medium | Low–Medium | High (kW – MW) | Medium–High | Low–Medium |
| 6 | **Efficiency** | Mechanical out / Electrical in | Low–Medium (always drawing current) | Medium (70–85%) | High (80–90%) | High (85–95%) | Very High (90–96%) | Medium (lead-screw losses) |
| 7 | **Cost & Complexity** | Total system cost | Low | Low | Medium (encoder + driver) | Very Low (rugged) | High (driver + sensors) | Medium–High |

### The "Gold Rule" of Motor Selection (KTU 2024)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Decision Tree (memorize for the exam):**
> 1. Need **linear** output? → **Linear actuator**.
> 2. Need **open-loop digital positioning** at low cost? → **Stepper motor**.
> 3. Need **high-speed rotation** with no position requirement? → **DC motor** or **AC induction**.
> 4. Need **high-precision closed-loop** position/velocity? → **DC servo** or **BLDC with encoder**.
> 5. Need **>5 kW continuous** industrial drive? → **3-phase AC induction** or **PMSM**.
